topics.title: Properties of Fluid: Density
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT741 - FLUID MECHANICS & MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Properties of Fluid: Density

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Density (ρ)** is defined as the mass of a fluid per unit volume. It is a fundamental, intensive thermodynamic property of matter that quantifies how much mass is contained in a given three-dimensional space.

The mathematical representation of density is given by:

$$\rho = \lim_{\Delta V \to \Delta V_0} \frac{\Delta m}{\Delta V} = \frac{dm}{dV}$$

where:
- $\rho$ (rho) = Density of the fluid in **kg/m³**
- $dm$ = Infinitesimal mass element in **kilograms (kg)**
- $dV$ = Infinitesimal volume element in **cubic meters (m³)**

The limit is taken to a mathematically small (differential) volume $\Delta V_0$ because density may vary from point to point within a non-homogeneous fluid. In the case of a **homogeneous (uniform) fluid**, the density remains constant throughout the body, and the simple ratio $\rho = m/V$ is sufficient.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Values at 4 °C and 1 atm (101.325 kPa):**
> - **Density of pure water:** $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 1.0 \text{ g/cm}^3$
> - **Density of air (at 15 °C, 1 atm):** $\rho_{air} = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - **Density of mercury (at 20 °C):** $\rho_{Hg} = 13{,}546 \text{ kg/m}^3$

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine two identical **one-litre water bottles** sitting on a desk. Both bottles have the **exact same volume** ($1 \text{ L} = 0.001 \text{ m}^3$). However, if you fill one with **water** and the other with **honey**, the honey-filled bottle will feel noticeably heavier when you pick it up. Why?

Even though both fluids occupy identical space, the **honey contains more "stuff" (mass) packed into the same volume** compared to water. This "stuff-per-space" measurement is precisely what **density** captures. A high-density fluid like honey is "compact" with mass, while a low-density fluid like air is mostly empty space with a few molecules scattered throughout.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Visual Trick for Exams:** When asked to intuitively compare two fluids, the one that feels "heavier" per bucket is the denser one. This is why a small iron block sinks in water (iron ≈ 7874 kg/m³ > water = 1000 kg/m³), while a massive block of balsa wood floats (balsa wood ≈ 160 kg/m³ < water).

---

## 1.3 Classification of Density in Fluid Mechanics

For engineering analysis, density is typically classified into the following sub-categories based on how the fluid behaves under varying physical conditions:

| Sub-Property | Physical Meaning | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Mass Density (ρ)** | Mass per unit volume | General fluid statics and dynamics |
| **Specific Weight (γ)** | Weight per unit volume ($\gamma = \rho g$) | Pressure head calculations |
| **Specific Gravity (S)** | Dimensionless ratio of fluid density to water density | Comparing fluid heaviness |
| **Relative Density** | Synonymous with Specific Gravity in most KTU contexts | Material identification |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** Although **specific weight** and **specific gravity** are technically separate properties, KTU module 1 frequently tests them together with density under the umbrella "Properties of Fluid." A thorough understanding of all four sub-properties is essential for Part A short-answer questions.

---

## 1.4 Why Density Matters in KTU Fluid Mechanics

Density is the **cornerstone property** upon which nearly every subsequent topic in the syllabus rests:

- **Pressure calculation** depends directly on $\rho$ (via hydrostatic pressure: $P = \rho g h$).
- **Buoyancy and floatation** rely on comparing fluid densities.
- **Reynolds Number** uses $\rho$ to determine flow regime (laminar vs. turbulent).
- **Continuity equation** uses $\rho$ to distinguish between incompressible and compressible flows.
- **Bernoulli's equation** embeds $\rho$ in its energy-per-unit-volume form.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic: The "Why" Behind Density

The concept of density emerges from a fundamental engineering question: *"How do we predict whether a fluid will sink, float, exert pressure, or accelerate under a given force?"* The answer to all of these depends on how much mass is concentrated in a unit volume.

**Step 1 — Mass Concentration:** A denser fluid packs more molecules into the same container, making it heavier per unit volume. This affects weight, momentum, and inertia.

**Step 2 — Pressure Generation:** In a gravitational field, each layer of fluid must support the weight of all fluid above it. The heavier the column (higher $\rho$), the higher the pressure at the bottom.

**Step 3 — Buoyancy Behavior:** Archimedes' principle states that a body submerged in a fluid experiences an upward force equal to the weight of fluid displaced. This buoyant force is directly proportional to $\rho$.

**Step 4 — Flow Behavior:** In dynamic systems (pumps, turbines, pipes), the kinetic energy of a moving fluid scales with $\rho v^2$, where $v$ is velocity. The denser the fluid, the greater the momentum flux.

---

## 2.2 Related Property: Specific Weight (γ)

Specific weight is the **weight per unit volume** of a fluid, defined as:

$$\gamma = \rho \cdot g$$

where $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ is the acceleration due to gravity in SI units.

- **SI unit of γ:** $\text{N/m}^3$
- **Significance:** It directly converts fluid density into a force-per-volume metric, which is convenient when calculating hydrostatic pressure loads on dam walls, tank bottoms, and submerged surfaces.

---

## 2.3 Related Property: Specific Gravity (S)

Specific gravity (also called **relative density**) is a **dimensionless ratio** that expresses how dense a fluid is compared to a reference fluid, which is **pure water at 4 °C** for liquids and **air at standard conditions** for gases.

$$S = \frac{\rho_{\text{fluid}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for KTU Exams:** "Specific Gravity is **Specific to water**." Always remember that unless explicitly stated, water at 4 °C with $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ is the reference.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates **all density-related formulas** you must memorize for KTU 2024 ESE and internal assessments.

| # | Formula | Description | SI Units | Cognitive Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | Basic mass density | $\text{kg/m}^3$ | Defining density |
| 2 | $\rho = \dfrac{W}{Vg}$ | Density via weight | $\text{kg/m}^3$ | When only weight is given |
| 3 | $\gamma = \rho \cdot g$ | Specific weight | $\text{N/m}^3$ | Pressure calculations |
| 4 | $S = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{fluid}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}}$ | Specific gravity | Dimensionless | Comparing fluids |
| 5 | $\rho_{\text{water at 4°C}} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ | Reference value | $\text{kg/m}^3$ | Standard reference |
| 6 | $w = \dfrac{1}{\rho}$ | Specific volume | $\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$ | Thermodynamics |
| 7 | $\rho_{\text{mix}} = \dfrac{\sum m_i}{\sum V_i}$ | Density of a mixture | $\text{kg/m}^3$ | Mixing problems |
| 8 | $\rho_{\text{mix}} = \dfrac{m_1 + m_2}{\dfrac{m_1}{\rho_1} + \dfrac{m_2}{\rho_2}}$ | Two-fluid mixture | $\text{kg/m}^3$ | Common KTU problem |
| 9 | $P = \rho g h$ | Hydrostatic pressure | $\text{Pa}$ | Pressure head |
| 10 | $K = -V \dfrac{dP}{dV} = \rho \dfrac{dP}{d\rho}$ | Bulk modulus (compressibility) | $\text{Pa}$ | Compressibility link |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Confused Pair:** Students often interchange $\rho$ and $\gamma$. Remember — $\rho$ is in **kg/m³** (mass basis), whereas $\gamma$ is in **N/m³** (force basis). The conversion factor is **g = 9.81**.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Density is not merely a textbook number — it drives critical engineering decisions:

- **Hydraulic Dam Design:** The hydrostatic thrust on a dam face is $F = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho g h^2 \cdot L$, directly dependent on water density.
- **Aerospace Engineering:** Air density variation with altitude (using the International Standard Atmosphere model) determines lift on aircraft wings.
- **Petroleum Industry:** Crude oil density (often quoted in °API) governs pipeline flow rates and storage tank stratification.
- **HVAC Systems:** Duct design uses air density to size fans and estimate pressure drops.
- **Hydrometers and Pycnometers:** Density is the measured variable in these devices to assess battery acid, alcohol content, and milk purity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Fundamental Definition of Density

We start from the basic postulate that mass is a fundamental conserved quantity and volume is a geometric measure of three-dimensional space. The density $\rho$ at a point in a fluid is defined as the limiting value of the ratio of mass to volume as the volume shrinks to that point.

**Step 1:** Consider an arbitrary fluid element containing mass $m$ and occupying volume $V$. The average density over this finite element is:

$$\bar{\rho} = \frac{m}{V}$$

**Step 2:** To obtain the *point* density (which may differ from the average if the fluid is non-homogeneous), we take the differential limit:

$$\rho = \lim_{\Delta V \to dV} \frac{\Delta m}{\Delta V} = \frac{dm}{dV}$$

**Step 3:** Taking the differential of both sides yields the relationship between an infinitesimal mass element and its volume:

$$dm = \rho \, dV$$

**Step 4:** To find the total mass of a fluid body, integrate over the entire volume $V$:

$$m = \int_V \rho \, dV$$

For a **homogeneous (uniform) fluid**, $\rho$ is constant, so the integral collapses to the simple product:

$$m = \rho V \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \rho = \frac{m}{V}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Density of a Two-Fluid Mixture

A very common KTU 2024 problem type involves two immiscible fluids being mixed in a container. The challenge is that the **volumes are not simply additive** in the way masses are.

**Step 1:** Let two fluids have masses $m_1$ and $m_2$ with individual densities $\rho_1$ and $\rho_2$. The individual volumes are:

$$V_1 = \frac{m_1}{\rho_1} \qquad \text{and} \qquad V_2 = \frac{m_2}{\rho_2}$$

**Step 2:** The total mass of the mixture is additive (mass is conserved):

$$m_{\text{total}} = m_1 + m_2$$

**Step 3:** The total volume of the mixture is also additive for two immiscible fluids (no chemical reaction):

$$V_{\text{total}} = V_1 + V_2 = \frac{m_1}{\rho_1} + \frac{m_2}{\rho_2}$$

**Step 4:** The density of the mixture is defined as the ratio of total mass to total volume:

$$\rho_{\text{mix}} = \frac{m_{\text{total}}}{V_{\text{total}}}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute Steps 2 and 3 into Step 4:

$$\rho_{\text{mix}} = \frac{m_1 + m_2}{\dfrac{m_1}{\rho_1} + \dfrac{m_2}{\rho_2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the canonical **two-fluid mixture density formula** you must memorize. The KTU examiner loves testing this with a numerical twist (e.g., "Given volumes and densities, find the mixture density" — which requires first computing masses).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: KTU-Style Density Problem

> **Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
> A tank contains **0.4 m³** of an oil with density **850 kg/m³** mixed uniformly with **0.6 m³** of another oil with density **920 kg/m³**. Find:
> (a) The mass of each oil
> (b) The density of the mixture
> (c) The specific weight of the mixture
> (d) The specific gravity of the mixture

**Solution:**

**Part (a): Mass of each oil**

$$m_1 = \rho_1 \cdot V_1 = 850 \times 0.4 = 340 \text{ kg}$$

$$m_2 = \rho_2 \cdot V_2 = 920 \times 0.6 = 552 \text{ kg}$$

**Part (b): Density of the mixture**

Total mass:
$$m_{\text{total}} = 340 + 552 = 892 \text{ kg}$$

Total volume:
$$V_{\text{total}} = 0.4 + 0.6 = 1.0 \text{ m}^3$$

Mixture density:
$$\rho_{\text{mix}} = \frac{892}{1.0} = 892 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Part (c): Specific weight of the mixture**

$$\gamma_{\text{mix}} = \rho_{\text{mix}} \cdot g = 892 \times 9.81 = 8750.52 \text{ N/m}^3$$

**Part (d): Specific gravity of the mixture**

$$S_{\text{mix}} = \frac{\rho_{\text{mix}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}} = \frac{892}{1000} = 0.892$$

> [!TIP]
> **Verification Check:** Since both oils are lighter than water (850 < 1000 and 920 < 1000), the mixture **must** have a specific gravity less than 1. Our result 0.892 satisfies this sanity check. ✓

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison: Density of Common Substances

| Substance | State | Density $\rho$ (kg/m³) | Specific Gravity $S$ | Comment |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Air (15 °C, 1 atm) | Gas | 1.225 | 0.001225 | Lighter than water by factor ≈ 816 |
| Fresh Water (4 °C) | Liquid | 1000 | 1.000 | Reference fluid |
| Sea Water (25 °C) | Liquid | 1025 | 1.025 | Salt increases density |
| Mercury (20 °C) | Liquid | 13,546 | 13.546 | Used in barometers |
| Glycerine | Liquid | 1260 | 1.260 | Viscous industrial fluid |
| Petrol / Gasoline | Liquid | 740 | 0.740 | Highly flammable, low density |
| Milk (whole) | Liquid | 1030 | 1.030 | Used in dairy industry |
| Crude Oil | Liquid | 870 | 0.870 | Varies with source |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Concept Map of Density and Related Properties

The following Mermaid block diagram visualizes how density connects to the rest of fluid properties, illustrating the central role of $\rho$ in the KTU Module 1 syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["FLUID PROPERTY"] --> nodeB["Density rho"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Specific Weight gamma"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["Specific Gravity S"]
    nodeB --> nodeE["Specific Volume w"]
    nodeB --> nodeF["Bulk Modulus K"]
    nodeC --> nodeG["Hydrostatic Pressure P"]
    nodeD --> nodeH["Buoyancy and Floatation"]
    nodeE --> nodeI["Thermodynamic State"]
    nodeF --> nodeJ["Compressibility Analysis"]
    nodeG --> nodeK["Engineering Outputs"]
    nodeH --> nodeK
    nodeI --> nodeK
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL["Dam and Tank Design"]
    nodeK --> nodeM["Pump and Turbine Sizing"]
    nodeK --> nodeN["Pipeline Flow"]
    nodeK --> nodeO["Aircraft and Vehicles"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The central node "Density rho" branches outward into four derived properties (specific weight, specific gravity, specific volume, and bulk modulus), each of which feeds into a specific engineering outcome at the bottom. This is the **taxonomy** the KTU examiner expects you to recognize.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Methods of Measuring Fluid Density

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["FLUID SAMPLE"] --> node2{"Fluid State?"}
    node2 --> node3["Liquid"] --> node4["Hydrometer Method"]
    node2 --> node5["Gas"] --> node6["Gas Pycnometer"]
    node4 --> node7["Buoyancy Balance"]
    node6 --> node8["Reference Volume Chamber"]
    node7 --> node9["Density Value rho in kg per cubic meter"]
    node8 --> node9
    node9 --> node10["Specific Gravity S"]
    node9 --> node11["Specific Weight gamma"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Industrial laboratories use **oscillating U-tube density meters** (e.g., Anton Paar DMA series) that measure the natural frequency of a vibrating tube containing the fluid. The frequency depends on the mass inside, allowing real-time density readout to **0.0001 g/cm³** accuracy. This is the modern standard for battery acid and pharmaceutical quality control.

---

## 4.3 Comparative Visual: Density Hierarchy of Common Fluids

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["LIGHTER"] --> nodeB["Hydrogen 0.09 kg per cubic meter"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Air 1.225 kg per cubic meter"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Petrol 740 kg per cubic meter"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Water 1000 kg per cubic meter"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Sea Water 1025 kg per cubic meter"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Glycerine 1260 kg per cubic meter"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Mercury 13546 kg per cubic meter"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["HEAVIEST"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Shortcut:** KTU numerical problems often give you a fluid and ask "Will it float or sink in water?" The answer is immediate from this hierarchy: if the fluid's density < 1000 kg/m³, it floats; if > 1000 kg/m³, it sinks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q1. Define fluid density. State its SI unit and the standard value of density of water at 4 °C.**

**Model Answer:**

Fluid density is defined as the mass of a fluid per unit volume. Mathematically,

$$\rho = \frac{m}{V}$$

The SI unit of density is **kg/m³**. The standard density of pure water at 4 °C and 1 atm pressure is **1000 kg/m³**. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for unit, 1 for water value.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2. Distinguish between specific weight and specific gravity. Why is specific gravity dimensionless?**

**Model Answer:**

- **Specific Weight (γ)** is the weight per unit volume of a fluid, given by $\gamma = \rho g$. Its SI unit is **N/m³**.
- **Specific Gravity (S)** is the ratio of the density of a given fluid to the density of a reference fluid (water at 4 °C for liquids), given by $S = \rho_{\text{fluid}} / \rho_{\text{water}}$.

Specific gravity is dimensionless because it is a ratio of two quantities having identical units (kg/m³ ÷ kg/m³). The units cancel out, leaving a pure number. *(3 marks: 1 for each definition, 1 for dimensionless justification.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**

### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Define the term density of a fluid and derive an expression for the density of a mixture of two immiscible fluids. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hydraulic oil of volume **0.3 m³** and density **880 kg/m³** is mixed with another oil of volume **0.5 m³** and density **910 kg/m³**. Find (i) the mass of each oil, (ii) the density of the mixture, (iii) the specific weight, and (iv) the specific gravity of the mixture. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Definition and Derivation:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Note:** In KTU valuation, you must (1) clearly state the definition, (2) write the formula, and (3) show every algebraic step of the derivation to secure full marks. Skipping the integration step loses 2 marks immediately.

**Definition:** Density is the mass per unit volume of a fluid. For a homogeneous fluid, $\rho = m/V$. For a point in a non-homogeneous fluid, $\rho = dm/dV$.

**Derivation:** Consider two immiscible fluids of masses $m_1$, $m_2$ and densities $\rho_1$, $\rho_2$ respectively. Their individual volumes are:

$$V_1 = \frac{m_1}{\rho_1} \quad \text{and} \quad V_2 = \frac{m_2}{\rho_2}$$

By conservation of mass and additivity of volume for immiscible fluids:

$$m_{\text{total}} = m_1 + m_2$$
$$V_{\text{total}} = V_1 + V_2 = \frac{m_1}{\rho_1} + \frac{m_2}{\rho_2}$$

The mixture density is therefore:

$$\rho_{\text{mix}} = \frac{m_1 + m_2}{\dfrac{m_1}{\rho_1} + \dfrac{m_2}{\rho_2}}$$

This is the required expression. *(7 marks: 2 for definition, 5 for derivation.)*

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

Given: $V_1 = 0.3 \text{ m}^3$, $\rho_1 = 880 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $V_2 = 0.5 \text{ m}^3$, $\rho_2 = 910 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

**(i) Mass of each oil:**

$$m_1 = \rho_1 \cdot V_1 = 880 \times 0.3 = 264 \text{ kg}$$
$$m_2 = \rho_2 \cdot V_2 = 910 \times 0.5 = 455 \text{ kg}$$

**(ii) Density of the mixture:**

Total mass:
$$m_{\text{total}} = 264 + 455 = 719 \text{ kg}$$

Total volume:
$$V_{\text{total}} = 0.3 + 0.5 = 0.8 \text{ m}^3$$

Mixture density:
$$\rho_{\text{mix}} = \frac{719}{0.8} = 898.75 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**(iii) Specific weight of the mixture:**

$$\gamma_{\text{mix}} = \rho_{\text{mix}} \cdot g = 898.75 \times 9.81 = 8816.14 \text{ N/m}^3$$

**(iv) Specific gravity of the mixture:**

$$S_{\text{mix}} = \frac{\rho_{\text{mix}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}} = \frac{898.75}{1000} = 0.89875 \approx 0.899$$

*(7 marks: 2 for (i), 2 for (ii), 1.5 for (iii), 1.5 for (iv).)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the working principle of a **hydrometer**. How is it used to measure the specific gravity of liquids? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A storage tank has a base area of **4 m²** and contains **glycerine** of density **1260 kg/m³** up to a depth of **2.5 m**. Calculate: (i) the total mass of glycerine, (ii) the hydrostatic pressure at the base, (iii) the force exerted on the base. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Hydrometer Working:**

A hydrometer is a thin, sealed glass tube with a weighted bulb at the bottom (typically filled with mercury or lead shots) and a calibrated stem with graduation marks at the top.

**Working Principle:** It is based on **Archimedes' principle of buoyancy**. When floated in a liquid, the hydrometer displaces a volume of liquid whose weight equals the weight of the hydrometer. The denser the liquid, the less it sinks (less submerged depth); the lighter the liquid, the deeper it sinks.

The buoyancy equilibrium equation is:

$$W_{\text{hydro}} = \rho_{\text{liquid}} \cdot g \cdot V_{\text{submerged}}$$

Rearranging for density:

$$\rho_{\text{liquid}} = \frac{W_{\text{hydro}}}{g \cdot V_{\text{submerged}}}$$

By reading the graduation mark at the liquid surface, the specific gravity is read directly. For liquids **denser than water**, the scale starts at 1.000 at the bottom of the stem; for **lighter liquids**, the scale is read in reverse.

*(7 marks: 3 for description, 2 for principle, 2 for specific gravity reading.)*

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

Given: Base area $A = 4 \text{ m}^2$, $\rho = 1260 \text{ kg/m}^3$, depth $h = 2.5 \text{ m}$.

**(i) Total mass of glycerine:**

$$V = A \times h = 4 \times 2.5 = 10 \text{ m}^3$$

$$m = \rho \cdot V = 1260 \times 10 = 12{,}600 \text{ kg}$$

**(ii) Hydrostatic pressure at the base:**

$$P = \rho g h = 1260 \times 9.81 \times 2.5 = 30{,}901.5 \text{ Pa} = 30.90 \text{ kPa}$$

**(iii) Force on the base:**

$$F = P \times A = 30{,}901.5 \times 4 = 123{,}606 \text{ N} \approx 123.6 \text{ kN}$$

*(7 marks: 2 for (i), 2 for (ii), 3 for (iii).)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in final answer** — Always write $\text{kg/m}^3$, $\text{N/m}^3$, or $\text{Pa}$ explicitly. A numerical answer without a unit loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing $\rho$ with $\gamma$** — Students sometimes write "density of the mixture = 8816 N/m³" — this is specific weight, not density. **Density is always in kg/m³.**
> 3. **Using $g = 9.8$ instead of $g = 9.81$** — KTU officially uses **9.81 m/s²**. A 0.01 deviation is acceptable, but using 10 (round-off) is **not** unless the problem states so.
> 4. **Mixing up volume-additive and mass-additive** — When volumes are given, **first convert to masses** before applying the mixture formula. Skipping this step yields an incorrect answer.
> 5. **Not writing the formula before substitution** — Always write the governing equation symbolically *before* plugging in numbers. This earns the "method" mark even if arithmetic goes wrong.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Checklist for Density:**

- **Definition:** Density $\rho$ = mass per unit volume; SI unit = $\text{kg/m}^3$.
- **Reference value:** Water at 4 °C has $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (memorize this — it appears in 90% of KTU problems).
- **Specific Weight:** $\gamma = \rho g$ → units are $\text{N/m}^3$.
- **Specific Gravity:** $S = \rho_{\text{fluid}} / \rho_{\text{water}}$ → dimensionless number.
- **Mixture Formula:** $\rho_{\text{mix}} = (m_1 + m_2) / (V_1 + V_2) = (m_1 + m_2) / (m_1/\rho_1 + m_2/\rho_2)$.
- **Point Density:** $\rho = dm/dV$ (used for non-homogeneous fluids).
- **Bulk Modulus Connection:** $K = \rho (dP/d\rho)$ — shows how density changes with pressure.
- **Floating Rule:** Fluid floats on water if its $S < 1$; sinks if $S > 1$.
- **Engineering Linkage:** Density appears in hydrostatic pressure, buoyancy, Reynolds number, and Bernoulli's equation.
- **Experimental Methods:** Hydrometer (liquids), pycnometer (precise lab measurement), oscillating U-tube meter (industrial real-time).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Sentence Takeaway:** *"Density is the master property of a fluid — every pressure, force, and flow equation in KTU Module 1 ultimately traces its roots back to the value of $\rho$."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Specific Weight
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT741 - FLUID MECHANICS & MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Specific Weight

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 1 – Specific Weight (γ)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standard Board Terminology):**
> **Specific Weight (γ)** of a fluid is defined as the **weight per unit volume** of the fluid. It is a measure of the heaviness of a fluid and is also commonly called **Weight Density**.

Mathematically, it is expressed as the ratio of the weight ($W$) of a fluid to its volume ($V$):

$$\gamma = \frac{W}{V} = \frac{mg}{V} = \rho \cdot g$$

Where:
- $\gamma$ = Specific Weight of the fluid (Unit: **N/m³** in SI, **lbf/ft³** in FPS)
- $W$ = Weight of the fluid (N)
- $V$ = Volume of the fluid (m³)
- $m$ = Mass of the fluid (kg)
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**
- $\rho$ = Mass Density of the fluid (kg/m³)

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you are carrying two identical buckets — one filled with water and the other with honey. Both buckets occupy the **same volume** (say 10 litres), but the bucket of honey feels significantly **heavier** than the water bucket. This "heaviness per unit volume" is exactly what specific weight quantifies. Honey has a higher specific weight than water because a given volume of honey weighs more. In simple terms — **Specific Weight tells you how 'heavy' a fluid feels for its size.**

### Key Conceptual Highlights

- **Dimensional Formula:** $[M L^{-2} T^{-2}]$
- **Vector / Scalar?** Specific weight is a **scalar quantity**.
- **Dependence on Location:** Since it depends on $g$, specific weight **varies with gravitational field** (e.g., it changes from the poles to the equator, and is zero in outer space).
- **Standard Reference Value:** For **water at 4 °C**, $\gamma_{water} = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$ (often rounded to $9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3$).
- **Compressed Fluids vs Liquids:** For liquids (incompressible), $\gamma$ is practically constant. For gases (compressible), $\gamma$ varies significantly with pressure and temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Confused Pair:** Students often confuse **Specific Weight ($\gamma$)** with **Mass Density ($\rho$)**. The key difference is:
> - **Density ($\rho$)** = Mass per unit volume → kg/m³
> - **Specific Weight ($\gamma$)** = Weight per unit volume → N/m³
> - The conversion is simply: $\gamma = \rho \cdot g$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation – The 'Why' Behind Specific Weight

The concept of specific weight is born from the necessity to understand the **gravitational force exerted by a fluid per unit volume**. In fluid statics and hydrostatics, pressure at any depth is generated by the weight of the fluid column above it. Hence, the property that bridges **mass** and **gravitational force per unit volume** is specific weight.

**Operational Logic Flow:**
1. Every fluid particle has a mass $m$.
2. The Earth exerts a gravitational force $W = mg$ on this mass.
3. When we normalize this weight by the volume occupied ($V$), we get the specific weight.
4. Therefore, $\gamma$ quantifies the **intensity of gravitational pull per unit spatial footprint** of a fluid.

### 2.2 Mathematical Relationships

Since $\gamma = \rho g$, we can re-derive related fluid properties in terms of $\gamma$:

- **Mass Density:** $\rho = \dfrac{\gamma}{g}$
- **Specific Gravity (S):** The ratio of specific weight of a fluid to that of water at 4 °C.

$$S = \frac{\gamma_{fluid}}{\gamma_{water}} = \frac{\rho_{fluid}}{\rho_{water}}$$

- **Pressure Relationship:** Hydrostatic pressure $P = \gamma h$, where $h$ is the depth of fluid column.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Specific Weight Matters in Engineering:** In pipe flow design, dam construction, hydraulic machinery, and manometer calculations, engineers use $\gamma$ extensively to compute **hydrostatic thrust**, **buoyancy forces**, and **fluid column pressures** without having to invoke mass and gravity separately. It dramatically simplifies hydrostatic calculations.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Property | Formula | SI Unit | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|---------|---------|
| 1 | Specific Weight (Definition) | $\gamma = \dfrac{W}{V}$ | N/m³ | Weight per unit volume |
| 2 | Specific Weight (in terms of $\rho$) | $\gamma = \rho g$ | N/m³ | Most used form |
| 3 | Specific Weight (in terms of S) | $\gamma = S \cdot \gamma_{water}$ | N/m³ | $S$ is dimensionless |
| 4 | Mass Density | $\rho = \dfrac{\gamma}{g}$ | kg/m³ | Mass per unit volume |
| 5 | Specific Gravity | $S = \dfrac{\gamma_{fluid}}{\gamma_{water}}$ | Dimensionless | Water at 4 °C reference |
| 6 | Standard Value for Water (4 °C) | $\gamma_{w} = 9810$ | N/m³ | Used in all KTU numericals |
| 7 | Hydrostatic Pressure | $P = \gamma h$ | Pa (N/m²) | Depth-based pressure |
| 8 | Dimensional Formula of $\gamma$ | $M L^{-2} T^{-2}$ | — | Verify in 1-mark questions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always use $\gamma_{water} = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$ (or $9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3$) unless the problem explicitly states otherwise. Using $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$ gives $\gamma_{water} = 10000 \text{ N/m}^3$ — read the problem carefully!

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Specific Weight from Mass Density

**Starting Point:** Newton's Second Law states that weight is the gravitational force on a mass.

**Step 1:** Express weight in terms of mass.

$$W = m \cdot g$$

**Step 2:** Recall the definition of mass density.

$$\rho = \frac{m}{V} \quad \Rightarrow \quad m = \rho V$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $m = \rho V$ into the weight equation.

$$W = (\rho V) \cdot g = \rho \, g \, V$$

**Step 4:** Apply the primary definition of specific weight: $\gamma = W / V$.

$$\gamma = \frac{W}{V} = \frac{\rho \, g \, V}{V}$$

**Step 5:** Cancel $V$ from numerator and denominator.

$$\boxed{\gamma = \rho \cdot g}$$

This is the **fundamental governing relation** for specific weight in KTU problems.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hydrostatic Pressure from Specific Weight

Consider a fluid column of cross-sectional area $A$ and depth $h$. The pressure at the base equals the weight of the fluid column divided by the base area.

**Step 1:** Volume of the fluid column.

$$V = A \cdot h$$

**Step 2:** Weight of the fluid column.

$$W = \gamma \cdot V = \gamma \cdot A \cdot h$$

**Step 3:** Pressure = Force / Area.

$$P = \frac{W}{A} = \frac{\gamma \cdot A \cdot h}{A}$$

**Step 4:** Simplify.

$$\boxed{P = \gamma \cdot h}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** This is why **dam walls are thicker at the base** — pressure increases linearly with depth because the specific weight is constant (for incompressible fluids). A dam 100 m deep experiences pressure $P = 9810 \times 100 = 981 \text{ kPa}$ at its base.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Solved Analytical Problem (KTU Numerical Style)

> **Problem (Model):** A storage tank contains **0.5 m³** of an oil whose mass is **440 kg**. Find:
> (a) The mass density of the oil.
> (b) The specific weight of the oil.
> (c) The specific gravity of the oil.
> *(Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ and $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$.)*

#### Solution:

**Part (a): Mass Density**

Using the definition of density:

$$\rho = \frac{m}{V} = \frac{440 \text{ kg}}{0.5 \text{ m}^3}$$

$$\boxed{\rho = 880 \text{ kg/m}^3}$$

**Part (b): Specific Weight**

Using the governing relation $\gamma = \rho g$:

$$\gamma = 880 \text{ kg/m}^3 \times 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$$

$$\gamma = 8632.8 \text{ N/m}^3$$

$$\boxed{\gamma = 8.6328 \text{ kN/m}^3}$$

**Part (c): Specific Gravity**

Using the definition:

$$S = \frac{\rho_{oil}}{\rho_{water}} = \frac{880}{1000}$$

$$\boxed{S = 0.88}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-Verification:** $S = \gamma_{oil} / \gamma_{water} = 8632.8 / 9810 = 0.88$. ✓ The result matches from both routes — this is a KTU-validated problem-solving pattern.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Variation of Specific Weight with Altitude (for Gases)

For gases, density varies with altitude as $\rho = \rho_0 e^{-z/H}$ (isothermal atmosphere approximation). Consequently:

$$\gamma(z) = \rho(z) \cdot g = \gamma_0 \, e^{-z/H}$$

Where $H$ is the scale height. This shows that **specific weight of air decreases exponentially with altitude**, which is why air pressure drops as we climb mountains.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Fluid Property Relationship Network

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mass m, kg] --> B[Mass Density rho, kg per m cube]
    A --> C[Weight W equals m times g]
    C --> D[Specific Weight gamma, N per m cube]
    B --> D
    E[Reference Water at 4 deg C] --> F[Specific Gravity S, dimensionless]
    D --> F
    G[Fluid Column Depth h, m] --> H[Hydrostatic Pressure P equals gamma h, Pa]
    D --> H
    B --> I[Dynamic Viscosity mu, Pa s]
    J[Acceleration g equals 9.81 m per s square] --> D
    J --> C
    B --> K[Specific Volume v equals 1 by rho, m cube per kg]
    D --> L[Weight Density Verification gamma equals W by V]
    C --> L
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Hierarchy: Classification of Fluid Properties

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Fluid Properties] --> P1[Intensive Properties]
    P --> P2[Extensive Properties]
    P1 --> Q1[Specific Weight gamma]
    P1 --> Q2[Mass Density rho]
    P1 --> Q3[Specific Gravity S]
    P1 --> Q4[Pressure P]
    P1 --> Q5[Viscosity mu]
    P2 --> R1[Mass m]
    P2 --> R2[Volume V]
    P2 --> R3[Weight W]
    Q1 -. depends on .-> Q2
    Q1 -. depends on .-> J2[Acceleration g]
    Q3 -. derived from .-> Q1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The flowchart above maps how **Specific Weight ($\gamma$)** is a derived intensive property that depends on both **density ($\rho$)** and **gravitational acceleration ($g$)**. It is the critical link between the mass-based world (kg, m³) and the force-based world (N, Pa) of fluid mechanics.

### 4.3 Schematic: Hydrostatic Pressure in a Fluid Column

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Top[Open Surface of Fluid] --> Mid1[Layer 1: Pressure P1 equals gamma h1]
    Mid1 --> Mid2[Layer 2: Pressure P2 equals gamma h1 plus h2]
    Mid2 --> Base[Base: Pressure P base equals gamma H]
    Base --> Wall[Vertical Wall Thickness Increases with Depth]
    style Top fill:#E0F7FA
    style Base fill:#FFCDD2
    style Wall fill:#FFE0B2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The diagram illustrates the **linear increase of pressure with depth** in a static fluid — a direct consequence of specific weight. The triangular distribution shown is the basis of **hydrostatic force calculations on submerged surfaces** (a key KTU Module 1/2 topic).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model 1]**

> **Define Specific Weight. State its SI unit and dimensional formula.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Specific Weight is defined as the weight per unit volume of a fluid. It is denoted by the symbol $\gamma$ (gamma) and mathematically expressed as $\gamma = W / V = \rho g$, where $W$ is the weight, $V$ is the volume, $\rho$ is the mass density, and $g$ is the acceleration due to gravity.
- **SI Unit (0.5 mark):** $\text{N/m}^3$
- **Dimensional Formula (0.5 mark):** $[M L^{-2} T^{-2}]$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model 2]**

> **Distinguish between Mass Density and Specific Weight. A liquid has a specific gravity of 0.85. Find its specific weight. Take $\gamma_{water} = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Distinction (1.5 marks):**
  - *Mass Density ($\rho$):* Mass per unit volume, Unit: kg/m³, depends only on mass.
  - *Specific Weight ($\gamma$):* Weight per unit volume, Unit: N/m³, depends on mass and gravitational field.
- **Numerical Solution (1.5 marks):**

$$\gamma_{liquid} = S \times \gamma_{water} = 0.85 \times 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$$

$$\boxed{\gamma_{liquid} = 8338.5 \text{ N/m}^3}$$

---

### PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]

> **Q. (a)** Define the following fluid properties with their SI units: **(7 Marks)**
> (i) Mass Density  
> (ii) Specific Weight  
> (iii) Specific Volume  
> (iv) Specific Gravity

> **Q. (b)** A vessel contains **3.2 m³** of a liquid weighing **28.8 kN**. Calculate the mass density, specific weight, and specific gravity of the liquid. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Definitions (7 Marks — 1.75 each):**

| Property | Definition | SI Unit |
|----------|------------|---------|
| Mass Density ($\rho$) | Mass per unit volume of fluid | kg/m³ |
| Specific Weight ($\gamma$) | Weight per unit volume of fluid | N/m³ |
| Specific Volume ($v$) | Volume per unit mass of fluid ($1/\rho$) | m³/kg |
| Specific Gravity ($S$) | Ratio of density (or specific weight) of fluid to that of water at 4 °C | Dimensionless |

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Identify given data.

$$V = 3.2 \text{ m}^3, \quad W = 28.8 \text{ kN} = 28800 \text{ N}, \quad g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 2:** Specific weight (2 marks).

$$\gamma = \frac{W}{V} = \frac{28800}{3.2} = 9000 \text{ N/m}^3$$

$$\boxed{\gamma = 9.0 \text{ kN/m}^3}$$

**Step 3:** Mass density (2 marks).

$$\rho = \frac{\gamma}{g} = \frac{9000}{9.81} = 917.43 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

$$\boxed{\rho \approx 917.43 \text{ kg/m}^3}$$

**Step 4:** Specific gravity (2 marks).

$$S = \frac{\gamma_{liquid}}{\gamma_{water}} = \frac{9000}{9810} = 0.9174$$

$$\boxed{S \approx 0.917}$$

**Step 5:** Verification.

$$\rho = S \times \rho_{water} = 0.917 \times 1000 = 917 \text{ kg/m}^3 \checkmark$$

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice (Module 1 Coverage)

> **Q. (a)** Explain the difference between specific weight and specific gravity. Why is specific weight a function of location, but specific gravity is not? **(7 Marks)**

> **Q. (b)** Mercury has a specific gravity of **13.6**. Find: **(7 Marks)**
> (i) Its specific weight in N/m³  
> (ii) Its mass density in kg/m³  
> (iii) The mass of mercury occupying a volume of **250 litres**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Conceptual Answer (7 Marks):**

- **Specific Weight ($\gamma$):** Weight per unit volume, Unit: N/m³, Depends on $g$ → **varies with location** (poles vs equator vs moon).
- **Specific Gravity ($S$):** Dimensionless ratio relative to water, Unit: none, Independent of $g$ → **does not vary with location** (a pure ratio).
- **Reason (4 marks):** Since $\gamma = \rho g$ and $g$ varies with position, $\gamma$ varies. However, $S = \gamma_{fluid} / \gamma_{water} = \rho_{fluid} / \rho_{water}$ — the $g$ factor cancels out, making $S$ location-independent. For example, a fluid on the moon has $\gamma$ reduced by a factor of ~1/6, but its $S$ relative to water remains unchanged.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $S = 13.6$, $V = 250 \text{ L} = 0.25 \text{ m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

**Step 1:** Specific weight (2.5 marks).

$$\gamma_{Hg} = S \times \gamma_{water} = 13.6 \times 9810 = 133416 \text{ N/m}^3$$

$$\boxed{\gamma_{Hg} = 133.416 \text{ kN/m}^3}$$

**Step 2:** Mass density (2.5 marks).

$$\rho_{Hg} = \frac{\gamma_{Hg}}{g} = \frac{133416}{9.81} = 13599.18 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

$$\boxed{\rho_{Hg} \approx 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3}$$

**Step 3:** Mass occupying 0.25 m³ (2 marks).

$$m = \rho \times V = 13600 \times 0.25 = 3400 \text{ kg}$$

$$\boxed{m = 3400 \text{ kg}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversions:** 1 litre = $10^{-3}$ m³. Many students lose 1 mark by leaving volume in litres without conversion.
> 2. **Mixing up $g$ value:** Some problems use $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, others $g = 9.8$ or even $g = 10$. Always use the value specified in the problem statement.
> 3. **Confusing $\gamma$ with $\rho$:** A common 1-mark loss is writing "specific weight = 1000 kg/m³" instead of "specific weight = 9810 N/m³". Units tell the difference.
> 4. **Dimensional errors in Part A:** Writing $[M L^{-3}]$ for specific weight (which is density) costs 0.5 marks. The correct dimensional formula is $[M L^{-2} T^{-2}]$.
> 5. **Not showing substitution steps in Part B:** KTU examiners award marks for **every algebraic step**, not just the final answer. Always show $\gamma = \rho g \rightarrow \rho = \gamma / g$ with substitution.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Specific Weight ($\gamma$) = Weight / Volume = $\rho g$** — this is the master formula.
- ✅ **SI Unit of $\gamma$ is N/m³** (not kg/m³ — that is density).
- ✅ **Dimensional formula** is $[M L^{-2} T^{-2}]$.
- ✅ **Standard value:** $\gamma_{water} \text{ at } 4°C = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3 = 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3$.
- ✅ **Specific gravity is dimensionless** and **location-independent**.
- ✅ **Specific weight is location-dependent** because it contains $g$.
- ✅ For **liquids (incompressible)**, $\gamma$ is treated as constant. For **gases (compressible)**, $\gamma$ varies with pressure and temperature.
- ✅ The hydrostatic pressure relation $P = \gamma h$ is a **direct consequence** of specific weight.
- ✅ Always **show complete substitution steps** in KTU numericals — partial marking rewards working.
- ✅ Use **$\gamma_{water} = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$** as the default reference unless the problem states otherwise.

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Mnemonic — "W-V-R-G" for Specific Weight:**  
> **W**eight per unit **V**olume → $\gamma$ in **N**/**m**³ → multiply Density by **G**ravity. Memorize this 4-step chain to never confuse the properties again!

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Viscosity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT741 - FLUID MECHANICS & MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Viscosity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1: Viscosity

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Viscosity** is the property of a fluid that **resists the relative motion (or deformation) of its adjacent layers**. It is the internal friction of a fluid in motion — the measure of a fluid's resistance to gradual deformation by shear stress or tensile stress.

Mathematically, for a Newtonian fluid, viscosity $\mu$ is defined by **Newton's Law of Viscosity**:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

where:
- $\tau$ = Shear stress (resistance force per unit area) $[\text{N/m}^2]$
- $\mu$ = Dynamic (or absolute) viscosity $[\text{N·s/m}^2 \text{ or Pa·s}]$
- $\dfrac{du}{dy}$ = Velocity gradient (rate of change of velocity with respect to distance perpendicular to flow) $[\text{s}^{-1}]$

The proportionality constant $\mu$ is called the **coefficient of dynamic viscosity**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world analogy:** Think of pouring **honey** versus **water** from a bottle. Honey flows slowly because its internal layers "grip" each other strongly — it has **high viscosity**. Water flows freely — it has **low viscosity**. 

Another simple mental image: imagine two parallel glass plates with a thin fluid layer trapped in between. If you push the top plate, the fluid layer just beneath it moves with it, the next layer moves a little slower (dragged by the lower one), and so on. This layer-by-layer "dragging" effect is **viscosity**.

| Fluid | Approx. Viscosity (Pa·s) | Feel |
|---|---|---|
| Air | $1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ | Almost frictionless |
| Water (20°C) | $1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ | Thin, easy to pour |
| Blood | $3.5 \times 10^{-3}$ | Slightly thicker than water |
| Honey | $2 - 10$ | Thick, slow |
| Tar / Bitumen | $\sim 10^2 - 10^3$ | Barely flows |

---

### 1.3 Two Important Variants of Viscosity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dynamic Viscosity ($\mu$):** The true measure of internal fluid friction. Units: **N·s/m²** or **Pa·s**. In CGS: **Poise** (1 Poise = 0.1 Pa·s).
>
> **Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$):** The ratio of dynamic viscosity to density of the fluid.
>
> $$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} \quad [\text{m}^2/\text{s}]$$
>
> In CGS: **Stokes** (1 St = $10^{-4}$ m²/s).

---

### 1.4 Newton's Hypothesis of Viscosity

According to **Newton**, the shear stress $\tau$ between two fluid layers is:
1. **Directly proportional** to the velocity gradient $\dfrac{du}{dy}$ (Newtonian assumption).
2. **Directly proportional** to the area of contact $A$.
3. **Independent** of the pressure between the layers.

Fluids that obey Newton's law of viscosity are called **Newtonian fluids** (e.g., water, air, oil, gasoline, alcohol).

Fluids that do **not** obey it are called **Non-Newtonian fluids** (e.g., blood, mud, paint, ketchup, toothpaste).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Newton's Law of Viscosity — Stepwise Breakdown

Consider a fluid element bounded between two parallel layers separated by a small distance $dy$:

- **Step 1:** The lower layer moves with velocity $u$ and the upper layer moves with velocity $u + du$.
- **Step 2:** Due to molecular cohesion and momentum transfer, the upper layer exerts a **retarding force** $F$ on the faster-moving upper fluid, while the lower layer pulls it forward.
- **Step 3:** This internal friction is quantified as shear stress $\tau = F/A$.
- **Step 4:** For a small relative velocity $du$ over a small distance $dy$, the **velocity gradient** is $\dfrac{du}{dy}$.
- **Step 5:** Newton's postulate: $\tau \propto \dfrac{du}{dy} \;\Rightarrow\; \tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter?** This is the foundation of viscous flow analysis — used in deriving **Navier-Stokes equations**, **Hagen-Poiseuille flow**, **boundary layer theory**, and **lubrication mechanics**.

---

### 2.2 Physical Significance

- High $\mu$ ⇒ fluid offers more resistance to flow (e.g., grease in bearings).
- Low $\mu$ ⇒ fluid flows easily (e.g., water, air).
- $\mu = 0$ ⇒ **Ideal (inviscid) fluid** — a theoretical assumption used to simplify Euler's equations.

---

### 2.3 Effect of Temperature on Viscosity

| Fluid Type | Effect of ↑ Temperature | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Liquids** (e.g., water, oil) | Viscosity **decreases** | Molecules move apart, cohesive forces weaken |
| **Gases** (e.g., air, N₂) | Viscosity **increases** | Molecular momentum transfer increases |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For water: $\mu$ drops from $\sim 1.79 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s at 0°C to $\sim 0.28 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s at 100°C. For air: $\mu$ rises from $\sim 1.71 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s at 0°C to $\sim 2.18 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s at 100°C.

For **lubricating oils** (used in machinery), viscosity is conventionally measured in **Saybolt Seconds (SUS)** or **Redwood Seconds** at standard temperatures (40°C and 100°C) because oils are liquids and their viscosity drops with heat.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Newton's law of viscosity | $\text{N/m}^2$ |
| 2 | $\mu = \dfrac{\tau}{du/dy}$ | Coefficient of dynamic viscosity | $\text{Pa·s}$ |
| 3 | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | Kinematic viscosity | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |
| 4 | $\mu = \rho \nu$ | Dynamic viscosity from kinematic | $\text{Pa·s}$ |
| 5 | Dimensions of $\mu$ | $[M L^{-1} T^{-1}]$ | — |
| 6 | Dimensions of $\nu$ | $[L^2 T^{-1}]$ | — |
| 7 | $\tau = \rho \nu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Shear stress via kinematic viscosity | $\text{N/m}^2$ |
| 8 | $F = \mu A \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Viscous force on a layer | N |
| 9 | $\dfrac{\mu_1}{\mu_2} = \dfrac{t_1 \, \rho_1}{t_2 \, \rho_2}$ (Capillary viscometer) | For comparing viscosities of two fluids | dimensionless |
| 10 | $h = \dfrac{12 \mu V L}{w t^3}$ | Flow between parallel plates (Couette) | pressure head |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world engineering utility:**
> - **Hydraulic systems:** Higher $\mu$ = greater pump energy loss.
> - **Journal bearings:** Lubricant viscosity determines load-carrying capacity.
> - **Pipeline design:** Viscosity dictates pressure drop (Darcy-Weisbach equation).
> - **Aerodynamics:** Determines boundary layer thickness and skin-friction drag.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Dynamic and Kinematic Viscosity

**Given:**
- Dynamic viscosity $\mu$ has units $\text{Pa·s} = \text{N·s/m}^2$
- Density $\rho$ has units $\text{kg/m}^3$

**To derive:** $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

**Substituting units:**

$$\text{Units of } \nu = \frac{\text{N·s/m}^2}{\text{kg/m}^3}$$

We know that $\text{N} = \text{kg·m/s}^2$ (Newton's second law), so:

$$= \frac{(\text{kg·m/s}^2) \cdot \text{s}}{\text{m}^2} \cdot \frac{\text{m}^3}{\text{kg}}$$

**Simplifying step-by-step:**

$$= \frac{\text{kg·m·s}}{\text{s}^2 \cdot \text{m}^2} \cdot \frac{\text{m}^3}{\text{kg}}$$

$$= \frac{\text{m} \cdot \text{m}^3}{\text{s} \cdot \text{m}^2}$$

$$= \frac{\text{m}^2}{\text{s}}$$

> Hence the unit of kinematic viscosity is **m²/s**, consistent with its name "kinematic" (it has the dimensions of motion, not force).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Dimensional Formula of Dynamic Viscosity $\mu$

**Start from the definition:**

$$\mu = \frac{\tau}{du/dy}$$

**Express $\tau$ in terms of base units:**
- $\tau = F/A$ has units $\text{N/m}^2 = \text{kg/(m·s}^2\text{)}$
- $du/dy$ has units $(\text{m/s})/\text{m} = 1/\text{s}$

**Therefore:**

$$[\mu] = \frac{\text{kg/(m·s}^2\text{)}}{1/\text{s}} = \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m·s}}$$

In dimensional form (M, L, T):

$$[\mu] = [M \, L^{-1} \, T^{-1}]$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Velocity Distribution Between Two Parallel Plates (Couette Flow)

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **KTU favourite derivation** — Couette flow (plane Poiseuille / drag flow).

**Setup:** Two large parallel plates separated by a distance $h$. The bottom plate is stationary, and the top plate moves with a constant velocity $U$. The flow is steady, laminar, and incompressible. There is **no pressure gradient** ($\dfrac{dp}{dx} = 0$).

**Navier-Stokes equation (x-direction, simplified):**

$$0 = \mu \frac{d^2 u}{dy^2}$$

**Integrate once:**

$$\frac{du}{dy} = C_1$$

**Integrate again:**

$$u(y) = C_1 y + C_2$$

**Boundary conditions:**
- At $y = 0$ (bottom plate): $u = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; C_2 = 0$
- At $y = h$ (top plate): $u = U \;\Rightarrow\; C_1 h + 0 = U \;\Rightarrow\; C_1 = \dfrac{U}{h}$

**Final velocity profile:**

$$u(y) = U \cdot \frac{y}{h}$$

> This is a **linear velocity distribution** — characteristic of pure Couette (shear-driven) flow.

**Shear stress is constant throughout:**

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy} = \mu \cdot \frac{U}{h} = \text{constant}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Capillary Tube Viscometer (Working Equation)

When a fluid flows through a vertical capillary tube under its own weight (or head $h$), the discharge $Q$ is governed by **Hagen-Poiseuille equation**:

$$Q = \frac{\pi \, \rho \, g \, h \, D^4}{128 \, \mu \, L}$$

**Rearranging for viscosity:**

$$\mu = \frac{\pi \, \rho \, g \, h \, D^4}{128 \, Q \, L}$$

If we collect a fixed volume $V$ in time $t$, then $Q = V/t$:

$$\mu = \frac{\pi \, \rho \, g \, h \, D^4 \, t}{128 \, V \, L}$$

> This is the **working formula** used in Redwood, Saybolt, and Ubbelohde viscometers to compute $\mu$ experimentally.

---

### 3.5 Comparison: Common Methods of Viscosity Measurement

| Viscometer | Working Principle | Typical Use | Direct Measurement |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Redwood No. 1** | Efflux time of 50 ml | Oils (industrial) | Time (seconds) |
| **Redwood No. 2** | Efflux time of 50 ml | Very viscous oils | Time (seconds) |
| **Saybolt Universal** | Efflux time of 60 ml | Light oils, fuels | Time (SUS) |
| **Saybolt Furol** | Efflux time of 60 ml | Heavy fuel oils | Time (SFS) |
| **Engler** | Efflux time ratio vs water | General oils | Time (°E) |
| **Capillary (Ostwald / Ubbelohde)** | Hagen-Poiseuille | Lab accuracy | $\mu$ directly |
| **Falling Sphere (Stokes' law)** | Terminal velocity of ball | High-viscosity fluids | $\mu$ directly |
| **Rotational (Brookfield)** | Torque on spindle | Paints, polymers | $\mu$ directly |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow: How Viscosity Arises Between Fluid Layers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Two parallel fluid layers] --> B[Layer at y has velocity u]
    A --> C[Layer at y plus dy has velocity u plus du]
    B --> D[Relative motion du over distance dy]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Velocity gradient du over dy]
    E --> F[Shear stress tau equals mu times du over dy]
    F --> G[Newtonian fluid if mu is constant]
    F --> H[Non-Newtonian if mu varies with du over dy]
    G --> I[Examples: Water, Air, Oil]
    H --> J[Examples: Blood, Paint, Ketchup]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Classification of Fluids by Viscosity Behaviour

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FL[Fluid] --> N[Newtonian]
    FL --> NN[Non-Newtonian]
    N --> N1[Water]
    N --> N2[Air]
    N --> N3[Thin oils]
    NN --> PS[Pseudo-plastic]
    NN --> DV[Dilatant]
    NN --> BV[Bingham plastic]
    NN --> TH[Thixotropic]
    PS --> PS1[Paint, blood, ketchup]
    DV --> DV1[Starch suspension, wet sand]
    BV --> BV1[Toothpaste, drilling mud]
    TH --> TH1[Yogurt, printer ink]
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture: Viscosity Measurement Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Collect fluid sample at controlled temperature T] --> S2[Step 2: Pour fixed volume V into viscometer cup]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Open orifice and start stopwatch]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Record efflux time t for volume V]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Apply empirical formula for that viscometer]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Compute kinematic viscosity nu in m squared per s]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Multiply by density rho to get mu in Pa s]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Report value at standard reference temperature]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid:** The three diagrams above map out (1) the **physical origin** of viscosity between layers, (2) the **taxonomy** of fluids, and (3) the **measurement workflow**. Together, they form a complete visual mental model of the topic.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper PEMRT741]**

**Q1. Define viscosity. Differentiate between dynamic and kinematic viscosity.**

**Model Answer:**

Viscosity is the property of a fluid by which it resists the relative motion between its layers. It is quantitatively expressed by Newton's law of viscosity: $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$.

| Feature | Dynamic Viscosity ($\mu$) | Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Resistance to flow | Resistance due to inertia+viscosity |
| Formula | $\mu = \tau \cdot \dfrac{dy}{du}$ | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ |
| SI Unit | Pa·s | m²/s |
| CGS Unit | Poise | Stokes |
| Dimensions | $[ML^{-1}T^{-1}]$ | $[L^2 T^{-1}]$ |

> **Examiner's note:** Many students confuse the two. The trick is in the **density factor** — kinematic is dynamic **divided by density**.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper PEMRT741]**

**Q2. State Newton's law of viscosity. List any three Newtonian and three Non-Newtonian fluids.**

**Model Answer:**

**Newton's Law of Viscosity:** *The shear stress between two adjacent fluid layers is directly proportional to the rate of shear deformation (velocity gradient), and the constant of proportionality is called the dynamic viscosity.*

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

**Newtonian fluids (3 examples):** Water, Air, Kerosene (also: gasoline, alcohol, glycerin at moderate conditions).

**Non-Newtonian fluids (3 examples):** Blood, Paint, Toothpaste (also: ketchup, mud, starch suspension, printer ink).

> **Examiner's note:** The most common error is writing the proportionality as $\tau \propto u$ instead of $\tau \propto \dfrac{du}{dy}$. Always include the **gradient**.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, End Semester Model Paper]**

**Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between dynamic and kinematic viscosity. State their SI and CGS units with conversion factors. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A flat plate of area $0.5 \text{ m}^2$ is pulled with a velocity of $0.3 \text{ m/s}$ over another stationary flat plate, with a fluid layer of thickness $5 \text{ mm}$ in between. If the dynamic viscosity of the fluid is $\mu = 0.9 \text{ Pa·s}$, determine: **(i)** the shear stress, and **(ii)** the force required to pull the plate. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Derivation and Units:**

Starting from the basic definition:

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

**Unit analysis:**

$$[\nu] = \frac{[\mu]}{[\rho]} = \frac{\text{N·s/m}^2}{\text{kg/m}^3}$$

Using $\text{N} = \text{kg·m/s}^2$:

$$[\nu] = \frac{(\text{kg·m/s}^2) \cdot \text{s}}{\text{m}^2} \cdot \frac{\text{m}^3}{\text{kg}} = \frac{\text{m}^2}{\text{s}}$$

| Viscosity | SI Unit | CGS Unit | Conversion |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dynamic $\mu$ | Pa·s (= N·s/m²) | Poise (P) | 1 Pa·s = 10 P |
| Kinematic $\nu$ | m²/s | Stokes (St) | 1 m²/s = $10^4$ St |
| Dynamic $\mu$ (sub-unit) | cP (centipoise) | — | 1 cP = 0.001 Pa·s |

> Water at 20°C: $\mu = 1.0 \text{ cP} = 0.001 \text{ Pa·s}$, $\nu = 1.0 \text{ cSt} = 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $A = 0.5 \text{ m}^2$, $U = 0.3 \text{ m/s}$, $h = 5 \text{ mm} = 0.005 \text{ m}$, $\mu = 0.9 \text{ Pa·s}$.

**Step 1 — Velocity gradient:**

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{U - 0}{h - 0} = \frac{0.3}{0.005} = 60 \; \text{s}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — (i) Shear stress (Newton's law):**

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy} = 0.9 \times 60 = 54 \; \text{N/m}^2$$

**Step 3 — (ii) Force required to pull the plate:**

$$F = \tau \times A = 54 \times 0.5 = 27 \; \text{N}$$

> ✅ **Final Answers:** $\tau = 54 \text{ N/m}^2$, $F = 27 \text{ N}$.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, End Semester Model Paper]**

**Question B (14 Marks):**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the concept of viscosity between two fluid layers. Define the velocity gradient and derive Newton's law of viscosity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Redwood viscometer gives an efflux time of 540 seconds for 50 ml of oil at 60°C. The density of the oil is $870 \text{ kg/m}^3$. Using the Redwood No. 1 empirical formula, calculate: **(i)** the kinematic viscosity in m²/s, and **(ii)** the dynamic viscosity in Pa·s. Take $k = 0.0026$ and the discharge coefficient $C = 0.74$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Concept and Derivation:**

*(Sketch: Two parallel layers; layer at y has velocity u; layer at y+dy has velocity u+du; arrow showing shear force F along upper face; velocity gradient du/dy marked between layers.)*

> Refer to the conceptual diagram in Section 4.1.

**Derivation:**

Consider two adjacent fluid layers at a distance $dy$. The upper layer moves faster ($u + du$) than the lower one ($u$).

The relative velocity = $du$ over distance $dy$ gives the velocity gradient $\dfrac{du}{dy}$.

By Newton's hypothesis:

$$\tau \propto \frac{du}{dy} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

This is **Newton's law of viscosity**, and $\mu$ is the **dynamic viscosity** with units $\text{N·s/m}^2$.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (Redwood No. 1):**

**Given:** $t = 540 \text{ s}$, $V = 50 \text{ ml} = 50 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$, $\rho = 870 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $k = 0.0026$, $C = 0.74$.

**Redwood No. 1 empirical formula (kinematic viscosity):**

$$\nu = C \cdot t - \frac{k}{t} \; \text{[m}^2/\text{s]}$$

**Step 1 — Compute $C \cdot t$:**

$$C \cdot t = 0.74 \times 540 = 399.6 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $k/t$:**

$$\frac{k}{t} = \frac{0.0026}{540} = 4.815 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — (i) Kinematic viscosity:**

$$\nu = 399.6 \times 10^{-6} - 4.815 \times 10^{-6} = 394.78 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{m}^2/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{\nu \approx 3.95 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^2/\text{s}}$$

**Step 4 — (ii) Dynamic viscosity:**

$$\mu = \rho \cdot \nu = 870 \times 394.78 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\boxed{\mu \approx 0.3435 \; \text{Pa·s}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Units mismatch:** Forgetting to convert mm → m, or ml → m³. This alone can cost **2–3 marks** out of 7.
> 2. **Sign of viscosity:** $\mu$ is **always positive** — a negative viscosity answer means an error in velocity gradient sign.
> 3. **Misidentifying Newtonian fluid:** Students often confuse "viscous" (high $\mu$) with "non-Newtonian" — they are different concepts. Honey is Newtonian, not non-Newtonian.
> 4. **Forgetting the velocity gradient:** Writing $\tau = \mu u$ (without the $dy$) is a classic error costing **1 mark** minimum.
> 5. **Temperature effect direction:** Liquids' viscosity **decreases** with temperature, gases' viscosity **increases**. Mixing these up costs marks in viva.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Definition:** Viscosity = resistance of a fluid to relative motion of its layers.
> - **Newton's Law:** $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ — memorize as the *cornerstone equation*.
> - **Two types:** Dynamic $\mu$ (Pa·s) and Kinematic $\nu$ (m²/s) with $\nu = \mu / \rho$.
> - **Dimensions:** $\mu \rightarrow [M L^{-1} T^{-1}]$, $\nu \rightarrow [L^2 T^{-1}]$.
> - **CGS Units:** 1 Poise = 0.1 Pa·s, 1 Stokes = $10^{-4}$ m²/s, 1 cP = 0.001 Pa·s (water at 20°C).
> - **Newtonian vs Non-Newtonian:** Linear $\tau$–$du/dy$ relationship = Newtonian; otherwise = Non-Newtonian.
> - **Temperature:** Liquids → $\mu \downarrow$ as $T \uparrow$; Gases → $\mu \uparrow$ as $T \uparrow$.
> - **Couette flow:** Linear velocity profile $u(y) = U \cdot y / h$ (no pressure gradient).
> - **Viscometers:** Redwood, Saybolt, Engler (empirical/industrial); Capillary, Falling sphere, Rotational (absolute/lab).
> - **Hagen-Poiseuille:** $Q = \dfrac{\pi \rho g h D^4}{128 \mu L}$ — backbone of capillary viscometer.
> - **Engineering impact:** Higher $\mu$ = more pumping cost, more bearing friction, more pipe-line pressure drop.
> - **Ideal fluid:** $\mu = 0$ — theoretical assumption, never achieved in reality.
> - **Numericals to master:** (i) shear stress from $\tau = \mu (du/dy)$, (ii) $\mu$ from Redwood/Saybolt times, (iii) Couette flow velocity profile.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Surface Tension
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT741 - FLUID MECHANICS & MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Surface Tension

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Surface Tension (σ)** is defined as the tensile force per unit length acting on an imaginary line drawn on a free liquid surface, the force being perpendicular to the line and tangent to the liquid surface at every point. Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{L} \quad \left[ \text{Units: N/m} \right]$$

In the more rigorous thermodynamic sense, surface tension is the **excess free energy per unit area** of the liquid surface compared to the bulk liquid:

$$\sigma = \frac{dW}{dA} \quad \left[ \text{Units: J/m}^2 = \text{N/m} \right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** The two interpretations — force per unit length and energy per unit area — are **dimensionally equivalent** and **numerically identical**. Students must remember both interpretations as questions on either definition are frequently asked (2 marks).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Physical Constant (at 20 °C for water–air interface):**
> Surface tension of water, **σ_water ≈ 0.0728 N/m**.
> Surface tension of mercury, **σ_mercury ≈ 0.510 N/m**.
> Surface tension of soap solution, **σ_soap ≈ 0.025 – 0.030 N/m**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Real-World Intuition

Imagine a tightly stretched elastic sheet lying flat on the ground — if you cut a small slit in it, the sheet immediately pulls itself together to close the gap. The free surface of a liquid behaves in a very similar way: molecules at the surface are pulled inward by stronger cohesive forces from within the liquid than the weak adhesive forces from the air above, effectively "stretching" the surface into a taut membrane.

### Real-World Examples
- **Water strider insects** walking on pond surfaces — their legs depress but do not pierce the surface, thanks to surface tension.
- **Soap bubbles** assume a spherical shape because a sphere has the **minimum surface area** for a given volume, thereby minimizing the surface free energy.
- **Droplets of dew** on a lotus leaf form near-perfect spheres — again due to surface tension minimizing surface energy.
- **Capillary rise** of kerosene in a lamp wick — surface tension draws the liquid up into narrow spaces, defying gravity for small tube diameters.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for the student:** "Surface tension is to liquids what a stretched drumhead is to solids — it tries to contract to the smallest possible area."

## 1.3 Molecular Origin of Surface Tension

Molecules in the **bulk (interior)** of a liquid are surrounded on all sides by other molecules of the same kind. They experience equal and opposite intermolecular cohesive forces in every direction, so the **net force on a bulk molecule is zero**.

However, a molecule at the **free surface** has very few molecules above it (only the sparse air molecules), while there are many more molecules below and beside it. The unbalanced inward pull causes the surface molecules to be pulled toward the bulk, creating a state of tension along the surface.

To bring a molecule from the bulk to the surface, work must be done against this inward pull. This work is stored as **potential (free) energy** at the surface, which is the surface energy per unit area — equal in magnitude to surface tension.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Analysis of the Phenomenon

1. **Cohesion vs. Adhesion:**
   - *Cohesion* → force of attraction between molecules of the **same** liquid.
   - *Adhesion* → force of attraction between molecules of **different** substances (e.g., liquid and glass).
   - If **adhesion > cohesion** → liquid **wets** the solid (e.g., water on glass) → **concave meniscus**.
   - If **cohesion > adhesion** → liquid does **not wet** the solid (e.g., mercury on glass) → **convex meniscus**.

2. **Contact Angle (θ):**
   The angle formed between the **liquid–solid interface** and the **liquid–gas interface** (tangent to the liquid surface) at the point of contact. Measured **through the liquid**.
   - θ < 90° → wetting (hydrophilic surface).
   - θ > 90° → non-wetting (hydrophobic surface).
   - θ = 0° → perfect wetting.
   - θ = 180° → perfect non-wetting.

3. **Wetting vs. Non-Wetting Liquids:**
   - **Wetting liquid (water–glass):** θ is small; liquid rises in a capillary tube.
   - **Non-wetting liquid (mercury–glass):** θ is obtuse; liquid depresses in a capillary tube.

4. **Pressure Difference Across a Curved Interface (Laplace's Law):**
   The surface tension creates a pressure difference across any curved liquid surface. The pressure is always **higher on the concave side** (inside the droplet or bubble).

5. **Capillarity:**
   The phenomenon of a liquid rising or falling in a narrow tube (capillary) due to the combined effect of surface tension and the wettability of the tube material.

6. **Practical Engineering Relevance:**
   - Design of **inkjet printers** (droplet formation).
   - **Detergents and surfactants** reduce σ of water, improving cleaning efficiency.
   - **Lubricant spread** in engine bearings.
   - **Coating and painting technology** — wetting of surfaces.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains every formula a KTU student must memorize for Module 1 — Surface Tension. All values of σ are in **N/m**, R in **m**, and pressure in **N/m² = Pa** unless otherwise noted.

| **S.No** | **Phenomenon / Quantity** | **Formula** | **Explanation of Variables** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Surface tension as force per length | $\sigma = F \div L$ | $F$ = tangential force, $L$ = length of imaginary line |
| 2 | Surface tension as energy per area | $\sigma = dW \div dA$ | $dW$ = work done, $dA$ = change in surface area |
| 3 | Pressure inside a **liquid droplet** (one surface) | $p = 2\sigma \div R$ | $R$ = radius of the spherical droplet |
| 4 | Pressure inside a **soap bubble** (two surfaces) | $p = 4\sigma \div R$ | Soap film has an inner and an outer surface |
| 5 | Pressure inside a **liquid jet** (cylindrical) | $p = \sigma \div R$ | $R$ = radius of the cylindrical jet |
| 6 | Excess pressure inside a curved surface (general) | $\Delta p = \sigma \left( 1 \div R_1 + 1 \div R_2 \right)$ | $R_1, R_2$ = principal radii of curvature |
| 7 | Capillary rise (wetting liquid) | $h = 2\sigma \cos\theta \div \left( \rho g R \right)$ | $\theta$ = contact angle, $\rho$ = density, $g$ = 9.81 m/s² |
| 8 | Capillary rise for clean water in glass ($\theta = 0$) | $h = 2\sigma \div \left( \rho g R \right)$ | Simplification when $\cos\theta = 1$ |
| 9 | Capillary depression (non-wetting, e.g., mercury) | $h = 2\sigma \cos\theta \div \left( \rho g R \right)$ | $\cos\theta$ becomes **negative** |
| 10 | Work done to blow a droplet | $W = \sigma \cdot \Delta A = 8\pi R^2 \sigma$ | Surface area of a sphere = $4\pi R^2$, factor 2 for two new surfaces (bubble) |
| 11 | Work done to blow a soap bubble | $W = \sigma \cdot \Delta A = 8\pi R^2 \sigma$ | Two surfaces (inner + outer) → $8\pi\sigma$ |
| 12 | Force due to surface tension on a contact line | $F = \sigma \cdot L$ | $L$ = length of contact perimeter |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students frequently use $p = 2\sigma \div R$ for a **soap bubble**. The correct formula is $p = 4\sigma \div R$ because a soap bubble has **two** liquid–air interfaces (inner and outer). A droplet has only **one** interface.

## 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility

Surface tension governs numerous practical engineering scenarios:

- **Microfluidics & Lab-on-a-chip devices:** Droplet-based microfluidics relies on controlled surface tension to manipulate tiny fluid volumes.
- **Medical diagnostics:** The shape of blood drops and the capillary action in diagnostic strips depend on surface tension.
- **Oil recovery (Enhanced Oil Recovery - EOR):** Surfactants are injected to reduce the surface tension between oil and water, freeing trapped oil from rock pores.
- **Cooling towers and heat exchangers:** Thin liquid films form due to surface tension effects.
- **Spray painting and inkjet printing:** Droplet breakup is controlled by surface tension vs. inertia (Weber number).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Pressure Difference Across a Spherical Liquid Droplet (Laplace's Law for a Droplet)

**Statement:** The pressure inside a spherical liquid droplet is greater than the pressure outside by an amount $\Delta p = 2\sigma \div R$, where σ is the surface tension and R is the radius of the droplet.

### Setup
Consider a spherical liquid droplet of radius R. The droplet has only **one** free surface (the outer liquid–air interface). Let the internal pressure be $p_i$ and the external pressure be $p_o$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Imagine the droplet split into two hemispheres:**

Consider the upper hemisphere. The forces acting on it are:
- The internal pressure force pushing it upward (outward direction).
- The external pressure force pushing it downward (inward direction).
- The surface tension force acting along the circular rim of radius R, pulling the two hemispheres together.

**Step 2 — Compute the pressure force on the hemispherical cap:**

The projected area of the hemisphere on the equatorial plane is a circle of radius R. The net pressure force acting outward on the upper hemisphere is:

$$F_{\text{pressure}} = (p_i - p_o) \cdot \pi R^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute the surface tension force:**

The surface tension acts along the perimeter of the circular rim. The length of this circular rim is $L = 2\pi R$. The surface tension force is therefore:

$$F_{\text{surface tension}} = \sigma \cdot L = \sigma \cdot 2\pi R$$

This force pulls the upper hemisphere **downward**, balancing the pressure force.

**Step 4 — Apply equilibrium of forces:**

For the hemisphere to be in static equilibrium:

$$(p_i - p_o) \cdot \pi R^2 = \sigma \cdot 2\pi R$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the equation:**

Dividing both sides by $\pi R$:

$$(p_i - p_o) \cdot R = 2\sigma$$

**Step 6 — Final expression:**

$$\boxed{\Delta p = p_i - p_o = \frac{2\sigma}{R}}$$

This excess pressure is the reason why smaller droplets have **higher internal pressure** — which is why two small droplets tend to coalesce into one larger droplet (reducing total surface energy).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Pressure Inside a Soap Bubble

**Statement:** The pressure inside a soap bubble of radius R exceeds the outside pressure by $\Delta p = 4\sigma \div R$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Recognize the geometry:**
A soap bubble is a thin film enclosing a gas. It has **two** liquid–air surfaces: an **inner** surface and an **outer** surface. Both surfaces are under tension and both contribute to the pressure difference.

**Step 2 — Apply the hemisphere equilibrium to one surface (inner surface, radius R):**

For the inner surface alone, the excess pressure would be $2\sigma \div R$ (as derived above).

**Step 3 — Apply the same reasoning to the outer surface (also radius R):**

The outer surface also contributes an excess pressure of $2\sigma \div R$.

**Step 4 — Total excess pressure:**

Since the two surfaces act **in series** (both push the gas inward from opposite sides):

$$\Delta p = \frac{2\sigma}{R} + \frac{2\sigma}{R}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta p = \frac{4\sigma}{R}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** For any spherical interface, the factor in the denominator's numerator is **2 × (number of surfaces)**. A droplet has 1 surface → factor = 2. A soap bubble has 2 surfaces → factor = 4. A liquid jet (cylindrical) has 1 surface → factor = 1.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Capillary Rise in a Narrow Tube

**Statement:** A wetting liquid rises to a height $h = 2\sigma \cos\theta \div (\rho g R)$ inside a capillary tube of radius R, where θ is the contact angle.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Physical picture:**
A glass capillary tube of internal radius R is dipped into a wetting liquid (e.g., water). The liquid rises inside the tube to a height h above the free surface of the bulk liquid, forming a concave meniscus at the top.

**Step 2 — Identify the forces acting on the liquid column of height h inside the capillary:**

- **Upward force:** Surface tension acting along the contact line (perimeter of the tube) pulling the liquid upward.
- **Downward force:** Weight of the liquid column inside the tube.

**Step 3 — Compute the upward surface tension force:**

The contact line is a circle of radius R, so its length is $L = 2\pi R$. The surface tension pulls along this line at an angle θ to the vertical (since the meniscus makes contact angle θ with the tube wall).

The **vertical component** of the surface tension force is:

$$F_{\text{up}} = \sigma \cdot L \cdot \cos\theta = \sigma \cdot 2\pi R \cdot \cos\theta$$

**Step 4 — Compute the downward weight of the liquid column:**

The volume of liquid raised inside the capillary is $V = \pi R^2 \cdot h$. The mass of this column is $m = \rho \cdot V = \rho \pi R^2 h$. The downward weight is:

$$F_{\text{down}} = m g = \rho \pi R^2 h g$$

**Step 5 — Apply static equilibrium:**

For the liquid column to be in equilibrium, the upward and downward forces must balance:

$$\sigma \cdot 2\pi R \cdot \cos\theta = \rho \pi R^2 h g$$

**Step 6 — Solve for h:**

Cancel $\pi R$ from both sides:

$$2\sigma \cos\theta = \rho R h g$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{h = \frac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g R}}$$

**Step 7 — Special case — clean water in clean glass ($\theta \approx 0°$):**

$$\boxed{h = \frac{2\sigma}{\rho g R}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Inverse Proportionality:** Note that $h \propto 1 \div R$. This means **thinner tubes produce a taller capillary rise**. This is why the wick of an oil lamp or kerosene stove works — the narrow pores of the wick create a strong capillary action.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Examples

### Example 1: Soap Bubble Internal Pressure

**Problem (KTU Typical):** A soap bubble of diameter 20 mm contains air at 1 atm. The surface tension of the soap solution is 0.030 N/m. Find the pressure inside the bubble and the excess pressure.

**Given:**
- Diameter $d = 20 \text{ mm} \Rightarrow R = 0.010 \text{ m}$
- $\sigma = 0.030 \text{ N/m}$
- $p_{\text{outside}} = 1.01325 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$

**Solution:**

Excess pressure inside a soap bubble:

$$\Delta p = \frac{4\sigma}{R} = \frac{4 \times 0.030}{0.010} = 12 \text{ Pa}$$

Inside pressure:

$$p_i = p_o + \Delta p = 101325 + 12 = 101337 \text{ Pa} \approx 101.34 \text{ kPa}$$

---

### Example 2: Capillary Rise of Water in a Glass Tube

**Problem:** Find the height to which water will rise in a glass capillary tube of 1.0 mm diameter at 20 °C. Take $\sigma = 0.0728 \text{ N/m}$, $\theta = 0°$, $\rho_{\text{water}} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

**Given:**
- $R = 0.5 \text{ mm} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$
- $\sigma = 0.0728 \text{ N/m}$
- $\theta = 0° \Rightarrow \cos\theta = 1$
- $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Solution:**

$$h = \frac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g R} = \frac{2 \times 0.0728 \times 1}{1000 \times 9.81 \times 5 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$h = \frac{0.1456}{4.905} = 0.0297 \text{ m} \approx 29.7 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{h \approx 29.7 \text{ mm} = 2.97 \text{ cm}}$$

---

### Example 3: Work Done in Breaking Up a Liquid Jet

**Problem:** A liquid jet of 5 mm diameter is broken up into 1000 droplets each of 0.5 mm diameter. If the surface tension is 0.07 N/m, find the work done in the process.

**Given:**
- $R_{\text{jet}} = 2.5 \text{ mm} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$
- $R_{\text{droplet}} = 0.25 \text{ mm} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$
- $N = 1000$ droplets
- $\sigma = 0.07 \text{ N/m}$

**Solution:**
