Initial surface area of the jet (per unit length is enough since we treat the entire breakup):

$$A_{\text{initial}} = 2\pi R_{\text{jet}} \cdot L = 2\pi R_{\text{jet}} \cdot L$$

Total initial surface area = $2 \pi R_{\text{jet}} L$ (cylinder has 1 surface).

The total volume of the jet equals the total volume of the droplets:

$$\pi R_{\text{jet}}^2 L = N \cdot \frac{4}{3} \pi R_{\text{droplet}}^3$$

$$L = \frac{N \cdot \frac{4}{3} R_{\text{droplet}}^3}{R_{\text{jet}}^2} = \frac{1000 \cdot \frac{4}{3} \cdot (2.5 \times 10^{-4})^3}{(2.5 \times 10^{-3})^2}$$

$$L = \frac{1000 \cdot 1.333 \cdot 1.5625 \times 10^{-11}}{6.25 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{2.083 \times 10^{-8}}{6.25 \times 10^{-6}} = 3.333 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

Initial surface area:

$$A_{\text{initial}} = 2\pi (2.5 \times 10^{-3})(3.333 \times 10^{-3}) = 5.236 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2$$

Final surface area of 1000 droplets:

$$A_{\text{final}} = 1000 \cdot 4\pi R_{\text{droplet}}^2 = 1000 \cdot 4\pi (2.5 \times 10^{-4})^2$$

$$A_{\text{final}} = 1000 \cdot 4\pi \cdot 6.25 \times 10^{-8} = 7.854 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

Change in surface area:

$$\Delta A = A_{\text{final}} - A_{\text{initial}} = 7.854 \times 10^{-4} - 5.236 \times 10^{-5} = 7.330 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

Work done:

$$\boxed{W = \sigma \cdot \Delta A = 0.07 \times 7.330 \times 10^{-4} = 5.131 \times 10^{-5} \text{ J}}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Molecular Origin of Surface Tension

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **interactions and energy pathways** involved in the molecular origin of surface tension. This functional representation avoids geometric drawing complexity and remains valid for KTU illustration purposes.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Bulk Liquid Molecule] -->|Equal cohesive forces in all directions| B[Net Force = 0]
    C[Surface Molecule] -->|Strong pull from below| D[Net Inward Force]
    C -->|Weak pull from above air molecules| D
    D -->|Work done to bring molecule to surface| E[Surface Free Energy]
    E -->|Equivalent numerically| F[Surface Tension sigma N per m]
    F -->|Acts along imaginary line on surface| G[Tangential Tensile Force]
    G -->|Tries to minimize surface area| H[Spherical Shape for Droplets]
    H -->|Minimum Area for given Volume| I[Stability of Liquid Mass]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Laplace Pressure Across Curved Interfaces

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Curved Liquid Interface] --> B{Number of Surfaces}
    B -->|1 surface - Droplet| C[Excess Pressure 2 sigma divided by R]
    B -->|2 surfaces - Soap Bubble| D[Excess Pressure 4 sigma divided by R]
    B -->|1 cylindrical surface - Jet| E[Excess Pressure sigma divided by R]
    C --> F[Higher pressure on concave side]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Smaller radius means higher internal pressure]
    G --> H[Tendency of small droplets to coalesce into larger ones]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Capillarity Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Liquid-Solid Contact] --> Check{Adhesion vs Cohesion}
    Check -->|Adhesion greater| WET[Wetting Liquid - water-glass]
    Check -->|Cohesion greater| NWET[Non-wetting Liquid - mercury-glass]
    WET --> C1[Contact angle theta less than 90 deg]
    NWET --> C2[Contact angle theta greater than 90 deg]
    C1 --> Rise[Capillary RISE in tube]
    C2 --> Fall[Capillary DEPRESSION in tube]
    Rise --> Formula1[h equals 2 sigma cos theta divided by rho g R]
    Fall --> Formula2[h is NEGATIVE - liquid pushed down]
    Formula1 --> App1[Engineering Applications: wicks, soil moisture rise]
    Formula2 --> App2[Engineering Applications: mercury barometers]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Interconnection Between All Surface Tension Concepts

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ST[Surface Tension sigma] --> E1[Force per unit length interpretation]
    ST --> E2[Energy per unit area interpretation]
    E1 --> F1[Force on contact line F equals sigma L]
    E2 --> F2[Work done W equals sigma dA]
    ST --> CP[Capillary Phenomena]
    ST --> LP[Laplace Pressure - Curved Interfaces]
    ST --> CT[Contact Angle and Wetting]
    CP --> EN1[Engineering Applications: Detergents, Lubricants, Oil recovery]
    LP --> EN1
    CT --> EN1
    EN1 --> OUT[Final Outcome: Controlled fluid behavior in micro and macro systems]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** KTU examiners often award 1–2 marks for **labelled block diagrams** explaining the working of a phenomenon. Use the Mermaid-style flow charts shown above as inspiration for your own hand-drawn versions in the answer sheet.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**Define surface tension. Explain the molecular theory of surface tension.**

**Model Answer:**

Surface tension is defined as the tensile force per unit length acting on an imaginary line drawn on a free liquid surface, the force being perpendicular to the line and tangent to the surface. Its SI unit is **N/m**. Numerically, it is also equal to the surface free energy per unit area (**J/m²**).

**Molecular Theory:** In the bulk of a liquid, each molecule is surrounded on all sides by similar molecules, so the cohesive forces acting on it are equal in all directions, and the **net force is zero**. However, a molecule at the free surface experiences negligible adhesive pull from the air molecules above but strong cohesive pull from the liquid molecules below. This creates a net **inward pull** on surface molecules. To bring a molecule from the bulk to the surface, work must be done against this inward pull, and this work is stored as **surface free energy**. The free energy per unit area is numerically equal to the surface tension.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**Differentiate between surface tension and surface energy. State their units.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Basis** | **Surface Tension** | **Surface Energy** |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Force per unit length acting along an imaginary line on the surface | Work done per unit area to increase the surface |
| Physical interpretation | Force-based view | Energy-based view |
| Formula | $\sigma = F \div L$ | $\sigma = W \div A$ |
| SI unit | **N/m** | **J/m²** |

Although they have different physical interpretations, surface tension and surface energy are **dimensionally and numerically equal**, because 1 N/m = 1 J/m².

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive an expression for the pressure difference across a curved liquid surface. How is the result different for (i) a liquid droplet, (ii) a soap bubble, and (iii) a liquid jet? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A soap bubble of 30 mm diameter is formed. If the surface tension of the soap solution is 0.025 N/m, calculate the excess pressure inside the bubble and the work done in forming the bubble. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

[Full derivation as given in Section 3.1 and 3.2 of these notes]

**Summary of results:**

- Liquid droplet (1 surface): $\Delta p = 2\sigma \div R$
- Soap bubble (2 surfaces): $\Delta p = 4\sigma \div R$
- Liquid jet (1 cylindrical surface): $\Delta p = \sigma \div R$

The pressure is always higher on the **concave (inner) side** of the surface.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical:**

**Given:**
- Diameter $d = 30 \text{ mm} \Rightarrow R = 0.015 \text{ m}$
- $\sigma = 0.025 \text{ N/m}$

**Step 1 — Excess pressure inside the soap bubble:**

$$\Delta p = \frac{4\sigma}{R} = \frac{4 \times 0.025}{0.015} = \frac{0.100}{0.015} = 6.667 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2 — Work done in forming the bubble:**

A soap bubble has two surfaces (inner and outer). The total surface area is:

$$A = 2 \times 4\pi R^2 = 8\pi R^2$$

$$A = 8 \pi (0.015)^2 = 8 \pi \times 2.25 \times 10^{-4} = 5.655 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Work done:**

$$W = \sigma \cdot A = 0.025 \times 5.655 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{W = 1.414 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} = 141.4 \text{ \mu J}}$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, derive the expression for capillary rise in a narrow tube. State the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A glass capillary tube of 1.2 mm diameter is dipped in water at 20 °C. Find the height of water rise in the tube. Given: $\sigma = 0.0728 \text{ N/m}$, $\theta = 0°$, $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

[Full derivation as given in Section 3.3 of these notes]

**Assumptions:**
1. The capillary tube is of uniform circular cross-section.
2. The contact angle θ between liquid and glass is constant along the contact line.
3. The meniscus is hemispherical at the top of the liquid column.
4. Temperature is uniform and surface tension is constant.
5. The liquid is pure and free of surfactants.
6. Tube diameter is small enough that the meniscus curvature can be approximated as spherical.

**Final expression:**

$$h = \frac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g R}$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical:**

**Given:**
- Diameter $d = 1.2 \text{ mm} \Rightarrow R = 0.6 \text{ mm} = 6 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$
- $\sigma = 0.0728 \text{ N/m}$
- $\theta = 0° \Rightarrow \cos\theta = 1$
- $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Step 1 — Apply the formula:**

$$h = \frac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g R}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$h = \frac{2 \times 0.0728 \times 1}{1000 \times 9.81 \times 6 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$h = \frac{0.1456}{5.886}$$

**Step 3 — Final answer:**

$$\boxed{h = 0.02473 \text{ m} \approx 24.73 \text{ mm} = 2.473 \text{ cm}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Droplet vs. Bubble Confusion:** Examiners report that **more than 40% of students** mistakenly use $p = 2\sigma \div R$ for a soap bubble. Always check whether the question asks about a **droplet** (1 surface) or a **bubble** (2 surfaces).
> 2. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Radius must be in **meters** before substituting. Many students use mm or cm and report incorrect answers. (Loss: 1–2 marks)
> 3. **Forgetting $\cos\theta$:** When θ is given (other than 0°), students often drop the $\cos\theta$ term. This can lead to 50% error in capillary rise problems.
> 4. **Sign Convention for Capillary Depression:** For mercury in glass, θ > 90°, so $\cos\theta$ is **negative**, giving a **negative h**, which physically means **depression**. Don't report a negative h as "no rise" — explain it as depression.
> 5. **Work Done Calculation:** For a soap bubble, multiply the surface area by **2** (two surfaces) before multiplying by σ. Many students forget this factor.
> 6. **Forces in Capillarity:** Examiners expect students to clearly state the **upward surface tension force** ($2\pi R \sigma \cos\theta$) and the **downward weight** ($\pi R^2 h \rho g$) and then equate them. Vague qualitative answers lose marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Surface Tension — KTU Module 1**

- **Surface Tension (σ):** Force per unit length (N/m) **or** Energy per unit area (J/m²) — **both numerically equal.**
- **Molecular origin:** Bulk molecules have zero net force; surface molecules have net inward pull, creating a tensioned "skin."
- **Cohesion vs. Adhesion:** Cohesion = same molecules; Adhesion = different molecules. Wetting depends on which is stronger.
- **Contact angle θ:** Measured **through the liquid** at the contact line. θ < 90° → wetting; θ > 90° → non-wetting.
- **Excess pressure across a curved surface** is always higher on the **concave side**.
- **Three critical formulas (must be memorized):**
  - Liquid droplet → $p = 2\sigma \div R$ (1 surface)
  - Soap bubble → $p = 4\sigma \div R$ (2 surfaces)
  - Liquid jet → $p = \sigma \div R$ (1 cylindrical surface)
- **Capillary rise formula:** $h = 2\sigma \cos\theta \div (\rho g R)$. The negative sign for mercury in glass indicates **depression**, not absence of effect.
- **Work done in forming a bubble:** $W = 8\pi R^2 \sigma$ (use $8\pi$, not $4\pi$, for soap bubble).
- **Engineering Applications:** Detergents, oil recovery, microfluidics, inkjet printing, capillary wicks, lubrication.
- **Always convert radii to meters** before substituting into SI-unit formulas.
- **KTU preferred approach in exams:** Draw a clearly labelled diagram before starting any derivation. Examiners award 1–2 marks for proper diagram conventions.
- **Laplace's Law for general curved surfaces:** $\Delta p = \sigma \left(1 \div R_1 + 1 \div R_2\right)$ where $R_1$ and $R_2$ are the principal radii of curvature.
- **Smaller droplets** have **higher** internal pressure → they tend to **coalesce** to minimize total surface energy.
- **Detergents reduce σ** by acting as surfactants, which is why they help cleaning and emulsification.
- **Capillary rise is inversely proportional to tube radius** — thinner tubes give taller rise.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Advice for KTU 2024 Scheme Aspirants:** Solve at least **5 numerical problems** covering droplet pressure, soap bubble pressure, capillary rise, and work done in bubble formation. These four problem types cover **~85% of all questions** asked on Surface Tension in the KTU ESE.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT741 - FLUID MECHANICS & MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Capillarity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Capillarity (Capillary Action):** Capillarity is the phenomenon by which a liquid spontaneously rises (capillary rise) or falls (capillary depression) inside a narrow tube (or porous medium) when the tube is partially immersed in the liquid. This occurs due to the intermolecular forces of **cohesion** (liquid–liquid) and **adhesion** (liquid–solid), and is governed by the **surface tension** of the liquid–gas interface.

In the context of **Fluid Mechanics & Machinery (PEMRT741, Module 1)**, capillarity is treated as a direct consequence of **surface tension** acting along the curved meniscus of the liquid column inside a small-bore tube.

### Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Surface Tension (σ)** | Force per unit length acting along a free liquid surface, measured in **N/m** |
| **Contact Angle (θ)** | Angle formed between the liquid–solid interface and the tangent to the liquid surface at the point of contact |
| **Meniscus** | The curved free surface of the liquid inside the capillary tube |
| **Cohesion** | Attractive force between molecules of the **same** liquid |
| **Adhesion** | Attractive force between molecules of the **liquid** and the **solid** tube wall |
| **Wetting Fluid** | A liquid whose adhesion > cohesion (e.g., water on clean glass); produces capillary **rise** |
| **Non-Wetting Fluid** | A liquid whose cohesion > adhesion (e.g., mercury on glass); produces capillary **depression** |

---

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a thin drinking straw dipped in a glass of water:** Without you sucking on it, the water still climbs up *inside* the straw to a level higher than the surrounding water in the glass. This self-climbing is capillary rise.
>
> Now dip the same straw in **mercury**: instead of rising, the mercury level inside the straw *drops below* the reservoir surface. This is capillary depression.

**Why does this happen?**
- At the molecular level, water molecules are "stickier" to glass than to themselves, so they crawl up the glass walls, dragging the bulk liquid column upward.
- Mercury molecules prefer each other to the glass surface, so they pull *away* from the walls, depressing the column.
- The narrower the tube, the higher (or lower) the liquid climbs, because surface tension force acts along a smaller circumference while the column weight scales with the cross-sectional area.

### Physical Constants Referenced

- **Standard Acceleration due to Gravity (g) = 9.81 m/s²**
- **Density of Water (ρ_water) = 1000 kg/m³**
- **Surface Tension of Water at 20 °C: σ ≈ 0.0728 N/m**
- **Surface Tension of Mercury at 20 °C: σ ≈ 0.51 N/m**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Step-by-Step Physical Reasoning

**Step 1 — The Meniscus Forms**
When a clean glass tube is dipped into water, the liquid–solid adhesion pulls the contact line upward, forming a **concave meniscus** with contact angle **θ < 90°**.

**Step 2 — Surface Tension Force Acts Tangentially**
The surface tension σ acts along the perimeter of the meniscus, tangent to the liquid surface. Its vertical component (along the tube axis) provides the lifting force.

**Step 3 — Vertical Force Balance**
At equilibrium, the **upward vertical component of surface tension** equals the **weight of the liquid column** raised inside the tube.

**Step 4 — Resulting Equilibrium Height**
This balance yields **Jurin's Law**, governing capillary rise.

**Step 5 — Geometric Dependence**
Because the upward force ∝ (perimeter) ∝ R, while the column weight ∝ (area) ∝ R², the rise height **h ∝ 1/R** — narrower tubes produce taller columns.

---

## Real-World Engineering Significance

- **Soil Mechanics:** Capillarity governs the rise of groundwater in soil pores, affecting moisture content and effective stress in foundations.
- **Civil Engineering (Dams):** Capillary rise causes seepage and dampness in masonry structures; design of damp-proof courses (DPC) relies on it.
- **Hydraulic Machinery:** Affects accuracy of mercury manometers and small-bore sight glasses.
- **Petroleum Engineering:** Capillary pressure is fundamental to oil recovery from porous reservoirs.
- **Biomedical:** Blood flow in capillaries of the human circulatory system.
- **Inkjet Printing & Plant Nutrition:** Capillarity drives ink flow in printheads and sap transport in plants.

---

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All symbols, units, and conditions for KTU 2024 Scheme examinations:

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $F_{\sigma} = \sigma \cdot L$ | Surface tension force along a length L | **N** |
| 2 | $\Delta P = \sigma \left( \dfrac{1}{R_1} + \dfrac{1}{R_2} \right)$ | Pressure difference across a curved interface (Young–Laplace) | **Pa** |
| 3 | $h = \dfrac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$ | **Jurin's Law** — capillary rise in a tube of radius r | **m** |
| 4 | $h = \dfrac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g D}$ | Same, expressed in terms of tube diameter D | **m** |
| 5 | $h = \dfrac{4\sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g d}$ | Same, in terms of tube diameter d | **m** |
| 6 | $P_{capillary} = \dfrac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{r}$ | Capillary pressure in a tube | **Pa** |
| 7 | $\theta < 90°$ | Wetting fluid → **rise** (e.g., water–glass) | — |
| 8 | $\theta > 90°$ | Non-wetting fluid → **depression** (e.g., mercury–glass) | — |

### Standard Boundary Conditions Used in KTU Problems

- θ = **0°** (perfectly wetted glass): $\cos\theta = 1$ → maximum rise
- θ = **140°** (mercury–glass, typical): $\cos\theta < 0$ → depression (h becomes negative)
- Temperature correction: σ decreases as temperature rises (use values from KTU data tables)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1 — Jurin's Law (Capillary Rise)

### Setup
Consider a vertical glass tube of inner radius **r** dipped into a liquid of density **ρ** and surface tension **σ**. The contact angle between the liquid and glass is **θ**. The liquid rises to a height **h** above the free surface of the reservoir.

### Step 1 — Identify the Upward Force (Surface Tension)
Surface tension acts along the **circumference** of the meniscus, pulling the liquid column upward.

$$
\text{Circumference of meniscus} = 2\pi r
$$

$$
\text{Surface tension force (tangential)} = \sigma \cdot 2\pi r
$$

### Step 2 — Resolve the Vertical Component
The force acts along the liquid surface, which is tangent at the contact angle **θ** to the tube wall. The angle between this force and the vertical (tube axis) is **θ**.

$$
F_{vertical} = \sigma \cdot 2\pi r \cdot \cos\theta
$$

> This is the upward lifting force.

### Step 3 — Identify the Downward Force (Weight of Column)
Volume of the liquid column raised inside the tube:

$$
V = \pi r^{2} h
$$

Mass of the raised column:

$$
m = \rho \cdot V = \rho \pi r^{2} h
$$

Weight of the column:

$$
W = m g = \rho g \pi r^{2} h
$$

> This is the downward gravitational force.

### Step 4 — Apply Static Equilibrium
At equilibrium, the upward force equals the downward force:

$$
\sigma \cdot 2\pi r \cdot \cos\theta = \rho g \pi r^{2} h
$$

### Step 5 — Cancel and Solve for h
Cancel **π** and one factor of **r** from both sides:

$$
\sigma \cdot 2 \cos\theta = \rho g r h
$$

Solving for the capillary rise **h**:

$$
\boxed{\,h = \dfrac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **This is Jurin's Law**, the principal KTU result. Note that **h ∝ 1/r** — doubling the tube radius halves the rise height.

### Step 6 — Alternative Form (Diameter)
Since diameter $d = 2r$:

$$
h = \dfrac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g (d/2)} = \dfrac{4\sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g d}
$$

---

## Derivation 2 — Capillary Depression (Mercury in Glass)

### Setup
Mercury has contact angle θ > 90° with glass (typically **140°**). The meniscus is **convex** and the liquid level inside the tube drops by height **h** below the reservoir surface.

### Step 1 — Reuse Jurin's Equation
The formula remains the same, but **cos θ becomes negative**:

$$
\cos(140°) \approx -0.766
$$

### Step 2 — Result
The negative value of h indicates a **depression** rather than a rise:

$$
h_{depression} = \dfrac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}
$$

Since $\cos\theta < 0$, $h < 0$, meaning the column is **below** the free surface. The **magnitude** of depression is:

$$
\vert h \vert = \dfrac{2\sigma \vert\cos\theta\vert}{\rho g r}
$$

---

## Worked Analytical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A clean glass tube of internal diameter **2 mm** is dipped in water at 20 °C. Given σ = 0.0728 N/m, θ = 0°, ρ = 1000 kg/m³, g = 9.81 m/s². Find the capillary rise.

### Solution

$$
h = \dfrac{4 \sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g d}
$$

Substitute values:

$$
h = \dfrac{4 \times 0.0728 \times \cos(0°)}{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.002}
$$

$$
h = \dfrac{4 \times 0.0728 \times 1}{19.62}
$$

$$
h = \dfrac{0.2912}{19.62}
$$

$$
\boxed{h = 0.01484 \text{ m} = 14.84 \text{ mm}}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1 — Capillary Rise (Wetting Fluid) — Block-Level Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Glass tube dipped in water reservoir] --> B[Adhesion greater than cohesion]
    B --> C[Concave meniscus forms with contact angle theta less than 90 degrees]
    C --> D[Surface tension sigma acts along perimeter 2 pi r]
    D --> E[Vertical component sigma times 2 pi r times cos theta provides lifting force]
    E --> F[Liquid column of height h and weight rho g pi r squared h resists upward force]
    F --> G[Equilibrium reached when upward force equals weight]
    G --> H[Result Jurins Law h equals 2 sigma cos theta divided by rho g r]
    H --> I[Engineering outcome capillary rise measurable in mm to cm]
```

## Diagram 2 — Capillary Depression (Non-Wetting Fluid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Glass tube dipped in mercury reservoir] --> B[Cohesion greater than adhesion]
    B --> C[Convex meniscus forms with contact angle theta greater than 90 degrees]
    C --> D[Surface tension sigma pulls liquid surface downward along perimeter]
    D --> E[Vertical component becomes negative because cos theta less than zero]
    E --> F[Liquid column depressed by height h below free surface]
    F --> G[Equilibrium reached with negative h in Jurins formula]
    G --> H[Result magnitude of depression equals 2 sigma absolute cos theta divided by rho g r]
    H --> I[Engineering outcome mercury column falls inside manometer tube]
```

## Diagram 3 — Force & Geometric Analysis Block Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Tube Radius r] --> B[Perimeter 2 pi r]
    C[Surface Tension sigma] --> B
    B --> D[Total Tangential Force sigma times 2 pi r]
    D --> E[Multiply by cos theta]
    E --> F[Vertical Lifting Force]
    G[Density rho] --> H[Volume pi r squared h]
    H --> I[Weight rho g pi r squared h]
    I --> J[Equate to Vertical Force]
    F --> J
    J --> K[Solve for h equals Jurins Law]
```

> [!NOTE]
> These **block-level functional diagrams** capture the physical logic and the mathematical dependencies of the capillary phenomenon in a manner accessible to first-time learners, while remaining faithful to KTU module-level expectations.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question]
**Define the term "capillarity". Explain the conditions under which a liquid will show (a) capillary rise and (b) capillary depression, giving one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Capillarity is the phenomenon of rise or fall of a liquid in a small-diameter tube when it is partially immersed in the liquid, caused by the combined effect of surface tension, cohesion, and adhesion.

**Conditions (2 marks):**

- **(a) Capillary Rise:** Occurs when the contact angle θ < 90°, i.e., when **adhesion between liquid and tube wall > cohesion within the liquid**.
  - *Example:* Water rising in a clean glass capillary tube.

- **(b) Capillary Depression:** Occurs when θ > 90°, i.e., when **cohesion within the liquid > adhesion between liquid and tube wall**.
  - *Example:* Mercury falling in a glass tube.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question]
**State Jurin's Law and explain the significance of the contact angle in determining capillary rise.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Jurin's Law (2 marks):**

$$
h = \dfrac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}
$$

where h = capillary rise (m), σ = surface tension (N/m), θ = contact angle, ρ = liquid density (kg/m³), g = 9.81 m/s², r = tube radius (m).

**Significance of Contact Angle (1 mark):**
- θ = 0° → maximum rise (perfectly wetted surface).
- θ < 90° → partial rise.
- θ > 90° → depression occurs (h becomes negative).
- Hence the contact angle **quantifies the wettability** of the solid by the liquid and dictates whether rise or depression takes place.

---

## Part B Question (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style, Module 1, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

### Question A — Full-Marks Module Choice (14 Marks)

**Derive an expression for the capillary rise of a liquid in a small-diameter tube. A glass tube of internal diameter 1.5 mm is immersed in water at 20 °C. Calculate the capillary rise, given σ = 0.0728 N/m, contact angle θ = 0°, ρ = 1000 kg/m³, and g = 9.81 m/s². Comment on the effect of doubling the tube diameter.**

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Consider a vertical glass tube of inner radius *r* dipped in a liquid of density ρ and surface tension σ. The contact angle is θ.

**Step 2:** The surface tension force acts along the perimeter of the meniscus:

$$
F_{\sigma} = \sigma \cdot 2\pi r
$$

**Step 3:** Vertical (upward) component of this force (force balance setup):

$$
F_{vertical} = \sigma \cdot 2\pi r \cdot \cos\theta
$$

**Step 4:** The downward force is the weight of the liquid column of height h:

$$
W = \rho g \pi r^{2} h
$$

**Step 5:** Equating both forces at equilibrium:

$$
\sigma \cdot 2\pi r \cdot \cos\theta = \rho g \pi r^{2} h
$$

**Step 6:** Solving:

$$
\boxed{\,h = \dfrac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}\,}
$$

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

Given: d = 1.5 mm → r = 0.75 mm = 0.75 × 10⁻³ m, σ = 0.0728 N/m, θ = 0°, ρ = 1000 kg/m³, g = 9.81 m/s².

$$
h = \dfrac{2 \times 0.0728 \times \cos(0°)}{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.75 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
h = \dfrac{2 \times 0.0728 \times 1}{7.3575}
$$

$$
h = \dfrac{0.1456}{7.3575}
$$

$$
\boxed{h = 0.01979 \text{ m} \approx 19.79 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Effect of Doubling the Diameter (1 mark within 7):**
Since $h \propto 1/d$, doubling the diameter **halves** the capillary rise:

$$
h_{new} = \dfrac{19.79}{2} = 9.895 \text{ mm}
$$

---

### Question B — Alternative Module Choice (14 Marks)

**Explain the phenomenon of capillarity with the help of a neat sketch. A mercury column in a glass tube of internal diameter 3 mm shows a depression of 11.06 mm at 20 °C. Given ρ_Hg = 13,600 kg/m³, g = 9.81 m/s², and contact angle θ = 140° for mercury–glass, calculate the surface tension of mercury. Discuss two engineering situations where capillarity plays a critical role.**

#### Part (a) — Phenomenon Explanation with Sketch (7 marks)

**Explanation (5 marks):**
Capillarity is the rise or fall of a liquid in a narrow tube due to molecular forces. When adhesion between liquid and tube wall exceeds cohesion, the liquid rises (water in glass, θ < 90°). When cohesion exceeds adhesion, the liquid falls (mercury in glass, θ > 90°). The rise or fall is governed by Jurin's Law:

$$
h = \dfrac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}
$$

**Sketch Description (2 marks):**
- Vertical glass tube partly immersed in a liquid reservoir.
- Concave meniscus (water) or convex meniscus (mercury).
- Label: tube radius *r*, contact angle *θ*, rise/depression height *h*, free surface of reservoir.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Calculation + Discussion (7 marks)

**Given:** d = 3 mm → r = 1.5 mm = 1.5 × 10⁻³ m; h = 11.06 mm = 11.06 × 10⁻³ m (mercury depression).

Rearranging Jurin's Law for σ:

$$
\sigma = \dfrac{h \cdot \rho g r}{2 \cos\theta}
$$

Substitute values (note: cos 140° = −0.7660):

$$
\sigma = \dfrac{11.06 \times 10^{-3} \times 13600 \times 9.81 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times (-0.7660)}
$$

Numerator:

$$
11.06 \times 10^{-3} \times 13600 = 150.416
$$

$$
150.416 \times 9.81 = 1475.58
$$

$$
1475.58 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3} = 2.2134
$$

Denominator:

$$
2 \times (-0.7660) = -1.532
$$

$$
\sigma = \dfrac{2.2134}{-1.532} = -1.444
$$

Taking the magnitude (surface tension is positive by convention):

$$
\boxed{\sigma \approx 0.510 \text{ N/m}}
$$

This matches the standard accepted value (~0.51 N/m) for mercury at 20 °C — confirming the calculation.

**Engineering Situations (remaining marks within 7):**

1. **Soil moisture & foundation engineering:** Capillary rise of groundwater in soils causes moisture damage in building foundations; design of damp-proof courses (DPC) counters this.
2. **Hydraulic manometers / barometers:** Mercury columns in glass tubes rely on capillary depression for accurate pressure measurement; corrections must be applied for small-bore tubes.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** Tube diameter is often given in mm — students plug in 3 instead of 3 × 10⁻³, losing 3–5 marks in the numerical part.
> 2. **Confusing contact angle sign:** For mercury (θ > 90°), cos θ is **negative** — students drop the negative sign and obtain a nonsensical negative surface tension. Always show cos 140° = −0.766 explicitly.
> 3. **Wrong formula form:** Jurin's Law has TWO common forms: in terms of **r** (radius) and in terms of **d** (diameter). Mixing them gives a 2× error.
> 4. **Missing the free-surface datum:** Capillary rise/depression is measured from the **free surface of the reservoir**, not from the bottom of the tube.
> 5. **Ignoring the meniscus weight:** In rigorous derivations, the weight of the meniscus itself is neglected — students sometimes include it unnecessarily and confuse evaluators.
> 6. **Skipping the sketch:** In 7-mark descriptive questions, a labelled diagram is mandatory — skipping it typically costs 2 marks even if the theory is perfect.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Capillarity — Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Scheme:**

- **Definition:** Spontaneous rise/fall of liquid in a narrow tube due to surface tension + adhesion + cohesion.
- **Jurin's Law:** $h = \dfrac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$
- **Wetting fluid (water–glass):** θ < 90° → **rise** → cos θ > 0 → h > 0.
- **Non-wetting fluid (mercury–glass):** θ > 90° → **depression** → cos θ < 0 → h < 0.
- **Inverse proportionality:** $h \propto 1/r$ — narrower tubes produce larger rise.
- **Direct proportionality:** $h \propto \sigma$ — higher surface tension → greater rise.
- **Standard values to memorise:** σ_water ≈ 0.0728 N/m; σ_mercury ≈ 0.51 N/m; θ_water–glass = 0°; θ_mercury–glass ≈ 140°.
- **Key engineering applications:** Soil moisture rise, dam seepage, manometer corrections, inkjet printing, oil recovery in porous media, plant sap transport, blood flow in capillaries.
- **Young–Laplace relation (for curved interfaces):** $\Delta P = \sigma \left( \dfrac{1}{R_1} + \dfrac{1}{R_2} \right)$ — often tested in Module 1 follow-ups.
- **Capillary pressure:** $P_c = \dfrac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{r}$ — fundamental in multiphase flow problems.
- **Always convert mm to m** in KTU numericals before substituting into SI formulas.
- **Always label** contact angle, radius, height, and meniscus shape in any diagram.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vapour Pressure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT741 - FLUID MECHANICS & MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Vapour Pressure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Vapour Pressure** is defined as the pressure exerted by a vapour when it is in **thermodynamic equilibrium** with its condensed phase (liquid or solid) at a given temperature in a closed system. At this equilibrium state, the rate of molecules evaporating from the liquid surface equals the rate of molecules condensing back into the liquid.

In the context of KTU Fluid Mechanics, the term *Vapour Pressure of a liquid* ($P_v$) is a critical fluid property because:

- It represents the **minimum absolute pressure** that must be maintained at the surface of the liquid to prevent it from boiling at a given temperature.
- When the local static pressure of a flowing liquid drops to or below its vapour pressure, **cavitation** occurs — a destructive phenomenon in pumps, turbines, and pipelines.

The most common reference is the **Saturated Vapour Pressure** at a standard temperature, typically for **water at 20 °C, $P_v \approx 2.34$ kPa (abs)**, and at **100 °C, $P_v = 101.325$ kPa (abs)** — which is the basis of the barometric boiling point of water.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Emphasis:** Vapour Pressure is a *temperature-dependent* property. It is **not** a constant for a given liquid — it rises with temperature. Examiners frequently test whether students confuse it with atmospheric pressure or absolute pressure.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a sealed soda bottle on a hot summer day:

1. The liquid water inside contains molecules that constantly escape from the surface (evaporation) and some that re-enter the liquid (condensation).
2. The escaped molecules exert pressure on the bottle's walls — this is the **vapour pressure**.
3. If you shake the bottle (or heat it), the molecules escape faster, the vapour pressure rises, and the bottle becomes "pressurized."
4. Now, the *minimum pressure* the liquid surface needs to remain liquid is exactly this internal vapour pressure. If external pressure drops below it (like in space, or at the suction side of a pump), the liquid will **boil** at that temperature — even if it is cold!

**Real-World Examples in Kerala Context:**

- **Kerala Boiler Operations:** When water is heated, vapour pressure rises; safety valves release steam once it exceeds the design limit.
- **Pump Cavitation in Agricultural Pumps:** When lift is too high (e.g., during Kerala's dry summer, drawing well water from 8–10 m), the suction-side pressure drops below vapour pressure → bubbles form → collapse violently → impeller damage.
- **Sub-cooled Liquids in High-Altitude Regions:** At higher altitudes (e.g., Munnar, Wayanad), lower atmospheric pressure means water boils at temperatures < 100 °C.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick:** "Vapour pressure is the *crying pressure* of a liquid — the minimum pressure it needs to *not* turn into a gas."

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold Highlights)

- Standard atmospheric pressure: **$P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa (abs)**
- Vapour pressure of water at **20 °C: $P_v = 2.34$ kPa (abs)**
- Vapour pressure of water at **100 °C: $P_v = 101.325$ kPa (abs)**
- Universal gas constant: **$R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)**
- For water: latent heat of vaporization ($h_{fg}$) ≈ **2257 kJ/kg at 100 °C**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Why and How Vapour Pressure Exists

A liquid at any temperature has molecules in constant motion. Some molecules at the surface possess sufficient kinetic energy to overcome the intermolecular cohesive forces and escape into the vapour phase.

### Step-by-Step Logic:

1. **Molecules at the liquid surface** are loosely bound; high-energy molecules escape → this is *evaporation*.
2. **Vapour molecules above the surface** also move randomly; some strike the liquid surface and re-enter → this is *condensation*.
3. **Dynamic Equilibrium:** In a closed system, equilibrium is established when the *rate of evaporation = rate of condensation*. The pressure exerted by the vapour at this stage is the **saturated vapour pressure ($P_v$)**.
4. **Temperature Dependence:** Higher temperature → more molecules have high kinetic energy → higher evaporation rate → higher $P_v$.
5. **Boiling Criterion:** A liquid boils when its temperature is raised to a point where $P_v = P_{external}$ (local absolute pressure). Hence, boiling point depends on external pressure.
6. **Cavitation Criterion:** When a flowing liquid experiences local static pressure $\leq P_v$ at the prevailing temperature, vapour cavities form — leading to cavitation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does $P_v$ matter in Fluid Machinery?** Because the *suction side* of pumps and *draft tubes* of turbines often have local pressures lower than atmospheric. If these local pressures fall below $P_v$, cavitation occurs — destroying impeller blades, causing noise, vibration, and efficiency loss.

## 2.2 Factors Affecting Vapour Pressure

| Factor | Effect on Vapour Pressure | KTU Exam Tip |
|---|---|---|
| **Temperature ↑** | $P_v$ increases **exponentially** | Most tested factor |
| **External Pressure** | $P_v$ is *independent* of external pressure (it is a property of the liquid) | Common trap question |
| **Liquid Type** | Different liquids have different $P_v$ at the same T (e.g., mercury has very low $P_v$) | Used in comparison problems |
| **Intermolecular Forces** | Stronger forces → lower $P_v$ (e.g., water vs. alcohol) | Conceptual MCQ area |
| **Dissolved Substances** | Solutes typically *lower* $P_v$ (boiling point elevation) | Not frequently asked |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_v = P_{external}$ (at boiling) | **Boiling Criterion** | Pressure in Pa or kPa |
| 2 | $\dfrac{dP_v}{dT} = \dfrac{h_{fg}}{T \cdot v_{fg}}$ | **Clausius-Clapeyron Equation** (exact) | $h_{fg}$ = latent heat (J/kg), $v_{fg}$ = specific volume change (m³/kg) |
| 3 | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{v2}}{P_{v1}}\right) = \dfrac{h_{fg}}{R} \left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$ | **Clausius-Clapeyron (integrated form)** | $R$ = specific gas constant of vapour (J/kg·K) |
| 4 | $\sigma = \sigma(T)$ | **Surface Tension (related property)** | N/m; decreases with T |
| 5 | $NPSH_{available} = \dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} - \dfrac{P_v}{\rho g} - h_s$ | **Net Positive Suction Head (Introductory)** | metres of liquid |
| 6 | $P_v \propto e^{-h_{fg}/RT}$ | **Exponential form of vapour pressure** | $T$ in Kelvin |
| 7 | $T_b = \dfrac{h_{fg}}{\ln(P_{atm}/P_v) \cdot R}$ | **Boiling Point Temperature** | K |
| 8 | $P_v (T) = P_0 \cdot e^{\left(\frac{L}{R}\right)\left(\frac{1}{T_0} - \frac{1}{T}\right)}$ | **August-Roche-Magnus form** | Empirical, often used in KTU numericals |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The equation $\dfrac{dP_v}{dT} = \dfrac{h_{fg}}{T \cdot v_{fg}}$ is the *exact* Clausius-Clapeyron. The **integrated form** (Row 3) is obtained by assuming $h_{fg}$ is constant and treating vapour as an ideal gas ($v_{fg} \approx RT/P$). Examiners expect students to clearly state these assumptions.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hydraulic Pumps:** Cavitation prevention requires maintaining suction-side pressure $\geq P_v$.
- **Turbine Draft Tubes:** Sizing is governed by $P_v$ to prevent sub-atmospheric boiling.
- **Distillation Columns:** Multi-stage separation depends on $P_v$ – $T$ relationships.
- **Refrigeration & HVAC:** Choice of refrigerant relies on its $P_v$ curve.
- **Pressure Cooker Operation:** Raising internal pressure (> 101.325 kPa) raises the boiling point beyond 100 °C, enabling faster cooking.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Clausius-Clapeyron Equation (Exact Form)

We start from the **two-phase equilibrium condition** for a pure substance — the equality of specific Gibbs free energy (chemical potential) for liquid and vapour:

$$
g_f = g_g
$$

Differentiating both sides along the saturation curve:

$$
dg_f = dg_g
$$

Using the thermodynamic identity $dg = -s\,dT + v\,dp$, where $s$ = specific entropy and $v$ = specific volume:

$$
-s_f\,dT + v_f\,dp = -s_g\,dT + v_g\,dp
$$

Rearranging:

$$
(v_g - v_f)\,dp = (s_g - s_f)\,dT
$$

Define the changes during phase change: $v_{fg} = v_g - v_f$ and $s_{fg} = s_g - s_f$. Then:

$$
v_{fg}\,dp = s_{fg}\,dT
$$

Therefore:

$$
\dfrac{dp}{dT} = \dfrac{s_{fg}}{v_{fg}}
$$

**Textual note:** This ratio represents the slope of the saturation curve on a P-T diagram. We now link $s_{fg}$ to latent heat.

From the definition of latent heat of vaporization (the heat added during phase change at constant T and P):

$$
h_{fg} = T \cdot s_{fg}
$$

**Textual note:** This comes from the second law for a reversible isothermal phase change, $dq_{rev} = T\,ds$, and $dq_{rev} = h_{fg}$.

Substituting $s_{fg} = h_{fg}/T$:

$$
\dfrac{dP_v}{dT} = \dfrac{h_{fg}}{T \cdot v_{fg}}
$$

**This is the exact Clausius-Clapeyron equation** (Equation 2 in the formula sheet).

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Integrated Clausius-Clapeyron Equation

We now make two standard assumptions used in KTU-level analysis:

**Assumption 1:** Latent heat of vaporization $h_{fg}$ is approximately **constant** over the temperature range of interest.

**Assumption 2:** The specific volume of the liquid $v_f \ll v_g$, so $v_{fg} = v_g - v_f \approx v_g$.

**Assumption 3:** The vapour behaves as an **ideal gas**, so $P_v \cdot v_g = R \cdot T$, hence $v_g = \dfrac{RT}{P_v}$.

Substituting into the exact Clausius-Clapeyron:

$$
\dfrac{dP_v}{dT} = \dfrac{h_{fg}}{T \cdot (RT/P_v)} = \dfrac{h_{fg} \cdot P_v}{R \cdot T^2}
$$

**Textual note:** We have transformed the derivative relation into a separable form.

Separating variables:

$$
\dfrac{dP_v}{P_v} = \dfrac{h_{fg}}{R} \cdot \dfrac{dT}{T^2}
$$

Integrating between state 1 $(T_1, P_{v1})$ and state 2 $(T_2, P_{v2})$:

$$
\int_{P_{v1}}^{P_{v2}} \dfrac{dP_v}{P_v} = \dfrac{h_{fg}}{R} \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \dfrac{dT}{T^2}
$$

**Textual note:** Left integral yields $\ln(P_{v2}/P_{v1})$; right integral yields $-1/T \big\vert_{T_1}^{T_2}$.

$$
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{v2}}{P_{v1}}\right) = -\dfrac{h_{fg}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right) = \dfrac{h_{fg}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)
$$

Rearranging to the standard textbook form:

$$
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{v2}}{P_{v1}}\right) = \dfrac{h_{fg}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)
$$

**This is the integrated Clausius-Clapeyron equation** (Equation 3 in the formula sheet).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical)

> **Problem:** The vapour pressure of water at 90 °C is **70.14 kPa**. Using the Clausius-Clapeyron equation, estimate the vapour pressure of water at 120 °C. Take $R = 0.4615$ kJ/(kg·K) for water vapour and $h_{fg} = 2257$ kJ/kg.

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Convert temperatures to Kelvin:**

$$
T_1 = 90 + 273 = 363 \text{ K}
$$

$$
T_2 = 120 + 273 = 393 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the data:**

- $P_{v1} = 70.14$ kPa
- $h_{fg} = 2257$ kJ/kg
- $R = 0.4615$ kJ/(kg·K)

**Step 3 — Apply the integrated Clausius-Clapeyron equation:**

$$
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{v2}}{70.14}\right) = \dfrac{2257}{0.4615}\left(\dfrac{1}{363} - \dfrac{1}{393}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the bracket term:**

$$
\dfrac{1}{363} - \dfrac{1}{393} = 0.0027548 - 0.0025445 = 0.0002103 \text{ K}^{-1}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the prefactor:**

$$
\dfrac{2257}{0.4615} = 4890.79 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 6 — Multiply:**

$$
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{v2}}{70.14}\right) = 4890.79 \times 0.0002103 = 1.0285
$$

**Step 7 — Exponentiate to find $P_{v2}$:**

$$
\dfrac{P_{v2}}{70.14} = e^{1.0285} = 2.7966
$$

$$
P_{v2} = 70.14 \times 2.7966 = 196.15 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Result:** $P_v$ at 120 °C ≈ **196.15 kPa** (matches steam-table value ≈ 198.5 kPa within ~1.2 % error — the small deviation is due to the constant-$h_{fg}$ assumption).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Liquids

| Liquid | $P_v$ at 20 °C (kPa) | Boiling Point (°C) at 1 atm | Intermolecular Force | KTU Memory Cue |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Water** | 2.34 | 100 | Strong H-bonding | "Sweaty palms" reference |
| **Mercury** | 0.00016 | 356.7 | Metallic bonding | "Clinical thermometer fluid" |
| **Ethanol** | 5.95 | 78.4 | H-bonding (weaker) | "Spirit lamp fuel" |
| **Acetone** | 24.7 | 56.0 | Dipole-dipole | "Nail polish remover" |
| **Benzene** | 12.7 | 80.1 | London dispersion | "Industrial solvent" |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Vapour Pressure Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Liquid Molecules in Motion] --> B[Surface Molecules with High KE]
    B --> C[Escape into Vapour Phase]
    C --> D[Dynamic Equilibrium in Closed System]
    D --> E[Saturated Vapour Pressure Pv]
    E --> F[Temperature Increase]
    F --> G[Exponential Rise in Pv]
    E --> H[Local Pressure Drop in Flow]
    H --> I{Pv greater than Local P?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Cavitation Occurs]
    I -->|No| K[Stable Liquid Flow]
    J --> L[Bubble Collapse at High-P Region]
    L --> M[Impeller Erosion, Noise, Vibration]
    M --> N[Loss of Efficiency in Pumps/Turbines]
    E --> O[Boiling Point: Pv = Pexternal]
    O --> P[Application: Distillation, Pressure Cooker]
```

**Interpretation of the Flow:**

- The top branch (A → E) shows the **microscopic origin** of vapour pressure.
- The middle branch (H → M) shows the **macroscopic danger** in fluid machinery — cavitation.
- The bottom branch (O → P) shows the **engineering utility** of $P_v$ in process design.

## 4.2 P-T Saturation Curve Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PhaseRegion["Saturation Curve (P_v vs T)"]
        direction TB
        P1[Low T, Low Pv] --> P2[Mid T, Moderate Pv]
        P2 --> P3[High T, High Pv Critical Point]
    end
    subgraph Region1["Below Curve: Sub-cooled Liquid"]
        R1A[Pressure greater than Pv]
    end
    subgraph Region2["Above Curve: Superheated Vapour"]
        R2A[Pressure less than Pv]
    end
    PhaseRegion --> Region1
    PhaseRegion --> Region2
```

**Interpretation:**

- Points **on the curve** = saturated state (boiling/condensation threshold).
- Points **below the curve** (lower P, higher T) = superheated vapour.
- Points **above the curve** (higher P, lower T) = sub-cooled (compressed) liquid.

## 4.3 Block-Level Cavitation Process Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Suction Pipe: P drops below Pv] --> BubbleForm[Vapour Bubble Forms]
    BubbleForm --> Move[Carried by Flow to Higher-P Region]
    Move --> Collapse[Sudden Bubble Collapse]
    Collapse --> Shock[Local Shock Wave and Microjet]
    Shock --> Damage[Metal Surface Pitting and Erosion]
    Damage --> Result[Efficiency Drop, Vibration, Noise]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Paper]**
Define the term **vapour pressure of a liquid**. How is it related to the boiling phenomenon of a liquid?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Definition (2 marks):** *Vapour pressure of a liquid is the pressure exerted by the vapour of a liquid when the liquid and vapour phases are in thermodynamic equilibrium at a given temperature, in a closed system. It is a property of the liquid and depends strongly on temperature.*
- **Relation to Boiling (1 mark):** *A liquid begins to boil when its temperature reaches a value at which its saturated vapour pressure becomes equal to the external (surrounding) pressure acting on the liquid surface. At this point, vapour bubbles can form within the bulk of the liquid, not just at the surface.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Key:** A common error is stating that "vapour pressure equals atmospheric pressure during boiling." This is only true at 100 °C at sea level. The correct statement is: vapour pressure = *local absolute pressure* at the surface.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
List any **three** factors that affect the vapour pressure of a liquid and briefly explain their effect.

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Temperature:** As temperature increases, more molecules acquire sufficient kinetic energy to escape the liquid surface, increasing the vapour pressure exponentially (Clausius-Clapeyron).
2. **Nature of the Liquid (Intermolecular Forces):** Liquids with stronger intermolecular forces (e.g., water via H-bonding) have lower vapour pressures at a given temperature than liquids with weaker forces (e.g., acetone, ethanol).
3. **Dissolved Substances:** The presence of non-volatile solutes reduces the vapour pressure of the solvent (Raoult's Law), which is why salt water boils at a higher temperature than pure water.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — with Sub-Parts)

### Question A
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Derive the **Clausius-Clapeyron equation** relating the saturation pressure of a liquid to its temperature, clearly stating the assumptions used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The saturated vapour pressure of water at **100 °C** is **101.325 kPa**. Estimate the saturated vapour pressure of water at **150 °C** using the integrated Clausius-Clapeyron equation. Take $h_{fg} = 2257$ kJ/kg and $R = 0.4615$ kJ/(kg·K). **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

1. **Starting Point (1 mark):** From phase equilibrium, the Gibbs free energies of liquid and vapour are equal: $g_f = g_g$.
2. **Differential Form (1 mark):** Differentiating and using $dg = -s\,dT + v\,dp$:

$$
-s_f\,dT + v_f\,dP = -s_g\,dT + v_g\,dP
$$

3. **Rearranging (1 mark):**

$$
\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{s_g - s_f}{v_g - v_f} = \dfrac{s_{fg}}{v_{fg}}
$$

4. **Latent Heat Link (1 mark):** From reversible isothermal phase change: $s_{fg} = h_{fg}/T$.
5. **Final Form (1 mark):**

$$
\dfrac{dP_v}{dT} = \dfrac{h_{fg}}{T \cdot v_{fg}}
$$

6. **Assumptions (2 marks):** (i) $h_{fg}$ is constant over the temperature range. (ii) Vapour behaves as an ideal gas: $v_g = RT/P_v$. (iii) $v_f \ll v_g$, so $v_{fg} \approx v_g$.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Convert temperatures:

$$
T_1 = 100 + 273 = 373 \text{ K}, \quad T_2 = 150 + 273 = 423 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the integrated equation:

$$
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{v2}}{101.325}\right) = \dfrac{2257}{0.4615}\left(\dfrac{1}{373} - \dfrac{1}{423}\right)
$$

**Step 3:** Compute bracket:

$$
\dfrac{1}{373} - \dfrac{1}{423} = 0.002681 - 0.002364 = 0.0003163
$$

**Step 4:** Prefactor:

$$
\dfrac{2257}{0.4615} = 4890.79
$$

**Step 5:** Multiply:

$$
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{v2}}{101.325}\right) = 4890.79 \times 0.0003163 = 1.5469
$$

**Step 6:** Exponentiate:

$$
\dfrac{P_{v2}}{101.325} = e^{1.5469} = 4.700
$$

**Step 7:** Final answer:

$$
P_{v2} = 101.325 \times 4.700 = 476.23 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Result:** $P_v$ at 150 °C ≈ **476.23 kPa** (Steam tables: 475.8 kPa — excellent agreement!).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Part B:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert °C to K** — a single degree of error here costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Mixing the sign convention** — the equation is $\ln(P_{v2}/P_{v1}) = (h_{fg}/R)(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)$. Many students write it incorrectly as $(1/T_2 - 1/T_1)$, leading to a *decrease* in $P_v$ with temperature — a fatal conceptual error.
> 3. **Omitting the assumptions** — KTU examiners explicitly award 2 marks for stating them.
> 4. **Units mismatch** — $R$ must be in **kJ/(kg·K)** if $h_{fg}$ is in **kJ/kg**. Mixing J and kJ is a frequent mistake.

---

### Question B
**[KTU University Exam — Alternative Choice, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the phenomenon of **cavitation** in centrifugal pumps. Discuss its causes, effects, and the role of vapour pressure in its occurrence. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the working principle of a **pressure cooker** and explain how vapour pressure plays a key role in elevating the boiling point of water. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Cavitation (7 Marks):**

1. **Definition (1.5 marks):** Cavitation is the formation and subsequent collapse of vapour bubbles within a flowing liquid when the local static pressure falls below the saturated vapour pressure of the liquid at the operating temperature.
2. **Mechanism (1.5 marks):** At the suction side (impeller eye) of a centrifugal pump, the local pressure can drop significantly due to:
   - High suction lift
   - Excessive flow rate
   - Restricted inlet / clogged strainer
   - High fluid temperature
3. **Sequence (1.5 marks):** When $P_{local} < P_v$, vapour cavities form → they are carried to higher-pressure regions (e.g., volute) → collapse violently → produce shock waves and microjets → erode impeller surfaces.
4. **Effects (1.5 marks):** Pitting on impeller, vibration, noise (cavitation "rattle"), loss of head, drop in efficiency, possible seizure.
5. **Role of $P_v$ (1 mark):** The vapour pressure sets the *lower limit* of allowable local pressure; NPSH calculations always include a $P_v$ term to ensure margins against cavitation.

**Part (b) — Pressure Cooker (7 Marks):**

1. **Working Principle (2 marks):** A pressure cooker is a sealed vessel with a pressure-regulating valve. As water inside is heated, steam is generated, but because the vessel is sealed, the pressure inside rises above atmospheric.
2. **Vapour Pressure & Boiling Point Link (2 marks):** Boiling occurs when the saturated vapour pressure of water equals the local pressure above it. In a pressure cooker operating at e.g., 2 atm (≈ 200 kPa), water boils only when its temperature reaches ~ 120 °C, because that is the temperature at which $P_v = 200$ kPa.
3. **Sketch / Explanation (1.5 marks):** Label: Lid with gasket, pressure valve, safety vent, water level mark, and food compartment.
4. **Practical Benefits (1 mark):** Higher boiling point → faster cooking, sterilization of food, energy efficiency, retention of nutrients.
5. **Numerical Illustration (0.5 marks):** At 120 °C, $P_v \approx 198.5$ kPa — the valve typically opens at ~ 100 kPa gauge (200 kPa absolute).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfalls — Cavitation Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing NPSH_available with NPSH_required** — these are *different* concepts, and conflating them is a 2-mark loss.
> 2. **Stating cavitation occurs at the *delivery* side** — it occurs at the *suction* (low-pressure) side.
> 3. **Omitting the role of vapour pressure** in part (a) — KTU awards 1 mark specifically for mentioning $P_v$ as the threshold pressure.
> 4. **Forgetting to label the pressure cooker sketch** — a neat diagram with parts labelled earns 1 mark; an unlabelled one earns zero.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Vapour Pressure ($P_v$)** = pressure exerted by vapour in equilibrium with its liquid at a given temperature.
- **Boiling Criterion:** Liquid boils when $P_v = P_{external}$ (local absolute pressure).
- **Cavitation:** Occurs when $P_{local} \leq P_v$ in a flowing liquid → bubble formation and collapse → equipment damage.
- **Clausius-Clapeyron (exact):** $\dfrac{dP_v}{dT} = \dfrac{h_{fg}}{T \cdot v_{fg}}$.
- **Clausius-Clapeyron (integrated):** $\ln(P_{v2}/P_{v1}) = (h_{fg}/R)(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)$.
- **Assumptions for integrated form:** constant $h_{fg}$, ideal gas vapour, $v_f \ll v_g$.
- **Temperature conversion:** Always use **Kelvin** in Clausius-Clapeyron calculations.
- **Vapour pressure increases exponentially with temperature** — not linearly.
- **Vapour pressure is independent of external pressure** — it is an *intrinsic* property.
- **Real-world relevance:** Pump cavitation, pressure cooker, distillation, refrigeration, draft tubes of turbines.
- **At 20 °C, $P_v$ of water ≈ 2.34 kPa; at 100 °C, $P_v$ = 101.325 kPa (atmospheric).**
- **KTU marks split for derivation Q:** 3 marks derivation, 2 marks assumptions, 2 marks problem setup.
- **KTU marks split for numerical:** 1 mark unit conversion, 2 marks formula application, 2 marks calculation, 1 mark result, 1 mark comment on accuracy.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Strategy for KTU Exam:** Memorize the *integrated* Clausius-Clapeyron form, the *three assumptions*, and the *boiling criterion*. Most 14-mark derivation questions in Module 1 follow the exact pattern shown in Section 3. Practice one numerical with $h_{fg}$ and $R$ values for water — this is the most commonly tested combination.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bulk Modulus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT741 - FLUID MECHANICS & MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bulk Modulus

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1 — FLUID PROPERTIES
## Topic: Bulk Modulus of Elasticity

---

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bulk Modulus (K)** is defined as the ratio of the compressive (or hydrostatic) stress — that is, the increase in pressure applied uniformly on a fluid element — to the volumetric strain produced in that fluid element, with a negative sign included to make the modulus a **positive quantity**.

Mathematically, when a fluid element of original volume $V$ is subjected to an infinitesimal increase in pressure $dP$, it undergoes an infinitesimal decrease in volume $dV$. The **volumetric strain** is defined as $\dfrac{-dV}{V}$, and the Bulk Modulus is therefore expressed as:

$$K = -\dfrac{dP}{\left(\dfrac{dV}{V}\right)}$$

The SI unit of Bulk Modulus is **N/m²** or **Pascal (Pa)**, which is dimensionally identical to pressure. In engineering practice, it is often expressed in **MPa** or **GPa**. Typical reference values at standard atmospheric conditions are:
- Water at 20 °C: $\approx 2.2 \times 10^9$ Pa (**2.2 GPa**)
- Mercury: $\approx 2.8 \times 10^{10}$ Pa (**28 GPa**)
- Air (treated as fluid): $\approx 1.01 \times 10^5$ Pa

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the negative sign?** A positive increase in pressure $dP > 0$ always causes a volume decrease $dV < 0$, which makes the raw ratio $dP/(dV/V)$ negative. The bulk modulus of all real fluids is universally defined as a **positive number**, hence the minus sign.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Squeezing a Water Balloon" Picture

Imagine a completely sealed, air-free balloon fully filled with water. If you slowly press on it from **all sides equally** (this uniform all-around pressing is exactly what hydrostatic pressure does), the balloon will deform and its internal volume will shrink very, very slightly. If you press twice as hard, the volume reduction will (approximately) double. If the water inside were magically replaced with mercury, the same pressure increase would cause a much, much smaller reduction in volume — mercury is "stiffer" against uniform squeezing.

The **Bulk Modulus is simply a number that quantifies "how stiff"** a fluid is against such uniform all-around compression.

- A **high Bulk Modulus** $\Rightarrow$ the fluid is nearly **incompressible** (e.g., water, oil, mercury).
- A **low Bulk Modulus** $\Rightarrow$ the fluid is highly **compressible** (e.g., gases like air).

> [!NOTE]
> Although water is often treated as incompressible in introductory fluid statics problems, it actually **does compress** — by about 0.05 % per atmosphere of pressure. The Bulk Modulus captures exactly how much it compresses.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Reference Values

| Fluid | Bulk Modulus $K$ | Typical Application Context |
|---|---|---|
| Water (20 °C) | **2.2 × 10⁹ N/m²** | Hydraulic machines, water supply |
| Water (0 °C) | **2.04 × 10⁹ N/m²** | Cold reservoir systems |
| Sea Water | **2.3 × 10⁹ N/m²** | Marine hydraulics |
| Mercury | **2.8 × 10¹⁰ N/m²** | Manometer fluids, barometers |
| Oil (SAE 30) | **1.6 × 10⁹ N/m²** | Lubrication systems |
| Air (at 1 atm) | **1.01 × 10⁵ N/m²** | Pneumatic systems |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bulk Modulus is NOT a constant** for any real fluid. It increases with pressure and generally varies with temperature. KTU problems typically assume an average constant value unless temperature/pressure dependence is explicitly asked.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

---

### 2.1 Logical Breakdown of the Concept

The operational logic behind Bulk Modulus can be broken into four sequential steps that explain the **"why" and "how"**:

- **Step 1 — Apply Uniform Pressure Change:** A fluid element at rest is surrounded uniformly by a hydrostatic pressure $P$. When this surrounding pressure is increased by a small amount $dP$, every internal surface of the fluid element experiences an inward compressive push of magnitude $dP$.

- **Step 2 — Observe Volume Change:** Because the fluid is continuous, the inward push squeezes the molecules closer together, leading to a reduction $dV$ in the element's volume. The **volumetric strain** is the fractional change $\dfrac{-dV}{V}$.

- **Step 3 — Form the Ratio:** Dividing the applied stress ($dP$) by the resulting volumetric strain gives a measure of stiffness. The negative sign forces the result to be positive, because $dP$ and $dV$ always carry opposite signs.

- **Step 4 — Interpret Physically:** A larger value of $K$ means a **smaller fractional volume change** for a given pressure rise. Hence, fluids with high $K$ behave almost like rigid solids against compression — this is the fundamental reason why water hammer effects are so violent in long pipelines.

---

### 2.2 The Four Equivalent Forms of the Bulk Modulus Equation

Because volume and density are inversely related ($\rho \cdot V = \text{constant mass}$), the Bulk Modulus can be equivalently expressed in **four mathematically equivalent forms**. Mastering all four is essential for KTU problem-solving.

| S.No. | Formula | Most Useful When… |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $K = -\dfrac{dP}{\left(\dfrac{dV}{V}\right)}$ | Volume change $dV$ is directly given or measured |
| 2 | $K = \dfrac{dP}{\left(\dfrac{-dV}{V}\right)}$ | Emphasis on positive volumetric strain |
| 3 | $K = V \dfrac{dP}{-dV}$ | Working with finite volume $V$ and $dV$ values |
| 4 | $K = \rho \dfrac{dP}{d\rho}$ | Working with density change $d\rho$ instead of $dV$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The relationship between Forms 1 and 4 uses the fact that from $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$, taking the differential gives $d\rho = -\dfrac{m}{V^2}dV = -\dfrac{\rho}{V}dV$. Rearranging, $\dfrac{d\rho}{\rho} = -\dfrac{dV}{V}$. Substituting this into Form 1 yields Form 4.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Quantity | Expression | SI Unit | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bulk Modulus (Volume form) | $K = -V \dfrac{dP}{dV}$ | Pa or N/m² | Pressure needed per unit volumetric strain |
| Bulk Modulus (Density form) | $K = \rho \dfrac{dP}{d\rho}$ | Pa or N/m² | Equivalent form using density change |
| Compressibility ($\beta$) | $\beta = \dfrac{1}{K}$ | m²/N or Pa⁻¹ | Reciprocal of Bulk Modulus |
| Volumetric strain | $\epsilon_v = \dfrac{-dV}{V}$ | Dimensionless | Fractional reduction in volume |
| Relation to density | $\dfrac{d\rho}{\rho} = \dfrac{dP}{K}$ | Dimensionless | Useful in compressible flow |
| Adiabatic sound speed | $c = \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{\rho}}$ | m/s | Speed of pressure wave in fluid |
| Isothermal sound speed | $c_{iso} = \sqrt{\dfrac{P}{\rho}}$ | m/s | For isothermal gas processes |

> [!WARNING]
> **Absolute Value Rule in KTU:** Use $\vert x \vert$ style is broken in markdown. Always write magnitudes as $\left( \dfrac{1}{K} \right)$ or use words like "magnitude" rather than the vertical pipe $\vert \cdot \vert$ inside the same table row.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Bulk Modulus is not just an abstract textbook quantity — it appears directly in the design and analysis of:

- **Hydraulic systems and hydraulic presses:** Determines how much oil compresses under high pressure, affecting piston displacement accuracy.
- **Water-hammer analysis in pipelines:** The celerity (speed of pressure wave) $c = \sqrt{K/\rho}$ determines how fast shock waves travel after a valve closure — this dictates surge protection design.
- **Hydraulic accumulators:** The compressibility of gas pre-charge affects accumulator performance.
- **Acoustic engineering:** Sound speed in fluids (sonar, underwater acoustics) depends directly on $\sqrt{K/\rho}$.
- **Automotive brake systems:** Brake fluid bulk modulus affects "spongy pedal" feel.
- **Fuel injection systems in diesel engines:** High-pressure injection relies on near-incompressibility of fuel.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Pattern Tip:** Almost every time Bulk Modulus appears in a KTU numerical, it is tied to either (a) computing the volume/pressure change, (b) finding the speed of a pressure wave (water hammer), or (c) using compressibility $\beta$ to find percentage density change.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

---

### 3.1 Derivation: Equivalence of $K = -V \dfrac{dP}{dV}$ and $K = \rho \dfrac{dP}{d\rho}$

This is the single most-tested derivation in KTU for Module 1. We derive it rigorously below.

**Starting Assumption:** Consider a fixed mass $m$ of fluid. Its volume is $V$ and its density is $\rho$. By definition of density:

$$\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$$

**Step 1 — Differentiate both sides with respect to pressure $P$:**

Treating $m$ as constant (closed system, no mass added/removed), differentiate the relation $\rho V = m$:

$$\rho \cdot dV + V \cdot d\rho = 0$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $dV$ in terms of $d\rho$:**

$$dV = -\dfrac{V}{\rho}\, d\rho$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into Form 1 of Bulk Modulus:**

Start from the volume-based form:

$$K = -V \dfrac{dP}{dV}$$

Insert the expression for $dV$ obtained in Step 2:

$$K = -V \dfrac{dP}{\left( -\dfrac{V}{\rho}\, d\rho \right)}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the double negative and the $V$ terms:**

$$K = -V \cdot \left( -\dfrac{\rho}{V} \right) \dfrac{dP}{d\rho}$$

The two minus signs cancel, and the $V$ in numerator cancels with the $V$ in denominator:

$$K = \rho \dfrac{dP}{d\rho}$$

**Conclusion:** Both forms are mathematically identical, with the density form often being more convenient when compressibility charts give density changes directly.

$$\boxed{\,K = -V \dfrac{dP}{dV} \;=\; \rho \dfrac{dP}{d\rho}\,}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Expression for $\dfrac{d\rho}{\rho}$ in Terms of $dP$ and $K$

Starting from the density form derived above:

$$K = \rho \dfrac{dP}{d\rho}$$

Rearrange to isolate $\dfrac{d\rho}{\rho}$:

$$\dfrac{d\rho}{\rho} = \dfrac{dP}{K}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The fractional density increase per unit pressure rise equals $1/K$. This is precisely the **coefficient of compressibility** $\beta = 1/K$, expressed per unit pressure. KTU frequently asks students to compute percentage change in density, so this small result is heavily tested.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Speed of Sound (Pressure-Wave Celerity) in a Fluid

Consider a small fluid element in a long pipe. Apply Newton's second law to a pressure wave propagating at speed $c$. Using the bulk modulus definition, the pressure wave speed is:

$$c = \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{\rho}}$$

**Derivation Outline (standard KTU module coverage):**

Consider a control volume of fluid of cross-sectional area $A$ and length $dx$. A pressure wave of intensity $dP$ propagates with velocity $c$. The fluid element behind the wave has density $\rho + d\rho$ and is moving with velocity $c$. Applying **continuity** and **momentum** equations across the wave front:

- **Continuity:** $\rho c A = (\rho + d\rho)(c - dc_v) A$ where $dc_v$ is the small particle velocity behind the wave.
- **Momentum (impulse-momentum):** Pressure difference $dP \cdot A$ accelerates the mass behind.

Solving the two equations simultaneously and using $d\rho \ll \rho$ gives:

$$c^2 = \dfrac{dP}{d\rho/\rho} = \dfrac{K}{\rho}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\,c = \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{\rho}}\,}$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical — KTU Style (14-Mark Practice Model)

**Problem [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]:** An oil of density $850\ \text{kg/m}^3$ and bulk modulus $1.6 \times 10^9\ \text{N/m}^2$ is used in a hydraulic press. Find:
- **(a)** The volumetric strain when the pressure is increased from $1 \times 10^5\ \text{Pa}$ to $6 \times 10^5\ \text{Pa}$.
- **(b)** The percentage change in density.
- **(c)** The speed of the pressure wave (celerity) through the oil.

**Given Data:**
$\rho = 850\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $K = 1.6 \times 10^9\ \text{N/m}^2$, $P_1 = 1 \times 10^5\ \text{Pa}$, $P_2 = 6 \times 10^5\ \text{Pa}$.

**Part (a): Volumetric Strain**

Pressure change:

$$dP = P_2 - P_1 = 6 \times 10^5 - 1 \times 10^5 = 5 \times 10^5\ \text{Pa}$$

Using Bulk Modulus definition:

$$K = -V\dfrac{dP}{dV} \;\Rightarrow\; \dfrac{-dV}{V} = \dfrac{dP}{K}$$

Substitute numerical values:

$$\dfrac{-dV}{V} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^5}{1.6 \times 10^9}$$

$$\dfrac{-dV}{V} = 3.125 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Volumetric strain** $= 3.125 \times 10^{-4}$ (dimensionless, fractional reduction).

In percentage: $\approx 0.03125\,\%$.

**Part (b): Percentage Change in Density**

Since $\dfrac{d\rho}{\rho} = \dfrac{dP}{K}$ (from Section 3.2):

$$\dfrac{d\rho}{\rho} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^5}{1.6 \times 10^9} = 3.125 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Percentage change in density** $= 0.03125\,\%$ increase.

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that the fractional change in density is **exactly equal** to the magnitude of the volumetric strain — this is a direct consequence of mass conservation, and it is a frequently asked KTU conceptual question.

**Part (c): Celerity (Wave Speed)**

$$c = \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.6 \times 10^9}{850}}$$

$$c = \sqrt{1.882 \times 10^{6}} \approx 1372\ \text{m/s}$$

**Final Answer Summary:**
- Volumetric strain: $3.125 \times 10^{-4}$
- Density change: $+0.03125\,\%$
- Wave speed: $\approx 1372\ \text{m/s}$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

---

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow: How Bulk Modulus Connects Pressure to Volume Change

This block diagram traces the causal chain — from the applied pressure rise all the way to the final engineering parameters (wave speed, density change, etc.) — that the Bulk Modulus governs.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Applied Hydrostatic Pressure dP"] --> B["Compressive Stress on Fluid Element"]
    B --> C["Reduction in Molecular Spacing"]
    C --> D["Volume Decreases by dV negative"]
    D --> E["Volumetric Strain minus dV by V"]
    E --> F["Bulk Modulus K equals minus V dP by dV"]
    F --> G1["Equivalent Form rho dP by d rho"]
    F --> G2["Compressibility beta equals 1 by K"]
    F --> G3["Celerity c equals sqrt K by rho"]
    F --> G4["Density Change d rho by rho equals dP by K"]
    G1 --> H["Engineering Outputs: Water Hammer, Hydraulic Press, Pipe Surge"]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
```

---

### 4.2 Comparison Block Diagram: High-K vs Low-K Fluids

This diagram contrasts the qualitative behaviour of fluids with very high Bulk Modulus (nearly incompressible) versus those with low Bulk Modulus (highly compressible).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HighK["High K Fluids nearly Incompressible"]
        H1["Water 2.2 GPa"] --> H2["Mercury 28 GPa"]
        H2 --> H3["Oil 1.6 GPa"]
        H3 --> H4["Tiny dV for Large dP"]
        H4 --> H5["Fast Pressure Wave Celerity ~ 1000 m per s"]
    end
    subgraph LowK["Low K Fluids Highly Compressible"]
        L1["Air 0.0001 GPa"] --> L2["Steam near saturation"]
        L2 --> L3["Natural Gas"]
        L3 --> L4["Large dV for Small dP"]
        L4 --> L5["Slow Pressure Wave ~ 300 to 400 m per s"]
    end
    HighK --- Compare["Both linked by same Equation K equals minus V dP by dV"]
    LowK --- Compare
```

---

### 4.3 Engineering Application Architecture: Hydraulic Press + Pressure Wave

This block diagram maps the role of Bulk Modulus inside a typical hydraulic system, showing both the steady-state compression behaviour and the dynamic water-hammer phenomenon.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Input["Pump Generates Pressure Rise dP"] --> Pipe["Oil Enters Hydraulic Cylinder and Pipe Network"]
    Pipe --> Branch1["Steady State Branch: Bulk Modulus K determines Volume Reduction dV"]
    Pipe --> Branch2["Dynamic Branch: Sudden Valve Closure Creates Pressure Surge"]
    Branch1 --> Out1["Piston Displaces Slightly less than Ideal due to Oil Compression"]
    Branch2 --> Out2["Celerity c equals sqrt K by rho governs Surge Speed"]
    Out1 --> Design["Design Compromise: Higher K reduces Energy Loss but Increases Surge Severity"]
    Out2 --> Design
    Design --> Final["Surge Tank or Accumulator Selected to Absorb Water Hammer"]
```

---

### 4.4 Identification Quick-Reference Flowchart

For KTU exam-time recall, the following decision flowchart helps you choose the right form of the Bulk Modulus equation based on which quantity is given in the problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["What is given in the Problem?"] --> Q2["Volume Change dV"]
    Q1 --> Q3["Density Change d rho"]
    Q1 --> Q4["Wave Speed c"]
    Q2 --> A1["Use K equals minus V dP by dV"]
    Q3 --> A2["Use K equals rho dP by d rho"]
    Q4 --> A3["Use c equals sqrt K by rho therefore K equals c squared rho"]
    A1 --> Result["Solve for the Unknown Quantity"]
    A2 --> Result
    A3 --> Result
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1, 3 Marks]**
**Define Bulk Modulus of a fluid. State its SI unit.**

**Model Answer:**
Bulk Modulus of a fluid is defined as the ratio of compressive stress (infinitesimal pressure increase $dP$ applied uniformly to a fluid element) to the corresponding volumetric strain $(-dV/V)$. It is a measure of the fluid's resistance to uniform compression.

$$K = -\dfrac{dP}{\left( \dfrac{dV}{V} \right)}$$

**SI Unit:** N/m² or Pascal (Pa). It is often conveniently expressed in MPa or GPa for liquids.

**Examiner Note:** A complete answer must include the formula, the meaning of numerator/denominator, and the SI unit. Without the formula, full marks are not awarded.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1, 3 Marks]**
**State the relationship between Bulk Modulus $K$ and the speed of pressure wave $c$ in a fluid. What does this relationship imply for water-hammer analysis?**

**Model Answer:**
The speed at which a pressure disturbance (shock wave or sound wave) travels through a fluid is called the **celerity** $c$, and is given by:

$$c = \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{\rho}}$$

This means that fluids with higher Bulk Modulus transmit pressure disturbances faster. In water-hammer analysis (which occurs when a valve in a long pipeline is suddenly closed), a higher $c$ produces more severe pressure surges, because the wave returns to the valve before the system can bleed off energy. Hence, Bulk Modulus is a direct input to surge protection design.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

## Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Derive the relation $K = \rho \dfrac{dP}{d\rho}$ starting from the basic definition $K = -V \dfrac{dP}{dV}$.  **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A liquid of bulk modulus $2.0 \times 10^9\ \text{N/m}^2$ and density $1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$ is used in a hydraulic press. If the pressure rises by $5 \times 10^5\ \text{N/m}^2$, determine:
- (i) The volumetric strain produced.
- (ii) The percentage increase in density.
- (iii) The speed at which the pressure wave travels through the liquid.  **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

Assume a fixed mass $m$ of fluid, so that $m = \rho V = \text{constant}$.

Differentiating both sides with respect to pressure $P$:

$$\dfrac{d}{dP}(\rho V) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \rho \, dV + V \, d\rho = 0$$

Solving for $dV$:

$$dV = -\dfrac{V}{\rho}\, d\rho$$

Substituting into the basic definition of Bulk Modulus:

$$K = -V \dfrac{dP}{dV} = -V \dfrac{dP}{\left( -\dfrac{V}{\rho}\, d\rho \right)} = \rho \dfrac{dP}{d\rho}$$

$$\boxed{\,K = \rho \dfrac{dP}{d\rho}\,}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $K = 2.0 \times 10^9\ \text{N/m}^2$, $\rho = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $dP = 5 \times 10^5\ \text{N/m}^2$.

**(i) Volumetric Strain:**

$$\dfrac{-dV}{V} = \dfrac{dP}{K} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^5}{2.0 \times 10^9} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$$

**(ii) Percentage Increase in Density:**

$$\dfrac{d\rho}{\rho} = \dfrac{dP}{K} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$$

Percentage increase $= 0.025\,\%$.

**(iii) Pressure Wave Speed (Celerity):**

$$c = \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2.0 \times 10^9}{1000}} = \sqrt{2.0 \times 10^6} \approx 1414\ \text{m/s}$$

---

## Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Define Bulk Modulus and Compressibility. Explain the physical significance of both, and state the relationship between them.  **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** In a hydraulic system, oil of bulk modulus $1.6 \times 10^9\ \text{N/m}^2$ and density $870\ \text{kg/m}^3$ fills a 2 m long cylinder of cross-section $0.02\ \text{m}^2$. The piston at one end advances by $50\ \text{mm}$ against a load. Find:
- (i) The pressure rise in the oil.
- (ii) The volumetric strain produced.
- (iii) The reduction in volume of the oil in $\text{cm}^3$.  **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Definitions and Relationship:**
