- **Bulk Modulus ($K$):** The ratio of infinitesimal compressive stress ($dP$) to the resulting volumetric strain $(-dV/V)$ in a fluid.

$$K = -\dfrac{dP}{(dV/V)}$$

**Physical significance:** $K$ measures the fluid's resistance to uniform compression. A higher $K$ means the fluid behaves almost like a solid under pressure.

- **Compressibility ($\beta$):** The reciprocal of Bulk Modulus, representing the fractional reduction in volume per unit increase in pressure.

$$\beta = \dfrac{1}{K} = -\dfrac{(dV/V)}{dP}$$

**Physical significance:** $\beta$ measures "how compressible" a fluid is. A higher $\beta$ means the fluid compresses more easily.

- **Relationship:** $\beta = \dfrac{1}{K}$ — they are mathematical reciprocals of each other.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $K = 1.6 \times 10^9\ \text{N/m}^2$, $\rho = 870\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $L = 2\ \text{m}$, $A = 0.02\ \text{m}^2$, piston displacement $x = 50\ \text{mm} = 0.05\ \text{m}$.

Initial volume of oil:

$$V = A \times L = 0.02 \times 2 = 0.04\ \text{m}^3$$

**Step 1:** When the piston advances by $x$, it displaces a volume equal to $A \cdot x = 0.02 \times 0.05 = 0.001\ \text{m}^3 = 1000\ \text{cm}^3$. Since the oil is essentially incompressible but not perfectly so, this displaced volume is accommodated by a small **compression** of the oil plus a small forward advance of the piston face.

**Step 2:** Apply the definition of volumetric strain. The fractional volume decrease:

$$\dfrac{-dV}{V} = \dfrac{A \cdot x}{V} = \dfrac{0.001}{0.04} = 0.025$$

**Step 3:** Compute pressure rise using $K = -V \dfrac{dP}{dV}$, hence $dP = K \cdot \dfrac{-dV}{V}$:

$$dP = K \cdot \dfrac{-dV}{V} = 1.6 \times 10^9 \times 0.025 = 4.0 \times 10^7\ \text{N/m}^2 = 40\ \text{MPa}$$

**Final Answers (Part b):**
- (i) Pressure rise: $dP = 4.0 \times 10^7\ \text{Pa} = 40\ \text{MPa}$
- (ii) Volumetric strain: $0.025$ (i.e., $2.5\,\%$)
- (iii) Reduction in volume: $1000\ \text{cm}^3$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Bulk Modulus Problems — What KTU Examiners Penalize:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in the formula $K = -V\dfrac{dP}{dV}$. Many students drop it, leading to a negative Bulk Modulus (which is physically meaningless and earns 0 marks for the derivation step).
>
> 2. **Confusing Bulk Modulus with Young's Modulus.** Young's Modulus applies to **linear (uniaxial) stress**, while Bulk Modulus applies to **uniform volumetric (hydrostatic) stress**. Marks are deducted if the wrong modulus formula is used.
>
> 3. **Mixing up Compressibility $\beta$ and Bulk Modulus $K$.** They are reciprocals, not the same. A student who writes $K = \dfrac{1}{\beta}$ correctly gets credit; writing $K = \beta$ loses the entire 3-mark relationship step.
>
> 4. **Unit errors in the final answer.** $c = \sqrt{K/\rho}$ must come out in **m/s**. If $K$ is left in N/mm² without conversion, the numerical answer will be wrong by a factor of 1000.
>
> 5. **Skipping intermediate steps in the derivation.** KTU Board examiners award **partial marks** for each correctly written step in the derivation. A "jump-cut" answer that simply states $K = \rho dP/d\rho$ without showing the differential step loses 4 to 5 marks out of 7.
>
> 6. **Confusing $dP$ with absolute pressure $P$.** Bulk Modulus uses the **change** in pressure, not the absolute pressure. This error is especially common in water-hammer problems.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Bulk Modulus $K$ = compressive stress / volumetric strain, with negative sign to keep $K > 0$.
- **Formula:** $K = -V \dfrac{dP}{dV}$ is the master equation; equivalent forms are $K = \rho \dfrac{dP}{d\rho}$ and $K = \dfrac{dP}{(-\Delta V/V)}$.
- **SI Unit:** Pa (or N/m²); common practical units are MPa and GPa.
- **Reciprocal Relationship:** Compressibility $\beta = \dfrac{1}{K}$.
- **Density Form:** $\dfrac{d\rho}{\rho} = \dfrac{dP}{K}$ — this is a heavily tested micro-formula.
- **Celerity:** Pressure-wave (sound) speed $c = \sqrt{K/\rho}$ — direct input to water-hammer analysis.
- **Order of Magnitude:** Liquids $\sim 10^9$ to $10^{10}$ Pa; gases $\sim 10^5$ Pa — always quote in **GPa** for liquids to avoid huge numbers.
- **Physical Interpretation:** $K$ measures fluid "stiffness" against uniform compression.
- **Typical Values to Memorize:** Water ($2.2$ GPa), Mercury ($28$ GPa), Oil ($\sim 1.6$ GPa), Air ($0.0001$ GPa).
- **Engineering Use Cases:** Hydraulic presses, water-hammer/pipe surge, hydraulic accumulators, sonar, brake systems, fuel injection.
- **Always Watch For:** negative sign preservation, unit consistency, and using $dP$ (pressure change) rather than absolute $P$ in the formula.
- **KTU Most-Likely Questions:** definition + formula (3 marks), derivation of density form (7 marks), numerical on volumetric strain + density change (7 marks), celerity / water-hammer linkage (14 marks).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compressibility
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT741 - FLUID MECHANICS & MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Compressibility

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compressibility** is the property of a fluid by virtue of which its **volume (and hence density)** changes under the application of an external pressure. It quantifies the ability of a fluid to undergo volume reduction when subjected to compressive stress.

The reciprocal property is called **Elasticity**. Compressibility is mathematically characterized using the **Bulk Modulus of Elasticity (K)** and the **Coefficient of Compressibility (β)**.

### Key Formal Definitions

- **Bulk Modulus of Elasticity (K):** Defined as the ratio of the change in pressure to the **volumetric strain** (relative change in volume).

$$K = - \frac{dP}{\left(\dfrac{dV}{V}\right)}$$

- **Coefficient of Compressibility (β):** Defined as the reciprocal of the bulk modulus.

$$\beta = \frac{1}{K} = -\frac{dV/V}{dP}$$

- **Isothermal Bulk Modulus (K_T):** Bulk modulus evaluated when the process of compression is **isothermal** (temperature held constant). Valid for slow processes in gases.
- **Adiabatic Bulk Modulus (K_s):** Bulk modulus evaluated when the process is **adiabatic** (no heat transfer). Valid for rapid processes such as sound propagation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Sponge vs. the Steel Balloon**
> Imagine two sealed balloons, one filled with **water (incompressible)** and one with **air (compressible)**. When you press them with your hand:
> - The **air balloon** shrinks easily — air volume reduces, density increases → **high compressibility, low bulk modulus**.
> - The **water balloon** barely changes shape — water resists compression → **low compressibility, high bulk modulus (≈ 2.1 GPa)**.
> - A **steel block** is nearly impossible to compress — its bulk modulus is enormous (~160 GPa).

This intuitive picture is the basis for the **incompressible vs compressible flow assumption**:
- **Liquids (water, oil)** → Compressibility is negligible; treated as incompressible.
- **Gases (air, steam)** → Compressibility is significant, especially at high velocities.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Values to Memorize for KTU Exams:**
> - Bulk modulus of water: **K ≈ 2.1 × 10⁹ N/m² (≈ 2.1 GPa)**
> - Bulk modulus of air (at STP, isothermal): **K_T = P_atm = 101.325 kPa**
> - Bulk modulus of air (adiabatic): **K_s = γP = 1.4 × 101.325 kPa ≈ 141.86 kPa**
> - Ratio of specific heats for air: **γ = 1.4**
> - Speed of sound in air at 15°C: **c ≈ 340 m/s**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Framework

### Step 1: The Volumetric Strain Concept
When an infinitesimal pressure increment **dP** is applied to a fluid element of volume **V**, the volume reduces by **dV**. The **volumetric strain** is defined as:

$$\text{Volumetric Strain} = \frac{dV}{V}$$

### Step 2: Defining Bulk Modulus (K)
By definition (Newton's empirical law for fluids):

$$K = -\frac{dP}{\left(\dfrac{dV}{V}\right)}$$

The **negative sign** ensures **K is positive** because volume decreases (dV < 0) when pressure increases (dP > 0).

### Step 3: Expressing in Terms of Density (ρ)
Using conservation of mass for a fixed mass **m = ρV**:

$$\frac{dV}{V} = -\frac{d\rho}{\rho}$$

Substituting back:

$$\boxed{K = \rho \, \frac{dP}{d\rho}}$$

### Step 4: Two Thermodynamic Paths

| Path Type | Condition | Governing Relation | Resulting K |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Isothermal** | T = constant | $P/\rho = \text{const}$ (Boyle's Law) | $K_T = P$ |
| **Adiabatic** | s = constant (isentropic) | $P/\rho^\gamma = \text{const}$ | $K_s = \gamma P$ |

The ratio:

$$\boxed{K_s = \gamma \, K_T}$$

### Step 5: Mach Number (M) — The Master Parameter
The **Mach number** is the dimensionless ratio of flow velocity to the local speed of sound:

$$M = \frac{V}{c} \quad \text{where} \quad c = \sqrt{\frac{K}{\rho}}$$

For an ideal gas:

$$c = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$$

### Step 6: Flow Classification by Mach Number

| Regime | Mach Range | Assumption |
|---|---|---|
| Incompressible | M < 0.3 | Density constant |
| Subsonic | 0.3 < M < 0.8 | Compressibility starts mattering |
| Transonic | 0.8 < M < 1.2 | Mixed subsonic/supersonic |
| Supersonic | M > 1.2 | Shock waves possible |
| Hypersonic | M > 5 | Strong shock, high heating |

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **All Critical Equations for Compressibility — Memorize This Table.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units (SI) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bulk Modulus | $K = -V\dfrac{dP}{dV} = \rho\dfrac{dP}{d\rho}$ | N/m² or Pa | Always positive |
| 2 | Coefficient of Compressibility | $\beta = \dfrac{1}{K}$ | m²/N | Reciprocal of K |
| 3 | Isothermal Bulk Modulus (Gas) | $K_T = P$ | Pa | Slow process |
| 4 | Adiabatic Bulk Modulus (Gas) | $K_s = \gamma P$ | Pa | Fast process / sound |
| 5 | Ratio K_s to K_T | $K_s = \gamma K_T$ | dimensionless | Since $\gamma = C_p/C_v$ |
| 6 | Speed of Sound (general) | $c = \sqrt{K/\rho}$ | m/s | In any fluid |
| 7 | Speed of Sound (ideal gas) | $c = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ | m/s | Function of T only |
| 8 | Mach Number | $M = V/c$ | dimensionless | Key compressibility index |
| 9 | Critical Velocity | $V_c = c = \sqrt{K/\rho}$ | m/s | Velocity at M = 1 |
| 10 | Density Ratio (isentropic) | $\rho/\rho_0 = (1 + \dfrac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2)^{-1/(\gamma-1)}$ | dimensionless | From isentropic flow |
| 11 | Pressure Ratio (isentropic) | $P/P_0 = (1 + \dfrac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2)^{-\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$ | dimensionless | Pitot-static relation |
| 12 | Temperature Ratio (isentropic) | $T/T_0 = (1 + \dfrac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2)^{-1}$ | dimensionless | Stagnation temperature |

> [!WARNING]
> **Note on Units:** In KTU numericals, use **kN/m² (kPa)** consistently when working with atmospheric pressures. Do **not** mix SI prefixes mid-calculation.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Designing airfoils, rockets, and jet engines requires Mach number analysis.
- **Hydraulic Systems:** Water hammer (transient surge) in pipelines depends on the bulk modulus of the liquid (water → K = 2.1 GPa).
- **Hydraulic Press / Accumulators:** Use compressibility of fluid (often a trapped gas bubble) to store energy.
- **Acoustics & SONAR:** Underwater sound speed depends on $c = \sqrt{K/\rho}$ for seawater.
- **Pumps & Turbines:** Cavitation analysis requires compressibility of the liquid phase.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Bulk Modulus in Terms of Density

**Given:** A fluid element of volume $V$, density $\rho$, mass $m$, subjected to pressure change $dP$.

**Step 1** — Write the mass conservation relation:

$$m = \rho V = \text{constant}$$

Differentiating both sides with respect to pressure:

$$0 = V \, d\rho + \rho \, dV$$

**Step 2** — Solve for $dV$:

$$dV = -\frac{V}{\rho} \, d\rho$$

**Step 3** — Substitute into the definition $K = -dP/(dV/V)$:

$$K = - \frac{dP}{\left(\dfrac{-\frac{V}{\rho}d\rho}{V}\right)} = - \frac{dP}{-\frac{1}{\rho}d\rho}$$

**Step 4** — Simplify to obtain the density form:

$$\boxed{K = \rho \, \frac{dP}{d\rho}}$$

This form is most useful when integrating along a thermodynamic path.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Isothermal Bulk Modulus for an Ideal Gas

**Given:** For an ideal gas at constant temperature (Boyle's Law):

$$P = \rho R T \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{P}{\rho} = RT = \text{constant}$$

**Step 1** — Differentiate Boyle's Law ($P = C\rho$, where $C = RT$):

$$dP = C \, d\rho$$

**Step 2** — Hence the ratio:

$$\frac{dP}{d\rho} = C = RT = \frac{P}{\rho}$$

**Step 3** — Apply the density form of K:

$$K_T = \rho \cdot \frac{P}{\rho} = P$$

$$\boxed{K_T = P}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Adiabatic Bulk Modulus for an Ideal Gas

**Given:** For an isentropic (adiabatic, reversible) process in an ideal gas:

$$P \rho^{-\gamma} = \text{constant}$$

**Step 1** — Differentiate implicitly:

$$\rho^{-\gamma} dP + P \cdot (-\gamma) \rho^{-\gamma - 1} d\rho = 0$$

**Step 2** — Divide throughout by $\rho^{-\gamma}$:

$$dP - \gamma \frac{P}{\rho} d\rho = 0$$

**Step 3** — Solve for $dP/d\rho$:

$$\frac{dP}{d\rho} = \gamma \frac{P}{\rho}$$

**Step 4** — Substitute into $K = \rho \, dP/d\rho$:

$$\boxed{K_s = \gamma P}$$

This explains why sound travels faster than expected from isothermal data — sound is a rapid (adiabatic) disturbance.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Speed of Sound (Newton–Laplace Equation)

**Given:** Consider a small pressure disturbance (sound wave) traveling through a fluid at the local speed of sound $c$. Treat the propagation as an **adiabatic** process for gases and **isentropic** for liquids.

**Step 1** — Start with Newton's second law applied to a fluid element disturbed by a sound wave of small amplitude. The wave satisfies the 1D wave equation, leading to:

$$c^2 = \frac{dP}{d\rho} \quad \text{(at constant entropy)}$$

**Step 2** — By the adiabatic relation $dP/d\rho = \gamma P/\rho$:

$$c^2 = \frac{\gamma P}{\rho}$$

**Step 3** — Using the ideal gas law $P = \rho R T$:

$$\boxed{c = \sqrt{\gamma R T}}$$

**Step 4** — For liquids, $c = \sqrt{K/\rho}$ where $K$ is the (isentropic) bulk modulus of the liquid.

For **water** at 20°C: $\rho = 998 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $K \approx 2.2 \text{ GPa}$ → $c \approx 1485 \text{ m/s}$.

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Relation Between K_s and K_T (General Fluids)

**Given:** Any fluid has both isothermal and adiabatic bulk moduli. From thermodynamics:

$$\frac{K_T}{K_s} = \frac{C_p - C_v}{C_p} = \frac{1}{\gamma} \cdot \gamma \cdot \frac{1}{\gamma} \cdot \gamma \; \text{(refining)}$$

A more rigorous derivation uses the general thermodynamic identity:

$$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial \rho}\right)_T = \frac{c^2}{\gamma}$$

Combining with $K_s = \rho c^2$:

$$K_T = \rho \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial \rho}\right)_T = \rho \cdot \frac{c^2}{\gamma} = \frac{K_s}{\gamma}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{K_s = \gamma \, K_T}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** Since $\gamma > 1$, adiabatic compression is "stiffer" than isothermal compression — heat cannot escape, so the fluid resists compression more strongly. This is why sound (adiabatic) feels faster than slow compression (isothermal) in gas dynamics.

## 3.6 Derivation 6: Critical Velocity and Mach Number Identity

**Given:** Critical (sonic) velocity is defined as the flow velocity at which Mach number equals 1:

$$V_c = c = \sqrt{\frac{K_s}{\rho}}$$

**Step 1** — Define Mach number as:

$$M = \frac{V}{V_c} = \frac{V}{\sqrt{K_s/\rho}}$$

**Step 2** — Solve for V in terms of M:

$$V = M \sqrt{\frac{K_s}{\rho}}$$

This is the **fundamental relation** used throughout compressible flow theory (Rayleigh Pitot formula, area-velocity relation, stagnation quantities, etc.).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Architecture — Compressibility Master Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["FLUID PROPERTIES"] --> nodeB["Compressibility Study"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Volume and Density Change Under Pressure"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["Quantified by Bulk Modulus K"]
    nodeB --> nodeE["Quantified by Coefficient beta"]

    nodeC --> nodeF["Liquids: Low Compressibility"]
    nodeC --> nodeG["Gases: High Compressibility"]

    nodeD --> nodeH["Isothermal KT equals P"]
    nodeD --> nodeI["Adiabatic KS equals gamma P"]

    nodeE --> nodeJ["beta equals 1 over K"]

    nodeI --> nodeK["Speed of Sound c equals sqrt K over rho"]
    nodeI --> nodeL["Mach Number M equals V over c"]

    nodeL --> nodeM["M less than 0.3: Incompressible Flow"]
    nodeL --> nodeN["M between 0.3 and 1: Subsonic Compressible"]
    nodeL --> nodeO["M greater than 1: Supersonic Flow"]

    nodeK --> nodeP["Applications: Aerodynamics, Water Hammer, SONAR, Turbomachinery"]
```

## 4.2 Thermodynamic Path Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
        A1 --> A2
    end

    A2 --> B1{"Process Type?"}
    B1 -->|Slow / Heat Exchange| C1["ISOTHERMAL PATH"]
    B1 -->|Fast / No Heat Transfer| C2["ADIABATIC PATH"]

    C1 --> D1["KT equals P"]
    C1 --> E1["Used for Slow Liquid Loading"]

    C2 --> D2["KS equals gamma P"]
    C2 --> E2["Used for Sound, Shocks, Explosions"]

    D1 --> F1["Final K depends on gamma factor"]
    D2 --> F1
```

## 4.3 Bulk Modulus — Energy Storage Analogy Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Bulk Modulus K"] --> node2["High K value"]
    node1 --> node3["Low K value"]

    node2 --> node4["STEEL: 160 GPa"]
    node2 --> node5["WATER: 2.1 GPa"]
    node2 --> node6["HYDRAULIC OIL: 1.6 GPa"]

    node3 --> node7["AIR at 1 atm: 0.10 MPa"]
    node3 --> node8["AIR at 10 atm: 1.0 MPa"]
    node3 --> node9["STEAM at high T: very low K"]

    node4 --> n10["Stiff, low compressibility"]
    node5 --> n10
    node6 --> n10
    node7 --> n11["Soft, high compressibility"]
    node8 --> n11
    node9 --> n11
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading these diagrams in exam:** When asked to draw a schematic of "how compressibility affects flow classification," reproduce the Mach number tree (4.1). For process-type comparison, reproduce the **thermodynamic path block (4.2)**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define Bulk Modulus of Elasticity and Coefficient of Compressibility. State the relationship between them.**

**Model Answer:**
> The **Bulk Modulus of Elasticity (K)** of a fluid is defined as the ratio of the change in pressure to the volumetric strain produced. Mathematically:
>
> $$K = -\frac{dP}{\left(\dfrac{dV}{V}\right)} = \rho \, \frac{dP}{d\rho}$$
>
> Units: N/m² or Pa. The negative sign ensures K is positive.
>
> The **Coefficient of Compressibility (β)** is defined as the reciprocal of the bulk modulus:
>
> $$\beta = \frac{1}{K} = -\frac{dV/V}{dP}$$
>
> Units: m²/N. The relationship between them is simply $\beta = 1/K$. Larger β implies more compressible fluid; larger K implies stiffer fluid.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between Isothermal and Adiabatic Compressibility. Give one example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

> | Aspect | Isothermal Compressibility | Adiabatic Compressibility |
> |---|---|---|
> | Process | Temperature constant (T = const) | No heat transfer (dq = 0) |
> | Bulk modulus for ideal gas | $K_T = P$ | $K_s = \gamma P$ |
> | Time scale | Slow processes | Rapid processes |
> | Example | Slow charging of an air tank | Sound wave propagation in air |
>
> Ratio: $K_s = \gamma K_T$. Since $\gamma > 1$, adiabatic compression produces stiffer fluid response.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **bulk modulus of an ideal gas** under both isothermal and adiabatic conditions. Show that $K_s = \gamma K_T$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A submarine is moving at **36 km/h** through seawater at 15°C. The bulk modulus of seawater is **2.3 × 10⁹ N/m²** and the density is **1030 kg/m³**. Determine: **(i)** Speed of sound in seawater, **(ii)** Mach number of the submarine, **(iii)** Whether the flow can be treated as incompressible. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Derivation [7 Marks]

**Step 1:** Starting from $K = -V \dfrac{dP}{dV}$. Using mass conservation $m = \rho V$, differentiate to get $dV/V = -d\rho/\rho$, hence:

$$K = \rho \, \frac{dP}{d\rho}$$

**Step 2 — Isothermal path** ($P = \rho RT$, with $RT$ constant): Differentiate:

$$dP = RT \, d\rho \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{dP}{d\rho} = RT = \frac{P}{\rho}$$

Therefore:

$$K_T = \rho \cdot \frac{P}{\rho} \Rightarrow \boxed{K_T = P}$$

**Step 3 — Adiabatic path** ($P\rho^{-\gamma} = \text{const}$): Differentiate implicitly:

$$\rho^{-\gamma} dP - \gamma P \rho^{-\gamma-1} d\rho = 0$$

$$\frac{dP}{d\rho} = \gamma \frac{P}{\rho}$$

Therefore:

$$K_s = \rho \cdot \gamma \frac{P}{\rho} \Rightarrow \boxed{K_s = \gamma P}$$

**Step 4 — Ratio:**

$$\frac{K_s}{K_T} = \frac{\gamma P}{P} = \gamma \Rightarrow \boxed{K_s = \gamma K_T}$$

Hence proved.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- Submarine velocity: $V = 36 \text{ km/h} = 36 \times \dfrac{1000}{3600} = 10 \text{ m/s}$
- Bulk modulus of seawater: $K = 2.3 \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2$
- Density of seawater: $\rho = 1030 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**(i) Speed of sound in seawater:**

$$c = \sqrt{\frac{K}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{2.3 \times 10^9}{1030}}$$

$$c = \sqrt{2.233 \times 10^6} = 1494.3 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{c \approx 1494 \text{ m/s}}$$

**(ii) Mach number of submarine:**

$$M = \frac{V}{c} = \frac{10}{1494.3} = 0.00669$$

$$\boxed{M \approx 0.0067}$$

**(iii) Incompressibility check:**

Since $M = 0.0067 < 0.3$, the flow around the submarine can safely be treated as **incompressible**. Density changes will be less than 1%, negligible for engineering analysis.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — Alternative Choice`

**(a)** Define **Mach number** and explain the **flow regimes** classified by it. Derive the expression for the **speed of sound** in an ideal gas. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Air at 30°C flows over an aircraft wing at a velocity of **250 m/s**. Take $\gamma = 1.4$ and $R = 287 \text{ J/kg·K}$. Calculate: **(i)** Speed of sound, **(ii)** Mach number, **(iii)** Stagnation temperature assuming adiabatic flow, **(iv)** Stagnation pressure ratio. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Theory [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Definition of Mach Number:**
> Mach number is the dimensionless ratio of the local flow velocity to the local speed of sound:
> $$M = \frac{V}{c}$$

**Step 2 — Flow Regimes:**

| Regime | Mach Range | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| Incompressible | M < 0.3 | Density constant |
| Subsonic | 0.3 ≤ M < 0.8 | Compressibility effects begin |
| Transonic | 0.8 ≤ M < 1.2 | Mixed sub/sonic, shock onset |
| Supersonic | 1.2 ≤ M ≤ 5 | Shock waves present |
| Hypersonic | M > 5 | Strong shocks, dissociation |

**Step 3 — Speed of Sound Derivation:**
From Newton's formula applied to a sound wave (assumed adiabatic):

$$c^2 = \left(\frac{dP}{d\rho}\right)_s = \frac{\gamma P}{\rho}$$

Using $P = \rho R T$:

$$c^2 = \frac{\gamma \rho R T}{\rho} = \gamma R T$$

$$\boxed{c = \sqrt{\gamma R T}}$$

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks]

**Given:** $T = 30°C = 303 \text{ K}$, $V = 250 \text{ m/s}$, $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 287 \text{ J/kg·K}$.

**(i) Speed of sound:**

$$c = \sqrt{\gamma R T} = \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 303}$$

$$c = \sqrt{121{,}793.4} = 348.99 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{c \approx 349 \text{ m/s}}$$

**(ii) Mach number:**

$$M = \frac{V}{c} = \frac{250}{348.99} = 0.716$$

$$\boxed{M \approx 0.716 \text{ (Subsonic Compressible)}}$$

**(iii) Stagnation temperature** (from isentropic energy equation $T_0 = T + V^2/(2C_p)$, with $C_p = \gamma R/(\gamma-1)$):

$$T_0 = T\left(1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2} M^2\right)$$

$$T_0 = 303 \times \left(1 + \frac{0.4}{2} \times 0.716^2\right)$$

$$T_0 = 303 \times (1 + 0.2 \times 0.5127) = 303 \times 1.1025$$

$$\boxed{T_0 \approx 334.06 \text{ K}}$$

**(iv) Stagnation pressure ratio:**

$$\frac{P_0}{P} = \left(1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2} M^2\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma - 1)}$$

$$\frac{P_0}{P} = (1.1025)^{1.4/0.4} = (1.1025)^{3.5}$$

$$P_0/P = 1.4143$$

$$\boxed{P_0 \approx 1.414 \, P}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Examiners Penalize (Avoid These!):**
> 1. **Sign Error in Bulk Modulus:** Forgetting the negative sign in $K = -dP/(dV/V)$. Always show the negative sign explicitly to get full marks.
> 2. **Mixing Up $K_T$ and $K_s$:** Using isothermal bulk modulus for sound/speed-of-sound problems. Sound is **adiabatic**, so use $K_s = \gamma P$.
> 3. **Unit Conversion Slip:** Failing to convert km/h to m/s or °C to Kelvin. **Always do unit conversion FIRST** in your solution.
> 4. **Mach Number Misjudgment:** Treating M = 0.35 as "incompressible" — the threshold is **M < 0.3**. Just above 0.3, compressibility already matters.
> 5. **Stagnation vs Static Confusion:** $T_0$ (stagnation) is always greater than or equal to $T$ (static). Never the other way round.
> 6. **Forgetting the Coefficient:** $\beta = 1/K$ — students sometimes write $\beta = K$ under exam pressure.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Final One-Page Summary — Read This Before the Exam!**

- **Compressibility** = property of fluid to change volume under pressure; quantified by **Bulk Modulus K** and **Coefficient of Compressibility β = 1/K**.
- Two key forms of bulk modulus:
  - Density form: $K = \rho \, dP/d\rho$
  - Volume form: $K = -V \, dP/dV$
- **Isothermal (KT = P)** and **Adiabatic (Ks = γP)** — remember the ratio **Ks = γ·KT**.
- **Speed of sound (general):** $c = \sqrt{K/\rho}$ | **(ideal gas):** $c = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$.
- **Mach number:** $M = V/c$. Threshold for compressibility = **M = 0.3**.
- **Stagnation relations (isentropic):**
  - $T_0/T = 1 + (\gamma-1)/2 \cdot M^2$
  - $P_0/P = [1 + (\gamma-1)/2 \cdot M^2]^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$
  - $\rho_0/\rho = [1 + (\gamma-1)/2 \cdot M^2]^{1/(\gamma-1)}$
- **Water has K ≈ 2.1 GPa** → speed of sound ≈ 1485 m/s.
- **Air at STP:** KT = 101.325 kPa; Ks = 1.4 × 101.325 ≈ 141.86 kPa; c ≈ 340 m/s.
- **Engineering uses:** water hammer, hydraulic accumulators, SONAR, jet engine design, aerodynamics.
- **In exam:** Always convert units first, state assumptions (ideal gas or not), and clearly mark Mach regimes.
- **Most tested 14-mark combination:** Bulk modulus derivation + numerical involving Mach number, stagnation quantities, and incompressibility check.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Velocity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT741 - FLUID MECHANICS & MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Velocity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Fluid Mechanics**, **velocity** is defined as the *time rate of change of position* of a fluid particle with respect to a fixed reference frame. It is a **vector quantity** having both magnitude and direction, and is typically denoted by the symbol $\vec{V}$ or simply $V$.

Mathematically, the velocity vector at any point in a fluid flow field is given by:

$$\vec{V} = \frac{d\vec{s}}{dt}$$

where:
- $\vec{V}$ = Velocity vector ($\text{m/s}$)
- $\vec{s}$ = Displacement vector ($\text{m}$)
- $t$ = Time ($\text{s}$)

In a three-dimensional Cartesian coordinate system, the velocity vector can be resolved into three orthogonal components:

$$\vec{V} = u\,\hat{i} + v\,\hat{j} + w\,\hat{k}$$

where $u$, $v$, and $w$ are the velocity components along the $x$, $y$, and $z$ axes respectively.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Velocity is the **primary kinematic variable** in fluid mechanics. Every subsequent module—continuity, Bernoulli's equation, momentum equation, dimensional analysis—depends fundamentally on the correct conceptualization of velocity.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine standing on the bank of a river. The water surface moves past you. If you drop a leaf at point A, it travels to point B in some time. The leaf's "velocity" is the speed and direction of its motion. But here is the catch — every droplet of water in the river moves with its *own* velocity! The water at the center moves fast, while the water near the banks moves slowly due to friction with the shore.

This means that in fluid mechanics, **velocity is a field quantity** — it has a different value at *every point* in space and at *every instant* in time. We do not speak of "the velocity of the fluid"; we speak of "the velocity **at a point** in the fluid at a given time."

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Takeaway:** Velocity in fluids is not a single number — it is a *function of position and time*, much like temperature varies from place to place on a weather map.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard Unit (SI):** $\text{m/s}$ (metres per second)
- **Alternative Units:** $\text{ft/s}$, $\text{cm/s}$, $\text{knots}$ (1 knot = 0.5144 m/s)
- **Common Engineering Range:** $10^{-6}$ m/s (groundwater flow) to $10^{3}$ m/s (supersonic jets)
- **Dimensional Formula:** $[L\,T^{-1}]$

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** When numerical values are given in $\text{cm/s}$ or $\text{ft/s}$, always convert to $\text{m/s}$ before substituting into standard formulas. This is a common KTU valuation pitfall.

---

## 1.4 Types of Velocity in Fluid Flow

The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies velocity in fluid mechanics under several important categories:

### 1.4.1 Local Velocity (Point Velocity)
The velocity of a fluid particle at a *specific point* in the flow field at a *specific instant*. Denoted as $V_{(x,y,z,t)}$.

### 1.4.2 Average (Mean) Velocity
The hypothetical uniform velocity with which the fluid would have to flow through the cross-section to deliver the same discharge $Q$. Defined as:

$$V_{\text{avg}} = \frac{Q}{A}$$

where $Q$ is the volumetric discharge and $A$ is the cross-sectional area.

### 1.4.3 Uniform vs. Non-Uniform Velocity

| Type | Definition | Example |
|------|------------|---------|
| **Uniform Velocity** | Magnitude and direction remain *constant* at every point in the flow at a given instant | Flow through a long straight pipe of constant diameter |
| **Non-Uniform Velocity** | Velocity changes from point to point in the flow field | Flow through a contracting nozzle, flow around a bend |

### 1.4.4 Classification Based on Time Dependence

| Type | Definition | Mathematical Condition |
|------|------------|------------------------|
| **Steady Velocity** | Velocity at any point *does not change* with time | $\dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} = 0$ |
| **Unsteady Velocity** | Velocity at a point *changes* with time | $\dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} \neq 0$ |

### 1.4.5 Classification Based on Dimensions

- **One-Dimensional (1-D) Flow:** Velocity varies in only one coordinate direction (e.g., flow in a long pipe where $V = V(x)$ only).
- **Two-Dimensional (2-D) Flow:** Velocity varies in two coordinate directions ($V = V(x, y)$).
- **Three-Dimensional (3-D) Flow:** Velocity varies in all three directions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:** *Uniform flow* and *steady flow* are **NOT** the same thing. Uniform flow refers to *spatial* variation (no change with position), while steady flow refers to *temporal* variation (no change with time).

---

## 1.5 The Velocity Field Concept

A **velocity field** is a mathematical description of how velocity is distributed throughout a region of space occupied by the fluid:

$$\vec{V}(x, y, z, t) = u(x, y, z, t)\,\hat{i} + v(x, y, z, t)\,\hat{j} + w(x, y, z, t)\,\hat{k}$$

This field-based representation is the foundation of the **Eulerian approach** to fluid mechanics, as opposed to the **Lagrangian approach** which tracks individual fluid particles.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two approaches?** In Lagrangian, we follow a *particle* (like tracking a specific car on a highway). In Eulerian, we fix our position and watch *who passes by* (like a traffic camera on a pole). Most engineering problems use the Eulerian approach because it leads to easier mathematical formulation.

---

## 1.6 Velocity Gradient and Its Significance

The **velocity gradient** is the spatial rate of change of velocity:

$$\frac{du}{dy}, \quad \frac{dv}{dx}, \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}, \quad \text{etc.}$$

The velocity gradient is critically important because it directly relates to:
1. **Shear stress** in the fluid (Newton's law of viscosity: $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$)
2. **Vorticity** ($\omega = \nabla \times \vec{V}$)
3. **Boundary layer** behavior near solid surfaces

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Analysis of Velocity Types and Their Physical Meaning

### 2.1.1 Why Average Velocity Matters in Engineering

In real flow problems, the velocity distribution across a cross-section is rarely uniform. Due to the **no-slip condition** at solid boundaries, fluid in contact with the pipe wall has zero velocity, while fluid at the center moves fastest. This creates a **velocity profile** (typically parabolic for laminar flow in a circular pipe).

For engineering calculations, it is impractical to integrate the local velocity over the entire cross-section for every problem. Therefore, we use **average velocity**, defined as the equivalent uniform velocity that would produce the same volumetric flow rate:

$$V_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{A} \int_A V\,dA$$

The ratio of average velocity to maximum velocity (centerline velocity) is a known constant for standard flow cases:
- **Laminar flow in circular pipe:** $V_{\text{avg}} = 0.5 \cdot V_{\text{max}}$
- **Turbulent flow in circular pipe:** $V_{\text{avg}} \approx 0.8$ to $0.85 \cdot V_{\text{max}}$

### 2.1.2 Steady vs. Unsteady Flow — Operational Significance

The condition for steady flow is:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial v}{\partial t} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial w}{\partial t} = 0$$

**Why it matters:**
- **Steady flow assumption** vastly simplifies the governing equations (Navier-Stokes equations reduce to elliptic form).
- Most pipeline, pump, and turbine design problems assume steady flow.
- **Unsteady flow** must be considered for water hammer, surge tanks, pulsating flows, and pump startups/shutdowns.

### 2.1.3 Uniform vs. Non-Uniform Flow — Operational Significance

The condition for uniform flow is that the velocity vector is the same at every point in the flow field at a given instant:

$$\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial s} = 0$$

where $s$ is the spatial coordinate along the flow direction.

**Key engineering implications:**
- A **converging nozzle** produces *accelerating* non-uniform flow.
- A **diverging diffuser** produces *decelerating* non-uniform flow.
- **Rapidly varying flow (RVF)** is a special case of non-uniform flow where the rate of change of velocity with position is very large (e.g., hydraulic jump, flow over a weir).

### 2.1.4 Rotational vs. Irrotational Flow

A flow is **irrotational** if the fluid element at any point does not rotate about its own axis — even though the fluid may be moving in a curved path overall.

The condition for irrotationality is that the **vorticity vector** is zero:

$$\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V} = 0$$

In 2-D flow, this becomes:

$$\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** The concept of irrotational flow is essential for deriving the **velocity potential function** and **stream function**, which appear extensively in later modules (potential flow theory, flow nets, groundwater flow).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $V = \dfrac{ds}{dt}$ | Instantaneous velocity from displacement | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 2 | $V_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | Average velocity from discharge | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 3 | $Q = A \cdot V$ | Volumetric discharge | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 4 | $\rho Q = \rho A V$ | Mass flow rate | $\text{kg/s}$ |
| 5 | $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ | Continuity equation (incompressible, 1-D) | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 6 | $\vec{V} = u\hat{i} + v\hat{j} + w\hat{k}$ | Velocity vector in 3-D | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 7 | $\dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} = 0$ | Steady flow condition | - |
| 8 | $\dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial s} = 0$ | Uniform flow condition | - |
| 9 | $\omega_z = \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y}$ | Vorticity (z-component, 2-D) | $\text{s}^{-1}$ |
| 10 | $u = \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}, \quad v = \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}$ | Velocity from potential function | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 11 | $u = \dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}, \quad v = -\dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$ | Velocity from stream function | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 12 | $\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = \dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}$ | Cauchy-Riemann equations (2-D, irrotational) | - |
| 13 | $\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = -\dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$ | Cauchy-Riemann equations (2-D, irrotational) | - |
| 14 | $V_{\text{avg}} = 0.5\,V_{\text{max}}$ | Laminar flow, circular pipe | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 15 | $V_{\text{avg}} \approx 0.82\,V_{\text{max}}$ | Turbulent flow, circular pipe | $\text{m/s}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Formatting Note:** In the formulas above, the absolute-value or modulus bars are rendered using the LaTeX vertical bar $\vert$ or contextual equation syntax, **never** the markdown pipe $\vert$ character, to preserve table rendering integrity in the KTU study material format.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility of Velocity Concepts

| Engineering Field | Application of Velocity Concepts |
|-------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Pipeline Design** | Average velocity determines pumping power and pipe diameter selection |
| **Hydraulic Turbines** | Velocity triangles at inlet/outlet determine energy transfer efficiency |
| **Wind Energy** | Wind velocity distribution (Weibull) predicts turbine power output |
| **Aerodynamics** | Velocity potential and stream functions predict pressure distribution on airfoils |
| **Open Channel Flow** | Chezy's and Manning's equations use average velocity for channel design |
| **Blood Flow Modeling** | Velocity profiles in arteries diagnose stenosis and cardiovascular diseases |
| **Chemical Engineering** | Residence time calculations in reactors use average velocity in pipes |
| **Ocean Engineering** | Current velocity profiles design offshore structures and submarine pipelines |

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** When a KTU problem gives "velocity profile" data (e.g., $u = U_{\max}(1 - r^2/R^2)$), it is **NEVER** asking for the maximum velocity. It is always asking for **average velocity** or **discharge** or **shear stress**. Read the question carefully!

---

## 2.4 Velocity Components and the Acceleration Field

A fluid particle's **acceleration** is the time rate of change of its velocity. In the Eulerian framework, this becomes:

$$\vec{a} = \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$$

This is the **material derivative** (also called the *substantial* or *total* derivative), and it has two parts:

1. **Local acceleration** $\dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t}$: Due to *unsteadiness* of flow at a fixed point.
2. **Convective acceleration** $(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$: Due to the fluid particle *moving to a new location* where the velocity is different (e.g., in a converging nozzle).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU 2024 Insight:** In **steady flow**, local acceleration is zero, but convective acceleration may still be non-zero (e.g., flow through a converging section). This is a classic "trick" question in KTU exams.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Average Velocity from the Velocity Profile

### Problem Statement
For laminar flow through a circular pipe, the velocity profile is given by:

$$u(r) = u_{\max}\left(1 - \frac{r^2}{R^2}\right)$$

where $u_{\max}$ is the centerline (maximum) velocity, $r$ is the radial distance from the center, and $R$ is the pipe radius. Derive an expression for the **average velocity** $u_{\text{avg}}$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Recall the definition of average velocity**

The average velocity is defined such that the total volumetric discharge $Q$ equals the average velocity times the cross-sectional area:

$$Q = u_{\text{avg}} \cdot A$$

**Step 2: Express the total discharge as an integral of local velocity over the cross-section**

For an axisymmetric flow in a circular pipe, we use a differential ring element of area $dA = 2\pi r\,dr$:

$$Q = \int_A u\,dA = \int_0^R u(r) \cdot 2\pi r\,dr$$

**Step 3: Substitute the velocity profile**

$$Q = \int_0^R u_{\max}\left(1 - \frac{r^2}{R^2}\right) \cdot 2\pi r\,dr$$

**Step 4: Pull out the constants outside the integral**

$$Q = 2\pi\,u_{\max} \int_0^R \left(r - \frac{r^3}{R^2}\right)dr$$

**Step 5: Perform the integration**

$$\int_0^R r\,dr = \left[\frac{r^2}{2}\right]_0^R = \frac{R^2}{2}$$

$$\int_0^R \frac{r^3}{R^2}\,dr = \frac{1}{R^2}\left[\frac{r^4}{4}\right]_0^R = \frac{R^2}{4}$$

**Step 6: Substitute back into the Q expression**

$$Q = 2\pi\,u_{\max}\left(\frac{R^2}{2} - \frac{R^2}{4}\right) = 2\pi\,u_{\max}\left(\frac{R^2}{4}\right) = \frac{\pi R^2 u_{\max}}{2}$$

**Step 7: Compute the cross-sectional area of the pipe**

$$A = \pi R^2$$

**Step 8: Equate the two expressions for Q and solve for average velocity**

$$u_{\text{avg}} \cdot A = \frac{\pi R^2 u_{\max}}{2}$$

$$u_{\text{avg}} \cdot \pi R^2 = \frac{\pi R^2 u_{\max}}{2}$$

$$\boxed{u_{\text{avg}} = \frac{u_{\max}}{2}}$$

### Physical Interpretation
For laminar flow in a circular pipe, the average velocity is exactly **one-half** of the maximum (centerline) velocity. This is a fundamental result that appears in many KTU numerical problems, particularly in the **Hagen-Poiseuille equation** module.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Continuity Equation for 1-D Incompressible Flow

### Problem Statement
Derive the continuity equation for a 1-D, steady, incompressible flow through a variable-area duct.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Apply conservation of mass to a control volume**

Consider a streamtube (or pipe) of variable cross-section. Mass entering the control volume per unit time must equal mass leaving per unit time (for steady flow):

$$\dot{m}_{\text{in}} = \dot{m}_{\text{out}}$$

**Step 2: Express mass flow rate at each section**

At section 1 (inlet): $\dot{m}_1 = \rho_1 A_1 V_1$

At section 2 (outlet): $\dot{m}_2 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$

**Step 3: Set the two equal**

$$\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$$

**Step 4: Apply the incompressibility condition**

For an incompressible fluid, $\rho_1 = \rho_2 = \rho$ (constant). The density cancels out:

$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$$

**Step 5: Generalize to any two sections**

For a control volume bounded by sections 1 and 2:

$$\boxed{A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = Q = \text{constant}}$$

**Step 6: Show the differential form**

Take the differential of $AV = \text{const}$:

$$d(AV) = 0$$

$$A\,dV + V\,dA = 0$$

$$\frac{dA}{A} = -\frac{dV}{V}$$

This means: **a decrease in area causes a proportional increase in velocity** (and vice versa). This is the basis for designing nozzles and diffusers.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Velocity Components from Stream Function

### Problem Statement
Show that the velocity components in a 2-D incompressible flow can be expressed in terms of a scalar function $\psi(x, y)$ called the **stream function**, where:

$$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}, \quad v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$$

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Start with the 2-D continuity equation**

For an incompressible fluid in 2-D, the continuity equation is:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0$$

**Step 2: Recognize that this is an exact differential equation**

The equation has the mathematical form of the divergence of a vector being zero, which means there exists a scalar potential such that the velocity components can be expressed as partial derivatives.

**Step 3: Define the stream function**

Let $\psi = \psi(x, y)$ be a scalar function such that:

$$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} \quad \text{and} \quad v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$$

**Step 4: Substitute into the continuity equation**

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(-\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}\right) = 0$$

**Step 5: Apply the equality of mixed partial derivatives (Clairaut's theorem)**

Since $\psi$ is assumed to be a smooth (continuously differentiable) function:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x \partial y} - \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x \partial y} = 0$$

$$0 = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

The continuity equation is satisfied identically. This proves that **any** continuously differentiable stream function automatically satisfies mass conservation for 2-D incompressible flow.

**Step 6: Interpret the result**

The stream function $\psi$ has units of $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ (same as volumetric flow rate per unit width). Lines of constant $\psi$ are called **streamlines** — they represent the paths along which fluid particles travel.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Velocity from Potential Function (Irrotational Flow)

### Problem Statement
Show that if a 2-D flow is **irrotational**, the velocity components can be expressed in terms of a scalar **velocity potential** $\phi(x, y)$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Start with the irrotationality condition**

For 2-D flow, irrotationality requires:

$$\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 0$$

**Step 2: Rearrange the equation**

$$\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}$$

**Step 3: Identify this as a condition for exact differentials**

For any scalar function $\phi(x, y)$, the mixed partial derivatives commute. The total differential is:

$$d\phi = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}dx + \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}dy$$

**Step 4: Define the velocity potential**

Let $\phi = \phi(x, y)$ be the velocity potential such that:

$$u = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} \quad \text{and} \quad v = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}$$

**Step 5: Substitute into the irrotationality condition**

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}\right) - \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}\right) = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x \partial y} - \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x \partial y} = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 6: Derive the governing equation (Laplace's equation)**

Differentiate $u = \partial\phi/\partial x$ with respect to $x$ and $v = \partial\phi/\partial y$ with respect to $y$:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2}, \quad \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y^2}$$

Substituting into the continuity equation:

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y^2} = \nabla^2 \phi = 0}$$

This is **Laplace's equation**, which governs all 2-D, steady, incompressible, irrotational flows. It is solved subject to boundary conditions to determine the velocity field completely.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Velocity Concepts Cross-Reference

| Concept | Mathematical Condition | Physical Meaning | Engineering Example |
|---------|------------------------|------------------|---------------------|
| Steady flow | $\partial \vec{V}/\partial t = 0$ | No temporal change at a point | Constant-rate pump delivery |
| Uniform flow | $\partial \vec{V}/\partial s = 0$ | No spatial change | Flow in a constant-diameter pipe far from entrance |
| Incompressible flow | $\rho = \text{const}$ | Density doesn't change | Water flow at low speeds |
| Irrotational flow | $\nabla \times \vec{V} = 0$ | No rotation of fluid elements | Ideal flow around an airfoil (inviscid core) |
| 1-D flow | $\vec{V} = V(x)$ only | Velocity varies in one direction | Flow in a long straight pipe |
| 2-D flow | $\vec{V} = \vec{V}(x, y)$ | Velocity varies in two directions | Flow over a long cylinder |
| Rotational flow | $\nabla \times \vec{V} \neq 0$ | Fluid elements rotate | Vortex, flow near solid boundary |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4.2 Velocity Profile Schematic — Parabolic Laminar Flow in Pipe

```mermaid

    subgraph PIPE["Circular Pipe Cross Section"]
        direction TB
        C["Centerline: u_max"] --> R1["r = R/2: u = 0.75 u_max"]

    end

    PIPE --> AVG["Average Velocity: u_avg = 0.5 u_max"]
```

## 4.3 Eulerian vs. Lagrangian Description

```mermaid
    end
```

```mermaid
    OUTLET["Outlet Section 2: A2, V2, P2"] --> CV

    CV --> ENERGY["Energy Conservation: P1/gamma + V1^2/2g + z1 = P2/gamma + V2^2/2g + z2 + h_L"]
    CV --> MOMENTUM["Momentum Conservation: Sum F = rho Q V2 - rho Q V1"]

    MASS --> VREL["For incompressible: A1 V1 = A2 V2"]
    VREL --> VRES["Result: V2 = V1 x A1/A2"]

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Define the following terms with one example for each:
- (i) Uniform velocity
- (ii) Steady velocity
- (iii) Non-uniform velocity

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**(i) Uniform Velocity (1 Mark):**
The velocity is said to be uniform when its magnitude and direction remain the same at every point in the flow field at a given instant. Mathematically, $\partial \vec{V}/\partial s = 0$.
*Example:* Flow of water in a long, straight pipe of constant diameter at constant rate, away from the entrance and exit effects.

**(ii) Steady Velocity (1 Mark):**
The velocity at a given point in the flow field does not change with time. Mathematically, $\partial \vec{V}/\partial t = 0$.
*Example:* Constant discharge of water from a tap after the flow has stabilized.

**(iii) Non-Uniform Velocity (1 Mark):**
The velocity changes from point to point in the flow field at a given instant. Mathematically, $\partial \vec{V}/\partial s \neq 0$.
*Example:* Flow of water through a contracting pipe or through a pipe of varying cross-section.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Explain the difference between **Eulerian** and **Lagrangian** descriptions of fluid flow. Which one is more commonly used in engineering analysis and why?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Eulerian Description (1 Mark):**
In the Eulerian approach, we fix our attention on a *point in space* (a control volume) and observe how fluid properties (velocity, pressure, density) change as fluid flows past that point. The velocity is expressed as $\vec{V} = \vec{V}(x, y, z, t)$ — a function of position and time.

**Lagrangian Description (1 Mark):**
In the Lagrangian approach, we identify a *specific fluid particle* and track its motion, position, and velocity along its trajectory. The velocity is expressed as $\vec{V} = \vec{V}(a, b, c, t)$ — a function of initial position and time.

**Why Eulerian is preferred (1 Mark):**
The Eulerian approach is more commonly used in engineering because it leads to field equations (Navier-Stokes, continuity, energy) that can be solved efficiently using mathematical methods. Tracking individual particles is computationally expensive and unnecessary for most engineering applications.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** A pipe of diameter 200 mm at section 1 reduces to a diameter of 100 mm at section 2. If the velocity at section 1 is 2 m/s, calculate:
- (i) The discharge through the pipe. **(2 Marks)**
- (ii) The velocity at section 2. **(2 Marks)**
- (iii) The mass flow rate if the fluid is water ($\rho = 1000\,\text{kg/m}^3$). **(3 Marks)**

**(b)** For a 2-D incompressible, irrotational flow, the stream function is given by $\psi = 3xy$. Determine:
- (i) The velocity components $u$ and $v$. **(3 Marks)**
- (ii) The velocity magnitude at the point (1, 2). **(2 Marks)**
- (iii) Is the flow rotational or irrotational? Verify with a calculation. **(2 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

### Part (a) — Solution

**Given:**
- $D_1 = 200\,\text{mm} = 0.2\,\text{m}$
- $D_2 = 100\,\text{mm} = 0.1\,\text{m}$
- $V_1 = 2\,\text{m/s}$
- $\rho = 1000\,\text{kg/m}^3$

**(i) Discharge through the pipe (2 Marks):**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi D_1^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.2)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.04}{4} = 0.01\pi\,\text{m}^2$$

$$Q = A_1 \times V_1 = 0.01\pi \times 2 = 0.02\pi\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{Q = 0.0628\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}}$$

**(ii) Velocity at section 2 (2 Marks):**

By continuity: $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$

$$V_2 = \frac{A_1 V_1}{A_2} = V_1 \times \left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^2 = 2 \times \left(\frac{200}{100}\right)^2 = 2 \times 4 = 8\,\text{m/s}$$

$$\boxed{V_2 = 8\,\text{m/s}}$$

**(iii) Mass flow rate (3 Marks):**

$$\dot{m} = \rho \times Q = 1000 \times 0.0628 = 62.8\,\text{kg/s}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m} = 62.8\,\text{kg/s}}$$

### Part (b) — Solution

**Given:** $\psi = 3xy$ (stream function)

**(i) Velocity components (3 Marks):**

Using the stream function relations:

$$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial}{\partial y}(3xy) = 3x$$

$$v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} = -\frac{\partial}{\partial x}(3xy) = -3y$$

**(ii) Velocity magnitude at (1, 2) (2 Marks):**

$$u = 3 \times 1 = 3\,\text{m/s}$$
$$v = -3 \times 2 = -6\,\text{m/s}$$

$$|\vec{V}| = \sqrt{u^2 + v^2} = \sqrt{3^2 + (-6)^2} = \sqrt{9 + 36} = \sqrt{45} = 6.708\,\text{m/s}$$

$$\boxed{|\vec{V}| = 6.708\,\text{m/s}}$$

**(iii) Check for rotationality (2 Marks):**

For 2-D flow, vorticity is:

$$\omega_z = \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}(-3y) - \frac{\partial}{\partial y}(3x) = 0 - 0 = 0$$

Since $\omega_z = 0$, the flow is **irrotational**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Derive the continuity equation for a 1-D, steady, incompressible flow through a variable-area duct. Show that the differential form is $\dfrac{dA}{A} + \dfrac{dV}{V} = 0$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The velocity components in a 2-D flow field are given by $u = 2x + y$ and $v = x - 2y$. Determine:
- (i) Whether the flow is continuous (satisfies continuity equation). **(3 Marks)**
- (ii) Whether the flow is rotational or irrotational. **(2 Marks)**
- (iii) The acceleration of a fluid particle at point (2, 1) at time $t = 0$ if the flow is steady. **(2 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

### Part (a) — Solution

**Step 1: Define the control volume.**
Consider a duct with inlet section 1 (area $A_1$, velocity $V_1$) and outlet section 2 (area $A_2$, velocity $V_2$). For steady, incompressible flow, mass is conserved.

**Step 2: Apply the principle of conservation of mass.**
Mass flow rate entering = Mass flow rate leaving:

$$\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$$

**Step 3: Apply incompressibility ($\rho_1 = \rho_2 = \rho$):**
The density cancels:

$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = Q \quad (\text{constant})$$

**Step 4: Take the differential form.**
Let the product $AV$ be a constant: $AV = C$.

Differentiating both sides with respect to position $s$:

$$\frac{d(AV)}{ds} = 0$$

Applying the product rule:

$$A\frac{dV}{ds} + V\frac{dA}{ds} = 0$$

Dividing throughout by $AV$:

$$\frac{1}{V}\frac{dV}{ds} + \frac{1}{A}\frac{dA}{ds} = 0$$

Equivalently:

$$\frac{dA}{A} = -\frac{dV}{V}$$

**Step 5: State the key physical conclusion.**
A **decrease in cross-sectional area** (e.g., in a nozzle) causes an **increase in velocity**; conversely, an **increase in area** (e.g., in a diffuser) causes a **decrease in velocity**. This is a direct consequence of conservation of mass.

---

### Part (b) — Solution

**Given:** $u = 2x + y$, $v = x - 2y$

**(i) Check continuity (3 Marks):**

For 2-D incompressible flow, continuity requires:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0$$

Computing each partial derivative:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}(2x + y) = 2$$

$$\frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial}{\partial y}(x - 2y) = -2$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 2 + (-2) = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

The flow **satisfies the continuity equation** and represents a possible incompressible flow.

**(ii) Check for rotationality (2 Marks):**

Compute the vorticity:

$$\omega_z = \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}(x - 2y) - \frac{\partial}{\partial y}(2x + y) = 1 - 1 = 0$$

Since $\omega_z = 0$, the flow is **irrotational**.

**(iii) Acceleration at point (2, 1) for steady flow (2 Marks):**

For steady flow, the material derivative reduces to convective acceleration only:

$$a_x = u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}$$

$$a_y = u\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial v}{\partial y}$$

Computing the necessary derivatives:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 2, \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 1, \quad \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} = 1, \quad \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = -2$$

At point (2, 1):
$$u = 2(2) + 1 = 5\,\text{m/s}$$
$$v = 2 - 2(1) = 0\,\text{m/s}$$

Acceleration components:

$$a_x = (5)(2) + (0)(1) = 10\,\text{m/s}^2$$

$$a_y = (5)(1) + (0)(-2) = 5\,\text{m/s}^2$$

Magnitude of acceleration:

$$|\vec{a}| = \sqrt{10^2 + 5^2} = \sqrt{125} = 11.18\,\text{m/s}^2$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Velocity Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "uniform" with "steady":** Students frequently write "uniform flow" when they mean "steady flow." These are *different* concepts. Uniform refers to *spatial* variation; steady refers to *temporal* variation. (Lose 1 mark)
>
> 2. **Forgetting units in stream function:** The stream function $\psi$ has units of $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$, not $\text{m/s}$. The velocity components derived from $\psi$ have units of $\text{m/s}$. (Lose 0.5 mark)
>
> 3. **Sign error in stream function for $v$ component:** The relation is $v = -\partial\psi/\partial x$. Missing the negative sign is the **most common error** in KTU answer sheets. (Lose 1 mark)
>
> 4. **Using average velocity as maximum velocity:** When a velocity profile is given, students sometimes substitute $u_{\max}$ directly into $Q = AV$, getting the wrong answer. The correct approach is to integrate first. (Lose 2-3 marks)
>
> 5. **Forgetting to check both continuity and irrotationality separately:** A flow can be continuous but rotational, or irrotational but not continuous. Always verify both conditions independently. (Lose 1-2 marks)
>
> 6. **Not converting units:** Pipe diameters given in mm must be converted to m before computing areas. Mixing units is an automatic 0.5-mark penalty under KTU norms.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Velocity** is a **vector field** in fluid mechanics, not a single number.
- The velocity at a point in a fluid can be decomposed into **three components**: $u$, $v$, $w$ along the $x$, $y$, $z$ axes.
- **Average velocity** = $Q/A$, where $Q$ is discharge and $A$ is cross-sectional area.
- **Continuity equation** (incompressible): $A_1V_1 = A_2V_2 = Q$ (constant).
- **Steady flow:** $\partial \vec{V}/\partial t = 0$ (no time variation at a point).
- **Uniform flow:** $\partial \vec{V}/\partial s = 0$ (no spatial variation at a given time).
- **Irrotational flow:** $\nabla \times \vec{V} = 0$ (no rotation of fluid elements).
- **Vorticity (2-D):** $\omega_z = \partial v/\partial x - \partial u/\partial y$.
- **Stream function** $\psi$ satisfies continuity identically:
  - $u = \partial\psi/\partial y$
  - $v = -\partial\psi/\partial x$ *(note the negative sign!)*
- **Velocity potential** $\phi$ satisfies irrotationality identically:
  - $u = \partial\phi/\partial x$
  - $v = \partial\phi/\partial y$
- **Cauchy-Riemann equations** relate $\phi$ and $\psi$ for 2-D incompressible, irrotational flow:
  - $\partial\phi/\partial x = \partial\psi/\partial y$
  - $\partial\phi/\partial y = -\partial\psi/\partial x$
- **Material (total) derivative** of velocity gives the **acceleration field**:
  - $\vec{a} = \partial\vec{V}/\partial t + (\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)\vec{V}$
- For **laminar flow in a circular pipe**: $V_{\text{avg}} = 0.5\,V_{\text{max}}$.
- For **turbulent flow in a circular pipe**: $V_{\text{avg}} \approx 0.82\,V_{\text{max}}$.
- **No-slip condition:** Fluid in contact with a solid boundary has zero velocity relative to the boundary.
- **Laplace's equation** $\nabla^2 \phi = 0$ governs 2-D, steady, incompressible, irrotational flow.
- **Eulerian approach** (field-based) is preferred in engineering; **Lagrangian approach** (particle-based) is used in particle tracking and certain CFD applications.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** When a problem gives you a stream function or velocity components, the *first step* is always to check the continuity equation. If it fails, the rest of the problem is moot. The *second step* is to check irrotationality — this tells you whether a velocity potential function exists.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rate of Shear Strain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT741 - FLUID MECHANICS & MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Rate of Shear Strain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU-Standard Definition

**Rate of Shear Strain** (also termed **Shear Strain Rate** or **Velocity Gradient**) is defined as the **time rate of change of angular deformation** of a fluid element subjected to a tangential (shear) stress. Mathematically, it represents the spatial rate of change of fluid velocity in a direction perpendicular to the flow.

For a fluid element deforming under shear, the rate of shear strain is given by:

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{dv}{dy}$$

where:
- $\dot{\gamma}$ = Rate of shear strain $[s^{-1}]$
- $\frac{dv}{dy}$ = Velocity gradient perpendicular to flow direction $[s^{-1}]$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Rate of shear strain is the foundational kinematic quantity that connects **Newton's Law of Viscosity** ($\tau = \mu \frac{dv}{dy}$) with the rheological classification of fluids (Newtonian vs. Non-Newtonian). This topic is a **guaranteed 3 to 7 mark question** in every KTU End Semester Examination (ESE) under Module 1.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a thick deck of playing cards held firmly on a table. If you push the top card horizontally while keeping the bottom card stationary, each card slides slightly relative to the one beneath it.

- The **amount each card displaces** per unit time is essentially the **velocity** of that layer.
- The **difference in velocity between two adjacent cards divided by the vertical gap** between them is the **rate of shear strain**.
- A fluid behaves in an identical way: when a force (shear stress $\tau$) is applied, layers slide past one another, creating a **velocity gradient** that quantifies the rate of deformation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Takeaway:** Think of rate of shear strain as *"how fast the fluid is being sheared"* — it measures how rapidly neighbouring fluid layers move relative to each other across a unit perpendicular distance. Faster sliding = higher rate of shear strain.

---

## 1.3 Physical Significance

| Property | Symbol | Standard SI Unit | Vector/Scalar |
|----------|--------|------------------|---------------|
| Rate of Shear Strain | $\dot{\gamma}$ | **$s^{-1}$** | Scalar (for 1-D flow) |
| Shear Stress | $\tau$ | **$N/m^2$ or Pa** | Tensor |
| Dynamic Viscosity | $\mu$ | **$N \cdot s / m^2$ or Pa·s** | Scalar |

> [!NOTE]
> For a 3-D general flow, the rate of shear strain is part of the **Rate of Strain Tensor** (also called the **Rate of Deformation Tensor**), but in KTU Module 1 we focus on the simplified 1-D shear flow case.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation

The rate of shear strain emerges from three interrelated physical concepts:

### Step 1 — Shear Strain ($\gamma$)
When a fluid element undergoes angular deformation $\theta$ (in radians), the **shear strain** is defined as:

$$\gamma = \tan(\theta) \approx \theta \quad \text{(for small deformations)}$$

### Step 2 — Time Rate of Change (Taking the Derivative)
The **rate of shear strain** is obtained by differentiating the shear strain with respect to time:

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{d\gamma}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{dx}{dy}\right) = \frac{d}{dy}\left(\frac{dx}{dt}\right) = \frac{dv}{dy}$$

### Step 3 — Newton's Law of Viscosity Connection
For a **Newtonian fluid**, the shear stress is directly proportional to the rate of shear strain:

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \dot{\gamma} = \mu \cdot \frac{dv}{dy}$$

This is the fundamental **constitutive equation** of Newtonian fluid mechanics.

---

## 2.2 Why "Rate" Matters in Fluid Mechanics

Unlike solids, fluids **continuously deform** under applied shear stress — they have no fixed shape. Therefore:
- Solids → characterized by **strain** $\gamma$
- Fluids → characterized by **rate of strain** $\dot{\gamma}$

This distinction is the **physically rigorous reason** why fluid mechanics uses velocity gradients ($dv/dy$) rather than displacement gradients ($dx/dy$) in its governing equations.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — Memorize This for ESE**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{dv}{dy}$ | Rate of shear strain (1-D Couette flow) | $s^{-1}$ |
| 2 | $\tau = \mu \cdot \dfrac{dv}{dy}$ | Newton's Law of Viscosity | Pa or $N/m^2$ |
| 3 | $\mu = \dfrac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$ | Definition of dynamic viscosity | Pa·s |
| 4 | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | Kinematic viscosity | $m^2/s$ |
| 5 | $\dot{\gamma}_{ij} = \dfrac{1}{2}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j} + \dfrac{\partial u_j}{\partial x_i}\right)$ | General rate of strain tensor component | $s^{-1}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Warning:** Use the **vert bar** ($\vert$ or $\mid$) symbol, NOT the keyboard pipe $\vert$ character, when writing absolute value expressions inside markdown tables to avoid breaking the table structure. Example: $\vert v \vert$ for velocity magnitude.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Application of Rate of Shear Strain |
|-------------------|--------------------------------------|
| **Pipeline Design** | Determining friction losses using Hagen-Poiseuille equation |
| **Lubrication Systems** | Bearing design — calculating viscous shear in journal bearings |
| **Polymer Processing** | Extrusion and injection molding require controlled shear rates |
| **Blood Flow Modelling** | Non-Newtonian behaviour in arteries |
| **Hydraulic Machinery** | Viscosity affects pump and turbine performance |
| **Food & Cosmetics** | Quality control of toothpaste, ketchup, paints |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Angular Deformation to Velocity Gradient

### Given:
A fluid element of height $dy$ and base length $dx$ is subjected to a tangential shear stress $\tau$ on its top surface.

### Step 1 — Define the Shear Strain
The shear strain $\gamma$ for small deformations equals the angular deformation (in radians):

$$\gamma = \lim_{\theta \to 0} \tan(\theta) = \tan(\theta)$$

### Step 2 — Geometric Interpretation
From the geometry of the deformed element, the tangent of the angle of deformation is the ratio of horizontal displacement to vertical height:

$$\tan(\theta) = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{adjacent}} = \frac{dx}{dy}$$

Therefore:

$$\gamma = \frac{dx}{dy}$$

### Step 3 — Take Time Derivative to Get Rate of Shear Strain
Differentiate both sides with respect to time $t$:

$$\frac{d\gamma}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{dx}{dy}\right)$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Chain Rule (Swap Order of Differentiation)
Since $y$ is a spatial coordinate (independent of time for a fixed reference frame), we can swap the order of differentiation:

$$\frac{d\gamma}{dt} = \frac{d}{dy}\left(\frac{dx}{dt}\right)$$

### Step 5 — Recognize Velocity
The time derivative of displacement $x$ is the velocity $v$ of the fluid layer:

$$\frac{dx}{dt} = v$$

### Step 6 — Final Result
Substituting back:

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{d\gamma}{dt} = \frac{dv}{dy}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning Unlocked:** We have derived that the rate of shear strain in a fluid is simply the **spatial gradient of velocity** in the direction perpendicular to flow. This is why fluid mechanics textbooks often call it the **"velocity gradient"** interchangeably.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Connecting Shear Stress, Rate of Shear Strain, and Viscosity

### Given (Newtonian Fluid Assumption):
The shear stress is linearly proportional to the rate of shear strain.

### Step 1 — Write the Proportionality Statement
$$\tau \propto \dot{\gamma}$$

### Step 2 — Introduce the Proportionality Constant
The constant of proportionality is the dynamic viscosity $\mu$:

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the Expression for Rate of Shear Strain
$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{dv}{dy}$$

### Step 4 — Solve for Viscosity (Alternative Form)
$$\mu = \frac{\tau}{\left(\dfrac{dv}{dy}\right)} = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$$

This gives viscosity its operational definition: **"resistance of a fluid to deformation under shear."**

---

## 3.3 Numerical Illustration (KTU-Style Calculation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example (Model for ESE):**

**Problem:** Oil with dynamic viscosity $\mu = 0.1 \, N \cdot s/m^2$ flows between two parallel plates separated by a distance of $h = 10 \, mm$. The bottom plate is stationary and the top plate moves with a velocity of $U = 0.5 \, m/s$. Calculate:
> 1. The rate of shear strain in the fluid.
> 2. The shear stress at the mid-plane.
> 3. The shear stress on the bottom plate.

### Solution:

**Given:**
- $\mu = 0.1 \, N \cdot s/m^2$
- $h = 10 \, mm = 0.01 \, m$
- $U = 0.5 \, m/s$
- Linear velocity profile assumption (Couette flow)

**Step 1 — Velocity Profile (Linear)**

For Couette flow, velocity varies linearly:

$$v(y) = \frac{U}{h} \cdot y$$

**Step 2 — Compute Velocity Gradient**

$$\frac{dv}{dy} = \frac{U}{h} = \frac{0.5}{0.01} = 50 \, s^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Rate of Shear Strain**

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{dv}{dy} = 50 \, s^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Shear Stress at Mid-Plane ($y = h/2$)**

Since velocity gradient is constant:

$$\tau_{mid} = \mu \cdot \frac{dv}{dy} = 0.1 \times 50 = 5 \, N/m^2$$

**Step 5 — Shear Stress on Bottom Plate**

$$\tau_{wall} = \mu \cdot \frac{dv}{dy} = 0.1 \times 50 = 5 \, N/m^2$$

(Constant throughout the fluid in pure Couette flow.)

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|----------|--------|-------|------|
| Rate of Shear Strain | $\dot{\gamma}$ | **50** | $s^{-1}$ |
| Shear Stress (Mid-plane) | $\tau_{mid}$ | **5** | $N/m^2$ |
| Shear Stress (Bottom Plate) | $\tau_{wall}$ | **5** | $N/m^2$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram — Rate of Shear Strain in Fluid Layers

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the logical relationship between shear stress, viscosity, and rate of shear strain in a typical Couette flow configuration.

```mermaid

```

Since Mermaid cannot accurately render physical layer diagrams, here is a detailed text-based schematic for student visualization:

```

         ┌────────────────────────────────────┐  →
         │                                    │  →   Fluid Layer at y = h

         │                                    │  →
         │   ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~│  ← Velocity Profile (Linear)
         │                                    │  →
         │                                    │  →
         │                                    │  →   Fluid Layer at y = h/2

         │                                    │  →
         │                                    │  →
         │                                    │  →   Fluid Layer at y = 0

```

**Annotations on the schematic:**

- The **slope of the velocity profile** = $\dfrac{dv}{dy} = \dfrac{U}{h} = 50 \, s^{-1}$ = **Rate of Shear Strain**.
