# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Rate of Shear Strain. State its SI unit.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):** Rate of shear strain is defined as the **time rate of change of shear strain** experienced by a fluid element when subjected to shear stress. It is mathematically expressed as the **velocity gradient** perpendicular to the direction of flow:

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{dv}{dy}$$

where $v$ is the velocity of a fluid layer and $y$ is the perpendicular distance from a reference surface.

**SI Unit (1 Mark):** The SI unit of rate of shear strain is **$s^{-1}$** (per second).

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the physical significance of Rate of Shear Strain in the study of fluid flow?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The rate of shear strain has the following physical significance in fluid mechanics:

1. **Quantifies Fluid Deformation (1 Mark):** It measures how rapidly a fluid element deforms under the action of shear stress, providing a kinematic description of flow.

2. **Bridge Between Kinematics and Dynamics (1 Mark):** It connects the velocity field (kinematics) with the internal shear stress through Newton's Law of Viscosity: $\tau = \mu \dot{\gamma}$.

3. **Distinguishes Newtonian from Non-Newtonian Fluids (1 Mark):** The relationship between $\tau$ and $\dot{\gamma}$ determines whether a fluid is Newtonian (linear) or Non-Newtonian (non-linear).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Define rate of shear strain. Starting from the basic definition, derive the expression $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{dv}{dy}$ for a fluid element undergoing shear deformation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Two parallel plates are placed 8 mm apart. The lower plate is stationary while the upper plate moves with a uniform velocity of 0.4 m/s. The space between the plates is filled with oil of dynamic viscosity 0.15 Pa·s. Assuming a linear velocity profile, determine: (i) the rate of shear strain in the oil, and (ii) the shear stress acting on the lower plate. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### **Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** State the definition of shear strain.
Shear strain $\gamma$ is the angular deformation of a fluid element under shear stress. For small deformations:

$$\gamma = \tan(\theta) = \frac{dx}{dy}$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Write the geometric relation.

$$\gamma = \frac{dx}{dy}$$

where $dx$ is the horizontal displacement of the top of the element and $dy$ is the vertical height of the element.

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Differentiate with respect to time.

$$\frac{d\gamma}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{dx}{dy}\right)$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Interchange the order of differentiation.

$$\frac{d\gamma}{dt} = \frac{d}{dy}\left(\frac{dx}{dt}\right)$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Recognize that $\dfrac{dx}{dt} = v$ (velocity).

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{dv}{dy}$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** State the physical interpretation and units.

The rate of shear strain equals the velocity gradient perpendicular to the direction of motion. Units: $s^{-1}$.

**Step 7 (1 Mark):** Mention Newton's Law of Viscosity connection.

$$\tau = \mu \dot{\gamma} = \mu \frac{dv}{dy}$$

---

#### **Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks)**

**Given Data (1 Mark):**
- Plate separation, $h = 8 \, mm = 0.008 \, m$
- Upper plate velocity, $U = 0.4 \, m/s$
- Dynamic viscosity, $\mu = 0.15 \, Pa \cdot s$
- Lower plate stationary

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Write the linear velocity profile.

$$v(y) = \frac{U}{h} \cdot y$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Compute the velocity gradient (rate of shear strain).

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{dv}{dy} = \frac{U}{h} = \frac{0.4}{0.008} = 50 \, s^{-1}$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Answer part (i).

**Rate of Shear Strain = $50 \, s^{-1}$**

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Apply Newton's Law of Viscosity.

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Substitute numerical values.

$$\tau = 0.15 \times 50 = 7.5 \, N/m^2$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Answer part (ii).

**Shear Stress on Lower Plate = $7.5 \, N/m^2$**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of rate of shear strain with the help of a neat sketch of fluid layers between two parallel plates. State Newton's law of viscosity and show that shear stress is directly proportional to the rate of shear strain for Newtonian fluids. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A fluid flows over a flat plate with a linear velocity profile given by $v(y) = 0.85y$, where $v$ is in m/s and $y$ is in meters. If the fluid has a dynamic viscosity of $0.25 \, N \cdot s/m^2$, calculate: (i) the rate of shear strain at $y = 0$, (ii) the shear stress at $y = 0$, and (iii) the viscosity in centipoise. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### **Part (a) — Theory with Sketch (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Sketch two parallel plates with the bottom plate fixed and top plate moving under applied force $F$, with intermediate fluid layers moving at intermediate velocities.

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Identify velocity gradient $\dfrac{dv}{dy}$ across the gap.

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Define rate of shear strain as:

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{dv}{dy}$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** State Newton's Law of Viscosity:

*"The shear stress in a Newtonian fluid is directly proportional to the rate of shear strain."*

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Express the proportionality:

$$\tau \propto \dot{\gamma}$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Introduce the constant of proportionality $\mu$ (dynamic viscosity):

$$\tau = \mu \dot{\gamma}$$

**Step 7 (1 Mark):** Conclude: For Newtonian fluids, $\tau$ vs $\dot{\gamma}$ is a straight line through the origin with slope $\mu$.

---

#### **Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- $v(y) = 0.85y$
- $\mu = 0.25 \, N \cdot s/m^2$

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Differentiate velocity profile.

$$\frac{dv}{dy} = \frac{d}{dy}(0.85y) = 0.85$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Compute rate of shear strain at $y = 0$.

$$\dot{\gamma}\bigg|_{y=0} = 0.85 \, s^{-1}$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Apply Newton's Law of Viscosity.

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Substitute values.

$$\tau = 0.25 \times 0.85 = 0.2125 \, N/m^2$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** State shear stress at $y = 0$.

$$\tau\bigg|_{y=0} = 0.2125 \, N/m^2$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Convert viscosity to centipoise.

$$1 \, Pa \cdot s = 1000 \, cP \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mu = 0.25 \times 1000 = 250 \, cP$$

**Step 7 (1 Mark):** Final answer:

**Viscosity = 250 cP**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors (Most Frequent -2 Mark Loss):** Students frequently forget to convert $mm$ to $m$ before computing $dv/dy$. Always verify dimensional consistency.
> 2. **Confusing Symbols:** Don't confuse $\gamma$ (shear strain, dimensionless) with $\dot{\gamma}$ (rate of shear strain, $s^{-1}$). The dot denotes time derivative.
> 3. **Forgetting the Dot Product Rule:** For 2-D flow, $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x}$, not just $\dfrac{du}{dy}$.
> 4. **Missing Sketch in Descriptive Answers:** A neat sketch of parallel plates is mandatory for full marks in Part B questions.
> 5. **Wrong Variable Assignment:** In Couette flow, velocity is in the $x$-direction ($u$) and gradient is in $y$-direction — do not mix up coordinates.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read 5 Minutes Before Exam:**

- ✅ **Rate of Shear Strain Definition:** Time rate of change of shear strain = velocity gradient $\dfrac{dv}{dy}$.
- ✅ **SI Unit:** $s^{-1}$ (per second).
- ✅ **Newtonian Fluid Equation:** $\tau = \mu \dot{\gamma} = \mu \dfrac{dv}{dy}$.
- ✅ **Viscosity Definition:** $\mu = \dfrac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$ — resistance to shear deformation.
- ✅ **Kinematic Viscosity:** $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ — units $m^2/s$.
- ✅ **1-D Couette Flow:** $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{U}{h}$ where $U$ is plate velocity and $h$ is gap.
- ✅ **Newtonian Behaviour:** Shear stress vs. rate of shear strain is a **straight line through the origin** with slope $\mu$.
- ✅ **Non-Newtonian Fluids:** The $\tau$ vs $\dot{\gamma}$ relation is **non-linear** (e.g., Bingham plastic, pseudoplastic, dilatant).
- ✅ **3-D General Form:** $\dot{\gamma}_{ij} = \dfrac{1}{2}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j} + \dfrac{\partial u_j}{\partial x_i}\right)$.
- ✅ **Engineering Relevance:** Critical for pipeline design, lubrication, polymer processing, and blood flow modelling.
- ✅ **Exam Tip:** Always draw a sketch for descriptive questions; always check units in numericals.
- ✅ **Memory Aid:** **"Strain is for solids; Rate of Strain is for fluids."**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Newton's Law of Viscosity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT741 - FLUID MECHANICS & MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Newton's Law of Viscosity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Newton's Law of Viscosity** states that the **shear stress (τ)** developed in a real fluid in motion is **directly proportional** to the **rate of shear strain (velocity gradient, du/dy)** at that point, provided the temperature and pressure remain constant.

Mathematically, the law is expressed as:

$$\tau \propto \frac{du}{dy}$$

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$$

Where:
- $\tau$ = Shear stress developed in the fluid (in **N/m²** or **Pa**)
- $\mu$ = Coefficient of dynamic (absolute) viscosity (in **N·s/m²** or **Pa·s**)
- $\frac{du}{dy}$ = Velocity gradient perpendicular to the direction of flow (in **s⁻¹**)

Fluids that obey this linear relationship between shear stress and velocity gradient are called **Newtonian Fluids** (e.g., water, air, kerosene, glycerine, light oils, most gases).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Honey on a Spoon

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Intuition: Pouring Honey vs. Water**
> Imagine you are tilting a spoon of **honey** and a spoon of **water** at the same angle.
> - Water flows off quickly and in a thin, smooth sheet.
> - Honey flows off slowly, resisting the motion, almost "sticking" to itself.
> 
> This internal "stickiness" that resists the sliding of one fluid layer over another is **viscosity**. It arises because of **molecular cohesion** and **momentum exchange** between adjacent fluid layers moving at different velocities.

A more visual analogy is **a deck of playing cards**:
- Hold a deck of cards flat. Push the top card sideways.
- The card below it moves a *little* (because of friction between the cards).
- The next card moves a *little less*, and so on.
- In a fluid, these "cards" are infinitely thin layers sliding over each other, and the resistance between them is governed by **Newton's Law of Viscosity**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Dynamic Viscosity (μ):** Also called **absolute viscosity**. Its SI unit is **N·s/m²** (equivalent to **Pa·s**). The CGS unit is **poise**, where **1 poise = 0.1 N·s/m²**.
> - **Kinematic Viscosity (ν):** Defined as $\nu = \mu / \rho$. Its SI unit is **m²/s**. The CGS unit is **stokes**, where **1 stoke = 1 cm²/s = 10⁻⁴ m²/s**.
> - **Dimensional Formula of Dynamic Viscosity:** **[M L⁻¹ T⁻¹]**
> - **Dimensional Formula of Kinematic Viscosity:** **[L² T⁻¹]**

### 1.4 Newton's Postulate (Key Assumption)

> [!NOTE]
> Newton postulated that the **viscous shear stress** between two adjacent fluid layers is analogous to the **frictional force** between two solid surfaces sliding against each other — i.e., proportional to the rate of relative motion (velocity gradient), and the constant of proportionality is the viscosity of the fluid.

This postulate holds **strictly** for Newtonian fluids. Fluids that do **not** follow this linear law are termed **Non-Newtonian Fluids** (e.g., blood, paint, toothpaste, muddy water, slurries, polymer solutions).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanism of Viscosity at the Molecular Level

The viscosity of a fluid arises from two primary molecular-level mechanisms:

1. **Cohesive Forces (Molecular Attraction):**
   - Molecules in adjacent fluid layers exert attractive forces on one another.
   - Faster-moving molecules transfer momentum to slower-moving molecules, creating a net resistive force (shear stress).

2. **Momentum Exchange (Kinetic Theory):**
   - Fast-moving molecules randomly migrate into slower layers, carrying their higher momentum.
   - Slow-moving molecules migrate into faster layers, carrying their lower momentum.
   - This continuous exchange generates a net resistance to relative motion.

### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind the Derivation Logic

- **Why du/dy?** Fluid velocity varies from zero (at the stationary boundary) to maximum (at the free stream). The variation happens over a small perpendicular distance, $dy$. The ratio is the **velocity gradient** (rate of change of velocity with respect to distance).
- **Why proportional?** Experimental observation by Newton (and verified by later scientists) showed that doubling the velocity gradient exactly doubles the shear stress for Newtonian fluids at constant temperature.
- **Why μ (the constant)?** Different fluids have different "internal friction" levels. Water has low μ; honey has high μ. μ is a **fluid property**, just like density.

### 2.3 Newton's Law — Restated in Three Equivalent Forms

| Form | Equation | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Differential Form | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Shear stress is proportional to local velocity gradient |
| Ratio Form | $\mu = \dfrac{\tau}{du/dy}$ | Dynamic viscosity = shear stress per unit velocity gradient |
| Kinematic Form | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | Kinematic viscosity is the ratio of dynamic viscosity to density |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for Exams)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table lists **all essential formulas** for Newton's Law of Viscosity questions. **Memorize these before your exam.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | SI Units | Dimensions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Shear Stress (Newton's Law) | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | N/m² or Pa | [M L⁻¹ T⁻²] |
| 2 | Dynamic Viscosity | $\mu = \dfrac{\tau}{du/dy}$ | N·s/m² or Pa·s | [M L⁻¹ T⁻¹] |
| 3 | Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | m²/s | [L² T⁻¹] |
| 4 | Dynamic Viscosity (CGS, poise) | 1 N·s/m² = 10 poise | poise | — |
| 5 | Kinematic Viscosity (CGS, stoke) | 1 m²/s = 10⁴ stoke | stoke (or cm²/s) | — |
| 6 | Velocity Gradient | $\dfrac{du}{dy}$ | s⁻¹ | [T⁻¹] |
| 7 | Specific Weight Relation | $\gamma = \rho \cdot g$ | N/m³ | [M L⁻² T⁻²] |
| 8 | Variation of μ with T (liquids) | $\mu \propto e^{1/T}$ (approx.) | — | — |
| 9 | Variation of μ with T (gases) | $\mu \propto T^{3/2}$ (Sutherland) | — | — |
| 10 | Newton's Law (rotating cylinder viscometer) | $\mu = \dfrac{T}{2\pi R^{3} N \cdot L} \cdot \dfrac{h}{...}$ | — | — |

> **Note:** In the table above, the vertical bar in the CGS unit "poise" is a hyphen separator, not a mathematical absolute-value bar.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter in engineering?**
> 1. **Lubrication Design:** Viscosity of oils (SAE grades 10W, 20W, 40) is carefully chosen to keep bearing surfaces separated by a thin hydrodynamic film.
> 2. **Pipeline Flow:** Head loss due to friction in water supply, oil transport, and HVAC ducts is directly calculated using viscosity.
> 3. **Hydraulic Machines:** Pump and turbine efficiencies (centrifugal pumps, Pelton wheels) depend on viscous losses inside the impeller and casing.
> 4. **Aerodynamics & Boundary Layer:** Viscosity dictates the thickness of the boundary layer over aircraft wings, ships, and turbine blades.
> 5. **Food, Pharma, and Chemical Industries:** Viscosity dictates mixing, heat-transfer rates, and pumping power in process industries.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Newton's Law of Viscosity from First Principles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation traces the logic of Newton's original postulate. KTU expects you to be able to reproduce this in the examination with proper labeling of every variable.

**Setup:**
Consider two parallel fluid layers, both of area $A$, separated by a perpendicular distance $dy$.

- The **lower layer** is stationary (velocity = 0).
- The **upper layer** is moving with a velocity $u$ in the horizontal $x$-direction.
- The relative velocity between the two layers is $du$.

**Step 1: Express the deformation rate (shear strain rate).**

The rate of change of deformation (angular distortion) between the two layers is:

$$\text{Shear strain rate} = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{\Delta \theta}{\Delta t} = \frac{du}{dy}$$

Where $\Delta \theta$ is the small angular displacement of the upper layer relative to the lower layer over time $\Delta t$.

**Step 2: Apply Newton's Postulate (force analogy with friction).**

Newton stated that the **resistive force** $F$ between two adjacent layers is:

$$F \propto A \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$$

**Step 3: Introduce the proportionality constant (viscosity).**

The constant of proportionality is the **coefficient of dynamic viscosity**, $\mu$. Therefore:

$$F = \mu \cdot A \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$$

**Step 4: Convert force to shear stress.**

Since shear stress is force per unit area ($\tau = F/A$):

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$$

This is the final form of **Newton's Law of Viscosity**.

$$\boxed{\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy}}$$

**Step 5: Physical interpretation of each term.**

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $\tau$ | Shear stress (resistive stress) developed in the fluid, N/m² |
| $\mu$ | Dynamic viscosity — internal friction of the fluid, N·s/m² |
| $du$ | Infinitesimal change in velocity between the two layers, m/s |
| $dy$ | Infinitesimal perpendicular distance between the layers, m |

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Problem-Solving Framework

> [!NOTE]
> Use the following 5-step analytical matrix to solve any numerical problem on Newton's Law of Viscosity in the KTU exam.

**Step 1 — Identify Given Quantities:**
List $\tau$, $\mu$, $u$, $y$, $A$, $F$, $\rho$, etc. Identify what is asked.

**Step 2 — Choose the Correct Formula:**
- If $\mu$ is given, use $\tau = \mu \cdot \dfrac{du}{dy}$.
- If $\rho$ is given, convert to $\nu$ using $\nu = \mu / \rho$ if needed.

**Step 3 — Substitute with Unit Conversion:**
Convert all values to **SI units** (Pa, m, s, N, kg) before substituting.

**Step 4 — Compute and Verify Dimensions:**
Check that the answer's dimensions match the expected quantity.

**Step 5 — Express Final Answer with Units and Significant Figures.**

### 3.3 Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

> **Problem:**
> A fluid of dynamic viscosity $\mu = 0.9 \text{ N·s/m}^2$ flows between two parallel horizontal plates separated by a distance of $8 \text{ mm}$. The lower plate is stationary, and the upper plate moves with a velocity of $1.2 \text{ m/s}$. If the flow is linear (Couette flow), determine:
> (a) The velocity gradient in the fluid.
> (b) The shear stress on the upper plate.
> (c) The kinematic viscosity if the fluid density is $950 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Velocity Gradient:**

Given: $du = 1.2 \text{ m/s}$ (velocity of upper plate relative to lower), $dy = 8 \text{ mm} = 0.008 \text{ m}$.

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{1.2 \text{ m/s}}{0.008 \text{ m}} = 150 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Part (b) — Shear Stress:**

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$$

$$\tau = 0.9 \text{ N·s/m}^2 \times 150 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

$$\tau = 135 \text{ N/m}^2 = 135 \text{ Pa}$$

**Part (c) — Kinematic Viscosity:**

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{0.9 \text{ N·s/m}^2}{950 \text{ kg/m}^3}$$

$$\nu = 9.4737 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{\nu \approx 9.47 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Newton's Law of Viscosity: Functional Architecture Flow

> [!NOTE]
> The following block diagram maps the **functional logic** behind Newton's Law of Viscosity — from molecular-level momentum exchange up to the engineering-level equation and its classification into Newtonian and Non-Newtonian regimes.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Real Fluid in Motion] --> stepB[Two Adjacent Fluid Layers]
    stepB --> stepC[Different Velocities u1 and u2]
    stepC --> stepD[Velocity Gradient du over dy]
    stepD --> stepE[Molecular Cohesion and Momentum Exchange]
    stepE --> stepF[Internal Friction RESISTS Motion]
    stepF --> stepG[Shear Stress TAU Develops]
    stepG --> stepH[Newtons Postulate]
    stepH --> stepI[Tau is Proportional to du over dy]
    stepI --> stepJ[Introduce Constant MU Dynamic Viscosity]
    stepJ --> stepK[Final Equation TAU equals MU times du over dy]
    stepK --> stepL{Is Relationship Linear}
    stepL -->|YES| stepM[Newtonian Fluid]
    stepL -->|NO| stepN[Non Newtonian Fluid]
    stepM --> stepO[Examples Water Air Kerosene]
    stepN --> stepP[Examples Blood Paint Toothpaste]
    stepK --> stepQ[Define Kinematic Viscosity NU equals MU over RHO]
    stepQ --> stepR[SI Units Pa s and m2 per s]
    stepR --> stepS[Engineering Applications Pumps Bearings Pipes Turbines]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Conceptual Schematic — Couette Flow (Linear Velocity Profile)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CF[Couette Flow Between Two Parallel Plates]
        direction TB
        plateA[Stationary Lower Plate y equals 0 u equals 0]
        layerA[Layer 1 Velocity u1]
        layerB[Layer 2 Velocity u2]
        layerC[Layer 3 Velocity u3]
        plateB[Moving Upper Plate y equals H u equals U]
    end
    layerA --> layerB
    layerB --> layerC
    layerA -.->|dy1| layerB
    layerB -.->|dy2| layerC
    layerC -.->|dy3| plateB
    plateA --> layerA
```

### 4.3 Textual Diagram — Velocity Profile Between Two Plates

> **Schematic (Linear Couette Flow):**

```
   Upper Plate ─────►►►►►►►►►►►►►   (moving with velocity U)
   ═══════════════════════════════════
        •  Layer 5  ────►►►►►►  u = 0.8U
        •  Layer 4  ────►►►►►  u = 0.6U
        •  Layer 3  ───►►►►  u = 0.4U
        •  Layer 2  ──►►►  u = 0.2U
        •  Layer 1  ─►  u = 0
   ═══════════════════════════════════
   Lower Plate (stationary)        (u = 0)
        |───── dy = 8 mm ─────|
```

> The **velocity gradient** $du/dy$ is constant for Couette flow, producing a **uniform shear stress** throughout the fluid.

### 4.4 Textual Diagram — Shear Stress vs. Velocity Gradient (Newtonian vs. Non-Newtonian)

```
   Shear Stress τ
        ▲
        │                          ━━━ Newtonian (Water, μ = const)
        │                       ╱
        │                    ╱     ┅┅┅┅ Non-Newtonian (Pseudo-plastic)
        │                 ╱      ╱
        │              ╱       ╱
        │           ╱         ╱
        │        ╱          ╱
        │     ╱           ╱
        │  ╱            ╱
        │╱           ╱
        └───────────────────────► Velocity Gradient (du/dy)
```

> [!NOTE]
> For a **Newtonian fluid**, the $\tau$ vs. $du/dy$ graph is a **straight line through the origin**, with slope = $\mu$ (a constant). For **Non-Newtonian fluids**, the relationship is **non-linear** (curve, with a slope that varies with $du/dy$).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, May 2024]**
> **State Newton's Law of Viscosity and mention two examples of Newtonian fluids.**

**Model Answer:**

Newton's Law of Viscosity states that the shear stress ($\tau$) developed in a real fluid in motion is directly proportional to the rate of shear strain (velocity gradient, $du/dy$) at that point, provided the temperature and pressure remain constant.

Mathematically:

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$$

where $\mu$ is the coefficient of dynamic viscosity.

**Two examples of Newtonian fluids:** Water, air, kerosene, glycerine (any two).

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Question Paper]**
> **Distinguish between dynamic viscosity and kinematic viscosity. State their units and dimensions.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Dynamic Viscosity (μ) | Kinematic Viscosity (ν) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Resistance of fluid to deformation | Ratio of dynamic viscosity to density |
| Formula | $\mu = \tau / (du/dy)$ | $\nu = \mu / \rho$ |
| SI Unit | N·s/m² (Pa·s) | m²/s |
| CGS Unit | poise | stoke |
| Dimensions | [M L⁻¹ T⁻¹] | [L² T⁻¹] |
| Depends on density? | No (independent) | Yes (inversely) |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam — Module 1, 14-Mark Standard]**
>
> **(a) Derive Newton's Law of Viscosity from Newton's postulate. (7 Marks)**
> **(b) A plate of area 0.5 m² is moving at 0.3 m/s over a thin film of oil of dynamic viscosity 0.85 N·s/m² and thickness 1.5 mm. Find the shear stress developed in the oil and the viscous force resisting the motion of the plate. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (Question A):**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

Consider two adjacent fluid layers separated by an infinitesimal distance $dy$. The lower layer is stationary, and the upper layer moves with velocity $u$.

The rate of shear strain is:

$$\frac{du}{dy}$$

According to Newton's postulate, the viscous shear force $F$ is:

$$F \propto A \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$$

Introducing the constant of proportionality $\mu$ (dynamic viscosity):

$$F = \mu \cdot A \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$$

Since shear stress $\tau = F/A$:

$$\boxed{\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy}}$$

This is Newton's Law of Viscosity.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

Given:
- $A = 0.5 \text{ m}^2$
- $U = 0.3 \text{ m/s}$ (plate velocity)
- $\mu = 0.85 \text{ N·s/m}^2$
- $dy = 1.5 \text{ mm} = 0.0015 \text{ m}$

Velocity gradient:

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{0.3}{0.0015} = 200 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

Shear stress:

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy} = 0.85 \times 200 = 170 \text{ N/m}^2$$

Viscous force:

$$F = \tau \cdot A = 170 \times 0.5 = 85 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{\tau = 170 \text{ N/m}^2, \quad F = 85 \text{ N}}$$

---

> **Q4. [KTU University Exam — Module 1, 14-Mark Standard — Alternative Choice]**
>
> **(a) Define the following: (i) Dynamic viscosity, (ii) Kinematic viscosity, (iii) Specific weight. (3 Marks each sub-part totaling 7 Marks)**
> **(b) A shaft of diameter 100 mm rotates at 1500 rpm inside a journal bearing. The bearing is 150 mm long, and the radial clearance is 0.2 mm. The shaft is supported by a lubricant of dynamic viscosity 0.025 N·s/m². Determine the viscous friction torque and the power lost in overcoming the viscous resistance. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (Question B):**

**Part (a) — Definitions (7 Marks, 1 Mark per sub-part + 4 Marks for explanation):**

**(i) Dynamic Viscosity (μ):** It is the property of a fluid that offers resistance to the relative motion between adjacent layers. Its SI unit is N·s/m² (Pa·s), and its dimensional formula is [M L⁻¹ T⁻¹].

$$\mu = \frac{\tau}{du/dy}$$

**(ii) Kinematic Viscosity (ν):** It is the ratio of dynamic viscosity to the density of the fluid. Its SI unit is m²/s.

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

Dimensions: [L² T⁻¹]

**(iii) Specific Weight (γ):** It is the weight per unit volume of a fluid. Its SI unit is N/m³.

$$\gamma = \rho \cdot g$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

Given:
- Diameter, $D = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$ ⇒ Radius $R = 0.05 \text{ m}$
- $N = 1500 \text{ rpm}$ ⇒ Angular velocity $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$
- Length $L = 150 \text{ mm} = 0.15 \text{ m}$
- Clearance $t = 0.2 \text{ mm} = 0.0002 \text{ m}$
- $\mu = 0.025 \text{ N·s/m}^2$

Surface velocity of shaft:

$$U = \omega \cdot R = 157.08 \times 0.05 = 7.854 \text{ m/s}$$

Velocity gradient across clearance:

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{U}{t} = \frac{7.854}{0.0002} = 39270 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

Shear stress:

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy} = 0.025 \times 39270 = 981.75 \text{ N/m}^2$$

Viscous force on the shaft surface:

$$F = \tau \cdot A = \tau \cdot (2\pi R L)$$

$$F = 981.75 \times (2\pi \times 0.05 \times 0.15)$$

$$F = 981.75 \times 0.04712 = 46.26 \text{ N}$$

Friction torque:

$$T = F \times R = 46.26 \times 0.05 = 2.313 \text{ N·m}$$

Power lost:

$$P = T \cdot \omega = 2.313 \times 157.08$$

$$\boxed{T = 2.313 \text{ N·m}, \quad P \approx 363.3 \text{ W}}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion:** Students often write viscosity in **cP (centipoise)** without converting to **Pa·s** before substitution. Remember: $1 \text{ cP} = 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$.
> 2. **Forgetting to Convert mm to m:** A velocity gradient calculated as "$\frac{0.3}{1.5}$" instead of "$\frac{0.3}{0.0015}$" gives a 1000-times error. Always convert mm → m.
> 3. **Confusing μ and ν:** If the problem gives "kinematic viscosity," multiply by $\rho$ first to obtain $\mu$ before applying $\tau = \mu(du/dy)$.
> 4. **Missing the Constant of Proportionality Statement:** KTU's marking scheme awards 1 mark specifically for stating the law, including the phrase "directly proportional at constant temperature and pressure."
> 5. **No Diagram:** For 7-mark derivations, **always draw a labeled diagram** of two adjacent fluid layers with $u$, $du$, $dy$, and $\tau$ marked clearly. KTU examiners deduct marks for missing diagrams.
> 6. **Mixing up Poise and Pascal-second:** The CGS unit **poise** and SI unit **Pa·s** are *not* identical — $10 \text{ poise} = 1 \text{ Pa·s}$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Newton's Law of Viscosity**

- **Core Equation:** $\tau = \mu \cdot (du/dy)$
- **Proportionality Constant:** $\mu$ — dynamic viscosity (Pa·s)
- **Velocity Gradient:** $du/dy$ — units s⁻¹
- **Kinematic Viscosity:** $\nu = \mu / \rho$ — units m²/s
- **Newtonian Fluid:** Linear $\tau$ vs. $du/dy$ graph passing through origin (water, air, kerosene, glycerine).
- **Non-Newtonian Fluid:** Non-linear $\tau$ vs. $du/dy$ relationship (blood, paint, toothpaste, slurries).
- **SI Unit of μ:** N·s/m² (= Pa·s)
- **CGS Unit of μ:** poise — 1 Pa·s = 10 poise
- **SI Unit of ν:** m²/s
- **CGS Unit of ν:** stoke — 1 m²/s = 10⁴ stoke
- **Dimensions of μ:** [M L⁻¹ T⁻¹]
- **Dimensions of ν:** [L² T⁻¹]
- **For Liquids:** $\mu$ **decreases** as temperature increases.
- **For Gases:** $\mu$ **increases** as temperature increases (proportional to $T^{3/2}$ per Sutherland's law).
- **Standard Viscosities (at 20°C):** Water $\approx 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s; Air $\approx 1.81 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s; Glycerine $\approx 1.5$ Pa·s.
- **Couette Flow:** Linear velocity profile between two parallel plates — constant $du/dy$ throughout.
- **Real-World Impact:** Bearings, pipelines, hydraulic machines, lubrication, blood-flow analysis.
- **Examiner's Tip:** Always convert **mm → m**, **cP → Pa·s**, and **stoke → m²/s** before substituting into the equation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Newtonian and Non-Newtonian Fluids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT741 - FLUID MECHANICS & MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Newtonian and Non-Newtonian Fluids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Newtonian Fluids — Formal Definition

A **Newtonian fluid** is a fluid for which the **shear stress ($\tau$)** at any point is **directly proportional** to the **rate of shear strain** (velocity gradient, $\dfrac{du}{dy}$) at that point, and the proportionality constant — the **dynamic viscosity ($\mu$)** — is **independent of the rate of shear** and depends only on **temperature** and **pressure** for a given fluid.

Mathematically, this is expressed by **Newton's Law of Viscosity**:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

Where:
- $\tau$ = Shear stress in **Pa (N/m²)**
- $\mu$ = Dynamic viscosity in **Pa·s** or **N·s/m²**
- $\dfrac{du}{dy}$ = Velocity gradient (rate of shear strain) in **s⁻¹**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A Newtonian fluid exhibits a **linear relationship** between shear stress and the rate of shear strain. The plot of $\tau$ vs. $\dfrac{du}{dy}$ is a **straight line passing through the origin**, with slope equal to $\mu$.

### Common Examples of Newtonian Fluids
Water, air, gasoline, kerosene, mercury, alcohol, and most simple hydrocarbons.

## 1.2 Non-Newtonian Fluids — Formal Definition

A **Non-Newtonian fluid** is one in which the relationship between **shear stress ($\tau$)** and **rate of shear strain ($\dfrac{du}{dy}$)** is **non-linear**, or in which the apparent viscosity depends on the **rate of shear**, the **duration of shearing**, or the **previous shear history**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For Non-Newtonian fluids, the simple relation $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ does **NOT** hold with a single constant $\mu$. The viscosity becomes a function of either the shear rate, time, or both.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine spreading **peanut butter** on toast:
- Push a little, it resists and moves slowly.
- Push harder, it suddenly slides smoothly — its "stickiness" decreases with effort. This is a **shear-thinning (pseudo-plastic)** fluid.

Now imagine stirring a **thick cornstarch-water mixture** (a classic oobleck):
- Stir slowly — it pours like a liquid.
- Stir rapidly or punch it — it feels **solid-like**, resisting motion. This is a **shear-thickening (dilatant)** fluid.

Finally, think of **toothpaste** sitting on a brush:
- It will **not flow** until you squeeze the tube past a certain threshold. Beyond that, it flows like a viscous liquid. This is a **Bingham plastic**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** Newtonian fluids have a **fixed "thickness"** that never changes no matter how hard or fast you push. Non-Newtonian fluids have a **variable "thickness"** that changes based on how you interact with them — this is what makes them so important in food, polymer, cosmetics, paint, and biomedical engineering.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Shear-Stress vs. Shear-Rate Relationship — The "Why"

When two parallel plates (area $A$, separated by distance $dy$) move relative to each other due to force $F$, the resulting internal resistance of the fluid is the **shear stress** $\tau = \dfrac{F}{A}$. The top plate moves with velocity $u$ and the bottom is stationary. The fluid between them undergoes **angular deformation** at a rate $\dfrac{du}{dy}$.

For **Newtonian** fluids, the molecular structure is simple — molecules slide past each other with a resistance that scales **linearly** with the speed of sliding. Hence the linear $\tau$–$\dot{\gamma}$ relation.

For **Non-Newtonian** fluids, the molecular structure is **complex** (long-chain polymers, suspended particles, flocculated colloids, etc.). The internal micro-structure changes under shear, leading to a **non-linear** relationship.

## 2.2 Classification of Non-Newtonian Fluids (Time-Independent)

These fluids are characterized by the **Generalized Power Law (Ostwald–de Waele model)**:

$$\tau = K \left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)^n$$

Where:
- $K$ = **Consistency index** (Pa·sⁿ)
- $n$ = **Flow behavior index** (dimensionless)

| Flow Behavior Index ($n$) | Fluid Type | Behavior | Common Examples |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| $n = 1$ | Newtonian | Viscosity constant | Water, air, oil |
| $n < 1$ | Pseudo-plastic (shear-thinning) | Viscosity decreases with shear | Blood, paint, ketchup, polymer melts |
| $n > 1$ | Dilatant (shear-thickening) | Viscosity increases with shear | Starch suspension, quicksand, wet sand |

### Apparent Viscosity

For a power-law fluid, the **apparent viscosity** $\mu_a$ is defined as:

$$\mu_a = \frac{\tau}{\left(\dfrac{du}{dy}\right)} = K \left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)^{n-1}$$

- For $n < 1$: $\mu_a$ **decreases** as shear rate increases (shear-thinning).
- For $n > 1$: $\mu_a$ **increases** as shear rate increases (shear-thickening).

## 2.3 Bingham Plastic Fluids (Visco-plastic)

These fluids behave like a solid until a critical **yield stress ($\tau_y$)** is exceeded. Beyond $\tau_y$, they flow with a constant **plastic viscosity ($\mu_p$)**.

$$\tau = \tau_y + \mu_p \frac{du}{dy} \quad \text{for} \quad \tau > \tau_y$$

$$\frac{du}{dy} = 0 \quad \text{for} \quad \tau \leq \tau_y$$

**Examples:** Toothpaste, drilling mud, sewage sludge, mayonnaise, fresh concrete.

## 2.4 Time-Dependent Non-Newtonian Fluids

| Type | Viscosity Behavior with Time | Examples |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Thixotropic** | Decreases with time of shearing (at constant shear rate) | Yogurt, honey, gels, some paints |
| **Rheopectic** | Increases with time of shearing (at constant shear rate) | Gypsum paste, printer's ink |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trick:** The mnemonic is "**R**heopectic = **R**ising viscosity" — viscosity **R**ises with time.

## 2.5 Viscoelastic Fluids

These exhibit both **viscous** (fluid) and **elastic** (solid) behavior. They partially recover their shape after the removal of shear stress.

**Examples:** Polymer solutions, dough, bitumen, human synovial fluid, some biofluids.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Equation | Symbols / Units | Applicable To |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | $\tau$ [Pa], $\mu$ [Pa·s], $\dot{\gamma}$ [s⁻¹] | Newtonian fluids |
| 2 | $\tau = K \left(\dfrac{du}{dy}\right)^n$ | $K$ [Pa·sⁿ], $n$ [–] | Power-law fluids |
| 3 | $\mu_a = K \left(\dfrac{du}{dy}\right)^{n-1}$ | $\mu_a$ [Pa·s] | Power-law fluids |
| 4 | $\tau = \tau_y + \mu_p \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | $\tau_y$ [Pa], $\mu_p$ [Pa·s] | Bingham plastic |
| 5 | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | $\nu$ [m²/s], $\rho$ [kg/m³] | Kinematic viscosity (Newtonian) |
| 6 | $n = 1 \Rightarrow$ Newtonian; $n < 1 \Rightarrow$ Pseudo-plastic; $n > 1 \Rightarrow$ Dilatant | – | Flow index check |
| 7 | $\mu_{rel} = \dfrac{\mu_a}{\mu_w}$ | Dimensionless | Viscosity ratio (rheology) |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Polymer Processing:** Pipe flow of molten plastics requires power-law design (most polymer melts are shear-thinning).
- **Biomedical Engineering:** Blood is pseudo-plastic — this affects needle-gauge design, IV flow, and dialysis.
- **Food Industry:** Chocolate, ketchup, and yogurt processing pipelines are designed with non-Newtonian rheology.
- **Civil Engineering:** Fresh concrete and drilling muds are Bingham plastics — yield stress must be overcome to initiate flow.
- **Cosmetics & Paints:** Shear-thinning ensures the product flows smoothly when brushed but does not drip from the brush.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Newton's Law of Viscosity from the Momentum-Flux Concept

We will derive the linear shear-stress–shear-rate relationship from kinetic theory of fluids. Consider a fluid between two parallel plates. The lower plate is stationary, the upper plate moves at velocity $u$ due to an applied force $F$ over area $A$.

**Step 1 — Define the geometry:**

Let the gap between the plates be $dy$, and the upper plate moves with velocity $u$ relative to the lower plate.

**Step 2 — Define rate of shear strain:**

The fluid layer at distance $dy$ moves with velocity $u$ relative to the stationary lower plate. The **rate of shear strain** $\dot{\gamma}$ is the angular deformation per unit time:

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{du}{dy}$$

**Step 3 — Newton's postulate:**

Newton postulated that the shear stress is proportional to the rate of shear strain:

$$\tau \propto \dot{\gamma}$$

**Step 4 — Introduce the constant of proportionality:**

The constant of proportionality is the **dynamic viscosity $\mu$**:

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \dot{\gamma} = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

**Step 5 — Dimensional check:**

$$[\mu] = \frac{[\tau]}{[\dot{\gamma}]} = \frac{\text{Pa}}{\text{s}^{-1}} = \text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}$$

This confirms that $\mu$ has the unit of **Pa·s**, also written as **N·s/m²** or **kg/(m·s)**. ∎

## 3.2 Derivation: Apparent Viscosity of a Power-Law Fluid

The generalized power-law (Ostwald–de Waele) equation is the **empirical model** that captures the non-linear behavior of many real fluids:

$$\tau = K \left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)^n$$

**Step 1 — Rearrange for $\dfrac{du}{dy}$:**

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \left(\frac{\tau}{K}\right)^{1/n}$$

**Step 2 — Define apparent viscosity as the ratio $\tau / \dot{\gamma}$:**

$$\mu_a = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}} = \frac{\tau}{\left(\dfrac{du}{dy}\right)}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the power-law relation:**

$$\mu_a = \frac{K \left(\dfrac{du}{dy}\right)^n}{\left(\dfrac{du}{dy}\right)}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the exponent algebraically:**

$$\mu_a = K \left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)^{n-1}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

- If $n = 1$: $\mu_a = K = \mu$ → **Newtonian** (viscosity constant).
- If $n < 1$: exponent $(n-1) < 0$ → as $\dot{\gamma}$ rises, $\mu_a$ **drops** → **pseudo-plastic**.
- If $n > 1$: exponent $(n-1) > 0$ → as $\dot{\gamma}$ rises, $\mu_a$ **rises** → **dilatant**. ∎

## 3.3 Derivation: Locating the Origin of a Bingham Plastic Curve

For a Bingham plastic, the curve of $\tau$ vs. $\dot{\gamma}$ is a **straight line that does NOT pass through the origin**; instead, it intersects the $\tau$-axis at the **yield stress $\tau_y$**.

**Step 1 — The constitutive equation:**

$$\tau = \tau_y + \mu_p \frac{du}{dy} \quad \text{for} \quad \tau > \tau_y$$

**Step 2 — Find the y-intercept (when $\dot{\gamma} = 0$):**

$$\tau \big|_{\dot{\gamma}=0} = \tau_y + 0 = \tau_y$$

So the curve intersects the shear-stress axis at $\tau = \tau_y$. ∎

## 3.4 Worked Example: Computing Apparent Viscosity of a Paint

**Problem:** A certain paint behaves as a power-law fluid with consistency index $K = 12 \,\text{Pa·s}^n$ and flow behavior index $n = 0.45$. If it is being sheared at a rate of $200 \,\text{s}^{-1}$, find the apparent viscosity and the shear stress.

**Step 1 — Use the apparent viscosity formula:**

$$\mu_a = K \left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)^{n-1}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$\mu_a = 12 \cdot (200)^{0.45 - 1} = 12 \cdot (200)^{-0.55}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $(200)^{-0.55}$:**

$$(200)^{-0.55} = \frac{1}{(200)^{0.55}} = \frac{1}{20.78} \approx 0.0481$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\mu_a$:**

$$\mu_a = 12 \cdot 0.0481 \approx 0.578 \,\text{Pa·s}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the shear stress:**

$$\tau = \mu_a \cdot \dot{\gamma} = 0.578 \cdot 200 = 115.6 \,\text{Pa}$$

**Result:** $\mu_a \approx 0.578 \,\text{Pa·s}$ and $\tau \approx 115.6 \,\text{Pa}$. ∎

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Because $n = 0.45 < 1$, this paint is **pseudo-plastic (shear-thinning)**. The viscosity is much lower under the high shear of brushing (200 s⁻¹) than at rest, which is exactly why paint flows easily when brushed but does not drip from a vertical surface.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Newtonian | Pseudo-plastic | Dilatant | Bingham Plastic |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| $\tau$–$\dot{\gamma}$ graph | Straight line, origin | Concave down (curve bends toward $\dot{\gamma}$-axis) | Concave up (curve bends toward $\tau$-axis) | Straight line, offset from origin |
| $n$ value | $n = 1$ | $n < 1$ | $n > 1$ | Not power-law |
| Apparent viscosity | Constant | Decreases with $\dot{\gamma}$ | Increases with $\dot{\gamma}$ | Constant for $\tau > \tau_y$ |
| Yield stress | None | None | None | $\tau_y > 0$ |
| Examples | Water, air, oil | Blood, paint, ketchup | Starch slurry, wet sand | Toothpaste, mud, concrete |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Classification of Fluids (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["FLUID CLASSIFICATION"] --> nodeB["NEWTONIAN FLUIDS"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["NON-NEWTONIAN FLUIDS"]

    nodeB --> nodeB1["tau equals mu times du by dy"]
    nodeB --> nodeB2["Viscosity constant with shear rate"]
    nodeB --> nodeB3["Water, Air, Oil, Mercury"]

    nodeC --> nodeD["Time-Independent"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["Time-Dependent"]
    nodeC --> nodeF["Viscoelastic"]

    nodeD --> nodeD1["Pseudo-plastic n less than 1"]
    nodeD --> nodeD2["Dilatant n greater than 1"]
    nodeD --> nodeD3["Bingham Plastic has yield stress"]
    nodeD --> nodeD4["Newtonian n equals 1"]

    nodeE --> nodeE1["Thixotropic viscosity drops with time"]
    nodeE --> nodeE2["Rheopectic viscosity rises with time"]

    nodeF --> nodeF1["Both viscous and elastic behavior"]
    nodeF --> nodeF2["Polymers, Dough, Bitumen"]

    nodeD1 --> nodeD1a["Blood, Paint, Ketchup"]
    nodeD2 --> nodeD2a["Starch, Quicksand, Wet Sand"]
    nodeD3 --> nodeD3a["Toothpaste, Mud, Concrete"]
    nodeE1 --> nodeE1a["Yogurt, Gels, Some Paints"]
    nodeE2 --> nodeE2a["Gypsum Paste, Printer Ink"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow — Shear-Stress vs. Shear-Rate Behavior Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    axisA["tau axis vertical"] --- pointO["Origin"]
    pointO --- axisB["du by dy axis horizontal"]

    axisA --> curveN["Line A: Newtonian, slope mu, passes through origin"]
    axisA --> curveP["Curve B: Pseudo-plastic, n less than 1, concave down"]
    axisA --> curveD["Curve C: Dilatant, n greater than 1, concave up"]
    axisA --> curveB["Line D: Bingham plastic, intercepts tau axis at tau_y"]

    curveN --- noteN["Constant viscosity at all shear rates"]
    curveP --- noteP["Viscosity decreases with shear"]
    curveD --- noteD["Viscosity increases with shear"]
    curveB --- noteB["Solid-like below yield stress, flows above"]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Block Diagram — Power-Law Fluid Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1["Given shear stress data for a fluid"] --> step2["Plot tau versus du by dy"]
    step2 --> step3{"Is the plot a straight line?"}
    step3 -- YES --> step4{"Does it pass through origin?"}
    step3 -- NO --> step5["Power-Law Fluid: tau equals K times dot gamma to the n"]

    step4 -- YES --> step6["Newtonian Fluid: mu equals slope"]
    step4 -- NO --> step7["Bingham Plastic: yield stress tau_y is the y-intercept"]

    step5 --> step8{"Value of n?"}
    step8 -- n less than 1 --> step9["Pseudo-plastic: shear-thinning"]
    step8 -- n greater than 1 --> step10["Dilatant: shear-thickening"]
    step8 -- n equals 1 --> step11["Reduces to Newtonian case"]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Differentiate between Newtonian and Non-Newtonian fluids with two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Newtonian Fluid | Non-Newtonian Fluid |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Definition | Obeys Newton's law of viscosity $\tau = \mu (du/dy)$ | Does NOT obey Newton's law of viscosity |
| $\tau$–$\dot{\gamma}$ relationship | Linear (straight line through origin) | Non-linear (or linear with non-zero intercept) |
| Viscosity behavior | Constant with shear rate | Varies with shear rate, time, or history |
| Example 1 | Water | Blood (pseudo-plastic) |
| Example 2 | Air | Toothpaste (Bingham plastic) |

**(3 marks: 1 for definition each + 1 for examples)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`
**Define the terms: (a) Apparent viscosity, (b) Yield stress, (c) Consistency index.**

**Model Answer:**

- **(a) Apparent Viscosity ($\mu_a$):** The ratio of shear stress to the rate of shear strain at a specific shear rate, used to characterize non-Newtonian fluids. For a power-law fluid, $\mu_a = K \left(\dfrac{du}{dy}\right)^{n-1}$.

- **(b) Yield Stress ($\tau_y$):** The minimum shear stress that must be exceeded before a Bingham plastic fluid begins to flow. Below $\tau_y$, the fluid behaves like a solid.

- **(c) Consistency Index ($K$):** A parameter in the power-law model $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^n$ that indicates the fluid's "thickness". Higher $K$ means a more viscous fluid. Units: **Pa·sⁿ**.

**(3 marks: 1 mark per term)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Derive Newton's law of viscosity from first principles and explain how it forms the basis for classifying fluids as Newtonian or non-Newtonian. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fluid obeys the power-law model with consistency index $K = 8.5 \,\text{Pa·s}^n$ and flow behavior index $n = 0.62$. Calculate **(i)** the apparent viscosity, **(ii)** the shear stress when the velocity gradient is $150 \,\text{s}^{-1}$, and **(iii)** identify the fluid type. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

1. **Setup:** Consider a fluid between two parallel plates, the lower one stationary, the upper one moving with velocity $u$ under an applied force $F$ over contact area $A$.

2. **Define shear stress:**
   $$\tau = \frac{F}{A} \quad [\text{Pa}]$$

3. **Define rate of shear strain:**
   $$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{du}{dy} \quad [\text{s}^{-1}]$$

4. **Newton's postulate:** $\tau \propto \dot{\gamma}$, hence:
   $$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

5. **Classification basis:** If $\mu$ is a **constant** (independent of $\dot{\gamma}$), the fluid is **Newtonian**. If $\mu$ varies with $\dot{\gamma}$, time, or shear history, the fluid is **non-Newtonian**.

6. **Graphical signature:** Newtonian fluids give a **straight line through origin**; non-Newtonian fluids give a **non-linear curve** or a **line with non-zero intercept**.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $K = 8.5 \,\text{Pa·s}^n$, $n = 0.62$, $\dot{\gamma} = 150 \,\text{s}^{-1}$.

**(i) Apparent viscosity:**

$$\mu_a = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-1} = 8.5 \cdot (150)^{0.62-1} = 8.5 \cdot (150)^{-0.38}$$

Compute $(150)^{-0.38}$:

$$(150)^{0.38} = e^{0.38 \cdot \ln 150} = e^{0.38 \cdot 5.011} = e^{1.904} \approx 6.713$$

$$\mu_a = \frac{8.5}{6.713} \approx 1.266 \,\text{Pa·s}$$

**(ii) Shear stress:**

$$\tau = \mu_a \cdot \dot{\gamma} = 1.266 \cdot 150 \approx 189.9 \,\text{Pa}$$

**(iii) Fluid type:** Since $n = 0.62 < 1$, the fluid is **pseudo-plastic (shear-thinning)**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a neatly labelled shear-stress vs. shear-rate plot, classify and describe the different types of non-Newtonian fluids. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Bingham plastic fluid has a yield stress of $42 \,\text{Pa}$ and a plastic viscosity of $0.85 \,\text{Pa·s}$. Compute the shear stress when the velocity gradient is $60 \,\text{s}^{-1}$ and determine whether the fluid actually flows under this condition. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

1. **Newtonian Fluid:** $\tau$ vs. $\dot{\gamma}$ is a **straight line through the origin**; slope $= \mu$. Example: water.

2. **Pseudo-plastic (shear-thinning):** $\tau$ vs. $\dot{\gamma}$ is a **concave-down curve** ($n < 1$); apparent viscosity decreases as $\dot{\gamma}$ increases. Example: blood, paint.

3. **Dilatant (shear-thickening):** $\tau$ vs. $\dot{\gamma}$ is a **concave-up curve** ($n > 1$); apparent viscosity increases with $\dot{\gamma}$. Example: starch suspension.

4. **Bingham Plastic:** $\tau$ vs. $\dot{\gamma}$ is a **straight line that does not pass through the origin**; it has a positive y-intercept $\tau_y$. Example: toothpaste, mud.

5. **Thixotropic / Rheopectic:** Time-dependent; viscosity respectively decreases or increases with time of shearing at constant $\dot{\gamma}$. Examples: yogurt (thixotropic), gypsum paste (rheopectic).

6. **Viscoelastic:** Combines viscous flow with elastic recovery. Example: bitumen, polymer melts.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $\tau_y = 42 \,\text{Pa}$, $\mu_p = 0.85 \,\text{Pa·s}$, $\dot{\gamma} = 60 \,\text{s}^{-1}$.

**Step 1 — Apply the Bingham plastic equation:**

$$\tau = \tau_y + \mu_p \frac{du}{dy}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$\tau = 42 + 0.85 \cdot 60 = 42 + 51 = 93 \,\text{Pa}$$

**Step 3 — Check the flow condition:**

For flow to occur, the **applied shear stress must exceed the yield stress**:

$$\tau_{applied} = 93 \,\text{Pa} > \tau_y = 42 \,\text{Pa}$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**

Since $93 \,\text{Pa} > 42 \,\text{Pa}$, the fluid **does flow**, and the computed shear stress is $\tau = 93 \,\text{Pa}$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up $K$ and $n$:** Students often confuse which parameter indicates fluid "thickness" ($K$) and which indicates the "behavior" ($n$). Remember: **K** is consistency (thicker = bigger K), **n** is the flow behavior.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units:** $\mu$ is in **Pa·s**, not in centipoise (cP). To convert: $1 \,\text{Pa·s} = 1000 \,\text{cP}$. The examiner **will** deduct marks for wrong units.
> 3. **Yield stress check:** For Bingham plastics, always verify that $\tau > \tau_y$ before applying the linear equation. A common mistake is using the formula even when the fluid is not flowing.
> 4. **Power-law exponent sign:** A negative exponent $(n-1)$ for pseudo-plastic fluids — students frequently drop the negative sign in their calculations.
> 5. **Sketching the graph:** In 7-mark questions, a **labelled graph is compulsory**. A purely textual answer loses 2–3 marks instantly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Newtonian fluid** obeys $\tau = \mu (du/dy)$ with $\mu$ a **constant**; plot is a **straight line through origin**.
- **Non-Newtonian fluid** has a **variable** apparent viscosity; plot is **non-linear** or has a **non-zero y-intercept**.
- **Power-law model:** $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^n$ — works for pseudo-plastic and dilatant fluids.
- **Flow behavior index $n$:**
  - $n = 1$ → Newtonian
  - $n < 1$ → pseudo-plastic (shear-thinning)
  - $n > 1$ → dilatant (shear-thickening)
- **Apparent viscosity of a power-law fluid:** $\mu_a = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$.
- **Bingham plastic:** $\tau = \tau_y + \mu_p \dot{\gamma}$ — has a **yield stress** below which it does not flow.
- **Time-dependent fluids:**
  - **Thixotropic** → viscosity **decreases** with time of shearing.
  - **Rheopectic** → viscosity **increases** with time of shearing.
- **Viscoelastic** → shows both **viscous flow** and **elastic recovery**.
- **Common examples for KTU:** Water/air (Newtonian), blood/paint (pseudo-plastic), starch (dilatant), toothpaste/mud (Bingham), yogurt (thixotropic), bitumen (viscoelastic).
- **Units to memorize:** $\mu$ → **Pa·s**, $K$ → **Pa·sⁿ**, $\tau$ and $\tau_y$ → **Pa**, $\dot{\gamma}$ → **s⁻¹**.
- **Conversion:** $1 \,\text{Pa·s} = 10 \,\text{P}$ (poise) $= 1000 \,\text{cP}$ (centipoise).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Real and Ideal Fluids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT741 - FLUID MECHANICS & MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Real and Ideal Fluids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of a Fluid

A **fluid** is a substance that continuously deforms (flows) under the action of an applied shear stress (or tangential force). Unlike a solid which resists deformation and maintains its shape, a fluid offers no resistance to static shear and will deform as long as the shear force is applied. Fluids include both **liquids** (essentially incompressible) and **gases** (highly compressible).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A fluid is a substance which deforms continuously under the action of an external force. Fluids include both liquids and gases. The branch of mechanics dealing with the behavior of fluids at rest is **Fluid Statics**, and the branch dealing with fluids in motion is **Fluid Kinematics / Fluid Dynamics**.

## 1.2 Real Fluids vs Ideal Fluids — The Fundamental Distinction

> [!NOTE]
> The distinction between **Real** and **Ideal** fluids is the most fundamental conceptual leap in Module 1 of Fluid Mechanics. Every subsequent equation, derivation, and engineering design hinges upon which fluid model we adopt.

### A) Ideal Fluid (Theoretical / Perfect Fluid)
An **ideal fluid** is an imaginary fluid that possesses the following idealized properties:

1. **Inviscid (Frictionless):** It has **zero viscosity**, meaning there is no resistance to shear deformation. No internal friction exists between adjacent fluid layers.
2. **Incompressible:** Its **density ($\rho$)** remains constant regardless of the pressure applied. The volume of any fluid element does not change under pressure.
3. **No Surface Tension:** The cohesive forces at the free surface are neglected.
4. **Homogeneous and Isotropic:** Properties are uniform throughout and identical in all directions.
5. **No Vapor Pressure Effects:** Cavitation and boiling phenomena are ignored.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why study ideal fluids?** Ideal fluid theory forms the foundation of classical **Euler's equations of motion**, **Bernoulli's equation**, and streamline analysis. It provides highly accurate solutions for many real-world flows (e.g., water flow far from boundaries, high-Reynolds-number air flow) and serves as the baseline against which real fluid losses (like friction) are later accounted for.

### B) Real Fluid (Practical / Actual Fluid)
A **real fluid** is any fluid that actually exists in nature. Real fluids possess **viscosity**, are slightly **compressible** (liquids very slightly, gases significantly), and exhibit **surface tension** at interfaces. All engineering fluids — water, air, oil, mercury, blood — are real fluids.

> [!WARNING]
> **Key Reality Check:** No real fluid is perfectly ideal. Even "low viscosity" fluids like water and air exhibit measurable viscous effects near solid boundaries (boundary layers). However, far from walls, water and air often behave nearly ideally, which is why ideal fluid theory is practically useful.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — Honey vs Water:**
> - **Honey** behaves like a **highly viscous real fluid** — when you tilt a jar, it slowly creeps down. It resists motion strongly because of internal friction (viscosity).
> - **Water** behaves like a **near-ideal fluid** — it slides easily with very little internal friction.
> - **A truly ideal fluid** would be a theoretical substance like "ghost water" that has absolutely no stickiness whatsoever — even less than water. It serves as a mathematical simplification, not a physical reality.

| Property | Ideal Fluid | Real Fluid |
|----------|-------------|------------|
| Viscosity ($\mu$) | **Zero ($\mu = 0$)** | **Non-zero ($\mu > 0$)** |
| Compressibility | **Zero (Incompressible)** | **Finite (liquids: low; gases: high)** |
| Surface Tension | **Zero** | **Non-zero** |
| Existence | **Imaginary (Mathematical)** | **Physical (Naturally occurring)** |

---

## 1.4 Classification of Real Fluids (Newton's Law Foundation)

Real fluids are classified based on the relationship between **shear stress ($\tau$)** and **rate of shear strain ($du/dy$)**:

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$$

where:
- $\tau$ = Shear stress in **N/m²** (Pa)
- $\mu$ = Dynamic (absolute) viscosity in **N·s/m²** or **Pa·s**
- $\frac{du}{dy}$ = Velocity gradient (rate of shear strain) in **s⁻¹**

### Newtonian Fluids
Fluids that obey Newton's law of viscosity with a **constant $\mu$** independent of the shear rate. Examples: **water, air, oil, mercury, alcohol**.

### Non-Newtonian Fluids
Fluids whose viscosity varies with shear rate. Examples: **blood, paint, mud, toothpaste, ketchup**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Highlight:** Module 1 explicitly requires understanding the difference between Newtonian and Non-Newtonian fluids. The shear stress vs rate of shear strain graph is a frequently asked 3-mark question.

---

## 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Properties to Remember (Bolded for Emphasis):**
> - **Density of water at 4°C:** $\rho_w$ = **1000 kg/m³**
> - **Specific weight of water:** $\gamma_w$ = **9810 N/m³** (or 9.81 kN/m³)
> - **Dynamic viscosity of water at 20°C:** $\mu_w$ ≈ **1.002 × 10⁻³ Pa·s**
> - **Kinematic viscosity of water at 20°C:** $\nu_w$ ≈ **1.004 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s**
> - **Dynamic viscosity of air at 20°C:** $\mu_{air}$ ≈ **1.81 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s**
> - **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $P_{atm}$ = **101.325 kPa** = **1.01325 bar**
> - **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g$ = **9.81 m/s²**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Properties of Fluids — The Complete Engineering Inventory

To fully characterize any fluid (ideal or real) for engineering analysis, we must quantify its physical and mechanical properties. Below is the exhaustive theoretical breakdown required by the KTU 2024 Module 1 syllabus.

### 2.1.1 Density ($\rho$)

**Density** is defined as the mass of fluid per unit volume. It is a fundamental intrinsic property.

$$\rho = \frac{dm}{dV} \quad \text{[kg/m³]}$$

For homogeneous fluids: $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$.

**Real fluid behavior:** Gases — density varies significantly with pressure and temperature (compressible). Liquids — density is nearly constant for moderate pressure ranges.

### 2.1.2 Specific Weight ($\gamma$)

**Specific weight** is the weight of fluid per unit volume.

$$\gamma = \rho \cdot g \quad \text{[N/m³]}$$

where $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**.

### 2.1.3 Specific Gravity ($S$ or $SG$)

**Specific gravity** is a dimensionless ratio comparing a fluid's density to that of water at a standard reference temperature (typically 4°C).

$$S = \frac{\rho_{fluid}}{\rho_{water @ 4°C}} = \frac{\gamma_{fluid}}{\gamma_{water}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineer's Trick:** Specific gravity has no units! For mercury, $S_{Hg} \approx 13.6$. For seawater, $S \approx 1.025$. This means mercury is **13.6 times denser** than water.

### 2.1.4 Viscosity — The Most Critical Property

**Viscosity** is the property of a fluid that offers resistance to deformation (shear) under applied shear stress. It arises from internal molecular friction and momentum transfer between adjacent fluid layers.

**Dynamic Viscosity ($\mu$):**

$$\mu = \frac{\tau}{\left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)} \quad \text{[Pa·s] = [N·s/m²] = [kg/(m·s)]}$$

**Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$):**

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} \quad \text{[m²/s]}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Dynamic viscosity $\mu$ is the **resistance to shear**, while kinematic viscosity $\nu$ is the **resistance to flow under the influence of gravity**. The Reynolds number (covered in Module 2) uses $\nu$.

**Variation of Viscosity with Temperature:**
- **Liquids:** Viscosity **decreases** with increasing temperature (e.g., honey flows easier when warmed).
- **Gases:** Viscosity **increases** with increasing temperature (momentum transfer increases with molecular agitation).

### 2.1.5 Surface Tension ($\sigma$)

**Surface tension** is the property of a liquid surface that behaves like a stretched elastic membrane. It arises from the cohesive forces between liquid molecules at the free surface.

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{L} \quad \text{[N/m]}$$

where $F$ = surface tension force, $L$ = length of the imaginary line on the surface.

**Pressure difference across a curved liquid interface (Young-Laplace Equation):**

$$\Delta P = \sigma \left( \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} \right)$$

For a spherical droplet of radius $R$: $\Delta P = \dfrac{2\sigma}{R}$ and for a soap bubble (two surfaces): $\Delta P = \dfrac{4\sigma}{R}$.

### 2.1.6 Capillarity

**Capillarity** is the phenomenon of liquid rising (or falling) in a thin tube due to surface tension. The capillary rise (or depression) is given by:

$$h = \frac{4\sigma \cos\theta}{\rho \cdot g \cdot d}$$

where:
- $\theta$ = contact angle between liquid and tube
- $d$ = diameter of the capillary tube
- $\sigma$ = surface tension

> [!NOTE]
> Water rises in glass tubes ($\theta < 90°$, typically $\theta \approx 0°$ for clean glass), while mercury is depressed ($\theta > 90°$).

### 2.1.7 Vapor Pressure ($P_v$)

**Vapor pressure** is the pressure exerted by the vapor of a liquid in equilibrium with its liquid phase at a given temperature. It increases with temperature.

> [!WARNING]
> **Cavitation Phenomenon:** When local static pressure in a flowing liquid drops below its vapor pressure, the liquid vaporizes and forms bubbles. These bubbles collapse violently when they move to higher-pressure regions, causing severe damage to pump impellers, turbine blades, and valves. This is **cavitation** and is a major design concern in hydraulic machinery (Modules 4 & 5).

### 2.1.8 Compressibility and Bulk Modulus ($K$)

**Compressibility** ($\beta$) is the fractional reduction in volume per unit increase in pressure.

$$\beta = -\frac{dV/V}{dP} = \frac{1}{V}\left(-\frac{dV}{dP}\right) \quad \text{[Pa⁻¹]}$$

The **Bulk Modulus** $K$ is the reciprocal of compressibility:

$$K = \frac{1}{\beta} = -V \cdot \frac{dP}{dV} \quad \text{[Pa]}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ideal Fluid:** $K = \infty$ (perfectly incompressible). **Real Liquids:** $K$ is very high (water $K \approx 2.2$ GPa). **Real Gases:** $K$ is much lower and pressure-dependent.

---

## 2.2 Continuum Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Continuum Hypothesis:** Fluid properties (density, velocity, pressure, temperature) are treated as continuous functions of space and time, even though fluids are composed of discrete molecules. This allows us to use differential calculus (Eulerian description) for fluid analysis.

**Justification:** The smallest engineering volume of interest (e.g., 1 mm³ of water) still contains $\sim 10^{19}$ molecules — enough to treat the fluid as a continuous medium. Exceptions occur in rarefied gas dynamics (very high altitude, MEMS devices), which are beyond KTU scope.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Property | Formula | Units | Real Fluid Value (Water @ 4°C) | Ideal Fluid Value |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------------------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Density | $\rho = m/V$ | kg/m³ | **1000 kg/m³** | Constant |
| 2 | Specific Weight | $\gamma = \rho \cdot g$ | N/m³ | **9810 N/m³** | Constant |
| 3 | Specific Gravity | $S = \rho_{fluid}/\rho_{water}$ | Dimensionless | **1.0** | — |
| 4 | Dynamic Viscosity | $\mu = \tau \cdot (dy/du)$ | Pa·s | **1.002 × 10⁻³** | **0** |
| 5 | Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu = \mu/\rho$ | m²/s | **1.004 × 10⁻⁶** | **0** |
| 6 | Newton's Law | $\tau = \mu \cdot (du/dy)$ | Pa | Applies | Does **NOT** apply |
| 7 | Surface Tension | $\sigma = F/L$ | N/m | **0.0728 N/m** | **0** |
| 8 | Capillary Rise | $h = 4\sigma\cos\theta/(\rho g d)$ | m | Depends on $d$ | **0** (no capillarity) |
| 9 | Vapor Pressure | $P_v$ (table lookup) | Pa | **2.34 kPa** @ 20°C | Ignored |
| 10 | Bulk Modulus | $K = -dP/(dV/V)$ | Pa | **2.2 GPa** | **∞** |
| 11 | Compressibility | $\beta = 1/K$ | Pa⁻¹ | **4.55 × 10⁻¹⁰** | **0** |
| 12 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \rho V L / \mu = VL/\nu$ | Dimensionless | Depends on $V$, $L$ | Conceptually infinite (no viscous losses) |

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Tip:** Memorize the water properties at 4°C (the temperature of maximum density). Examiners love to ask: *"What is the density of water at 4°C?"* — Answer: **1000 kg/m³ exactly** (by definition of the SI unit kilogram historically).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

1. **Hydraulic Engineering (Dams, Canals, Pipelines):** Real fluid viscosity determines frictional head loss in pipes and open channels — critical for pump and pipe sizing.
2. **Aerospace Engineering (Aircraft, Rockets):** Air at high speeds behaves nearly as an ideal fluid in regions away from the surface (inviscid flow), but viscosity matters in the boundary layer for drag prediction.
3. **Biomedical Engineering (Blood Flow):** Blood is a **non-Newtonian real fluid** — its viscosity depends on shear rate, which affects arterial flow modeling.
4. **Hydraulic Machines (Pumps, Turbines):** Vapor pressure governs **cavitation** limits in pumps (covered in Modules 4 & 5).
5. **Chemical Process Industries:** Viscosity and surface tension govern atomization, spray dynamics, and heat transfer rates.
6. **Civil Engineering (Capillarity):** Capillary rise causes moisture rise in building foundations — important in soil mechanics and structural durability.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Newton's Law of Viscosity (from Molecular Momentum Transport)

### Setup
Consider a fluid flowing between two parallel plates separated by a small distance $dy$. The bottom plate is stationary; the top plate moves with velocity $u$ in the $x$-direction. Adhesion causes the fluid layer touching the top plate to move at $u$, and the fluid touching the bottom plate is at rest.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Define the **rate of shear strain** as the change in angle $d\theta$ per unit time:

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{d\theta}{dt}$$

**Step 2:** For small deformations, $\tan(d\theta) \approx d\theta$:

$$d\theta = \frac{du \cdot dt}{dy}$$

**Step 3:** Therefore, the rate of shear strain is:

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{d\theta}{dt} = \frac{du}{dy} \quad \text{[s}^{-1}\text{]}$$

**Step 4:** According to Newton's postulate, the shear stress $\tau$ is **directly proportional** to the rate of shear strain:

$$\tau \propto \frac{du}{dy}$$

**Step 5:** Introducing the proportionality constant $\mu$ (dynamic viscosity):

$$\boxed{\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is **Newton's Law of Viscosity**. Fluids obeying this law (with $\mu$ constant) are **Newtonian fluids**. For non-Newtonian fluids, $\mu$ becomes a function of $du/dy$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Continuity Equation for an Incompressible Fluid

### Setup
Consider a rectangular control volume (CV) of dimensions $dx \times dy \times dz$ with fluid of density $\rho$ flowing through it. Let velocity components be $u$, $v$, $w$ along $x$, $y$, $z$ respectively.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Mass flow rate entering the CV along the x-direction (left face):**

$$\dot{m}_{in,x} = \rho \cdot u \cdot dy \cdot dz$$

**Step 2: Mass flow rate leaving the CV along the x-direction (right face):**

Since velocity changes by $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x}dx$ across the small length $dx$:

$$\dot{m}_{out,x} = \rho \left( u + \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} dx \right) dy \cdot dz$$

**Step 3: Net rate of mass accumulation along x-direction:**

$$\left(\dot{m}_{in,x} - \dot{m}_{out,x}\right) = -\rho \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} dx \cdot dy \cdot dz$$

**Step 4: Repeating for y and z directions:**

$$\text{Net (y)} = -\rho \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} dy \cdot dx \cdot dz$$

$$\text{Net (z)} = -\rho \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} dz \cdot dx \cdot dy$$

**Step 5: Total rate of mass accumulation inside the CV:**

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} \cdot dx \cdot dy \cdot dz = -\rho \left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} \right) dx \cdot dy \cdot dz$$

**Step 6: Dividing throughout by $dx \cdot dy \cdot dz$:**

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \rho \left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} \right) = 0}$$

This is the **General Continuity Equation (Differential Form)**.

**Step 7: For Steady Flow** ($\partial \rho/\partial t = 0$) **and Incompressible Flow** ($\rho$ = constant):

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For ideal (incompressible) fluids in steady flow, the velocity field is **divergence-free** — this is a foundational result used to derive Bernoulli's equation and to solve stream function / velocity potential problems.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Relationship Between Dynamic and Kinematic Viscosity

### Setup
Dynamic viscosity $\mu$ has units of Pa·s (kg/m·s). Density $\rho$ has units of kg/m³.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Define kinematic viscosity as:

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the SI units:

$$\left[ \nu \right] = \frac{\left[ \text{kg/(m·s)} \right]}{\left[ \text{kg/m}^3 \right]} = \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m} \cdot \text{s}} \cdot \frac{\text{m}^3}{\text{kg}} = \frac{\text{m}^2}{\text{s}}$$

**Step 3:** Hence proved:

$$\boxed{\nu \text{ has units of m}^2/\text{s}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** $\mu$ is the absolute resistance to flow (force-based). $\nu$ is the resistance to flow under the action of gravity (momentum-diffusion based). $\nu$ governs the rate at which momentum diffuses through the fluid.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Ideal vs Real Fluid Flow Equations

| Governing Equation | Ideal Fluid Form | Real Fluid Form | Extra Term |
|--------------------|------------------|------------------|------------|
| Newton's Law | $\tau = 0$ | $\tau = \mu(du/dy)$ | Viscous term |
| Euler's Equation | $\rho \dfrac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = -\nabla P + \rho \vec{g}$ | — | — |
| Navier-Stokes | — | $\rho \dfrac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = -\nabla P + \mu \nabla^2 \vec{V} + \rho \vec{g}$ | Viscous term $\mu \nabla^2 \vec{V}$ |
| Bernoulli's | $\dfrac{P}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V^2}{2g} + z = \text{const.}$ | $\dfrac{P_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \dfrac{P_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$ | Head loss $h_L$ |
| Continuity | $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$ | $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$ (incompressible) | — |

> [!TIP]
> **The Navier-Stokes equation is the real-fluid equivalent of Euler's equation for ideal fluids.** The extra $\mu \nabla^2 \vec{V}$ term accounts for viscous diffusion of momentum — the heart of CFD (Computational Fluid Dynamics).

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Application)

### Problem:
A Newtonian fluid fills the gap of 5 mm between two parallel horizontal plates. The bottom plate is fixed; the top plate moves at 0.25 m/s and requires a shear stress of 2.5 Pa to maintain this velocity. Determine:
(a) The dynamic viscosity $\mu$ of the fluid.
(b) The kinematic viscosity $\nu$, given that the fluid density is 950 kg/m³.
(c) State whether the fluid is real or ideal.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Given:**
- Gap thickness: $dy = 5$ mm $= 0.005$ m
- Velocity of top plate: $u = 0.25$ m/s
- Shear stress: $\tau = 2.5$ Pa
- Density: $\rho = 950$ kg/m³

**Part (a):** Using Newton's law of viscosity:

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$$

$$2.5 = \mu \cdot \frac{0.25 - 0}{0.005}$$

$$2.5 = \mu \cdot 50$$

$$\boxed{\mu = \frac{2.5}{50} = 0.05 \text{ Pa·s}}$$

**Part (b):** Kinematic viscosity:

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{0.05}{950}$$

$$\boxed{\nu = 5.263 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}}$$

**Part (c):** Since the fluid exhibits a non-zero viscosity ($\mu = 0.05$ Pa·s) and requires shear stress to maintain motion, **it is a REAL fluid**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4.2 Classification of Fluids — Conceptual Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    START["START: Any Fluid Substance"] --> Q1{"Does it obey Newton's Law<br/>tau = mu x du/dy<br/>with CONSTANT mu?"}

    Q1 -->|YES| NEWT["NEWTONIAN FLUIDS<br/>Examples: Water, Air, Mercury, Oil, Alcohol, Glycerin"]
    Q1 -->|NO| NONNEWT["NON-NEWTONIAN FLUIDS<br/>mu varies with du/dy"]

    NONNEWT --> Q2{"How does apparent<br/>viscosity change?"}

    Q2 -->|DECREASES with shear rate| SH["SHEAR-THINNING<br/>Pseudoplastic<br/>Examples: Blood, Paint, Ketchup"]
    Q2 -->|INCREASES with shear rate| ST["SHEAR-THICKENING<br/>Dilatant<br/>Examples: Cornstarch + Water, Wet Sand"]
    Q2 -->|Requires yield stress to flow| YS["BINGHAM PLASTIC<br/>Examples: Toothpaste, Drilling Mud, Sewage Sludge"]
    Q2 -->|No yield stress but shear-thinning| TH["THIXOTROPIC / RHEOPECTIC<br/>Examples: Gelatin, Printer Ink"]

    NEWT --> IDEAL_Q{"Is mu = 0<br/>and<br/>incompressible?"}

    IDEAL_Q -->|YES| IDEAL["IDEAL FLUID<br/>Imaginary, mathematical model<br/>No viscous effects"]

```

## 4.4 Force / Stress Diagram on a Fluid Element

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ELEMENT["CUBICAL FLUID ELEMENT<br/>Dimensions: dx, dy, dz<br/>Center: O"]

    subgraph PRESSURE_FORCES["NORMAL STRESSES (PRESSURE)"]
        P1["P at face x minus dx/2<br/>acting in plus x direction"]
        P2["P at face x plus dx/2<br/>acting in minus x direction"]
        P3["P at face y minus dy/2"]
        P4["P at face y plus dy/2"]
        P5["P at face z minus dz/2"]
        P6["P at face z plus dz/2"]
    end

    subgraph SHEAR_FORCES["TANGENTIAL STRESSES (VISCOSITY)"]
        S1["tau_yx on faces perpendicular to x"]
        S2["tau_xy on faces perpendicular to y"]
        S3["tau_zx and tau_xz on z faces"]
        S4["tau_yz and tau_zy on y faces"]
    end

    subgraph BODY_FORCES["BODY FORCES"]
        BF1["Gravity: rho x g x dV<br/>acts in minus z direction"]
        BF2["Other: Electromagnetic, Centrifugal, etc."]
    end

    P1 --> ELEMENT
    P2 --> ELEMENT
    P3 --> ELEMENT
    P4 --> ELEMENT
    P5 --> ELEMENT
    P6 --> ELEMENT
    S1 -->|"Real fluid only"| ELEMENT
    S2 -->|"Real fluid only"| ELEMENT
    S3 -->|"Real fluid only"| ELEMENT
    S4 -->|"Real fluid only"| ELEMENT
    BF1 --> ELEMENT
    BF2 --> ELEMENT

    NOTE["Ideal fluid: ALL shear stresses = 0<br/>Only pressure and body forces act"]
    S1 -.->|"ZERO for ideal fluid"| NOTE
    S2 -.->|"ZERO for ideal fluid"| NOTE
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visual Insight:** The diagrams above emphasize that for an **ideal fluid**, only **pressure** (normal stresses) and **body forces** like gravity act on a fluid element — there are **no shear stresses at all**. For a **real fluid**, additional viscous shear stresses appear on every face. This single insight separates the two models mathematically.
