KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define the following with proper units. *(3 Marks)* (a) Absolute Pressure (b) Gauge Pressure (c) Vacuum Pressure **Model Answer:** > **(a) Absolute Pressure:** The total pressure measured with reference to a perfect vacuum (absolute zero) as the datum. It is the sum of atmospheric pressure and gauge pressure. **Unit:** Pa, kPa, bar, psi. > > **(b) Gauge Pressure:** The pressure measured with reference to the local atmospheric pressure as the datum. It is positive when above atmospheric and equal to the difference $P_{abs} - P_{atm}$. **Unit:** Pa, kPa, bar, psi. > > **(c) Vacuum Pressure:** The magnitude of pressure below atmospheric pressure. It equals $P_{atm} - P_{abs}$ when $P_{abs} < P_{atm}$. It is essentially the negative of gauge pressure. **Unit:** Pa, kPa, bar, psi. --- ### Question 2: State Pascal's Law and give any two of its practical applications. *(3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** > **Pascal's Law:** "A pressure change occurring anywhere in a confined incompressible static fluid is transmitted equally and undiminished to all portions of the fluid and to the walls of the container." > > **Two Applications:** > 1. **Hydraulic Jack / Lift:** Used in automobile service stations to lift cars using a small effort. > 2. **Hydraulic Brakes:** Used in automobiles and aircraft for transmitting brake pedal force equally to all wheel cylinders. > > *(Other acceptable answers: hydraulic press, power steering, hydraulic crane, hydraulic punch.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) **(a) Derive the relationship between absolute pressure, gauge pressure, and atmospheric pressure. Show the cases of positive gauge pressure and vacuum pressure with neat sketches.** *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Definitions:** - **Absolute Pressure ($P_{abs}$):** Pressure measured from a perfect vacuum (zero) reference. - **Atmospheric Pressure ($P_{atm}$):** Pressure exerted by the weight of the earth's atmosphere. At sea level, $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa. - **Gauge Pressure ($P_{gauge}$):** Pressure measured relative to atmospheric pressure. **Step 2 — Derivation:** Consider a closed vessel containing a fluid at absolute pressure $P_{abs}$. The walls of the vessel experience an outward force from the inside fluid and an inward force from the outside atmosphere. The *net* force per unit area is the **gauge pressure**: $$P_{gauge} = P_{abs} - P_{atm}$$ Rearranging: $$P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$$ **Step 3 — Case 1: Positive Gauge Pressure ($P_{abs} > P_{atm}$):** The fluid inside is at a higher pressure than the surrounding atmosphere. This is typical of compressed gas cylinders, hydraulic lines, boilers, and inflated tires. **Sketch (Schematic):** ``` +---------------------+ | FLUID at Pabs | ----> outward push +---------------------+ <---- Patm (inward) Net push outward = Pgauge = Pabs - Patm ``` **Step 4 — Case 2: Vacuum Pressure ($P_{abs} < P_{atm}$):** The fluid inside is at a lower pressure than the atmosphere. The atmospheric pressure outside exceeds the inside pressure, so the *net* force is inward. The magnitude of the deficit is the vacuum pressure: $$P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs} = -P_{gauge}$$ This occurs in condensers, suction side of pumps, and vacuum chambers. **Sketch (Schematic):** ``` +---------------------+ | FLUID at Pabs | <---- inward push (net) +---------------------+ ----> Patm (outward) Net push inward = Pvac = Patm - Pabs ``` **Step 5 — Numerical Illustration:** A compressed air cylinder shows a gauge reading of 800 kPa. Find absolute pressure. $$P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge} = 101.325 + 800 = 901.325 \text{ kPa}$$ --- **(b) A hydraulic press has a ram of 30 cm diameter and a plunger of 5 cm diameter. It is used to lift a load of 50 kN. Determine:** *(7 Marks)* - (i) The pressure developed in the press fluid. - (ii) The effort required at the plunger. - (iii) The distance moved by the plunger to raise the load by 15 cm. **Model Solution:** **Given:** - Ram diameter $D_2 = 30 \text{ cm} = 0.30 \text{ m}$ - Plunger diameter $D_1 = 5 \text{ cm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$ - Load $W = F_2 = 50 \text{ kN} = 50{,}000 \text{ N}$ - Distance lifted $d_2 = 15 \text{ cm} = 0.15 \text{ m}$ **Step 1 — Compute areas:** $$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} D_1^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.05)^2 = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$ $$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} D_2^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.30)^2 = 7.0686 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^2$$ **Part (i) — Pressure in the fluid:** By Pascal's Law, the pressure under the ram equals the load divided by the ram area: $$P = \frac{F_2}{A_2} = \frac{50{,}000}{7.0686 \times 10^{-2}} = 707.36 \times 10^3 \text{ Pa} = 707.36 \text{ kPa}$$ **Part (ii) — Effort required at plunger:** The same pressure acts on the plunger: $$F_1 = P \cdot A_1 = 707.36 \times 10^3 \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$F_1 = 1389.0 \text{ N} \approx 1.389 \text{ kN}$$ **Alternative using the ratio formula:** $$F_1 = F_2 \cdot \frac{A_1}{A_2} = 50{,}000 \times \frac{1.9635 \times 10^{-3}}{7.0686 \times 10^{-2}}$$ $$F_1 = 50{,}000 \times 0.02778 = 1389.0 \text{ N}$$ **Part (iii) — Distance moved by plunger:** By incompressibility: $A_1 d_1 = A_2 d_2$ $$d_1 = d_2 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1} = 0.15 \times \frac{7.0686 \times 10^{-2}}{1.9635 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$d_1 = 0.15 \times 36 = 5.40 \text{ m}$$ **Results Summary:** | Quantity | Value | |----------|-------| | Pressure in fluid | 707.36 kPa | | Effort at plunger | 1389 N (1.389 kN) | | Plunger distance | 5.40 m | | Mechanical Advantage | 36 | --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **(a) With a neat sketch, explain the working of a hydraulic jack. State Pascal's Law. Derive the expression for mechanical advantage.** *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Pascal's Law Statement:** "A pressure change occurring anywhere in a confined incompressible static fluid is transmitted equally and undiminished to all portions of the fluid and to the walls of the container." **Step 2 — Working of a Hydraulic Jack (Sketch & Description):** ``` [Lever arm] [Load W] | | v v +-------+ +-----------+ | | | | | F1 | | W | | | | | +---+---+ +-----+-----+ | | v v =======+==========OIL============+========= A1 (small plunger) A2 (large ram) ^ ^ |______PASCAL'S LAW_____| Pressure P = F1/A1 = W/A2 ``` **Components:** - A small plunger (piston) of area $A_1$ where effort $F_1$ is applied (often through a lever arm for additional mechanical advantage). - A large ram (piston) of area $A_2$ on which the load $W$ rests. - An incompressible fluid (hydraulic oil) connecting both pistons in a sealed chamber. - A check valve to prevent backflow and allow incremental lifting. **Working Principle:** When effort $F_1$ is applied on the small plunger, pressure $P = F_1/A_1$ is generated. By Pascal's Law, this same pressure acts on the large ram, producing a lifting force $W = P \cdot A_2$. Since $A_2 \gg A_1$, a small effort lifts a large load. **Step 3 — Derivation of Mechanical Advantage:** $$\frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{W}{A_2}$$ $$\frac{W}{F_1} = \frac{A_2}{A_1}$$ $$\boxed{MA = \frac{A_2}{A_1} = \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^2}$$ **Numerical example (for completeness):** If $D_1 = 2$ cm, $D_2 = 20$ cm, then $MA = (20/2)^2 = 100$. A 50 N effort lifts $50 \times 100 = 5000$ N. --- **(b) A hydraulic brake system has a master cylinder of 5 cm diameter and four wheel cylinders each of 8 cm diameter. If the pedal force applied is 250 N, find:** *(7 Marks)* - (i) The pressure generated in the master cylinder. - (ii) The braking force developed at each wheel cylinder. **Model Solution:** **Given:** - Master cylinder diameter $D_m = 5 \text{ cm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$ - Wheel cylinder diameter $D_w = 8 \text{ cm} = 0.08 \text{ m}$ - Pedal force $F_m = 250 \text{ N}$ - Number of wheel cylinders: 4 **Step 1 — Compute areas:** $$A_m = \frac{\pi}{4} D_m^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.05)^2 = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$ $$A_w = \frac{\pi}{4} D_w^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.08)^2 = 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$ **Part (i) — Pressure in master cylinder:** $$P = \frac{F_m}{A_m} = \frac{250}{1.9635 \times 10^{-3}} = 127.32 \times 10^3 \text{ Pa} = 127.32 \text{ kPa}$$ **Part (ii) — Braking force at each wheel cylinder:** By Pascal's Law, the same pressure acts on each wheel cylinder: $$F_w = P \cdot A_w = 127.32 \times 10^3 \times 5.0265 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$F_w = 639.97 \text{ N} \approx 640 \text{ N}$$ **Total braking force from all 4 wheels:** $$F_{total} = 4 \times 640 = 2560 \text{ N} = 2.56 \text{ kN}$$ **Per-wheel force amplification:** $$\frac{F_w}{F_m} = \frac{A_w}{A_m} = \frac{5.0265 \times 10^{-3}}{1.9635 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.56$$ So each wheel cylinder produces 2.56 times the pedal force. **Results Summary:** | Quantity | Value | |----------|-------| | Pressure in system | 127.32 kPa | | Force per wheel cylinder | 640 N | | Total braking force (4 wheels) | 2560 N | | Force amplification ratio per wheel | 2.56 | --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (Read Carefully Before Exam):** > > 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Forgetting to convert mm → m before computing $\pi D^2/4$. This alone causes a 50–70% calculation error. > 2. **Confusing Gauge vs Absolute:** A Bourdon gauge reads *gauge*, not absolute. State which one your answer represents. > 3. **Mixing Up Force Ratios:** Students often write $F_2 = F_1 \cdot A_1/A_2$ (inverted). Remember: **larger area = larger force**. > 4. **Neglecting the Work Equation:** Examiner often gives 1–2 marks for the energy balance check ($A_1 d_1 = A_2 d_2$). > 5. **Pascal's Law Preconditions:** Mentioning the fluid must be *incompressible* and *static* — students skip this and lose marks. > 6. **Sign Convention for Vacuum:** When $P_{abs} < P_{atm}$, write it explicitly; do not just write a negative number without labeling. > 7. **Sketch Quality:** A simple labeled schematic in a 7-mark question can fetch 1–2 easy marks. Always include a sketch. > 8. **No Intermediate Steps:** Show $A_1$, $A_2$, $P$, and substitution separately. Do not jump directly to the final number. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### Rapid Revision Checklist - **Pressure is a scalar**, not a vector. It acts normal to surfaces in all directions. - **Three Reference Datums:** Absolute vacuum (for $P_{abs}$), local atmosphere (for $P_{gauge}$ or $P_{vac}$), and arbitrary local datum (for differential pressure). - **The Master Equation:** $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$ — never forget this. Rearrange as needed. - **Vacuum Pressure exists only when** $P_{abs} < P_{atm}$, and equals $P_{atm} - P_{abs}$. - **Standard Atmosphere:** $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa = 1.01325 bar = 760 mmHg = 10.332 m H₂O. Memorize at least the bar and kPa values. - **Pascal's Law requires:** (i) Confined fluid, (ii) Incompressible fluid, (iii) Static equilibrium. - **Pascal's Law Statement:** Pressure is transmitted *equally* and *undiminished* in *all directions*. Mention all three adverbs. - **Hydraulic Press Formula:** $F_2/F_1 = A_2/A_1 = (D_2/D_1)^2$ (for circular pistons). - **Force × Distance is constant:** Force is amplified, but distance is reduced by the same factor — energy is conserved. - **Typical Conversions:** $1 \text{ bar} = 100 \text{ kPa}$, $1 \text{ psi} = 6.895 \text{ kPa}$, $1 \text{ atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$. - **Engineering Use:** Thermodynamics uses **absolute**; mechanical gauges read **gauge**; condensers/suction operate in **vacuum**. - **Common Applications to List:** Hydraulic jack, hydraulic press, hydraulic brakes, power steering, hydraulic crane, pressure intensifier. - **Key Difference Between $P$ and $p$ in Some Texts:** Capital $P$ often denotes total/stagnation pressure; lowercase $p$ denotes static. In KTU Module 1, both generally mean static pressure. - **Sample Numerical Pattern (always appears in KTU):** Diameter/area conversion → pressure calculation → force calculation → distance/MA check. Practice this exact flow. ### High-Frequency Examiner Keywords (use these in answers for bonus marks) *"transmitted equally and undiminished"* • *"incompressible static fluid"* • *"perfect vacuum datum"* • *"mechanical advantage = A₂/A₁"* • *"volume conservation"* • *"force-distance trade-off"* • *"Pascal's Law"* • *"hydraulic multiplication"* ---

topics.title: Measurement of Pressure: Piezometer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT741 - FLUID MECHANICS & MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Pressure: Piezometer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Fluid Pressure?

In fluid statics, **pressure** is defined as the normal force exerted by a fluid per unit area on a surface. Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$P = \frac{dF}{dA}$$

where $F$ is the normal force and $A$ is the area over which the force acts. The SI unit of pressure is **Pascal (Pa = N/m²)**, and it is a **scalar quantity** acting equally in all directions at a point (Pascal's Law).

There are three primary classifications of pressure that a B.Tech student must master for the KTU examination:

1. **Atmospheric Pressure ($P_{atm}$):** The pressure exerted by the weight of the Earth's atmosphere. At mean sea level, standard atmospheric pressure is **101.325 kPa** (or **1.01325 bar**).
2. **Gauge Pressure ($P_{gauge}$):** The pressure measured relative to local atmospheric pressure. $P_{gauge} = P_{absolute} - P_{atm}$.
3. **Absolute Pressure ($P_{abs}$):** The total pressure measured with respect to a perfect vacuum (true zero reference). $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:** Whenever a piezometer or manometer problem is solved, you MUST clearly state whether the pressure computed is *gauge* or *absolute*. Piezometers inherently measure **gauge pressure** because the liquid column rises above the local atmospheric pressure.

## 1.2 The Piezometer — Formal Definition

A **Piezometer** is the simplest, most direct device used for measuring the **static pressure** of a fluid at a point within a pipeline, tank, or vessel. It consists of a **transparent vertical tube** (usually made of glass or clear acrylic) that is connected (or inserted) directly into the fluid container whose pressure is to be measured. The fluid rises into this tube, and the vertical height of this fluid column is a direct measure of the gauge pressure at the point of insertion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A piezometer is a pressure-measuring instrument consisting of a small-diameter vertical tube open to the atmosphere, connected to a vessel or pipeline containing a fluid, in which the liquid rises to a height directly proportional to the pressure at the point of connection.

## 1.3 The Intuitive Analogy — "The Straw in Your Juice Box"

Imagine you are drinking from a sealed juice box using a straw. When you suck on the straw, you reduce the pressure inside your mouth below atmospheric. The atmospheric pressure pushing down on the juice surface forces the juice up the straw. 

A piezometer works on a very similar principle, but in **reverse**:
- The fluid inside the tank has pressure higher than atmospheric.
- This pressure pushes the fluid **up** the open vertical tube.
- The fluid stops rising when the **weight of the column it has lifted** exactly balances the pressure at the connection point.
- The higher the pressure, the higher the fluid climbs — like a built-in liquid "thermometer" of pressure.

If you place a ruler alongside the tube and measure how high the fluid climbed above the point of insertion, you have a direct reading of pressure.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Metrics

- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ (standard KTU convention for Kerala)
- **Density of water at 4°C:** $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Specific weight of water:** $\gamma_{water} = \rho g = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$
- **Density of mercury (Hg):** $\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (often rounded to 13.6 × 1000 in KTU problems)
- **Specific weight of mercury:** $\gamma_{Hg} = 13.6 \times \gamma_{water} = 133416 \text{ N/m}^3$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Mercury?** Mercury is used in many manometric devices (though NOT in piezometers — see Section 2.4) because its high density (13.6 times that of water) allows for compact measurements of large pressures. A piezometer using mercury would have a *shorter* column, but this is rarely done because piezometers are typically used for low pressures in water systems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle of a Piezometer

The piezometer operates on the fundamental principle of **hydrostatic pressure distribution** in a static fluid. The core logic is built on two pillars:

1. **Pascal's Law:** At any point in a static fluid, the pressure is the same in all directions.
2. **Hydrostatic Equation:** The pressure at any depth $h$ below a free surface open to the atmosphere is given by $P = \rho g h$.

When the piezometer tube is connected to a point in the fluid system, the fluid from the system rises into the tube until equilibrium is established. At equilibrium, the pressure at the connection point (inside the system) must equal the pressure at the same horizontal level inside the piezometer tube (a free surface open to the atmosphere).

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

- **Step 1: Connection** — A small-bore transparent tube is connected perpendicular (or radially) to the pipe/vessel at the point of interest.
- **Step 2: Pressure Transmission** — The fluid inside the system, being at gauge pressure $P$, pushes fluid up into the open tube.
- **Step 3: Column Rise** — The fluid rises to a height $h$ above the centerline of the pipe (or the point of measurement).
- **Step 4: Equilibrium** — At the level of the connection point, pressure from the system = pressure at the base of the liquid column in the piezometer.
- **Step 5: Reading** — The vertical height $h$ of the liquid column in the piezometer, measured from the pipe centerline, gives the gauge pressure: $P = \rho g h$.

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind the Formula

Imagine a control volume: the liquid column in the piezometer tube is in static equilibrium. The forces acting on it are:
- **Weight of the column** pulling it down ($W = \rho g h A$)
- **Atmospheric pressure** pushing it down at the top ($P_{atm} \times A$)
- **System pressure** pushing it up at the bottom ($P_{system} \times A$)

For equilibrium: $P_{system} \cdot A = P_{atm} \cdot A + \rho g h A$

Subtracting atmospheric pressure from both sides:

$$P_{gauge} = P_{system} - P_{atm} = \rho g h$$

This is why the piezometer gives **gauge pressure directly**.

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind the Piezometer's Limitations

While the piezometer is the simplest device, it has two critical limitations that every KTU student must understand:

1. **Cannot measure negative gauge pressure (vacuum):** If the system pressure is below atmospheric, the fluid in the piezometer will be drawn DOWN into the system, and air will enter the tube. The reading becomes meaningless.
2. **Cannot be used for high pressures:** For a pressure of, say, 1 MPa (≈ 10 bar), the water column would need to be 102 meters tall! This is impractical and gives rise to errors from tube instability and measurement difficulty.
3. **Cannot be used with very dense or opaque fluids:** A piezometer is essentially useless for measuring pressure in a heavy oil pipeline or mercury system because the fluid in the piezometer tube would be the *same* as the system fluid — but more importantly, the height would be impractically small for dense fluids, and a different fluid is needed for differential measurements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** Piezometers are used in **laboratory setups, low-pressure water systems, and educational demonstrations**. For industrial high-pressure or vacuum measurements, manometers (U-tube, inclined, inverted) or Bourdon gauges are preferred. This is a **favorite 3-mark question** in KTU exams: *"Discuss the limitations of a piezometer."*

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Symbol Meaning** | **Typical Units** | **Use Case** |
|-------------|-------------------|-------------------|--------------|
| $P = \rho g h$ | Gauge pressure | Pa (N/m²) | Standard piezometer reading |
| $h = \dfrac{P}{\rho g}$ | Height of liquid column | m (metres) | Computing the required tube length |
| $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$ | Absolute pressure | Pa | Converting gauge to absolute |
| $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$ | Standard atmospheric pressure | kPa | Reference value (often given as 10 m of water or 760 mm Hg) |
| $P = \gamma h$ | Pressure using specific weight | Pa | Compact form, where $\gamma = \rho g$ |
| $h_{Hg} = \dfrac{\rho_{water}}{\rho_{Hg}} \cdot h_{water} = \dfrac{h_{water}}{13.6}$ | Mercury equivalent height | m or mm | Converting between manometer fluids |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Many problems state the pressure "at a depth of $h$ from the free surface of a liquid." In such cases, $P = \rho g h$ is applied directly. Always mark the point where the pressure is being computed — usually, it is at the same level as the piezometer connection or at the bottom of the column.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of the Piezometer

- **Hydraulic Engineering:** Measuring piezometric head in open channels, weirs, and spillways.
- **Groundwater Studies:** Observation wells (which are essentially piezometers) measure the water table elevation in aquifers.
- **Soil Mechanics:** Standpipes and piezometers measure pore water pressure in soil samples.
- **Dam Engineering:** Piezometers installed in the body of an earth dam monitor pore pressure to detect potential piping failures — a critical safety application.
- **Educational Labs:** Demonstrating Pascal's Law and the hydrostatic pressure variation in fluid mechanics laboratories.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Fundamental Piezometer Equation

We now derive the master equation $P = \rho g h$ from first principles using a control volume approach on the fluid column inside the piezometer.

### Given:
- A closed vessel containing a liquid of density $\rho$ at gauge pressure $P_A$ at point A.
- A vertical piezometer tube of cross-sectional area $a$ connected to point A.
- The liquid rises in the tube to a height $h$ above point A, with the top open to atmosphere.
- The system is in static equilibrium.

### Step 1: Identify the Control Volume

Consider the column of liquid inside the piezometer tube as a free body. This column has:
- Cross-sectional area = $a$
- Height = $h$
- Volume = $a \cdot h$
- Mass = $\rho \cdot a \cdot h$
- Weight = $W = \rho g a h$ (acting downward)

### Step 2: Identify All Forces Acting on the Column

Three forces act on this liquid column:
1. **Pressure force from below** at point A (pushing the column upward): $F_{up} = P_A \cdot a$
2. **Atmospheric pressure force from above** at the top free surface (pushing the column downward): $F_{atop} = P_{atm} \cdot a$
3. **Weight of the column** (acting downward at the center of gravity): $W = \rho g a h$

### Step 3: Apply the Static Equilibrium Condition ($\sum F = 0$)

For the column to be in static equilibrium, the net force must be zero. Taking upward as positive:

$$F_{up} - F_{atop} - W = 0$$

Substituting the values:

$$P_A \cdot a - P_{atm} \cdot a - \rho g a h = 0$$

### Step 4: Simplify the Equation

Divide throughout by the area $a$ (since $a \neq 0$):

$$P_A - P_{atm} - \rho g h = 0$$

Rearranging to solve for the gauge pressure $P_A - P_{atm}$:

$$P_A - P_{atm} = \rho g h$$

### Step 5: Define Gauge Pressure

By definition, the gauge pressure at point A is $P_{A, gauge} = P_A - P_{atm}$:

$$\boxed{P_{A, gauge} = \rho g h}$$

This is the **fundamental piezometer equation**, which forms the basis of all KTU 14-mark problems on this topic.

## 3.2 Comprehensive Numerical Example — KTU Board Style

**Problem Statement:** A closed tank contains water (with a small air space pressurized above it). A piezometer is connected to the side of the tank at a point 2 m below the free water surface. The water rises in the piezometer to a height of 5 m above the point of connection. Calculate: (a) the gauge pressure at the point of connection, (b) the absolute pressure at the point of connection, and (c) the gauge pressure at the free water surface inside the tank. Take $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$.

### Part (a): Gauge Pressure at Point of Connection

The height of the water column in the piezometer above the connection point is $h = 5 \text{ m}$.

$$P_{A, gauge} = \rho g h$$

$$P_{A, gauge} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 5$$

$$P_{A, gauge} = 49050 \text{ Pa}$$

$$P_{A, gauge} = 49.05 \text{ kPa}$$

### Part (b): Absolute Pressure at Point of Connection

$$P_{A, abs} = P_{A, gauge} + P_{atm}$$

$$P_{A, abs} = 49.05 + 101.325$$

$$P_{A, abs} = 150.375 \text{ kPa}$$

### Part (c): Gauge Pressure at the Free Water Surface

The free water surface is 2 m above the connection point. Since the piezometer is at the level of the connection point, we apply the hydrostatic equation to find the pressure difference:

$$P_{surface} = P_{A, gauge} - \rho g \times (\text{depth of A below surface})$$

$$P_{surface} = 49.05 \times 10^3 - 1000 \times 9.81 \times 2$$

$$P_{surface} = 49050 - 19620$$

$$P_{surface} = 29430 \text{ Pa} = 29.43 \text{ kPa (gauge)}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — KTU-Standard Problem Variations

| **Problem Type** | **Given Information** | **Key Formula** | **Common Mistake to Avoid** |
|------------------|----------------------|----------------|-----------------------------|
| Direct reading of pressure | Height $h$, density $\rho$ | $P = \rho g h$ | Using $g = 10$ instead of $9.81$ — KTU expects **9.81** unless stated otherwise |
| Finding height of piezometer | Pressure $P$, density $\rho$ | $h = P / (\rho g)$ | Forgetting unit conversion from kPa to Pa |
| Absolute vs gauge | Any of the above | $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$ | Not specifying which pressure is being asked |
| Multi-fluid system | Different fluids in tank and piezometer | $\sum \rho g h$ with sign convention | Sign errors in up/down column directions |
| Pressure at depth in tank | Surface pressure $P_0$, depth $d$ | $P = P_0 + \rho g d$ | Forgetting to add the surface pressure $P_0$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Piezometer Architecture — Functional Block Diagram

Below is a Mermaid block diagram illustrating the functional architecture of a piezometer system. Each block is labelled with a purely alphanumeric identifier and a clean uppercase text label (no markdown formatting inside quotes).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[TANK CONTAINING PRESSURIZED FLUID] --> B[POINT OF CONNECTION ON TANK WALL]
    B --> C[PIEZOMETER TUBE INLET]
    C --> D[VERTICAL GLASS TUBE OF SMALL DIAMETER]
    D --> E[FLUID COLUMN RISES DUE TO SYSTEM PRESSURE]
    E --> F[FLUID COLUMN REACHES EQUILIBRIUM HEIGHT H]
    F --> G[ATMOSPHERIC PRESSURE ACTS ON TOP FREE SURFACE]
    G --> H[RULER OR SCALE MEASURES HEIGHT H ABOVE TAP POINT]
    H --> I[APPLY FORMULA P EQUALS RHO G H TO OBTAIN GAUGE PRESSURE]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Multi-Point Piezometer Network

The diagram below shows a more advanced setup where multiple piezometers are used to measure pressure at different elevations within a tank — a common KTU 14-mark problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T[CLOSED TANK WITH PRESSURIZED AIR SPACE ABOVE WATER] --> P1[POINT P1 AT BOTTOM OF TANK]
    T --> P2[POINT P2 AT MID HEIGHT OF TANK]
    T --> P3[POINT P3 NEAR FREE WATER SURFACE]
    P1 --> Z1[PIEZOMETER 1 RISES TO HEIGHT H1]
    P2 --> Z2[PIEZOMETER 2 RISES TO HEIGHT H2]
    P3 --> Z3[PIEZOMETER 3 RISES TO HEIGHT H3]
    Z1 --> X[OBSERVATION H1 IS HIGHEST H3 IS LOWEST]
    Z2 --> X
    Z3 --> X
    X --> Y[CONCLUSION PRESSURE INCREASES LINEARLY WITH DEPTH]
```

## 4.3 Schematic of Pressure Variation vs Depth

The Mermaid block below conceptually maps how pressure readings (via piezometer height) vary with depth inside a static fluid tank.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D0[DEPTH ZERO AT FREE SURFACE] --> D1[DEPTH 1 M]
    D1 --> D2[DEPTH 2 M]
    D2 --> D3[DEPTH 3 M]
    D3 --> D4[DEPTH H METERS]
    D0 --> R0[READING H0 = SURFACE GAUGE PRESSURE DIVIDED BY RHO G]
    D1 --> R1[READING H1 = H0 PLUS 1]
    D2 --> R2[READING H2 = H0 PLUS 2]
    D3 --> R3[READING H3 = H0 PLUS 3]
    D4 --> R4[READING HH = H0 PLUS H]
    R0 --> LIN[LINEAR RELATIONSHIP H = P0 DIVIDED BY RHO G PLUS DEPTH]
    R1 --> LIN
    R2 --> LIN
    R3 --> LIN
    R4 --> LIN
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for the KTU student:** The linear relationship $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ (where $P_0$ is the surface gauge pressure) means that if you plot pressure versus depth, you get a **straight line** with slope $\rho g$ and y-intercept $P_0$. This is often asked as a short conceptual question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**"What is a piezometer? Mention its limitations."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A piezometer is a simple pressure-measuring device consisting of a vertical transparent tube open to the atmosphere, connected to a vessel or pipeline at the point where pressure is to be measured. The fluid from the system rises in the tube to a height directly proportional to the gauge pressure at the connection point, given by $P = \rho g h$.

**Limitations:**
- It cannot measure **vacuum pressure** (negative gauge pressure), as air will be drawn into the system.
- It is **impractical for high-pressure measurements**, as the required column height becomes excessive (e.g., 1 MPa of water pressure needs a 102 m tall tube).
- It can only be used with **transparent fluids** where the column height can be visually observed.

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**"Distinguish between gauge pressure and absolute pressure with suitable examples."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Gauge Pressure | Absolute Pressure |
|--------|----------------|-------------------|
| Reference | Measured relative to atmospheric pressure | Measured relative to perfect vacuum |
| Formula | $P_{gauge} = P_{abs} - P_{atm}$ | $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$ |
| Value at sea level | Zero for atmospheric conditions | 101.325 kPa |
| Reading in piezometer | Directly obtained ($P = \rho g h$) | Requires addition of atmospheric pressure |

**Examples:** The pressure in a car tire (≈ 30 psi gauge) is 30 + 14.7 = 44.7 psi absolute. A piezometer connected to a water pipeline gives the gauge pressure of the water directly.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q. (a)** A piezometer is attached to a pipe carrying oil of specific gravity 0.85. The piezometer shows a column height of 4.2 m above the centerline of the pipe. Determine the gauge pressure at the centerline of the pipe in kPa. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Specific gravity of oil, $S = 0.85$
- Density of oil, $\rho_{oil} = S \times 1000 = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Height of oil column in piezometer, $h = 4.2 \text{ m}$
- Acceleration due to gravity, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**To find:** Gauge pressure at the centerline of the pipe.

**Formula:** $P_{gauge} = \rho g h$

**Calculation:**

$$P_{gauge} = 850 \times 9.81 \times 4.2$$

$$P_{gauge} = 850 \times 41.202$$

$$P_{gauge} = 35021.7 \text{ Pa}$$

$$P_{gauge} = 35.02 \text{ kPa}$$

**Answer:** The gauge pressure at the centerline of the pipe is **35.02 kPa**.

---

**Q. (b)** A closed tank contains water under a pressurized air space. A piezometer connected at a point 3 m below the water surface in the tank indicates a pressure of 78.5 kPa (gauge). Determine: (i) the air pressure in the space above the water, and (ii) the height to which water would rise in a piezometer connected at a point 6 m below the water surface. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Depth of piezometer connection point 1, $z_1 = 3 \text{ m}$ below water surface
- Pressure at point 1, $P_1 = 78.5 \text{ kPa (gauge)}$
- Density of water, $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**To find:** (i) Air pressure $P_{air}$, (ii) Height $h_2$ of water in piezometer at point 2 (depth 6 m).

**Part (i): Air Pressure**

The pressure at depth $z$ below the free water surface in a tank with air pressure $P_{air}$ on top is given by:

$$P_1 = P_{air} + \rho g z_1$$

Solving for $P_{air}$:

$$P_{air} = P_1 - \rho g z_1$$

$$P_{air} = 78.5 \times 10^3 - 1000 \times 9.81 \times 3$$

$$P_{air} = 78500 - 29430$$

$$P_{air} = 49070 \text{ Pa} = 49.07 \text{ kPa (gauge)}$$

**Part (ii): Height of Water in Piezometer at Point 2**

The pressure at depth 6 m below the water surface is:

$$P_2 = P_{air} + \rho g z_2$$

$$P_2 = 49070 + 1000 \times 9.81 \times 6$$

$$P_2 = 49070 + 58860$$

$$P_2 = 107930 \text{ Pa} = 107.93 \text{ kPa (gauge)}$$

The height $h_2$ of water in the piezometer at point 2 is:

$$h_2 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g}$$

$$h_2 = \frac{107930}{1000 \times 9.81}$$

$$h_2 = \frac{107930}{9810}$$

$$h_2 = 11.002 \text{ m}$$

**Answer:** (i) The air pressure in the space above the water is **49.07 kPa (gauge)**, and (ii) the water would rise to a height of **11.00 m** in the piezometer at point 2.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q. (a)** A pipe carrying water has a piezometer and a U-tube mercury manometer attached at the same section. The piezometer shows a water column height of 1.2 m above the centerline of the pipe. Calculate the gauge pressure at the centerline. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Height of water column in piezometer, $h = 1.2 \text{ m}$
- Density of water, $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**To find:** Gauge pressure at the centerline of the pipe.

**Formula:** $P_{gauge} = \rho g h$

**Calculation:**

$$P_{gauge} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 1.2$$

$$P_{gauge} = 11772 \text{ Pa}$$

$$P_{gauge} = 11.772 \text{ kPa}$$

**Answer:** The gauge pressure at the centerline of the pipe is **11.772 kPa**.

---

**Q. (b)** A closed tank of height 6 m and diameter 3 m contains water up to a height of 4 m. Above the water, there is air at a pressure of 50 kPa (gauge). Piezometers are connected at depths of 1 m, 2 m, 3 m, and 4 m below the free water surface. Calculate: (i) the gauge pressure at each of the four points, and (ii) the height of the water column in each piezometer above the connection point. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Air pressure above water, $P_{air} = 50 \text{ kPa} = 50000 \text{ Pa (gauge)}$
- Depths of piezometer connection points: $z_1 = 1 \text{ m}$, $z_2 = 2 \text{ m}$, $z_3 = 3 \text{ m}$, $z_4 = 4 \text{ m}$
- Density of water, $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Part (i): Gauge Pressure at Each Point**

The pressure at depth $z$ below the free water surface is given by:

$$P = P_{air} + \rho g z$$

For $z_1 = 1 \text{ m}$:

$$P_1 = 50000 + 1000 \times 9.81 \times 1 = 50000 + 9810 = 59810 \text{ Pa} = 59.81 \text{ kPa}$$

For $z_2 = 2 \text{ m}$:

$$P_2 = 50000 + 1000 \times 9.81 \times 2 = 50000 + 19620 = 69620 \text{ Pa} = 69.62 \text{ kPa}$$

For $z_3 = 3 \text{ m}$:

$$P_3 = 50000 + 1000 \times 9.81 \times 3 = 50000 + 29430 = 79430 \text{ Pa} = 79.43 \text{ kPa}$$

For $z_4 = 4 \text{ m}$:

$$P_4 = 50000 + 1000 \times 9.81 \times 4 = 50000 + 39240 = 89240 \text{ Pa} = 89.24 \text{ kPa}$$

**Part (ii): Height of Water Column in Each Piezometer**

The height of the water column in the piezometer above the connection point is given by:

$$h = \frac{P}{\rho g}$$

For point 1: $h_1 = \frac{59810}{9810} = 6.097 \text{ m}$

For point 2: $h_2 = \frac{69620}{9810} = 7.097 \text{ m}$

For point 3: $h_3 = \frac{79430}{9810} = 8.097 \text{ m}$

For point 4: $h_4 = \frac{89240}{9810} = 9.097 \text{ m}$

**Answer:** (i) The gauge pressures are **59.81 kPa, 69.62 kPa, 79.43 kPa, and 89.24 kPa** at depths 1 m, 2 m, 3 m, and 4 m respectively. (ii) The heights of the water columns in the piezometers are **6.097 m, 7.097 m, 8.097 m, and 9.097 m** above the respective connection points.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully!**
> - **Mistake 1 (1-mark penalty):** Not converting kPa to Pa (or vice versa) before substituting into the formula. **Always show units in every step.**
> - **Mistake 2 (1–2 mark penalty):** Forgetting to add atmospheric pressure when absolute pressure is asked. KTU specifically tests the **gauge vs absolute** distinction.
> - **Mistake 3 (2-mark penalty):** Using $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$ in numerical problems. KTU follows the convention $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. Using 10 will be marked as an approximation error.
> - **Mistake 4 (2–3 mark penalty):** Not specifying whether the final answer is gauge or absolute. Always end with a clear statement: *"Hence, the gauge pressure is X kPa."*
> - **Mistake 5 (Common in piezometer + manometer combined questions):** Sign errors in manometric equations. Always use the **isobaric method** — start at one point, move through the fluid, and apply $+ \rho g h$ when going down and $- \rho g h$ when going up.
> - **Mistake 6 (in diagrams):** Drawing piezometer tubes that are NOT vertical. Piezometers must be vertical (or inclined in a variant) — horizontal tubes do not work.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Piezometer Definition:** A vertical open tube connected to a fluid system to measure gauge pressure directly.
- **Master Equation:** $P_{gauge} = \rho g h$ (where $h$ is the height of fluid column above the tap point).
- **Inverse Form:** $h = P / (\rho g)$ — used to compute the required tube length for a given pressure.
- **Standard $g$ value:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ (KTU Kerala convention).
- **Atmospheric Pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} \approx 10.33 \text{ m of water} \approx 760 \text{ mm of Hg}$.
- **Three Key Limitations of Piezometer:** (1) Cannot measure vacuum, (2) Impractical for high pressure, (3) Suitable only for transparent fluids.
- **Piezometer always gives GAUGE pressure** — to get absolute, add atmospheric pressure.
- **Pressure increases linearly with depth:** $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ in a static fluid.
- **Unit Consistency:** Always convert kPa to Pa (×1000) before using $P = \rho g h$.
- **Specific Weight:** $\gamma = \rho g$, so $P = \gamma h$ — useful shorthand.
- **For Multi-fluid Systems:** When a piezometer fluid differs from the system fluid, use the manometric equation with proper sign convention.
- **Use Cases:** Laboratory measurement, observation wells, dam safety monitoring, soil pore pressure tests, hydraulic model studies.
- **Diagram Must Show:** Closed/vented tank, vertical piezometer tube, fluid level in the tube, point of connection, ruler or scale for reading, atmospheric pressure acting on the open top of the tube.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Manometers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT741 - FLUID MECHANICS & MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Manometers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Manometer (KTU 2024 Definition):** A *manometer* is a device used for the **measurement of fluid pressure** (static, atmospheric, or differential) by balancing the fluid column against the pressure to be measured. It operates on the fundamental principle of **hydrostatic equilibrium**, wherein the pressure at any point within a static fluid is governed by the equation $P = \rho g h$.

The word *manometer* is derived from the Greek words *manos* (thin/rare) and *metron* (measure). In KTU syllabus terminology, manometers are classified as **mechanical pressure measuring devices** that do not require any external power source (unlike transducers or electrical pressure gauges) and are considered highly accurate for low to moderate pressure measurements.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to measure how hard water is pushing inside a pipe. You cannot "see" pressure, but you can see water. Now imagine inserting a transparent vertical glass tube into the pipe. The water will climb up the tube to a height that exactly balances the pressure pushing it up. The higher it climbs, the higher the pressure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a manometer like a **weighing scale for pressure**. Instead of weighing apples with weights, it weighs "pressure" using a column of liquid (usually mercury, water, or oil). Gravity pulls the column down with a known force ($\rho g h$), and when this matches the unknown pressure pushing it up — we have our answer!

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value |
|-----------|--------|----------------|
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Density of Mercury (Hg) | $\rho_{Hg}$ | **13,600 kg/m³** |
| Density of Water (at 4°C) | $\rho_{water}$ | **1000 kg/m³** |
| Density of standard air | $\rho_{air}$ | **1.225 kg/m³** |
| Atmospheric pressure (1 atm) | $P_{atm}$ | **101.325 kPa** or **760 mm of Hg** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always use $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ unless the problem specifies otherwise. Pressure can be expressed in multiple units — **Pa (N/m²)**, **kPa**, **bar**, **m of water**, or **mm of Hg**. Conversion factors must be remembered!
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Manometers

Manometers are broadly classified into two major categories based on their functional utility:

**A) Simple Manometers** — Measure pressure at a single point.
1. **Piezometer**
2. **U-Tube Manometer**
3. **Single Column Manometer**

**B) Differential Manometers** — Measure the difference in pressure between two points.
1. **U-Tube Differential Manometer**
2. **Inverted U-Tube Differential Manometer**

### 2.2 Operating Principle (Step-by-Step Logic)

The operational logic of any manometer follows these structured steps:

- **Step 1:** Connect the manometer to the point where pressure is to be measured.
- **Step 2:** The unknown pressure pushes the manometric fluid (a denser liquid) up against gravity.
- **Step 3:** At equilibrium, the downward pressure of the manometric fluid column equals the unknown pressure.
- **Step 4:** Mathematically: $P_{unknown} = \rho_{fluid} \cdot g \cdot h$, where $h$ is the height difference of the fluid column.

### 2.3 Detailed Description of Each Type

#### 2.3.1 Piezometer

A piezometer is the simplest form of manometer — essentially a **vertical glass tube** open to the atmosphere, connected to a pipe or vessel containing the fluid whose pressure is to be measured.

- The fluid rises in the tube to a height $h$ above the centerline of the pipe.
- Pressure at point A (in the pipe): $P_A = \rho g h$
- **Limitation:** Cannot measure **gauge pressure of gases** (because gas density is too low) and cannot measure **negative (vacuum) pressures**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mercury is rarely used in piezometers?** Because for low pressures, the height of the mercury column would be impractically large. For example, 1 atm = 0.76 m of Hg, which is manageable, but 1 kPa = only 7.5 mm of Hg — accuracy becomes poor.

#### 2.3.2 U-Tube Manometer

A U-tube manometer consists of a **U-shaped glass tube** containing a heavier manometric fluid (typically mercury). One end is connected to the point of measurement, while the other end is either open to the atmosphere or connected to a reference point.

- Let $P_A$ be the pressure to be measured.
- Let $h$ be the difference in mercury levels in the two limbs.
- The manometric fluid is mercury ($\rho_{Hg}$), and the fluid in the pipe is, say, water ($\rho_w$).
- **Governing Equation:** $P_A = \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h - \rho_w \cdot g \cdot x$, where $x$ is the height of water column above mercury on the pipe side.

#### 2.3.3 Inverted U-Tube Manometer

Used when the manometric fluid is **lighter than the fluid in the pipe** (e.g., oil as manometric fluid in a water pipeline). The U-tube is inverted, with the lighter fluid trapped at the top.

- Used to measure **differential pressure** in water pipelines, especially for small pressure differences.
- The lighter fluid (air/oil) moves toward the higher-pressure side.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below is the master formula table for solving any KTU manometer problem. Memorize these before any test!

| # | Manometer Type | Governing Equation | Units | Application |
|---|----------------|-------------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Piezometer | $P_A = \rho g h$ | Pa (N/m²) | Pressure in liquid pipes |
| 2 | U-Tube (Mercury) | $P_A = \rho_{Hg} g h \pm \rho_w g x$ | Pa | Gas or liquid pressure |
| 3 | Inverted U-Tube | $P_A - P_B = (\rho_w - \rho_{oil}) g h$ | Pa | Differential pressure |
| 4 | Differential U-Tube | $P_A - P_B = (\rho_{Hg} - \rho_w) g h$ | Pa | Pressure drop across flowmeters |
| 5 | Micromanometer | $P_A - P_B = \rho_w g h_2 \left[1 - \dfrac{\rho_w}{\rho_{Hg}}\right]$ | Pa | Very low pressures |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention:** Pressures **above atmospheric** are **positive (gauge)** and pressures **below atmospheric** are **negative (vacuum)**. Always mention whether your answer is **absolute** or **gauge** pressure.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

Manometers are extensively used in:
- **Hydraulic systems** — measuring pump discharge pressure.
- **Refrigeration & HVAC** — measuring refrigerant line pressures.
- **Pipeline flow** — measuring pressure drop across orifices, Venturis, and Pitot tubes.
- **Aerospace** — measuring altitude via barometric manometers.
- **Medical devices** — sphygmomanometers (blood pressure measurement).

### 2.6 Real-World Example

In a **Bourdon Pressure Gauge** calibration lab, a **dead-weight tester** uses mercury manometers as the reference standard. This is because manometers are inherently **self-calibrating** — they rely only on length ($h$), density ($\rho$), and gravity ($g$), all of which are traceable to SI standards with very low uncertainty.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Pressure in a Simple Piezometer

**Setup:** A piezometer tube is connected to a pipe carrying water. Water rises to a height $h$ above the centerline of the pipe.

**Step 1:** Identify the points where pressure is known.
- At the free surface of water in the piezometer tube: $P = P_{atm} = 0$ (gauge).
- At the pipe centerline: $P = P_A$ (unknown, to be found).

**Step 2:** Apply the hydrostatic pressure equation along the vertical water column.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_A &= P_{atm} + \rho_{water} \cdot g \cdot h \\
P_A &= 0 + \rho_{water} \cdot g \cdot h \quad \text{(in gauge pressure)} \\
P_A &= \rho_{water} \cdot g \cdot h
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the fundamental piezometer equation. **Conversion logic:** Pressure at a deeper point equals pressure at the upper point plus $\rho g h$ (the weight of the fluid column above per unit area).

### 3.2 Derivation: U-Tube Manometer Equation

**Setup:** A U-tube containing mercury is connected to a pipe carrying water. The water-mercury interface is at a height $x$ below the pipe centerline on the pipe side, and the mercury level on the open side is $h$ above the interface.

**Step 1:** Identify the two equal-pressure points — the lower mercury interface (Point 1) and the same horizontal level on the open side (Point 2).

**Step 2:** Write pressure at Point 1 (from the pipe side):
$P_1 = P_A + \rho_w \cdot g \cdot x$

**Step 3:** Write pressure at Point 2 (from the open side):
$P_2 = P_{atm} + \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h$

**Step 4:** Equate $P_1 = P_2$ (hydrostatic equilibrium at the same horizontal level in a connected fluid):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_A + \rho_w \cdot g \cdot x &= P_{atm} + \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h \\
P_A &= P_{atm} + \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h - \rho_w \cdot g \cdot x
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** For gauge pressure ($P_{atm} = 0$):

$$
P_A = \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h - \rho_w \cdot g \cdot x
$$

**Conversion logic:** The mercury column contributes the major pressure component (since $\rho_{Hg} \gg \rho_w$), and the water column above mercury subtracts from it (because it pushes mercury down on the pipe side).

### 3.3 Analytical Worked Example

**Problem Statement:** A pipe carrying oil (specific gravity = 0.8) is connected to a U-tube manometer containing mercury. The mercury level on the open side is 250 mm above the pipe centerline, and the oil-mercury interface is 150 mm below the pipe centerline. Determine the gauge pressure at the pipe centerline.

**Given:**
- $\rho_{oil} = 0.8 \times 1000 = 800 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- $h = 250 \text{ mm} = 0.25 \text{ m}$ (mercury height)
- $x = 150 \text{ mm} = 0.15 \text{ m}$ (oil column above mercury)

**Applying the U-tube equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_A &= \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h - \rho_{oil} \cdot g \cdot x \\
P_A &= (13600)(9.81)(0.25) - (800)(9.81)(0.15) \\
P_A &= 33,354 - 1,177.2 \\
P_A &= 32,176.8 \text{ Pa} \\
P_A &\approx 32.18 \text{ kPa (gauge)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The gauge pressure at the pipe centerline is approximately **32.18 kPa**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Problem Identification

| Problem Scenario | Manometer to Use | Key Indicator |
|------------------|------------------|---------------|
| Single-point pressure, liquid pipe | Piezometer | Vertical tube, no manometric fluid mentioned |
| High-pressure gas pipeline | U-tube manometer | Mercury is the manometric fluid |
| Differential pressure across orifice | Differential U-tube | Two connection points labeled A and B |
| Small pressure difference in water line | Inverted U-tube | "Lighter fluid" mentioned |
| Very small differential pressure | Micromanometer | Air/oil over mercury |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Manometer Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Manometers] --> B[Simple Manometers]
    A --> C[Differential Manometers]
    B --> D[Piezometer]
    B --> E[U-Tube Manometer]
    B --> F[Single Column Manometer]
    C --> G[Differential U-Tube]
    C --> H[Inverted U-Tube Manometer]
    C --> I[Micromanometer]
    D --> D1[Measures pressure at single point]
    E --> E1[Uses mercury as manometric fluid]
    G --> G1[Measures difference between two points]
    H --> H1[Uses lighter fluid at top]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Piezometer Operation Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    PIPE[Pipe with Fluid] --> VALVE[Connection Valve]
    VALVE --> TUBE[Vertical Glass Tube]
    TUBE --> FLUID_RISE[Fluid Rises to Height h]
    FLUID_RISE --> EQUILIBRIUM[Hydrostatic Equilibrium Reached]
    EQUILIBRIUM --> READING[Read Height h from Scale]
    READING --> CALC[Apply P equals rho g h]
    CALC --> RESULT[Compute Pressure in Pa or kPa]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: U-Tube Manometer Operation Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    INLET[Pipe Connection Point A] --> DOWN[Water Column Down x meters]
    DOWN --> MERC_INT[Mercury Water Interface Point 1]
    MERC_INT --> EQBAL[Pressure Equality at Same Level]
    EQBAL --> OPEN_SIDE[Open Limb Side]
    OPEN_SIDE --> MERC_RISE[Mercury Rises h meters above interface]
    MERC_RISE --> ATMOS[Exposed to Atmosphere]
    EQBAL --> SOLVE[Apply Hydrostatic Equation]
    SOLVE --> OUT[Pressure at A equals rho_Hg g h minus rho_w g x]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Differential Manometer Operation Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Point A in Pipe] --> LIMB1[Left Limb of U-Tube]
    B[Point B in Pipe] --> LIMB2[Right Limb of U-Tube]
    LIMB1 --> INT1[Mercury Interface Left]
    LIMB2 --> INT2[Mercury Interface Right]
    INT1 --> DIFF[Height Difference h between Interfaces]
    INT2 --> DIFF
    DIFF --> DELTA_P[Compute Delta P equals rho_Hg minus rho_fluid times g times h]
    DELTA_P --> RESULT[Pressure Difference Output]
```

### 4.5 Schematic Diagram (Mermaid Block Representation) of U-Tube Manometer

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph PIPE_SIDE [Pipe Side]
        P1[Point A in Pipe]
        COL_W[Water Column Height x]
        P1 --> COL_W
    end
    subgraph MANOMETER [U-Tube Manometer]
        INT[Mercury Interface]
        COL_HG[Mercury Column Height h]
        INT --> COL_HG
    end
    subgraph OPEN_SIDE [Open Side]
        ATM[Atmospheric Pressure]
        COL_HG --> ATM
    end
    PIPE_SIDE --> MANOMETER
    MANOMETER --> OPEN_SIDE
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define a manometer. Mention any two types with one application each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **manometer** is a device used for measuring fluid pressure by balancing it against the weight of a fluid column, based on the hydrostatic principle $P = \rho g h$. *(1 mark)*

**Two types and applications:**
1. **Piezometer:** A simple vertical tube used to measure pressure in liquid-containing pipes at points where pressure is above atmospheric. *(1 mark)*
2. **U-Tube Manometer:** A U-shaped tube containing mercury, used to measure pressure of gases (e.g., boiler steam pressure, compressed air systems). *(1 mark)*

---

#### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**State the hydrostatic principle on which manometers operate. Write the equation for pressure measured using a piezometer.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The hydrostatic principle states: **"The pressure at any point in a static fluid is directly proportional to the depth of the point below the free surface and to the density of the fluid."** *(2 marks)*

**Piezometer equation:** $P = \rho g h$, where $\rho$ = density of fluid, $g$ = acceleration due to gravity, $h$ = height of fluid column. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** A U-tube manometer containing mercury is connected to a pipe carrying water. The mercury level on the open side is 300 mm above the pipe centerline. The water-mercury interface on the pipe side is 200 mm below the pipe centerline. Find the gauge pressure at the pipe centerline. Take specific gravity of mercury as 13.6. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of an inverted U-tube differential manometer. Mention one engineering application. *(7 marks)*

---

**Solution to Part (a):**

**Given:**
- $\rho_{Hg} = 13.6 \times 1000 = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $h = 300 \text{ mm} = 0.3 \text{ m}$
- $x = 200 \text{ mm} = 0.2 \text{ m}$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Formula (U-tube manometer):**

$$
P_A = \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h - \rho_w \cdot g \cdot x
$$

**Step-by-step substitution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_A &= (13600)(9.81)(0.3) - (1000)(9.81)(0.2) \\
P_A &= 40,024.8 - 1,962 \\
P_A &= 38,062.8 \text{ Pa} \\
P_A &\approx 38.06 \text{ kPa (gauge)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $P_A \approx \mathbf{38.06 \text{ kPa (gauge)}}$ *(7 marks: 2 marks for formula, 3 marks for substitution, 2 marks for final answer with units)*

---

**Solution to Part (b):**

An **inverted U-tube manometer** consists of a U-shaped tube turned upside down, with the **manometric fluid (lighter than the pipe fluid)** trapped at the top of the inverted U. *(2 marks)*

**Construction:** The two open ends of the inverted U-tube are connected to two points A and B in the pipe whose pressure difference is to be measured. The manometric fluid (typically oil or air) sits at the top bend. The pipe fluid (e.g., water) fills the rest of the tube. *(2 marks)*

**Working:** When $P_A > P_B$, the interface on side A moves down, and the interface on side B moves up. The manometric fluid shifts toward the lower pressure side. The difference in heights $h$ of the pipe fluid in the two limbs is measured. *(2 marks)*

**Equation:** $P_A - P_B = (\rho_{pipe\,fluid} - \rho_{manometric\,fluid}) \cdot g \cdot h$

**Application:** Used in **water supply pipelines** to measure small pressure drops across flow meters or filters, where conventional U-tube manometers would give impractically small readings. *(1 mark)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a differential U-tube manometer. Derive the equation for the difference in pressure between two points A and B connected to it. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A differential manometer is connected at two points A and B in a horizontal pipe carrying oil (specific gravity = 0.85). The heavier fluid is mercury, and the manometer reading is 250 mm. Calculate the pressure difference between A and B. *(7 marks)*

---

**Solution to Part (a):**

A **differential U-tube manometer** is used to measure the pressure difference between two points A and B in a pipe. It consists of a U-tube containing mercury (or any heavier manometric fluid) connected to points A and B by two limbs. *(2 marks)*

**Construction (refer to Section 4.4 diagram):** Two vertical limbs connected at the bottom by a U-bend. The lower portion is filled with mercury, and the upper portions are filled with the pipe fluid (e.g., water or oil). The two top ends connect to points A and B. *(2 marks)*

**Derivation:**

Let $h$ = difference in mercury levels between the two limbs.
Let $x$ = height of pipe fluid (say water) above mercury on the A side.
Let $y$ = height of pipe fluid above mercury on the B side.

At the lower mercury interface (same horizontal level), pressures must be equal:

$$
P_A + \rho_w \cdot g \cdot x + \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h = P_B + \rho_w \cdot g \cdot y
$$

Rearranging:

$$
P_A - P_B = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot (y - x) - \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h
$$

For practical cases where pipe fluid levels are at the same height ($x = y$):

$$
P_A - P_B = (\rho_{Hg} - \rho_w) \cdot g \cdot h \quad \text{... (Main Equation)}
$$

**Conversion logic:** The mercury column provides the dominant pressure-balancing effect, while the pipe fluid columns on either side nearly cancel out. *(3 marks total for derivation)*

---

**Solution to Part (b):**

**Given:**
- Specific gravity of oil = 0.85 → $\rho_{oil} = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $h = 250 \text{ mm} = 0.25 \text{ m}$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Formula:**

$$
P_A - P_B = (\rho_{Hg} - \rho_{oil}) \cdot g \cdot h
$$

**Step-by-step calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_A - P_B &= (13600 - 850) \times 9.81 \times 0.25 \\
P_A - P_B &= (12750)(9.81)(0.25) \\
P_A - P_B &= 31,275.375 \text{ Pa} \\
P_A - P_B &\approx 31.28 \text{ kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $P_A - P_B \approx \mathbf{31.28 \text{ kPa}}$ *(7 marks: 2 marks for formula, 3 marks for substitution, 2 marks for final answer with correct units and sign)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Errors:** Converting mm to m is often missed — marks deducted for using $h = 250$ instead of $h = 0.25$. Always convert all dimensions to SI units (meters) before calculation.
> 2. **Sign Convention Confusion:** Many students forget whether to add or subtract the water column. **Rule of thumb:** Mercury column pressure is *positive*; pipe-fluid column pressure above mercury on the pipe side is *subtracted*.
> 3. **Atmospheric Pressure Inclusion:** If the problem asks for **gauge pressure**, set $P_{atm} = 0$. If it asks for **absolute pressure**, add 101.325 kPa to the gauge answer.
> 4. **Specific Gravity vs. Density:** Specific gravity is dimensionless; multiply by 1000 to get density in kg/m³.
> 5. **Wrong Formula Selection:** Students often confuse differential manometer with simple U-tube. Always identify whether the problem involves one pressure point or two.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Checklist for Manometers:**

- ✅ **Definition:** Manometer = pressure measurement device based on hydrostatic equilibrium ($P = \rho g h$).
- ✅ **Piezometer formula:** $P = \rho g h$ — only for liquids, only for positive pressure.
- ✅ **U-tube manometer formula:** $P_A = \rho_{Hg} g h - \rho_{fluid} g x$ — for both liquids and gases.
- ✅ **Differential U-tube formula:** $P_A - P_B = (\rho_{Hg} - \rho_{fluid}) g h$.
- ✅ **Inverted U-tube formula:** $P_A - P_B = (\rho_{fluid} - \rho_{light\,fluid}) g h$.
- ✅ **Standard values to memorize:** $\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$.
- ✅ **Unit conversions:** 1 bar = 100 kPa; 1 m of water = 9.81 kPa; 760 mm Hg = 1 atm.
- ✅ **Sign convention:** Gauge pressure = Absolute pressure $- P_{atm}$.
- ✅ **Piezometer limitations:** Cannot measure gas pressure, vacuum, or high-pressure liquid accurately.
- ✅ **Manometric fluid selection:** Mercury for high pressures; oil/air for inverted U-tube differential measurements.
- ✅ **Hydrostatic principle:** Pressure at equal heights in a connected static fluid is the same — used to equate pressures on both limbs.
- ✅ **Engineering applications:** Pump testing, boiler pressure monitoring, flow meter calibration, HVAC systems.
- ✅ **Always mention units** in the final answer — KTU examiners deduct marks for missing units.
- ✅ **Draw a labeled diagram** for every manometer problem — it earns easy 2-3 marks in Part B.
- ✅ **Read the problem twice** before writing — identify if it's *simple* or *differential* manometer first.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pressure Gauges
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT741 - FLUID MECHANICS & MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Pressure Gauges

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pressure Gauge:** A pressure gauge (or manometer/vacuum gauge) is a precision instrument used to measure the pressure of a fluid (liquid or gas)—defined as the normal force per unit area exerted by the fluid on a containing surface. Pressure gauges are broadly classified into **hydrostatic gauges** (manometers), **mechanical elastic-element gauges** (Bourdon, diaphragm, bellows), **dead-weight piston gauges** (primary standards), and **electrical/electronic transducers** (strain-gauge, piezoelectric, capacitive, and resonant types).

In the formal KTU 2024 syllabus framework, pressure measurement is foundational because virtually every fluid statics, kinematics, and dynamics problem begins with the reliable determination of a fluid's pressure state.

### 1.2 Intuitive Overview — Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Straw in a Juice Box**
> When you sip juice from a sealed box, the liquid rises up the straw because atmospheric pressure pushes down on the juice surface. A pressure gauge works in a *reverse* way—it lets a fluid push against a calibrated spring or liquid column and then translates that push into a readable number. The higher the push, the larger the deflection (more bent straw or higher mercury column).

Think of a Bourdon tube gauge like a flattened party whistle: low pressure leaves it straight, high pressure causes it to curl outward, and a tiny gear mechanism converts that tiny curl into the motion of a needle on a dial.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- Standard atmospheric pressure: **P_atm = 101.325 kPa = 1.01325 bar = 760 mmHg**
- Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
- Density of mercury (at 0 °C): **ρ_Hg = 13,600 kg/m³**
- Density of water (at 4 °C): **ρ_water = 1000 kg/m³**
- 1 N/m² = **1 Pa (Pascal, SI unit)**
- 1 bar = **10⁵ Pa**
- 1 kgf/cm² = **98,066.5 Pa ≈ 0.98 bar**

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examinations frequently use **g = 9.81 m/s²** for numerical answers; some coaching material uses 9.80665. Stick to **9.81 m/s²** unless a problem statement dictates otherwise.

### 1.4 Classification of Pressure Gauges (Tree View)

```
PRESSURE GAUGES
├── 1. Hydrostatic (Manometric)
│   ├── Piezometer
│   ├── U-Tube Manometer

│
├── 2. Mechanical (Elastic-Element)
```

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hydrostatic Gauges — The Manometer Family

#### 2.1.1 Piezometer
- A vertical open tube is connected to the pressurized fluid.
- The liquid rises to a height **h** until the column weight balances the gauge pressure.
- **Limitation:** Only measures positive (gauge) pressure; not suitable for high pressure or gases.

#### 2.1.2 U-Tube Manometer
A U-shaped glass tube containing a heavier *manometric fluid* (mercury or carbon tetrachloride) measures the pressure difference between two points.

**Why it works:** The pressure at the lowest common level in a static continuous fluid is equal everywhere (Pascal's principle).

#### 2.1.3 Single-Column (Well-Type) Manometer
- One leg is a large reservoir (well), the other is a narrow graduated tube.
- The level change in the well is negligible due to its large area.
- Gives a *magnified* reading—useful for sensitive laboratory work.

#### 2.1.4 Inverted U-Tube Manometer
- Used for fluids *lighter* than the manometric fluid (e.g., oil in a water pipeline).
- The manometric fluid is trapped at the top of the inverted U.

#### 2.1.5 Differential Manometer
- Measures the *difference* in pressure between two points in a system.
- Common variants: U-tube differential manometer, inverted U-tube differential.

### 2.2 Mechanical (Elastic-Element) Gauges

#### 2.2.1 Bourdon Tube Gauge
- A curved, flattened metal tube of **elliptical cross-section**.
- Internal pressure tends to *straighten* the tube.
- The tip motion is converted by a **gear-and-pinion linkage** to rotate a pointer.
- **C-type:** Single curve (~270°).
- **Spiral / Helical:** Multi-turn—gives larger deflection for the same pressure.

#### 2.2.2 Diaphragm Gauge
- A thin metal or non-metal disc that deflects under pressure.
- Deflection measured via a pointer mechanism (mechanical) or strain gauge (electronic).

#### 2.2.3 Bellows Gauge
- A corrugated cylindrical element that expands/contracts axially with pressure.

### 2.3 Dead-Weight Pressure Tester (Primary Standard)

- A precisely known weight is placed on a piston of known area.
- Pressure is generated in the hydraulic fluid beneath.
- The applied pressure is calculated as **P = W / A** (with friction correction).
- Used to *calibrate* other pressure gauges—hence the title "primary standard."

### 2.4 Electronic / Transducer-Based Gauges

| Transducer | Operating Principle | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| Strain Gauge | Resistance change of a foil under strain | Industrial process control |
| Piezoelectric | Voltage generated by crystal deformation | Dynamic / high-frequency pressure |
| Capacitive | Change in capacitance between plates | Low-pressure, high-accuracy |
| Resonant Wire | Frequency change of a tensioned wire | Precision laboratory standards |

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the following table — these equations appear in **almost every KTU Part A and Part B question** on pressure gauges.

| # | Gauge / Concept | Key Formula | Variable Definitions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pressure definition | $P = F / A$ | F = normal force (N), A = area (m²) |
| 2 | Hydrostatic pressure (depth h) | $P = \rho g h$ | ρ = fluid density, g = 9.81 m/s² |
| 3 | Absolute vs Gauge | $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$ | Atmospheric ≈ 101.325 kPa |
| 4 | Piezometer head | $h = P / (\rho g)$ | For liquid only |
| 5 | U-Tube Manometer (open end) | $P_A = \rho_m g h - \rho_f g z$ | ρ_m = manometric fluid density |
| 6 | U-Tube Manometer (closed end, vacuum) | $P_A = - \rho_m g h$ | Negative indicates vacuum |
| 7 | Differential U-tube manometer | $P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$ | h = differential mercury height |
| 8 | Inverted U-tube differential | $P_A - P_B = (\rho_f - \rho_m) g h$ | For lighter manometric fluid |
| 9 | Dead-weight tester | $P = W / A_p$ | W = applied weight, A_p = piston area |
| 10 | Dead-weight (with friction) | $P = (W + F_f) / A_p$ | F_f = friction force on piston |
| 11 | Bourdon tube sensitivity | $P = \dfrac{E \cdot t^2 \cdot \Delta\theta}{1.12 \cdot r^2}$ | E = Young's modulus, t = wall thickness, Δθ = angular tip deflection, r = tube radius |
| 12 | Diaphragm deflection (flat) | $\delta = \dfrac{3 P a^4 (1-\nu^2)}{16 E t^3}$ | a = effective radius, ν = Poisson's ratio, t = thickness |
| 13 | Strain gauge (resistive) | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \varepsilon$ | G = gauge factor, ε = strain |
| 14 | Piezoelectric charge | $Q = d \cdot F$ | d = piezoelectric constant, F = applied force |
| 15 | Pressure unit conversion | 1 bar = 10⁵ Pa = 750.06 mmHg | Use cross-multiplication |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why KTU asks this:** Pressure gauges are the eyes of every hydraulic, pneumatic, and process plant. A mechanical engineer services centrifugal pumps, hydraulic presses, boiler systems, gas pipelines, and HVAC systems—all of which demand *calibrated* pressure measurement. KTU 2024 questions on this topic usually combine a simple manometer derivation with a numerical problem (7-mark) and a comparative/descriptive question (7-mark) on Bourdon tubes, dead-weight testers, or electronic transducers.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: U-Tube Manometer Equation (Open-End Type)

**Setup:** A pipe contains a fluid of specific weight **w_f = ρ_f · g**. A U-tube manometer using a heavier manometric fluid of specific weight **w_m = ρ_m · g** is connected at point **A**. The other end of the U-tube is open to the atmosphere.

**Goal:** Find the gauge pressure at point A in terms of the manometer reading **h** and elevation difference **z** between the pipe centerline and the lower meniscus.

**Step-by-step derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Starting at point A, move down through fluid by } z &: \quad P_A + \rho_f \, g \, z \\[4pt]
\text{Pass through the manometric fluid up by } h &: \quad - \rho_m \, g \, h \\[4pt]
\text{At the open end, pressure equals atmospheric} &: \quad - P_{atm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Setting the sum equal to atmospheric pressure at the open end:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_A + \rho_f g z - \rho_m g h &= P_{atm} \\[6pt]
P_A - P_{atm} &= \rho_m g h - \rho_f g z \\[6pt]
\boxed{\,P_{A,\text{gauge}} = \rho_m g h - \rho_f g z\,}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic check:** If **z = 0** (pipe at the same level as the lower meniscus) and the manometric fluid is mercury (ρ_m = 13,600 kg/m³), then **P_A, gauge = ρ_m · g · h**, the standard textbook form.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Differential U-Tube Manometer

**Setup:** Two points **A** and **B** in a flowing pipeline are connected via a U-tube containing mercury. Let **ρ_f** = density of the flowing fluid, **ρ_m** = mercury density, and **h** = the difference between the two mercury levels.

**Goal:** Show that **P_A − P_B = (ρ_m − ρ_f) · g · h**.

**Step-by-step derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pressure at the lower meniscus (left leg) starting from A} &: P_A + \rho_f g (y_1 + h) \\
\text{Pressure at the lower meniscus (right leg) starting from B} &: P_B + \rho_f g y_1
\end{aligned}
$$

Setting the two equal (Pascal's principle at the common level on the right):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_A + \rho_f g (y_1 + h) - \rho_m g h &= P_B + \rho_f g y_1 \\[6pt]
P_A + \rho_f g y_1 + \rho_f g h - \rho_m g h &= P_B + \rho_f g y_1 \\[6pt]
P_A - P_B &= \rho_m g h - \rho_f g h \\[6pt]
\boxed{\,P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) \, g \, h\,}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic check:** Since ρ_m ≫ ρ_f, the term (ρ_m − ρ_f) · g · h is dominated by mercury, justifying why mercury is the standard manometric fluid.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Inverted U-Tube Differential Manometer

**Setup:** The U-tube is *inverted*; the manometric fluid (typically oil or a lighter liquid) is trapped at the top. The two legs dip into the flowing fluid.

**Step-by-step derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{At the upper meniscus (left leg) from A} &: P_A - \rho_f g y_2 - \rho_m g h \\
\text{At the upper meniscus (right leg) from B} &: P_B - \rho_f g (y_2 + h)
\end{aligned}
$$

Equating both expressions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_A - \rho_f g y_2 - \rho_m g h &= P_B - \rho_f g y_2 - \rho_f g h \\[6pt]
P_A - P_B &= \rho_m g h - \rho_f g h \\[6pt]
\boxed{\,P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) \, g \, h\,}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic check:** Identical form to the regular U-tube—but here the *manometric fluid is lighter* than the flowing fluid, so we use a low-density oil.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Dead-Weight Piston Gauge

**Setup:** A piston of effective area **A_p** carries known weights **W₁, W₂, …, W_n**. Hydraulic fluid underneath pressurizes the system. The piston floats in equilibrium.

**Step-by-step derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Forces on piston (vertical equilibrium)} &: P \cdot A_p = W_{\text{total}} + F_f \\[6pt]
\text{Hence} &: P = \frac{W_{\text{total}} + F_f}{A_p}
\end{aligned}
$$

where **F_f** is the friction force on the piston. If the piston is *spun* (rotated), the friction reduces to a known small value. The instrument's accuracy is ±0.1 % of full scale when calibrated against national standards.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Bourdon Tube Tip Deflection

The angular tip deflection **Δθ** of a C-type Bourdon tube is given by the empirical formula (derived from thin-shell theory):

$$
\Delta\theta = K \cdot \frac{P \cdot r^2}{E \cdot t^2}
$$

where **K ≈ 1.12** is a constant for a C-type tube, **r** is the mean tube radius, **t** is the wall thickness, and **E** is Young's modulus of the tube material.

Rearranging for pressure:

$$
\boxed{\,P = \frac{1.12 \, E \, t^2 \, \Delta\theta}{r^2}\,}
$$

**Logic check:** Higher Young's modulus (steel) or thinner walls → smaller deflection for a given pressure. This is why Bourdon tubes are *precision-engineered* from phosphor-bronze or beryllium-copper.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Choice of Gauge

| Application / Property | Manometer | Bourdon | Diaphragm | Dead-Weight | Strain-Gauge |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pressure range** | Low–medium | Medium–high | Low–medium | High (calibration) | Wide |
| **Accuracy** | High (±0.1%) | Medium (±1%) | Medium | Very high (±0.05%) | High (±0.25%) |
| **Dynamic response** | Poor | Slow | Slow | Static only | Fast |
| **Cost** | Low | Low | Medium | Very high | Medium–high |
| **Calibration** | Reference | Needs reference | Needs reference | Self-calibrating | Needs reference |
| **Best for** | Lab / classroom | Field / industrial | Differential | Primary standard | Process control |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define a manometer. Name any two types of manometers used for pressure measurement.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A manometer is a device used to measure fluid pressure by balancing the unknown pressure against the hydrostatic pressure of a column of liquid (the manometric fluid).

**Two types of manometers:**
1. **U-tube manometer** – a U-shaped tube containing a heavier manometric fluid such as mercury.
2. **Inverted U-tube manometer** – used when the manometric fluid is lighter than the fluid being measured.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for naming the two types — full 3 marks.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**What is a dead-weight pressure gauge tester? Why is it called a primary standard?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A dead-weight tester consists of a precision piston of accurately known effective area loaded with calibrated weights. The pressure of the hydraulic fluid is determined from the fundamental relation **P = W / A**, making it independent of the calibration of any other instrument.

It is called a **primary standard** (1 mark) because the pressure is *derived from the base SI units* of force (weight) and area — no other gauge is required to calibrate it (1 mark). It achieves an accuracy of ±0.1 % or better when the piston is rotated to reduce friction (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B Question — Internal Choice Pattern (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Part B questions on Module 1 Pressure Gauges typically consist of two sub-parts worth **7 + 7 marks**. The two questions below are independent alternatives — exactly as they would appear in the university ESE question paper.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a)** A U-tube manometer is connected to a pipe carrying oil of specific gravity **0.85**. The mercury level in the open limb is **240 mm** above the pipe centerline, and the mercury level in the limb connected to the pipe is **60 mm** below the pipe centerline. Determine the gauge pressure at the pipe centerline. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a neat sketch the working of a **Bourdon tube pressure gauge**. State its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Part (a):**

**Given:**
- Specific gravity of oil = 0.85 → ρ_oil = 850 kg/m³
- Mercury level in open limb above pipe: +240 mm = +0.24 m
- Mercury level in pipe-side limb below pipe: −60 mm = −0.06 m
- Mercury column height difference: h = 0.24 − (−0.06) = **0.30 m**
- ρ_Hg = 13,600 kg/m³, g = 9.81 m/s²

**Using the manometer equation** (starting at the pipe centerline, moving through oil, then through mercury, to the open end):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{pipe}} + \rho_{\text{oil}} \, g \, z_1 - \rho_{Hg} \, g \, h &= P_{atm} \\[6pt]
P_{\text{pipe,gauge}} &= \rho_{Hg} \, g \, h - \rho_{\text{oil}} \, g \, z_1
\end{aligned}
$$

where **z₁ = 0.06 m** (oil column from pipe to mercury level on the pipe side).

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{pipe,gauge}} &= (13{,}600)(9.81)(0.30) - (850)(9.81)(0.06) \\[6pt]
&= 40{,}030.8 \, - \, 500.31 \\[6pt]
&= 39{,}530.49 \, \text{Pa} \\[6pt]
&\approx 39.53 \, \text{kPa} \quad \text{(gauge)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** **P_pipe,gauge ≈ 39.53 kPa** (positive, indicating the pipe is above atmospheric pressure).

---

**Solution to Part (b):**

**Working of a Bourdon Tube Pressure Gauge:**

A Bourdon tube is a curved, flattened metal tube of **elliptical cross-section**, bent into a "C" shape. One end is fixed and connected to the fluid whose pressure is to be measured; the other end is sealed and free to move. The internal pressure tends to *straighten* the curved tube because the inner surface of the ellipse has a smaller radius of curvature (higher stress) than the outer surface.

**Components:**
- **Bourdon tube (C-type)** — sensing element.
- **Linkage and pinion-gear mechanism** — converts linear tip motion into rotary motion.
- **Pointer** — rotates over a calibrated dial.

**Working steps:**
1. Pressurized fluid enters the fixed end of the Bourdon tube.
