2. The internal pressure causes the tube to deflect outward (straighten).
3. The free tip moves by a small distance **δ**.
4. A connecting link transfers this motion to a **rack**.
5. The rack engages a **pinion gear**, rotating the pointer.
6. The pointer reads directly on the calibrated dial in **kPa, bar, or kg/cm²**.

**Advantages:**
- Simple, robust, inexpensive.
- Direct reading on a dial.
- Wide pressure range (vacuum to ~700 MPa).

**Limitations:**
- Susceptible to shock and vibration.
- Hysteresis and fatigue in the spring material over time.
- Requires periodic calibration.
- Slow dynamic response — not suitable for rapidly fluctuating pressures.
- Affected by temperature changes (thermal expansion of fluid).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** A differential U-tube manometer is connected across two sections of a horizontal water pipe. The manometric fluid is mercury (S.G. = 13.6). The difference in mercury levels is **100 mm**. Calculate the pressure difference between the two sections. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the principle and working of a **piezoelectric pressure transducer**. Mention any two industrial applications. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Part (a):**

**Given:**
- Pipe fluid: water → ρ_w = 1000 kg/m³
- Manometric fluid: mercury → ρ_Hg = 13,600 kg/m³
- Mercury height difference: h = 100 mm = 0.1 m
- Pipe is horizontal (z difference between the two tapping points is negligible for the manometer equation).

**Using the differential U-tube manometer formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_A - P_B &= (\rho_{Hg} - \rho_w) \, g \, h \\[6pt]
&= (13{,}600 - 1{,}000)(9.81)(0.1) \\[6pt]
&= (12{,}600)(9.81)(0.1) \\[6pt]
&= 12{,}360.6 \, \text{Pa} \\[6pt]
&\approx 12.36 \, \text{kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** **P_A − P_B ≈ 12.36 kPa**.

---

**Solution to Part (b):**

**Principle of Piezoelectric Pressure Transducer:**

Certain crystals (quartz, tourmaline, Rochelle salt, lead zirconate titanate — PZT) develop an electric charge on their surfaces when subjected to mechanical stress. This is the *piezoelectric effect* (direct effect — discovered by Pierre and Jacques Curie in 1880).

**Block diagram — working:**

```
[Pressure Input] -> [Diaphragm] -> [Piezoelectric Crystal] -> [Charge Output Q = d F] -> [Charge Amplifier] -> [Voltage Signal] -> [Digital Display / Recorder]
```

**Step-by-step working:**
1. The fluid pressure acts on a metallic **diaphragm** in contact with the piezoelectric crystal.
2. The diaphragm deflects, applying a force **F = P × A** to the crystal.
3. The crystal generates a charge **Q = d · F**, where **d** is the piezoelectric charge constant (C/N).
4. The very high output impedance of the crystal requires a **charge amplifier** to convert charge into a usable voltage.
5. The voltage is proportional to the applied pressure and is read on a digital display or recorder.

**Key formula:** **Q = d · F = d · P · A**

**Industrial applications:**
1. **Engine cylinder pressure measurement** in IC engines — to compute indicated power and combustion analysis.
2. **Shock-wave and blast-pressure measurement** in defence and ballistics research.
3. **Acoustic and ultrasonic pressure sensing** in sonar and medical ultrasound equipment.
4. **Dynamic pressure measurement in rocket engines** and high-speed wind tunnels.

*(Any two applications carry full marks.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students typically lose marks on Pressure Gauges questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert mm to m** — a very common slip. Always convert all height values to **meters** before substituting into **P = ρ g h**. (Penalty: 1 mark)
> 2. **Confusing gauge pressure with absolute pressure** — when the open end is exposed to atmosphere, the result is **gauge**; mention this explicitly. (Penalty: 0.5 mark)
> 3. **Sign convention errors in manometer problems** — work from the high-pressure side toward the open/known end, and label **+** for downward travel, **−** for upward travel. (Penalty: 1 mark)
> 4. **Mixing ρ and S.G.** in numerical problems — when a problem states "specific gravity = 13.6", multiply by **1000** to obtain ρ in **kg/m³**. (Penalty: 1 mark)
> 5. **Skipping units in the final answer** — always state **Pa, kPa, bar, or N/m²**. A bare number loses 0.5 mark.
> 6. **Forgetting g = 9.81 m/s²** — KTU expects this in numerical problems unless a problem specifies another value. (Penalty: 1 mark if completely missed)

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pressure Gauges — KTU 2024 One-Glance Recap:**

- **Pressure definition:** $P = F / A$ in **Pa (N/m²)**.
- **Hydrostatic pressure:** $P = \rho g h$.
- **Manometer working principle:** Equal pressure at the same horizontal level in a continuous static fluid (Pascal's principle).
- **U-tube (open-end) gauge pressure:** $P_{A,\text{gauge}} = \rho_m g h - \rho_f g z$.
- **Differential U-tube:** $P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$ — mercury heavier than flowing fluid.
- **Inverted U-tube:** $P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$ — manometric fluid *lighter* than flowing fluid.
- **Bourdon tube:** Curved elliptical-cross-section metal tube that straightens under pressure; tip motion → gear linkage → pointer.
- **Diaphragm gauge:** Thin disc deflects under pressure; deflection is proportional to pressure.
- **Dead-weight tester:** **Primary standard**; pressure derived from $P = W / A_p$ (plus friction correction). Used to *calibrate* other gauges.
- **Piezoelectric transducer:** Generates charge **Q = d · F** under stress; ideal for *dynamic* pressure.
- **Strain-gauge transducer:** Resistance change $\Delta R / R = G \cdot \varepsilon$; needs a Wheatstone bridge.
- **Capacitive transducer:** Capacitance change between plates under pressure deflection.
- **Standard atmospheric pressure:** **101.325 kPa = 1.01325 bar = 760 mmHg**.
- **Key constants to remember:** **g = 9.81 m/s²**, **ρ_Hg = 13,600 kg/m³**, **ρ_water = 1000 kg/m³**.
- **Conversions:** 1 bar = 10⁵ Pa, 1 kgf/cm² = 9.8066 × 10⁴ Pa.
- **Classification logic for exam:** Hydrostatic → Manometric; Mechanical → Bourdon/Diaphragm/Bellows; Dead-weight → Primary Standard; Electronic → Strain/Piezo/Capacitive/Resonant.
- **Exam tip:** Always draw a clear manometer diagram showing all fluid columns, levels, and labelled heights. Examiners award **0.5–1 mark** for a neat sketch even in numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energies in Flowing Fluid
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT741 - FLUID MECHANICS & MACHINERIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Energies in Flowing Fluid

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

In **Fluid Mechanics**, a fluid in motion possesses mechanical energy in three principal forms. The **total energy per unit weight** of a flowing fluid at any cross-section is the algebraic sum of **Pressure Energy**, **Kinetic Energy (Velocity Energy)**, and **Potential Energy (Datum Energy)**.

Mathematically, for an ideal (inviscid, incompressible) fluid flowing along a streamline:

$$
\frac{p}{\gamma} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + z = \text{Constant}
$$

Where each term carries the dimensions of **length (metres)**, representing **energy per unit weight** — also called **head**.

| Term | Engineering Name | Physical Significance |
|------|------------------|----------------------|
| $p/\gamma$ | Pressure Head | Energy due to fluid pressure |
| $V^2/2g$ | Velocity Head / Kinetic Head | Energy due to fluid motion |
| $z$ | Datum Head / Elevation Head | Energy due to position above reference |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** In the 2024 scheme, questions are frequently framed on the **physical interpretation of each energy head** and on recognizing that the constant in Bernoulli's equation represents the **Total Energy per unit weight (Total Head)** of the flowing fluid.

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Condition:** This identity is strictly valid only for **steady, incompressible, irrotational flow along a streamline with no friction losses and no shaft work**. For real systems, a **Head Loss (h_L)** and **Pump/Turbine head** terms must be appended.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Mountain River Story"

Imagine water tumbling down a rocky hillside from a reservoir at the top to a turbine at the bottom.

1. At the **reservoir**, the water is almost still but sits **high up** → it has high **Potential Energy** but low kinetic and pressure energy.
2. As it flows down a **narrow pipe**, the fluid speeds up → its **Kinetic Energy** rises, while potential energy drops.
3. Inside a **pump chamber**, mechanical work pushes the fluid → its **Pressure Energy** increases sharply.

The total of all three "balances the books" — energy is neither created nor destroyed (in an ideal scenario). This is the intuitive essence of **Energy Conservation in Flowing Fluids**, which is formalized as **Bernoulli's Equation**.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick (KTU Viva Hot-Spot):** Remember the three heads as **P-V-Z** standing for **Pressure head, Velocity head, and Datum (elevation) head** — the canonical sequence in the KTU 2024 textbook (Mod. 1, fluid kinematics & dynamics).

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Specific weight of water at 4°C:** $\gamma_w = 9.81\ \text{kN/m}^3 = 9810\ \text{N/m}^3$
- **Density of water:** $\rho_w = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$
- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$
- **Universal gas constant R:** $8.314\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$ (relevant when extending to compressible flow)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These constants appear in nearly every numerical problem in KTU university exams on this topic. Failing to substitute $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$ (not $10$) is the **single most common error** flagged by KTU external examiners.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Three Energy Heads — Decomposed

### 2.1.1 Pressure Energy (Pressure Head)
The energy a fluid possesses by virtue of its pressure is the **Pressure Energy per unit weight**, expressed as:

$$
\text{Pressure Head} = \frac{p}{\gamma}
$$

- **Why it exists:** Molecules in a fluid under pressure store potential to do work.
- **How it works:** In a static fluid column of height $h_p$, the pressure at the base is $p = \gamma h_p$, hence the equivalence of height to pressure.
- **Unit:** metres of fluid (m).

### 2.1.2 Kinetic Energy (Velocity Head)
The energy due to bulk fluid motion, expressed per unit weight:

$$
\text{Velocity Head} = \frac{V^2}{2g}
$$

- **Why it exists:** Any mass in motion possesses kinetic energy by Newton's laws.
- **How it works:** Dividing kinetic energy $\tfrac{1}{2}mv^2$ by weight $mg$ yields $\tfrac{V^2}{2g}$.
- **Unit:** metres.

### 2.1.3 Potential Energy (Datum Head)
The energy due to elevation above a chosen datum (reference horizontal plane):

$$
\text{Datum Head} = z
$$

- **Why it exists:** Work must be done to lift a fluid parcel against gravity.
- **Unit:** metres.

### 2.1.4 Total Energy / Total Head

$$
H = \frac{p}{\gamma} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + z
$$

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Identity | Description | Unit / Domain |
|---|-------------------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $E = \dfrac{p}{\gamma} + \dfrac{V^2}{2g} + z$ | Total energy per unit weight (Total Head) along a streamline | metres (m) |
| 2 | $\dfrac{p_1}{\gamma} + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \dfrac{p_2}{\gamma} + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2$ | Bernoulli's Equation (Ideal flow) | metres |
| 3 | $\dfrac{p_1}{\gamma} + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 + H_p = \dfrac{p_2}{\gamma} + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + H_t + h_L$ | Modified Bernoulli's Equation (Real flow with pump, turbine, and losses) | metres |
| 4 | $h_L = h_f + h_m$ | Total head loss = friction loss + minor losses | metres |
| 5 | $h_f = \dfrac{4fLV^2}{2gd}$ (Fanning) or $\dfrac{fLV^2}{2gd}$ (Darcy) | Frictional head loss in pipes | metres |
| 6 | $Q = A_1V_1 = A_2V_2$ | Continuity Equation (incompressible flow) | m³/s |
| 7 | $V = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$ | Average velocity from discharge and pipe diameter | m/s |
| 8 | $H_p = \dfrac{\text{Power}}{\gamma Q}$ | Pump head from imparted power | metres |
| 9 | $\text{HGL elevation} = z + \dfrac{p}{\gamma}$ | Hydraulic Grade Line vertical coordinate | metres |
| 10 | $\text{EGL elevation} = z + \dfrac{p}{\gamma} + \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | Energy Grade Line vertical coordinate | metres |
| 11 | $\text{EGL} - \text{HGL} = \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | Vertical separation between EGL and HGL | metres |
| 12 | $\gamma = \rho g$ | Specific weight from density | N/m³ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall Tip:** When applying modified Bernoulli's equation, students often confuse $H_p$ (head **added by pump**) with $H_t$ (head **extracted by turbine**). The direction of sign convention is the **single most-tested nuance** in 14-mark derivation problems.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Role of Energy Equation |
|-------------|-------------------------|
| **Venturi Meter / Orifice Meter** | Pressure drop directly gives flow velocity via Bernoulli + continuity |
| **Pitot Tube** | Measures stagnation pressure → velocity head → aircraft airspeed |
| **Hydraulic Machines (Pumps/Turbines)** | Net head across runner determines shaft power input/output |
| **Spillways, Weirs, Sluice Gates** | Energy at crest converted from pressure to velocity head |
| **Aircraft Wing Lift Generation** | Pressure differential from velocity head variation (Bernoulli) |
| **Pipe Network Design** | Power required by pump = $\gamma Q H_p$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-Module Link (KTU 2024):** Energy equations form the **backbone of Module 2 (Flow Measurement)** and **Module 3 (Hydraulic Turbines & Pumps)** — mastery here directly affects subsequent modules.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Bernoulli's Equation from Euler's Equation of Motion

### Step 1 — Establish Euler's Equation Along a Streamline

Consider an ideal fluid element flowing along a curved streamline. By applying Newton's second law to a small fluid particle of mass $\delta m$ moving along the streamline, we obtain **Euler's Equation of Motion**:

$$
\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial s} + g\frac{dz}{ds} + V\frac{\partial V}{\partial s} = 0
$$

Here $s$ denotes the coordinate along the streamline.

### Step 2 — Multiply Through by $ds$

$$
\frac{\partial p}{\rho} + g\,dz + V\,\partial V = 0
$$

### Step 3 — Recognise That $V\,\partial V = \tfrac{1}{2}\,d(V^2)$

This is a standard differential identity:

$$
\frac{d(V^2)}{dV} = 2V \quad\Longrightarrow\quad V\,dV = \tfrac{1}{2}\,d(V^2)
$$

Hence the Euler equation becomes:

$$
\frac{dp}{\rho} + g\,dz + \frac{1}{2}d(V^2) = 0
$$

### Step 4 — Integrate Between Two Points on the Same Streamline

Assuming the fluid is **incompressible**, $\rho$ is constant:

$$
\int_{1}^{2}\frac{dp}{\rho} + \int_{1}^{2}g\,dz + \int_{1}^{2}\frac{1}{2}d(V^2) = 0
$$

### Step 5 — Evaluate Each Integral

$$
\frac{p_2 - p_1}{\rho} + g(z_2 - z_1) + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} = 0
$$

### Step 6 — Divide Throughout by $g$

$$
\frac{p_2 - p_1}{\rho g} + (z_2 - z_1) + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g} = 0
$$

### Step 7 — Rearrange to Classical Bernoulli Form

$$
\frac{p_1}{\gamma} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\gamma} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2
$$

This identity is **Bernoulli's Equation** — the conservation statement for mechanical energy per unit weight along a streamline of an ideal fluid.

$$
\boxed{\;\dfrac{p}{\gamma} + \dfrac{V^2}{2g} + z = \text{Constant} = H\;}
$$

### Step 8 — Physical Interpretation of Each Term

| Term | Energy Per Unit Weight | Energy Per Unit Mass | Energy Per Unit Volume |
|------|------------------------|----------------------|------------------------|
| $p/\gamma$ | Pressure head | $p/\rho$ | $p$ |
| $V^2/2g$ | Velocity head | $V^2/2$ | $\rho V^2/2$ |
| $z$ | Datum head | $gz$ | $\rho g z$ |

> [!NOTE]
> Multiplying the **head form** of Bernoulli's equation by $\gamma$ yields **energy per unit volume**, and multiplying by $g$ yields **energy per unit mass** — all three forms are examinable in KTU 2024.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of Modified Bernoulli's Equation (Including Losses, Pump, and Turbine)

### Step 1 — Acknowledge Real-Flow Losses

In real fluids, viscous friction and local disturbances (bends, valves, expansions) dissipate mechanical energy as heat. This **head loss** must be subtracted from the right-hand side of Bernoulli's equation.

### Step 2 — Add Mechanical Work Inputs and Outputs

If a **pump** adds head $H_p$ between sections 1 and 2, and a **turbine** extracts head $H_t$:

$$
\frac{p_1}{\gamma} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 + H_p = \frac{p_2}{\gamma} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + H_t + h_L
$$

Where:

$$
h_L = h_f + h_m
$$

- $h_f$ = frictional (major) loss due to viscous shear at the walls
- $h_m$ = minor losses due to fittings, bends, sudden contractions/expansions, valves, etc.

### Step 3 — Express Each Loss Component

For a circular pipe using **Darcy's friction factor** $f$:

$$
h_f = \frac{4fLV^2}{2gd}
$$

For sudden expansion (Borda-Carnot loss):

$$
h_m = \frac{(V_1 - V_2)^2}{2g}
$$

### Step 4 — Final Modified Bernoulli's Equation

$$
\boxed{\;\dfrac{p_1}{\gamma} + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 + H_p = \dfrac{p_2}{\gamma} + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + H_t + h_f + h_m\;}
$$

### Step 5 — Derive the Power Transmitted to / Extracted from the Fluid

Multiplying both sides by $\gamma Q$ gives:

$$
\gamma Q H_p = \gamma Q H_t + \gamma Q h_L + \text{(kinetic + pressure + potential differences)}
$$

Hence:

$$
P_{\text{pump}} = \rho g Q H_p \quad\text{(watts)}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Exam Pattern

> **Problem (Model):** Water flows steadily through a tapering pipe. At section 1, the diameter is **150 mm**, gauge pressure is **98.1 kN/m²**, and velocity is **2.5 m/s**. At section 2, the diameter is **300 mm**, and section 2 lies **3 m above** section 1. If the head loss between the two sections is **2 m of water**, find the pressure at section 2. Take $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$.

### Solution Step-by-Step

**Step A — Apply Continuity to Find $V_2$:**

$$
A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \frac{\pi}{4}(0.15)^2 (2.5) = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.30)^2 (V_2)
$$

$$
V_2 = 2.5 \times \left(\frac{0.15}{0.30}\right)^2 = 2.5 \times (0.5)^2 = 0.625\ \text{m/s}
$$

**Step B — Identify Pressure Heads:**

$$
\frac{p_1}{\gamma} = \frac{98.1 \times 10^3}{9810} = 10\ \text{m of water}
$$

**Step C — Compute Velocity Heads:**

$$
\frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{(2.5)^2}{2(9.81)} = \frac{6.25}{19.62} = 0.3186\ \text{m}
$$

$$
\frac{V_2^2}{2g} = \frac{(0.625)^2}{2(9.81)} = \frac{0.3906}{19.62} = 0.0199\ \text{m}
$$

**Step D — Set Reference Datum at Section 1:**

$$
z_1 = 0,\quad z_2 = 3\ \text{m}
$$

**Step E — Apply Modified Bernoulli's Equation (1 → 2):**

$$
\frac{p_1}{\gamma} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\gamma} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L
$$

$$
10 + 0.3186 + 0 = \frac{p_2}{\gamma} + 0.0199 + 3 + 2
$$

$$
10.3186 = \frac{p_2}{\gamma} + 5.0199
$$

$$
\frac{p_2}{\gamma} = 10.3186 - 5.0199 = 5.2987\ \text{m}
$$

**Step F — Recover Pressure:**

$$
p_2 = 5.2987 \times 9810 = 51{,}980\ \text{N/m}^2 \approx 51.98\ \text{kN/m}^2
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $p_2 \approx 51.98\ \text{kN/m}^2$ (gauge)

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Energy Form Dominates?

| Flow Regime | Dominant Head | Reason |
|-------------|---------------|--------|
| Quiescent reservoir | Datum head ($z$) | $V \approx 0$, $p = \gamma h$ at free surface |
| Free jet from nozzle | Velocity head ($V^2/2g$) | Atmospheric pressure, $z$ drop |
| High-pressure penstock | Pressure head ($p/\gamma$) | $p$ very large |
| Slow viscous pipe flow | Pressure head + head loss | $V$ small but $h_L$ significant |
| Flow over spillway crest | Velocity head (max) | Datum head converted to kinetic |
| Flow downstream of pump | Pressure head spike | $H_p$ added by pump |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Energy Composition Flow (Block Architecture)

The following Mermaid block diagram traces how the three primitive energies combine into the **Total Head** and how the **HGL** and **EGL** relate to them.

```mermaid
graph LR
    n1["PRESSURE ENERGY P OVER GAMMA"] --> n4["TOTAL HEAD H"]
    n2["KINETIC ENERGY V SQUARED OVER 2g"] --> n4
    n3["POTENTIAL ENERGY Z"] --> n4
    n4 --> n5["ENERGY GRADE LINE EGL ELEVATION"]
    n4 --> n6["HYDRAULIC GRADE LINE HGL ELEVATION"]
    n2 --> n7["VELOCITY HEAD SEPARATION"]
    n5 --> n7
    n7 --> n6
    n5 --> n8["REPRESENTS TOTAL MECHANICAL ENERGY PER UNIT WEIGHT"]
    n6 --> n9["REPRESENTS PRESSURE PLUS DATUM HEAD ONLY"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Bernoulli's Equation

```mermaid
graph TB
    inp1["SECTION 1 INLET: p1 V1 z1"] --> sum1["SUM P1 OVER GAMMA PLUS V1 SQUARED OVER 2g PLUS z1"]
    inp2["PUMP HEAD Hp if any"] --> sum1
    sum1 --> loss["SUBTRACT HEAD LOSS hL FRICTION PLUS MINOR"]
    inp3["TURBINE HEAD Ht if any"] --> loss
    loss --> out1["SECTION 2 OUTLET: p2 V2 z2"]
    out1 --> sum2["SUM P2 OVER GAMMA PLUS V2 SQUARED OVER 2g PLUS z2"]
    sum2 --> check{"CONSERVATION EQUALITY HOLDS"}
    sum1 --> check
    check --> result["MODIFIED BERNOULLI EQUATION VERIFIED"]
```

## 4.3 Schematic: EGL & HGL Along a Converging Pipe With Pump

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["SECTION 1 LARGE DIAMETER LOW VELOCITY"] --> B["PUMP IMPARTS HEAD Hp"]
    B --> C["SECTION 2 SMALL DIAMETER HIGH VELOCITY"]
    A --> P1["PRESSURE HEAD P1 OVER GAMMA"]
    A --> D1["DATUM HEAD z1"]
    B --> HP["ADDED PUMP HEAD Hp"]
    C --> P2["PRESSURE HEAD P2 OVER GAMMA"]
    C --> VEL["HIGH VELOCITY HEAD V2 SQUARED OVER 2g"]
    P1 --> EGL1["EGL LEVEL AT SECTION 1"]
    D1 --> HGL1["HGL LEVEL AT SECTION 1"]
    HP --> EGL2["EGL RISES SHARPLY AT PUMP"]
    P2 --> EGL3["EGL LEVEL AT SECTION 2"]
    VEL --> GAP["EGL HGL GAP EQUALS VELOCITY HEAD"]
    EGL3 --> GAP
    EGL1 --> EGL2
    EGL2 --> EGL3
    HGL1 --> HGL2["HGL LEVEL AT SECTION 2"]
    EGL3 --> HGL2
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visual Takeaway:** In every horizontal converging pipe, the **HGL always rises** when the section diameter expands (and vice versa), because the **velocity head is transferred** to/from the **pressure head**. The **EGL always drops monotonically** due to friction unless a pump boosts it.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

---

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the following terms associated with energy in a flowing fluid:**
(a) Pressure head, (b) Velocity head, (c) Datum head.

#### Model Answer (3 Marks Distribution: 1 Mark Each)

**Pressure Head:** The energy possessed by a fluid per unit weight by virtue of its pressure. It is mathematically expressed as $p/\gamma$, where $p$ is the pressure intensity and $\gamma$ is the specific weight of the fluid. Its unit is metres of fluid. **(1 Mark)**

**Velocity Head:** The kinetic energy of the flowing fluid per unit weight. It is expressed as $V^2/(2g)$, where $V$ is the velocity of flow and $g$ is acceleration due to gravity. Its unit is also metres. **(1 Mark)**

**Datum Head:** The potential energy of the fluid per unit weight due to its elevation above a chosen horizontal reference plane (datum). It is represented by $z$ and has the unit of metres. **(1 Mark)**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**State Bernoulli's equation for the flow of an ideal fluid and write its three assumptions.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Bernoulli's Equation (2 Marks):**

For an ideal fluid flowing steadily along a streamline, the sum of pressure head, velocity head, and datum head remains constant between any two points:

$$
\frac{p_1}{\gamma} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\gamma} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2
$$

**Assumptions (½ Mark each — any three):**

1. The fluid is **ideal** (inviscid, no friction).
2. The flow is **steady** and **incompressible**.
3. The flow is along a **single streamline** (or irrotational, for the extended form).
4. **No external work** is added or extracted (no pump or turbine between sections).
5. **No energy losses** occur due to friction, bend, or obstruction.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Starting from Euler's equation of motion along a streamline, derive Bernoulli's equation for an ideal fluid. State clearly all the assumptions involved. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Water is flowing through a pipe of diameter **200 mm** at a velocity of **3 m/s**. The pipe has a venturi contraction to a diameter of **100 mm**. The pressure at the larger section is **120 kN/m²** (gauge). Calculate:
(i) The pressure at the contracted (throat) section, **(3 Marks)**
(ii) The discharge through the pipe, **(2 Marks)**
(iii) The head difference between the two sections in metres of water. **(2 Marks)**

---

### Model Answer — Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Bernoulli's Equation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Euler's Equation Along a Streamline (2 Marks):**
For a fluid particle moving along a curved streamline under the action of gravity and pressure forces, Newton's second law gives:

$$
\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial s} + g\frac{dz}{ds} + V\frac{\partial V}{\partial s} = 0
$$

Multiplying throughout by $ds$:

$$
\frac{dp}{\rho} + g\,dz + V\,dV = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Differential Identity (1 Mark):**
Recognising $V\,dV = \tfrac{1}{2}d(V^2)$:

$$
\frac{dp}{\rho} + g\,dz + \frac{1}{2}d(V^2) = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Integration (2 Marks):**
Integrating between points 1 and 2 along the same streamline, assuming $\rho = \text{constant}$ (incompressible):

$$
\int_{p_1}^{p_2}\frac{dp}{\rho} + \int_{z_1}^{z_2}g\,dz + \int_{V_1}^{V_2}\frac{1}{2}d(V^2) = 0
$$

$$
\frac{p_2 - p_1}{\rho} + g(z_2 - z_1) + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Rearrangement (1 Mark):**
Dividing by $g$ and rearranging:

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{p_1}{\gamma} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\gamma} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2\;}
$$

**Step 5 — Assumptions (1 Mark — list any three):**
1. Fluid is inviscid (no friction).
2. Flow is steady and incompressible.
3. Motion is along a streamline.
4. No shaft work or heat transfer.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $D_1 = 200\ \text{mm} = 0.20\ \text{m}$, $\quad V_1 = 3\ \text{m/s}$
- $D_2 = 100\ \text{mm} = 0.10\ \text{m}$
- $p_1 = 120\ \text{kN/m}^2 = 120{,}000\ \text{N/m}^2$
- $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2,\ \gamma_w = 9810\ \text{N/m}^3$

**Step 1 — Continuity to find $V_2$ (1 Mark):**

$$
V_2 = V_1 \left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^2 = 3 \times \left(\frac{0.20}{0.10}\right)^2 = 3 \times 4 = 12\ \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Bernoulli (no head loss, horizontal pipe $z_1 = z_2$) (2 Marks):**

$$
\frac{p_1}{\gamma} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{p_2}{\gamma} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}
$$

$$
\frac{120{,}000}{9810} + \frac{(3)^2}{2(9.81)} = \frac{p_2}{9810} + \frac{(12)^2}{2(9.81)}
$$

$$
12.232 + 0.4587 = \frac{p_2}{9810} + 7.339
$$

$$
\frac{p_2}{9810} = 12.691 - 7.339 = 5.352\ \text{m}
$$

**(i) Pressure at throat:**
$$
p_2 = 5.352 \times 9810 = 52{,}503\ \text{N/m}^2 \approx 52.50\ \text{kN/m}^2 \quad \textbf{(3 Marks)}
$$

**Step 3 — Discharge (2 Marks):**

$$
Q = A_1 V_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.20)^2 (3) = 0.0942\ \text{m}^3/\text{s} = 94.2\ \text{L/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Head Difference (2 Marks):**

$$
\Delta H = \frac{p_1 - p_2}{\gamma} = \frac{120{,}000 - 52{,}503}{9810} = \frac{67{,}497}{9810} = 6.88\ \text{m of water}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. Forgetting to **square** the diameter ratio in the continuity equation is the most common error ($-1$ mark).
> 2. Mixing up whether $z_1$ and $z_2$ are to be set equal — students often leave them symbolic in horizontal pipe problems ($-0.5$ marks).
> 3. Pressure must be in **N/m²**, not kN/m², before substituting into $p/\gamma$ — dividing 120 by 9810 is the fatal mistake ($-1$ mark).
> 4. Final answer must be to **3 significant figures** for full credit.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain, with a neat sketch, the **Energy Grade Line (EGL)** and **Hydraulic Grade Line (HGL)** for a pipe carrying fluid. Show how the EGL always lies above the HGL by an amount equal to the velocity head. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A horizontal pipe of diameter **300 mm** carries oil of specific gravity **0.85** at a velocity of **4 m/s**. At section A the pressure is **150 kN/m²**, and at section B (located 50 m downstream) the pressure is **140 kN/m²**. Determine:
(i) The head loss due to friction between A and B, **(4 Marks)**
(ii) The Darcy friction factor $f$, **(2 Marks)**
(iii) The power lost due to friction in kW. **(1 Mark)**

---

### Model Answer — Question B

#### Part (a) — EGL and HGL Explanation (7 Marks)

**The Hydraulic Grade Line (HGL): (3 Marks)**
The HGL is the locus of points representing the sum of **pressure head and datum head** at every cross-section along the pipe. Its elevation is given by:

$$
\text{HGL elevation} = z + \frac{p}{\gamma}
$$

In a static reservoir, the free water surface coincides with the HGL. The HGL is the level to which liquid would rise in a **piezometer tube** inserted into the pipe.

**The Energy Grade Line (EGL): (3 Marks)**
The EGL is the locus of points representing the **total energy head** at every cross-section. Its elevation is:

$$
\text{EGL elevation} = z + \frac{p}{\gamma} + \frac{V^2}{2g}
$$

The vertical distance between the EGL and the HGL at any section is exactly equal to the **velocity head** $V^2/(2g)$.

**Key Properties (1 Mark):**
- EGL always lies above HGL by $V^2/(2g)$.
- EGL is a horizontal line in an **ideal** frictionless pipe of constant diameter.
- EGL drops monotonically in real pipes due to friction.
- A pump causes a sudden **vertical jump** in the EGL equal to $H_p$.
- A turbine causes a sudden **vertical drop** in the EGL equal to $H_t$.

**Sketch (must be drawn — verbal description here for notes):**

```
EGL  ___________/\_______________ (Pump lifts EGL by Hp)
                |
                | (V²/2g gap)
                |
HGL  ___________ \________________
       |        |
     Piezometer Pipe
```

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $D = 300\ \text{mm} = 0.30\ \text{m}$, $\quad V = 4\ \text{m/s}$
- $SG = 0.85 \Rightarrow \gamma = 0.85 \times 9810 = 8338.5\ \text{N/m}^3$
- $p_A = 150\ \text{kN/m}^2 = 150{,}000\ \text{N/m}^2$
- $p_B = 140\ \text{kN/m}^2 = 140{,}000\ \text{N/m}^2$
- $L = 50\ \text{m},\quad g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$
- Horizontal pipe: $z_A = z_B$

**Step 1 — Apply Modified Bernoulli from A to B (no pump, no turbine):**

$$
\frac{p_A}{\gamma} + \frac{V_A^2}{2g} + z_A = \frac{p_B}{\gamma} + \frac{V_B^2}{2g} + z_B + h_L
$$

Since $D$ and $V$ are constant, $V_A = V_B = 4\ \text{m/s}$, hence the velocity heads cancel.

$$
\frac{p_A - p_B}{\gamma} = h_L
$$

**(i) Head Loss Calculation (4 Marks):**

$$
h_L = \frac{150{,}000 - 140{,}000}{8338.5} = \frac{10{,}000}{8338.5} = 1.199\ \text{m of oil}
$$

Equivalently in metres of water: $h_L = 1.199 \times 0.85 = 1.019\ \text{m of water}$.

**Step 2 — Apply Darcy's Formula for Friction Loss (2 Marks):**

$$
h_L = \frac{4fLV^2}{2gD}
$$

$$
1.199 = \frac{4 \cdot f \cdot 50 \cdot (4)^2}{2(9.81)(0.30)}
$$

$$
1.199 = \frac{3200\,f}{5.886}
$$

$$
f = \frac{1.199 \times 5.886}{3200} = \frac{7.057}{3200} = 0.002205
$$

**(ii) Darcy Friction Factor:**
$$
\boxed{f \approx 0.0022}
$$

**Step 3 — Power Lost Due to Friction (1 Mark):**

$$
P_{\text{loss}} = \gamma Q h_L
$$

Where $Q = A \cdot V = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.30)^2 (4) = 0.2827\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$

$$
P_{\text{loss}} = 8338.5 \times 0.2827 \times 1.199 = 2826\ \text{W} \approx 2.83\ \text{kW}
$$

**(iii) Power Lost:**
$$
\boxed{P_{\text{loss}} \approx 2.83\ \text{kW}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Using $\gamma_w$ (water) instead of $\gamma_{oil}$** when specific gravity is given — this is the most penalised error ($-2$ marks).
> 2. Forgetting to **cancel velocity head** when pipe diameter is constant in horizontal pipes ($-1$ mark).
> 3. **Friction factor confusion:** Darcy's factor $f$ vs Fanning's factor $f'$ — they differ by a factor of 4. KTU convention is **Darcy's** unless specified otherwise.
> 4. Failing to convert pipe length and diameter to **metres** before substituting ($-1$ mark).
> 5. **Unit consistency check is mandatory** in all 14-mark numericals — examiners specifically scan for this.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### 🎯 Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **Three energies** of a flowing fluid per unit weight: $p/\gamma$, $V^2/(2g)$, $z$.
- [x] **Bernoulli's Equation** states these three heads sum to a **constant** along a streamline in ideal flow.
- [x] **Modified Bernoulli's Equation** includes **head loss** $h_L$, **pump head** $H_p$, and **turbine head** $H_t$.
- [x] **HGL elevation** = $z + p/\gamma$ — shows where fluid rises in a piezometer.
- [x] **EGL elevation** = $z + p/\gamma + V^2/(2g)$ — total mechanical energy.
- [x] **EGL − HGL = velocity head** $V^2/(2g)$.
- [x] **Continuity** for incompressible flow: $A_1V_1 = A_2V_2 = Q$.
- [x] **Velocity from discharge:** $V = 4Q/(\pi D^2)$.
- [x] **Bernoulli assumptions**: ideal, steady, incompressible, along streamline, no work exchange.
- [x] **Pump work**: $P_{\text{pump}} = \rho g Q H_p$.
- [x] **Friction loss (Darcy):** $h_f = 4fLV^2/(2gD)$.
- [x] **Specific weight of water** $\gamma_w = 9810\ \text{N/m}^3$ at $4°\text{C}$.
- [x] **Direction of sign convention:** Pump adds to LHS; Turbine and losses subtract from RHS.

### ⚠️ Common Pitfalls to Avoid

1. Never forget to **square** the velocity difference in venturi/throat problems.
2. Never confuse **gauge pressure** with **absolute pressure** — KTU problems usually give gauge.
3. **Datum selection is arbitrary** — choose it to make calculations simplest (typically at the lower section).
4. **Hydraulic Grade Line** is the more useful engineering reference for **pressure analysis**.
5. **Energy Grade Line** is the more useful reference for **total energy / pump work analysis**.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU 2024 Viva Advice:** When asked "What are the energies in a flowing fluid?", answer confidently with the **head form** of Bernoulli's equation, then immediately follow up with the **modified form** (with $h_L$, $H_p$, $H_t$) — examiners reward the **complete** answer in NEP 2020-style outcome-based evaluation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Head - Pressure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT741 - FLUID MECHANICS & MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Head - Pressure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Fluid Mechanics**, the term **"Head"** refers to the height of a fluid column (usually water) that would produce a specific pressure at its base due to the force of gravity. When we talk about **"Pressure Head"** specifically, we are converting a pressure value into an equivalent height of fluid, thereby expressing pressure as a length quantity rather than a force-per-unit-area quantity.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEMRT741 Module 1):**
> Pressure head is defined as the height of a homogeneous fluid column that corresponds to a given hydrostatic pressure. Mathematically expressed as **h = P / (ρg)**, where:
> - **P** = Hydrostatic Pressure (in N/m² or Pascal)
> - **ρ** = Mass Density of the fluid (in kg/m³)
> - **g** = Acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**)

The pressure head is a fundamental concept because it allows engineers to add, subtract, and compare pressures directly with elevations (heads of fluid) using a single common unit: **metres (m)** of fluid. This unification is the bedrock of the famous **Bernoulli's Equation**, where all forms of mechanical energy in a flowing fluid (pressure energy, kinetic energy, and potential energy) are expressed in metres of fluid.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a tall, transparent vertical tube standing in a swimming pool. The water inside the tube rises to exactly the same level as the water outside. Why? Because the atmospheric pressure pushing down on the pool water matches the weight of the water column inside the tube.

Now, suppose you suck on the top of the tube (reducing the pressure inside). The water inside the tube rises *higher* than the pool level. The extra height to which the water climbs is called the **pressure head** — it directly represents the difference in pressure between the top of the tube and the atmosphere.

**Real-world connections every KTU student should recognize:**
- **Water supply tanks** placed at the top of buildings create "head pressure" to push water through taps on lower floors — no electric pump needed.
- **Dam walls** are thicker at the bottom because the pressure head of the stored water is maximum there.
- **Blood pressure** measured by doctors in **mmHg** (millimetres of Mercury) is literally a pressure head!
- **Tire pressure gauges** in PSI can be converted to a head of water for engineering comparison.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Intuition:** Pressure head is not a physical liquid height in most engineering problems — it is a *mathematical conversion* that lets us treat pressure as elevation. This is incredibly powerful because it lets us draw Energy Grade Lines (EGL) and Hydraulic Grade Lines (HGL) on a single diagram.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| Standard atmospheric pressure | P_atm | Pa (N/m²) | **101,325 Pa** |
| Atmospheric pressure head (water) | h_atm | m | **10.33 m of water** |
| Atmospheric pressure head (mercury) | h_atm | m | **0.76 m of Hg** |
| Acceleration due to gravity | g | m/s² | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Density of water at 4°C | ρ_water | kg/m³ | **1000 kg/m³** |
| Density of mercury at 0°C | ρ_Hg | kg/m³ | **13,600 kg/m³** |
| Specific weight of water | γ | N/m³ | **9810 N/m³** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hydrostatic Pressure Principle (Foundation)

To understand pressure head, we must first understand *why* pressure exists in a static fluid. Consider a point submerged at a depth **h** below the free surface of a liquid of density **ρ**. The pressure at that point arises purely from the **weight of the fluid column above it** pressing down.

### Step-by-Step Logic Flow:
1. **Take a fluid element** of cross-sectional area **A** at depth **h**.
2. **Calculate its volume**: V = A × h
3. **Calculate its mass**: m = ρ × V = ρ × A × h
4. **Calculate its weight (force)**: F = m × g = ρ × A × h × g
5. **Pressure is force per unit area**: P = F / A = (ρ × A × h × g) / A
6. **Cancel the area term**: P = ρgh

This derivation shows that pressure in a static fluid is **directly proportional to depth**. The deeper you go, the higher the pressure head — which is exactly why deep-sea submarines need reinforced hulls!

## 2.2 Converting Pressure to Head (The Core Operation)

The conversion is a simple algebraic rearrangement of P = ρgh:

**Given:** Pressure P (in Pa), fluid density ρ (in kg/m³), gravity g (in m/s²)
**Find:** Equivalent pressure head h (in metres)

**Procedure:**
- Step 1: Identify the fluid and look up its density ρ.
- Step 2: Compute the specific weight γ = ρ × g (this is the weight per unit volume).
- Step 3: Divide the pressure by the specific weight: **h = P / γ = P / (ρg)**

## 2.3 Why Pressure Head Matters in Engineering

- **Bernoulli's Equation:** All energy terms (P/ρg, V²/2g, z) are expressed as heads in metres, allowing direct algebraic addition.
- **Pump Calculations:** Pump head is the mechanical energy imparted per unit weight of fluid, expressed in metres.
- **Pipe Flow:** Head loss due to friction is calculated and subtracted from total head.
- **Hydraulic Structures:** Dam design, spillway design, and tank overflow all rely on head-based calculations.
- **Flow Measurement:** Devices like Pitot tubes and piezometers directly measure head.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hydrostatic Pressure | $P = \rho g h$ | Pressure at depth h in static fluid | Pa |
| 2 | Pressure Head | $h = \dfrac{P}{\rho g}$ | Converting pressure to head | m |
| 3 | Specific Weight | $\gamma = \rho g$ | Weight per unit volume | N/m³ |
| 4 | Head from Specific Weight | $h = \dfrac{P}{\gamma}$ | Alt. form using specific weight | m |
| 5 | Absolute Pressure | $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$ | True pressure from vacuum | Pa |
| 6 | Gauge Pressure | $P_{gauge} = P_{abs} - P_{atm}$ | Pressure above atmosphere | Pa |
| 7 | Vacuum Pressure | $P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$ | Pressure below atmosphere | Pa |
| 8 | Atmospheric Head (water) | $h_{atm} = \dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho_w g} \approx 10.33$ m | Std. atmosphere as water head | m |
| 9 | Manometer Equation | $h = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho g}$ | Head from manometer deflection | m |
| 10 | Total Head (Bernoulli) | $H = \dfrac{P}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V^2}{2g} + z$ | Sum of all energy heads | m |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Hack:** Always convert pressures to *head of the working fluid* before applying Bernoulli's equation. Never mix Pa and metres in the same calculation!

## 2.5 Reference Datum Concept

When we talk about pressure head, we need a **datum** — a reference horizontal line from which heads are measured. The choice of datum is arbitrary (any horizontal plane works), but it must remain **consistent** throughout the problem. Pressure head is *always positive* (below the free surface or measurement point), and elevation head **z** is measured from the chosen datum (positive upward).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Water Supply Engineering:** Pumps are rated by the head they can deliver (e.g., a "50-metre head pump" can lift water 50 m vertically).
- **Hydraulic Machines:** Turbine power output is calculated using net head across the runner.
- **Sewage Systems:** Gravity-driven flow depends on available head between inlet and outlet.
- **Building Plumbing:** Multi-storey buildings require booster pumps to maintain pressure head at upper floors.
- **Irrigation:** Sprinkler and drip systems rely on maintaining minimum pressure head at emitters.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Hydrostatic Pressure P = ρgh

**Setup:** Consider a static fluid of constant density ρ in a container. We want to find the pressure at a point located at depth h below the free surface of the liquid.

**Step 1 — Define the Control Volume:**
Imagine a vertical, cylindrical column of fluid of cross-sectional area A, extending from the free surface (top) down to the point of interest (bottom).

**Step 2 — Express the Volume of the Column:**
The volume V of the cylindrical fluid column is its base area multiplied by its height:

$$V = A \times h$$

**Step 3 — Express the Mass of the Column:**
Using the definition of mass density ρ = mass / volume, we rearrange to get:

$$m = \rho \times V = \rho \times A \times h$$

**Step 4 — Express the Weight (Gravitational Force) of the Column:**
Weight is mass times gravitational acceleration:

$$W = m \times g = \rho \times A \times h \times g$$

**Step 5 — Apply Newton's Equilibrium Condition:**
Since the fluid column is in static equilibrium, the forces acting on the bottom face must balance the weight above it. The atmospheric pressure P_atm acts downward on the top surface, and the fluid pressure P acts upward on the bottom surface. The weight W acts downward through the column's centre of gravity.

Force balance on the bottom face:

$$P \times A = P_{atm} \times A + W$$

**Step 6 — Substitute and Simplify:**

$$P \times A = P_{atm} \times A + \rho \times A \times h \times g$$

Dividing both sides by A:

$$P = P_{atm} + \rho g h$$

**Step 7 — Gauge Pressure Convention:**
In most engineering problems, we measure pressure *relative to atmospheric*. Defining gauge pressure as P_gauge = P − P_atm:

$$P_{gauge} = \rho g h$$

**Step 8 — Pressure Head Extraction:**
Rearranging for h to obtain the pressure head:

$$h = \dfrac{P_{gauge}}{\rho g}$$

**Conclusion:** This is the fundamental relationship used in every fluid mechanics problem at KTU. It establishes that pressure head is purely a geometric representation of pressure.

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Example: Converting Pump Pressure to Head

**Problem Statement:**
A pump delivers water at a gauge pressure of **4.905 bar**. Convert this pressure into:
(a) Pressure head in metres of water.
(b) Pressure head in millimetres of mercury (mmHg).

Given data:
- P = 4.905 bar = 4.905 × 10⁵ Pa = 490,500 Pa
- ρ_water = 1000 kg/m³
- ρ_Hg = 13,600 kg/m³
- g = 9.81 m/s²

**Part (a) — Head in metres of water:**

Apply the formula h = P / (ρg):

$$h_{water} = \dfrac{490{,}500}{1000 \times 9.81}$$

Compute the denominator first:

$$1000 \times 9.81 = 9810$$

Now divide:

$$h_{water} = \dfrac{490{,}500}{9810} = 50 \text{ m}$$

**Part (b) — Head in mm of mercury:**

Using mercury density ρ_Hg = 13,600 kg/m³:

$$h_{Hg} = \dfrac{P}{\rho_{Hg} \times g} = \dfrac{490{,}500}{13{,}600 \times 9.81}$$

Compute the denominator:

$$13{,}600 \times 9.81 = 133{,}416$$

Now divide:

$$h_{Hg} = \dfrac{490{,}500}{133{,}416} = 3.677 \text{ m} = 3676.9 \text{ mm}$$

**Verification using the ratio method:** Since pressure head is inversely proportional to density:

$$h_{Hg} = h_{water} \times \dfrac{\rho_{water}}{\rho_{Hg}} = 50 \times \dfrac{1000}{13{,}600} = 3.676 \text{ m}$$

This matches our direct calculation. ✓

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Pressure-Vs-Head Conversion Table (Water at 4°C)

| Pressure (kPa) | Pressure (bar) | Pressure (m H₂O) | Pressure (mmHg) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 9.81 | 0.0981 | 1.00 | 73.55 |
| 49.05 | 0.4905 | 5.00 | 367.76 |
| 98.10 | 0.9810 | 10.00 | 735.52 |
| 101.325 | 1.01325 | 10.33 | 760.00 |
| 196.20 | 1.9620 | 20.00 | 1471.04 |
| 490.50 | 4.9050 | 50.00 | 3677.60 |
| 981.00 | 9.8100 | 100.00 | 7355.20 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this:** 1 bar ≈ 10.197 m of water ≈ 750.06 mmHg. This is the most common conversion KTU examiners test.

## 3.4 Derivation: Pressure at the Same Horizontal Level (Pascal's Law Implication)

**Setup:** Two points A and B lie at the *same elevation* within a connected static fluid.

**Step 1:** Pressure at point A: P_A = P_atm + ρ g h_A
**Step 2:** Pressure at point B: P_B = P_atm + ρ g h_B
**Step 3:** Since h_A = h_B (same level), subtract: P_A − P_B = 0
**Step 4:** Conclusion: **P_A = P_B**

This is the principle behind manometers and shows why pressure depends only on *vertical depth*, not horizontal position.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Concept Map: The Pressure–Head Relationship

The following Mermaid block diagram maps out the conceptual flow from physical pressure to its head representation, illustrating how pressure head integrates with the broader energy-head framework of fluid mechanics.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical Pressure P in Pascal] --> B[Identify Working Fluid]
    B --> C{Fluid Type?}
    C --> D[Water rho equals 1000 kg per m3]
    C --> E[Mercury rho equals 13600 kg per m3]
    C --> F[Oil or Other rho variable]
    D --> G[Compute Specific Weight gamma equals rho times g]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Apply Formula h equals P divided by gamma]
    H --> I[Pressure Head h in metres]
    I --> J[Use in Bernoulli Equation]
    J --> K[Pressure Head P by rho g]
    J --> L[Velocity Head V squared by 2g]
    J --> M[Elevation Head z]
    K --> N[Total Energy Head H]
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Apply to Pumps Turbines and Pipe Networks]
    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000
    style I fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    style N fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000
    style O fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Pressure Measurement → Head Conversion

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Static Fluid Body] --> B[Hydrostatic Pressure P]
    B --> C[Piezometer Tube]
    B --> D[U-Tube Manometer]
    B --> E[Bourdon Gauge]
    C --> F[Direct Height Reading h in metres]
    D --> G[Deflection delta h]
    G --> H[Apply Manometer Equation h equals delta P by rho g]
    E --> I[Gauge Dial Reading in Pa or bar]
    I --> J[Convert using h equals P by rho g]
    F --> K[Pressure Head Output]
    H --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Energy Grade Line EGL]
    K --> M[Hydraulic Grade Line HGL]
    K --> N[Pump Characteristic Curve]
    style A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,color:#000
    style K fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f9a825,color:#000
    style L fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#388e3c,color:#000
    style M fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#388e3c,color:#000
    style N fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#e64a19,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Process Flow: How a Piezometer Measures Pressure Head

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Open Vertical Tube Inserted into Pipe] --> B[Fluid Rises Inside Tube]
    B --> C[Height Reaches Equilibrium]
    C --> D[Atmospheric Pressure Acts on Top]
    D --> E[Pressure at Base equals P at Pipe Centerline]
    E --> F[Rise Height h equals Pressure Head]
    F --> G[Direct Readout of P by rho g]
    style A fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#6a1b9a,color:#000
    style F fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    style G fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define pressure head. State its SI unit and explain its physical significance.**

**Model Answer:**
Pressure head is defined as the height of a fluid column that produces a given hydrostatic pressure at its base. It is mathematically expressed as **h = P / (ρg)**, where P is the pressure, ρ is the density of the fluid, and g is the acceleration due to gravity.

**SI Unit:** metres (m)

**Physical Significance:** Pressure head allows pressures to be expressed as equivalent heights of fluid, enabling direct comparison with elevation and velocity heads in Bernoulli's equation. It represents the *potential energy per unit weight* of the fluid due to pressure.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A pipe carries water at a gauge pressure of 2.5 bar. Calculate the pressure head of water in metres.**

**Model Answer:**
Given: P = 2.5 bar = 2.5 × 10⁵ Pa = 250,000 Pa
ρ = 1000 kg/m³, g = 9.81 m/s²

Apply the formula:

$$h = \dfrac{P}{\rho g} = \dfrac{250{,}000}{1000 \times 9.81} = \dfrac{250{,}000}{9810} = 25.48 \text{ m}$$

**Pressure head = 25.48 m of water**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`**

**(a)** Derive the expression for hydrostatic pressure at a point in a static fluid and define the term *pressure head*. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A U-tube manometer containing mercury is connected to a pipe carrying water. The manometer shows a deflection of 200 mm of Hg. Find the pressure of water in the pipe in (i) kPa, and (ii) metres of water head. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

Consider a static fluid of uniform density ρ. Take a cylindrical fluid column of cross-sectional area A and height h below the free surface.

The volume of the column:

$$V = A h$$

The mass:

$$m = \rho V = \rho A h$$

The weight of the column (downward force):

$$W = mg = \rho A h g$$

This weight is balanced by the net upward pressure force on the base of the column. If P is the pressure at depth h and P_atm is the atmospheric pressure on the free surface:

$$P \times A = P_{atm} \times A + \rho A h g$$

Dividing by A:

$$P = P_{atm} + \rho g h$$

The **gauge pressure** at depth h is:

$$P_{gauge} = \rho g h$$

Rearranging, the **pressure head** is defined as:

$$h = \dfrac{P_{gauge}}{\rho g}$$

Pressure head is the height of the fluid column that would produce the given pressure at its base.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:**
- Manometer deflection: x = 200 mm = 0.2 m of Hg
- ρ_water = 1000 kg/m³, ρ_Hg = 13,600 kg/m³
- g = 9.81 m/s²

**Step 1 — Pressure from mercury deflection:**

$$P = \rho_{Hg} \times g \times x = 13{,}600 \times 9.81 \times 0.2$$

$$P = 13{,}600 \times 1.962 = 26{,}683.2 \text{ Pa}$$

**Wait** — recalculation: 9.81 × 0.2 = 1.962; then 13,600 × 1.962 = 26,683.2 Pa

This pressure must equal the water pressure in the pipe (assuming the manometer tap is at the pipe centreline).

**Part (i) — Pressure in kPa:**

$$P = 26{,}683.2 \text{ Pa} = 26.68 \text{ kPa}$$

**Part (ii) — Pressure head in metres of water:**

$$h_{water} = \dfrac{P}{\rho_{water} \times g} = \dfrac{26{,}683.2}{1000 \times 9.81}$$

$$h_{water} = \dfrac{26{,}683.2}{9810} = 2.72 \text{ m of water}$$

**Answers:**
- (i) P = **26.68 kPa**
- (ii) h = **2.72 m of water**

---

### **Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch how a piezometer and a U-tube manometer measure pressure head. Compare their working principles. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A tank of oil (specific gravity 0.85) is 6 m deep. Find: (i) the gauge pressure at the bottom of the tank in kPa, and (ii) the pressure head in metres of water and metres of oil. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Piezometer:**
A piezometer is a simple open vertical tube connected to a pipe or vessel in which pressure is to be measured. The fluid rises in the tube to a height where the weight of the fluid column balances the pressure at the connection point. The height of the fluid column above the tap is the **pressure head** directly.

**Limitation:** Can only measure pressures greater than atmospheric, and only works with the *same fluid* (no mercury). Unsuitable for high pressures (tube becomes impractically tall).

**U-tube Manometer:**
A U-tube manometer is a U-shaped tube partially filled with a heavy manometric fluid (typically mercury). One limb connects to the pipe; the other is open to atmosphere (or another reference). The pressure at the pipe pushes the manometric fluid, causing a height difference (deflection) Δh.

The pressure is calculated as:

$$P_{pipe} = \rho_{manometer} \times g \times \Delta h \pm \rho_{fluid} \times g \times y$$

where y is the height difference of the working fluid above the manometer.

**Advantages of U-tube manometer:** Can measure high pressures, can use mercury (which gives small Δh for large pressures), and can handle different fluids.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Piezometer | U-tube Manometer |
|---|---|---|
| Construction | Simple vertical tube | U-shaped with heavy fluid |
| Best for | Low pressures, same fluid | High pressures, any fluid |
| Accuracy | Less accurate | More accurate |
| Range | Limited | Wide range possible |

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:**
- Specific gravity of oil: S = 0.85, so ρ_oil = 0.85 × 1000 = **850 kg/m³**
- Depth: h = 6 m
- g = 9.81 m/s²

**Part (i) — Gauge pressure at the bottom:**

$$P_{gauge} = \rho_{oil} \times g \times h = 850 \times 9.81 \times 6$$

Compute step by step:
- 850 × 9.81 = 8,338.5
- 8,338.5 × 6 = 50,031 Pa = **50.03 kPa**

**Part (ii) — Pressure head:**

*In metres of oil:*

$$h_{oil} = \dfrac{P}{\rho_{oil} \times g} = \dfrac{50{,}031}{850 \times 9.81} = \dfrac{50{,}031}{8{,}338.5} = 6.0 \text{ m of oil}$$

(Sanity check: this simply returns the original depth, as expected! ✓)

*In metres of water:*

$$h_{water} = \dfrac{P}{\rho_{water} \times g} = \dfrac{50{,}031}{1000 \times 9.81} = \dfrac{50{,}031}{9810} = 5.10 \text{ m of water}$$

**Verification using specific gravity:**

$$h_{water} = S \times h_{oil} = 0.85 \times 6.0 = 5.10 \text{ m} \checkmark$$

**Answers:**
- (i) P = **50.03 kPa**
- (ii) h = **6.0 m of oil = 5.10 m of water**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Deduction Pitfalls in Pressure-Head Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert bar to Pa:** 1 bar = 10⁵ Pa. Mixing units is the #1 reason students lose 2-3 marks.
> 2. **Using gauge pressure where absolute is needed:** Read the question carefully! *"Atmospheric pressure = 101.3 kPa"* is a hint to use absolute pressure.
> 3. **Manometer direction sign errors:** When the manometer fluid is pushed *down* on the pipe side and *up* on the open side, the pressure at the pipe is *higher* than atmospheric. Reverse logic applies for vacuum measurements.
> 4. **Specific gravity confusion:** SG is dimensionless. To get density, multiply SG × 1000 kg/m³.
> 5. **Mixing fluid heads:** Never write "5 m of oil + 3 m of water" without converting both to a common fluid first. Always convert to metres of the *same* fluid before adding.
> 6. **Not specifying the datum:** In multi-point problems, define your datum clearly at the start of the solution to avoid elevation-head sign errors.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Core Definition:** Pressure head h = P / (ρg) — the height of fluid column equivalent to a given pressure.
- **Hydrostatic Pressure:** P = ρgh — directly proportional to depth and density.
- **Units:** Pressure in Pa or N/m²; Pressure head in **metres (m)**.
- **Specific Weight:** γ = ρg — use this for cleaner calculations.
- **Atmospheric Pressure Head:** 10.33 m of water ≈ 0.76 m of Hg ≈ 1.01325 bar.
- **Absolute vs Gauge:** Absolute = Atmospheric + Gauge; Vacuum = Atmospheric − Absolute.
- **Conversion Factors to Memorize:**
   - 1 bar ≈ 10.197 m H₂O
   - 1 m H₂O ≈ 73.55 mmHg
   - 1 kgf/cm² ≈ 10 m H₂O
- **Pascal's Law Implication:** Pressure is the same at all points on the same horizontal level in a connected static fluid.
- **Manometer Equation:** P_pipe = ρ_manometer × g × Δh (for simple mercury manometer).
- **Application Areas:** Pump head calculations, turbine net head, Bernoulli's equation, EGL/HGL diagrams.
- **Always Identify Datum:** Choose a horizontal reference line and stick with it throughout.
- **Dimension Check:** [P/ρg] = (N/m²) / (kg/m³ × m/s²) = (N/m²) / (N/m³) = m ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Tip:** If a problem gives pressure in multiple units (bar, kPa, psi, m of fluid), **convert all to pressure head in metres of the working fluid FIRST**, then solve. This single habit will save you from 80% of unit-conversion mistakes.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT741 - FLUID MECHANICS & MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Dynamic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dynamic Viscosity (Absolute Viscosity, μ):** Dynamic viscosity is defined as the **shear stress** developed per unit **rate of shear strain (velocity gradient)** in a flowing fluid. It quantifies the internal resistance of a fluid to flow under an applied tangential (shearing) force.

Mathematically, it is derived directly from **Newton's Law of Viscosity**:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

Where:
- $\tau$ = Shear stress (N/m²)
- $\mu$ = Dynamic viscosity (N·s/m² or Pa·s)
- $\dfrac{du}{dy}$ = Velocity gradient or rate of shear strain (s⁻¹)

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In the KTU 2024 Scheme, dynamic viscosity is positioned under **Module 1 – Properties of Fluids**, specifically under the sub-topic *"Viscosity – Newton's law of viscosity, Dynamic and Kinematic viscosity."* This is one of the most frequently tested 3-mark and 14-mark concepts in the entire syllabus.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two parallel glass plates with a thin layer of **honey** trapped between them. Now imagine trying to slide the top plate while the bottom plate remains fixed. You will immediately feel a **strong resistive force** — this resistance is what dynamic viscosity measures.

- **Honey** has a **high dynamic viscosity** (~10 Pa·s) → it resists flow strongly.
- **Water** has a **low dynamic viscosity** (~0.001 Pa·s) → it flows almost effortlessly.
- **Air** has an even **lower** dynamic viscosity (~1.8 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s) → almost no perceptible resistance.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic:** Dynamic viscosity = "**D**rag **M**etric **U**nits." Think of $\mu$ as a fluid's internal "**stickiness**" — the higher the $\mu$, the stickier the fluid.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard reference values are **must-remember** for KTU exams:

| Fluid | Dynamic Viscosity (μ) | Temperature |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Water** | **1.002 × 10⁻³ Pa·s** | 20 °C |
| **Air** | **1.81 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s** | 20 °C |
| **Mercury** | **1.55 × 10⁻³ Pa·s** | 20 °C |
| **Blood (whole)** | **3–4 × 10⁻³ Pa·s** | 37 °C |
| **SAE 30 Oil** | **0.29 Pa·s** | 20 °C |

---

**Standard Units of Dynamic Viscosity:**

- **SI Unit:** N·s/m² or **Pa·s** (Pascal-second)
- **CGS Unit:** **Poise (P)** — named after Jean Léonard Marie Poiseuille
- **Relationship:** 1 Pa·s = **10 Poise** = 1000 Centipoise (cP)
- **Dimensional Formula:** **[M L⁻¹ T⁻¹]**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Newton's Law of Viscosity — The Foundation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Newton's Law of Viscosity states:** *"The shear stress developed in a fluid undergoing deformation is directly proportional to the rate of shear strain."*

This is valid only for **Newtonian fluids**. Fluids that do not obey this linear relationship are termed **Non-Newtonian fluids**.

### 2.2 Structured Logic Breakdown

**Step 1 — Consider Two Parallel Plates:**
- Stationary bottom plate and a top plate moving with velocity $U$ in the $+x$ direction.
- Fluid film of thickness $h$ is sandwiched between them.

**Step 2 — Velocity Profile (Linear for Newtonian Fluid):**
- Fluid in direct contact with the stationary plate has velocity = 0 (**No-Slip Condition**).
- Fluid in contact with the moving plate has velocity = $U$.
- A linear velocity gradient develops across the gap.

**Step 3 — Define Rate of Shear Strain:**
- Rate of change of deformation = $\dfrac{du}{dy}$ (velocity change per unit perpendicular distance).

**Step 4 — Apply Newton's Hypothesis:**
- Newton postulated that shear stress is proportional to velocity gradient:

$$\tau \propto \frac{du}{dy}$$

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

**Step 5 — The Constant of Proportionality:**
- The constant $\mu$ is the **dynamic viscosity** — a fluid property that varies with temperature (and slightly with pressure).

### 2.3 Newtonian vs. Non-Newtonian Fluids

| Type | Behavior | Examples |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Newtonian** | Linear: $\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$ | Water, Air, Mercury, Thin oils |
| **Bingham Plastic** | Yield stress then linear | Toothpaste, Drilling mud |
| **Pseudo-plastic (Shear-thinning)** | Viscosity decreases with shear rate | Blood, Ketchup, Paint |
| **Dilatant (Shear-thickening)** | Viscosity increases with shear rate | Cornstarch + water slurry |
| **Thixotropic** | Viscosity decreases over time at constant shear | Honey, Printing ink |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are HIGH-YIELD for KTU 2024 Board Exams and have appeared repeatedly in past University Examinations.**

| # | Formula | Description | SI Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Newton's Law of Viscosity | Pa (N/m²) |
| 2 | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | Kinematic Viscosity | m²/s |
| 3 | $\mu = \rho \nu$ | Dynamic Viscosity in terms of kinematic viscosity | Pa·s |
| 4 | $F = \mu A \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Viscous Shear Force on a fluid layer | N |
| 5 | $\mu \approx \mu_0 \left[1 - \alpha(T - T_0)\right]$ | Temperature effect (gases) | Pa·s |
| 6 | $\mu = \mu_0 \, e^{\beta\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)}$ | Temperature effect (liquids, Andrade eqn.) | Pa·s |
| 7 | $\dfrac{\mu}{\mu_w} = 10^{\left[\dfrac{1.327(20 - T) - 0.001(T - 20)^2}{T + 105}\right]}$ | Viscosity of water at any temperature $T$ (°C) | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **Conversion Pitfall:** Many students confuse **Poise** with **Pa·s**. Remember: **1 Pa·s = 10 Poise**. So a viscosity given as **0.001 Pa·s** equals **0.01 Poise = 1 Centipoise (cP)** — this exact conversion is a frequent 3-mark question.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hydraulic Lubrication Systems:** Selecting correct lubricant viscosity ensures bearing life. (Mechanical Eng.)
- **Pipeline Design (Oil & Gas):** Pressure drop calculations depend directly on $\mu$. (Chemical/Civil Eng.)
- **Blood Flow Modeling (Biomedical Eng.):** Apparent viscosity of blood governs arterial flow dynamics.
- **Aerodynamics:** Skin friction drag on aircraft is a direct function of $\mu_{air}$.
- **Fire Sprinkler Systems:** Spray droplet dynamics rely on dynamic viscosity of water.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Viscous Shear Force Between Two Parallel Plates

**Problem Setup:**
A thin fluid film of thickness $h$ is trapped between two parallel plates, each of area $A$. The lower plate is stationary; the upper plate moves with velocity $U$. Determine the force required to maintain this motion.

**Step 1 — Apply the No-Slip Condition:**

The fluid layer in contact with the stationary lower plate has zero velocity, and the fluid in contact with the moving upper plate has velocity $U$.

**Step 2 — Establish the Linear Velocity Profile:**

For a Newtonian fluid, velocity varies linearly between the two plates:

$$u(y) = \frac{U}{h} \, y$$

This implies that the velocity at any height $y$ above the lower plate is proportional to $y$.

**Step 3 — Compute the Velocity Gradient (Rate of Shear Strain):**

Differentiating the velocity profile with respect to $y$:

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{d}{dy}\left(\frac{U}{h}\,y\right) = \frac{U}{h}$$

This is a constant, confirming that the rate of shear strain is uniform across the gap.

**Step 4 — Apply Newton's Law of Viscosity:**

The shear stress at any fluid layer is given by:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

Substituting the velocity gradient:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{U}{h}$$

**Step 5 — Convert Shear Stress to Force:**

Since $\tau$ is force per unit area, the total viscous resisting force on the upper plate is:

$$F = \tau \cdot A = \mu A \frac{U}{h}$$

$$\boxed{F = \frac{\mu A U}{h}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** This exact formula is the **most commonly tested 14-mark derivation** in KTU Module 1. Memorize the setup, the no-slip condition, and the substitution steps precisely.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Relationship Between Dynamic and Kinematic Viscosity

**Step 1 — Definition of Specific Weight:**

$$\gamma = \rho \, g$$

Where $\rho$ is mass density and $g$ is acceleration due to gravity.

**Step 2 — Definition of Specific Mass (Density):**

$$\rho = \frac{m}{V} \quad \text{(kg/m}^3\text{)}$$

**Step 3 — Dynamic Viscosity Definition:**

From Newton's law:

$$\mu = \tau \cdot \frac{dy}{du} \quad \text{(Pa·s)}$$

**Step 4 — Kinematic Viscosity Definition:**

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and Simplify:**

The SI units:

$$[\mu] = \text{N·s/m}^2 = \text{kg/(m·s)}$$

$$[\rho] = \text{kg/m}^3$$

$$[\nu] = \frac{[\mu]}{[\rho]} = \frac{\text{kg/(m·s)}}{\text{kg/m}^3} = \text{m}^2/\text{s}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} \quad \text{with units of m}^2/\text{s}}$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A fluid of dynamic viscosity $\mu = 0.1$ N·s/m² flows between two parallel plates separated by a distance of **5 mm**. The upper plate moves at **1 m/s** while the lower plate is stationary. The plate area is **0.5 m²**. Calculate:
1. The velocity gradient.
2. The shear stress on the plate.
3. The viscous force resisting motion.

**Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $\mu = 0.1$ N·s/m²
- $h = 5 \text{ mm} = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $U = 1$ m/s
- $A = 0.5$ m²

**Part (a) — Velocity Gradient:**

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{U}{h} = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 200 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Part (b) — Shear Stress:**

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy} = 0.1 \times 200 = 20 \text{ N/m}^2$$

**Part (c) — Viscous Force:**

$$F = \tau \cdot A = 20 \times 0.5 = 10 \text{ N}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Always write units explicitly** — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks per question for missing unit specifications.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Temperature on Dynamic Viscosity

| Property | **Liquids (e.g., Water)** | **Gases (e.g., Air)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| As temperature **increases** | Dynamic viscosity **decreases** | Dynamic viscosity **increases** |
| Reason | Intermolecular bonds weaken; molecules slide past more easily | Increased molecular momentum transfer between layers |
| Governing Equation | $\mu = \mu_0 \, e^{\beta(1/T - 1/T_0)}$ | $\mu \approx \mu_0 \, (T/T_0)^{3/2} \cdot \frac{T_0 + S}{T + S}$ (Sutherland's Law) |
| Practical Example | Hot honey pours faster than cold honey | Hot air exerts more drag than cold air |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Newton's Law of Viscosity — Physical Setup

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Stationary Bottom Plate\nVelocity = 0"]:::plateStyle
    B["Fluid Layer at y = 0\nVelocity = 0"]:::fluidStyle
    C["Fluid Layer at y = y1\nVelocity = u1"]:::fluidStyle
    D["Fluid Layer at y = y2\nVelocity = u2"]:::fluidStyle
    E["Moving Top Plate\nVelocity = U"]:::plateStyle
    F["Shear Stress tau\ntau = mu * du/dy"]:::stressStyle

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F

    classDef plateStyle fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef fluidStyle fill:#FFF9E6,stroke:#FFA500,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef stressStyle fill:#E8F8E8,stroke:#2CA02C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> **Interpretation:** This block-flow schematic shows the layered nature of fluid flow between two plates. Each adjacent fluid layer slides relative to the next, generating internal shear stress proportional to the local velocity gradient. The variable $\mu$ (dynamic viscosity) acts as the coupling constant that converts motion differences into resistance.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Dynamic vs. Kinematic Viscosity Relationships

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Dynamic Viscosity\nmu (Pa.s)"]:::dynamicStyle
    B["Density\nrho (kg/m3)"]:::densityStyle
    C["Kinematic Viscosity\nnu = mu / rho (m2/s)"]:::kinematicStyle
    D["Specific Weight\ngamma = rho * g (N/m3)"]:::weightStyle
    E["Dynamic Viscosity in CGS\nmu_cgs = mu / 10 (Poise)"]:::cgsStyle

    A --> C
    B --> C
    A --> E
    B --> D

    classDef dynamicStyle fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef densityStyle fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef kinematicStyle fill:#F0FFF0,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef weightStyle fill:#FFF0F5,stroke:#FF1493,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef cgsStyle fill:#FFFAF0,stroke:#D2691E,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Viscosity

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Input: Applied Force F"]:::inputStyle --> P2["Top Plate\nTranslates with Velocity U"]:::plateStyle
    P2 --> P3["Fluid Film\nDynamic Viscosity mu"]:::fluidStyle
    P3 --> P4["Velocity Gradient\ndu/dy = U/h"]:::gradientStyle
    P4 --> P5["Shear Stress\ntau = mu * du/dy"]:::stressStyle
    P5 --> P6["Resisting Force\nF_resist = tau * A"]:::outputStyle
    P6 --> P7["Output: Equilibrium\nF_applied = F_resist"]:::resultStyle

    classDef inputStyle fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef plateStyle fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef fluidStyle fill:#FFF9E6,stroke:#FFA500,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef gradientStyle fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef stressStyle fill:#E8F8E8,stroke:#2CA02C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outputStyle fill:#FFEFD5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef resultStyle fill:#F5FFFA,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> **Interpretation:** This block architecture traces the physical mechanism: an external force moves the top plate, the fluid layer resists via dynamic viscosity, generating a velocity gradient, which produces a shear stress, which finally results in a resisting force that balances the input. This mirrors the **cause → effect → equilibrium** flow that KTU examiners expect in derivations.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

Define **Dynamic Viscosity**. State its **SI unit** and **dimensional formula**.

**Model Answer:**

> Dynamic Viscosity ($\mu$) is defined as the property of a fluid that offers resistance to the relative motion between its adjacent layers. Mathematically, from Newton's law of viscosity, it is the ratio of shear stress to the rate of shear strain:
> 
> $$\mu = \frac{\tau}{du/dy}$$
> 
> - **SI Unit:** N·s/m² or **Pa·s**
> - **CGS Unit:** **Poise** (1 Pa·s = 10 Poise)
> - **Dimensional Formula:** **[M L⁻¹ T⁻¹]**

**(3 Marks)** — 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for SI unit + 1 mark for dimensional formula.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

Differentiate between **Dynamic Viscosity** and **Kinematic Viscosity**.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Dynamic Viscosity ($\mu$) | Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Definition** | Resistance to shear deformation | Resistance to flow under gravity |
| **Formula** | $\mu = \tau \, / \, (du/dy)$ | $\nu = \mu \, / \, \rho$ |
| **SI Unit** | Pa·s or N·s/m² | m²/s |
| **CGS Unit** | Poise | Stoke (cm²/s) |
| **Dimensional Formula** | $[M L^{-1} T^{-1}]$ | $[L^2 T^{-1}]$ |

**(3 Marks)** — 1 mark each for any three distinguishing points.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** State and explain **Newton's Law of Viscosity**. Derive the expression for the **viscous force** acting on a fluid layer between two parallel plates, one moving and one stationary. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A plate of area **0.6 m²** is separated from a stationary plate by a film of oil of viscosity **0.05 N·s/m²** and thickness **1.5 mm**. Find: **(i)** the force required to drag the plate at a velocity of **0.3 m/s**, and **(ii)** the resulting shear stress. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a):

**Newton's Law Statement (2 marks):**

> *"The shear stress developed in a fluid is directly proportional to the rate of shear strain (velocity gradient) and the constant of proportionality is called the dynamic viscosity."*
> 
> $$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

**Explanation (2 marks):**

Consider a fluid between two parallel plates. The lower plate is stationary, the upper plate moves with velocity $U$. The fluid adheres to both surfaces due to the **no-slip condition**. Layers of fluid slide relative to each other, generating a shear stress $\tau$. The faster layer drags the slower layer along, but is itself retarded by an internal viscous resistance.

**Derivation (3 marks):**

Let $h$ be the gap between plates, $A$ be the area. Velocity gradient:

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{U}{h}$$

Shear stress:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{U}{h}$$

Force on the upper plate:

$$\boxed{F = \tau \cdot A = \frac{\mu A U}{h}}$$

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b):

**Given:**
- $A = 0.6$ m²
- $\mu = 0.05$ N·s/m²
- $h = 1.5 \text{ mm} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $U = 0.3$ m/s

**(i) Force Required:**

$$F = \frac{\mu A U}{h} = \frac{0.05 \times 0.6 \times 0.3}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$F = \frac{0.009}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 6 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F = 6 \text{ N}}$$

**(ii) Shear Stress:**

$$\tau = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{6}{0.6} = 10 \text{ N/m}^2$$

$$\boxed{\tau = 10 \text{ N/m}^2}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between **dynamic viscosity** and **kinematic viscosity**. A fluid has a dynamic viscosity of **0.065 N·s/m²** and density **780 kg/m³**. Find its kinematic viscosity in **Stokes**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **effect of temperature on dynamic viscosity** of liquids and gases. Why does honey become less viscous when heated? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a):

**Derivation (4 marks):**

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

Where:
- $\mu$ = Dynamic viscosity (Pa·s)
- $\rho$ = Mass density (kg/m³)
- $\nu$ = Kinematic viscosity (m²/s)

Dimensional check:

$$[\nu] = \frac{[\mu]}{[\rho]} = \frac{\text{Pa·s}}{\text{kg/m}^3} = \frac{\text{kg/(m·s)}}{\text{kg/m}^3} = \frac{\text{m}^2}{\text{s}}$$

**Numerical Computation (3 marks):**

Given:
- $\mu = 0.065$ N·s/m²
- $\rho = 780$ kg/m³

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{0.065}{780} = 8.333 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

Convert to **Stokes** (1 Stoke = 10⁻⁴ m²/s):

$$\nu = \frac{8.333 \times 10^{-5}}{10^{-4}} = 0.8333 \text{ Stoke}$$

$$\boxed{\nu = 0.8333 \text{ Stoke}}$$

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b):

**For Liquids (3 marks):**

As temperature **increases**, dynamic viscosity of liquids **decreases**. This is because:
- Molecular bonds (cohesive forces) weaken with temperature.
- Molecules gain kinetic energy and slide past each other more easily.
- The empirical **Andrade equation** applies:

$$\mu = \mu_0 \, e^{\beta \left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)}$$

**For Gases (2 marks):**

As temperature **increases**, dynamic viscosity of gases **increases**. This is because:
- Molecular momentum exchange between layers increases with thermal agitation.
- Governed by **Sutherland's Law**:

$$\mu = \mu_0 \left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right)^{3/2} \cdot \frac{T_0 + S}{T + S}$$

**Honey Example (2 marks):**

Honey is a liquid with strong hydrogen-bonded intermolecular forces. On heating, these bonds weaken and molecules flow more freely, so honey "pours" more easily — its dynamic viscosity drops sharply with temperature.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Mixing Pa·s with Poise without converting (factor of 10). **Always** state the final unit clearly.
> 2. **No-Slip Condition Omission:** Failing to mention that fluid particles in contact with solid surfaces share the surface's velocity. **−1 mark** in derivations.
> 3. **Linear Profile Assumption:** Newton's law assumes a linear velocity profile; many students use a parabolic profile (Couette flow) by mistake.
> 4. **Dimensional Formula Mistakes:** Writing dynamic viscosity as $[MLT^{-1}]$ instead of $[ML^{-1}T^{-1}]$. **−0.5 mark.**
> 5. **Confusing μ with ν:** Dynamic viscosity has units of Pa·s; kinematic viscosity has units of m²/s. Examiners specifically test this trap.
