> 6. **Missing Force Direction:** In derivation problems, state that the resisting force acts **opposite** to the direction of plate motion.
> 7. **Forgetting to Convert mm to m:** Thickness values like $h = 5$ mm **must** be $5 \times 10^{-3}$ m. **Full mark deduction** if dimensionally inconsistent.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Dynamic Viscosity (Module 1)**

- ✅ **Definition:** Dynamic viscosity $\mu$ = shear stress / velocity gradient (Pa·s).
- ✅ **Newton's Law:** $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ — valid **only for Newtonian fluids**.
- ✅ **SI Unit:** N·s/m² or Pa·s ; **CGS Unit:** Poise (1 Pa·s = 10 Poise).
- ✅ **Dimensional Formula:** $[M L^{-1} T^{-1}]$.
- ✅ **Kinematic Viscosity:** $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ with SI unit m²/s and CGS unit **Stoke**.
- ✅ **No-Slip Condition:** Fluid layer adjacent to a solid surface has the same velocity as the surface.
- ✅ **Temperature Effect:**
  - Liquids → $\mu$ **decreases** with $T$.
  - Gases → $\mu$ **increases** with $T$.
- ✅ **Parallel-Plate Force Formula:** $F = \dfrac{\mu A U}{h}$.
- ✅ **Newtonian vs Non-Newtonian:** Only water, air, mercury, and thin oils obey $\tau \propto du/dy$ linearly.
- ✅ **Conversion Factor:** 1 Centipoise (cP) = 10⁻³ Pa·s = 0.001 Pa·s.
- ✅ **Always** convert mm → m before substituting thickness $h$ in formulas.
- ✅ **Engineering Use:** Pipeline pressure drop, bearing lubrication, aerodynamic drag, biomedical flow modeling.

---

**End of Module 1 — Dynamic Viscosity Notes**

*Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 outcomes, and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Cognitive Levels: Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyze).*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Static and Total Head
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT741 - FLUID MECHANICS & MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Static and Total Head

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In fluid mechanics, the term **"Head"** refers to the energy possessed by a fluid per unit weight, expressed as an equivalent height of a fluid column (measured in meters or feet). It is essentially a convenient way of representing fluid energy in terms of length rather than Joules per Newton.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Head (h)** = Energy per unit weight of fluid = **E / W** = **E / (mg)**
> Units: meters (m) of fluid, or feet (ft) of fluid

For a flowing fluid, the **Total Head (H)** is the summation of three distinct forms of energy heads:

$$H = h_p + h_v + h_z$$

Where:
- $h_p$ = **Pressure Head** (Static Head component)
- $h_v$ = **Velocity Head** (Kinetic Head)
- $h_z$ = **Elevation Head** / **Datum Head** (Potential Head)

> [!NOTE]
> **Static Head** generally refers to the head equivalent of the pressure energy in a fluid at rest, while **Total Head** represents the cumulative mechanical energy of a flowing fluid per unit weight, referenced to an arbitrary horizontal datum.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine a rolling ball moving along a hilly track:

- The **height of the ball above the ground** (its elevation) is like the **Elevation Head ($h_z$)** — this is potential energy.
- The **speed of the ball** is like the **Velocity Head ($h_v$)** — this is kinetic energy.
- If the ball is compressed against a spring, that stored energy is like the **Pressure Head ($h_p$)** — this is flow (pressure) energy.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Picture of Total Head:** Think of Total Head as the "total reach" of water if we could convert all its mechanical energy into a vertical column. If you puncture a pipe, water shoots up to a height equal to the pressure head. If you measure the velocity with a Pitot tube, you get the velocity head. The sum tells you the "total energy level" at that point.

### 1.3 Physical Significance of Each Head Component

| Head Type | Energy Form | Physical Meaning | Standard Symbol |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pressure Head | Flow / Pressure Energy | Height of fluid column producing the local static pressure | $h_p$ |
| Velocity Head | Kinetic Energy | Height to which fluid would rise if its velocity is converted to potential | $h_v$ |
| Elevation Head | Potential Energy | Height of the point above an arbitrary horizontal reference (datum) | $h_z$ |

### 1.4 Standard Constants and Metrics

- Acceleration due to gravity: **$g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$**
- Density of water at standard conditions: **$\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$**
- Specific weight of water: **$\gamma_{water} = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$**
- Atmospheric pressure head (water column): **$P_{atm}/\gamma \approx 10.3 \text{ m of water}$**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Constituent Heads — A Logical Breakdown

#### (A) Static Head / Pressure Head ($h_p$)

The pressure head represents the **pressure energy per unit weight** of a fluid. It is the height of a fluid column that would exert the given hydrostatic pressure at its base.

$$h_p = \frac{P}{\rho g} = \frac{P}{\gamma}$$

**Why this matters in engineering:**
- In water supply systems, pressure head at consumer points ensures adequate flow.
- In hydraulic machinery, suction and delivery heads are critical for priming.
- In manometry, pressure differences are measured as differences in static head.

> [!NOTE]
> Pressure **P** can be either **absolute** (referenced to perfect vacuum) or **gauge** (referenced to atmospheric). In most engineering problems involving open systems, **gauge pressure** is used. Remember: $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$.

#### (B) Velocity Head / Kinetic Head ($h_v$)

The velocity head represents the **kinetic energy per unit weight** of the fluid. It is the vertical height the fluid would attain if its entire velocity were converted into potential energy (like a vertical jet rising to its maximum height).

$$h_v = \frac{v^2}{2g}$$

**How to visualize it:** When water issues from a vertical nozzle pointing upward, the maximum height the jet reaches equals the velocity head (neglecting air resistance).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In real (viscous) flow, velocity head is partially lost to friction and turbulence. This loss is known as **head loss ($h_L$)** and is fundamental to the design of pipelines, pumps, and turbines.

#### (C) Elevation Head / Datum Head ($h_z$)

The elevation head is the **potential energy per unit weight** of the fluid, measured relative to an arbitrary horizontal reference plane called the **datum**.

$$h_z = z$$

**Key principle:** Only *differences* in elevation head are physically meaningful, since the datum can be chosen anywhere. The choice of datum does not affect the value of total head differences.

### 2.2 Total Head and Bernoulli's Equation

The **Total Head (H)** at a point in a steady, incompressible, inviscid flow is given by:

$$H = \frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{v^2}{2g} + z = h_p + h_v + h_z$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bernoulli's Principle (1752):** For an ideal (frictionless) fluid flowing along a streamline, the total head remains **constant**:
> 
> $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$
> 
> This is the **mechanical energy balance** for ideal flow.

For real (viscous) flow, the total head **decreases** in the direction of flow due to friction:

$$H_1 = H_2 + h_L$$

Where $h_L$ is the head loss between sections 1 and 2.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| S.No. | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $h_p = \dfrac{P}{\rho g}$ | Pressure (Static) Head | m |
| 2 | $h_v = \dfrac{v^2}{2g}$ | Velocity (Kinetic) Head | m |
| 3 | $h_z = z$ | Elevation (Datum) Head | m |
| 4 | $H = h_p + h_v + h_z$ | Total Head (per unit weight) | m |
| 5 | $H = \dfrac{P}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v^2}{2g} + z$ | Total Head — Expanded form | m |
| 6 | $P = \rho g h_p$ | Pressure from head | Pa (N/m²) |
| 7 | $v = \sqrt{2 g h_v}$ | Velocity from head | m/s |
| 8 | $H_1 - H_2 = h_L$ | Head loss between two sections | m |
| 9 | $\dfrac{P}{\gamma} + \dfrac{v^2}{2g} + z = C$ | Bernoulli's equation (ideal flow) | m |
| 10 | $\gamma = \rho g$ | Specific weight | N/m³ |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Static & Total Head |
|---|---|
| **Hydraulic Engineering** | Design of spillways, dams, open channels; energy grade line (EGL) and hydraulic grade line (HGL) |
| **Pumps & Turbines** | Net Positive Suction Head (NPSH), suction head, delivery head, manometric head |
| **Pipe Flow** | Head loss computation using Darcy-Weisbach / Hazen-Williams |
| **Flow Measurement** | Pitot-static tube (measures velocity head), Venturi meter, orifice meter |
| **Aircraft Design** | Pitot tubes for airspeed measurement; total head vs. static pressure |
| **Hydropower** | Gross head, net head, available head for power generation |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Pressure Head

**Starting point:** Hydrostatic pressure at a depth $h_p$ below the free surface of a static fluid of density $\rho$:

$$P = \rho g h_p$$

**Step 1 — Rearrange the equation** to solve for $h_p$:

$$h_p = \frac{P}{\rho g}$$

**Step 2 — Recognize that** the specific weight $\gamma = \rho g$, so:

$$h_p = \frac{P}{\gamma}$$

**Step 3 — Verbal interpretation:** The pressure head is the height of the fluid column whose weight per unit area at its base equals the local fluid pressure $P$.

**Conversion check:**
$$\frac{P \; [N/m^2]}{\rho g \; [kg/(m^3) \cdot m/s^2]} = \frac{N/m^2}{kg/(m^2 \cdot s^2)} = \frac{kg \cdot m/s^2 / m^2}{kg/(m^2 \cdot s^2)} = m \;\;\checkmark$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Velocity Head (from Kinetic Energy)

**Starting principle:** The kinetic energy of a fluid mass $m$ moving with velocity $v$ is:

$$KE = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$$

**Step 1 — Divide by weight** $W = mg$ to get head (energy per unit weight):

$$h_v = \frac{KE}{W} = \frac{\frac{1}{2} m v^2}{m g} = \frac{v^2}{2g}$$

**Step 2 — Physical interpretation:** If all this kinetic energy were converted to potential energy (e.g., a vertical jet shooting up against gravity), the rise height would be $h_v$.

**Step 3 — Inverse relationship:** The velocity corresponding to a velocity head $h_v$ is:

$$v = \sqrt{2 g h_v}$$

**Unit verification:**
$$\frac{(m/s)^2}{m/s^2} = \frac{m^2/s^2}{m/s^2} = m \;\;\checkmark$$

### 3.3 Derivation of the Elevation Head

For a fluid particle of mass $m$ at a vertical height $z$ above a horizontal datum:

**Step 1 — Potential energy:**
$$PE = m g z$$

**Step 2 — Divide by weight $W = mg$:**
$$h_z = \frac{PE}{W} = \frac{m g z}{m g} = z$$

**Step 3 — Note:** The datum choice is arbitrary, but only *differences* in $h_z$ between two points are physically meaningful.

### 3.4 Derivation of Total Head from First Principles (Bernoulli)

**Step 1 — Consider unit weight of fluid** (i.e., 1 Newton) moving from section (1) to section (2) along a streamline.

**Step 2 — Energy at section 1 (per unit weight):**
$$E_1 = \underbrace{\frac{P_1}{\rho g}}_{\text{Pressure head}} + \underbrace{\frac{v_1^2}{2g}}_{\text{Velocity head}} + \underbrace{z_1}_{\text{Elevation head}}$$

**Step 3 — Energy at section 2 (per unit weight):**
$$E_2 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$

**Step 4 — Apply the principle of conservation of energy** (no friction, no pump, no turbine):
$$E_1 = E_2$$

**Step 5 — Final Bernoulli Equation:**

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$

**Step 6 — For real (viscous) flow,** include head loss:
$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$$

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard)

> **Problem:** Water ($\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$) flows through a pipe of diameter 150 mm at a velocity of 3 m/s. At a certain point, the pressure is 200 kPa (gauge) and the pipe centerline is 5 m above the datum. Calculate:
> (i) Pressure head
> (ii) Velocity head
> (iii) Elevation head
> (iv) Total head

**Given data:**
- $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- $v = 3 \text{ m/s}$
- $P = 200{,}000 \text{ Pa}$
- $z = 5 \text{ m}$

**Solution:**

**Step (i) — Pressure head:**
$$h_p = \frac{P}{\rho g} = \frac{200{,}000}{1000 \times 9.81} = \frac{200{,}000}{9810} \approx 20.387 \text{ m}$$

**Step (ii) — Velocity head:**
$$h_v = \frac{v^2}{2g} = \frac{(3)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{9}{19.62} \approx 0.459 \text{ m}$$

**Step (iii) — Elevation head:**
$$h_z = z = 5 \text{ m}$$

**Step (iv) — Total head:**
$$H = h_p + h_v + h_z = 20.387 + 0.459 + 5 = 25.846 \text{ m}$$

> [!TIP]
> Always write units in each step. In KTU valuation, leaving units out can cost you **0.5 to 1 mark per answer** even if the numerical value is correct.

### 3.6 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Property | Static Head ($h_p$) | Velocity Head ($h_v$) | Elevation Head ($h_z$) | Total Head ($H$) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Energy type | Flow / Pressure | Kinetic | Potential | Sum of all three |
| Formula | $\dfrac{P}{\rho g}$ | $\dfrac{v^2}{2g}$ | $z$ | $\dfrac{P}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v^2}{2g} + z$ |
| Units | m | m | m | m |
| Datum-dependent? | No | No | **Yes** | Yes |
| Affected by fluid type? | Yes (via $\rho$) | No (independent of $\rho$) | No | Yes (via $\rho$ for $h_p$ term) |
| Constant along a streamline? | Generally varies | Generally varies | Generally varies | **Constant (ideal flow)** |
| Reduced by friction? | Indirectly | Indirectly | Not directly | **Yes, by $h_L$** |
| Measured by | Manometer, piezometer | Pitot-static tube | Surveyor's level | Energy grade line (EGL) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram: Components of Total Head

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the functional architecture of Total Head, decomposing it into its constituent heads and showing how they interact in a typical fluid flow system.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FLUID FLOW POINT IN PIPE] --> B[TOTAL HEAD H]
    B --> C[PRESSURE HEAD hp]
    B --> D[VELOCITY HEAD hv]
    B --> E[ELEVATION HEAD hz]
    C --> C1[Formula: P divided by rho g]
    C --> C2[Measured by piezometer]
    D --> D1[Formula: v squared divided by 2g]
    D --> D2[Measured by pitot static tube]
    E --> E1[Formula: z above datum]
    E --> E2[Measured by surveying level]
    C1 --> F1[Units: meters of fluid]
    D1 --> F1
    E1 --> F1
    F1 --> G[BERNOULLI EQUATION: H1 equals H2 plus hL]
    G --> G1[Ideal flow: hL equals 0]
    G --> G2[Real flow: hL greater than 0]
    G2 --> H1[Head loss due to friction]
    G2 --> H2[Head loss due to fittings]
    G2 --> H3[Head loss due to sudden contraction]
```

### 4.2 Energy Grade Line vs. Hydraulic Grade Line (Schematic)

This diagram illustrates the geometric interpretation of the various heads in a pipe with varying elevation and diameter.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[Section 1: Wider Pipe]
        P1[Piezometric Level] --> EL1
        EGL1[Energy Grade Line H1] --> EL1
        DATUM1[Datum Line z equals 0] --> EL1
    end
    subgraph S2[Section 2: Narrower Pipe]
        P2[Piezometric Level HGL] --> EL2
        EGL2[Energy Grade Line H2] --> EL2
        DATUM2[Datum Line z equals 0] --> EL2
    end
    EGL1 -->|H1 greater than H2| EGL2
    P1 -->|HGL drops in real flow| P2
    EGL1 -.- NOTE1[Vertical distance between EGL and pipe centerline equals velocity head hv]
    EGL2 -.- NOTE1
    P1 -.- NOTE2[Vertical distance between HGL and pipe centerline equals pressure head hp]
    P2 -.- NOTE2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:**
> - **Energy Grade Line (EGL)** = $H = h_p + h_v + h_z$ (total energy level)
> - **Hydraulic Grade Line (HGL)** = $h_p + h_z$ (total energy minus velocity head)
> - Vertical gap between EGL and HGL = $h_v$ (velocity head)
> - For ideal flow, EGL is horizontal; for real flow, it slopes downward due to $h_L$

### 4.3 Decision Tree for Selecting Head Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Given a fluid flow problem] --> Q1{Are you asked about energy at a point?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Fluid is moving?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3{Fluid is static?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q4[Use TOTAL HEAD H equals hp plus hv plus hz]
    Q2 -->|No| Q5[Fluid is at rest; use STATIC HEAD only]
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q6[Use PRESSURE HEAD hp equals P divided by rho g]
    Q4 --> Q7{Is flow ideal or real?}
    Q7 -->|Ideal, no losses| Q8[Bernoulli: H1 equals H2]
    Q7 -->|Real, viscous| Q9[Bernoulli: H1 equals H2 plus hL]
    Q5 --> Q10[Apply hydrostatic equation: P equals rho g h]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]**
**Define the following terms with respect to fluid flow:**
(i) Static Head, (ii) Velocity Head, (iii) Total Head

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Static Head ($h_p$):** The static head, also called **pressure head**, at a point in a fluid is defined as the height of a fluid column that would produce the local static pressure at its base. It is given by the formula:

$$h_p = \frac{P}{\rho g}$$

where $P$ is the static pressure, $\rho$ is the fluid density, and $g$ is acceleration due to gravity. Units: meters (m) of fluid.

**(ii) Velocity Head ($h_v$):** The velocity head, also called **kinetic head**, at a point in a flowing fluid is the equivalent height to which the fluid would rise if its entire kinetic energy were converted into potential energy. It is given by:

$$h_v = \frac{v^2}{2g}$$

where $v$ is the flow velocity. Units: meters (m).

**(iii) Total Head ($H$):** The total head at a point in a flowing fluid is the **sum of the pressure head, velocity head, and elevation head**. It represents the total mechanical energy of the fluid per unit weight:

$$H = \frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{v^2}{2g} + z = h_p + h_v + h_z$$

For ideal (inviscid) flow along a streamline, the total head is constant (Bernoulli's principle). Units: meters (m).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, May 2023]**
**Distinguish between Static Head and Total Head. Why is total head constant along a streamline in ideal flow?**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Static Head ($h_p$) | Total Head ($H$) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Energy per unit weight due to pressure alone | Total energy per unit weight = sum of $h_p$, $h_v$, $h_z$ |
| Formula | $\dfrac{P}{\rho g}$ | $\dfrac{P}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v^2}{2g} + z$ |
| Components | Only pressure head | Pressure + velocity + elevation heads |
| Applicable to | Static and moving fluids | Only moving (flowing) fluids |
| Conservation | Not conserved (varies with pressure) | **Constant along a streamline in ideal flow** |

**Why total head is constant along a streamline (ideal flow):**
For an **ideal (inviscid, incompressible) fluid** with no external work (no pump or turbine) and no losses, the principle of **conservation of energy** applies. The total mechanical energy per unit weight (i.e., total head) cannot be created or destroyed along a streamline — it can only be interconverted between pressure, velocity, and elevation forms. This is the basis of **Bernoulli's equation**:

$$H_1 = \frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2 = H_2$$

For real (viscous) flow, friction converts some mechanical energy into heat, so total head decreases in the direction of flow by an amount equal to the head loss $h_L$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style, Module 1 Internal Choice Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the total head of a flowing fluid from fundamental principles. State the assumptions clearly. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A pipe of diameter 200 mm carries water with a mean velocity of 4 m/s. At a section, the pressure is 150 kPa (gauge) and the pipe is 8 m above the datum. Calculate:
(i) Static head
(ii) Velocity head
(iii) Elevation head
(iv) Total head at the section (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation of Total Head:**

**Assumptions:**
1. Fluid is **incompressible** (constant $\rho$).
2. Flow is **steady** (properties at any point do not change with time).
3. Flow is **inviscid** (frictionless).
4. Flow occurs along a **streamline** (or the flow is irrotational).
5. No external work input (no pump) or output (no turbine) between the two sections.

**Step 1:** Consider a fluid particle of mass $m$ moving along a streamline from section (1) to section (2).

**Step 2:** Total mechanical energy at section (1) consists of:
- Pressure energy: $E_{P,1} = P_1 \cdot V_1$ (where $V_1$ is volume)
- Kinetic energy: $E_{K,1} = \frac{1}{2} m v_1^2$
- Potential energy: $E_{PE,1} = m g z_1$

Total energy at section (1):
$$E_1 = P_1 V_1 + \frac{1}{2} m v_1^2 + m g z_1$$

**Step 3:** Similarly, total energy at section (2):
$$E_2 = P_2 V_2 + \frac{1}{2} m v_2^2 + m g z_2$$

**Step 4:** Apply **conservation of energy** (ideal flow): $E_1 = E_2$:

$$P_1 V_1 + \frac{1}{2} m v_1^2 + m g z_1 = P_2 V_2 + \frac{1}{2} m v_2^2 + m g z_2$$

**Step 5:** Since the fluid is incompressible, mass is conserved, and $V_1 = V_2 = V$ (per unit mass, divide by $m$ and use $V = m/\rho$):

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho} + \frac{v_1^2}{2} + g z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho} + \frac{v_2^2}{2} + g z_2$$

**Step 6:** Divide through by $g$ to obtain **head form (energy per unit weight)**:

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$

**Step 7:** Recognize the three head components:
- $h_p = \dfrac{P}{\rho g}$ = **Pressure head**
- $h_v = \dfrac{v^2}{2g}$ = **Velocity head**
- $h_z = z$ = **Elevation head**

**Step 8:** Therefore, the **Total Head** at any point is:

$$H = h_p + h_v + h_z = \frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{v^2}{2g} + z$$

And for ideal flow: $H_1 = H_2$ = constant along a streamline.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given data:**
- Pipe diameter $D = 200 \text{ mm} = 0.2 \text{ m}$ (not directly needed for head calculation)
- Velocity $v = 4 \text{ m/s}$
- Pressure $P = 150 \text{ kPa} = 150{,}000 \text{ Pa}$ (gauge)
- Elevation $z = 8 \text{ m}$
- Fluid: water, $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Step (i) — Static (Pressure) Head:**
$$h_p = \frac{P}{\rho g} = \frac{150{,}000}{1000 \times 9.81} = \frac{150{,}000}{9810} \approx 15.290 \text{ m}$$

**Step (ii) — Velocity Head:**
$$h_v = \frac{v^2}{2g} = \frac{(4)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{16}{19.62} \approx 0.815 \text{ m}$$

**Step (iii) — Elevation Head:**
$$h_z = z = 8 \text{ m}$$

**Step (iv) — Total Head:**
$$H = h_p + h_v + h_z = 15.290 + 0.815 + 8 = 24.105 \text{ m}$$

**Final Answer (with units):**
- Static head = **15.29 m of water**
- Velocity head = **0.815 m of water**
- Elevation head = **8 m of water**
- Total head = **24.105 m of water**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style, Module 1 Internal Choice Pattern]**

**(a)** State and explain Bernoulli's equation for a steady, incompressible, inviscid flow. What modifications are made to account for real (viscous) fluid flow? (7 Marks)

**(b)** A horizontal pipe of diameter 300 mm has a constriction of diameter 150 mm. Water flows through it with a discharge of 60 L/s. At the larger section, the pressure is 120 kPa. Calculate:
(i) Velocity head at both sections
(ii) Pressure head at the constriction (assuming no head loss)
(iii) Total head at both sections (Take datum at pipe centerline) (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Bernoulli's Equation:**

**Statement:** For a **steady, incompressible, inviscid** fluid flow along a streamline, the sum of the pressure head, velocity head, and elevation head remains constant. Mathematically:

$$\frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{v^2}{2g} + z = \text{constant}$$

**Explanation:** This equation represents the **conservation of mechanical energy** in fluid flow. The three terms represent:
1. $\dfrac{P}{\rho g}$ — **Pressure head** (flow energy per unit weight)
2. $\dfrac{v^2}{2g}$ — **Velocity head** (kinetic energy per unit weight)
3. $z$ — **Elevation head** (potential energy per unit weight)

In ideal flow, energy is neither created nor destroyed; it merely transforms from one form to another. Hence the total head is constant.

**Modifications for real (viscous) fluid flow:**

For real fluids, viscous effects cause **friction losses** that convert mechanical energy into heat (irreversible). The modified Bernoulli equation becomes:

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$$

where $h_L$ is the **head loss** between sections 1 and 2, given by:

$$h_L = \frac{4 f L v^2}{2 g D} + \Sigma h_{\text{minor}}$$

(Darcy-Weisbach equation + minor losses due to fittings, valves, contractions, expansions)

If a **pump** adds energy $H_p$, or a **turbine** extracts energy $H_t$, the equation is further modified:

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 + H_p = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L + H_t$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given data:**
- Larger diameter $D_1 = 300 \text{ mm} = 0.3 \text{ m}$
- Smaller diameter (constriction) $D_2 = 150 \text{ mm} = 0.15 \text{ m}$
- Discharge $Q = 60 \text{ L/s} = 0.060 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- Pressure at section 1: $P_1 = 120 \text{ kPa} = 120{,}000 \text{ Pa}$
- Fluid: water, $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Horizontal pipe: $z_1 = z_2 = 0$ (datum at centerline)
- Ideal flow assumed: $h_L = 0$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Step 1 — Calculate areas:**
$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} D_1^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.3)^2 = 0.07069 \text{ m}^2$$
$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} D_2^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.15)^2 = 0.01767 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Calculate velocities using continuity ($Q = A v$):**
$$v_1 = \frac{Q}{A_1} = \frac{0.060}{0.07069} \approx 0.8488 \text{ m/s}$$
$$v_2 = \frac{Q}{A_2} = \frac{0.060}{0.01767} \approx 3.3953 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step (i) — Velocity heads:**
$$h_{v1} = \frac{v_1^2}{2g} = \frac{(0.8488)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{0.7205}{19.62} \approx 0.0367 \text{ m}$$
$$h_{v2} = \frac{v_2^2}{2g} = \frac{(3.3953)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{11.528}{19.62} \approx 0.5876 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate pressure head at section 1:**
$$h_{p1} = \frac{P_1}{\rho g} = \frac{120{,}000}{1000 \times 9.81} = \frac{120{,}000}{9810} \approx 12.232 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Bernoulli's equation** (horizontal pipe, no losses):
$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$
$$h_{p1} + h_{v1} + 0 = h_{p2} + h_{v2} + 0$$
$$12.232 + 0.0367 = h_{p2} + 0.5876$$
$$h_{p2} = 12.232 + 0.0367 - 0.5876 = 11.681 \text{ m}$$

**Step (ii) — Pressure head at constriction:**
$$\boxed{h_{p2} \approx 11.681 \text{ m of water}}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate total heads:**

Total head at section 1:
$$H_1 = h_{p1} + h_{v1} + z_1 = 12.232 + 0.0367 + 0 = 12.269 \text{ m}$$

Total head at section 2:
$$H_2 = h_{p2} + h_{v2} + z_2 = 11.681 + 0.5876 + 0 = 12.269 \text{ m}$$

**Step (iii) — Total heads:**
$$\boxed{H_1 = H_2 = 12.269 \text{ m of water}}$$

> [!TIP]
> Note that $H_1 = H_2$, **verifying Bernoulli's principle** for ideal (frictionless) flow. The increase in velocity head at the constriction is exactly balanced by the decrease in pressure head.

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — Pressure in kPa must be converted to Pa (multiply by 1000). Diameter in mm must be converted to m (divide by 1000). This is the #1 source of computational errors and may cost **1–2 marks per sub-question**.
> 
> 2. **Using absolute pressure instead of gauge** — If the problem states "gauge pressure" or does not specify, use it directly. Mixing absolute and gauge pressures is a serious error.
> 
> 3. **Confusing datum choice** — The datum (z = 0) can be chosen arbitrarily, but **it must be consistent** for both sections in a comparison. The total head values change with datum choice, but **differences** do not.
> 
> 4. **Omitting units in final answers** — Always write "m of water" or simply "m" after numerical answers. Marks are explicitly allocated for correct units in KTU.
> 
> 5. **Writing "head = pressure"** — Head is **NOT** pressure. Head has units of length (m), while pressure has units of force/area (Pa). They are related by $h_p = P/\rho g$.
> 
> 6. **Neglecting elevation head in horizontal pipes** — Even if the pipe is horizontal, the elevation head may be non-zero if the datum is chosen below the pipe. Be careful to **read the problem statement carefully**.
> 
> 7. **Forgetting the energy balance modifications** — For real flow problems, students often forget to include $h_L$, or they add $h_L$ to the wrong side of Bernoulli's equation. **Rule of thumb:** $h_L$ always goes on the **right side** (subtracted from upstream total head).
> 
> 8. **In derivations, skipping assumption statements** — KTU examiners allocate **at least 1–2 marks** explicitly for stating the assumptions. Never skip this step.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid Revision Checklist — Static and Total Head**

- [x] **Head** = Energy per unit weight of fluid (units: meters of fluid)
- [x] **Three components:** Pressure head ($h_p$), Velocity head ($h_v$), Elevation head ($h_z$)
- [x] **Pressure head:** $h_p = \dfrac{P}{\rho g} = \dfrac{P}{\gamma}$ — represents flow energy
- [x] **Velocity head:** $h_v = \dfrac{v^2}{2g}$ — represents kinetic energy (independent of fluid type)
- [x] **Elevation head:** $h_z = z$ — represents potential energy (depends on datum)
- [x] **Total Head:** $H = h_p + h_v + h_z$ — represents total mechanical energy per unit weight
- [x] **Bernoulli's Equation (Ideal flow):** $H_1 = H_2$ (constant along a streamline)
- [x] **Real flow:** $H_1 = H_2 + h_L$ (head loss due to friction)
- [x] **Energy Grade Line (EGL):** Represents total head $H$
- [x] **Hydraulic Grade Line (HGL):** Represents $H - h_v = h_p + h_z$
- [x] **Pitot-static tube:** Measures $h_v$ (velocity head) directly
- [x] **Piezometer:** Measures $h_p$ (pressure head) directly
- [x] **Constant to remember:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\gamma_{water} = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$
- [x] **Pressure conversion:** 1 atm ≈ 101.325 kPa ≈ 10.3 m of water
- [x] **Key engineering application:** Pumps add $H_p$ to flow; turbines extract $H_t$ from flow
- [x] **Head loss equation (Darcy-Weisbach):** $h_L = \dfrac{4 f L v^2}{2 g D}$
- [x] **Continuity equation (companion to Bernoulli):** $A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$ for incompressible flow

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic to remember the three heads:** **"P-V-Z"** = **P**ressure, **V**elocity, ele**Z**ation. Or think: **"Pump-Vortex-Zero"** — pump (pressure), vortex (velocity/swirl), zero (ground level).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Forces on Planar and Curved Surfaces Immersed in Fluids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT741 - FLUID MECHANICS & MACHINERY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Forces on Planar and Curved Surfaces Immersed in Fluids # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Fluid Pressure:** Fluid pressure at a point is the normal force per unit area exerted by the fluid on a surface, acting equally in all directions at that point. $$P = \lim_{\Delta A \to 0} \frac{\Delta F}{\Delta A}$$ **Pascal's Law:** The pressure at a point in a static fluid is the same in all directions and acts perpendicular to any surface in contact with the fluid. **Hydrostatic Force on a Submerged Surface:** The resultant force exerted by a static fluid on a planar or curved surface is obtained by integrating the pressure distribution over the wetted area of the surface. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** This topic forms the foundation of fluid statics and is heavily tested in **KTU Module 1 (15–20% weightage in Part B questions)**. Mastery of pressure prism concepts, center of pressure location, and curved surface force decomposition is mandatory. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine you are at the bottom of a swimming pool. You can feel water pushing on your eardrums, and the deeper you go, the stronger the push. Now picture a submarine hatch — it must be incredibly strong because the ocean pushes on it from every direction with enormous force. Engineers designing **dam gates**, **ship hulls**, **lock gates**, and **storage tanks** must calculate these forces precisely to prevent catastrophic failure. Think of it this way: - **Water is like a stack of invisible heavy blankets** — each layer weighs something and presses down on the layer below. - The deeper you go, the more "blankets" are stacked above you, so the pressure increases linearly with depth. - This pressure pushes **perpendicular** to any surface it touches (just like your hand feels the table push straight up when you rest it on a desk). > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Hydrostatic forces always act **normal (perpendicular)** to the wetted surface because fluids at rest cannot sustain shear stress. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Specific weight of water** ($\gamma_w$): **9.81 kN/m³** (at 4°C, standard conditions) - **Specific weight of sea water**: **10.05 kN/m³** - **Density of water** ($\rho_w$): **1000 kg/m³** - **Atmospheric pressure** ($P_{atm}$): **101.325 kN/m²** (≈ **10.3 m of water column**) - **Standard gravity** ($g$): **9.81 m/s²** > [!NOTE] > In KTU problems, always use $\gamma = 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3$ for water unless explicitly stated otherwise. For numerical convenience, $\gamma = 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3$ is most common; **never approximate to 10**. ## 1.4 Fundamental Pressure Equation The pressure variation in a static incompressible fluid is governed by: $$P = P_0 + \rho g h = P_0 + \gamma h$$ where: - $P$ = absolute pressure at depth $h$ (Pa or N/m²) - $P_0$ = pressure at the free surface (Pa) - $\rho$ = fluid density (kg/m³) - $g$ = acceleration due to gravity (m/s²) - $\gamma$ = specific weight (N/m³) - $h$ = vertical depth below free surface (m) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Gauge Pressure vs Absolute Pressure:** > - **Gauge Pressure:** $P_g = \gamma h$ (measured above atmospheric) > - **Absolute Pressure:** $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + \gamma h$ > - **Vacuum Pressure:** $P_v = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$ > - KTU problems typically use **gauge pressure** unless specified. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Hydrostatic Force on a Plane Surface (Inclined) Consider an inclined plane surface submerged in a static fluid, making an angle $\theta$ with the free surface (or horizontal). Let the surface have a wetted area $A$. ### Setup & Definitions - $\bar{h}$ = vertical depth of the **centroid** of the submerged area from the free surface - $h_c$ = vertical depth of the **center of pressure** from the free surface - $\theta$ = angle between the inclined plane and the horizontal free surface - $I_G$ = second moment of area about the **centroidal axis** parallel to the free surface - $A$ = total wetted area of the plane surface ### Step-by-Step Logic Breakdown 1. **Pressure at any depth $h$:** $P = \gamma h$ 2. **Differential force on elemental strip of area $dA$:** $dF = P \, dA = \gamma h \, dA$ 3. **Total force by integration:** $F = \int_A \gamma h \, dA = \gamma \int_A h \, dA$ 4. **Using the centroid property:** $\int_A h \, dA = \bar{h} \cdot A$, so $F = \gamma \bar{h} A$ 5. **Location of center of pressure:** Found by taking moments about an axis through the free surface. ### Resultant Force Magnitude $$\boxed{F_R = \gamma \bar{h} A}$$ where $\bar{h}$ is the vertical depth of the centroid of the submerged area. ### Center of Pressure Location The center of pressure lies **below** the centroid (for surfaces with free surface above): $$h_c = \bar{h} + \frac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{\bar{h} A}$$ The distance of the center of pressure from the centroid along the inclined plane is: $$\boxed{e = \frac{I_G \sin\theta}{\bar{h} A}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Interpretation:** The center of pressure shifts downward from the centroid as the surface becomes deeper (smaller $\bar{h}$). For very deep surfaces, $e \to 0$, meaning the center of pressure approaches the centroid. ## 2.2 Hydrostatic Force on a Curved Surface For a curved surface, the resultant force is **NOT** simply $\gamma \bar{h} A$ because pressure acts normal to the surface at every point, and the surface normal direction varies. ### Decomposition Strategy The resultant force on a curved surface is resolved into: 1. **Horizontal Force ($F_H$):** Acts on the **vertical projection** of the curved surface 2. **Vertical Force ($F_V$):** Equal to the **weight of the fluid vertically above the curved surface** (real or imaginary) ### Horizontal Component $$\boxed{F_H = \gamma \bar{h}_V A_V}$$ where: - $A_V$ = vertical projection of the curved surface (area of projection on a vertical plane) - $\bar{h}_V$ = vertical depth of the centroid of $A_V$ ### Vertical Component $$\boxed{F_V = \gamma \cdot \mathcal{V}}$$ where $\mathcal{V}$ = volume of fluid (real or imaginary) directly above the curved surface, bounded by the curved surface and its vertical projection on the free surface. > [!NOTE] > **Direction of $F_V$:** > - If fluid is **above** the curved surface: $F_V$ acts **downward** > - If fluid is **below** the curved surface: $F_V$ acts **upward** ### Resultant Magnitude $$F_R = \sqrt{F_H^2 + F_V^2}$$ The angle of the resultant with horizontal: $$\tan\alpha = \frac{F_V}{F_H}$$ ## 2.3 Buoyancy & Archimedes' Principle **Archimedes' Principle:** When a body is fully or partially immersed in a fluid, it experiences an upward buoyant force equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by the body. $$\boxed{F_B = \gamma_f \cdot \mathcal{V}_{displaced}}$$ The buoyant force acts vertically upward through the **center of buoyancy**, which is the centroid of the displaced fluid volume. ## 2.4 Stability of Floating Bodies — Metacentric Height For a floating body in equilibrium, the **metacentric height** $GM$ determines stability: $$GM = \frac{I}{\mathcal{V}_{displaced}} - BG$$ where: - $I$ = second moment of area of the waterline plane about the axis of rotation - $\mathcal{V}_{displaced}$ = displaced volume of fluid - $BG$ = distance between center of buoyancy ($B$) and center of gravity ($G$) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Stability Criterion:** > - $GM > 0$ → Body is **STABLE** (metacenter above center of gravity) > - $GM = 0$ → Body is in **NEUTRAL** equilibrium > - $GM < 0$ → Body is **UNSTABLE** ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $P = \gamma h$ | Hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$ | Pa or N/m² | | 2 | $F_R = \gamma \bar{h} A$ | Force on inclined plane surface | N | | 3 | $h_c = \bar{h} + \frac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{\bar{h} A}$ | Center of pressure depth | m | | 4 | $e = \frac{I_G \sin\theta}{\bar{h} A}$ | Eccentricity (CP from centroid along plane) | m | | 5 | $F_H = \gamma \bar{h}_V A_V$ | Horizontal force on curved surface | N | | 6 | $F_V = \gamma \cdot \mathcal{V}$ | Vertical force on curved surface | N | | 7 | $F_R = \sqrt{F_H^2 + F_V^2}$ | Resultant force on curved surface | N | | 8 | $F_B = \gamma_f \mathcal{V}_{disp}$ | Buoyant force (Archimedes) | N | | 9 | $GM = \frac{I}{\mathcal{V}_{disp}} - BG$ | Metacentric height | m | | 10 | $I = \frac{LD^3}{12}$ | Waterline area moment (rectangle $L \times D$) | m⁴ | | 11 | $I = \frac{\pi D^4}{64}$ | Waterline area moment (circle of diameter $D$) | m⁴ | | 12 | $\tan\alpha = F_V / F_H$ | Direction of resultant on curved surface | dimensionless | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Values for Quick Reference:** > - $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³ (fresh water) > - $\gamma_{sw} = 10.05$ kN/m³ (sea water) > - $\gamma_{Hg} = 133.1$ kN/m³ (mercury) ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications | Application | Engineering Use | |-------------|-----------------| | **Dam Gates** | Design of sluice gates, tainter gates, and spillway crest gates | | **Ship Hulls** | Hydrostatic pressure distribution for plate thickness design | | **Storage Tanks** | Wall thickness calculations for cylindrical and rectangular tanks | | **Lock Gates** | Force calculations for navigation lock gates | | **Submarine Design** | Collapse depth and pressure hull calculations | | **Buoy Design** | Stability analysis of floating buoys and offshore platforms | # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Hydrostatic Force on an Inclined Plane Surface ### Problem Setup Consider an inclined plane surface submerged in a static fluid. Let the surface make an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal free surface. Let $X$–$Y$ axes lie in the plane of the surface with origin at the intersection of the plane and the free surface (along the line where the plane meets the free surface if extended). Let $x$ be the distance measured along the inclined plane from the free surface intersection, and $y$ be measured perpendicular to $x$ within the plane. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** The vertical depth $h$ of a point at inclined distance $x$ from the free surface is: $$h = x \sin\theta$$ **Step 2:** The pressure at this depth (gauge) is: $$P = \gamma h = \gamma x \sin\theta$$ **Step 3:** Consider an elemental strip parallel to the free surface intersection, of width $dy$ and length $x$, at distance $x$ along the inclined plane. The area of this strip is: $$dA = x \, dy$$ **Step 4:** The differential force on this strip is: $$dF = P \, dA = \gamma x \sin\theta \cdot x \, dy = \gamma \sin\theta \, x^2 \, dy$$ **Step 5:** Integrate over the full width of the surface to get the total force: $$F_R = \int dF = \gamma \sin\theta \int x^2 \, dy$$ **Step 6:** Recognize that $\int x^2 \, dy$ is the second moment of area of the plane surface about the axis where the plane meets the free surface (i.e., the $X$-axis through the origin $O$). Denote this as $I_O$: $$I_O = \int x^2 \, dy$$ **Step 7:** By the parallel axis theorem: $$I_O = I_G + A \bar{x}^2$$ where $\bar{x}$ is the distance of the centroid from the $O$-axis along the inclined plane. **Step 8:** Substituting: $$F_R = \gamma \sin\theta \left( I_G + A \bar{x}^2 \right)$$ **Step 9:** Note that $\bar{h} = \bar{x} \sin\theta$ (vertical depth of centroid). Therefore: $$F_R = \gamma \sin\theta \, I_G + \gamma \sin\theta \, A \bar{x}^2$$ $$F_R = \gamma \sin\theta \, I_G + \gamma A (\bar{x} \sin\theta) \bar{x}$$ $$F_R = \gamma \sin\theta \, I_G + \gamma A \bar{h} \bar{x}$$ **Step 10:** For a surface with free surface above, the line of action of $F_R$ passes through the center of pressure at inclined distance $x_c$ from $O$: $$F_R \cdot x_c = \int x \, dF = \int x \cdot \gamma x \sin\theta \, dy = \gamma \sin\theta \int x^3 \, dy$$ **Step 11:** Taking moments about the $y$-axis through $O$: $$F_R \cdot x_c = \gamma \sin\theta \int x^3 \, dy$$ This gives the inclined distance to the center of pressure: $$x_c = \frac{\int x^3 \, dy}{\int x^2 \, dy}$$ **Step 12:** The center of pressure formula simplification yields: $$h_c = \bar{h} + \frac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{\bar{h} A}$$ ### Final Result $$\boxed{F_R = \gamma \bar{h} A \quad \text{and} \quad h_c = \bar{h} + \frac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{\bar{h} A}}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Horizontal Force on a Curved Surface ### Problem Setup Consider a curved surface $ABCD$ submerged in a fluid. Consider the vertical projection of this curved surface onto a vertical plane (the projection $A'B'C'D'$). ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** Consider a small elemental strip of the curved surface at depth $h$ with area $dA$. The force on this element is: $$dF = \gamma h \, dA$$ This force acts **normal** to the curved surface. **Step 2:** Resolving $dF$ into horizontal ($dF_H$) and vertical ($dF_V$) components. Let $\phi$ be the angle the normal at this point makes with the horizontal: $$dF_H = dF \cos\phi = \gamma h \, dA \cos\phi$$ $$dF_V = dF \sin\phi = \gamma h \, dA \sin\phi$$ **Step 3:** The horizontal projection of $dA$ is $dA_H = dA \cos\phi$ (this is the projected area on the vertical plane). Substituting: $$dF_H = \gamma h \, dA_H$$ **Step 4:** The total horizontal force is found by integrating over the vertical projection area $A_V$: $$F_H = \int_{A_V} \gamma h \, dA_H = \gamma \int_{A_V} h \, dA_H$$ **Step 5:** Using the centroid property: $\int_{A_V} h \, dA_H = \bar{h}_V \cdot A_V$: $$\boxed{F_H = \gamma \bar{h}_V A_V}$$ This force acts through the **center of pressure of the projected area**, horizontally. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Vertical Force on a Curved Surface ### Problem Setup The vertical component of force on a curved surface equals the weight of the fluid column directly above (or virtually above) the curved surface, bounded by the surface and its vertical projection on the free surface. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** From Step 2 above, $dF_V = \gamma h \, dA \sin\phi$. Note that $dA \sin\phi = dA_V$ is the horizontal projection of $dA$: $$dF_V = \gamma h \, dA_V$$ **Step 2:** Alternatively, recognize that $dF_V = \gamma \cdot d\mathcal{V}$ where $d\mathcal{V}$ is the volume of a vertical fluid column above the elemental area $dA$, with height $h$. **Step 3:** Integrating over the entire curved surface: $$F_V = \gamma \int d\mathcal{V} = \gamma \cdot \mathcal{V}$$ where $\mathcal{V}$ = total volume of fluid (real or imaginary) above the curved surface. $$\boxed{F_V = \gamma \cdot \mathcal{V}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** This is a beautiful result! The vertical force on a curved surface equals the weight of the fluid column directly above it. This is known as the **"Pressure Prism"** concept in KTU terminology. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Buoyancy from First Principles ### Problem Setup Consider a fully submerged body of arbitrary shape in a static fluid. We will prove that the buoyant force equals the weight of displaced fluid. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** Consider an elemental vertical column within the body, of cross-section $dA$ at depth $h_1$ (top) to $h_2$ (bottom). The pressure forces on the top and bottom of this column are: $$dF_{top} = \gamma h_1 \, dA \quad \text{(downward)}$$ $$dF_{bottom} = \gamma h_2 \, dA \quad \text{(upward)}$$ **Step 2:** Net upward force on this column from the fluid: $$dF_{net} = \gamma h_2 \, dA - \gamma h_1 \, dA = \gamma (h_2 - h_1) \, dA = \gamma \, dh \cdot dA$$ But $dh \cdot dA = d\mathcal{V}$ is the volume of the elemental column. **Step 3:** So: $dF_{net} = \gamma \, d\mathcal{V}$ **Step 4:** Integrating over the entire body volume: $$F_B = \int \gamma \, d\mathcal{V} = \gamma \int d\mathcal{V} = \gamma \cdot \mathcal{V}_{body}$$ **Step 5:** Since the body is fully submerged, $\mathcal{V}_{body} = \mathcal{V}_{displaced}$: $$\boxed{F_B = \gamma_f \cdot \mathcal{V}_{displaced}}$$ This is **Archimedes' Principle**. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Problem-Type Identification Flow | Problem Type | Key Identifier | Primary Formula | Critical Step | |--------------|---------------|-----------------|---------------| | Plane vertical surface | "Vertical wall", "gate" | $F_R = \gamma \bar{h} A$ | $\bar{h} = H/2$ for rectangle | | Plane inclined surface | "Inclined at angle $\theta$" | $F_R = \gamma \bar{h} A$, find $h_c$ | Use $\bar{h} = \bar{x}\sin\theta$ | | Curved surface (dam) | "Curved gate", "arc" | $F_H$ and $F_V$ separately | Identify real or imaginary fluid | | Buoyancy | "Submerged block" | $F_B = \gamma \mathcal{V}$ | Determine displaced volume | | Floating stability | "Ship", "stability" | $GM = I/\mathcal{V} - BG$ | Compute $I$ of waterline area | | Gate hinge moment | "Moment about hinge" | $M = F_R \cdot$ (lever arm) | Locate CP first, then moment arm | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Conceptual Flow Diagram: Problem-Solving Strategy for Hydrostatic Forces ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Submerged Surface Problem] --> B{Surface Type?} B -->|Plane Surface| C[Inclined or Vertical?] B -->|Curved Surface| D[Decompose into H and V components] C -->|Vertical| E["Use F_R = γ · h̄ · A where h̄ = H/2 for rectangle"] C -->|Inclined| F["Find h̄ = x̄·sinθ
F_R = γ · h̄ · A"] E --> G[Locate Center of Pressure h_c] F --> G G --> H[Compute Moment if needed] D --> I["F_H = γ · h̄_V · A_V
(vertical projection)"] D --> J["F_V = γ · V
(weight of fluid above)"] I --> K["F_R = sqrt(F_H² + F_V²)"] J --> K K --> L[Final Answer with Direction] H --> L ``` ## 4.2 Block Architecture: Force Decomposition on Curved Surface ```mermaid graph TB subgraph INPUT [Input Parameters] A1[Curved Surface Geometry] A2[Fluid Specific Weight γ] A3[Submersion Depth h] end subgraph DECOMPOSE [Force Decomposition Engine] B1[Identify Vertical Projection A_V] B2[Identify Fluid Volume V above surface] B3[Compute h̄_V centroid depth of A_V] end subgraph COMPUTE [Force Computation] C1["F_H = γ · h̄_V · A_V"] C2["F_V = γ · V"] C3["F_R = sqrt(F_H² + F_V²)"] C4["tan α = F_V / F_H"] end subgraph OUTPUT [Final Output] D1[Horizontal Force Vector] D2[Vertical Force Vector] D3[Resultant Magnitude] D4[Direction Angle α] end A1 --> B1 A1 --> B2 A2 --> C1 A2 --> C2 A3 --> B3 B1 --> C1 B2 --> C2 B3 --> C1 C1 --> C3 C2 --> C3 C3 --> D3 C1 --> D1 C2 --> D2 C3 --> C4 C4 --> D4 ``` ## 4.3 Stability Analysis Flow for Floating Bodies ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Floating Body in Equilibrium] --> B[Identify Center of Gravity G] A --> C[Identify Center of Buoyancy B] A --> D[Compute Metacenter M using GM = I/V - BG] D --> E{GM Sign?} E -->|GM > 0| F[STABLE Body - G below M] E -->|GM = 0| G[NEUTRAL Equilibrium] E -->|GM < 0| H[UNSTABLE Body - Capsizes] F --> I[Restoring Couple Exists] H --> J[Overturning Couple Dominates] ``` ## 4.4 Schematic Representation: Inclined Plane Submerged in Fluid ``` Free Surface (P = P_atm) ───────────────────────────────────── \ \ \ \ Fluid \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \←—————————→\ \ x̄ (along plane) \ \ \ \ Centroid G \ \•—————→\ \ \ \bar{x}·sinθ \ \ G \ \ \ \ \ \ \θ ←———————\ \ \ \ \ \←————→\ \ \bar{h}\ \ \ \bar{x} \ \ \ \ Submerged Plane \ \ \ \______________________\ Center of Pressure P (below G by distance e) ``` **Key Reference Points:** - **G** = Centroid of submerged area, at depth $\bar{h} = \bar{x} \sin\theta$ - **P** = Center of pressure, at depth $h_c = \bar{h} + \frac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{\bar{h} A}$ - **$\theta$** = Angle between inclined plane and horizontal - **e** = Distance from centroid to center of pressure along the inclined plane ## 4.5 Schematic Representation: Curved Surface Force Decomposition ``` Free Surface ════════════════════════════════════════════ | | | Imaginary | Fluid Column | Fluid | (weight = F_V) | Volume V | | | |←—————A_V—————→| (Vertical projection) ↓ ↓ ╔══════════════════════════╗ ╱ Curved Surface ╲ ╱ (e.g., circular arc) ╲ ╱ ╲ ╱←———————————A————————————→ ╲ ╱ F_H acts horizontally ╲ ╱ through CP of A_V ╲ ╱__________________________________________╲ F_V acts upward/downward through centroid of V ``` **Force Vector Resolution:** - $F_H$ = Horizontal thrust (acts on vertical projection) - $F_V$ = Vertical thrust (equals weight of fluid above) - $F_R$ = Resultant force = $\sqrt{F_H^2 + F_V^2}$ # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1: Conceptual Short Answer **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]** **Q:** Define the term **center of pressure** for a submerged plane surface. Explain why the center of pressure is always located **below** the centroid of the submerged area (for surfaces with free liquid surface above). **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** The center of pressure is the point on a submerged surface where the resultant hydrostatic force is considered to act. **Explanation (2 marks):** The pressure on a submerged surface increases linearly with depth (P = γh). Hence, the lower portions of the surface experience greater pressure than the upper portions. This non-uniform pressure distribution causes the resultant force to act at a point shifted downward from the centroid. The shift is given by: $$e = \frac{I_G \sin\theta}{\bar{h} A}$$ Since $I_G > 0$, $\sin\theta > 0$, $\bar{h} > 0$, and $A > 0$, we have $e > 0$, confirming that the center of pressure always lies **below** the centroid. --- ### Question A2: Conceptual Short Answer **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]** **Q:** State **Archimedes' Principle**. A solid cube of side 0.5 m weighs 7.5 kN in air. Will it float or sink when placed in water ($\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³)? **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Statement (1.5 marks):** Archimedes' Principle states that when a body is fully or partially immersed in a fluid, it experiences an upward buoyant force equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by the body. $$F_B = \gamma_f \cdot \mathcal{V}_{displaced}$$ **Calculation (1.5 marks):** - Volume of cube: $\mathcal{V} = 0.5^3 = 0.125 \text{ m}^3$ - Maximum buoyant force (fully submerged): $F_B = 9.81 \times 0.125 = 1.226$ kN - Weight of cube: $W = 7.5$ kN Since $W$ (7.5 kN) $\gg$ $F_B$ (1.226 kN), the cube will **SINK** in water. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks): Forces on Plane and Curved Surfaces **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]** **(a)** A vertical rectangular gate, 2 m wide and 3 m high, is installed in a dam with its top edge at the water surface. Determine: (i) The magnitude of the resultant hydrostatic force on the gate. **[4 marks]** (ii) The location of the center of pressure from the water surface. **[3 marks]** **(b)** A curved gate in the form of a quadrant of a circle of radius 2 m is installed at the bottom of a water tank. The gate retains water on one side as shown. Determine: (i) The horizontal force on the gate. **[3.5 marks]** (ii) The vertical force on the gate. **[3.5 marks]** #### Complete Model Solution **Part (a) Solution:** **Given:** - Width $b = 2$ m, Height $H = 3$ m - Top edge at water surface - $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³ **(i) Resultant Force (4 marks):** Vertical surface with top at free surface: $$\bar{h} = \frac{H}{2} = \frac{3}{2} = 1.5 \text{ m}$$ Area $A = b \times H = 2 \times 3 = 6 \text{ m}^2$ $$F_R = \gamma \bar{h} A = 9.81 \times 1.5 \times 6$$ $$F_R = 88.29 \text{ kN}$$ **(ii) Center of Pressure (3 marks):** For vertical surface, $\theta = 90°$, so $\sin^2\theta = 1$. For a rectangle of width $b$ and height $H$ about its centroidal axis: $$I_G = \frac{bH^3}{12} = \frac{2 \times 3^3}{12} = \frac{54}{12} = 4.5 \text{ m}^4$$ $$h_c = \bar{h} + \frac{I_G}{\bar{h} A} = 1.5 + \frac{4.5}{1.5 \times 6} = 1.5 + 0.5 = 2.0 \text{ m}$$ **The center of pressure is at 2.0 m below the water surface.** --- **Part (b) Solution:** **Given:** - Quadrant of circle, radius $R = 2$ m - Water on convex side **(i) Horizontal Force (3.5 marks):** The horizontal projection of the quadrant is a vertical rectangle of width $R = 2$ m and height $R = 2$ m. $$A_V = R \times R = 2 \times 2 = 4 \text{ m}^2$$ $$\bar{h}_V = \frac{R}{2} = 1 \text{ m}$$ $$F_H = \gamma \bar{h}_V A_V = 9.81 \times 1 \times 4 = 39.24 \text{ kN}$$ **(ii) Vertical Force (3.5 marks):** The vertical force equals the weight of water in the volume bounded by the quadrant arc, its vertical projection, and the free surface (imaginary fluid column). The volume is the quarter-cylindrical volume above the quadrant: $$\mathcal{V} = \frac{\pi R^2}{4} \times R = \frac{\pi (2)^2}{4} \times 2 = 2\pi \text{ m}^3$$ $$\mathcal{V} = 6.283 \text{ m}^3$$ $$F_V = \gamma \mathcal{V} = 9.81 \times 6.283 = 61.64 \text{ kN}$$ (acting upward, since fluid is below the curved surface in this configuration) --- ### Question B (14 Marks): Buoyancy and Stability **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern]** **(a)** A wooden cylinder of diameter 0.6 m and height 0.8 m weighs 1.8 kN. Determine whether it will float in water. If it floats, find the depth of immersion. **[7 marks]** **(b)** A rectangular pontoon of length 6 m, width 4 m, and height 2 m weighs 200 kN. Calculate the metacentric height and comment on its stability. Assume the center of gravity is at the mid-height of the pontoon. **[7 marks]** #### Complete Model Solution **Part (a) Solution:** **Given:** - Diameter $D = 0.6$ m, Height $H = 0.8$ m - Weight $W = 1.8$ kN - $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³ **Check floating condition:** Volume of cylinder: $$\mathcal{V}_{cyl} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \times H = \frac{\pi (0.6)^2}{4} \times 0.8 = 0.2261 \text{ m}^3$$ Maximum buoyant force (fully submerged): $$F_B^{max} = 9.81 \times 0.2261 = 2.218 \text{ kN}$$ Since $W = 1.8$ kN $< F_B^{max} = 2.218$ kN, the cylinder **WILL FLOAT**. **Depth of immersion $h$:** Let $h$ be the depth of immersion. By floating equilibrium: $$W = F_B = \gamma_w \cdot \left(\frac{\pi D^2}{4}\right) \cdot h$$ $$1.8 = 9.81 \times \frac{\pi (0.6)^2}{4} \times h$$ $$1.8 = 9.81 \times 0.2827 \times h$$ $$1.8 = 2.7738 \, h$$ $$h = 0.6489 \text{ m}$$ **Depth of immersion = 0.649 m (approximately 81% of cylinder height)** --- **Part (b) Solution:** **Given:** - Pontoon: $L = 6$ m, $B = 4$ m, $H = 2$ m - Weight $W = 200$ kN - $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³ - G is at mid-height (1 m from base) **Step 1: Depth of immersion $h$:** $$W = \gamma_w \cdot L \cdot B \cdot h$$ $$200 = 9.81 \times 6 \times 4 \times h = 235.44 \, h$$ $$h = 0.849 \text{ m}$$ **Step 2: Displaced volume:** $$\mathcal{V}_{disp} = 6 \times 4 \times 0.849 = 20.38 \text{ m}^3$$ **Step 3: Center of Buoyancy B location (from base):** $$OB = \frac{h}{2} = \frac{0.849}{2} = 0.4245 \text{ m}$$ **Step 4: Center of Gravity G location (from base):** $$OG = \frac{H}{2} = 1.0 \text{ m}$$ **Step 5: Distance BG:** $$BG = OG - OB = 1.0 - 0.4245 = 0.5755 \text{ m}$$ **Step 6: Second moment of area of waterline plane:** Waterline is a rectangle $L \times B$ with axis of rotation along its length: $$I = \frac{L \cdot B^3}{12} = \frac{6 \times 4^3}{12} = \frac{6 \times 64}{12} = 32 \text{ m}^4$$ **Step 7: BM (distance from B to M):** $$BM = \frac{I}{\mathcal{V}_{disp}} = \frac{32}{20.38} = 1.570 \text{ m}$$ **Step 8: Metacentric height GM:** $$GM = BM - BG = 1.570 - 0.5755 = 0.994 \text{ m}$$ **Since GM = 0.994 m > 0, the pontoon is STABLE.** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Forgetting to use gauge pressure:** When the problem says "top at water surface," use $P = \gamma h$, not $P_{atm} + \gamma h$. **[Lose 1–2 marks]** > > 2. **Confusing centroid depth $\bar{h}$ with depth of center of pressure $h_c$:** Many students compute $F_R$ using $h_c$ instead of $\bar{h}$. **Correct formula: $F_R = \gamma \bar{h} A$ uses centroid depth.** **[Lose 2 marks]** > > 3. **Wrong direction for $F_V$ on curved surfaces:** Always check whether fluid is above or below the curved surface. If fluid is below (like inside a tank with curved bottom), $F_V$ acts **upward**. **[Lose 2 marks]** > > 4. **Forgetting $I_G$ formula:** Memorize the second moment of area for common shapes: > - Rectangle (about centroid): $\frac{bh^3}{12}$ > - Circle: $\frac{\pi D^4}{64}$ > - Triangle (about centroid): $\frac{bh^3}{36}$ > > 5. **Units inconsistency:** Use $\gamma$ in kN/m³ and lengths in m to get force directly in kN. Mixing units (e.g., cm with kN/m³) is a frequent error. **[Lose 1 mark]** > > 6. **Stability problem errors:** Students often forget to compute BG (distance between B and G) before computing GM. Also, the axis of rotation matters for computing $I$ — for a ship-like body, $I$ is about the longitudinal axis. **[Lose 3 marks]** --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### 🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist - [ ] **Pressure formula:** $P = \gamma h$ (gauge) — the foundation of all hydrostatic force problems. - [ ] **Force on plane surface:** $F_R = \gamma \bar{h} A$ — always use **centroid depth**, NOT center of pressure depth. - [ ] **Center of pressure:** $h_c = \bar{h} + \frac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{\bar{h} A}$ — always **below** the centroid. - [ ] **Eccentricity formula:** $e = \frac{I_G \sin\theta}{\bar{h} A}$ — distance along inclined plane from centroid to CP. - [ ] **Horizontal force on curved surface:** $F_H = \gamma \bar{h}_V A_V$ — act on **vertical projection**. - [ ] **Vertical force on curved surface:** $F_V = \gamma \cdot \mathcal{V}$ — weight of fluid column above. - [ ] **Resultant magnitude:** $F_R = \sqrt{F_H^2 + F_V^2}$. - [ ] **Buoyancy:** $F_B = \gamma_f \mathcal{V}_{displaced}$ — Archimedes' Principle. - [ ] **Metacentric height:** $GM = \frac{I}{\mathcal{V}_{disp}} - BG$ — positive value means stable. - [ ] **Waterline area moments:** Rectangle $LB^3/12$, Circle $\pi D^4/64$. ### 🎯 KTU 2024 High-Priority Topics | Priority | Topic | Frequency in KTU Papers | |----------|-------|------------------------| | 🔴 **HIGH** | Center of pressure on inclined surfaces | Every semester | | 🔴 **HIGH** | Horizontal/Vertical force on curved surfaces | Every semester | | 🟡 **MEDIUM** | Buoyancy & floating equilibrium | Frequently | | 🟡 **MEDIUM** | Metacentric height calculation | Frequently | | 🟢 **LOW** | Pressure prism method | Occasionally | ### 📌 Final Memory Aids 1. **"$\bar{h}$ for Force, $h_c$ for Location"** — Use centroid depth to compute force magnitude, then find center of pressure separately for moment calculations. 2. **"Curved Surface = Two Simple Problems"** — Always decompose into $F_H$ (acts on vertical projection) and $F_V$ (acts on fluid volume). 3. **"Archimedes says: weight of fluid pushed aside = upward push"** — Simple but powerful. 4. **"GM > 0 = Ship doesn't tip"** — The metacenter must be above the center of gravity for stability. 5. **Standard Values to Memorize:** - $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³ - $\gamma_{Hg} = 133.1$ kN/m³ - $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³ - $g = 9.81$ m/s² > [!IMPORTANT] > **End of Module 1 Notes — Forces on Planar and Curved Surfaces Immersed in Fluids** > > This topic is foundational for **Module 2 (Fluid Kinematics)** and **Module 3 (Fluid Dynamics)**. Mastery of pressure distribution, center of pressure, and buoyancy is essential for subsequent modules.

topics.title: Centre of Pressure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT741 - FLUID MECHANICS & MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Centre of Pressure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Centre of Pressure (CP)** is defined as the point on the immersed surface where the **resultant hydrostatic pressure force** acts. It is the line of action of the total (net) hydrostatic thrust on the submerged plane surface. Since pressure varies linearly with depth, the resultant force does NOT act at the geometric centroid of the surface — it acts at a point located **deeper** than the centroid.

In mathematical terms, for a plane surface submerged in a static fluid, the centre of pressure is located at a depth $h^*$ given by:

$$
h^* = \bar{h} + \frac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{A \bar{h}}
$$

where:
- $\bar{h}$ = depth of the **centroid** of the immersed surface from the free liquid surface.
- $I_G$ = **Second moment of area** (Moment of Inertia) of the surface about its own centroidal axis (parallel to the free surface).
- $A$ = Area of the immersed surface.
- $\theta$ = Angle of inclination of the surface with the **horizontal** free surface.
- $\rho$ = Mass density of the fluid (**ρ = 1000 kg/m³** for water at standard conditions).
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity (**g = 9.81 m/s²**).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine pushing your hand into a swimming pool, palm facing down.**
> 
> - If you keep your palm perfectly horizontal, the water pushes up **uniformly** on every square centimeter. The net upward push passes through the **center of your palm** (the centroid).
> - But now, **tilt your palm** so that one edge goes deeper. The deeper parts feel **more pressure** than the shallower parts. The combined "push" therefore shifts **downward** toward the deeper edge.
> - That shifted point is the **Centre of Pressure**. It is always *below* the centroid for any non-horizontal submerged surface.

**Why does this shift happen?**
Because hydrostatic pressure $P = \rho g h$ is **linear** in depth $h$, while area is spread out horizontally. The "weight" of water pushing deeper areas is much greater, so the average point of force application slides toward the deeper side.

## 1.3 Why Centre of Pressure Matters in Engineering

- **Dam Design:** The line of action of water thrust must be known precisely to prevent **overturning** and **sliding** failures.
- **Lock Gates & Ship Hulls:** Designers need to know exactly where the resultant hydrostatic force bites to size hinges, seals, and stiffeners.
- **Submarine Windows (Hulls):** Differential pressure across viewing ports requires knowledge of CP for safe window thickness calculations.
- **Gate Valves & Sluice Gates:** CP helps in determining hinge locations for self-actuating (automatic) gates.
- **Stability of Floating Bodies:** Metacentric height calculations depend on the shift of CP relative to the center of buoyancy.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Physical Logic

To locate the Centre of Pressure, we follow a structured reasoning sequence based on **Varignon's Principle of Moments**:

1. **Identify the elemental strip:** Consider an infinitesimal strip of area $dA$ at depth $h$ from the free liquid surface. Pressure on this strip is $P = \rho g h$.

2. **Compute the elemental force:** The elementary hydrostatic thrust acting on this strip is $dF = P \cdot dA = \rho g h \cdot dA$.

3. **Sum the total force (force integration):** The total hydrostatic thrust on the entire immersed surface is obtained by integrating over the whole area:
$$
F = \int_A \rho g h \, dA = \rho g \bar{h} A
$$
where $\bar{h}$ is the depth of the centroid of the area.

4. **Apply Varignon's Theorem (moment integration):** The moment of the total force about the free liquid surface equals the sum of the moments of all elemental forces about the same line.
$$
F \cdot h^* = \int_A h \cdot dF = \int_A h \cdot (\rho g h \cdot dA) = \rho g \int_A h^2 \, dA
$$

5. **Recognize the second moment of area:** The integral $\int_A h^2 \, dA$ is precisely the **second moment of area** $I_O$ of the surface about the axis where it intersects the free liquid surface (the free-surface axis).

6. **Substitute and simplify:** Using $F = \rho g \bar{h} A$ and the **Parallel Axis Theorem** $I_O = I_G + A \bar{h}^2$:
$$
h^* = \frac{I_O}{A \bar{h}} = \frac{I_G + A \bar{h}^2}{A \bar{h}} = \bar{h} + \frac{I_G}{A \bar{h}}
$$

7. **Generalize for inclined surfaces:** When the surface is inclined at angle $\theta$ to the horizontal, $h = y \sin\theta$ (where $y$ is the slant distance along the surface from the free liquid surface). Following the same logic:
$$
h^* = \bar{h} + \frac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{A \bar{h}}
$$

## 2.2 Key Observations & Boundary Conditions

- The CP is **always below** the centroid for any submerged plane surface (i.e., $h^* > \bar{h}$) because $\frac{I_G}{A\bar{h}} > 0$.
- For a **horizontal surface** ($\theta = 0$): $h^* = \bar{h}$ → CP and centroid **coincide** (pressure is uniform).
- For a **vertical surface** ($\theta = 90°$): $h^* = \bar{h} + \frac{I_G}{A\bar{h}}$ → maximum shift between CP and centroid.
- As the depth of submergence $\bar{h}$ **increases**, the CP shifts **closer** to the centroid (because $\frac{I_G}{A\bar{h}} \to 0$ as $\bar{h} \to \infty$). At very great depths, pressure is nearly uniform across the surface.
- For a **very deep** vertical surface, $h^* \approx \bar{h}$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU question on this topic.**

| # | Configuration | Depth of CP ($h^*$) | Total Thrust ($F$) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Horizontal surface (any shape) | $h^* = \bar{h}$ | $F = \rho g \bar{h} A$ |
| 2 | Vertical surface (any shape) | $h^* = \bar{h} + \dfrac{I_G}{A \bar{h}}$ | $F = \rho g \bar{h} A$ |
| 3 | Inclined surface (angle $\theta$ with horizontal) | $h^* = \bar{h} + \dfrac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{A \bar{h}}$ | $F = \rho g \bar{h} A$ |
| 4 | Vertical Rectangular Surface (width $b$, height $d$, top edge at free surface) | $h^* = \dfrac{2d}{3}$ | $F = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho g d^2 b$ |
| 5 | Vertical Circular Surface (radius $R$, top edge at free surface) | $h^* = \dfrac{5R}{4}$ | $F = \rho g R \cdot \pi R^2 = \pi \rho g R^3$ |
| 6 | Vertical Triangular Surface (apex at free surface, base at depth $d$) | $h^* = \dfrac{3d}{4}$ | $F = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho g d \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} d \cdot b$ |
| 7 | Vertical Triangular Surface (base at free surface, apex at depth $d$) | $h^* = \dfrac{d}{2}$ | $F = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho g d \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} d \cdot b$ |

**Standard $I_G$ values for reference:**

| Shape | $I_G$ about centroidal axis (parallel to free surface) |
|---|---|
| Rectangle ($b \times d$) | $\dfrac{b d^3}{12}$ |
| Circle (radius $R$) | $\dfrac{\pi R^4}{4}$ |
| Triangle (base $b$, height $d$) | $\dfrac{b d^3}{36}$ |
| Trapezoid | Depends on parallel-side lengths |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Centre of Pressure concept is **fundamental** in designing hydraulic structures. Engineers must know where the resultant thrust acts so they can:

- Place the **hinge** of a lock gate at the centre of pressure to eliminate hydrostatic moment on the gate (allowing minimal opening force).
- Compute the **overturning moment** on gravity dams about the toe.
- Determine the **buoyancy distribution** on flat-bottomed ship hulls.
- Analyze **sluice gate** stability and the torque required to operate them.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 General Derivation: Centre of Pressure for an Inclined Plane Surface

**Problem Setup:**
A plane surface of arbitrary shape is submerged in a static liquid of density $\rho$, inclined at angle $\theta$ to the horizontal free surface. The surface intersects the free liquid surface along line **OO′**.

**Step 1 — Choose a coordinate system:**
Let $y$ be the distance measured along the inclined surface from the free surface (line OO′) to any point on the surface. The depth of this point below the free surface is $h = y \sin\theta$.

**Step 2 — Consider an elemental strip:**
Take an infinitesimal strip of area $dA$ at slant distance $y$ from the free surface. The pressure acting on this strip is:
$$
P = \rho g h = \rho g y \sin\theta
$$

**Step 3 — Elemental force on the strip:**
$$
dF = P \cdot dA = \rho g y \sin\theta \cdot dA
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate to get total thrust:**
The total hydrostatic force on the entire immersed surface is:
$$
F = \int_A dF = \int_A \rho g y \sin\theta \, dA
$$
Since $\rho$, $g$, and $\sin\theta$ are constants:
$$
F = \rho g \sin\theta \int_A y \, dA
$$
By the **definition of centroid**, $\int_A y \, dA = \bar{y} \cdot A$, where $\bar{y}$ is the slant distance to the centroid. Hence:
$$
F = \rho g \sin\theta \cdot \bar{y} A = \rho g \bar{h} A
$$
where $\bar{h} = \bar{y} \sin\theta$ is the vertical depth of the centroid.

**Step 5 — Moment about the free surface (Varignon's Theorem):**
Taking moments about the line OO′ (intersection with free surface):
$$
F \cdot y^* = \int_A y \cdot dF = \int_A y \cdot (\rho g y \sin\theta \, dA) = \rho g \sin\theta \int_A y^2 \, dA
$$
The integral $\int_A y^2 \, dA = I_O$ is the **second moment of area** about the free-surface axis OO′.

**Step 6 — Apply the Parallel Axis Theorem:**
By the parallel axis theorem:
$$
I_O = I_G + A \bar{y}^2
$$
where $I_G$ is the second moment about the centroidal axis (parallel to OO′).

**Step 7 — Solve for the slant distance to the CP:**
$$
y^* = \frac{I_O}{A \bar{y}} = \frac{I_G + A \bar{y}^2}{A \bar{y}} = \bar{y} + \frac{I_G}{A \bar{y}}
$$

**Step 8 — Convert slant distance to vertical depth:**
Since $h^* = y^* \sin\theta$ and $\bar{h} = \bar{y} \sin\theta$:
$$
h^* = \bar{y}\sin\theta + \frac{I_G \sin\theta}{A \bar{y}}
$$
Dividing by $\sin\theta$ and recombining:
$$
h^* = \bar{h} + \frac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{A \bar{h}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the KTU master equation** for the depth of the centre of pressure on any inclined plane submerged surface. Memorize it.

## 3.2 Special Case: Vertical Rectangular Surface with Top Edge at the Free Surface

**Given:** Rectangle of width $b$ and height $d$, held vertically with its top edge flush with the free liquid surface.

**Step 1 — Centroid depth:**
The centroid lies at the geometric center:
$$
\bar{h} = \frac{d}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Area:**
$$
A = b \cdot d
$$

**Step 3 — Second moment of area about centroidal axis:**
For a rectangle, the $I_G$ about the horizontal centroidal axis is:
$$
I_G = \frac{b d^3}{12}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the master formula** (vertical surface → $\theta = 90°$, so $\sin^2\theta = 1$):
$$
h^* = \bar{h} + \frac{I_G}{A \bar{h}}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the values:**
$$
h^* = \frac{d}{2} + \frac{\dfrac{b d^3}{12}}{(b d) \cdot \dfrac{d}{2}}
$$

**Step 6 — Simplify the denominator:**
$$
(b d) \cdot \frac{d}{2} = \frac{b d^2}{2}
$$

**Step 7 — Divide:**
$$
\frac{\dfrac{b d^3}{12}}{\dfrac{b d^2}{2}} = \frac{b d^3}{12} \cdot \frac{2}{b d^2} = \frac{d}{6}
$$

**Step 8 — Final result:**
$$
h^* = \frac{d}{2} + \frac{d}{6} = \frac{3d + d}{6} = \frac{4d}{6} = \frac{2d}{3}
$$

**Step 9 — Total thrust:**
$$
F = \rho g \bar{h} A = \rho g \cdot \frac{d}{2} \cdot bd = \frac{1}{2} \rho g d^2 b
$$

## 3.3 Special Case: Vertical Circular Surface, Top Edge at Free Surface

**Given:** Circular plate of radius $R$ held vertically, with its topmost point just touching the free surface.

**Step 1 — Centroid depth:**
For a circle, the centroid is at the geometric center:
$$
\bar{h} = R
$$

**Step 2 — Area:**
$$
A = \pi R^2
$$

**Step 3 — Second moment of area:**
$$
I_G = \frac{\pi R^4}{4}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute:**
$$
h^* = R + \frac{\dfrac{\pi R^4}{4}}{\pi R^2 \cdot R} = R + \frac{\pi R^4}{4 \pi R^3} = R + \frac{R}{4}
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify:**
$$
h^* = \frac{5R}{4}
$$

## 3.4 Special Case: Vertical Triangular Surface, Apex at Free Surface

**Given:** Triangular plate with its apex at the free surface and horizontal base of width $b$ at depth $d$.

**Step 1 — Centroid depth:**
For a triangle, the centroid lies at $\frac{1}{3}$ from the base. Measured from the apex:
$$
\bar{h} = \frac{2d}{3}
$$

**Step 2 — Area:**
$$
A = \frac{1}{2} b d
$$

**Step 3 — Second moment of area about centroidal axis:**
For a triangle with base $b$ and height $d$:
$$
I_G = \frac{b d^3}{36}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute:**
$$
h^* = \frac{2d}{3} + \frac{\dfrac{b d^3}{36}}{\dfrac{1}{2} b d \cdot \dfrac{2d}{3}}
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the denominator:**
$$
\frac{1}{2} b d \cdot \frac{2d}{3} = \frac{b d^2}{3}
$$

**Step 6 — Divide:**
$$
\frac{\dfrac{b d^3}{36}}{\dfrac{b d^2}{3}} = \frac{b d^3}{36} \cdot \frac{3}{b d^2} = \frac{d}{12}
$$

**Step 7 — Final result:**
$$
h^* = \frac{2d}{3} + \frac{d}{12} = \frac{8d + d}{12} = \frac{9d}{12} = \frac{3d}{4}
$$

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem)

> **[KTU University Exam Pattern Problem]:**
> A rectangular gate 2 m wide and 3 m high is installed vertically with its top edge 1 m below the free water surface. Find:
> (a) The total hydrostatic thrust on the gate.
> (b) The depth of the centre of pressure from the free water surface.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the geometry:**
- Width $b = 2$ m
- Height $d = 3$ m
- Top of gate is at depth $h_{top} = 1$ m
- Bottom of gate is at depth $h_{bot} = 1 + 3 = 4$ m

**Step 2 — Centroid depth:**
$$
\bar{h} = h_{top} + \frac{d}{2} = 1 + \frac{3}{2} = 2.5 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Total thrust:**
$$
F = \rho g \bar{h} A = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 2.5 \times (2 \times 3)
$$

$$
F = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 2.5 \times 6 = 147{,}150 \text{ N} = 147.15 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4 — Second moment of area about centroidal axis:**
$$
I_G = \frac{b d^3}{12} = \frac{2 \times 3^3}{12} = \frac{2 \times 27}{12} = 4.5 \text{ m}^4
$$

**Step 5 — Depth of centre of pressure** (vertical surface, $\theta = 90°$):
$$
h^* = \bar{h} + \frac{I_G}{A \bar{h}}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute:**
$$
h^* = 2.5 + \frac{4.5}{6 \times 2.5} = 2.5 + \frac{4.5}{15} = 2.5 + 0.3
$$

$$
\boxed{h^* = 2.8 \text{ m from the free surface}}
$$

**Step 7 — Position of CP measured from the top of the gate:**
$$
h^*_{from\,top} = h^* - h_{top} = 2.8 - 1.0 = 1.8 \text{ m}
$$

---
