# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Centre of Pressure Concept Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Submerged Plane Surface of Area A] --> B[Decompose into Infinitesimal Strips dA]
    B --> C[Each strip at depth h = y sin theta]
    C --> D[Local Pressure P = rho g h]
    D --> E[Elemental Force dF = P dA]
    E --> F[Integrate dF over Area]
    F --> G[Total Thrust F = rho g h_bar A]
    E --> H[Elemental Moment h dF about free surface]
    H --> I[Integrate Moment over Area]
    I --> J[Total Moment M = rho g sin theta I_O]
    G --> K[Apply Varignon Theorem: F h* = M]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Use Parallel Axis Theorem: I_O = I_G + A y_bar squared]
    L --> M[Slant distance to CP: y* = y_bar + I_G / A y_bar]
    M --> N[Vertical depth to CP: h* = h_bar + I_G sin squared theta / A h_bar]
    N --> O[CP located below centroid]
    O --> P[Shift = I_G sin squared theta / A h_bar]
    P --> Q[Engineering Design: Dams, Gates, Hulls]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic: Geometry of an Inclined Submerged Surface

```mermaid
graph LR
    FreeSurface[Free Liquid Surface horizontal line]:::surf
    InclSurf[Inclined Plane Surface at angle theta]:::panel
    Centroid[Centroid G at depth h_bar]:::pt
    CP[Centre of Pressure CP at depth h_star]:::cp
    Intersection[Intersection Line OO with free surface]:::ref
    Element[Elemental Strip dA at slant distance y]:::elem
    ForceVec[Resultant Force F acting at CP]:::vec

    FreeSurface --- Intersection
    Intersection --- InclSurf
    InclSurf --- Element
    Centroid --- InclSurf
    CP --- InclSurf
    Centroid -.- CP
    ForceVec --- CP

    classDef surf fill:#a8d8ff,stroke:#003a75,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef panel fill:#fff4b3,stroke:#a07000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef pt fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef cp fill:#ff9999,stroke:#660000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000
    classDef ref fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef elem fill:#e0d0ff,stroke:#330099,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef vec fill:#ffd700,stroke:#665500,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000
```

## 4.3 Geometry Description (For Mental Visualization)

Since Mermaid cannot render physical fluid diagrams, here is the precise textual geometry:

- **Reference Frame:** A horizontal line at the top represents the free liquid surface.
- **Below the surface**, draw an inclined rectangle representing the submerged plate, making angle $\theta$ with the horizontal.
- The **top edge** of the plate intersects the free surface at a horizontal line (call it OO′).
- The **centroid** $G$ lies at the geometric center of the plate, at vertical depth $\bar{h}$ below the free surface.
- The **Centre of Pressure** $P$ lies on the **same vertical line through the centroid** (for symmetrical surfaces), at a slightly **greater depth** $h^* > \bar{h}$.
- The **shift** along the plate between $G$ and $P$ is $\Delta y = \dfrac{I_G}{A \bar{y}}$.
- The **resultant hydrostatic force** $F$ acts perpendicular to the plate, passing through $P$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** In KTU exams, always draw a clearly labelled free-body sketch of the submerged surface showing both $G$ and $P$ with their depths annotated. Examiners award method marks for clear diagrams even if calculations have minor slips.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define the term "Centre of Pressure" for a plane surface submerged in a static fluid. Why does the centre of pressure not coincide with the centroid of the surface in general?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Centre of Pressure** is the point on a submerged plane surface where the resultant hydrostatic pressure force is considered to act. It is the line of action of the total hydrostatic thrust on the surface.

The CP does not coincide with the centroid because **pressure is not uniform** over the submerged surface — it increases linearly with depth ($P = \rho g h$). The deeper portions of the surface experience larger pressure than the shallower portions, so the resultant force shifts **downward** toward the deeper side. The CP therefore lies **below** the centroid for any non-horizontal submerged surface. The two points coincide only when the surface is horizontal (uniform pressure) or when the surface is infinitely deep (pressure variation becomes negligible).

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for explanation of shift)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**A square plate of side 2 m is held vertically in water with its top edge at the free water surface. Determine the depth of the centre of pressure from the free surface.**

**Model Answer:**

Given: Side $a = 2$ m, vertical orientation, top edge at free surface.

Centroid depth:
$$
\bar{h} = \frac{a}{2} = 1 \text{ m}
$$

Area:
$$
A = a^2 = 4 \text{ m}^2
$$

Second moment of area about centroidal axis:
$$
I_G = \frac{a^4}{12} = \frac{2^4}{12} = \frac{16}{12} = \frac{4}{3} \text{ m}^4
$$

Depth of centre of pressure:
$$
h^* = \bar{h} + \frac{I_G}{A \bar{h}} = 1 + \frac{4/3}{4 \times 1} = 1 + \frac{1}{3} = \frac{4}{3} \text{ m} \approx 1.333 \text{ m}
$$

*(3 marks: 1 mark each for $\bar{h}$, $I_G$, and final $h^*$)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive an expression for the depth of the centre of pressure on an inclined plane surface submerged in a liquid, making an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal free surface.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A rectangular gate 1.5 m wide and 2 m high is hinged at the top and is inclined at 30° to the horizontal. The top edge of the gate is 1 m below the free water surface. Determine:
- (i) The magnitude of the resultant hydrostatic thrust on the gate.
- (ii) The location of the centre of pressure measured along the gate from the top edge.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Solution to (a) — Derivation:**

*Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes for the full step-by-step derivation. The final master equation is:*

$$
h^* = \bar{h} + \frac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{A \bar{h}}
$$

*with slant distance form:*

$$
y^* = \bar{y} + \frac{I_G}{A \bar{y}}
$$

*(Full marks require all 8 steps from Section 3.1 to be written out clearly with proper justification at each stage.)*

**Solution to (b) — Numerical Problem:**

**Given data:**
- Width $b = 1.5$ m
- Height along gate $L = 2$ m
- Inclination angle $\theta = 30°$
- Depth of top edge $h_{top} = 1$ m

**Step 1 — Centroid depth:**
$$
\bar{h} = h_{top} + \frac{L}{2} \sin\theta = 1 + \frac{2}{2} \sin 30° = 1 + 1 \times 0.5 = 1.5 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Area:**
$$
A = b \cdot L = 1.5 \times 2 = 3 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Total hydrostatic thrust (Part i):**
$$
F = \rho g \bar{h} A = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 1.5 \times 3 = 44{,}145 \text{ N} = 44.145 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4 — Second moment of area:**
$$
I_G = \frac{b L^3}{12} = \frac{1.5 \times 2^3}{12} = \frac{1.5 \times 8}{12} = 1.0 \text{ m}^4
$$

**Step 5 — Depth of CP from free surface:**
$$
h^* = \bar{h} + \frac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{A \bar{h}}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute:**
$$
h^* = 1.5 + \frac{1.0 \times \sin^2 30°}{3 \times 1.5}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute $\sin^2 30° = 0.25$:**
$$
h^* = 1.5 + \frac{1.0 \times 0.25}{4.5} = 1.5 + \frac{0.25}{4.5} = 1.5 + 0.0556
$$

$$
h^* \approx 1.5556 \text{ m below the free surface}
$$

**Step 8 — Slant distance to centroid from top edge:**
$$
\bar{y} = \frac{L}{2} = 1.0 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 9 — Slant distance to CP from intersection of plane with free surface:**
$$
y^* = \bar{y} + \frac{I_G}{A \bar{y}} = 1.0 + \frac{1.0}{3 \times 1.0} = 1.0 + 0.333 = 1.333 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 10 — Distance of CP from top edge of gate:**
$$
d_{CP\,from\,top} = y^* - (h_{top} / \sin\theta) = 1.333 - \frac{1.0}{0.5} = 1.333 - 2.0
$$

Wait — this gives a negative number, meaning the CP is actually *above* the top edge measured along the gate slant. This indicates that the top of the gate lies *below* the intersection of the inclined plane with the free surface. Let us instead measure directly from the top edge along the slant:

**Correct Step 10:** Distance from top of gate to CP measured along slant:
The vertical depth of CP from the top of the gate is:
$$
\Delta h = h^* - h_{top} = 1.5556 - 1.0 = 0.5556 \text{ m}
$$
Converting to slant distance:
$$
\Delta s = \frac{\Delta h}{\sin\theta} = \frac{0.5556}{0.5} = 1.111 \text{ m}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- **(i)** Total thrust $F = 44.145$ kN
- **(ii)** CP is located **1.111 m down the gate from the top edge** (slant distance).

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A circular gate of diameter 1.5 m is mounted vertically in a water tank, with its centre located 5 m below the free water surface. Find:
- (i) The total hydrostatic thrust on the gate.
- (ii) The position of the centre of pressure below the free surface.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A triangular plate with its base horizontal at the top and apex pointing downward is submerged vertically with its base at the free water surface. The base is 4 m wide and the plate is 6 m tall. Determine the location of the centre of pressure measured from the free surface. Also find the total thrust on the plate.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Solution to (a):**

**Given data:**
- Diameter $D = 1.5$ m, so radius $R = 0.75$ m
- Centroid depth $\bar{h} = 5$ m
- Vertical orientation ($\theta = 90°$)

**Step 1 — Area of the circular gate:**
$$
A = \pi R^2 = \pi \times 0.75^2 = \pi \times 0.5625 \approx 1.7671 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Total hydrostatic thrust (Part i):**
$$
F = \rho g \bar{h} A = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 5 \times 1.7671
$$

**Step 3 — Compute:**
$$
F = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 5 \times 1.7671 = 86{,}676 \text{ N} \approx 86.68 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4 — Second moment of area:**
$$
I_G = \frac{\pi R^4}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.75^4}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.31641}{4} \approx 0.2485 \text{ m}^4
$$

**Step 5 — Depth of CP (Part ii):**
$$
h^* = \bar{h} + \frac{I_G}{A \bar{h}}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute:**
$$
h^* = 5 + \frac{0.2485}{1.7671 \times 5} = 5 + \frac{0.2485}{8.8355}
$$

$$
h^* = 5 + 0.0281 = 5.0281 \text{ m}
$$

**Final Answers for (a):**
- (i) $F \approx 86.68$ kN
- (ii) $h^* \approx 5.028$ m (note how close to $\bar{h}$ because of large depth)

---

**Solution to (b):**

**Given data:**
- Base width $b = 4$ m (horizontal, at free surface)
- Height $d = 6$ m (apex pointing downward, at depth 6 m)
- Vertical orientation
- Base is at the free surface

**Step 1 — Centroid depth:**
For a triangle, the centroid lies at $\frac{1}{3}$ of the height from the base. Since the base is at the top (free surface) and the apex is at the bottom:
$$
\bar{h} = \frac{d}{3} = \frac{6}{3} = 2 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Area:**
$$
A = \frac{1}{2} \times b \times d = \frac{1}{2} \times 4 \times 6 = 12 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Second moment of area about centroidal axis:**
For a triangle with base $b$ and height $d$:
$$
I_G = \frac{b d^3}{36} = \frac{4 \times 6^3}{36} = \frac{4 \times 216}{36} = \frac{864}{36} = 24 \text{ m}^4
$$

**Step 4 — Location of centre of pressure:**
$$
h^* = \bar{h} + \frac{I_G}{A \bar{h}}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute:**
$$
h^* = 2 + \frac{24}{12 \times 2} = 2 + \frac{24}{24} = 2 + 1 = 3 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 6 — Total thrust on the plate:**
$$
F = \rho g \bar{h} A = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 2 \times 12 = 235{,}440 \text{ N} = 235.44 \text{ kN}
$$

**Final Answers for (b):**
- Centre of pressure depth $h^* = 3$ m below the free surface
- Total thrust $F = 235.44$ kN

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $h^*$ (CP) with $\bar{h}$ (centroid).** Many students write $F = \rho g h^* A$ for the total force. WRONG! The total force uses $\bar{h}$, NOT $h^*$. Examiners will deduct **1 full mark**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to apply $\sin^2\theta$ for inclined surfaces.** Using the vertical formula when the surface is inclined is a classic error. Always check the geometry before writing the formula.
>
> 3. **Mixing up slant distance and vertical depth.** When asked for "distance from top of gate along the gate," give the slant distance $y^*$, not the vertical depth $h^*$. Convert using $y^* = h^* / \sin\theta$.
>
> 4. **Wrong $I_G$ formula for triangles.** The formula is $I_G = \frac{bd^3}{36}$ (NOT $\frac{bd^3}{12}$ — that's for a rectangle). Examiners test this specifically.
>
> 5. **Incorrect identification of centroid for non-rectangular shapes.** For a triangle with apex at the free surface, $\bar{h} = \frac{2d}{3}$, but for a triangle with base at the free surface, $\bar{h} = \frac{d}{3}$. Draw the figure clearly!
>
> 6. **Units slip-up.** Always quote $F$ in Newtons (N) or kiloNewtons (kN), and depths in metres (m). Unit mistakes can cost **0.5 mark**.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Centre of Pressure**
>
> - ✅ **Definition:** CP = point of action of resultant hydrostatic force on a submerged surface.
> - ✅ **Master Formula:** $h^* = \bar{h} + \dfrac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{A \bar{h}}$
> - ✅ **Slant Version:** $y^* = \bar{y} + \dfrac{I_G}{A \bar{y}}$
> - ✅ **Total Force Formula:** $F = \rho g \bar{h} A$ (uses centroid depth, NOT CP depth!)
> - ✅ **Vertical Surface:** $\sin^2\theta = 1$ simplifies the formula.
> - ✅ **Horizontal Surface:** CP = Centroid (uniform pressure).
> - ✅ **Standard $I_G$ values:**
>   - Rectangle ($b \times d$): $\dfrac{bd^3}{12}$
>   - Circle (radius $R$): $\dfrac{\pi R^4}{4}$
>   - Triangle ($b \times d$): $\dfrac{bd^3}{36}$
> - ✅ **Special CP locations (vertical, top edge at free surface):**
>   - Rectangle: $h^* = \dfrac{2d}{3}$
>   - Circle: $h^* = \dfrac{5R}{4}$
>   - Triangle (apex up): $h^* = \dfrac{3d}{4}$
>   - Triangle (apex down): $h^* = \dfrac{d}{2}$
> - ✅ **CP is always below centroid** (for non-horizontal surfaces).
> - ✅ **As depth increases, CP approaches centroid.**
> - ✅ **Parallel Axis Theorem:** $I_O = I_G + A\bar{y}^2$ — required for derivation.
> - ✅ **Varignon's Theorem:** Moment of resultant = sum of moments of components.
> - ✅ **Standard values:** $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s².
> - ✅ **Engineering applications:** dams, lock gates, sluice gates, ship hulls, submarines.
> - ✅ **Always draw a labelled diagram** — earns method marks even with calculation errors.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Buoyancy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT741 - FLUID MECHANICS & MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Buoyancy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Buoyancy** is the upward thrust (force) exerted by a fluid on a body that is either fully submerged in it or floating on its surface. This force is generated by the pressure difference between the lower and upper regions of the fluid acting on the body, and it acts vertically upward through the **Center of Buoyancy (B)** — the centroid of the displaced fluid volume.

The phenomenon is governed by the classical **Archimedes' Principle** (3rd Century BCE, Archimedes of Syracuse), which states:

> *"When a body is immersed (fully or partially) in a fluid at rest, it experiences an upward buoyant force equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by the body."*

Mathematically expressed as:
$$F_B = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot \nabla$$

where $\rho_f$ is the **mass density of the fluid**, $g$ is the **acceleration due to gravity ($9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$)**, and $\nabla$ is the **displaced volume of the fluid**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Buoyancy is the foundational prerequisite for the stability analysis of floating vessels (ships, pontoons, submarines) and submerged structures. In KTU ESE questions, students must distinguish between **Equilibrium of Floating Bodies** and **Equilibrium of Submerged Bodies** — both are high-weightage areas.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine pushing a rubber ball underwater in a swimming pool. The moment you release it, the ball **shoots upward** to the surface and bobs there. Why?

- The water below the ball is under **higher pressure** than the water above it (because pressure increases with depth: $P = \rho g h$).
- This pressure imbalance creates a net upward push — the **buoyant force**.
- The ball rises until the upward buoyant force equals the downward weight of the ball, achieving **equilibrium**.

**Everyday Analogy — The Hot Air Balloon:**
A hot air balloon rises because the air inside (heated) is **less dense** than the surrounding cool air. The weight of the cool air displaced by the balloon's volume is *greater* than the balloon's own weight — so it floats, just like a cork floats on water.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A body floats, sinks, or remains neutrally buoyant based on the comparison between its **weight ($W = \rho_b \cdot g \cdot V$)** and the **buoyant force ($F_B = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot V$)**:
> - If $F_B > W$ → Body **rises** (floats with partial immersion)
> - If $F_B = W$ → Body is in **neutral equilibrium** (suspended)
> - If $F_B < W$ → Body **sinks** (fully submerged in stable position if properly ballasted)

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

| Constant | Value | Unit |
|----------|-------|------|
| Acceleration due to gravity ($g$) | **9.81** | m/s² |
| Density of water (at 4°C) | **1000** | kg/m³ |
| Specific weight of water ($\gamma_w$) | **9810** | N/m³ |
| Density of mercury | **13600** | kg/m³ |
| Density of air (at STP) | **1.225** | kg/m³ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic: Forces Acting on a Floating/Submerged Body

When a body is placed in a static fluid, it is subjected to **two primary vertical forces**:

1. **Weight ($W$)** — acts vertically downward through the **Center of Gravity (G)**.
   - $W = m \cdot g = \rho_b \cdot g \cdot V_{body}$

2. **Buoyant Force ($F_B$)** — acts vertically upward through the **Center of Buoyancy (B)**.
   - $F_B = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot V_{displaced}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Center of Gravity (G):** Fixed point in the body where its entire weight is assumed to act.
> **Center of Buoyancy (B):** Centroid of the displaced fluid volume. Unlike G, B shifts as the body tilts.

### 2.2 Stability Analysis — The Three Critical Points (G, B, M)

For a floating body tilted at a small angle $\theta$, the **Center of Buoyancy (B) shifts** to a new position $B'$. The vertical line through the new $B'$ intersects the original vertical axis (line GB) at a point called the **Metacenter (M)**.

The **metacentric height (GM)** is the distance between the Center of Gravity (G) and the Metacenter (M).

$$\overline{GM} = \overline{GB} + \overline{BM}$$

Where:
- $\overline{GB}$ = Distance between G and the original B (negative if G is below B).
- $\overline{BM}$ = $\dfrac{I}{V_{displaced}}$ (where $I$ is the **second moment of area** of the waterline plane about the axis of tilt, and $V_{displaced}$ is the submerged volume).

### 2.3 Conditions of Equilibrium

| State | Condition | Physical Meaning |
|-------|-----------|------------------|
| **Stable Equilibrium** | $\overline{GM} > 0$ (M is above G) | Body returns to original position after tilting |
| **Unstable Equilibrium** | $\overline{GM} < 0$ (M is below G) | Body capsizes after tilting |
| **Neutral Equilibrium** | $\overline{GM} = 0$ (M coincides with G) | Body remains at new tilted position |

### 2.4 Period of Oscillation (Transverse)

For a floating body performing small oscillations:

$$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{k^2}{\overline{GM} \cdot g}}$$

Where:
- $k$ = Radius of gyration of the body about its longitudinal axis through G.
- $\overline{GM}$ = Metacentric height.
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | SI Units |
|---|---------|-------------|----------|
| 1 | $F_B = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot \nabla$ | Buoyant force | N (Newton) |
| 2 | $W = \rho_b \cdot g \cdot V$ | Weight of body | N |
| 3 | $\rho_b < \rho_f$ | Floating condition | kg/m³ |
| 4 | $\rho_b = \rho_f$ | Neutral buoyancy | kg/m³ |
| 5 | $\rho_b > \rho_f$ | Sinking condition | kg/m³ |
| 6 | $\overline{BM} = \dfrac{I}{\nabla}$ | BM distance (stability) | m |
| 7 | $\overline{GM} = \overline{GB} + \overline{BM}$ | Metacentric height | m |
| 8 | $T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{k^2}{\overline{GM} \cdot g}}$ | Period of oscillation | s |
| 9 | $V_{submerged} = \dfrac{W}{\rho_f \cdot g}$ | Submerged volume | m³ |
| 10 | $\gamma_{apparent} = \gamma_b - F_B/V$ | Apparent specific weight | N/m³ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Buoyancy principles govern **ship design** (naval architecture), **submarine ballasting**, **hydrometers** (for measuring fluid density), **cable-laying barges**, and **float-type flowmeters**. The metacentric height is a critical safety parameter in IMO maritime regulations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Buoyant Force from First Principles (Pressure Integration)

**Setup:** Consider a vertical cylindrical body of cross-sectional area $A$ and height $h$, fully submerged in a fluid of density $\rho_f$. The top surface is at depth $h_1$ and the bottom surface is at depth $h_2 = h_1 + h$.

**Step 1:** Write the gauge pressures at the top and bottom of the cylinder.

$$P_{top} = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot h_1$$

$$P_{bottom} = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot h_2 = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot (h_1 + h)$$

**Step 2:** Compute the net vertical force (upward) by multiplying each pressure by the area $A$.

$$F_{down} = P_{top} \cdot A = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot h_1 \cdot A$$

$$F_{up} = P_{bottom} \cdot A = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot (h_1 + h) \cdot A$$

**Step 3:** Subtract to find the net upward (buoyant) force.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_B &= F_{up} - F_{down} \\
F_B &= \rho_f \cdot g \cdot (h_1 + h) \cdot A - \rho_f \cdot g \cdot h_1 \cdot A \\
F_B &= \rho_f \cdot g \cdot A \cdot h \\
F_B &= \rho_f \cdot g \cdot \nabla
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $A \cdot h = V_{displaced} = \nabla$ (volume of fluid displaced), we recover the standard form of **Archimedes' Principle**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Generalization:** Although derived for a cylinder, the result $\rho_f g \nabla$ holds for *any arbitrary shape* because horizontal pressure forces cancel out, leaving only the net vertical pressure difference equal to the weight of the displaced fluid column.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Metacentric Height ($\overline{BM}$)

**Setup:** A floating body of submerged volume $\nabla$ is tilted through a small angle $\theta$ about a longitudinal axis $O$. The waterline plane changes from $WL$ to $WL'$. The wedge $WOV$ emerges on one side; the wedge $WOW'$ submerges on the other.

**Step 1:** The Center of Buoyancy shifts from $B$ to $B'$. The new buoyant force acts through $B'$ vertically upward.

**Step 2:** Take moments about the tilt axis $O$ for the buoyant force before and after tilting.

- **Before tilting:** Buoyant force $F_B$ acts through $B$ on the axis. Moment = 0.
- **After tilting:** Buoyant force $F_B$ acts through $B'$, at a horizontal offset from the original vertical line.

**Step 3:** Equate the moments for equilibrium. The shift in $B$ is caused by the transfer of a wedge of volume $\delta V$ from one side to the other.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_B \cdot \overline{BB'} &= W_{wedge} \cdot x \\
\rho_f \cdot g \cdot \nabla \cdot \overline{BB'} &= \rho_f \cdot g \cdot \delta V \cdot x \\
\overline{BB'} \cdot \nabla &= \delta V \cdot x
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** The metacenter $M$ is found where the new vertical line of action of $F_B$ intersects the original vertical line $GB$.

For small angles, $\overline{BB'} = \overline{BM} \cdot \sin\theta$, and the wedge analysis yields $\delta V \cdot x = I \cdot \theta$ (where $I$ is the second moment of area of the waterline plane).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{BM} \cdot \sin\theta \cdot \nabla &= I \cdot \theta \\
\overline{BM} \cdot \nabla &= I \quad (\text{since } \sin\theta \approx \theta \text{ for small angles}) \\
\overline{BM} &= \frac{I}{\nabla}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Worked Example

**Problem:** A solid cube of side $0.5 \text{ m}$ and density $800 \text{ kg/m}^3$ is floating in water ($\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$). Find:
1. The depth of immersion.
2. The buoyant force.
3. The metacentric height if the waterline plane is a square of side $0.5 \text{ m}$.

**Given:**
- Side $a = 0.5 \text{ m}$
- $\rho_b = 800 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Solution:**

**Part (1): Depth of Immersion**

Let $h$ be the submerged depth. By Archimedes' Principle:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{body} &= F_B \\
\rho_b \cdot g \cdot a^3 &= \rho_w \cdot g \cdot a^2 \cdot h \\
800 \cdot a &= 1000 \cdot h \\
h &= \frac{800 \times 0.5}{1000} = 0.4 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (2): Buoyant Force**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_B &= \rho_w \cdot g \cdot a^2 \cdot h \\
F_B &= 1000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot (0.5)^2 \cdot 0.4 \\
F_B &= 1000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.25 \cdot 0.4 \\
F_B &= 981 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (3): Metacentric Height**

For a square waterline plane of side $a$, $I = \dfrac{a^4}{12}$ and $V_{displaced} = a^2 \cdot h$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{BM} &= \frac{I}{\nabla} = \frac{a^4 / 12}{a^2 \cdot h} = \frac{a^2}{12 \cdot h} \\
\overline{BM} &= \frac{(0.5)^2}{12 \times 0.4} = \frac{0.25}{4.8} \\
\overline{BM} &= 0.0521 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Now $\overline{GB} = \dfrac{h}{2} - \dfrac{a}{2} = \dfrac{0.4}{2} - \dfrac{0.5}{2} = 0.2 - 0.25 = -0.05 \text{ m}$ (G is below B because the body is less dense on top).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{GM} &= \overline{GB} + \overline{BM} \\
\overline{GM} &= -0.05 + 0.0521 \\
\overline{GM} &= 0.0021 \text{ m} \approx 2.1 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Since $\overline{GM} > 0$, the cube is in **stable equilibrium** (marginally — a very small positive metacentric height).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: Buoyancy Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Body Placed in Fluid] --> condA{Compare rho_body vs rho_fluid}
    condA -->|rho_body greater than rho_fluid| branch1[SINKS Completely]
    condA -->|rho_body equal to rho_fluid| branch2[NEUTRAL BUOYANCY - Suspended]
    condA -->|rho_body less than rho_fluid| branch3[FLOATS at Surface]
    branch3 --> stab{Stability Check}
    stab -->|GM greater than 0| stableA[STABLE - Returns to Upright]
    stab -->|GM less than 0| unstableA[UNSTABLE - Capsizes]
    stab -->|GM equals 0| neutralA[NEUTRAL - Stays at Tilted Angle]
    stableA --> periodA[Oscillates with Period T]
    periodA --> formulaA[T = 2 PI times sqrt k squared over GM times g]
    branch1 --> eq1[F_B less than W]
    branch2 --> eq2[F_B equals W]
    stableA --> eq3[F_B equals W at New Orientation]
```

### 4.2 Schematic: Stability Geometry of a Floating Body

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph FloatingBody[Stability Geometry of a Tilted Floating Vessel]
        pointG[Point G - Center of Gravity - Fixed in Body]
        pointB[Point B - Center of Buoyancy - Original Position]
        pointBP[Point B prime - New Center of Buoyancy After Tilt]
        pointM[Point M - Metacenter - Intersection Point]
        pointO[Point O - Tilt Axis on Waterline]
        lineWL[Original Waterline WL]
        lineWLP[New Tilted Waterline WL prime]
        vecW[Weight W acts downward through G]
        vecFB[Buoyant Force FB acts upward through B prime]
    end
    pointG -->|Vertical Line GB| pointM
    pointB -->|Shift due to Tilt| pointBP
    pointBP -->|Vertical Line B prime M| pointM
    pointO --- lineWL
    pointO --- lineWLP
    vecW -. Acts on .-> pointG
    vecFB -. Acts on .-> pointBP
```

### 4.3 Stability Condition Table (Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Body Geometry] --> B[Compute Volume Displaced]
    B --> C[Compute Weight of Body]
    C --> D{F_B vs W Comparison}
    D -->|F_B greater than W| E[Float Stable]
    D -->|F_B equals W| F[Neutral]
    D -->|F_B less than W| G[Submerge]
    E --> H[Compute I of Waterline Plane]
    H --> I[Compute BM = I / V]
    I --> J[Compute GM = GB + BM]
    J --> K{GM Sign Check}
    K -->|GM greater than 0| L[Stable Equilibrium]
    K -->|GM less than 0| M[Unstable Capsize]
    K -->|GM equals 0| N[Neutral Equilibrium]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Visualization Tip:** In ESE answers, always draw a clean labeled diagram showing G, B, M, and the waterline plane. Examiners award **2-3 marks** specifically for correct diagram labeling in stability problems.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

State **Archimedes' Principle** and write its mathematical expression. Mention any two practical applications.

**Model Answer:**
Archimedes' Principle states that *"when a body is immersed (fully or partially) in a fluid at rest, it experiences an upward buoyant force equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by the body."*

$$F_B = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot \nabla$$

**Applications:**
1. **Hydrometers** — float at different depths in fluids of varying densities to measure specific gravity.
2. **Submarines** — use ballast tanks to control buoyancy for diving and surfacing.
3. **Hot air balloons** — exploit buoyancy of warm air for lift.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

Define **metacentric height**. What is its significance in the stability of a floating body?

**Model Answer:**
The **metacentric height (GM)** is the distance between the **Center of Gravity (G)** of a floating body and its **Metacenter (M)** — the point where the vertical line through the new Center of Buoyancy (B') intersects the original vertical line through G and the original B.

$$\overline{GM} = \overline{GB} + \overline{BM} = \overline{GB} + \dfrac{I}{\nabla}$$

**Significance:**
- If $\overline{GM} > 0$ → Body is in **stable equilibrium** (returns to upright after tilting).
- If $\overline{GM} < 0$ → Body is **unstable** and will capsize.
- If $\overline{GM} = 0$ → Body is in **neutral equilibrium**.

A higher metacentric height means **greater initial stability** but a **shorter, stiffer rolling period**.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

**Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** [7 Marks] A solid cylinder of diameter $0.3 \text{ m}$, height $0.4 \text{ m}$, and density $750 \text{ kg/m}^3$ floats in water. Find the depth of immersion and the buoyant force. Verify if the body is in stable equilibrium if the height of the Center of Gravity above the base is $0.2 \text{ m}$.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $d = 0.3 \text{ m}$, $h_{cyl} = 0.4 \text{ m}$, $\rho_b = 750 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Height of G above base = $0.2 \text{ m}$

**Step 1:** Volume of cylinder: $V = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.3)^2 \times 0.4 = 0.02827 \text{ m}^3$

**Step 2:** Weight: $W = 750 \times 9.81 \times 0.02827 = 208.05 \text{ N}$

**Step 3:** Let submerged depth = $h$. Equating weight and buoyancy:

$$
\begin{aligned}
1000 \times 9.81 \times \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.3)^2 \times h &= 208.05 \\
h &= \frac{208.05}{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.07069} \\
h &= \frac{208.05}{693.43} = 0.3 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Buoyant force $F_B = W = 208.05 \text{ N}$

**Step 5:** For stability, find $\overline{BM}$, $\overline{GB}$:
- Waterline is a circle of diameter $0.3 \text{ m}$, so $I = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{64} = \dfrac{\pi (0.3)^4}{64} = 3.976 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^4$
- Submerged volume $\nabla = 0.07069 \times 0.3 = 0.02121 \text{ m}^3$

$$
\overline{BM} = \frac{3.976 \times 10^{-4}}{0.02121} = 0.01875 \text{ m}
$$

- $\overline{GB} = 0.2 - \dfrac{0.3}{2} = 0.2 - 0.15 = 0.05 \text{ m}$

$$
\overline{GM} = 0.05 + 0.01875 = 0.0688 \text{ m} > 0
$$

**Conclusion:** The body is in **stable equilibrium** since $\overline{GM} > 0$. ✓

---

**(b)** [7 Marks] Derive the expression for the **period of oscillation of a floating body** and explain the role of metacentric height.

**Model Solution:**

When a floating body is slightly tilted and released, it oscillates about the metacenter $M$. The restoring couple is provided by the couple formed by $W$ at $G$ and $F_B$ at $B'$.

**Step 1:** Restoring moment when tilted by angle $\theta$:

$$M_{restoring} = W \cdot \overline{GM} \cdot \sin\theta \approx W \cdot \overline{GM} \cdot \theta$$

**Step 2:** Equation of motion (torsional pendulum analogy):

$$I_O \cdot \ddot{\theta} = -W \cdot \overline{GM} \cdot \theta$$

where $I_O$ is the mass moment of inertia about the axis of tilt $O$. By parallel axis theorem: $I_O = I_G + m \cdot \overline{OG}^2$.

For small angles, $O \approx M$, so $I_O = m(k^2 + \overline{GM}^2) \approx m k^2$ (since $\overline{GM}$ is small).

**Step 3:** This is a SHM equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ddot{\theta} &= -\frac{W \cdot \overline{GM}}{I_O} \cdot \theta = -\frac{m g \cdot \overline{GM}}{m k^2} \cdot \theta \\
\omega_n^2 &= \frac{g \cdot \overline{GM}}{k^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Period of oscillation:

$$\boxed{T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{k^2}{g \cdot \overline{GM}}}}$$

**Role of GM:** Higher $\overline{GM}$ → **shorter** rolling period (stiffer, snappier motion). Lower $\overline{GM}$ → **longer** period (gentler, sluggish roll). Naval architects tune GM to balance stability vs. crew comfort.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** [7 Marks] A body of specific gravity 0.85 floats in seawater (specific gravity 1.025). If the volume of the body is $2 \text{ m}^3$, find the volume submerged. What additional load can be placed on the body so that it just floats at the waterline?

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Weight of body:
$$W_b = \rho_b \cdot g \cdot V = 0.85 \times 1000 \times 9.81 \times 2 = 16677 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2:** Submerged volume $\nabla$ from equilibrium:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot \nabla &= W_b \\
1025 \times 9.81 \times \nabla &= 16677 \\
\nabla &= \frac{16677}{10055.25} = 1.658 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Additional load $W_{load}$ so body just submerges ($\nabla = V = 2 \text{ m}^3$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_b + W_{load} &= 1025 \times 9.81 \times 2 = 20110.5 \text{ N} \\
W_{load} &= 20110.5 - 16677 = 3433.5 \text{ N} \\
m_{load} &= \frac{3433.5}{9.81} = 350 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**(b)** [7 Marks] A rectangular pontoon $6 \text{ m} \times 4 \text{ m}$ floats at a draft of $1 \text{ m}$ in fresh water. Find the metacentric height. Take the weight of the pontoon as $200 \text{ kN}$ and the center of gravity at $0.6 \text{ m}$ above the base.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Displaced volume:
$$\nabla = 6 \times 4 \times 1 = 24 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2:** Second moment of area of waterline plane (rectangle):

$$I = \frac{L \cdot B^3}{12} = \frac{6 \times (4)^3}{12} = \frac{6 \times 64}{12} = 32 \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 3:** $\overline{BM}$:

$$\overline{BM} = \frac{I}{\nabla} = \frac{32}{24} = 1.333 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4:** $\overline{GB}$:
- B is at $h/2 = 0.5 \text{ m}$ above base.
- G is at $0.6 \text{ m}$ above base.
- $\overline{GB} = 0.5 - 0.6 = -0.1 \text{ m}$ (G is above B)

**Step 5:** Metacentric height:

$$\overline{GM} = -0.1 + 1.333 = 1.233 \text{ m}$$

**Conclusion:** Since $\overline{GM} = 1.233 \text{ m} > 0$, the pontoon is **stable**. ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to use the new B' position** when computing moments after tilting. Always use the **original vertical line through B** for $\overline{GB}$ and the **shifted B'** for the new buoyant force line.
> 2. **Unit confusion between N and kgf**: KTU strictly uses SI units. $1 \text{ kgf} = 9.81 \text{ N}$.
> 3. **Sign of $\overline{GB}$**: If G is **above** B, $\overline{GB}$ is **negative** (reduces GM). If G is below B, $\overline{GB}$ is positive.
> 4. **Forgetting to verify $\overline{GM} > 0$**: Computing $\overline{BM}$ alone is not enough — final stability conclusion is mandatory for full marks.
> 5. **Wrong $I$ for waterline plane**: Rectangle → $I = LB^3/12$, Circle → $I = \pi d^4/64$. Always choose the correct axis (about which tilting occurs).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Buoyancy** is the upward force exerted by a fluid equal to the weight of the displaced fluid.
- **Archimedes' Principle:** $F_B = \rho_f g \nabla$ — the most fundamental equation.
- **Floating Condition:** $\rho_b < \rho_f$ (body density less than fluid density).
- **Center of Buoyancy (B):** Centroid of displaced fluid; **shifts** during tilting.
- **Center of Gravity (G):** Fixed point; weight acts here.
- **Metacenter (M):** Intersection of new vertical through B' and original line GB.
- **Metacentric Height Formula:** $\overline{GM} = \overline{GB} + \dfrac{I}{\nabla}$
- **Stability Rules:**
  - $\overline{GM} > 0$ → **Stable**
  - $\overline{GM} < 0$ → **Unstable**
  - $\overline{GM} = 0$ → **Neutral**
- **Period of Oscillation:** $T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{k^2}{g \cdot \overline{GM}}}$
- **Submerged Volume Fraction:** $\dfrac{\nabla}{V} = \dfrac{\rho_b}{\rho_f}$
- **Always draw a labeled diagram** showing G, B, B', M, and the waterline plane in KTU ESE answers.
- **Remember units:** Force in N, density in kg/m³, length in m, time in s.
- **For small angles:** $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta \approx \theta$ (in radians).
- **Rectangle $I$:** $LB^3/12$ | **Circle $I$:** $\pi d^4/64$ | **Triangle $I$:** $bh^3/36$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equilibrium of Floating Bodies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT741 - FLUID MECHANICS & MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Equilibrium of Floating Bodies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **floating body** is defined as a body that is partially or completely immersed in a fluid and is sustained at the surface by the upward buoyant force exerted by the fluid. A floating body is said to be in **equilibrium** when the net force and net moment acting on it are zero. For a floating body in static equilibrium, two fundamental conditions must be simultaneously satisfied:

1. **Force Equilibrium:** The weight of the body ($W$) acting vertically downward through the centre of gravity ($G$) must be exactly equal in magnitude and opposite in direction to the **buoyant force** ($F_B$) acting vertically upward through the centre of buoyancy ($B$).

$$W = F_B = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot \nabla$$

where $\rho_f$ is the **density of the fluid**, $g$ is the **acceleration due to gravity** ($9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$), and $\nabla$ is the **volume of fluid displaced**.

2. **Moment Equilibrium:** The centre of gravity ($G$) and the centre of buoyancy ($B$) must lie on the same vertical line (the axis of symmetry of the body).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Highlight:** The *Archimedes' Principle* states that the buoyant force acting on a submerged or partially submerged body is equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by the body. The line of action of this buoyant force always passes through the **centroid of the displaced volume**, which is termed the **Centre of Buoyancy (B)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine placing a **cork** in a bucket of water. What happens?

- The cork bobs up, pokes partly out, and stays there. Why? Because the water *pushes it up* with a force equal to the weight of the water that the cork has shoved out of the way. That upward push is the buoyant force.
- Now, if you gently tilt the cork sideways, you will observe that it either:
  - **Tilts and then rocks back to upright** (this is **stable equilibrium** — like a cruise ship that recovers from waves).
  - **Tilts and flips over completely** (this is **unstable equilibrium** — like a pencil balanced on its tip).
  - **Tilts and stays at the new angle** (this is **neutral equilibrium** — like a uniform cylinder floating on its side).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Connection:** This principle is the foundation of naval architecture. Ship designers carefully calculate the **metacentric height (GM)** to ensure vessels recover from tilting caused by waves, cargo shifts, or passenger movement. A positive GM means the ship rolls back; a negative GM means it capsizes.

## 1.3 Three Types of Equilibrium

| Type | Metacentric Height | Behaviour | Real-World Example |
|------|-------------------|-----------|-------------------|
| **Stable** | $GM > 0$ (positive) | Restoring couple brings body back to original position | A properly designed ship |
| **Unstable** | $GM < 0$ (negative) | Couple overturns the body | A narrow, top-heavy buoy |
| **Neutral** | $GM = 0$ | Body remains at any displaced position | A perfectly homogeneous sphere floating fully submerged |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition of Metacentre (M):** When a floating body is slightly tilted, the centre of buoyancy shifts from $B$ to $B_1$. The vertical line passing through the new centre of buoyancy $B_1$ intersects the original vertical axis (through $B$ and $G$) at a point called the **metacentre (M)**. This point is crucial because it determines whether the resulting couple is restoring or overturning.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **Density of fresh water:** $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Density of sea water:** $\rho_{sw} \approx 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Specific weight of water:** $\gamma_w = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of Equilibrium

Let us dissect *why* a floating body behaves the way it does when tilted by a small angle $\theta$:

**Step 1 — Initial Resting Position**
- The body floats with weight $W$ acting downward through $G$ and buoyant force $F_B$ acting upward through $B$.
- $G$ and $B$ lie on the same vertical line. There is **zero moment** — the body is in equilibrium.

**Step 2 — Application of External Disturbance**
- An external force (a wave, a passenger moving, a wind gust) tilts the body by a small angle $\theta$.
- The body rotates as a **rigid body** about some instantaneous axis.
- The volume submerged changes shape: a wedge of volume $V_1$ emerges on one side while a wedge $V_2$ submerges on the other side.

**Step 3 — Shift of Centre of Buoyancy**
- The new centre of buoyancy $B_1$ is no longer on the original vertical line.
- $B_1$ shifts to the side where more volume is submerged (i.e., the side from which the body has rotated *towards*).

**Step 4 — Formation of the Metacentre**
- The vertical line through the *new* $B_1$ intersects the original vertical axis (line $GB$) at a point called the **metacentre (M)**.
- For small angles of tilt ($\theta < 15°$), the position of $M$ is **independent of $\theta$** and depends only on the geometry of the waterline plane.

**Step 5 — Classification of Equilibrium**
- If $M$ lies **above** $G$: The moment of the couple $(W, F_B)$ is *restoring* → **Stable equilibrium**.
- If $M$ lies **below** $G$: The moment is *overturning* → **Unstable equilibrium**.
- If $M$ coincides **with** $G$: No moment is produced → **Neutral equilibrium**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Formula | Description | Units |
|---------------|---------|-------------|-------|
| Buoyant Force ($F_B$) | $F_B = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot \nabla$ | Weight of displaced fluid | N (Newton) |
| Weight of Floating Body | $W = \rho_b \cdot g \cdot V$ | Total body weight | N |
| Buoyancy Equality | $\rho_f \cdot \nabla = \rho_b \cdot V$ | Floating condition | kg |
| Submerged Depth Fraction | $h/H = \rho_b / \rho_f$ | Depth ratio for floating | dimensionless |
| Metacentric Radius ($BM$) | $BM = I / \nabla$ | $I$ = second moment of waterline area | m |
| Distance $BG$ | $BG = (\rho_b / \rho_f) \cdot (V \cdot y_b) - y_b$ | Vertical distance from $B$ to $G$ | m |
| Metacentric Height ($GM$) | $GM = BM - BG$ | For floating body (note: **minus** sign) | m |
| Metacentric Height (Submerged) | $GM = BM - BG$ | For submerged body — same form | m |
| Righting Couple | $C = W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta$ | Restoring moment for small $\theta$ | N·m |
| Time of Oscillation | $T = 2\pi \cdot \sqrt{k^2 / (g \cdot GM)}$ | Rolling period of a floating body | s |
| Radius of Gyration | $k$ | $I_{\text{body}} = M \cdot k^2$ | m |
| Second Moment (Rectangle) | $I = LB^3 / 12$ | $L$ = length, $B$ = breadth of waterline | m$^4$ |
| Second Moment (Circle) | $I = \pi D^4 / 64$ | $D$ = diameter of circular waterline | m$^4$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Sign Convention:** The formula $GM = BM - BG$ is the *only* sign convention used for **floating bodies** in KTU exams. Do NOT use $GM = BM + BG$; this is a common mistake that costs marks. The **minus** arises because $G$ is generally *above* $B$ for floating bodies, so we subtract to find the true height of $M$ above $G$.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The equilibrium of floating bodies is the cornerstone of:

- **Naval Architecture:** Stability analysis of ships, submarines, and offshore platforms.
- **Aeronautical Engineering:** Stability of lighter-than-air craft like airships and hot-air balloons.
- **Civil Engineering:** Design of pontoon bridges, floating docks, caissons, and submerged tunnels.
- **Hydraulic Engineering:** Stability of floating weirs, barrage gates, and floating solar platforms.
- **Storage Tank Design:** Floating roof tanks in oil refineries use this principle to maintain a seal against evaporative losses.

> [!NOTE]
> **Connection to Other Modules:** This topic directly feeds into Module 2 (kinematics of fluid flow) by establishing how submerged bodies respond to fluid forces, and into Module 4 (hydraulic turbines) where the buoyancy and stability of turbine runner blades submerged in water become important design considerations.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Metacentric Radius ($BM$)

We will now derive the most important relationship in floating body stability — the formula for $BM$.

### Setup
Consider a floating body (such as a ship) tilted by a small angle $\theta$ about a longitudinal axis. Let the original waterline plane be $W_1 L_1$ and the new waterline plane (after tilt) be $W_2 L_2$. The two waterlines intersect along an axis passing through the centre of flotation $F$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identification of Wedge Volumes**

When the body tilts, a wedge of volume $\delta V_1$ emerges on one side (say, the right side), and an equal wedge of volume $\delta V_2$ submerges on the other side (the left side). For a small angle of tilt, these two volumes are equal:

$$\delta V_1 = \delta V_2 = \delta V$$

This equality holds because the *total displaced volume* $\nabla$ must remain constant (since $W = F_B$ and $W$ is constant).

**Step 2 — Shift of Centre of Buoyancy**

The shift of the centre of buoyancy from $B$ to $B_1$ can be analysed by treating the change in displaced volume as the transfer of the wedge $\delta V$ from the right side to the left side. The horizontal distance moved by the centre of buoyancy is found by applying the **principle of moments**.

Let:
- $\bar{x}$ = horizontal distance from the centre of flotation $F$ to the centroid of the emerged wedge
- $\bar{x}_1$ = horizontal distance from $F$ to the centroid of the submerged wedge

By the principle of moments (taking moments about the original centre of buoyancy $B$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{BB_1} \cdot \nabla &= \delta V \cdot (\bar{x} + \bar{x}_1) \\
\overline{BB_1} &= \frac{\delta V \cdot (\bar{x} + \bar{x}_1)}{\nabla}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Relating Wedge Geometry to Angle $\theta$**

For a small tilt angle $\theta$, consider a small elemental strip of length $dx$ at a distance $x$ from the centre of flotation $F$. The vertical rise (or drop) of this strip is $x \tan\theta \approx x\theta$ for small angles. The area of this strip is $dA = b \cdot dx$, where $b$ is the breadth of the strip. The volume of the wedge element is:

$$d(\delta V) = x \theta \cdot b \cdot dx = \theta \cdot x \cdot dA$$

The moment of this elemental volume about $F$ is:

$$dM = x \cdot d(\delta V) = x \cdot (\theta \cdot x \cdot dA) = \theta \cdot x^2 \cdot dA$$

Integrating over the entire waterline area $A$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int dM &= \theta \int x^2 \, dA \\
\delta V \cdot \bar{x} &= \theta \cdot I
\end{aligned}
$$

where $I = \int x^2 \, dA$ is the **second moment of area** of the waterline plane about the axis of rotation (the longitudinal axis through $F$).

**Step 4 — Deriving the Metacentric Radius**

For a small angle $\theta$, from the geometry of the metacentre:

$$
\begin{aligned}
BM &= \frac{BB_1}{\tan\theta} \approx \frac{BB_1}{\theta} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

From Step 2, $BB_1 = \dfrac{\delta V \cdot (\bar{x} + \bar{x}_1)}{\nabla}$. Since for small angles the centroids of the emerged and submerged wedges are nearly equidistant from $F$, we can write $\bar{x} = \bar{x}_1 = x$ approximately. Using the result from Step 3, $\delta V \cdot \bar{x} = \theta \cdot I$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
BB_1 &= \frac{2 \cdot \delta V \cdot \bar{x}}{\nabla} = \frac{2 \cdot \theta \cdot I}{\nabla} \\
BM &= \frac{BB_1}{\theta} = \frac{2 \cdot I}{\nabla}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The factor of 2:** Some textbooks (and the KTU reference by Modi & Seth) write $BM = I / \nabla$ because they define $I$ as the *second moment of the waterline area about the centreline* (i.e., considering both sides of the body together). When $I$ is computed for the full waterline area, the factor of 2 is absorbed. For KTU 2024, the standard formula to use is:

$$\boxed{BM = \frac{I}{\nabla}}$$

where $I$ is the second moment of the **complete waterline area** about the longitudinal axis of rotation.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Metacentric Height ($GM$)

**Starting Point:** The metacentric height is defined as the vertical distance from the centre of gravity $G$ to the metacentre $M$. From the geometry of the triangle formed by $G$, $B$, and $M$:

$$GM = BM - BG$$

This is the **final working formula** for a floating body. There is no separate derivation — the formula is a direct consequence of the geometric definition. However, we must establish how to compute $BG$.

**Computing $BG$:** Let the body have total volume $V$ and the submerged volume be $\nabla$. The body is composed of the submerged portion and the portion above water. The centre of gravity $G$ of the entire body can be located using the principle of moments:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W \cdot \overline{BG} &= W_{\text{sub}} \cdot 0 + W_{\text{above}} \cdot h_G \\
(\rho_b V g) \cdot BG &= (\rho_f \nabla g) \cdot 0 + \rho_b (V - \nabla) g \cdot h_G
\end{aligned}
$$

where $h_G$ is the vertical distance from the waterline to the centre of gravity of the *emerged* portion. For most KTU problems, $BG$ is given directly or computed from the geometry.

## 3.3 Righting Couple Derivation

When a floating body is tilted by angle $\theta$, the weight $W$ acts downward through $G$ and the buoyant force $F_B$ acts upward through $B_1$. These two equal, opposite, and parallel forces form a **couple**. The moment arm of this couple is the horizontal distance between the lines of action of $W$ and $F_B$.

From the geometry of the metacentre, the horizontal distance between $G$ and $B_1$ projected perpendicular to the original vertical is:

$$\text{Moment arm} = GM \cdot \sin\theta$$

Therefore, the **restoring couple** is:

$$C_{\text{restoring}} = W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta$$

For small angles, $\sin\theta \approx \theta$:

$$C_{\text{restoring}} \approx W \cdot GM \cdot \theta$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability Criterion:** Stability of the floating body requires that the couple be *restoring*, which means $GM > 0$. If $GM < 0$, the couple becomes *overturning* and the body capsizes.

## 3.4 Period of Oscillation (Time of Roll)

For a floating body undergoing small oscillations under gravity, the equation of motion is analogous to a simple pendulum. The time period is:

$$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{k^2}{g \cdot GM}}$$

where $k$ is the **radius of gyration** of the body about the axis of rotation, defined by $I_{\text{body}} = M \cdot k^2$.

**Derivation outline:** The restoring moment $W \cdot GM \cdot \theta$ provides the angular acceleration:

$$I_{\text{body}} \cdot \ddot{\theta} = -W \cdot GM \cdot \theta$$

$$M \cdot k^2 \cdot \ddot{\theta} = -M \cdot g \cdot GM \cdot \theta$$

$$\ddot{\theta} + \frac{g \cdot GM}{k^2} \cdot \theta = 0$$

This is the SHM equation with $\omega^2 = g \cdot GM / k^2$, giving $T = 2\pi / \omega = 2\pi \sqrt{k^2 / (g \cdot GM)}$.

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Problem:** A wooden block of dimensions $2 \text{ m} \times 1 \text{ m} \times 0.8 \text{ m}$ and density $650 \text{ kg/m}^3$ floats in water. Find:
(a) The depth of immersion
(b) The metacentric height if the block floats with the $2 \text{ m} \times 1 \text{ m}$ face as the base.

**Solution:**

**(a) Depth of Immersion:**

Weight of block = Weight of water displaced

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_b \cdot V \cdot g &= \rho_w \cdot \nabla \cdot g \\
650 \times (2 \times 1 \times 0.8) &= 1000 \times (2 \times 1 \times h) \\
650 \times 1.6 &= 1000 \times 2 \times h \\
1040 &= 2000 \cdot h \\
h &= 0.52 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Metacentric Height:**

Submerged volume $\nabla = 2 \times 1 \times 0.52 = 1.04 \text{ m}^3$.

Second moment of waterline area (rectangle $2 \text{ m} \times 1 \text{ m}$, with the $1 \text{ m}$ side parallel to the axis of tilt):

For tilting about the longitudinal axis (length $L = 2 \text{ m}$), $I$ is computed about the axis parallel to the $1 \text{ m}$ breadth:

$$I = \frac{L \cdot B^3}{12} = \frac{2 \times 1^3}{12} = 0.1667 \text{ m}^4$$

Metacentric radius:

$$BM = \frac{I}{\nabla} = \frac{0.1667}{1.04} = 0.1603 \text{ m}$$

Distance $BG$: The centre of buoyancy $B$ is at $h/2 = 0.26 \text{ m}$ from the bottom. The centre of gravity $G$ is at $H/2 = 0.8/2 = 0.4 \text{ m}$ from the bottom.

$$BG = 0.4 - 0.26 = 0.14 \text{ m}$$

Metacentric height:

$$GM = BM - BG = 0.1603 - 0.14 = 0.0203 \text{ m} \approx 0.02 \text{ m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Interpretation:** $GM = +0.02 \text{ m} > 0$, so the block is in **stable equilibrium**, but with a very small positive metacentric height. This means the stability is marginal — the block will recover from small tilts but may overturn under larger disturbances.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Equilibrium Classification Logic

The following flowchart captures the decision logic used by naval architects to classify the equilibrium of a floating body:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Floating Body at Rest] --> B[Apply Small Tilt by Angle theta]
    B --> C[Body Rotates about Centre of Flotation F]
    C --> D[Centre of Buoyancy Shifts from B to B1]
    D --> E[New Vertical through B1 Intersects Original Axis at Metacentre M]
    E --> F{Compare Positions of M and G}
    F -->|M above G| G[GM greater than zero]
    F -->|M coincides with G| H[GM equals zero]
    F -->|M below G| I[GM less than zero]
    G --> J[Restoring Couple Acts]
    J --> K[Body Returns to Original Position]
    K --> L[STABLE EQUILIBRIUM]
    H --> N[No Couple Acts]
    N --> O[Body Stays at New Tilt]
    O --> P[NEUTRAL EQUILIBRIUM]
    I --> Q[Overturning Couple Acts]
    Q --> R[Body Capsizes]
    R --> S[UNSTABLE EQUILIBRIUM]
```

## 4.2 Free-Body Diagram of a Tilted Floating Body (Block Schematic)

Since physical drawings are difficult to render in Mermaid, the following block diagram maps the **force system and geometric relationships** that govern the stability analysis:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Vertical_Axis [Original Vertical Axis through G and B]
        GA[Point G - Centre of Gravity] --> DA[Point B - Original Centre of Buoyancy]
        DA --> EA[Point M - Metacentre]
    end

    subgraph Tilted_System [System After Tilt by theta]
        GB[Weight W acts downward through G] --> HB[Buoyant Force FB acts upward through B1]
        HB --> IB[Restoring Couple C equals W times GM times sin theta]
    end

    subgraph Key_Distances [Key Geometric Distances]
        JB[BM equals I divided by V_sub] --> KB[BG equals distance from B to G]
        KB --> LB[GM equals BM minus BG]
    end

    Vertical_Axis --> Tilted_System
    Tilted_System --> Key_Distances
```

## 4.3 Three-Case Stability Matrix

The following diagram visualises the three distinct cases encountered in KTU 2024 exam problems:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CASE_A [Case 1: Stable Equilibrium]
        A1[G at bottom] --> A2[B above G]
        A2 --> A3[M above B]
        A3 --> A4[GM positive]
    end

    subgraph CASE_B [Case 2: Neutral Equilibrium]
        B1[B at any position] --> B2[M coincides with G]
        B2 --> B3[GM equals zero]
    end

    subgraph CASE_C [Case 3: Unstable Equilibrium]
        C1[B above G] --> C2[M below G]
        C2 --> C3[GM negative]
    end
```

## 4.4 Key Geometric Labels Reference

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical KTU Notation |
|--------|---------|---------------------|
| G | Centre of Gravity of the body | Subscript or plain |
| B | Centre of Buoyancy (original) | Centroid of displaced volume |
| B$_1$ | Centre of Buoyancy (after tilt) | New centroid |
| M | Metacentre | Intersection point |
| F | Centre of Flotation | Centroid of waterline area |
| $\theta$ | Angle of tilt | Small angle assumption ($\leq 15°$) |
| $\nabla$ | Submerged volume | V in some textbooks |
| I | Second moment of waterline area | About axis of rotation |
