# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q:** Define the terms *Centre of Buoyancy* and *Metacentre*. State the condition for stable equilibrium of a floating body.

**Model Answer:**

The **Centre of Buoyancy (B)** is defined as the point through which the buoyant force (upward thrust of the fluid) is supposed to act. It is the **centroid of the volume of fluid displaced** by the body.

The **Metacentre (M)** is defined as the point at which the line of action of the buoyant force intersects the original vertical line (passing through the centre of gravity) when the floating body is given a small angular displacement.

**Condition for stable equilibrium:** The metacentre $M$ must lie **above** the centre of gravity $G$ of the body, i.e., the metacentric height must be positive:

$$GM > 0 \quad \text{(Stable equilibrium)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuator's Note:** A complete answer must mention *both* the geometric definition of $M$ *and* the algebraic condition $GM > 0$. Writing only the geometric definition without stating the condition forfeits 1 mark.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**Q:** Distinguish between a *floating body* and a *submerged body*. How does the formula for metacentric height differ in the two cases?

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Floating Body | Submerged Body |
|---------|---------------|----------------|
| Position | Floats at the free surface | Fully immersed in fluid |
| Volume relation | $V_{\text{sub}} < V_{\text{total}}$ | $V_{\text{sub}} = V_{\text{total}}$ |
| Buoyancy vs Weight | Equal ($W = F_B$) | Equal only if neutrally buoyant |

**Formula for Metacentric Height:**

- **Floating body:** $GM = BM - BG$
- **Submerged body:** $GM = BM + BG$ (here $G$ is *below* $B$, so the sign changes)

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction:** The sign convention flips because in a floating body, the centre of gravity is typically above the centre of buoyancy, while in a fully submerged body, the relationship depends on the densities. Always draw the free-body diagram first to determine the correct sign.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B questions in the End Semester Examination (ESE) carry 14 marks and offer an internal choice. Sub-parts (a) and (b) typically carry 7 marks each. The choice is provided **between two full questions** (not between sub-parts), so we present two fully-independent alternatives below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / Model Paper 2024]**

**Q:** A ship of mass $50{,}000 \text{ kg}$ and width $8 \text{ m}$ has its centre of gravity at $4 \text{ m}$ above the bottom of the ship. When the ship is floating in sea water (density $1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$), the metacentric height is observed to be $0.6 \text{ m}$. Determine:

**(a)** The metacentric radius $BM$ and the moment of inertia of the waterline area. (7 Marks)

**(b)** The depth of immersion of the ship in sea water. State whether the equilibrium is stable or not. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution

**Given Data:**
- Mass of ship, $M = 50{,}000 \text{ kg}$
- Width of ship, $B = 8 \text{ m}$
- Height of $G$ above bottom, $y_G = 4 \text{ m}$
- Density of sea water, $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Metacentric height, $GM = 0.6 \text{ m}$

#### Part (a) — Metacentric Radius and Moment of Inertia

**Step 1:** Find the location of the centre of buoyancy $B$.

For a ship approximated as a rectangular block, the centre of buoyancy lies at the **midpoint of the submerged depth**:

$$y_B = \frac{h}{2}$$

where $h$ is the depth of immersion.

**Step 2:** Express $BG$ in terms of $h$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
BG &= y_G - y_B \\
BG &= 4 - \frac{h}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the metacentric height formula.

$$GM = BM - BG$$

$$0.6 = BM - \left(4 - \frac{h}{2}\right)$$

$$BM = 4.6 - \frac{h}{2} \quad \cdots \text{(Equation 1)}$$

**Step 4:** Express the metacentric radius $BM$ in terms of moment of inertia.

$$BM = \frac{I}{\nabla}$$

For a rectangular waterline area (length $L$, width $B$):

$$I = \frac{L \cdot B^3}{12}$$

**Step 5:** Compute the depth of immersion from buoyancy equation.

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= F_B \\
M \cdot g &= \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot L \cdot B \cdot h \\
50{,}000 &= 1025 \times L \times 8 \times h \\
L \cdot h &= \frac{50{,}000}{1025 \times 8} = 6.0976 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Assuming $L = 20 \text{ m}$ (a typical ship length, which is needed since the problem gives only width):

$$h = \frac{6.0976}{20} = 0.3049 \text{ m} \approx 0.305 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6:** Compute $BM$ and $I$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
BM &= 4.6 - \frac{0.305}{2} = 4.6 - 0.1525 = 4.4475 \text{ m} \\
I &= BM \cdot \nabla = 4.4475 \times (20 \times 8 \times 0.305) \\
I &= 4.4475 \times 48.8 = 217.04 \text{ m}^4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answers for Part (a):**
- $BM \approx 4.45 \text{ m}$
- $I \approx 217.04 \text{ m}^4$

#### Part (b) — Depth of Immersion and Stability

**Depth of Immersion:** $h = 0.305 \text{ m}$ (computed in Step 5 above)

**Stability Check:**

Since $GM = +0.6 \text{ m} > 0$, the metacentre lies above the centre of gravity. Therefore, the equilibrium is **STABLE**.

The righting couple when tilted by angle $\theta$ is:

$$C = W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta = (50{,}000 \times 9.81) \times 0.6 \times \sin\theta$$

$$C = 294{,}300 \cdot \sin\theta \text{ N·m}$$

For $\theta = 5°$: $C = 294{,}300 \times 0.0872 = 25{,}662 \text{ N·m}$

**Answer for Part (b):** Depth of immersion is $0.305 \text{ m}$; the equilibrium is **stable**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistakes in This Type of Question:**
> 1. **Forgetting to use sea water density:** Students often use $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ for sea water. This yields a wrong answer that loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Sign error in $GM$ formula:** For floating bodies, it is **minus** ($GM = BM - BG$). Using $+$ gives a negative $GM$ and incorrect stability classification.
> 3. **Not stating the stability conclusion explicitly:** The question asks "stable or not" — the answer must clearly state "STABLE" with justification ($GM > 0$).
> 4. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing $h$ in cm and $I$ in m$^4$ causes dimensional errors. Always work in SI units throughout.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Model Paper 2024]**

**Q:** A rectangular pontoon $6 \text{ m}$ long, $3 \text{ m}$ wide and $1.2 \text{ m}$ deep weighs $45{,}000 \text{ N}$. It floats in water. Determine:

**(a)** The depth of immersion when the pontoon carries a uniformly distributed load of $30{,}000 \text{ N}$. (7 Marks)

**(b)** The metacentric height and state of equilibrium. Take the centre of gravity of the pontoon plus load as $0.5 \text{ m}$ above the bottom. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution

**Given Data:**
- Pontoon dimensions: $L = 6 \text{ m}$, $B = 3 \text{ m}$, $D = 1.2 \text{ m}$ (depth)
- Self-weight: $W_{\text{self}} = 45{,}000 \text{ N}$
- Load: $W_{\text{load}} = 30{,}000 \text{ N}$
- Density of water: $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\gamma_w = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$
- Height of $G$ above bottom: $y_G = 0.5 \text{ m}$

#### Part (a) — Depth of Immersion Under Load

**Step 1:** Total weight on the pontoon.

$$W = W_{\text{self}} + W_{\text{load}} = 45{,}000 + 30{,}000 = 75{,}000 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2:** Equate weight to buoyant force.

$$W = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla = \gamma_w \cdot L \cdot B \cdot h$$

$$75{,}000 = 9810 \times 6 \times 3 \times h$$

$$75{,}000 = 176{,}580 \cdot h$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
h &= \frac{75{,}000}{176{,}580} \\
h &= 0.4247 \text{ m} \approx 0.425 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** $h = 0.425 \text{ m} < D = 1.2 \text{ m}$, so the pontoon does not sink. ✓

**Answer for Part (a):** Depth of immersion $h = 0.425 \text{ m}$

#### Part (b) — Metacentric Height and State of Equilibrium

**Step 1:** Submerged volume.

$$\nabla = L \times B \times h = 6 \times 3 \times 0.425 = 7.65 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2:** Position of centre of buoyancy $B$ above the bottom.

$$y_B = \frac{h}{2} = \frac{0.425}{2} = 0.2125 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3:** Distance $BG$.

$$BG = y_G - y_B = 0.5 - 0.2125 = 0.2875 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4:** Second moment of waterline area.

For a rectangle with the $6 \text{ m}$ length as the axis of tilt (rolling side to side), the second moment about the longitudinal axis is:

$$I = \frac{L \cdot B^3}{12} = \frac{6 \times 3^3}{12} = \frac{6 \times 27}{12} = 13.5 \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 5:** Metacentric radius.

$$BM = \frac{I}{\nabla} = \frac{13.5}{7.65} = 1.7647 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6:** Metacentric height.

$$GM = BM - BG = 1.7647 - 0.2875 = 1.4772 \text{ m}$$

**Step 7:** Stability assessment.

Since $GM = +1.477 \text{ m} > 0$, the equilibrium is **STABLE**.

The large positive value indicates **good stability** — the pontoon will return strongly to its upright position when tilted. This makes pontoons ideal for floating bridges and docks.

**Answer for Part (b):** $GM = 1.477 \text{ m}$; equilibrium is **stable** (strongly stable).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistakes in This Type of Question:**
> 1. **Confusing $L$ and $B$ in the second moment formula:** Always use $I = LB^3/12$ where $B$ is the dimension *perpendicular* to the axis of tilt. Tilting about the length axis uses breadth as the perpendicular dimension.
> 2. **Forgetting to add the load weight:** Students often compute immersion based on self-weight only, missing the $30{,}000 \text{ N}$ load. This is the most common error in load-based problems.
> 3. **Computing $BG$ with wrong sign:** Always draw the FBD: $B$ is at $h/2$ (lower), $G$ is at $0.5 \text{ m}$ (higher). $BG = y_G - y_B$, not the reverse.
> 4. **Not verifying the floating condition:** If $h$ comes out greater than $D$, the body has sunk — this is a logical inconsistency the student should flag.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Equilibrium of Floating Bodies**

**Core Definitions:**
- ☐ **Buoyant Force** = weight of fluid displaced (Archimedes' Principle)
- ☐ **Centre of Buoyancy (B)** = centroid of displaced volume
- ☐ **Metacentre (M)** = intersection point of new and old vertical lines through buoyancy centres
- ☐ **Centre of Flotation (F)** = centroid of waterline plane

**Critical Formulas:**
- ☐ Buoyancy: $F_B = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot \nabla$
- ☐ Metacentric radius: $BM = I / \nabla$
- ☐ Metacentric height (floating): $GM = BM - BG$
- ☐ Metacentric height (submerged): $GM = BM + BG$ (sign reversal)
- ☐ Restoring couple: $C = W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta$
- ☐ Period of oscillation: $T = 2\pi\sqrt{k^2 / (g \cdot GM)}$

**Stability Criterion:**
- ☐ $GM > 0$ → **Stable** (M above G)
- ☐ $GM = 0$ → **Neutral** (M coincides with G)
- ☐ $GM < 0$ → **Unstable** (M below G)

**Standard Second Moments of Area:**
- ☐ Rectangle: $I = LB^3/12$ (B perpendicular to tilt axis)
- ☐ Circle: $I = \pi D^4/64$
- ☐ Triangle: $I = L H^3/36$ (H perpendicular to base)

**Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
- ☐ Never use $\rho = 1000$ for sea water — use $1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- ☐ Always check whether the body floats ($h < D$) or sinks
- ☐ Use the **minus** sign for floating bodies ($GM = BM - BG$)
- ☐ Convert all units to SI before calculation
- ☐ Draw the FBD first to determine the relative position of G and B
- ☐ The metacentric height is independent of tilt angle for small $\theta \leq 15°$

**Engineering Applications:**
- ☐ Ship and submarine design (naval architecture)
- ☐ Floating bridges, pontoons, docks
- ☐ Submerged storage tanks
- ☐ Offshore platform stability
- ☐ Hot-air balloon and airship design
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Metacentre and Metacentric Height
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT741 - FLUID MECHANICS & MACHINERY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Metacentre and Metacentric Height # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Foundational Concepts of a Floating Body Before defining the metacentre, we must establish three crucial geometric points that govern the rotational behaviour of any floating body. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic is a high-weightage concept under the "Buoyancy and Floatation" unit. It is a direct application of Archimedes' Principle in conjunction with the principles of static equilibrium. ### A. Center of Gravity (G) The **Center of Gravity (G)** is the point through which the entire weight of the floating body is considered to act vertically downward due to gravity. It is purely a mass property of the body and is **fixed relative to the body**. ### B. Center of Buoyancy (B) The **Center of Buoyancy (B)** is the centroid of the volume of the fluid displaced by the submerged portion of the floating body. According to **Archimedes' Principle**, the upward buoyant force acts through this centroid. > [!NOTE] > Unlike the Center of Gravity, the position of B **shifts** as the body tilts, because the submerged wedge shape changes its centroidal location. ### C. Metacentre (M) The **Metacentre (M)** is the point about which a floating body (when slightly tilted) appears to oscillate. It is the theoretical intersection point of: - The line of action of the **buoyant force** (vertical line passing through the new center of buoyancy B'), AND - The **vertical centerline** of the undisturbed floating body (the original axis of symmetry). ## 1.2 The Metacentric Height (GM) The **Metacentric Height (GM)** is defined as the vertical distance between the **Center of Gravity (G)** of the floating body and the **Metacentre (M)**. $$GM = \text{Distance between } G \text{ and } M$$ ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Iceberg Tip" Intuition Imagine a wooden toy boat floating in a bathtub. If you gently press one corner with your finger, the boat tilts. The moment you release it, it **rocks back to its upright position** (stable), or it **capsizes** (unstable), or it simply **stays at the new tilt** (neutral). > [!TIP] > **Intuitive Summary:** The metacentre is essentially a "balance pivot point" in the floating body. Think of it as a fulcrum. The relative position of your body's center of mass (G) above or below this fulcrum determines whether you fall, balance, or wobble — exactly like balancing a pencil on your finger. The "GM" is the **sensitivity knob** that tells engineers how stable a ship will be at sea. ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Acceleration due to gravity:** g = **9.81 m/s²** (standard for KTU numerical problems) - **Density of fresh water:** ρ = **1000 kg/m³** - **Density of sea water:** ρ = **1025 kg/m³** - **Submerged Volume:** V (m³) - **Waterline Area Moment of Inertia:** I (m⁴) # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Condition of Equilibrium for Floating Bodies A floating body can be in one of **three states of equilibrium**: > [!NOTE] > **Stable Equilibrium:** The body, when slightly tilted, returns to its original position. The metacentre M lies **above** G (i.e., GM is positive). > [!NOTE] > **Unstable Equilibrium:** The body, when slightly tilted, capsizes (moves further away). The metacentre M lies **below** G (i.e., GM is negative). > [!NOTE] > **Neutral Equilibrium:** The body, when tilted, remains in the new position. The metacentre M **coincides with** G (i.e., GM = 0). ## 2.2 Governing Conditions Table | Stability Condition | Position of M | Sign of GM | Physical Interpretation | |---|---|---|---| | **Stable** | M is above G | GM > 0 | Restoring couple acts; body returns to upright | | **Unstable** | M is below G | GM < 0 | Overturning couple acts; body capsizes | | **Neutral** | M coincides with G | GM = 0 | No couple; body remains at new tilt | ## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of GM Calculation When a floating body tilts through a small angle θ: **Step 1:** The submerged volume wedge on one side increases by ΔV; on the other side, it decreases by ΔV. **Step 2:** This volume change is mathematically expressed as: $$\Delta V = \frac{1}{2} \cdot A_w \cdot \bar{x}$$ Where: - $A_w$ = Area of the waterplane (waterline cross-section) - $\bar{x}$ = Horizontal distance of the centroid of the wedge from the axis of rotation **Step 3:** The new center of buoyancy shifts horizontally due to the additional buoyancy wedge. The vertical line through this new B' is the line of action of the new buoyant force. **Step 4:** The point where this new buoyant force line intersects the original vertical centerline of the body is the **Metacentre (M)**. **Step 5:** The distance BM depends purely on geometry: the waterline area moment of inertia and the displaced volume. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $BM = \dfrac{I}{V}$ | Distance B to M | metres (m) | | 2 | $GM = BM - BG$ | Metacentric Height (when M is above G) | metres (m) | | 3 | $GM = BM + GB$ | Alternate form when G is below B | metres (m) | | 4 | $I = \dfrac{L \cdot B^3}{12}$ | Rectangular waterline moment of inertia | m⁴ | | 5 | $I = \dfrac{\pi D^4}{64}$ | Circular waterline moment of inertia | m⁴ | | 6 | $\Delta V = \dfrac{1}{2} A_w \bar{x}$ | Wedge volume (submerged/un-submerged) | m³ | | 7 | $W \cdot \overline{GG_1} = \rho g V \cdot \overline{BB_1}$ | Moment balance equation | N·m | | 8 | $T_{\theta} = W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta$ | Restoring couple at tilt angle θ | N·m | | 9 | $T_R = W \cdot GM$ | Righting couple (at small angles, sin θ ≈ θ) | N·m | | 10 | $OB = \dfrac{V}{A_w} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\cos\theta}$ | New center of buoyancy location | m | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Variable Definitions:** > - **I** = Second moment of area of the waterplane about the longitudinal axis of tilting > - **V** = Volume of the submerged (displaced) portion > - **W** = Weight of the floating body = ρgV > - **BG** = Distance between B and G > - **OB** = Depth of center of buoyancy below the waterline ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility In marine and naval architecture, the metacentric height **GM** is a critical design parameter: - **Passenger Ships:** Require high GM values for **passenger comfort** (reduced rolling period). - **Cargo Ships:** Require moderate GM — too high makes the ride harsh; too low risks capsizing. - **Submarines:** GM is carefully controlled during submergence using **ballast tanks**. - **Floating Docks & Offshore Platforms:** Stability analysis is governed entirely by GM calculations. - **River Boats and Barges:** Flat-bottomed designs yield low I values, leading to lower stability margins. > [!TIP] > **KTU Mnemonic:** *"M is the meeting point of the new buoyant line and the old centerline."* Remember this for theory questions. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: The BM Formula (BM = I / V) **Statement to Prove:** The distance between the center of buoyancy B and the metacentre M is given by BM = I/V, where I is the moment of inertia of the waterplane area and V is the displaced volume. ### Setup and Assumptions Consider a floating body (e.g., a ship) tilted through a very small angle θ about its longitudinal axis. Let: - W = Original waterplane area - W₁W₂ = New waterplane area after tilting - O = Center of rotation (the intersection of the original and new waterplane planes) > [!NOTE] > For small angles of tilt, the wedge that is newly submerged on one side is equal in volume to the wedge that emerges on the other side. The body remains at the same draft. ### Step-by-Step Mathematical Development **Step 1: Identify the wedge geometry** The triangular wedge OWW₂ is submerged (added buoyancy), and the wedge OWW₁ emerges (lost buoyancy). Each wedge has a horizontal length (along the waterplane) and a vertical height. **Step 2: Express the wedge volume** For an elementary strip of width dA at horizontal distance x from the axis of tilt O, the vertical rise (or dip) due to rotation is $x \tan\theta$. The volume of this elementary strip is: $$d(\Delta V) = x \tan\theta \cdot dA$$ **Step 3: Integrate across the waterplane area** The total volume of the wedge (newly submerged) is: $$\Delta V = \int_A x \tan\theta \cdot dA = \tan\theta \int_A x \cdot dA$$ But $\int_A x \cdot dA$ is the **first moment of area** of the wedge. For a triangle with base b and centroid at distance 2b/3 from the apex O, this integral equals: $$\int_A x \cdot dA = A_w \cdot \bar{x}$$ where $\bar{x}$ is the horizontal distance of the centroid of the wedge from the axis of tilt. **Step 4: Locate the centroid of the wedge** For a triangle, the centroid lies at 2/3 of the base from the apex. So if the half-beam is b: $$\bar{x} = \frac{2b}{3}$$ **Step 5: Use the second moment of area definition** By the definition of the second moment of area about the axis of rotation (for the full waterplane area): $$I = \int_A x^2 \cdot dA$$ We can relate this to the wedge geometry as: $$I = 2 \cdot \int_{0}^{b} x^2 \cdot y \, dx = 2 \cdot \frac{b^3}{3} \cdot \frac{b}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot (\text{geometry factor})$$ For the standard derivation, the key relationship that emerges is: $$I = A_w \cdot \bar{x} \cdot b = A_w \cdot \frac{2b}{3} \cdot b = \frac{2A_w \cdot b^2}{3}$$ But more generally, the moment about the axis of rotation caused by the new buoyancy wedge is: $$F_{\text{buoy}} \cdot \overline{BB_1} = (\rho g \Delta V) \cdot \bar{x}$$ **Step 6: Apply the moment balance** The shift in buoyancy $\overline{BB_1}$ must equal $\overline{GG_1} = \overline{MM_1}$ for a small angle (where M moves horizontally with G). Setting up the moment equation: $$\rho g V \cdot \overline{BB_1} = W \cdot \overline{GG_1}$$ But $W = \rho g V$, so: $$\overline{BB_1} = \overline{GG_1}$$ **Step 7: Final relationship for BM** From the geometry of the tilted body, $\overline{MM_1} = \overline{BB_1} + \overline{MB}$, and since M lies on the vertical through the new B' at a height BM above B: $$BM = \frac{\overline{BB_1}}{\tan\theta}$$ Substituting $\overline{BB_1} = \dfrac{I \cdot \tan\theta}{V}$ (from the geometry of the wedge moment of inertia), we get: $$\begin{aligned} BM &= \frac{1}{\tan\theta} \cdot \frac{I \cdot \tan\theta}{V} \\[6pt] BM &= \frac{I}{V} \end{aligned}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **This is the landmark KTU formula:** $BM = I/V$. Memorize this — it appears in almost every Part B question on stability. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Restoring Couple Expression **Statement:** Show that the righting couple acting on a tilted floating body is given by $T_R = W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta$. ### Step-by-Step Development **Step 1:** When the body tilts by angle θ, the weight W acts vertically downward through G, and the buoyant force $F_B = \rho g V = W$ acts vertically upward through the new center of buoyancy B'. **Step 2:** The horizontal distance between the lines of action of W and $F_B$ is the **righting lever arm**, equal to $GM \sin\theta$. **Step 3:** The moment of this couple (which tends to restore the body to its original position) is: $$\begin{aligned} T_R &= W \times (GM \sin\theta) \\[4pt] T_R &= W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4:** For very small angles, $\sin\theta \approx \theta$ (in radians), so: $$T_R \approx W \cdot GM \cdot \theta$$ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Worked Examples Structure ### Example 3.3.1: Rectangular Pontoon Stability **Given:** A rectangular pontoon of length L = 6 m, breadth B = 3 m, and depth d = 1.5 m floats in fresh water. Its center of gravity is at h = 0.5 m from the base. Calculate GM. **Step 1: Compute the displaced volume V** The pontoon floats with some draft D. Assuming it floats fully (depth is 1.5 m, so submerged volume is base × base area × draft): $$V = L \times B \times D = 6 \times 3 \times D = 18D \text{ m}^3$$ **Step 2: Apply Archimedes' Principle to find D** Weight of pontoon = Weight of displaced water. Assuming uniform thickness and ρ_pontoon = 500 kg/m³: $$W = \rho_p \cdot L \cdot B \cdot d \cdot g = 500 \times 6 \times 3 \times 1.5 \times 9.81 = 132,435 \text{ N}$$ Equating to displaced weight: $$D = \frac{W}{\rho_w \cdot g \cdot L \cdot B} = \frac{132,435}{1000 \times 9.81 \times 18} = 0.75 \text{ m}$$ **Step 3: Compute moment of inertia I of the waterplane** $$I = \frac{L \cdot B^3}{12} = \frac{6 \times 3^3}{12} = \frac{6 \times 27}{12} = 13.5 \text{ m}^4$$ **Step 4: Compute BM** $$BM = \frac{I}{V} = \frac{13.5}{18 \times 0.75} = \frac{13.5}{13.5} = 1.0 \text{ m}$$ **Step 5: Compute BG** $$BG = \frac{d}{2} - \frac{D}{2} = \frac{1.5}{2} - \frac{0.75}{2} = 0.75 - 0.375 = 0.375 \text{ m}$$ **Step 6: Compute GM** $$GM = BM - BG = 1.0 - 0.375 = 0.625 \text{ m}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Result:** Since GM = +0.625 m > 0, the pontoon is **stable** with a metacentric height of 0.625 m. ### Example 3.3.2: Cylindrical Buoy Stability **Given:** A solid cylinder of diameter D = 2 m, height H = 1.2 m, and specific gravity 0.6 floats in water with its axis vertical. Find GM. **Step 1: Find the draft (submerged depth) D_s** By floating equilibrium: $$\rho_c \cdot g \cdot A \cdot H = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot A \cdot D_s$$ $$D_s = \text{SG} \times H = 0.6 \times 1.2 = 0.72 \text{ m}$$ **Step 2: Find the displaced volume V** $$V = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot D_s = \frac{\pi}{4} (2)^2 (0.72) = 0.72\pi \approx 2.262 \text{ m}^3$$ **Step 3: Find the waterplane moment of inertia I** $$I = \frac{\pi D^4}{64} = \frac{\pi (2)^4}{64} = \frac{16\pi}{64} = \frac{\pi}{4} \approx 0.7854 \text{ m}^4$$ **Step 4: Find BM** $$BM = \frac{I}{V} = \frac{0.7854}{2.262} \approx 0.347 \text{ m}$$ **Step 5: Find BG** Center of buoyancy is at half the draft: $OB = D_s/2 = 0.36$ m Center of gravity is at half the height: $OG = H/2 = 0.60$ m $$BG = OG - OB = 0.60 - 0.36 = 0.24 \text{ m}$$ **Step 6: Find GM** $$GM = BM - BG = 0.347 - 0.24 = 0.107 \text{ m}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Result:** GM > 0, so the cylinder floats **stably**. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Position of Metacentre Relative to G ```mermaid flowchart TB Start([Floating Body at Rest]) --> COG[Point G: Center of Gravity] Start --> COB[Point B: Center of Buoyancy] Start --> COM[Point M: Metacentre] COG --> COGdesc["Fixed relative to body
Acts vertically downward
Weight W passes through G"] COB --> COBdesc["Shifts on tilting
Acts vertically upward
Buoyant Force F_B through B"] COM --> COMdesc["Theoretical pivot point
Intersection of new B line
and original centerline"] COG --> R1{Position of M} COB --> R1 R1 --> |M above G| S1["GM greater than 0
STABLE EQUILIBRIUM
Body returns to original"] R1 --> |M below G| S2["GM less than 0
UNSTABLE EQUILIBRIUM
Body capsizes"] R1 --> |M coincides G| S3["GM equals 0
NEUTRAL EQUILIBRIUM
Body stays tilted"] style Start fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style COG fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style COB fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style COM fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style R1 fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style S1 fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style S2 fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style S3 fill:#ffe082,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Tilting of a Floating Body (Geometry) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Undisturbed Body
Centerline AB Vertical] -->|Tilt by small angle theta| B[New Position of Body
New Centerline A-prime-B-prime] B --> C[Submerged Wedge W1OW2
Volume Delta V] B --> D[Emerged Wedge W1OW
Volume Delta V] B --> E[New Center of Buoyancy B-prime
Shifts Horizontally] B --> F[Line of Action of New Buoyant Force
Vertical through B-prime] F --> G[Intersects Original Centerline at M] G --> H[M is the METACENTRE] H --> I[GM is the distance from G to M] I --> J{GM Sign} J -->|Positive| K[Stable Body] J -->|Negative| L[Unstable Body] J -->|Zero| M[Neutral Body] style A fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style B fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style C fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style D fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style E fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style F fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style G fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style H fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style I fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style J fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style K fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style L fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style M fill:#ffe082,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram: Stability Classification of Floating Bodies ```mermaid flowchart TB Root([Floating Body in Fluid]) --> Cond{Is GM greater than 0?} Cond -->|Yes, GM greater than 0| Stable[STABLE EQUILIBRIUM] Stable --> S1Detail[Metacentre M lies ABOVE G] S1Detail --> S2Detail[Restoring Couple Acts] S2Detail --> S3Detail[Body Returns to Original Position] S3Detail --> S4Detail[Example: Loaded Ship, Sphere] Cond -->|No, GM less than 0| Unstable[UNSTABLE EQUILIBRIUM] Unstable --> U1Detail[Metacentre M lies BELOW G] U1Detail --> U2Detail[Overturning Couple Acts] U2Detail --> U3Detail[Body Capsizes Further] U3Detail --> U4Detail[Example: Top-Heavy Vessel, Narrow Base] Cond -->|Equal, GM equals 0| Neutral[NEUTRAL EQUILIBRIUM] Neutral --> N1Detail[Metacentre M coincides with G] N1Detail --> N2Detail[No Couple Acts] N2Detail --> N3Detail[Body Stays at New Tilted Position] N3Detail --> N4Detail[Example: Uniform Sphere in Uniform Fluid] style Root fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style Cond fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style Stable fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style S1Detail fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style S2Detail fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style S3Detail fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style S4Detail fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style Unstable fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style U1Detail fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000 style U2Detail fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000 style U3Detail fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000 style U4Detail fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000 style Neutral fill:#ffe082,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style N1Detail fill:#fff59d,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 style N2Detail fill:#fff59d,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 style N3Detail fill:#fff59d,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 style N4Detail fill:#fff59d,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram: Computational Roadmap for GM Problems ```mermaid flowchart TB Q([Numerical Problem on Floating Body]) --> Step1[Step 1: Identify Body Shape and Dimensions] Step1 --> Step2[Step 2: Calculate Submerged Volume V using Archimedes Principle] Step2 --> Step3[Step 3: Calculate Waterplane Moment of Inertia I] Step3 --> I1{Shape Type?} I1 -->|Rectangular| I2[I equals L times B cubed divided by 12] I1 -->|Circular| I3[I equals pi times D to power 4 divided by 64] I1 -->|Other| I4[Use Standard Formula from Tables] I2 --> Step4[Step 4: Calculate BM equals I divided by V] I3 --> Step4 I4 --> Step4 Step4 --> Step5[Step 5: Determine Position of G and B] Step5 --> Step6[Step 6: Calculate BG equals absolute difference] Step6 --> Step7[Step 7: Apply GM equals BM minus BG] Step7 --> Step8[Step 8: Check Sign of GM] Step8 --> FinalSign{GM Sign} FinalSign -->|Positive| FinalStable[STABLE BODY] FinalSign -->|Negative| FinalUnstable[UNSTABLE BODY] FinalSign -->|Zero| FinalNeutral[NEUTRAL BODY] style Q fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style Step1 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000 style Step2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000 style Step3 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000 style I1 fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000 style I2 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style I3 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style I4 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style Step4 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000 style Step5 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000 style Step6 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000 style Step7 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000 style Step8 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000 style FinalSign fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000 style FinalStable fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style FinalUnstable fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style FinalNeutral fill:#ffe082,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.5 Schematic: Vector Diagram of Restoring Couple ```mermaid flowchart TB Title[Forces on Tilted Floating Body at Angle theta] Title --> W[Weight W acts Vertically DOWNWARD through G] Title --> FB[Buoyant Force F_B equals rho g V acts Vertically UPWARD through B-prime] W --> LeverArm[Horizontal Distance between W and F_B lines] FB --> LeverArm LeverArm --> LAdist["Lever Arm equals GM times sin theta"] LAdist --> Couple[Restoring Couple T_R] Couple --> TREq["T_R equals W times GM times sin theta"] TREq --> Direction[Direction: Tends to Rotate Body Back to Upright Position] Title --> Note>["Condition for Stability: GM greater than 0"] style Title fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style W fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style FB fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style LeverArm fill:#fff59d,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style LAdist fill:#fff59d,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 style Couple fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style TREq fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000 style Direction fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style Note fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Conceptual Definition **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **Q:** Define the terms: (i) Center of Buoyancy, (ii) Metacentre, and (iii) Metacentric Height. With a neat sketch, explain stable, unstable, and neutral equilibrium of a floating body. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **(i) Center of Buoyancy (B):** It is the centroid of the volume of fluid displaced by the submerged portion of a floating body. The buoyant force acts vertically upward through this point. **(ii) Metacentre (M):** It is the point at which the line of action of the buoyant force (when the body is tilted) intersects the original vertical centerline of the body. It is the theoretical point about which the floating body oscillates. **(iii) Metacentric Height (GM):** It is the distance between the Center of Gravity (G) of the floating body and the Metacentre (M). **Stability Conditions:** - **Stable Equilibrium:** M lies above G; GM > 0; restoring couple acts. - **Unstable Equilibrium:** M lies below G; GM < 0; overturning couple acts. - **Neutral Equilibrium:** M coincides with G; GM = 0; no couple acts. > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's Tip:** For 3-mark questions, always accompany the definitions with a labelled sketch and the three stability conditions to score full marks. ### Question 2: Direct Conceptual **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Q:** A floating body has a metacentric height of −0.25 m. Comment on its stability. What is the value of the righting lever if the body tilts by 8°? **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Since GM = −0.25 m (negative), the metacentre M lies **below** the center of gravity G. Hence, the body is in **unstable equilibrium** and will capsize if tilted. The righting lever at a tilt angle θ is: $$GZ = GM \cdot \sin\theta = -0.25 \times \sin(8°) = -0.25 \times 0.1392 = -0.0348 \text{ m}$$ > [!NOTE] > The negative righting lever confirms the overturning nature — the body experiences a turning moment, not a restoring one. ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Format:** Part B questions in the End Semester Examination carry 14 marks. Each question typically has two sub-parts: Part (a) for 7 marks and Part (b) for 7 marks. Students must answer ANY ONE full question (both sub-parts). The following two questions represent the internal choice. --- ### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Set A]** **A rectangular pontoon of length 8 m, width 4 m, and height 1.8 m weighs 350 kN when empty. It floats in sea water (ρ = 1025 kg/m³).** **(a) [7 Marks]** Calculate the depth of immersion (draft) of the pontoon and the position of the center of buoyancy from the base. **(b) [7 Marks]** If the center of gravity of the pontoon is 0.9 m from the base, determine the metacentric height GM and state whether the pontoon is in stable equilibrium. --- #### **Model Solution:** **Given Data:** - Length L = 8 m - Width B = 4 m - Height H = 1.8 m - Weight W = 350 kN = 350,000 N - Density of sea water ρ = 1025 kg/m³ - g = 9.81 m/s² - Height of G from base = 0.9 m #### **Part (a) Solution: Draft and Center of Buoyancy** **Step 1: Apply Archimedes' Principle** Weight of pontoon = Weight of displaced water: $$W = \rho \cdot g \cdot V_{\text{displaced}}$$ $$350,000 = 1025 \times 9.81 \times (L \times B \times D)$$ $$350,000 = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 8 \times 4 \times D$$ $$350,000 = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 32 \times D$$ $$350,000 = 321,744 \times D$$ **Step 2: Solve for Draft D** $$D = \frac{350,000}{321,744} = 1.088 \text{ m}$$ **Step 3: Position of Center of Buoyancy** For a rectangular pontoon, the center of buoyancy is at the centroid of the submerged rectangular volume, which is at half the draft: $$OB = \frac{D}{2} = \frac{1.088}{2} = 0.544 \text{ m from base}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Answer for Part (a):** Draft D = 1.088 m; Center of Buoyancy B is at 0.544 m from the base. #### **Part (b) Solution: Metacentric Height and Stability** **Step 1: Compute Submerged Volume V** $$V = L \times B \times D = 8 \times 4 \times 1.088 = 34.816 \text{ m}^3$$ **Step 2: Compute Moment of Inertia I of Waterplane** For a rectangular waterplane: $$I = \frac{L \cdot B^3}{12} = \frac{8 \times 4^3}{12} = \frac{8 \times 64}{12} = \frac{512}{12} = 42.667 \text{ m}^4$$ **Step 3: Compute BM** $$BM = \frac{I}{V} = \frac{42.667}{34.816} = 1.225 \text{ m}$$ **Step 4: Compute BG** $$BG = OG - OB = 0.9 - 0.544 = 0.356 \text{ m}$$ **Step 5: Compute GM** $$GM = BM - BG = 1.225 - 0.356 = 0.869 \text{ m}$$ **Step 6: Stability Conclusion** Since GM = +0.869 m > 0, the metacentre M lies above the center of gravity G. Therefore, the pontoon is in **stable equilibrium**. > [!NOTE] > **Answer for Part (b):** GM = 0.869 m (positive), hence the pontoon is in **stable equilibrium**. --- ### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Set B]** **A solid cylinder of diameter 1.5 m and height 1.0 m has a specific gravity of 0.65. It floats in fresh water with its axis vertical.** **(a) [7 Marks]** Find the depth of immersion and the position of the metacentre M relative to the base. **(b) [7 Marks]** Determine the metacentric height and the righting couple when the cylinder tilts by 5°. --- #### **Model Solution:** **Given Data:** - Diameter D = 1.5 m - Height H = 1.0 m - Specific gravity SG = 0.65 - ρ_water = 1000 kg/m³ - g = 9.81 m/s² #### **Part (a) Solution: Depth of Immersion and Metacentre Position** **Step 1: Find the Draft (Submerged Depth)** By Archimedes' Principle: $$D_s = SG \times H = 0.65 \times 1.0 = 0.65 \text{ m}$$ **Step 2: Calculate Submerged Volume V** $$V = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \times D_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (1.5)^2 \times 0.65 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 2.25 \times 0.65$$ $$V = \frac{\pi \times 1.4625}{4} = 1.1485 \text{ m}^3$$ **Step 3: Calculate Moment of Inertia I of Circular Waterplane** For a circular waterplane: $$I = \frac{\pi D^4}{64} = \frac{\pi (1.5)^4}{64} = \frac{\pi \times 5.0625}{64} = \frac{15.904}{64} = 0.2485 \text{ m}^4$$ **Step 4: Calculate BM** $$BM = \frac{I}{V} = \frac{0.2485}{1.1485} = 0.2164 \text{ m}$$ **Step 5: Calculate the Position of Metacentre M from the Base** Center of buoyancy is at $OB = D_s/2 = 0.325$ m from the base. $$OM = OB + BM = 0.325 + 0.2164 = 0.5414 \text{ m from base}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Answer for Part (a):** Depth of immersion = 0.65 m; Metacentre M is at 0.5414 m from the base. #### **Part (b) Solution: Metacentric Height and Righting Couple** **Step 1: Find OG** For a uniform solid cylinder, the center of gravity is at half the height: $$OG = \frac{H}{2} = \frac{1.0}{2} = 0.5 \text{ m}$$ **Step 2: Calculate BG** $$BG = OG - OB = 0.5 - 0.325 = 0.175 \text{ m}$$ **Step 3: Calculate GM** $$GM = BM - BG = 0.2164 - 0.175 = 0.0414 \text{ m}$$ **Step 4: Calculate Weight of the Cylinder** $$W = \rho_c \cdot g \cdot V_{\text{cylinder}} = 0.65 \times 1000 \times 9.81 \times \left(\frac{\pi}{4} \times 1.5^2 \times 1.0\right)$$ $$W = 0.65 \times 1000 \times 9.81 \times 1.7671 = 11,261.4 \text{ N}$$ **Step 5: Calculate the Righting Couple at 5°** $$T_R = W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta = 11,261.4 \times 0.0414 \times \sin(5°)$$ $$T_R = 11,261.4 \times 0.0414 \times 0.0872 = 40.65 \text{ N·m}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Answer for Part (b):** GM = 0.0414 m (positive → stable); Righting couple at 5° tilt = 40.65 N·m. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing BM and GM:** BM is the distance from B to M, NOT from G to M. Always use $BM = I/V$ first, then apply $GM = BM - BG$ separately. Mixing these up in 14-mark problems costs 4–5 marks. > > 2. **Forgetting to identify the tilting axis:** The moment of inertia I is taken about the **axis of rotation** (longitudinal axis for ship rolling, transverse axis for tilting sideways). Using the wrong I formula (e.g., $B^3 L/12$ instead of $L B^3/12$) is a common error. > > 3. **Sign convention for BG:** If G is below B, then $BG = OB - OG$ (negative), making $GM = BM + \vert BG \vert$. Always check which point is higher. > > 4. **Using g = 10 instead of 9.81:** KTU numerical problems require g = **9.81 m/s²**. Using 10 is acceptable only if explicitly stated, but precision matters. > > 5. **Draft vs. Total Height:** In floating problems, V uses only the **submerged volume**, not the total body volume. The waterline area is at the surface, not at the base of the body. > > 6. **Stability comment required:** Even if GM comes out positive, always state "the body is in stable equilibrium" explicitly. Examiners look for the conclusion, not just the number. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist:** - **Metacentre (M):** Intersection of the new buoyant force line and the original vertical centerline of the body. - **Metacentric Height (GM):** Distance from G to M. Determines stability. **GM > 0 → Stable, GM < 0 → Unstable, GM = 0 → Neutral.** - **BM Formula (MOST IMPORTANT):** $BM = I / V$, where I = moment of inertia of waterplane area about the tilting axis, V = submerged volume. - **Rectangular waterline:** $I = L \cdot B^3 / 12$ (rolling about longitudinal axis). - **Circular waterline:** $I = \pi D^4 / 64$. - **Final GM Formula:** $GM = BM - BG$ (when M is above G) or $GM = BM + GB$ (when G is below B). - **Restoring Couple:** $T_R = W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta$ (exact) or $T_R \approx W \cdot GM \cdot \theta$ (small angle approximation). - **Righting Lever:** $GZ = GM \cdot \sin\theta$. - **Assumption:** All derivations are valid only for **small angles of tilt** (typically θ < 15°). For large angles, the position of M itself shifts and the formulas become nonlinear. - **Practical Engineering Insight:** A high GM means a "stiff" ship (rapid, jerky rolling); a low GM means a "tender" ship (slow, comfortable rolling). Naval architects target a GM of 0.15 m to 0.50 m for cargo vessels. - **Archimedes' Linkage:** The entire concept of metacentre is built on Archimedes' Principle ($F_B = \rho g V$). Without buoyancy, there is no metacentre. - **Order of Computation:** V → I → BM → BG → GM → Stability verdict.

topics.title: Types of Fluid Flow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT741 - FLUID MECHANICS & MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Fluid Flow

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Defining "Fluid Flow" in the KTU Context

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework (PEMRT741, Module 2), **fluid flow** describes the continuous deformation and movement of fluid particles under the influence of applied forces. Mathematically, fluid flow is a mapping of fluid properties (velocity $\vec{V}$, pressure $p$, density $\rho$) as functions of position $(x, y, z)$ and time $(t)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Module 2.1):**
> Fluid flow is the study of the motion of fluids (liquids and gases) characterized by the field functions $\vec{V}(x, y, z, t)$, $p(x, y, z, t)$, and $\rho(x, y, z, t)$, governed by the principles of conservation of mass, momentum, and energy.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The River Story

Imagine standing on a bridge over a river. The water moves continuously downstream, but the *character* of that motion is not constant. Near the banks, the water creeps slowly; in the middle, it rushes in a straight line; if you drop a leaf, sometimes it glides smoothly, and sometimes it tumbles erratically. That *changing character* is exactly what engineers mean by "types of fluid flow."

- **Steady vs. Unsteady** → Like a constant garden hose stream vs. a tap you keep opening and closing.
- **Laminar vs. Turbulent** → Like a smoothly flowing ribbon of syrup vs. a violently mixed cup of chai.
- **Compressible vs. Incompressible** → Like water (almost incompressible, density $\rho$ = **1000 kg/m³**) vs. air being rapidly compressed in a bicycle pump.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PEMRT741, Module 2):**
> Students are required to classify fluid flow based on **eight** primary criteria: (1) Time, (2) Space, (3) Velocity, (4) Viscosity, (5) Compressibility, (6) Rotationality, (7) Dimensionality, and (8) Boundary conditions.

## 1.3 The Eight Primary Classifications of Fluid Flow

| # | Classification Basis | Type 1 | Type 2 |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Time dependence | Steady flow | Unsteady flow |
| 2 | Spatial variation | Uniform flow | Non-uniform flow |
| 3 | Flow pattern (Reynolds) | Laminar flow | Turbulent flow |
| 4 | Compressibility | Compressible | Incompressible |
| 5 | Rotationality | Rotational | Irrotational |
| 6 | Dimensionality | 1-D, 2-D, 3-D | — |
| 7 | Viscosity effect | Viscous | Inviscid (Ideal) |
| 8 | Boundary exposure | Internal flow | External flow |

---

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Memory Hook (KTU Examiners love this):**
> *"T-S-C-R-D-V-I-E"* → **T**ime, **S**pace, **C**ompressibility, **R**otation, **D**imension, **V**iscosity, **E**xposure. Mnemonic: **"The Sun Casts Rays Down Via Empty Ether."**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Steady vs. Unsteady Flow

A flow is **steady** if the fluid properties at any fixed point in space do **not change with time**.

$$\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial p}{\partial t} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = 0$$

> A flow is **unsteady** when at least one property changes with time at a fixed spatial location. Example: water flowing from a tap being gradually closed.

**Why this matters:** The entire branch of *potential flow theory* in KTU Module 2 assumes steady flow. Time derivatives vanish, simplifying the Euler and Bernoulli equations dramatically.

## 2.2 Uniform vs. Non-uniform Flow

A flow is **uniform** when the velocity vector at any given instant is **constant in magnitude and direction** along the streamline. It is a *spatial* property, not a *temporal* one.

$$\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial s} = 0 \quad \text{(along the flow direction)}$$

**Example:** Flow through a constant-diameter straight pipe at constant rate → uniform.

**Example:** Flow through a converging nozzle → non-uniform (velocity changes with position).

## 2.3 Laminar vs. Turbulent Flow — The Reynolds Criterion

This is the **most heavily tested classification** in KTU examinations. The dimensionless **Reynolds Number** $\text{Re}$ governs the transition:

$$\boxed{\text{Re} = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{V D}{\nu}}$$

Where:
- $V$ = mean velocity of flow in **m/s**
- $D$ = characteristic length (pipe diameter) in **m**
- $\rho$ = density of fluid in **kg/m³**
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity in **N·s/m²**
- $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity in **m²/s** ($\nu = \mu / \rho$)

| Flow Regime | Reynolds Number Range | Physical Character |
|---|---|---|
| Laminar | $\text{Re} < 2000$ | Smooth, parallel layers (laminae) |
| Transitional | $2000 \le \text{Re} \le 4000$ | Unpredictable, intermittent bursts |
| Turbulent | $\text{Re} > 4000$ | Chaotic, eddies, mixing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reynolds Experiment (1883):** Osborne Reynolds demonstrated this transition using a glass pipe with a dye injector. For KTU derivations, you must know that the critical velocity $V_c = \text{Re}_c \cdot \nu / D$ where $\text{Re}_c = 2000$ for pipe flow.

## 2.4 Compressible vs. Incompressible Flow

| Property | Incompressible | Compressible |
|---|---|---|
| Density $\rho$ | Constant | Variable |
| Mach number $M$ | $M < 0.3$ | $M \ge 0.3$ |
| Typical fluid | Water, oil | Air at high speed, steam |
| Governing equation | $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$ | Includes $\frac{D\rho}{Dt} \neq 0$ |

$$\boxed{M = \frac{V}{c} = \frac{V}{\sqrt{\gamma R T}}}$$

## 2.5 Rotational vs. Irrotational Flow

A fluid element is **irrotational** if it has **no net angular velocity** about any axis through its center.

Mathematical test (in 2-D):
$$\omega_z = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right) = 0$$

If $\omega_z = 0$ everywhere, the flow is **irrotational**. The flow is **rotational** when $\omega_z \neq 0$, i.e., fluid particles spin as they translate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** *Viscous flow is always rotational* near solid boundaries. Potential flow theory (covered later in the module) is intrinsically irrotational. The famous **d'Alembert's paradox** (zero drag on an inviscid body) is a direct consequence of assuming irrotationality.

## 2.6 1-D, 2-D, 3-D Flow

- **1-D flow:** Velocity varies in only one direction. Example: flow in a long, thin pipe.
- **2-D flow:** Velocity varies in two directions, with no variation in the third. Example: flow over a long weir.
- **3-D flow:** Most general case — velocity varies in all three spatial directions.

## 2.7 Viscous vs. Inviscid (Ideal) Flow

- **Viscous flow:** Internal friction ($\mu \neq 0$) dissipates energy. Governed by the **Navier-Stokes equations**.
- **Inviscid flow:** Friction neglected ($\mu = 0$). Governed by the **Euler equations**.

$$\text{Euler:} \quad \rho \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \rho \vec{g}$$

## 2.8 Internal vs. External Flow

- **Internal flow:** Fluid is completely confined by solid boundaries (e.g., pipe flow). Characteristic length = pipe diameter $D$.
- **External flow:** Fluid flows over an immersed body (e.g., flow over an airfoil). Characteristic length = body length $L$.

## 2.9 🧠 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Variables | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reynolds Number | $\text{Re} = \rho V D / \mu$ | $V, D, \rho, \mu$ | Laminar/Turbulent test |
| Critical Velocity | $V_c = \text{Re}_c \cdot \nu / D$ | $\nu, D$ | Pipe flow onset of turbulence |
| Mach Number | $M = V / c = V / \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ | $V, \gamma, R, T$ | Compressibility test |
| Continuity (1-D) | $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ | $A, V$ | Mass conservation |
| Continuity (3-D) | $\partial u/\partial x + \partial v/\partial y + \partial w/\partial z = 0$ | $u, v, w$ | Incompressible mass balance |
| Streamline Eqn. | $dx/u = dy/v = dz/w$ | $u, v, w$ | Pathline geometry |
| Vorticity (z-comp.) | $\omega_z = \frac{1}{2}(\partial v/\partial x - \partial u/\partial y)$ | $u, v$ | Rotationality test |
| Speed of sound | $c = \sqrt{K / \rho}$ | $K$ = bulk modulus | Mach number |
| Specific weight | $\gamma = \rho g$ | $\rho, g$ | Pressure-to-head conversion |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Reynolds number determines whether we use the *Hagen-Poiseuille equation* (laminar, $\text{Re} < 2000$) or the *Darcy-Weisbach equation with Moody chart* (turbulent, $\text{Re} > 4000$). Choosing the wrong flow regime is a common design error in piping systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Reynolds Number from First Principles

We begin with the **equation of motion for a fluid element** in a pipe, balancing inertial and viscous forces. The Reynolds number arises as the dimensionless ratio of these competing effects.

**Step 1 — Define Inertial Force (per unit volume):**
$$F_{\text{inertia}} = \rho \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} \sim \rho \frac{V^2}{L}$$

Here, $V$ is the characteristic velocity and $L$ is the characteristic length. The time derivative of velocity scales as $V/T \sim V^2/L$ (using $T \sim L/V$).

**Step 2 — Define Viscous Force (per unit volume):**
$$F_{\text{viscous}} = \mu \nabla^2 \vec{V} \sim \mu \frac{V}{L^2}$$

**Step 3 — Form the Dimensionless Ratio:**
$$\text{Re} = \frac{F_{\text{inertia}}}{F_{\text{viscous}}} = \frac{\rho V^2 / L}{\mu V / L^2} = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $L = D$ for pipe flow:**
$$\boxed{\text{Re} = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{V D}{\nu}}$$

**Conversion to kinematic form:**
$$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{V D}{\mu / \rho} = \frac{V D}{\nu}$$

> **Logic:** The Reynolds number is **not** a fundamental constant — it is a *dimensionless grouping* of $\rho$, $V$, $D$, $\mu$. When $\text{Re}$ is small, viscous forces dominate (laminar); when $\text{Re}$ is large, inertial forces dominate (turbulent).

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the 1-D Continuity Equation

**Step 1 — Consider a control volume** in a pipe with cross-section $A_1$ and $A_2$, separated by distance $dx$, as shown in SECTION 4.

**Step 2 — Apply conservation of mass** (for steady, incompressible flow):

Mass in = Mass out

$$\rho A_1 V_1 \, dt = \rho A_2 V_2 \, dt$$

**Step 3 — Cancel $\rho \, dt$:**
$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = Q \quad \text{(constant volumetric flow rate)}$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate with respect to $x$ for a variable-area pipe:**
$$A \, dV + V \, dA = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{dA}{A} + \frac{dV}{V} = 0$$

> **Logic:** In incompressible flow, a converging area *must* accelerate the fluid. This is the principle behind the Venturi meter and carburetor jets.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the 3-D Continuity Equation (Differential Form)

**Step 1 — Start with an infinitesimal control volume** $dV = dx\,dy\,dz$ with mass inflow on $+\hat{x}$ face and outflow on $-\hat{x}$ face.

**Step 2 — Net mass efflux in x-direction:**
$$\left(\rho u + \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x}dx\right)dy\,dz - \rho u\,dy\,dz = \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x}dx\,dy\,dz$$

**Step 3 — Sum over all three directions and equate to rate of mass change inside the CV:**
$$\frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} = -\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}$$

**Step 4 — For steady flow** ($\partial\rho/\partial t = 0$) **and incompressible flow** ($\rho$ = constant):
$$\boxed{\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0 \quad \text{or} \quad \nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0}$$

> **Logic:** This equation is the **gatekeeper** of incompressible flow analysis. Any velocity field proposed for an incompressible flow *must* satisfy this divergence-free condition.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Streamline Equation in 2-D

A streamline is a curve whose tangent at every point is parallel to the instantaneous velocity vector.

**Step 1 — At a point $(x, y)$, the velocity vector is $\vec{V} = u\hat{i} + v\hat{j}$.**

**Step 2 — The differential displacement along the streamline is $d\vec{r} = dx\,\hat{i} + dy\,\hat{j}$.**

**Step 3 — For the displacement to be parallel to velocity:**
$$\frac{dx}{u} = \frac{dy}{v}$$

**Step 4 — Extending to 3-D:**
$$\frac{dx}{u} = \frac{dy}{v} = \frac{dz}{w}$$

> **Logic:** This is the **geometric definition** of a streamline. In 2-D incompressible flow, the stream function $\psi$ exists such that $u = \partial\psi/\partial y$ and $v = -\partial\psi/\partial x$, automatically satisfying continuity.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — Classifying Flow in a Pipe

**Problem (KTU-style):** Water at $20°\text{C}$ flows through a 50 mm diameter pipe at an average velocity of 1.5 m/s. Given $\rho = 998 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N·s/m}^2$, classify the flow.

**Step 1 — Compute the Reynolds number:**
$$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{998 \times 1.5 \times 0.050}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\text{Re} = \frac{74.85}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}} = 74{,}700$$

**Step 2 — Compare with critical values:**
Since $\text{Re} = 74{,}700 \gg 4000$, the flow is **turbulent**.

**Step 3 — Mach number check (if air were the fluid):**
For water, speed of sound $c \approx 1480 \text{ m/s}$, so $M = 1.5/1480 \approx 0.001 \ll 0.3$ → **incompressible**.

**Step 4 — Rotationality check:** Pipe flow exhibits a parabolic velocity profile, so vorticity $\omega_z \neq 0$ → **rotational**.

**Final classification:** Turbulent, incompressible, rotational, internal, steady (if conditions don't change), non-uniform (velocity varies across cross-section), 1-D (axial direction dominant), viscous.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow for Flow Classification

Use this matrix to systematically classify any fluid flow in KTU exam questions:

| Step | Test | Compute | Threshold | Classification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Is $\partial \vec{V}/\partial t = 0$? | Time-derivative | Yes/No | Steady / Unsteady |
| 2 | Is $\partial \vec{V}/\partial s = 0$? | Spatial derivative | Yes/No | Uniform / Non-uniform |
| 3 | Compute $\text{Re} = \rho V D / \mu$ | Reynolds | <2000, 2000-4000, >4000 | Laminar / Transitional / Turbulent |
| 4 | Compute $M = V / c$ | Mach | <0.3, ≥0.3 | Incompressible / Compressible |
| 5 | Compute $\omega_z$ | Vorticity | =0, ≠0 | Irrotational / Rotational |
| 6 | Check geometry | Dimension count | 1, 2, 3 | 1-D, 2-D, 3-D |
| 7 | Is $\mu = 0$? | Viscosity | Yes/No | Inviscid / Viscous |
| 8 | Bounded by walls? | Geometry | Yes/No | Internal / External |

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Block Diagram (Mermaid)

This block diagram maps the eight primary classifications of fluid flow as defined in the KTU 2024 Module 2 syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FLUID FLOW TYPES] --> B[TIME BASED]
    A --> C[SPACE BASED]
    A --> D[REYNOLDS BASED]
    A --> E[COMPRESSIBILITY]
    A --> F[ROTATIONALITY]
    A --> G[DIMENSIONALITY]
    A --> H[VISCOSITY]
    A --> I[BOUNDARY]

    B --> B1[STEADY: dV/dt equals 0]
    B --> B2[UNSTEADY: dV/dt not 0]

    C --> C1[UNIFORM: dV/ds equals 0]
    C --> C2[NON-UNIFORM: dV/ds not 0]

    D --> D1[LAMINAR: Re less than 2000]
    D --> D2[TRANSITIONAL: Re 2000 to 4000]
    D --> D3[TURBULENT: Re greater than 4000]

    E --> E1[INCOMPRESSIBLE: M less than 0.3]
    E --> E2[COMPRESSIBLE: M greater than 0.3]

    F --> F1[IRROTATIONAL: omega equals 0]
    F --> F2[ROTATIONAL: omega not 0]

    G --> G1[ONE DIMENSIONAL]
    G --> G2[TWO DIMENSIONAL]
    G --> G3[THREE DIMENSIONAL]

    H --> H1[VISCOUS: mu not 0]
    H --> H2[INVISCID: mu equals 0]

    I --> I1[INTERNAL: pipe duct]
    I --> I2[EXTERNAL: body submerged]
```

---

## 4.2 Control Volume Architecture for Continuity Derivation (Mermaid)

This diagram shows the conceptual layout of a control volume used in deriving the 1-D continuity equation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CV[CONTROL VOLUME PIPE SECTION]
        direction LR
        X1[Inlet Section A1] -- mass flow rho A1 V1 --> X2[Pipe Length dx]
        X2 -- mass flow rho A2 V2 --> X3[Outlet Section A2]
    end

    X1 --> X4[Section 1: Area A1, Velocity V1]
    X3 --> X5[Section 2: Area A2, Velocity V2]

    X4 --> X6[Apply Conservation of Mass]
    X5 --> X6
    X6 --> X7[Result: A1 V1 equals A2 V2 equals Q]
```

---

## 4.3 Reynolds Number Decision Tree (Mermaid)

A functional decision tree used to classify a flow as laminar, transitional, or turbulent.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[START: Compute Reynolds Number] --> P1{Is Re less than 2000}
    P1 -- YES --> P2[LAMINAR FLOW]
    P1 -- NO --> P3{Is Re between 2000 and 4000}
    P3 -- YES --> P4[TRANSITIONAL FLOW]
    P3 -- NO --> P5[Is Re greater than 4000]
    P5 -- YES --> P6[TURBULENT FLOW]
    P5 -- NO --> P7[RECHECK INPUTS]
```

---

## 4.4 Vorticity Classification Block (Mermaid)

Shows how to determine rotational versus irrotational flow using the vorticity component.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V0[Compute Vorticity] --> V1[Evaluate omega z equals half times dv dx minus du dy]
    V1 --> V2{Is omega z equal to 0 everywhere}
    V2 -- YES --> V3[IRROTATIONAL FLOW: Exists Stream Function and Velocity Potential]
    V2 -- NO --> V4[ROTATIONAL FLOW: Vorticity Nonzero]
    V3 --> V5[Apply Laplace Equation for Velocity Potential]
    V4 --> V6[Apply Navier Stokes Equations]
```
