# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the Reynolds number. State its physical significance and the typical range of values for laminar flow in a pipe.*

**Model Answer:**

The Reynolds number is a dimensionless quantity that represents the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces in a fluid flow:

$$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{V D}{\nu}$$

**Physical Significance:** It determines the nature of flow — whether the flow remains smooth and orderly (laminar) or becomes chaotic and mixing (turbulent). It also serves as the criterion for dynamic similarity between model and prototype in scaled experiments.

For flow in a circular pipe:
- Laminar flow: $\text{Re} < 2000$
- Transitional flow: $2000 \le \text{Re} \le 4000$
- Turbulent flow: $\text{Re} > 4000$

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Distinguish between rotational and irrotational flow with suitable examples.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Rotational Flow | Irrotational Flow |
|---|---|---|
| Vorticity $\omega$ | $\omega \neq 0$ | $\omega = 0$ |
| Fluid element spin | Rotates about its own axis | Pure translation, no spinning |
| Example | Flow inside a rotating tank, flow through a pipe (parabolic profile) | Flow near the centre of a free vortex, ideal flow over a streamlined body |
| Mathematical test | $\partial v/\partial x - \partial u/\partial y \neq 0$ | $\partial v/\partial x - \partial u/\partial y = 0$ |

> **Example:** Consider a forced vortex where $v_\theta = \omega r$. Then $\omega_z = \omega \neq 0$, hence **rotational**. In a free vortex, $v_\theta = C/r$, giving $\omega_z = 0$ (except at the singular axis), hence **irrotational**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 | ESE Module 2]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the continuity equation in differential form for a 3-D, unsteady, compressible flow. Hence, simplify it for (i) steady flow and (ii) steady incompressible flow.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Water flows through a 100 mm diameter pipe at a rate of 15 litres/second. Compute the Reynolds number and classify the flow. Take $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N·s/m}^2$.

---

#### Solution to (a)

**Step 1 — Control Volume Setup:**
Consider an infinitesimal rectangular parallelepiped of sides $dx$, $dy$, $dz$ as the control volume.

**Step 2 — Mass Flux in x-direction (Taylor expansion):**
$$\dot{m}_x^{\text{in}} = \rho u \, dy\, dz$$
$$\dot{m}_x^{\text{out}} = \left[\rho u + \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x}dx\right]dy\,dz$$

**Step 3 — Net efflux in x-direction:**
$$\Delta \dot{m}_x = \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x}dx\,dy\,dz$$

**Step 4 — Summing all three directions and applying conservation of mass:**
$$\frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} + \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = 0$$

**Step 5 — Simplification (i) — Steady flow** ($\partial \rho / \partial t = 0$):
$$\frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} = 0$$

**Step 6 — Simplification (ii) — Steady and incompressible** ($\rho$ = constant):
$$\boxed{\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0 \quad \text{i.e.,} \quad \nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0}$$

---

#### Solution to (b)

**Step 1 — Compute the mean velocity:**
$$Q = 15 \text{ L/s} = 15 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$
$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.1)^2}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$
$$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{15 \times 10^{-3}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.910 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Reynolds number:**
$$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{1000 \times 1.910 \times 0.1}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.91 \times 10^{5}$$

**Step 3 — Classification:**
Since $\text{Re} = 1.91 \times 10^{5} \gg 4000$, the flow is **fully turbulent**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 | ESE Module 2]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** State the conditions for a flow to be considered (i) steady, (ii) uniform, (iii) incompressible, and (iv) irrotational. Derive the stream function and show that its existence guarantees continuity in 2-D incompressible flow.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Air at $30°\text{C}$ flows over an aircraft wing at 250 m/s. Given $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 287 \text{ J/kg·K}$, determine whether the flow is compressible or incompressible.

---

#### Solution to (a)

**(i) Steady flow condition:** $\partial \vec{V}/\partial t = 0$ at all points.

**(ii) Uniform flow condition:** $\partial \vec{V}/\partial s = 0$ along the flow direction (i.e., velocity does not change from point to point along the streamline).

**(iii) Incompressible flow condition:** $D\rho/Dt = 0$, or equivalently, the Mach number $M < 0.3$.

**(iv) Irrotational flow condition:** Vorticity $\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V} = 0$ everywhere.

**Stream Function Derivation:**

For 2-D incompressible flow, we define a scalar function $\psi(x, y)$ such that:
$$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}, \quad v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$$

**Step 1 — Substitute into continuity equation:**
$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(-\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Simplify:**
$$\frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x \partial y} - \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x \partial y} = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

> **Conclusion:** The mere *existence* of $\psi$ guarantees that the continuity equation is satisfied. This is why stream functions are the cornerstone of 2-D potential flow analysis.

---

#### Solution to (b)

**Step 1 — Compute the speed of sound:**
$$c = \sqrt{\gamma R T} = \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times (30 + 273)}$$
$$c = \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 303} = \sqrt{121{,}756} \approx 348.93 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Mach number:**
$$M = \frac{V}{c} = \frac{250}{348.93} \approx 0.716$$

**Step 3 — Classification:**
Since $M = 0.716 > 0.3$, the flow is **compressible**. (Note: $M < 1$, so it is subsonic compressible — not supersonic.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Module 2):**
> 1. **Mixing up "uniform" with "steady"** — Uniform is a *spatial* property, steady is a *temporal* property. Examiners deduct 1 mark per confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting units in Reynolds number** — Always show $\rho$ in kg/m³, $V$ in m/s, $D$ in m, and $\mu$ in N·s/m². A common error is using $D$ in mm without conversion.
> 3. **Critical Reynolds number value** — Use $\text{Re}_c = 2000$ (lower critical) for pipe flow, NOT 2300. The 2300 is sometimes cited in older textbooks but the KTU standard is **2000**.
> 4. **Mach number threshold** — Compressibility effects become important at $M \ge 0.3$, not $M \ge 1$. Many students write "Mach > 1" which is wrong.
> 5. **In the continuity derivation** — Students often miss the Taylor expansion step. Writing only "$\partial \rho/\partial t + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$" without derivation loses 4 of 7 marks.
> 6. **Streamline vs. Pathline vs. Streakline** — KTU Module 2.2 tests this. In *steady* flow, all three coincide; in *unsteady* flow, they differ.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **The Eight Classifications** (Time, Space, Reynolds, Compressibility, Rotation, Dimension, Viscosity, Boundary) form the spine of Module 2 — memorize them in order using the mnemonic *T-S-C-R-D-V-I-E*.
- **Reynolds Number:** $\text{Re} = \rho V D / \mu$. Critical for pipe flow: $\text{Re} < 2000$ laminar, $> 4000$ turbulent.
- **Mach Number:** $M = V / c$. Threshold for compressibility: $M \ge 0.3$.
- **Continuity Equation (1-D):** $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = Q$ for incompressible steady flow.
- **Continuity Equation (3-D):** $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$ for incompressible flow; $\partial \rho / \partial t + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$ in general.
- **Streamline Equation:** $dx/u = dy/v = dz/w$.
- **Vorticity Test (2-D):** $\omega_z = \frac{1}{2}(\partial v/\partial x - \partial u/\partial y) = 0$ → irrotational.
- **Euler Equation** (inviscid): $\rho D\vec{V}/Dt = -\nabla p + \rho \vec{g}$.
- **Navier-Stokes Equation** (viscous): adds the term $\mu \nabla^2 \vec{V}$ to the right side.
- **Steady ≠ Uniform:** Steady is time-invariance at a point; uniform is space-invariance along the streamline.
- **Inviscid ≠ Irrotational:** Inviscid is a *viscosity* statement; irrotational is a *kinematics* statement. The two are independent.
- **d'Alembert's Paradox:** Irrotational + inviscid flow around a body yields zero drag — purely theoretical, breaks down with real (viscous) fluids.
- **Practical Examples to recall:** Pipe flow = internal + viscous + rotational; Flow over a wing = external + viscous (in boundary layer) + irrotational (in free stream); Atmospheric wind = unsteady + compressible (locally) + 3-D + rotational.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Continuity Equation in 2D and 3D (Cartesian Co-ordinates only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT741 - FLUID MECHANICS & MACHINERY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Continuity Equation in 2D and 3D (Cartesian Co-ordinates only) # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Continuity Equation (Definition)** > The continuity equation is the **mathematical statement of the principle of conservation of mass** applied to a moving fluid. In Cartesian coordinates, it expresses that the **net rate of mass flow out of an infinitesimal fluid element** plus the **rate of mass accumulation within the element** must equal zero. In differential form, the **three-dimensional compressible** continuity equation in Cartesian coordinates is: $$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} = 0$$ where: - $\rho$ = fluid density (kg/m³) - $u, v, w$ = velocity components along $x, y, z$ axes (m/s) - $t$ = time (s) For an **incompressible fluid** ($\rho = \text{constant}$), the equation simplifies to: $$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0$$ > [!NOTE] > The operator $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial w}{\partial z}$ is called the **velocity divergence**. For an incompressible flow, **divergence of velocity is identically zero**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Water Bucket Picture" Imagine you are filling a small flexible rubber bucket with water from a tap, and there is a small hole at the bottom. - **If the bucket is rigid and the water level stays constant:** Whatever volume enters from the top **must** leave from the bottom. This is **incompressible continuity** — *no net accumulation*. - **If the bucket is a balloon that can stretch (and density can change):** Water can enter faster than it leaves, and the balloon swells. This represents **compressible continuity** — *net accumulation is allowed, but must be balanced by density change*. In a flowing river, the same idea applies at the *infinitesimal* level: the volume of fluid entering a tiny cube-shaped parcel of space from the three faces ($x, y, z$) must equal the volume leaving the opposite faces **plus** any change in density inside the parcel. > [!TIP] > **Engineering Realisation:** The continuity equation is the **first governing equation** of fluid flow. Without satisfying it, a velocity field is physically impossible — no matter how elegant the rest of the analysis looks. ## 1.3 Significance in Engineering | Application | Role of Continuity Equation | |-------------|------------------------------| | Pipe flow design | Relates area and velocity ($A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ for incompressible) | | Aerodynamics | Ensures mass conservation over an airfoil control volume | | Hydraulic machinery | Volume flow continuity between pump, pipe, and turbine | | Open channel flow | Used in deriving gradually-varied flow equations | | Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) | Forms one of the Navier–Stokes governing equations | # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Underlying Physics — Two Pillars The continuity equation rests on **two fundamental pillars**: **Pillar 1 — Conservation of Mass (Lavoisier, 1789):** > *"Mass can neither be created nor destroyed in a closed system."* For any control volume (CV) of fixed identity: $$\left(\frac{\partial m}{\partial t}\right)_{CV} = \sum \dot{m}_{in} - \sum \dot{m}_{out}$$ **Pillar 2 — Infinitesimal Control Volume Approach:** We shrink the CV to an *infinitesimal* size so that the velocity field may be treated as linear over the element. This converts the integral form into a **partial differential equation (PDE)**. ## 2.2 Assumptions Used in the Derivation | # | Assumption | Mathematical Implication | |---|------------|---------------------------| | 1 | No sources or sinks inside the fluid | Mass cannot appear or disappear | | 2 | Single-phase, single-component flow | Only one density $\rho$ exists | | 3 | No chemical reaction / nuclear conversion | $\rho$ changes only by compression/expansion | | 4 | Smooth, continuous velocity field | Partial derivatives exist | | 5 | Newtonian continuum | Fluid behaves as a continuum, not molecular | ## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic of the Derivation The logic flow used to obtain the equation is: 1. **Choose an infinitesimal CV** — a rectangular element of size $dx \times dy \times dz$ fixed in space (Eulerian approach). 2. **Compute mass flux in** through the three "in" faces ($x, y, z$). 3. **Compute mass flux out** through the three "out" faces ($x+dx, y+dy, z+dz$). 4. **Expand using Taylor series**, retaining only first-order terms. 5. **Equate net outflow + accumulation = 0** by conservation of mass. 6. **Divide by the volume** $dx\,dy\,dz$ and take the limit as the volume $\to 0$. 7. **Specialise to incompressible flow** by setting $\rho = \text{constant}$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why the Taylor Series?** > Because the velocity components $u, v, w$ and density $\rho$ **vary** from one face to the opposite face. To express their values at the "out" faces, we use the first-order Taylor expansion: > $$f(x+dx) \approx f(x) + \frac{\partial f}{\partial x} dx$$ ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > All formulas below are **exam-ready** and verified against standard fluid-mechanics references. Master this table — it covers **95% of KTU questions** on this topic. | # | Equation (Cartesian) | Physical Meaning | Validity | |---|----------------------|------------------|----------| | 1 | $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \dfrac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial(\rho v)}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial(\rho w)}{\partial z} = 0$ | 3D compressible continuity | General flow | | 2 | $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \dfrac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial(\rho v)}{\partial y} = 0$ | 2D compressible continuity | General 2D flow | | 3 | $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0$ | 3D incompressible continuity | $\rho = \text{const}$ | | 4 | $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0$ | 2D incompressible continuity | $\rho = \text{const}$ | | 5 | $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$ | Vector (divergence) form, incompressible | $\rho = \text{const}$ | | 6 | $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ | 1D integral form, incompressible | Pipe flow | | 7 | $\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$ | 1D integral form, compressible | Variable area flow | | 8 | $\psi(x,y)$: $u = \dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial y},\ v = -\dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$ | Stream function (2D incompressible) | Auto-satisfies continuity | | 9 | $\phi(x,y)$: $u = \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial x},\ v = \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}$ | Velocity potential (irrotational) | $\nabla^2 \phi = 0$ (Laplace) | > [!WARNING] > **LaTeX Tip for KTU Answer Sheets:** When writing the divergence form on paper, never write the vertical bar symbol inside tables or alignment blocks. Use `\vert` or `\mid` if absolute value must be shown, or simply use vector dot notation. ## 2.5 Stream Function and Velocity Potential — Pre-View For **2D incompressible flow**, the continuity equation $\partial u/\partial x + \partial v/\partial y = 0$ is **automatically satisfied** if we define a **stream function** $\psi(x, y)$ such that: $$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} \quad \text{and} \quad v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$$ This substitution is one of the most frequently tested techniques in KTU Module 2. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — 2D Continuity Equation (Compressible) **Setup:** Consider an infinitesimal rectangular fluid element fixed in space with sides $dx$ (along $x$) and $dy$ (along $y$), and unit depth in the $z$-direction (so that the analysis is 2D in the $xy$-plane). Let: - $\rho$ = density at the centre of the element - $u$ = $x$-component of velocity at the centre - $v$ = $y$-component of velocity at the centre ### Step 1 — Mass flux entering through the LEFT face (at $x$) The left face has area $dy \times 1 = dy$. Mass flow rate entering per unit time is: $$\dot{m}_{x,\,in} = (\rho u) \cdot dy$$ ### Step 2 — Mass flux leaving through the RIGHT face (at $x + dx$) By Taylor series, the values of $\rho u$ at the right face are: $$(\rho u)_{x+dx} = (\rho u) + \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} dx$$ Therefore, the mass leaving per unit time is: $$\dot{m}_{x,\,out} = \left[(\rho u) + \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} dx\right] dy$$ ### Step 3 — Net mass flow rate in the $x$-direction $$\Delta \dot{m}_x = \dot{m}_{x,\,out} - \dot{m}_{x,\,in} = \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} \, dx \, dy$$ ### Step 4 — Apply the same logic to the $y$-direction For the bottom face (entering at $y$) and top face (leaving at $y + dy$): $$\Delta \dot{m}_y = \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} \, dx \, dy$$ ### Step 5 — Total net mass flow OUT of the element $$\Delta \dot{m}_{total} = \left[\frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y}\right] dx \, dy$$ ### Step 6 — Rate of mass accumulation INSIDE the element The mass contained in the element at any instant is $\rho \cdot dx \cdot dy$. Its time rate of change is: $$\frac{\partial m}{\partial t} = \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} \, dx \, dy$$ ### Step 7 — Apply Conservation of Mass By Lavoisier's principle, the rate of mass accumulation inside the element equals the **net mass flow in** (which is $-\Delta \dot{m}_{total}$): $$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} \, dx \, dy = - \left[\frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y}\right] dx \, dy$$ ### Step 8 — Cancel $dx\,dy$ on both sides Rearranging, we obtain the **2D compressible continuity equation**: $$\boxed{\;\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} = 0\;}$$ ### Step 9 — Specialise to incompressible flow If $\rho$ is constant, then $\partial \rho / \partial t = 0$ and $\rho$ can be factored out of the partial derivatives: $$\boxed{\;\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0\;}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — 3D Continuity Equation (Compressible) **Setup:** The element is now a small cuboid of dimensions $dx \times dy \times dz$ fixed in space. We add a third pair of faces perpendicular to the $z$-axis. ### Step 1 — Mass flow through the pair of faces at $z$ and $z + dz$ $$\Delta \dot{m}_z = \frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} \, dx \, dy \, dz$$ ### Step 2 — Sum the contributions from all three directions The total net mass flow out of the cuboid is: $$\Delta \dot{m}_{total} = \left[\frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z}\right] dx \, dy \, dz$$ ### Step 3 — Mass accumulation in the cuboid The mass inside the cuboid is $\rho \cdot dx \, dy \, dz$, so: $$\frac{\partial m}{\partial t} = \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} \, dx \, dy \, dz$$ ### Step 4 — Conservation of mass $$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} \, dx \, dy \, dz = -\left[\frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z}\right] dx \, dy \, dz$$ ### Step 5 — Final 3D equation $$\boxed{\;\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} = 0\;}$$ ### Step 6 — Incompressible limit For $\rho = \text{constant}$: $$\boxed{\;\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0\;}$$ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Method to Solve Continuity-Based Problems > [!TIP] > **The "Three Cs" — KTU's standard approach to any continuity problem:** | C-Step | Action | What You Get | |--------|--------|--------------| | **C-1: Check** | Substitute the given $u, v$ into $\partial u/\partial x + \partial v/\partial y = 0$ | Whether the flow is **possible** | | **C-2: Compute** | Integrate $u = \partial \psi / \partial y$ w.r.t. $y$ to get $\psi$ | Stream function $\psi$ | | **C-3: Confirm** | Verify $-\partial \psi / \partial x = v$ | Consistency check | ### Worked Mini-Example Given: $u = 2x$, find $v(y)$ such that the 2D incompressible flow is possible. Using $\partial u / \partial x + \partial v / \partial y = 0$: $$\frac{\partial (2x)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 2 + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0$$ Integrating with respect to $y$: $$\frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = -2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; v = -2y + f(x)$$ The function $f(x)$ is zero if the flow is purely along the $xy$-plane with no swirl source. Hence: $$v = -2y$$ This flow pattern represents a **2D sink/source pair** — a classical KTU problem. ## 3.4 Vector / Divergence Form (Brief) Using the del operator $\nabla = \hat{i} \dfrac{\partial}{\partial x} + \hat{j} \dfrac{\partial}{\partial y} + \hat{k} \dfrac{\partial}{\partial z}$ and velocity vector $\vec{V} = u\hat{i} + v\hat{j} + w\hat{k}$: $$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$$ For an incompressible fluid this reduces to the elegant statement: $$\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$$ > [!NOTE] > In KTU, the **divergence form is optional** but frequently appears as a 2-mark sub-part. Always present it in vector form when the question says "state in vector form". # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Schematic — 2D Fluid Element with Mass Fluxes ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA["Fluid Element in 2D xy-plane
Dimensions dx by dy
Unit depth in z"] nodeB["Mass Entering
Left face: rho_u times dy"] nodeC["Mass Entering
Bottom face: rho_v times dx"] nodeD["Mass Leaving
Right face: rho_u plus d_by_dx_rho_u times dx times dy"] nodeE["Mass Leaving
Top face: rho_v plus d_by_dy_rho_v times dy times dx"] nodeF["Apply Conservation of Mass
Accumulation plus Net Outflow equals Zero"] nodeG["Expand using Taylor Series
Retain first-order terms"] nodeH["Divide by dx dy and take limit"] nodeI["Final 2D Continuity Equation
d_rho_by_dt plus d_rho_u_by_dx plus d_rho_v_by_dy equals 0"] nodeJ["Incompressible Specialisation
d_u_by_dx plus d_v_by_dy equals 0"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeA --> nodeC nodeA --> nodeD nodeA --> nodeE nodeB --> nodeF nodeC --> nodeF nodeD --> nodeF nodeE --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeG nodeG --> nodeH nodeH --> nodeI nodeI --> nodeJ style nodeA fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px style nodeI fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px style nodeJ fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — 3D Cuboid Control Volume ```mermaid flowchart LR cv["Cuboid CV
Volume dx dy dz
Fixed in space"] fluxX["X-direction fluxes
In at x, Out at x plus dx"] fluxY["Y-direction fluxes
In at y, Out at y plus dy"] fluxZ["Z-direction fluxes
In at z, Out at z plus dz"] summ["Sum all three directions
Net mass outflow"] accum["Mass accumulation
d_rho_by_dt times dx dy dz"] conseq["Conservation Principle
Accumulation plus Net Outflow equals Zero"] final["3D Continuity Equation
d_rho_by_dt plus d_rho_u_by_dx
plus d_rho_v_by_dy plus d_rho_w_by_dz equals 0"] inc["Incompressible form
d_u_by_dx plus d_v_by_dy plus d_w_by_dz equals 0"] vec["Vector form
d_rho_by_dt plus divergence of rho V equals 0"] cv --> fluxX cv --> fluxY cv --> fluxZ fluxX --> summ fluxY --> summ fluxZ --> summ summ --> conseq accum --> conseq conseq --> final final --> inc final --> vec style cv fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px style final fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px style inc fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px style vec fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Application Flow in Solving KTU Problems ```mermaid flowchart TB s1["Step 1: Read given velocity field
Identify u, v, w as functions of x, y, z, t"] s2["Step 2: Check flow type
Compressible or Incompressible"] s3["Step 3: For incompressible 2D
Apply d_u_by_dx plus d_v_by_dy equals 0"] s4["Step 4: Integrate to find missing component
OR check whether sum is zero"] s5["Step 5: For 2D incompressible
Define stream function psi"] s6["Step 6: Verify u equals d_psi_by_dy
and v equals minus d_psi_by_dx"] s7["Step 7: For irrotational flow
Define velocity potential phi"] s8["Step 8: State final answer with units
and physical interpretation"] s1 --> s2 s2 --> s3 s3 --> s4 s3 --> s5 s5 --> s6 s2 --> s7 s7 --> s8 s6 --> s8 style s1 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1 style s4 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style s6 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style s8 fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f ``` ## 4.4 Physical Picture (Text Schematic) ``` y ^ | v+dv | ^ -------+ | | | | dy | CV | | | | | -------+ v u u+du --> x <----dx----> ``` The figure shows the 2D rectangular control volume (CV) fixed in space with: - Velocity $u$ entering from the LEFT face (perpendicular to $x$) - Velocity $u + \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} dx$ leaving the RIGHT face - Velocity $v$ entering from the BOTTOM face (perpendicular to $y$) - Velocity $v + \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y} dy$ leaving the TOP face The NET imbalance (inflow − outflow) must equal the rate of mass accumulation within the CV. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Set A]** > **State the continuity equation in 3D Cartesian coordinates for a compressible fluid. Hence, write its incompressible form.** **Model Answer:** For a compressible fluid in 3D Cartesian coordinates, the continuity equation is: $$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} = 0$$ For an **incompressible fluid**, $\rho = \text{constant}$ so $\partial \rho / \partial t = 0$ and $\rho$ factors out of the partial derivatives, giving: $$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0$$ This can be written compactly as $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$, where $\vec{V} = (u, v, w)$. --- ### Question A2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** > **What is the physical significance of the continuity equation in fluid mechanics?** **Model Answer:** The continuity equation is the **mathematical expression of the law of conservation of mass** applied to a fluid in motion. Its physical significance is: 1. It guarantees that **mass is neither created nor destroyed** at any point in the flow field. 2. It ensures that the **net rate of mass inflow** into any infinitesimal fluid element equals the **rate of change of mass** within the element. 3. For an **incompressible fluid**, it implies that the **volume flow rate is conserved** along a streamline ($A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$). 4. It acts as a **kinematic constraint** that any valid velocity field must satisfy — without satisfying it, the flow is physically impossible. 5. It is one of the **fundamental governing equations** of fluid flow, used alongside the momentum (Navier–Stokes) and energy equations to solve real engineering problems in pipe flow, aerodynamics, and turbomachinery. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice ### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 2 Choice A]** > **(a)** Derive the continuity equation in **three-dimensional Cartesian coordinates** for a compressible fluid. **(7 Marks)** > **(b)** For a 2D incompressible flow, the $x$-component of velocity is $u = 3x^2 y - y^3$. Find the $y$-component $v$ such that the continuity equation is satisfied. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a): **Step 1 — Consider an infinitesimal cuboid CV** of dimensions $dx \times dy \times dz$ fixed in space, centred at point $(x, y, z)$. **Step 2 — Mass flux in the $x$-direction:** Mass entering at $x$ per unit time: $\rho u \cdot dy \cdot dz$ Mass leaving at $x + dx$ per unit time: $\left[\rho u + \dfrac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial x} dx\right] dy \, dz$ **Net mass flow in $x$-direction:** $$\Delta \dot{m}_x = \frac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial x} \, dx \, dy \, dz$$ **Step 3 — Similarly, in the $y$-direction:** $$\Delta \dot{m}_y = \frac{\partial(\rho v)}{\partial y} \, dx \, dy \, dz$$ **Step 4 — And in the $z$-direction:** $$\Delta \dot{m}_z = \frac{\partial(\rho w)}{\partial z} \, dx \, dy \, dz$$ **Step 5 — Total net mass flow out:** $$\Delta \dot{m}_{net} = \left[\frac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial(\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial(\rho w)}{\partial z}\right] dx \, dy \, dz$$ **Step 6 — Rate of mass accumulation inside the element:** $$\frac{\partial}{\partial t}(\rho \, dx \, dy \, dz) = \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} \, dx \, dy \, dz$$ **Step 7 — Apply conservation of mass:** $$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} \, dx \, dy \, dz + \left[\frac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial(\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial(\rho w)}{\partial z}\right] dx \, dy \, dz = 0$$ **Step 8 — Cancel $dx \, dy \, dz$ and write the final equation:** $$\boxed{\;\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} = 0\;}$$ **Step 9 (optional) — Incompressible limit:** If $\rho = \text{constant}$: $$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0$$ --- #### Model Solution for (b): **Given:** $u = 3x^2 y - y^3$ (2D incompressible flow, so $w = 0$). **Step 1 — Compute $\partial u / \partial x$:** $$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}(3x^2 y - y^3) = 6xy$$ **Step 2 — Apply 2D incompressible continuity:** $$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0$$ $$6xy + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = -6xy$$ **Step 3 — Integrate with respect to $y$:** $$v = \int -6xy \, dy = -6x \cdot \frac{y^2}{2} + f(x) = -3xy^2 + f(x)$$ **Step 4 — Apply boundary condition:** Assuming the flow has no $x$-dependent swirl (no source/sink in the $x$-direction), $f(x) = 0$. **Final Answer:** $$\boxed{\;v = -3xy^2\;}$$ **Verification:** $\partial v / \partial y = -6xy$ and $\partial u / \partial x + \partial v / \partial y = 6xy - 6xy = 0$ ✓ --- ### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 2 Choice B]** > **(a)** Derive the continuity equation in **two-dimensional Cartesian coordinates** for a compressible fluid. **(7 Marks)** > **(b)** A 2D flow field has velocity components $u = x^2 y$ and $v = -x y^2$. Verify whether the flow satisfies the continuity equation. If yes, obtain the stream function $\psi(x, y)$. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a): **Step 1 — Consider an infinitesimal rectangular CV** with sides $dx$ and $dy$, and unit depth in $z$. The CV is fixed in space at the origin of coordinates. **Step 2 — Mass flux in $x$-direction:** - Mass entering the LEFT face (area $dy$): $(\rho u) \, dy$ - Mass leaving the RIGHT face (area $dy$): $\left[(\rho u) + \dfrac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial x} dx\right] dy$ - Net outflow in $x$: $\dfrac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial x} dx \, dy$ **Step 3 — Mass flux in $y$-direction:** - Mass entering the BOTTOM face (area $dx$): $(\rho v) \, dx$ - Mass leaving the TOP face (area $dx$): $\left[(\rho v) + \dfrac{\partial(\rho v)}{\partial y} dy\right] dx$ - Net outflow in $y$: $\dfrac{\partial(\rho v)}{\partial y} dx \, dy$ **Step 4 — Total net mass flow out:** $$\Delta \dot{m}_{net} = \left[\frac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial(\rho v)}{\partial y}\right] dx \, dy$$ **Step 5 — Rate of mass accumulation:** $$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} \, dx \, dy$$ **Step 6 — Conservation of mass (Accumulation + Net Outflow = 0):** $$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} \, dx \, dy + \left[\frac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial(\rho v)}{\partial y}\right] dx \, dy = 0$$ **Step 7 — Final 2D equation:** $$\boxed{\;\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} = 0\;}$$ **Step 8 — Incompressible specialisation ($\rho$ = const):** $$\boxed{\;\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0\;}$$ --- #### Model Solution for (b): **Given:** $u = x^2 y$, $v = -x y^2$ **Step 1 — Compute $\partial u / \partial x$:** $$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}(x^2 y) = 2xy$$ **Step 2 — Compute $\partial v / \partial y$:** $$\frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial}{\partial y}(-x y^2) = -2xy$$ **Step 3 — Check the continuity equation:** $$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 2xy - 2xy = 0 \;\;\checkmark$$ **Conclusion:** The flow **satisfies continuity** and is a valid incompressible 2D flow. **Step 4 — Find the stream function $\psi$:** Using the definitions: $$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} \quad \text{and} \quad v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$$ From the first equation: $$\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} = x^2 y \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \psi = \int x^2 y \, dy = x^2 \cdot \frac{y^2}{2} + g(x) = \frac{x^2 y^2}{2} + g(x)$$ **Step 5 — Differentiate $\psi$ w.r.t. $x$ and match with $-v$:** $$-\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} = -\left[xy^2 + g'(x)\right] = -xy^2 - g'(x)$$ This must equal $v = -xy^2$. Hence: $$-xy^2 - g'(x) = -xy^2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; g'(x) = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; g(x) = \text{constant}$$ **Final Stream Function:** $$\boxed{\;\psi(x, y) = \frac{x^2 y^2}{2} + C\;}$$ (where $C$ is an arbitrary constant, often taken as zero). **Verification:** The streamline pattern of this flow is a family of **closed curves** $x^2 y^2 = \text{constant}$, representing a **2D stagnation-type flow**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Sign error in stream function:** Writing $v = +\partial \psi / \partial x$ instead of $v = -\partial \psi / \partial x$ costs **1 full mark** in every Part-B question. > 2. **Forgetting the chain rule in 3D:** When $\rho$ depends on $x, y, z, t$, students often write $\rho \, \partial u / \partial x$ instead of $\partial(\rho u)/\partial x$. The product rule **must be applied**: $\partial(\rho u)/\partial x = \rho \partial u/\partial x + u \partial \rho/\partial x$. Missing this loses up to **2 marks**. > 3. **Mixing up compressible and incompressible forms:** In a 2D incompressible question, students sometimes write $\partial \rho/\partial t + \cdots = 0$. This is wrong — for incompressible flow the density term **vanishes completely**. > 4. **Skipping the Taylor series justification:** Examiners expect at least one line saying "by Taylor series expansion" before writing $(\rho u) + \partial(\rho u)/\partial x \cdot dx$. Without it, **1 mark** is deducted. > 5. **Not cancelling $dx\,dy$ or $dx\,dy\,dz$:** Always divide by the elemental volume at the final step. Leaving the equation with differentials still present is considered incomplete. > 6. **Forgetting units in the final answer for $v$:** Write $v = -3xy^2$ m/s with units; numerical answers without units lose **0.5 mark** (a common cut in the 7-mark sub-parts). > 7. **Missing the verification step:** Always verify the final stream function by checking both $u = \partial \psi/\partial y$ and $v = -\partial \psi/\partial x$. This is a free **1-mark** that most students miss. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin This in Your Notebook!)** ### Core Equations to Memorise - **3D Compressible:** $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \dfrac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial(\rho v)}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial(\rho w)}{\partial z} = 0$ - **2D Compressible:** $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \dfrac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial(\rho v)}{\partial y} = 0$ - **3D Incompressible:** $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0$ - **2D Incompressible:** $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0$ - **Vector form:** $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$ (incompressible) ### Key Concepts - The continuity equation is the **conservation of mass** expressed for a fluid. - It is a **kinematic** equation — it does not depend on the forces causing the flow. - An infinitesimal **rectangular (2D)** or **cuboidal (3D)** control volume is the standard tool. - **Taylor series** of first order is used to evaluate the variables at the opposite face. - The product rule **must** be applied when expanding $\partial(\rho u)/\partial x$. - For **incompressible flow**, density drops out and only the velocity divergence survives. - In 2D incompressible flow, the **stream function** $\psi$ automatically satisfies continuity if defined as $u = \partial \psi/\partial y$ and $v = -\partial \psi/\partial x$. ### Quick Problem-Solving Recipe 1. Identify whether the flow is 2D or 3D, compressible or incompressible. 2. Write the appropriate continuity equation. 3. Substitute the given velocity components. 4. Either verify the sum is zero (checking flow possibility) OR integrate to find the missing component. 5. For stream-function problems, integrate $u$ w.r.t. $y$, then match with $-v$ to find the unknown function of $x$. ### Examiner's Favourite Traps - Forgetting chain rule on $\rho u$, $\rho v$, $\rho w$ terms. - Sign error in stream function definition. - Confusing $\partial(\rho u)/\partial x$ with $\rho \partial u/\partial x$. - Writing continuity for steady flow only — the equation is valid for both steady and unsteady. - Forgetting to verify the answer (a free 1 mark most students leave on the table). ### Real-World Applications to Quote in Answers - Water distribution networks (Bernoulli + Continuity). - Aircraft wing design (mass conservation over airfoil). - Venturi meter and orifice meter (use 1D integral form of continuity). - CFD solver validation (continuity is a built-in check). - Hydraulic jump analysis (continuity across the jump). > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "MAYA":** > **M**ass in − **A**ccumulation = **Y**ield (out) + **A**dded (sources). For a source-free flow, **M − A = Y**, i.e., *Net Mass In = Rate of Accumulation*.

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Velocity and Acceleration in Fluid Kinematics** refer to the spatial and temporal rates of change of the position and velocity of fluid particles, described mathematically as vector fields using the **Eulerian approach** and computed via the **Material (Substantial) Derivative**.

In a flowing fluid, every point in space at any instant has a **velocity vector** $\vec{V} = u\hat{i} + v\hat{j} + w\hat{k}$ assigned to it, and this velocity generally varies with both position and time. The **acceleration** of a fluid particle is the rate of change of its velocity as it moves through the flow field, and it is obtained using the **Material (Substantial) Derivative** $\dfrac{D}{Dt}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Definition (Board-Standard):**
> The acceleration of a fluid particle is the time rate of change of velocity **following the fluid particle** (not at a fixed point). Mathematically:
> $$\vec{a} = \dfrac{D\vec{V}}{Dt}$$

---

## 1.2 Two Ways to Describe Fluid Motion

| Approach | Description | Observer |
|----------|-------------|----------|
| **Lagrangian** | Tracks an **individual fluid particle** as it moves | Follows the particle (like tagging a fish) |
| **Eulerian** | Observes velocity at a **fixed point in space** as particles pass | Stands on a bridge and watches the river |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** The Eulerian approach is the standard in Fluid Mechanics because it is mathematically tractable and aligns with experimental measurements (e.g., Pitot tube, LDA, PIV) at a fixed location.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The River and the Boat"

Imagine standing on a bridge over a river:

- **Lagrangian (Particle Tracking):** Drop a leaf into the river and follow it. You record how its speed and direction change as it moves downstream. The acceleration you measure is the **total (material) acceleration** of that specific leaf.
- **Eulerian (Field Observation):** Stand still on the bridge. At the instant a leaf passes under you, record its velocity. A moment later, when a *different* leaf passes the same spot (which may be moving faster because the river widens below), record its velocity. The *change* between these two observations is **not** the acceleration of one particle — it is a mix of *time change* and *space change* of the velocity field.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Tip:** The Eulerian description gives you a "weather map" of velocities across the field, while the Lagrangian description gives you the "biography" of a single fluid particle.

---

## 1.4 Two Contributions to Acceleration

When we move from the Eulerian field description to the particle acceleration, two distinct physical effects combine:

1. **Local (Temporal / Unsteady) Acceleration** $\dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t}$ — the change in velocity *at a fixed location* with time (e.g., a tap being opened/closed gradually).
2. **Convective (Advective / Spatial) Acceleration** $(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$ — the change in velocity *as a particle moves* from a region of low velocity to a region of high velocity (e.g., water accelerating through a contracting pipe).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Misconception:** Convective acceleration can be **non-zero even in steady flow**! A nozzle with steady mass flow has no time variation, but fluid particles speed up as they move into the constriction.

---

## 1.5 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Acceleration units in SI:** $\text{m/s}^2$
- **Velocity units in SI:** $\text{m/s}$
- **Standard gravitational acceleration:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **Reference frame:** Inertial (Newtonian) frame is assumed unless stated otherwise.
- **Vector operator (del):** $\nabla = \hat{i}\dfrac{\partial}{\partial x} + \hat{j}\dfrac{\partial}{\partial y} + \hat{k}\dfrac{\partial}{\partial z}$

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Material (Substantial) Derivative — Operational Logic

The Material Derivative converts an **Eulerian field quantity** into the **rate of change following a fluid particle**. It is the central operator of continuum kinematics.

**Operational Steps:**

1. **Start with a scalar field** $\phi(x,y,z,t)$ (e.g., temperature, pressure, density) or a vector field $\vec{V}(x,y,z,t)$.
2. **Particle displacement** in time $dt$ is $d\vec{r} = \vec{V}\,dt$.
3. **Total differential** of $\phi$ along the particle path is $d\phi = \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial t}dt + \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}dx + \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}dy + \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial z}dz$.
4. **Divide by $dt$** and substitute $dx/dt = u$, $dy/dt = v$, $dz/dt = w$:

$$\dfrac{D\phi}{Dt} = \underbrace{\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial t}}_{\text{Local}} + \underbrace{\left(u\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} + v\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} + w\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial z}\right)}_{\text{Convective}}$$

5. **Why this matters:** This is the operator that appears in **every transport equation** in fluid mechanics — continuity (mass conservation), Navier–Stokes (momentum), energy equation, and species transport.

---

## 2.2 Acceleration Field — Vector Form

Applying the Material Derivative to the velocity vector $\vec{V} = u\hat{i} + v\hat{j} + w\hat{k}$:

$$\vec{a} = \dfrac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam-Boilerplate:** KTU examiners expect students to clearly identify and label the **local** and **convective** terms separately in any derivation.

---

## 2.3 Component Form in Cartesian Coordinates

$$a_x = \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial z}$$

$$a_y = \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial t} + u\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x} + v\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y} + w\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial z}$$

$$a_z = \dfrac{\partial w}{\partial t} + u\dfrac{\partial w}{\partial x} + v\dfrac{\partial w}{\partial y} + w\dfrac{\partial w}{\partial z}$$

---

## 2.4 Component Form in Cylindrical (Polar) Coordinates $(r, \theta, z)$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Hot Topic:** Cylindrical coordinates appear frequently in problems involving **tangential flow** (e.g., rotating fluids, vortex flow, centrifugal pumps).

$$a_r = \dfrac{\partial v_r}{\partial t} + v_r\dfrac{\partial v_r}{\partial r} + \dfrac{v_\theta}{r}\dfrac{\partial v_r}{\partial \theta} + v_z\dfrac{\partial v_r}{\partial z} - \dfrac{v_\theta^2}{r}$$

$$a_\theta = \dfrac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial t} + v_r\dfrac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial r} + \dfrac{v_\theta}{r}\dfrac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial \theta} + v_z\dfrac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial z} + \dfrac{v_r v_\theta}{r}$$

$$a_z = \dfrac{\partial v_z}{\partial t} + v_r\dfrac{\partial v_z}{\partial r} + \dfrac{v_\theta}{r}\dfrac{\partial v_z}{\partial \theta} + v_z\dfrac{\partial v_z}{\partial z}$$

The $\dfrac{v_\theta^2}{r}$ and $\dfrac{v_r v_\theta}{r}$ terms arise purely from the **curvature of the coordinate system** (centripetal and Coriolis-type effects).

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Velocity vector (Cartesian) | $\vec{V} = u\hat{i} + v\hat{j} + w\hat{k}$ | $\text{m/s}$ | $u, v, w$ are components |
| 2 | Material derivative (scalar) | $\dfrac{D\phi}{Dt} = \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} + (\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)\phi$ | $\text{(unit of }\phi\text{)}/\text{s}$ | General transport form |
| 3 | Acceleration vector | $\vec{a} = \dfrac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)\vec{V}$ | $\text{m/s}^2$ | Main KTU formula |
| 4 | Local acceleration | $\dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t}$ | $\text{m/s}^2$ | Zero in steady flow |
| 5 | Convective acceleration | $(\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)\vec{V}$ | $\text{m/s}^2$ | Zero in uniform flow |
| 6 | Cartesian $a_x$ | $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial z}$ | $\text{m/s}^2$ | Expand $\dfrac{Du}{Dt}$ |
| 7 | Cylindrical $a_r$ | $\dfrac{\partial v_r}{\partial t} + v_r\dfrac{\partial v_r}{\partial r} + \dfrac{v_\theta}{r}\dfrac{\partial v_r}{\partial \theta} + v_z\dfrac{\partial v_r}{\partial z} - \dfrac{v_\theta^2}{r}$ | $\text{m/s}^2$ | Contains $-\dfrac{v_\theta^2}{r}$ (centripetal) |
| 8 | Streamline equation | $\dfrac{dx}{u} = \dfrac{dy}{v} = \dfrac{dz}{w}$ | — | Family of curves tangent to $\vec{V}$ |
| 9 | Pathline ODE | $\dfrac{dx}{u(x,y,z,t)} = \dfrac{dy}{v} = \dfrac{dz}{w} = dt$ | — | Trajectory of a single particle |
| 10 | Steady-flow check | $\dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} = 0$ | — | All time partials vanish |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Aerodynamics:** Acceleration fields around airfoils determine pressure distribution and lift.
> - **Turbomachinery:** Convective acceleration in impeller passages sets up Euler's turbine equation.
> - **Open-channel hydraulics:** Acceleration components drive the Saint-Venant equations for unsteady flow.
> - **Weather prediction:** Material derivative of temperature, pressure, and velocity is the basis of atmospheric dynamics.

---

## 2.6 Streamline, Pathline, Streakline — Three KTU Essentials

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All three are *coincident* in steady flow**, but differ in unsteady flow — this is a frequently tested KTU concept.

- **Streamline:** Curve everywhere tangent to the instantaneous velocity vector. Mathematically, $\dfrac{dx}{u} = \dfrac{dy}{v} = \dfrac{dz}{w}$ at a fixed instant.
- **Pathline:** Trajectory of a single fluid particle over time (Lagrangian concept).
- **Streakline:** Locus of all particles that have *previously* passed through a given fixed point (e.g., the dye trace from a continuously injecting nozzle).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Eulerian Field to Material Derivative

We want to find the rate of change of a scalar $\phi$ *following a fluid particle*.

**Step 1:** A fluid particle that is at position $(x, y, z)$ at time $t$ moves to $(x + dx, y + dy, z + dz)$ at time $t + dt$, where:

$$dx = u\,dt, \quad dy = v\,dt, \quad dz = w\,dt$$

**Step 2:** The total change in $\phi$ for that particle is the total differential (chain rule for a function of 4 independent variables):

$$d\phi = \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial t}dt + \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}dx + \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}dy + \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial z}dz$$

**Step 3:** Divide both sides by $dt$:

$$\dfrac{d\phi}{dt} = \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} + \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}\dfrac{dx}{dt} + \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}\dfrac{dy}{dt} + \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial z}\dfrac{dz}{dt}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $dx/dt = u$, $dy/dt = v$, $dz/dt = w$:

$$\dfrac{d\phi}{dt} = \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} + u\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} + v\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} + w\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial z}$$

**Step 5:** Recognize the second group as the dot product $\vec{V}\cdot\nabla\phi$ and rename $\dfrac{d\phi}{dt} \rightarrow \dfrac{D\phi}{Dt}$ to emphasize that we are *following the particle*:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{D\phi}{Dt} = \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} + (\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)\phi}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The notation change from $d/D$ signals that this is a *substantial* derivative, not an ordinary time derivative at a stationary point.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Acceleration Vector Components in Cartesian Coordinates

**Step 1:** Apply the material derivative to each velocity component. For the $x$-component $u(x,y,z,t)$:

$$\dfrac{Du}{Dt} = \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial z}$$

**Step 2:** Repeat for $v$ and $w$:

$$\dfrac{Dv}{Dt} = \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial t} + u\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x} + v\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y} + w\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial z}$$

$$\dfrac{Dw}{Dt} = \dfrac{\partial w}{\partial t} + u\dfrac{\partial w}{\partial x} + v\dfrac{\partial w}{\partial y} + w\dfrac{\partial w}{\partial z}$$

**Step 3:** Assemble the acceleration vector:

$$\vec{a} = a_x\hat{i} + a_y\hat{j} + a_z\hat{k} = \dfrac{Du}{Dt}\hat{i} + \dfrac{Dv}{Dt}\hat{j} + \dfrac{Dw}{Dt}\hat{k}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The convective terms $u\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x}$, etc. carry the velocity *squared* dimensionally, which is consistent with acceleration $\text{(m/s)}/\text{s} = \text{m/s}^2$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Acceleration in Cylindrical Coordinates

Cylindrical coordinates $(r, \theta, z)$ have a unit basis $(\hat{e}_r, \hat{e}_\theta, \hat{e}_z)$ that **rotates with position**. This introduces extra terms.

**Step 1:** Write velocity in cylindrical components:

$$\vec{V} = v_r\hat{e}_r + v_\theta\hat{e}_\theta + v_z\hat{e}_z$$

**Step 2:** The derivatives of the basis vectors are non-zero:

$$\dfrac{\partial \hat{e}_r}{\partial \theta} = \hat{e}_\theta, \quad \dfrac{\partial \hat{e}_\theta}{\partial \theta} = -\hat{e}_r$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate $\vec{V}$ with respect to time while following a particle. After applying the chain rule and including the basis-derivative contributions:

$$a_r = \dfrac{\partial v_r}{\partial t} + v_r\dfrac{\partial v_r}{\partial r} + \dfrac{v_\theta}{r}\dfrac{\partial v_r}{\partial \theta} + v_z\dfrac{\partial v_r}{\partial z} - \dfrac{v_\theta^2}{r}$$

$$a_\theta = \dfrac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial t} + v_r\dfrac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial r} + \dfrac{v_\theta}{r}\dfrac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial \theta} + v_z\dfrac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial z} + \dfrac{v_r v_\theta}{r}$$

$$a_z = \dfrac{\partial v_z}{\partial t} + v_r\dfrac{\partial v_z}{\partial r} + \dfrac{v_\theta}{r}\dfrac{\partial v_z}{\partial \theta} + v_z\dfrac{\partial v_z}{\partial z}$$

> **Conversion Logic of the extra terms:** The $-\dfrac{v_\theta^2}{r}$ term is the **centripetal acceleration** directed inward (toward the axis) when fluid moves tangentially. The $+\dfrac{v_r v_\theta}{r}$ term is a **Coriolis-type term** that appears when a particle moves radially while also rotating.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Streamline Equation in 2D Steady Flow

A streamline is everywhere tangent to the velocity field. In 2D, the velocity is $\vec{V} = u\hat{i} + v\hat{j}$.

**Step 1:** A differential element $d\vec{r} = dx\,\hat{i} + dy\,\hat{j}$ along the streamline is parallel to $\vec{V}$, so:

$$d\vec{r} \times \vec{V} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad u\,dy - v\,dx = 0$$

**Step 2:** Rearranging into the standard KTU form:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{dx}{u} = \dfrac{dy}{v}}$$

**Step 3:** For 3D flow, extend to:

$$\dfrac{dx}{u} = \dfrac{dy}{v} = \dfrac{dz}{w}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This is *not* an ODE in time — it is a family of curves in space at a *single instant*. The time $t$ is held fixed.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Pathline Equation in Unsteady Flow

The pathline tracks one particle. Its position is $(x_p(t), y_p(t), z_p(t))$.

**Step 1:** The particle's velocity equals the field velocity at its current location:

$$\dfrac{dx_p}{dt} = u(x_p, y_p, z_p, t), \quad \dfrac{dy_p}{dt} = v(\cdots), \quad \dfrac{dz_p}{dt} = w(\cdots)$$

**Step 2:** Combine into a single parametric relation:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{dx_p}{u} = \dfrac{dy_p}{v} = \dfrac{dz_p}{w} = dt}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Unlike the streamline equation, this **includes time** on the RHS. Solving requires initial conditions $(x_p(0), y_p(0), z_p(0))$.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem (KTU Pattern):** A 2D velocity field is given by $\vec{V} = (3x)\hat{i} + (2t)\hat{j}$ m/s, where $x$ is in meters and $t$ in seconds. Find the acceleration of a fluid particle at the point $(x, y) = (2, 1)$ m at $t = 3$ s.

**Step 1: Identify components.**
$u = 3x$, $v = 2t$.

**Step 2: Local acceleration.**

$$a_{x,\text{local}} = \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t} = 0 \quad \text{(no } t \text{ in } u\text{)}$$

$$a_{y,\text{local}} = \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial t} = 2 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 3: Convective acceleration in $x$.**

$$a_{x,\text{conv}} = u\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} = (3x)(3) + (2t)(0) = 9x$$

At $x = 2$: $a_{x,\text{conv}} = 9(2) = 18 \text{ m/s}^2$.

**Step 4: Convective acceleration in $y$.**

$$a_{y,\text{conv}} = u\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x} + v\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y} = (3x)(0) + (2t)(0) = 0$$

**Step 5: Total acceleration at $(2, 1)$, $t = 3$.**

$$a_x = 0 + 18 = 18 \text{ m/s}^2$$

$$a_y = 2 + 0 = 2 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 6: Magnitude and direction.**

$$\vert \vec{a} \vert = \sqrt{18^2 + 2^2} = \sqrt{328} \approx 18.11 \text{ m/s}^2$$

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{a_y}{a_x}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{2}{18}\right) \approx 6.34^\circ$$

> **Answer:** $\vec{a} = 18\hat{i} + 2\hat{j}$ m/s², with magnitude $\approx 18.11$ m/s² directed about $6.34^\circ$ above the $x$-axis.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Lagrangian vs Eulerian — Observation Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FLUID FLOW DESCRIPTION] --> B{Choose Observation Strategy}
    B --> C[LAGRANGIAN APPROACH]
    B --> D[EULERIAN APPROACH]

    C --> C1[Tag a single fluid particle]
    C1 --> C2[Track its position x_p t, y_p t, z_p t over time]
    C2 --> C3[Output: PATHLINE of the particle]
    C3 --> C4[Used in: Particle Image Velocimetry, Molecular Dynamics]

    D --> D1[Fix a point x, y, z in space]
    D1 --> D2[Record velocity V of whatever particle passes by at time t]
    D2 --> D3[Output: VELOCITY FIELD V x y z t]
    D3 --> D4[Used in: Pitot tube, LDA, CFD solvers]
```

## 4.2 Anatomy of the Material Derivative

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[D phi by Dt] --> B[LOCAL TERM]
    A --> C[CONVECTIVE TERM]

    B --> B1[Partial phi by Partial t]
    B1 --> B2[Time change at fixed point]
    B2 --> B3[Zero in steady flow]

    C --> C1[V dot nabla phi]
    C1 --> C2[u Partial phi by Partial x + v Partial phi by Partial y + w Partial phi by Partial z]
    C2 --> C3[Change as particle moves through space]
    C3 --> C4[Zero in spatially uniform flow]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Acceleration Field Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[INPUT: Velocity Field V x y z t] --> S1[Step 1: Differentiate with respect to time]
    S1 --> S2[Local acceleration component Partial V by Partial t]
    IN --> S3[Step 2: Compute spatial gradients of V]
    S3 --> S4[Step 3: Form dyadic V dot nabla acting on V]
    S4 --> S5[Convective acceleration component]
    S2 --> S6[Add local and convective terms]
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> OUT[OUTPUT: Particle acceleration a = D V by D t in m by s squared]
```

## 4.4 Classification of Fluid Flow — Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FLOW CLASSIFICATION] --> B{Time dependence?}
    B -->|Yes| C[UNSTEADY: Partial V by Partial t is non zero]
    B -->|No| D[STEADY: Partial V by Partial t is zero]

    A --> E{Spatial dependence of V?}
    E -->|Yes| F[NON UNIFORM: convective acceleration may be non zero]
    E -->|No| G[UNIFORM: convective acceleration is zero]

    C --> H[Both local and convective may be non zero]
    D --> F2[Only convective may be non zero]
    F --> H2[Only convective may be non zero in steady non uniform]
    G --> H3[Uniform steady: a = 0 everywhere]
```
