# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Define the material derivative. Why is it required in fluid mechanics instead of the ordinary time derivative?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 marks):** The material derivative (also called substantial or total derivative) of a scalar field $\phi(x,y,z,t)$ is the rate of change of $\phi$ **following a fluid particle** as it moves through space and time:
> $$\dfrac{D\phi}{Dt} = \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} + u\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} + v\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} + w\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial z}$$
> Equivalently, in vector form: $\dfrac{D\phi}{Dt} = \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} + (\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)\phi$.

> **Justification (1 mark):** In fluid mechanics, the quantities of interest (velocity, temperature, pressure, density) are field variables that vary in both space and time. A fluid *particle* moves, so we must capture the change due to **time variation at a point** *plus* the **change as the particle relocates** through a spatially varying field. The ordinary time derivative $\partial/\partial t$ only accounts for the first effect and is therefore insufficient.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Distinguish between local and convective acceleration. Can convective acceleration be non-zero in a steady flow? Illustrate with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Local acceleration** is the rate of change of velocity at a *fixed point in space* with respect to time:
> $$\vec{a}_{\text{local}} = \dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t}$$
> It is zero in steady flow.

> **Convective acceleration** is the rate of change of velocity due to the *spatial translation* of the fluid particle from one location to another where the velocity is different:
> $$\vec{a}_{\text{conv}} = (\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)\vec{V}$$

> **Yes (1 mark),** convective acceleration can be non-zero in steady flow. **Example:** In a steadily running nozzle (converging duct), the flow is steady ($\partial \vec{V}/\partial t = 0$), but as a fluid particle moves from a wider section (low velocity) to a narrower section (high velocity), its speed increases — this is purely convective acceleration.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Common Pitfalls):**
> 1. **Forgetting to write the convective term in full** — students often write only the first term $u\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x}$ and lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up streamline and pathline equations** — streamlines have *no* $dt$ on the RHS; pathlines do.
> 3. **In cylindrical coordinates, omitting the centripetal term** $-\dfrac{v_\theta^2}{r}$ in $a_r$ — this is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
> 4. **Not stating units** in the final answer — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units consistently.
> 5. **Failing to mention steady-flow simplification** $\partial/\partial t = 0$ when the problem says "steady".

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Define the material derivative of a velocity field. Derive expressions for the components of acceleration of a fluid particle in **Cartesian coordinates**, clearly identifying local and convective parts. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The velocity field of a flow is given by $\vec{V} = (2x + 1)\hat{i} + (3y - t)\hat{j} + (z^2)\hat{k}$ m/s. Find the acceleration of a fluid particle at the point $(1, 2, 1)$ m at time $t = 1$ s. Also compute the magnitude of the acceleration. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

> **Step 1 (1 mark):** State the material derivative definition.
> The material derivative of the velocity vector gives the acceleration of a fluid particle:
> $$\vec{a} = \dfrac{D\vec{V}}{Dt}$$

> **Step 2 (2 marks):** Derive the general scalar form.
> Following a particle, total change in any field $\phi$ in time $dt$ is:
> $$d\phi = \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial t}dt + \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}dx + \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}dy + \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial z}dz$$
> Divide by $dt$, substitute $dx/dt = u$, etc.:
> $$\dfrac{D\phi}{Dt} = \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} + u\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} + v\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} + w\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial z}$$

> **Step 3 (2 marks):** Apply to each velocity component.
> For $u(x,y,z,t)$:
> $$a_x = \dfrac{Du}{Dt} = \underbrace{\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t}}_{\text{Local}} + \underbrace{u\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial z}}_{\text{Convective}}$$
> Similarly for $a_y$ (replace $u \to v$) and $a_z$ (replace $u \to w$).

> **Step 4 (2 marks):** Final compact vector form and physical interpretation.
> $$\vec{a} = \dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)\vec{V}$$
> The first term represents *unsteadiness* (time variation at a point); the second represents *spatial non-uniformity* (change as the particle moves).

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

> **Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify velocity components.
> $u = 2x + 1$, $v = 3y - t$, $w = z^2$.

> **Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute local acceleration components.
> $$a_{x,\text{local}} = \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t} = 0$$
> $$a_{y,\text{local}} = \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial t} = -1 \text{ m/s}^2$$
> $$a_{z,\text{local}} = \dfrac{\partial w}{\partial t} = 0$$

> **Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute convective acceleration components.
> $a_{x,\text{conv}} = u\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial z} = (2x+1)(2) + (3y-t)(0) + z^2(0) = 2(2x+1)$.
> At $(1,2,1)$: $a_{x,\text{conv}} = 2(3) = 6$ m/s².
> $a_{y,\text{conv}} = u\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x} + v\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y} + w\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial z} = 0 + (3y-t)(3) + 0 = 3(3y-t)$.
> At $(1,2,1)$, $t=1$: $a_{y,\text{conv}} = 3(6-1) = 15$ m/s².
> $a_{z,\text{conv}} = u\dfrac{\partial w}{\partial x} + v\dfrac{\partial w}{\partial y} + w\dfrac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0 + 0 + z^2(2z) = 2z^3$.
> At $(1,2,1)$: $a_{z,\text{conv}} = 2(1)^3 = 2$ m/s².

> **Step 4 (1 mark):** Total acceleration components.
> $a_x = 0 + 6 = 6$ m/s², $a_y = -1 + 15 = 14$ m/s², $a_z = 0 + 2 = 2$ m/s².

> **Step 5 (1 mark):** Magnitude.
> $$\vert \vec{a} \vert = \sqrt{6^2 + 14^2 + 2^2} = \sqrt{36 + 196 + 4} = \sqrt{236} \approx 15.36 \text{ m/s}^2$$

> **Final Answer:** $\vec{a} = 6\hat{i} + 14\hat{j} + 2\hat{k}$ m/s², $\vert \vec{a} \vert \approx 15.36$ m/s².

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – Sample, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Derive the expressions for the components of acceleration in **cylindrical (polar) coordinates** $(r, \theta, z)$. Clearly bring out the origin of the centripetal term. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A purely tangential flow in cylindrical coordinates has velocity $\vec{V} = \dfrac{K}{r}\hat{e}_\theta$, where $K$ is a constant (this is a free vortex). Find the acceleration field. State whether the flow is steady, and identify which component of acceleration is non-zero. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

> **Step 1 (1 mark):** Define the cylindrical basis and its derivatives.
> The basis vectors $(\hat{e}_r, \hat{e}_\theta, \hat{e}_z)$ satisfy:
> $$\dfrac{\partial \hat{e}_r}{\partial \theta} = \hat{e}_\theta, \quad \dfrac{\partial \hat{e}_\theta}{\partial \theta} = -\hat{e}_r, \quad \dfrac{\partial \hat{e}_z}{\partial \theta} = 0$$

> **Step 2 (2 marks):** Write the velocity in cylindrical components:
> $$\vec{V} = v_r\hat{e}_r + v_\theta\hat{e}_\theta + v_z\hat{e}_z$$

> **Step 3 (3 marks):** Differentiate $\vec{V}$ following a fluid particle, applying the chain rule *and* accounting for the rotation of the basis:
> $$\dfrac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \left(\dfrac{\partial v_r}{\partial t} + v_r\dfrac{\partial v_r}{\partial r} + \dfrac{v_\theta}{r}\dfrac{\partial v_r}{\partial \theta} + v_z\dfrac{\partial v_r}{\partial z} - \dfrac{v_\theta^2}{r}\right)\hat{e}_r$$
> $$+ \left(\dfrac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial t} + v_r\dfrac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial r} + \dfrac{v_\theta}{r}\dfrac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial \theta} + v_z\dfrac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial z} + \dfrac{v_r v_\theta}{r}\right)\hat{e}_\theta$$
> $$+ \left(\dfrac{\partial v_z}{\partial t} + v_r\dfrac{\partial v_z}{\partial r} + \dfrac{v_\theta}{r}\dfrac{\partial v_z}{\partial \theta} + v_z\dfrac{\partial v_z}{\partial z}\right)\hat{e}_z$$

> **Step 4 (1 mark):** Origin of the centripetal term.
> The $-\dfrac{v_\theta^2}{r}$ term in $a_r$ arises because the $\hat{e}_\theta$ basis vector rotates with $\theta$; when differentiated, $\hat{e}_\theta$ gains a $-\hat{e}_r$ component, yielding $-\dfrac{v_\theta^2}{r}\hat{e}_r$. This represents the centripetal acceleration directed radially inward.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

> **Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify velocity components.
> $v_r = 0$, $v_\theta = \dfrac{K}{r}$, $v_z = 0$.

> **Step 2 (1 mark):** Check steadiness.
> $K$ is constant, no explicit $t$ dependence $\Rightarrow$ **steady flow** ($\partial/\partial t = 0$ for all terms).

> **Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute $a_r$.
> All terms vanish *except* the centripetal term:
> $$a_r = -\dfrac{v_\theta^2}{r} = -\dfrac{(K/r)^2}{r} = -\dfrac{K^2}{r^3}$$
> (Negative sign indicates radially *inward*.)

> **Step 4 (2 marks):** Compute $a_\theta$ and $a_z$.
> $$a_\theta = v_r\dfrac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial r} + \dfrac{v_\theta}{r}\dfrac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial \theta} + \dfrac{v_r v_\theta}{r} = 0 + 0 + 0 = 0$$
> $$a_z = 0$$

> **Step 5 (1 mark):** Concluding statement.
> The acceleration is purely radial and directed inward (centripetal):
> $$\vec{a} = -\dfrac{K^2}{r^3}\hat{e}_r \quad \text{m/s}^2$$
> Although the flow is **steady**, the acceleration is **non-zero** — a classic demonstration that convective acceleration can exist without local acceleration.

> **Final Answer:** $\vec{a} = -\dfrac{K^2}{r^3}\hat{e}_r$ m/s², directed radially inward.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Quick-Fire Recap:**

- **Material Derivative:** $\dfrac{D}{Dt} = \dfrac{\partial}{\partial t} + (\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)$ — converts an Eulerian field rate into a particle rate.
- **Acceleration Vector:** $\vec{a} = \dfrac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \underbrace{\dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t}}_{\text{Local}} + \underbrace{(\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)\vec{V}}_{\text{Convective}}$.
- **Steady flow** $\Rightarrow \partial \vec{V}/\partial t = 0$ but convective acceleration may still be non-zero (e.g., nozzle, free vortex).
- **Uniform flow** $\Rightarrow$ convective acceleration $= 0$ but local acceleration may be non-zero (e.g., flow startup).
- **Streamline:** $\dfrac{dx}{u} = \dfrac{dy}{v} = \dfrac{dz}{w}$ (no $dt$ on RHS — *fixed instant*).
- **Pathline:** $\dfrac{dx_p}{u} = \dfrac{dy_p}{v} = \dfrac{dz_p}{w} = dt$ (includes $dt$ — *particle trajectory*).
- **Streakline:** Locus of all particles that have previously passed through a given point.
- **In steady flow, streamline = pathline = streakline** (high-frequency KTU question).
- **Cylindrical coordinate pitfall:** Always include $-\dfrac{v_\theta^2}{r}$ in $a_r$ and $+\dfrac{v_r v_\theta}{r}$ in $a_\theta$.
- **Units check:** Acceleration in SI is $\text{m/s}^2$; final numerical answers must always carry units.
- **Vector identity:** $(\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)\vec{V} = \nabla\!\left(\dfrac{\vert \vec{V}\vert^2}{2}\right) - \vec{V}\times(\nabla\times\vec{V})$ — useful in deriving Bernoulli's equation.
- **Practical appearance:** Material derivative is the foundation of the **continuity equation**, **Navier–Stokes equations**, and **energy equation** — the three pillars of fluid mechanics.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Velocity Potential Function and Stream Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT741 - FLUID MECHANICS & MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Velocity Potential Function and Stream Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2: VELOCITY POTENTIAL FUNCTION AND STREAM FUNCTION

## 1.1 Velocity Potential Function (φ) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):** A scalar function of position and time, denoted by **φ (phi)**, is called the **Velocity Potential Function** if its negative gradient (or partial derivative in each coordinate direction) gives the velocity components of the fluid flow at every point in the flow field.

Mathematically, for a 2D flow in Cartesian coordinates:

$$\vec{V} = \nabla \phi = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}\hat{i} + \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}\hat{j} + \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial z}\hat{k}$$

Or in component form:

$$u = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}, \quad v = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}, \quad w = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial z}$$

The negative sign convention is sometimes used (so that flow is in the direction of *decreasing* φ, analogous to electric potential), but the **positive gradient** convention is universally adopted in fluid mechanics textbooks as per KTU syllabus.

> [!NOTE]
> **Existence Condition:** A velocity potential function exists **if and only if** the flow is **irrotational** (i.e., the curl of the velocity vector is zero). Mathematically, $\nabla \times \vec{V} = 0$.

---

## 1.2 Stream Function (ψ) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):** A scalar function of position and time, denoted by **ψ (psi)**, is called the **Stream Function** if its partial derivatives with respect to coordinates give the velocity components in directions *perpendicular* to the coordinate axes.

For 2D flow in Cartesian coordinates:

$$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}, \quad v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$$

In 3D, the **Stokes Stream Function** is used, but KTU Module 2 focuses on 2D flows.

> [!NOTE]
> **Existence Condition:** The stream function always exists for a **2D incompressible flow** satisfying the continuity equation. It does not require irrotationality. In fact, it is widely used to analyze both rotational and irrotational 2D flows.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogies (Plain English Explanation)

### Velocity Potential — The "Topography" Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of velocity potential φ as a "hills and valleys" map (like a contour map of a mountain).** Water naturally flows from high elevation to low elevation. Similarly, fluid flows from regions of high φ to regions of low φ. The **equipotential lines** (lines of constant φ) are like contour lines on a topographic map — they are *perpendicular* to the direction of flow (just as contour lines on a mountain are perpendicular to the steepest descent direction).

### Stream Function — The "River Channel" Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of the stream function ψ as a "flow rate counter."** Imagine pouring colored dye into a river at specific points. The dye traces out **streamlines** — paths that no fluid crosses. The stream function value is like a "label" on each streamline. The **difference in ψ values** between two streamlines tells you the **volumetric flow rate** passing between them. It's like lane markers on a highway — fluid in "Lane 1" (ψ = 1) stays in Lane 1 and never crosses to "Lane 2" (ψ = 2).

### Combined Analogy — The "Flow Net"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When you superimpose the equipotential lines and the streamlines, you get a **flow net** — a grid-like pattern that looks like a **fishnet stretched over the flow field**. The fishnet's mesh size tells you the flow velocity (smaller mesh = faster flow), and the mesh shape tells you about flow obstructions and boundaries.

---

## 1.4 Physical Significance — Why These Functions Matter

| Function | Primary Utility | Common Engineering Application |
|----------|-----------------|-------------------------------|
| **φ (Velocity Potential)** | Simplifies solution of Laplace equation for irrotational flows | Aerofoil design, groundwater flow, potential flow around submerged bodies |
| **ψ (Stream Function)** | Automatically satisfies continuity; visualizes flow patterns | Flow visualization, computing discharge between points, flow nets |
| **Flow Net (φ + ψ)** | Complete solution of 2D irrotational flow | Civil engineering seepage analysis, Hele-Shaw analog, aerodynamic pressure plotting |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** For 2D **incompressible, irrotational** flow, BOTH φ and ψ exist simultaneously, and they form a powerful conjugate pair satisfying the **Cauchy-Riemann equations**. This allows us to use powerful complex variable techniques (analytic functions) to solve seemingly complex flow problems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Conditions for Existence — The Theoretical Foundation

### 2.1.1 Existence of Velocity Potential (φ)

A velocity potential exists when:

$$\nabla \times \vec{V} = 0$$

Expanded for 2D flow:

$$\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 0$$

This is the **irrotationality condition** (also called the **Kelvin's condition**). When it holds, the line integral of velocity between any two points is path-independent:

$$\phi = \int \vec{V} \cdot d\vec{s} \quad \text{(path independent)}$$

### 2.1.2 Existence of Stream Function (ψ)

A stream function exists when the continuity equation is satisfied for 2D incompressible flow:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0$$

When this holds, we can define:

$$u \, dy - v \, dx = d\psi = 0 \quad \text{(along a streamline)}$$

This means **ψ = constant along a streamline**.

---

## 2.2 The Cauchy-Riemann Equations — The Bridge

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When both φ and ψ exist (i.e., flow is **2D, incompressible, AND irrotational**), they satisfy the **Cauchy-Riemann equations**:

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Consequence:** The curves φ = constant (equipotential lines) and ψ = constant (streamlines) are **mutually perpendicular** at every point of intersection. This is the geometric foundation of the flow net.

---

## 2.3 Laplace Equations (Governing PDEs)

By differentiating the Cauchy-Riemann equations, we get the **Laplace equation** for both functions:

$$\nabla^2 \phi = \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y^2} = 0$$

$$\nabla^2 \psi = \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial y^2} = 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Both φ and ψ are **harmonic functions** (solutions to Laplace's equation). This means they are **analytic** in the complex plane when combined as a complex potential $w = \phi + i\psi$.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Unit / Remarks |
|---|---------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | Velocity from φ (Cartesian) | $u = \partial\phi/\partial x,\ v = \partial\phi/\partial y$ | m/s |
| 2 | Velocity from ψ (Cartesian) | $u = \partial\psi/\partial y,\ v = -\partial\psi/\partial x$ | m/s |
| 3 | Velocity in polar (φ) | $v_r = \partial\phi/\partial r,\ v_\theta = (1/r)\partial\phi/\partial\theta$ | m/s |
| 4 | Velocity in polar (ψ) | $v_r = (1/r)\partial\psi/\partial\theta,\ v_\theta = -\partial\psi/\partial r$ | m/s |
| 5 | Irrotationality | $\partial v/\partial x - \partial u/\partial y = 0$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Continuity (2D) | $\partial u/\partial x + \partial v/\partial y = 0$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Cauchy-Riemann (x-equation) | $\partial\phi/\partial x = \partial\psi/\partial y$ | — |
| 8 | Cauchy-Riemann (y-equation) | $\partial\phi/\partial y = -\partial\psi/\partial x$ | — |
| 9 | Laplace equation for φ | $\nabla^2 \phi = 0$ | — |
| 10 | Laplace equation for ψ | $\nabla^2 \psi = 0$ | — |
| 11 | Discharge between streamlines | $Q = \psi_2 - \psi_1$ (per unit depth) | m²/s |
| 12 | Vorticity (2D) | $\omega_z = \partial v/\partial x - \partial u/\partial y$ | 1/s |
| 13 | Complex potential | $w = \phi + i\psi$ | — |
| 14 | Complex velocity | $dw/dz = u - iv$ | m/s |
| 15 | Streamline equation | $\psi(x,y) = \text{const.}$ | — |
| 16 | Equipotential equation | $\phi(x,y) = \text{const.}$ | — |

---

## 2.5 Standard Elementary Flows (Building Blocks)

| # | Flow Type | Velocity Potential (φ) | Stream Function (ψ) | Complex Potential (w) |
|---|-----------|------------------------|---------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Uniform flow** (U at angle α to x-axis) | $U(x\cos\alpha + y\sin\alpha)$ | $U(y\cos\alpha - x\sin\alpha)$ | $Uz e^{-i\alpha}$ |
| 2 | **Source/Sink** (strength m at origin) | $(m/2\pi)\ln r$ | $(m/2\pi)\theta$ | $(m/2\pi)\ln z$ |
| 3 | **Free Vortex** (circulation Γ) | $(\Gamma/2\pi)\theta$ | $-(\Gamma/2\pi)\ln r$ | $-i(\Gamma/2\pi)\ln z$ |
| 4 | **Doublet** (strength μ at origin) | $-(\mu/r)\cos\theta$ | $-(\mu/r)\sin\theta$ | $\mu/z$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Source:** m > 0 (fluid emanates outward). **Sink:** m < 0 (fluid converges inward). **Vortex strength** is Γ (circulation).

---

## 2.6 Rotational vs. Irrotational Flow — Conceptual Comparison

| Property | Irrotational Flow | Rotational Flow |
|----------|------------------|-----------------|
| Vorticity ω | $\omega = 0$ everywhere | $\omega \neq 0$ somewhere |
| Velocity potential φ | Exists | Does NOT exist |
| Stream function ψ | Exists (for 2D incompressible) | Exists (for 2D incompressible) |
| Cauchy-Riemann | Applicable | NOT applicable |
| Flow net possible | YES (orthogonal φ and ψ lines) | NO (only streamlines meaningful) |
| Example | Ideal flow past aerofoil (far from boundary layer) | Flow inside boundary layer, swirling tank flow |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Velocity Potential Exists ⟺ Flow is Irrotational

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of φ.**

If φ exists, then $u = \partial\phi/\partial x$ and $v = \partial\phi/\partial y$.

**Step 2 — Compute the cross-derivative identity.**

For any well-behaved scalar function φ:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y \, \partial x} = \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x \, \partial y}$$

(Equality of mixed partial derivatives — Clairaut/Schwarz theorem).

**Step 3 — Express this in terms of u and v.**

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}\right) = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}\right)$$

Substituting $u$ and $v$:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to the irrotationality condition.**

$$\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 0$$

This is the **z-component of vorticity** $\omega_z = 0$. Hence the flow is irrotational.

**Step 5 — Converse direction (sufficient condition).**

If $\partial v/\partial x - \partial u/\partial y = 0$, then the differential $M\,dx + N\,dy = u\,dy - v\,dx$ is an **exact differential** (by Euler's test: $\partial M/\partial y = \partial N/\partial x$ holds). Therefore, an integrating function (here, simply 1) exists, and φ can be defined uniquely up to an additive constant.

$$\therefore \quad \text{Velocity potential exists} \iff \nabla \times \vec{V} = 0$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Stream Function Exists ⟺ Continuity Holds (2D Incompressible)

**Step 1 — Start with 2D continuity for incompressible flow.**

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Define ψ such that the continuity is automatically satisfied.**

Propose:

$$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}, \quad v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$$

**Step 3 — Verify by substitution.**

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(-\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}\right) = \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x \partial y} - \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial y \partial x} = 0$$

The mixed partials cancel, so continuity is **automatically satisfied** for ANY ψ. Therefore, ψ exists whenever continuity holds.

**Step 4 — Show that ψ = constant along a streamline.**

A streamline is defined by:

$$\frac{dx}{u} = \frac{dy}{v} \quad \Rightarrow \quad u\,dy - v\,dx = 0$$

Substituting $u = \partial\psi/\partial y$ and $v = -\partial\psi/\partial x$:

$$\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}dy + \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}dx = d\psi = 0$$

Therefore:

$$\psi = \text{constant along a streamline} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Discharge Between Two Streamlines

**Step 1 — Consider two streamlines ψ₁ and ψ₂ (per unit depth into the page).**

**Step 2 — Compute the volume flow rate crossing any curve C between them.**

The flow rate per unit depth is:

$$q = \int_C (u\,dy - v\,dx)$$

**Step 3 — Recognize that the integrand is dψ.**

Since $u\,dy - v\,dx = d\psi$ (from the streamline equation derivation), we have:

$$q = \int_{\psi_1}^{\psi_2} d\psi = \psi_2 - \psi_1$$

**Step 4 — Final result.**

$$\boxed{Q_{\text{per unit depth}} = \psi_2 - \psi_1}$$

This is one of the most useful results in exam problems: the **difference in ψ-values equals the discharge per unit depth**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Determining φ and ψ from Velocity Field

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Given $u = 2xy$ and $v = x^2 - y^2$, find the velocity potential φ and stream function ψ, if they exist. Also find the velocity at point (1, 2).

**Step 1 — Check irrotationality.**

$$\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 2x - 2x = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

Irrotational → φ exists.

**Step 2 — Check continuity (incompressibility).**

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 2y + (-2y) = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

Incompressible 2D → ψ exists.

**Step 3 — Integrate to find φ.**

$$u = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = 2xy \quad \Rightarrow \quad \phi = \int 2xy\,dx = x^2 y + f(y)$$

Differentiate with respect to y:

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = x^2 + f'(y) = v = x^2 - y^2$$

So:

$$f'(y) = -y^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad f(y) = -\frac{y^3}{3} + C$$

$$\boxed{\phi = x^2 y - \frac{y^3}{3} + C}$$

**Step 4 — Integrate to find ψ.**

$$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} = 2xy \quad \Rightarrow \quad \psi = \int 2xy\,dy = xy^2 + g(x)$$

Differentiate with respect to x:

$$\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} = y^2 + g'(x) = -v = -(x^2 - y^2) = -x^2 + y^2$$

So:

$$g'(x) = -x^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad g(x) = -\frac{x^3}{3} + C$$

$$\boxed{\psi = xy^2 - \frac{x^3}{3} + C}$$

**Step 5 — Compute velocity at (1, 2).**

$$u(1,2) = 2(1)(2) = 4 \text{ m/s}, \quad v(1,2) = 1 - 4 = -3 \text{ m/s}$$

$$V = \sqrt{4^2 + (-3)^2} = 5 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{-3}{4}\right) = -36.87°$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Polar Coordinate Form of Stream Function

**Step 1 — Transform Cartesian to polar.**

Let $x = r\cos\theta$ and $y = r\sin\theta$. Velocity components in polar are $v_r$ and $v_\theta$.

**Step 2 — Use the Jacobian transformation.**

$$u = v_r \cos\theta - v_\theta \sin\theta$$
$$v = v_r \sin\theta + v_\theta \cos\theta$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the Cartesian definition of ψ.**

$$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}, \quad v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$$

Apply the chain rule:

$$\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial r} = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}\cos\theta + \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}\sin\theta = -v\cos\theta + u\sin\theta$$

Substitute u and v from Step 2:

$$\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial r} = -(v_r \sin\theta + v_\theta \cos\theta)\cos\theta + (v_r \cos\theta - v_\theta \sin\theta)\sin\theta$$

Expand:

$$\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial r} = -v_r \sin\theta\cos\theta - v_\theta \cos^2\theta + v_r \cos\theta\sin\theta - v_\theta \sin^2\theta$$

Cancel the $v_r$ terms and use $\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta = 1$:

$$\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial r} = -v_\theta$$

Similarly, by differentiating with respect to θ:

$$\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial \theta} = r v_r$$

**Step 4 — Invert to get the polar forms.**

$$\boxed{v_r = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial \theta}, \quad v_\theta = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial r}}$$

**Step 5 — Analogous derivation for φ gives:**

$$\boxed{v_r = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial r}, \quad v_\theta = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial \theta}}$$

---

## 3.6 Verification: Source Flow Satisfies Laplace Equation

For a source of strength m:

$$\phi = \frac{m}{2\pi} \ln r = \frac{m}{4\pi} \ln(x^2 + y^2)$$

Compute $\nabla^2 \phi$:

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = \frac{m}{4\pi} \cdot \frac{2x}{x^2+y^2} = \frac{mx}{2\pi(x^2+y^2)}$$

$$\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} = \frac{m}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{(x^2+y^2) - x(2x)}{(x^2+y^2)^2} = \frac{m(y^2 - x^2)}{2\pi(x^2+y^2)^2}$$

By symmetry:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y^2} = \frac{m(x^2 - y^2)}{2\pi(x^2+y^2)^2}$$

Adding:

$$\nabla^2 \phi = \frac{m(y^2 - x^2) + m(x^2 - y^2)}{2\pi(x^2+y^2)^2} = 0 \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.7 Boundary Conditions Used in KTU Problems

| Boundary Type | Condition on φ | Condition on ψ |
|---------------|----------------|----------------|
| **Solid wall (impermeable)** | $\partial\phi/\partial n = V_n$ (no-penetration: $V_n = 0$) | $\psi = \text{constant}$ (wall is a streamline) |
| **Free surface** | $p = p_{atm}$ (constant pressure) | Streamline with specific shape |
| **Inlet/Outlet** | $\phi$ specified from known velocity | $\psi$ specified from known flow rate |
| **Axis of symmetry** | $\partial\phi/\partial n = 0$ (no normal flow) | $\partial\psi/\partial n = 0$ (no flow crossing) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Logical Flow from Velocity Field to φ and ψ

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input Velocity Field u x y and v x y"] --> B["Check Irrotationality"]
    B --> C{"dV/dx minus du/dy equals zero?"}
    C -- "Yes" --> D["Velocity Potential phi Exists"]
    C -- "No" --> E["phi Does Not Exist"]
    A --> F["Check Continuity 2D Incompressible"]
    F --> G{"du/dx plus dv/dy equals zero?"}
    G -- "Yes" --> H["Stream Function psi Exists"]
    G -- "No" --> I["psi Does Not Exist"]
    D --> J["Cauchy Riemann Equations Apply"]
    H --> J
    J --> K["phi and psi are Orthogonal"]
    K --> L["Flow Net Construction"]
    L --> M["Solve Engineering Problem"]
    D --> N["Integrate to find phi x y"]
    H --> O["Integrate to find psi x y"]
    N --> P["Verify Laplace Equation"]
    O --> P
    P --> Q["Compute Discharge Q equals psi2 minus psi1"]
```

---

## 4.2 Schematic: Equipotential Lines and Streamlines (Flow Net Concept)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FLOW["FLOW NET STRUCTURE"]
        direction LR
        A1["Streamline psi 1"] --- A2["Streamline psi 2"] --- A3["Streamline psi 3"] --- A4["Streamline psi 4"]
        B1["Equipotential phi 1"] -.-> B2["Equipotential phi 2"] -.-> B3["Equipotential phi 3"] -.-> B4["Equipotential phi 4"]
        A1 -. "90 deg" .-> B1
        A2 -. "90 deg" .-> B2
        A3 -. "90 deg" .-> B3
        A4 -. "90 deg" .-> B4
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the actual physical flow net, the equipotential lines (dashed) cross the streamlines (solid) **perpendicularly** at every intersection, forming a "curvilinear square" mesh in regions of uniform flow.

---

## 4.3 Architecture: Complex Potential and Superposition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Complex Potential w = phi plus i psi"] --> B["Analytic Function of z"]
    B --> C["dw/dz = u minus iv Complex Velocity"]
    C --> D["Elementary Flow Components"]
    D --> D1["Uniform Flow Uz"]
    D --> D2["Source Sink m ln z over 2 pi"]
    D --> D3["Vortex minus i Gamma ln z over 2 pi"]
    D --> D4["Doublet mu over z"]
    D1 --> E["Superposition Principle"]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F["Combined Complex Potential w total"]
    F --> G["Engineering Applications"]
    G --> G1["Flow Past Cylinder"]
    G --> G2["Flow Past Aerofoil Joukowski Transform"]
    G --> G3["Flow in Corners and Channels"]
```

---

## 4.4 Decision Diagram: When to Use φ vs ψ

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Given 2D Incompressible Flow Problem"] --> Q1{"Need to find velocity field?"}
    Q1 -- "Yes" --> Q2{"Is flow irrotational?"}
    Q1 -- "No, need to find flow rate" --> R1["Use Stream Function psi"]
    Q2 -- "Yes" --> R2["Use Velocity Potential phi"]
    Q2 -- "No, rotational flow" --> R3["Use only Stream Function psi"]
    R1 --> S1["Discharge Q equals psi2 minus psi1"]
    R2 --> S2["Velocity u = dphi/dx, v = dphi/dy"]
    R3 --> S3["Velocity u = dpsi/dy, v = -dpsi/dx"]
    S1 --> END["Solve Engineering Problem"]
    S2 --> END
    S3 --> END
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**Define velocity potential function and stream function. State the conditions for their existence.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Velocity Potential Function (φ):** A scalar function whose partial derivatives with respect to coordinates give the velocity components. For 2D flow: $u = \partial\phi/\partial x$ and $v = \partial\phi/\partial y$. **Condition for existence:** The flow must be **irrotational**, i.e., $\partial v/\partial x - \partial u/\partial y = 0$.
>
> **Stream Function (ψ):** A scalar function defined for 2D incompressible flow as: $u = \partial\psi/\partial y$ and $v = -\partial\psi/\partial x$. **Condition for existence:** The 2D continuity equation must be satisfied, i.e., $\partial u/\partial x + \partial v/\partial y = 0$.
>
> **Equally valid alternate statement:** Velocity potential exists for irrotational flow; stream function exists for incompressible 2D flow.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Show that the stream function satisfies the Laplace equation for irrotational flow.**

**Model Answer:**

For irrotational flow, both φ and ψ exist and satisfy the Cauchy-Riemann equations:

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}, \quad \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$$

Differentiate the first with respect to x and the second with respect to y:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} = \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x \partial y}, \quad \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y^2} = -\frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial y \partial x}$$

Adding:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y^2} = 0$$

But also adding the right sides (and using equality of mixed partials):

$$\frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x \partial y} - \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial y \partial x} = 0$$

This is trivially zero. To get the Laplace equation for ψ, we differentiate the first C-R equation wrt y and the second wrt x, then subtract to get:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x^2} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \nabla^2 \psi = 0$$

Hence ψ satisfies the Laplace equation for irrotational 2D incompressible flow. **(3 marks)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Format:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Internal choice means: student attempts **either** Question A **or** Question B from each module slot.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**For a 2D incompressible flow given by $u = 3x + 2y$ and $v = 2x - 3y$:**

**(a)** Check if the flow is (i) incompressible, (ii) irrotational. **(4 marks)**

**(b)** If both velocity potential and stream function exist, derive them. Find the equation of the streamline passing through the point (1, 2) and compute the discharge per unit depth between this streamline and the one passing through (3, 4). **(10 marks)**

**Complete Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Step-by-step checks:**

**(i) Incompressibility check (Continuity):**

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 3 + (-3) = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

Flow is **incompressible**.

**(ii) Irrotationality check:**

$$\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 2 - 2 = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

Flow is **irrotational**.

**(Both conditions satisfied — 4 marks)**

**Part (b) — Derivation of φ and ψ:**

**Step 1 — Find velocity potential φ.**

$$u = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = 3x + 2y \quad \Rightarrow \quad \phi = \frac{3x^2}{2} + 2xy + f(y)$$

Differentiate wrt y:

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = 2x + f'(y) = v = 2x - 3y$$

$$f'(y) = -3y \quad \Rightarrow \quad f(y) = -\frac{3y^2}{2} + C$$

$$\boxed{\phi = \frac{3x^2}{2} + 2xy - \frac{3y^2}{2} + C}$$

**Step 2 — Find stream function ψ.**

$$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} = 3x + 2y \quad \Rightarrow \quad \psi = 3xy + y^2 + g(x)$$

Differentiate wrt x:

$$\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} = 3y + g'(x) = -v = -(2x - 3y) = -2x + 3y$$

$$g'(x) = -2x \quad \Rightarrow \quad g(x) = -x^2 + C$$

$$\boxed{\psi = 3xy + y^2 - x^2 + C}$$

**Step 3 — Streamline through (1, 2).**

$$\psi(1, 2) = 3(1)(2) + (2)^2 - (1)^2 = 6 + 4 - 1 = 9 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

Equation of streamline:

$$\boxed{3xy + y^2 - x^2 = 9}$$

**Step 4 — Streamline through (3, 4).**

$$\psi(3, 4) = 3(3)(4) + (4)^2 - (3)^2 = 36 + 16 - 9 = 43 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Discharge per unit depth.**

$$q = \psi_2 - \psi_1 = 43 - 9 = 34 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Final Answer (boxed):**

$$\boxed{\phi = \frac{3x^2}{2} + 2xy - \frac{3y^2}{2} + C, \quad \psi = 3xy + y^2 - x^2 + C}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Streamline: } 3xy + y^2 - x^2 = 9, \quad q = 34 \text{ m}^2/\text{s} \text{ per unit depth}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**For a 2D flow field in polar coordinates, the stream function is given by $\psi = (5/2)r^2 \sin(2\theta)$.**

**(a)** Determine the velocity components $v_r$ and $v_\theta$. Also check whether the flow is irrotational. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the corresponding velocity potential function φ. Find the equation of the streamline passing through the point where $r = 2$ m and $\theta = \pi/6$ rad. **(7 marks)**

**Complete Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Step-by-step velocity computation:**

**Step 1 — Apply polar form formulas for stream function.**

$$v_r = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial \theta}, \quad v_\theta = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial r}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $v_r$.**

$$\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial \theta} = \frac{5}{2}r^2 \cdot 2\cos(2\theta) = 5r^2 \cos(2\theta)$$

$$v_r = \frac{1}{r} \cdot 5r^2 \cos(2\theta) = 5r \cos(2\theta)$$

**Step 3 — Compute $v_\theta$.**

$$\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial r} = \frac{5}{2} \cdot 2r \sin(2\theta) = 5r\sin(2\theta)$$

$$v_\theta = -5r\sin(2\theta)$$

**Step 4 — Check irrotationality.**

In polar coordinates, the z-component of vorticity is:

$$\omega_z = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial(rv_\theta)}{\partial r} - \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial v_r}{\partial \theta}$$

Compute $rv_\theta = -5r^2 \sin(2\theta)$:

$$\frac{\partial(rv_\theta)}{\partial r} = -10r\sin(2\theta)$$

$$\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial(rv_\theta)}{\partial r} = -10\sin(2\theta)$$

Compute $\partial v_r/\partial \theta$:

$$\frac{\partial v_r}{\partial \theta} = -10r\sin(2\theta)$$

$$\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial v_r}{\partial \theta} = -10\sin(2\theta)$$

Therefore:

$$\omega_z = -10\sin(2\theta) - (-10\sin(2\theta)) = 0$$

Flow is **irrotational** (since $\omega_z = 0$). Hence velocity potential exists.

$$\boxed{v_r = 5r\cos(2\theta), \quad v_\theta = -5r\sin(2\theta), \quad \omega_z = 0 \text{ (irrotational)}}$$

**Part (b) — Derivation of φ:**

**Step 1 — Use polar form of velocity potential.**

$$v_r = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial r} = 5r\cos(2\theta)$$

Integrate with respect to r:

$$\phi = \frac{5r^2}{2}\cos(2\theta) + h(\theta)$$

**Step 2 — Use the second equation to find h(θ).**

$$v_\theta = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial \theta} = -5r\sin(2\theta)$$

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial \theta} = -5r^2 \sin(2\theta)$$

Differentiate our expression for φ wrt θ:

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial \theta} = -\frac{5r^2}{2} \cdot 2\sin(2\theta) + h'(\theta) = -5r^2 \sin(2\theta) + h'(\theta)$$

Equate to $-5r^2 \sin(2\theta)$:

$$h'(\theta) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad h(\theta) = C$$

**Step 3 — Final φ.**

$$\boxed{\phi = \frac{5r^2}{2}\cos(2\theta) + C}$$

**Step 4 — Verify Cauchy-Riemann in polar form.**

Polar C-R equations are:

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial r} = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial \theta}, \quad \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial \theta} = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial r}$$

LHS: $\partial\phi/\partial r = 5r\cos(2\theta)$. RHS: $(1/r) \cdot 5r^2 \cos(2\theta) = 5r\cos(2\theta)$. ✓

LHS: $(1/r) \cdot (-5r^2 \sin(2\theta)) = -5r\sin(2\theta)$. RHS: $-5r\sin(2\theta)$. ✓

**Step 5 — Streamline through $(r, \theta) = (2, \pi/6)$.**

$$\psi(2, \pi/6) = \frac{5}{2}(2)^2 \sin(2 \cdot \pi/6) = 10 \sin(\pi/3) = 10 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = 5\sqrt{3}$$

$$\psi(2, \pi/6) \approx 8.66 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

Equation of streamline:

$$\boxed{\frac{5}{2}r^2 \sin(2\theta) = 5\sqrt{3} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad r^2 \sin(2\theta) = 2\sqrt{3}}$$

---

## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in Stream Function Definition:** The most common mistake is writing $v = +\partial\psi/\partial x$ instead of $v = -\partial\psi/\partial x$. This sign error cascades through the entire solution. **Always remember: ψ is defined so that ψ = constant along a streamline, and the sign convention is FIXED.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Constant of Integration (C):** Examiners deduct 1 mark if you forget the arbitrary constant "C" in φ or ψ. Always include "C" in your final answer.
>
> 3. **Confusing Polar Formulas:** Students frequently mix up the polar forms of φ and ψ. **Memory aid:** For ψ in polar, the derivative with respect to r goes to $v_\theta$ (with a MINUS sign), and derivative with respect to θ goes to $v_r$ (with a factor of $1/r$). For φ in polar, it's the direct opposite.
>
> 4. **Failing to Verify Cauchy-Riemann:** When asked to "show that φ and ψ are conjugates," you MUST verify both C-R equations explicitly. Skipping this loses 2 marks.
>
> 5. **Discharge Formula Misuse:** Many students write $Q = \psi_2 - \psi_1$ without specifying "**per unit depth**" or the unit (m²/s). Always state: $q = \psi_2 - \psi_1$ **m²/s per unit depth**.
>
> 6. **Insufficient Verification of Irrotationality/Incompressibility:** When a problem gives u and v, ALWAYS check both conditions first and present them explicitly before proceeding. This shows examiner you understand the foundational concepts.
>
> 7. **Mixing Up φ and ψ in Final Answer:** Writing the expression for φ when the question asks for ψ (or vice versa) is a fatal error. **Trick:** φ is for **P**otential (easy to remember: P → φ).
>
> 8. **Assuming φ exists without checking:** Not every flow has a velocity potential. Always check irrotationality FIRST.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary for Last-Minute Revision:**

- **φ (Velocity Potential):** Exists **ONLY** if flow is **irrotational** ($\nabla \times \vec{V} = 0$). Defined by $u = \partial\phi/\partial x$, $v = \partial\phi/\partial y$.

- **ψ (Stream Function):** Exists for **2D incompressible** flow (continuity satisfied). Defined by $u = \partial\psi/\partial y$, $v = -\partial\psi/\partial x$. **Always exists** in 2D incompressible flow — even in rotational flow.

- **Critical Relationship:** $\psi = $ **constant** along a streamline. The line $\psi(x,y) = C$ IS the streamline equation.

- **Discharge Formula:** $q = \psi_2 - \psi_1$ gives volumetric flow rate **per unit depth** between streamlines ψ₁ and ψ₂. (Units: m²/s)

- **Cauchy-Riemann Equations** (when BOTH exist):
  * $\partial\phi/\partial x = \partial\psi/\partial y$
  * $\partial\phi/\partial y = -\partial\psi/\partial x$
  * These guarantee that φ and ψ are **mutually orthogonal** families of curves.

- **Both φ and ψ satisfy Laplace equation** in irrotational 2D incompressible flow:
  * $\nabla^2 \phi = 0$
  * $\nabla^2 \psi = 0$

- **Polar Coordinate Forms:**
  * From φ: $v_r = \partial\phi/\partial r$, $v_\theta = (1/r)\partial\phi/\partial\theta$
  * From ψ: $v_r = (1/r)\partial\psi/\partial\theta$, $v_\theta = -\partial\psi/\partial r$

- **Equipotential lines and streamlines are ALWAYS perpendicular** at their intersection (when both exist). This is the foundation of the **flow net**.

- **Standard Elementary Flows to Remember:**
  * Uniform flow: $\phi = Ux\cos\alpha + Uy\sin\alpha$, $\psi = Uy\cos\alpha - Ux\sin\alpha$
  * Source/Sink: $\phi = (m/2\pi)\ln r$, $\psi = (m/2\pi)\theta$
  * Vortex: $\phi = (\Gamma/2\pi)\theta$, $\psi = -(\Gamma/2\pi)\ln r$
  * Doublet: $\phi = -(\mu/r)\cos\theta$, $\psi = -(\mu/r)\sin\theta$

- **Boundary Condition on Solid Wall:** $\psi = $ constant (wall is a streamline). **On free surface:** pressure is atmospheric.

- **Order of Solving:** (1) Check incompressibility → (2) Check irrotationality → (3) Integrate to find φ and ψ → (4) Verify Laplace equation → (5) Apply to specific problem.

- **Engineering Applications to Recall:** Aerofoil design (Joukowski transform), seepage under dams (flow nets), flow meters, hydrocyclones, lubrication theory.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Problems; Fluid Dynamics: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT741 - FLUID MECHANICS & MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Problems; Fluid Dynamics: Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Fluid Dynamics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fluid Dynamics** is the branch of fluid mechanics that studies the **motion of fluids (liquids and gases)** and the forces acting on them in motion. It is governed by the fundamental laws of conservation of **mass**, **momentum**, and **energy**.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMRT741)**, Module 2 introduces the *kinematic* and *dynamic* descriptions of fluid flow — meaning we describe **how fluids move** (kinematics) and **why** they move the way they do under applied forces (dynamics).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 2 – Fluid Dynamics):**
> - Types of fluid flow
> - Streamline, streakline, pathline
> - Continuity equation (conservation of mass)
> - Euler's equation and Bernoulli's equation
> - Momentum equation and its engineering applications
> - Pitot tube, Venturimeter, Orificemeter

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Understanding Fluid Dynamics Intuitively

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "The River and the Boatman"**
> Imagine a flowing river. A boatman wants to know:
> 1. **How fast** is the water moving at different points? *(Velocity field)*
> 2. **Is the flow smooth or chaotic?** *(Laminar vs Turbulent)*
> 3. **Does the water level drop when the river narrows?** *(Continuity & Bernoulli)*
> 4. **How much force does the water exert on a bridge pier?** *(Momentum equation)*
>
> **Fluid Dynamics** is the science that quantifies all of these — replacing intuition with rigorous mathematical laws.

## 1.3 Why Study Fluid Dynamics?

- It is the foundation for designing **pipes, pumps, turbines, aircraft, ships, and hydraulic machinery**.
- All engineering systems involving **flow of liquids or gases** rely on its principles.
- Real-world applications: blood flow in arteries, oil pipeline transport, water supply networks, jet engines, HVAC systems.

**Key Physical Constants Used in This Module:**

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Acceleration due to gravity | **g** | **9.81** | **m/s²** |
| Density of water (at 4 °C) | **ρ** | **1000** | **kg/m³** |
| Specific weight of water | **γ** | **9810** | **N/m³** |
| Atmospheric pressure (standard) | **pₐₜₘ** | **101.325 kPa** | **N/m²** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Fluid Flow — Structured Logic Breakdown

The classification of fluid flow is the **first conceptual step** in fluid dynamics. Each classification answers a different physical question.

### A. Steady vs Unsteady Flow
- **Steady Flow:** Fluid properties (velocity, pressure, density) at a point do **not change with time**.
  - *Example:* Flow in a pipe at constant discharge.
- **Unsteady Flow:** Properties **vary with time**.
  - *Example:* Flow from a emptying tank.

### B. Uniform vs Non-Uniform Flow
- **Uniform Flow:** Velocity is **constant along the direction of flow** at a given instant.
- **Non-Uniform Flow:** Velocity changes along the flow direction.
  - *Example:* Flow through a converging nozzle (non-uniform).

### C. Laminar vs Turbulent Flow
- **Laminar Flow:** Fluid particles move in **smooth, parallel layers** with no mixing.
  - Governed by **Reynolds Number: Re < 2000** (in pipes).
- **Turbulent Flow:** Chaotic, irregular motion with **eddy formation** and mixing.
  - **Re > 4000** (in pipes).

### D. Compressible vs Incompressible Flow
- **Incompressible:** Density (ρ) is **constant** (liquids, low-speed gases).
- **Compressible:** ρ varies with pressure (high-speed gas flow, M > 0.3).

### E. Rotational vs Irrotational Flow
- **Rotational:** Fluid particles **rotate about their own axis** (e.g., flow in a rotating tank).
- **Irrotational:** Fluid particles have **no angular velocity** about their mass center (idealized flow).

### F. 1-D, 2-D, 3-D Flow
- Based on the number of spatial coordinates used to describe velocity.

## 2.2 Streamline, Pathline, and Streakline

These are three distinct visualization tools used to track fluid motion:

| Concept | Definition | Equation/Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Streamline** | An imaginary curve **tangent to the velocity vector** at every point at a given instant. | $\frac{dx}{u} = \frac{dy}{v} = \frac{dz}{w}$ |
| **Pathline** | The actual **trajectory** traced by a single fluid particle over time. | Obtained by integrating $u = dx/dt$ |
| **Streakline** | The locus of all particles that have **previously passed through a given point**. | Visualized by dye injection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **steady flow**, streamlines, pathlines, and streaklines are **identical**. In unsteady flow, they differ.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Formulas for Module 2 – Fluid Dynamics (KTU 2024)**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Continuity Equation** | $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ | Mass conservation in 1-D flow |
| 2 | **Continuity (3-D)** | $\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$ | General form |
| 3 | **Euler's Equation** | $\frac{dp}{\rho} + g\,dz + V\,dV = 0$ | Motion of ideal fluid |
| 4 | **Bernoulli's Equation** | $\frac{p}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + z = \text{constant}$ | Energy conservation along streamline |
| 5 | **Hydrostatic Pressure** | $p = \rho g h$ | Pressure due to fluid column |
| 6 | **Discharge (Q)** | $Q = A \cdot V$ | Volumetric flow rate |
| 7 | **Momentum Equation** | $\sum F = \rho Q (V_2 - V_1)$ | Force on bends, jets, vanes |
| 8 | **Reynolds Number** | $Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{V D}{\nu}$ | Flow regime identification |
| 9 | **Mach Number** | $M = \frac{V}{c}$ | Compressibility check |
| 10 | **Pitot Tube** | $V = \sqrt{2gh}$ | Velocity measurement |
| 11 | **Venturimeter Discharge** | $Q = C_d \cdot \frac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \sqrt{2gh}$ | Flow measurement |
| 12 | **Orificemeter Discharge** | $Q = C_d \cdot \frac{a}{\sqrt{1 - (a/A)^2}} \sqrt{2gh}$ | Flow measurement |
| 13 | **Dynamic Pressure** | $p_d = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2$ | Kinetic energy per unit volume |
| 14 | **Stagnation Pressure** | $p_0 = p + \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2$ | Total pressure |
| 15 | **Acceleration of Fluid Particle** | $\vec{a} = \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$ | Local + convective |

> [!WARNING]
> **PITFALL:** Never confuse the **static pressure** ($p$), **dynamic pressure** ($\frac{1}{2}\rho V^2$), and **stagnation pressure** ($p + \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2$). This is a **favourite KTU examiner's trap** in 3-mark questions.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of Fluid Dynamics Equations

- **Continuity Equation** → Sizing pipes, nozzles, and channels in municipal water supply.
- **Bernoulli's Equation** → Aircraft wing lift generation, carburetor design, chimney draft.
- **Momentum Equation** → Force on pipe bends, propeller thrust, jet propulsion.
- **Venturi / Orifice / Pitot** → Flow meters in chemical plants, aircraft airspeed indicators.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Continuity Equation (1-D)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Starting Principle:** *Conservation of Mass* — mass can neither be created nor destroyed in a fluid flow (excluding nuclear reactions).

**Consider a control volume** between two sections of a pipe with cross-sectional areas $A_1$ and $A_2$, separated by distance $L$.

Let:
- $\rho_1, A_1, V_1$ = density, area, velocity at section 1
- $\rho_2, A_2, V_2$ = density, area, velocity at section 2

**Step 1:** Mass entering per second at section 1:

$$\dot{m}_1 = \rho_1 A_1 V_1$$

**Step 2:** Mass leaving per second at section 2:

$$\dot{m}_2 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$$

**Step 3:** Apply conservation of mass for steady flow (no accumulation):

$$\dot{m}_1 = \dot{m}_2$$

**Step 4:** Substituting:

$$\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$$

**Step 5:** For an **incompressible fluid** ($\rho_1 = \rho_2 = \rho$):

$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q = \text{constant}$$

This is the **Continuity Equation** — one of the most frequently tested equations in KTU Module 2.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Euler's Equation of Motion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Starting Principle:** Newton's second law applied to a fluid element.

Consider a small fluid element of:
- Cross-section = $dA$
- Length along streamline = $ds$
- Mass = $\rho \cdot dA \cdot ds$

**Forces acting on the element along the streamline direction:**

1. **Pressure force on upstream face:** $p \cdot dA$ (in direction of flow)
2. **Pressure force on downstream face:** $(p + dp) \cdot dA$ (opposite to flow)
3. **Weight component along flow:** $-\rho g \cdot dA \cdot ds \cdot \cos\theta$

Where $\cos\theta = \dfrac{dz}{ds}$ (geometric relation).

**Step 1:** Net force on element along streamline:

$$dF = p\,dA - (p + dp)\,dA - \rho g\, dA\, ds\, \frac{dz}{ds}$$

**Step 2:** Simplifying:

$$dF = -dp\,dA - \rho g\, dA\, dz$$

**Step 3:** Acceleration of the element: $a = \dfrac{V\,dV}{ds}$

**Step 4:** Apply Newton's second law ($F = m \cdot a$):

$$-dp\,dA - \rho g\,dA\,dz = (\rho\, dA\, ds) \cdot \frac{V\,dV}{ds}$$

**Step 5:** Cancel $dA$ and $ds$:

$$-dp - \rho g\,dz = \rho V\,dV$$

**Step 6:** Rearrange into the standard form:

$$\boxed{\frac{dp}{\rho} + V\,dV + g\,dz = 0}$$

This is **Euler's Equation of Motion** — the basis of Bernoulli's equation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Bernoulli's Equation from Euler's Equation

**Step 1:** Take Euler's equation and integrate along a streamline:

$$\int \frac{dp}{\rho} + \int V\,dV + \int g\,dz = \text{constant}$$

**Step 2:** For an **incompressible fluid** (ρ = constant), the first integral is:

$$\frac{p}{\rho}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluating all three integrals:

$$\frac{p}{\rho} + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz = C$$

**Step 4:** Divide through by **g** to express in head form:

$$\frac{p}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + z = \text{constant}$$

**Step 5:** Apply between two points (1) and (2):

$$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$

This is **Bernoulli's Equation** — the **workhorse equation** of fluid dynamics, applicable to:
- Steady, incompressible, inviscid flow along a streamline.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Momentum Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Starting Principle:** Newton's second law in momentum form — *Rate of change of momentum = Net external force*.

**Step 1:** Consider a control volume with fluid entering at section 1 and leaving at section 2.

**Step 2:** Mass flow rate:

$$\dot{m} = \rho Q = \rho A_1 V_1 = \rho A_2 V_2$$

**Step 3:** Rate of change of momentum in direction of flow:

$$\dot{m} (V_2 - V_1)$$

**Step 4:** Apply Newton's second law:

$$\sum F_x = \rho Q (V_2 - V_1)$$

**Step 5:** For a fluid jet striking a stationary flat plate normally:

- $V_1 = V$, $V_2 = 0$ (jet stops on plate)
- Force on plate: $F = \rho Q V = \rho A V^2$

This is the **momentum equation** — used to find forces on **pipe bends, nozzles, blades, and jets**.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (Model):** Water flows steadily through a pipe that contracts from a diameter of **20 cm** at section 1 to **10 cm** at section 2. The pressure at section 1 is **100 kPa**, and at section 2 is **60 kPa**. The flow is horizontal. Find the **volume flow rate** and the **velocity at section 2**.

**Given:**
- $D_1 = 0.20$ m, $D_2 = 0.10$ m
- $p_1 = 100{,}000$ Pa, $p_2 = 60{,}000$ Pa
- $z_1 = z_2$ (horizontal), $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³

**Step 1 — Areas:**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.20)^2 = 0.03142 \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.10)^2 = 0.007854 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Apply Bernoulli's Equation:**

$$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$$

$$\frac{p_1 - p_2}{\rho g} = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Continuity:**

$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_1 = V_2 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1} = \frac{V_2}{4}$$

(Since $A_1/A_2 = 4$)

**Step 4 — Substitute:**

$$\frac{40{,}000}{9810} = \frac{V_2^2 - (V_2/4)^2}{2 \times 9.81}$$

$$4.0775 = \frac{V_2^2 \left(1 - \frac{1}{16}\right)}{19.62} = \frac{V_2^2 \cdot \frac{15}{16}}{19.62}$$

$$V_2^2 = \frac{4.0775 \times 19.62 \times 16}{15} = 85.32 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2$$

**Step 5 — Solve:**

$$V_2 = 9.24 \text{ m/s}$$

$$Q = A_2 V_2 = 0.007854 \times 9.24 = 0.0726 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$Q = 72.6 \text{ L/s}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $V_2 = 9.24$ m/s, $Q = 0.0726$ m³/s

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
