# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**Define the following terms with one example each:**
**(a)** Streamline
**(b)** Steady flow
**(c)** Uniform flow

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Streamline:** A streamline is an imaginary curve drawn through a flowing fluid such that the **velocity vector at every point on the curve is tangent to it** at a given instant. *Example:* Flow lines around an airfoil at a particular instant.

**(b) Steady Flow:** A flow in which fluid properties (velocity, pressure, density) at any point **do not change with time** is called steady flow.

$$\frac{\partial V}{\partial t} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial p}{\partial t} = 0$$

*Example:* Water flowing through a pipe at constant discharge rate.

**(c) Uniform Flow:** A flow in which the **velocity remains constant in magnitude and direction** along the direction of flow at a given instant.

*Example:* Flow through a straight pipe of constant diameter at constant rate.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
**State and explain the continuity equation for a 1-D incompressible flow. Mention its significance.**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** For a steady, incompressible, 1-D flow, the **product of cross-sectional area and velocity** remains constant.

$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = Q \text{ (constant)}$$

**Significance:**
- It is the **mathematical expression of mass conservation**.
- It is used to determine the velocity at any section if area and velocity at another section are known.
- It forms the basis of **flow measurement devices** (Venturimeter, Orificemeter).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Format

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model]**

**A water pipe of diameter 30 cm at section 1 contracts to a diameter of 15 cm at section 2. The pressures at sections 1 and 2 are 150 kPa and 100 kPa respectively. The pipe is horizontal. Determine:**
**(a)** The volumetric flow rate of water.
**(b)** The velocity at section 2.

Assume water density $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

---

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**

$$D_1 = 0.30 \text{ m}, \quad D_2 = 0.15 \text{ m}$$

$$p_1 = 150 \text{ kPa} = 150{,}000 \text{ Pa}$$

$$p_2 = 100 \text{ kPa} = 100{,}000 \text{ Pa}$$

$$z_1 = z_2 \text{ (horizontal pipe)}$$

**Part (a) — Volumetric Flow Rate (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Calculate cross-sectional areas:

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} D_1^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.30)^2 = 0.07069 \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} D_2^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.15)^2 = 0.01767 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2:** Apply continuity equation:

$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$$

$$V_1 = V_2 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1} = V_2 \cdot \frac{0.01767}{0.07069} = 0.25 V_2$$

**Step 3:** Apply Bernoulli's equation (horizontal, so $z_1 = z_2$):

$$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute known values and $V_1 = 0.25 V_2$:

$$\frac{150{,}000}{9810} + \frac{(0.25 V_2)^2}{19.62} = \frac{100{,}000}{9810} + \frac{V_2^2}{19.62}$$

$$15.290 + \frac{0.0625 V_2^2}{19.62} = 10.194 + \frac{V_2^2}{19.62}$$

**Step 5:** Simplify:

$$15.290 - 10.194 = \frac{V_2^2}{19.62} - \frac{0.0625 V_2^2}{19.62}$$

$$5.096 = \frac{V_2^2 (1 - 0.0625)}{19.62} = \frac{0.9375 V_2^2}{19.62}$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $V_2$:

$$V_2^2 = \frac{5.096 \times 19.62}{0.9375} = 106.66 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2$$

$$V_2 = 10.33 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 7:** Calculate $Q$:

$$Q = A_2 V_2 = 0.01767 \times 10.33 = 0.1825 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{Q = 0.1825 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 182.5 \text{ L/s}}$$

**Part (b) — Velocity at Section 2 (7 marks):**

$$\boxed{V_2 = 10.33 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Verification of $V_1$:**

$$V_1 = 0.25 \times 10.33 = 2.58 \text{ m/s}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Question A):**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting to convert kPa → Pa (factor of 1000). Causes numerical errors.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Using the area ratio incorrectly — students often invert $A_1/A_2$. Remember: the **larger area has the smaller velocity** for incompressible flow.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Omitting the **density** in Bernoulli's equation when applying the pressure-head form. Always use $\dfrac{p}{\rho g}$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model]**

**A horizontal Venturimeter with inlet diameter 20 cm and throat diameter 10 cm is used to measure the flow of water. The differential manometer reading is 20 cm of mercury. The coefficient of discharge is 0.98. Calculate:**
**(a)** The discharge through the Venturimeter.
**(b)** The velocity at the throat.

Take density of water = 1000 kg/m³, density of mercury = 13,600 kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

---

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**

$$D_1 = 0.20 \text{ m}, \quad D_2 = 0.10 \text{ m}$$

$$h = 0.20 \text{ m of Hg}, \quad C_d = 0.98$$

**Part (a) — Discharge (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Calculate areas:

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.20)^2 = 0.03142 \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.10)^2 = 0.007854 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2:** Apply the Venturimeter discharge formula:

$$Q = C_d \cdot \frac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \cdot \sqrt{2 g h'}$$

**Step 3:** Convert manometer reading to equivalent head of water:

$$h' = h \left( \frac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_w} - 1 \right) = 0.20 \times (13.6 - 1) = 0.20 \times 12.6$$

$$h' = 2.52 \text{ m of water}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the area term:

$$A_1 A_2 = 0.03142 \times 0.007854 = 2.467 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^4$$

$$A_1^2 - A_2^2 = (0.03142)^2 - (0.007854)^2 = 9.872 \times 10^{-4} - 6.168 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$A_1^2 - A_2^2 = 9.255 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^4$$

$$\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2} = 0.03042 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 5:** Compute the velocity-head term:

$$\sqrt{2 g h'} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 2.52} = \sqrt{49.44} = 7.031 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute into the discharge equation:

$$Q = 0.98 \times \frac{2.467 \times 10^{-4}}{0.03042} \times 7.031$$

$$Q = 0.98 \times 8.109 \times 10^{-3} \times 7.031$$

$$Q = 0.98 \times 0.05700 = 0.0559 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{Q = 0.0559 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 55.9 \text{ L/s}}$$

**Part (b) — Velocity at the Throat (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Apply continuity equation:

$$V_2 = \frac{Q}{A_2} = \frac{0.0559}{0.007854} = 7.118 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{V_2 = 7.12 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Verification:**

Theoretical velocity at throat = $\sqrt{2 g h'} = 7.031$ m/s. With $C_d = 0.98$, the discharge is correctly reduced.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Question B):**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting the manometric fluid conversion $h' = h \left(\frac{S_{Hg}}{S_w} - 1\right)$. Many students directly use $h = 0.20$ m of water — this gives a grossly incorrect answer.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Mixing up the symbols $C_d$ (coefficient of discharge), $C_c$ (coefficient of contraction), and $C_v$ (coefficient of velocity). The formula uses $C_d$.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Using diameter in mm without converting to m in the area calculation. Always convert **all units to SI (m, s, kg)** before substituting.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Fluid Dynamics Introduction**

- **Three pillars of fluid dynamics:** Continuity (mass), Bernoulli (energy), Momentum (force).
- **Continuity Equation** in 1-D incompressible flow: $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ — velocity is **inversely proportional** to area.
- **Euler's Equation:** $\dfrac{dp}{\rho} + V\,dV + g\,dz = 0$ — derived from Newton's second law for a fluid element.
- **Bernoulli's Equation:** $\dfrac{p}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V^2}{2g} + z = \text{constant}$ — represents **conservation of energy** along a streamline.
- **Bernoulli is valid only for:** steady, incompressible, inviscid (frictionless) flow along a streamline.
- **Momentum Equation:** $\sum F = \rho Q (V_2 - V_1)$ — used for finding **forces on bends, nozzles, and blades**.
- **Streamline, pathline, streakline** are **identical in steady flow** but **differ in unsteady flow**.
- **Reynolds Number:**
  - $Re < 2000$: Laminar
  - $2000 < Re < 4000$: Transitional
  - $Re > 4000$: Turbulent
- **Mach Number:** $M < 0.3$ → Incompressible assumption valid.
- **Standard unit conversions:** 1 kPa = 1000 Pa, 1 m³/s = 1000 L/s, 1 bar = 100 kPa.
- **Always convert manometer readings** to equivalent head of the flowing fluid using $h' = h \left(\frac{S_m}{S_f} - 1\right)$.
- **In KTU problems**, assume water density = **1000 kg/m³** and $g = 9.81$ m/s² unless stated otherwise.
- **Common trap:** Static vs Dynamic vs Stagnation pressure — remember **Stagnation = Static + Dynamic**.
- **Always show units** in the final answer — KTU examiners deduct marks for missing units.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pro Tip for KTU 2024 Exam:** For 14-mark Part B questions, always:
> 1. **List the given data** with units.
> 2. **State the governing equation(s)** before substitution.
> 3. **Show ALL algebraic steps** — KTU examiners award **step marks** even if the final answer has a minor arithmetic error.
> 4. **Box the final answer** with units.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Euler's Equation of Motion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT741 - FLUID MECHANICS & MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Euler's Equation of Motion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Euler's Equation of Motion** is a fundamental, three-dimensional, vector differential equation that describes the inviscid (frictionless) flow of a fluid by applying **Newton's Second Law of Motion** to a fluid element. It is the foundational governing equation that, upon integration, yields the famous **Bernoulli's Equation** along a streamline.

Mathematically, for an inviscid, compressible or incompressible fluid, Euler's equation in vector form is:

$$\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\nabla p + \vec{g}$$

Where:
- $\vec{V}$ = Velocity vector of the fluid
- $\rho$ = Mass density of the fluid
- $p$ = Pressure at the point
- $\vec{g}$ = Acceleration due to gravity (body force per unit mass)

In component (scalar) form for the **x-direction**:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial u}{\partial z} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + g_x$$

Similar equations hold for the **y-direction** and **z-direction**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The River Raft on a Slope"

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine you are on a white-water rafting trip.** The raft represents a tiny parcel of fluid. As the raft moves:
> 1. **Its speed changes** because the river speeds up at narrow bends (this is the **convective acceleration** term $u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x}$).
> 2. **It bobs up and down** as it crosses bumps and dips (the **local acceleration** $\frac{\partial u}{\partial t}$).
> 3. **The water pushes it** from high-pressure zones to low-pressure zones (the **pressure gradient** $-\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}$).
> 4. **Gravity pulls it downhill** throughout the journey (the **body force** $\vec{g}$).

**Euler's equation simply balances all these "pushes" and "pulls" against the raft's mass times its acceleration** — exactly as Newton's second law dictates for any solid body, but here applied to a continuous fluid parcel.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | SI Unit | Typical Magnitude |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\rho$ | Fluid mass density | $\text{kg/m}^3$ | **1000** (water), **1.225** (air at STP) |
| $g$ | Acceleration due to gravity | $\text{m/s}^2$ | **9.81** |
| $p$ | Static pressure | $\text{Pa (N/m}^2\text{)}$ | **101,325** (atmospheric) |
| $V$ | Flow velocity | $\text{m/s}$ | 0 to 100+ in pipes |
| $\mu$ | Dynamic viscosity (assumed zero in Euler) | $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ | 0 (inviscid assumption) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Euler's equation **assumes the fluid to be inviscid (non-viscous)**. This means shear stresses due to viscosity are neglected. This is a critical limitation, and the reason real viscous flows (like boundary layers) are analyzed using the **Navier-Stokes equations** instead.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Conceptual Breakdown

Euler's equation is built on **five foundational assumptions** that the KTU examiner expects you to state clearly:

1. **The fluid is continuous** — no voids, treated as a continuum.
2. **The fluid is inviscid** — internal friction ($\mu = 0$) is neglected.
3. **The flow is along a streamline** (or, in general form, in any arbitrary direction).
4. **The fluid is non-rotational or rotational** (Euler's equation holds for both; it is more general than Bernoulli's).
5. **The pressure $p$ is a scalar, continuous, and differentiable function of space and time.**

### 2.2 The Three Scalar Forms of Euler's Equation

From the vector equation, three scalar equations emerge — one for each Cartesian axis:

**Along x-direction:**
$$\frac{Du}{Dt} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + g_x$$

**Along y-direction:**
$$\frac{Dv}{Dt} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial y} + g_y$$

**Along z-direction:**
$$\frac{Dw}{Dt} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial z} + g_z$$

The notation $\frac{D}{Dt}$ is the **material derivative** (or substantial derivative):

$$\frac{Du}{Dt} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial u}{\partial z}$$

### 2.3 Forms of Euler's Equation in Different Directions

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently asks the student to write **Euler's equation in a general arbitrary direction $\theta$** to the horizontal, which is then integrated to obtain Bernoulli's equation. This is the **most-tested form** in board exams.

**Euler's equation in a general direction (along a streamline):**

$$\frac{\partial p}{\partial s} = \rho g \sin\theta - \rho V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s}$$

**Euler's equation normal to a streamline (radial direction):**

$$\frac{\partial p}{\partial n} = \rho g \cos\theta + \rho \frac{V^2}{R}$$

Where:
- $s$ = direction along streamline
- $n$ = normal direction (perpendicular to streamline)
- $R$ = radius of curvature of the streamline
- $\theta$ = angle of streamline with horizontal

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| **Equation Name** | **Mathematical Form** | **Physical Meaning** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Euler's Vector Form | $\rho \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \rho \vec{g}$ | Newton's 2nd law for fluid | Foundation equation |
| Euler's x-component | $\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial u}{\partial z} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + g_x$ | Force balance in x | General flow analysis |
| Euler's along streamline | $\frac{\partial p}{\partial s} = \rho g \sin\theta - \rho V\frac{\partial V}{\partial s}$ | Pressure variation along motion | Pipe flow, jets |
| Euler's normal to streamline | $\frac{\partial p}{\partial n} = \rho g \cos\theta + \rho \frac{V^2}{R}$ | Centripetal pressure variation | Bend pipes, curved flows |
| Hydrostatic limit ($\vec{V}=0$) | $\frac{\partial p}{\partial z} = -\rho g$ | Pressure varies with depth | Static fluid problems |
| Integrated (Bernoulli) | $\frac{p}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + z = \text{constant}$ | Energy conservation | KTU Module 2/3 |
| Material Derivative | $\frac{D}{Dt} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial}{\partial z}$ | Rate following fluid particle | All fluid dynamics |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Euler's equation is the starting point for deriving:
> 1. **Bernoulli's equation** (energy conservation) — covered next in Module 2.
> 2. **Irrotational flow** solutions (velocity potential $\phi$ and stream function $\psi$).
> 3. **Pressure distribution in nozzles, diffusers, and Pitot tubes.**
> 4. **Lift and circulation** in aerodynamics (Kutta-Joukowski theorem).
> 5. **Free surface profiles** in open channel flow.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 DERIVATION 1: Euler's Equation in Vector Form from Newton's Second Law

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **primary derivation** KTU expects. Marks are awarded for: identifying forces, applying $\Sigma F = ma$, and converting to differential form.

**Step 1: Define the fluid element**

Consider a small, infinitesimally small fluid element of:
- Volume: $dV = dx \cdot dy \cdot dz$
- Mass: $dm = \rho \cdot dV = \rho \, dx \, dy \, dz$

The fluid element is so small that all properties (pressure, density, velocity) are uniform within it.

**Step 2: Identify the surface forces (pressure forces)**

Pressure acts **normal to every face** of the element. Consider the x-direction:

Pressure on the **left face** (at $x$): $p \cdot dy \cdot dz$ (acting in $+x$ direction, pushing into the element)

Pressure on the **right face** (at $x + dx$): 
$$\left(p + \frac{\partial p}{\partial x}dx\right) dy \cdot dz$$
(acting in $-x$ direction)

**Net pressure force in x-direction:**

$$F_{px} = \left[p - \left(p + \frac{\partial p}{\partial x}dx\right)\right] dy \cdot dz = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} dx \, dy \, dz$$

**Step 3: Identify the body force (gravity)**

If gravity has components $(g_x, g_y, g_z)$, the body force on the element in x-direction is:

$$F_{gx} = \rho \cdot g_x \cdot dx \, dy \, dz$$

**Step 4: Apply Newton's Second Law ($\Sigma F = ma$)**

The acceleration of the fluid particle is the **material derivative** of velocity:

$$a_x = \frac{Du}{Dt} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial u}{\partial z}$$

Applying Newton's second law:

$$-\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} dx \, dy \, dz + \rho \, g_x \, dx \, dy \, dz = \rho \, dx \, dy \, dz \cdot \frac{Du}{Dt}$$

**Step 5: Simplify by dividing throughout by $\rho \, dx \, dy \, dz$**

$$\frac{Du}{Dt} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + g_x$$

This is Euler's equation in the x-direction. Similarly, we obtain the y and z equations.

**Step 6: Write in compact vector form**

$$\boxed{\rho \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \rho \vec{g}}$$

This is the final, most general form of Euler's equation of motion.

---

### 3.2 DERIVATION 2: Euler's Equation Along a Streamline

> [!NOTE]
> This is the form **directly integrated to get Bernoulli's equation**. KTU almost always asks for this derivation.

**Step 1: Choose an elementary cylindrical fluid element along a streamline**

Consider a small cylindrical fluid element of:
- Cross-sectional area: $dA$
- Length along streamline: $ds$
- Mass: $dm = \rho \, dA \, ds$

**Step 2: Resolve forces along the streamline (tangential direction s)**

The net pressure force along the streamline:

$$dF_s = -dA \frac{\partial p}{\partial s} ds$$

The component of gravity along the streamline (if streamline makes angle $\theta$ with horizontal):

$$dG_s = \rho \, g \, dA \, ds \cdot \sin\theta$$

(gravity has a component $g\sin\theta$ along the streamline because it acts vertically downward)

**Step 3: Tangential acceleration of the fluid element**

The tangential acceleration is $V \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial s}$ (since $a = \dfrac{dV}{dt} = \dfrac{dV}{ds} \cdot \dfrac{ds}{dt} = V \dfrac{dV}{ds}$).

**Step 4: Apply Newton's Second Law along the streamline**

$$dF_s + dG_s = dm \cdot a_s$$

$$-dA \frac{\partial p}{\partial s} ds + \rho g \sin\theta \, dA \, ds = (\rho \, dA \, ds) \cdot V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s}$$

**Step 5: Simplify**

Dividing by $\rho \, dA \, ds$:

$$\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial s} = g \sin\theta - V\frac{\partial V}{\partial s}$$

Or, rearranging:

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial p}{\partial s} + \rho V\frac{\partial V}{\partial s} - \rho g \sin\theta = 0}$$

This is **Euler's equation along a streamline**.

---

### 3.3 DERIVATION 3: Euler's Equation Normal to a Streamline

**Step 1: Consider forces on the cylindrical element in the normal (radial) direction n**

Pressure force normal to streamline: $-dA \dfrac{\partial p}{\partial n} ds$

Gravity component normal to streamline: gravity has a component $g \cos\theta$ normal to the streamline (perpendicular to the motion direction).

**Step 2: Acceleration normal to streamline = centripetal acceleration**

For a curved streamline of radius of curvature $R$, the normal acceleration is $\dfrac{V^2}{R}$, directed **inward** (toward the center of curvature).

**Step 3: Apply Newton's Second Law in the normal direction**

$$-dA \frac{\partial p}{\partial n} ds - \rho g \cos\theta \, dA \, ds = (\rho \, dA \, ds) \cdot \frac{V^2}{R}$$

**Step 4: Simplify**

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial p}{\partial n} = -\rho g \cos\theta + \rho \frac{V^2}{R}}$$

(Depending on the sign convention used in textbooks, the $g\cos\theta$ term may appear with a positive sign — KTU accepts both if the physics is explained.)

---

### 3.4 DERIVATION 4: Integration of Euler's Equation to Obtain Bernoulli's Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most common 14-mark question** in KTU Module 2. The full step-by-step integration is shown below.

**Step 1: Start with Euler's equation along a streamline**

$$\frac{\partial p}{\partial s} = \rho g \sin\theta - \rho V\frac{\partial V}{\partial s}$$

**Step 2: Convert $\sin\theta$ to differential form**

Since $\theta$ is the angle the streamline makes with the horizontal, and the streamline lies in a vertical plane, the elevation $z$ changes as:

$$\sin\theta = -\frac{dz}{ds}$$

(The negative sign arises because the streamline rises when $\theta > 0$ in the conventional setup.)

**Step 3: Substitute into Euler's equation**

$$\frac{\partial p}{\partial s} = -\rho g \frac{dz}{ds} - \rho V\frac{\partial V}{\partial s}$$

Multiply through by $ds$:

$$dp = -\rho g \, dz - \rho V \, dV$$

**Step 4: Integrate from point 1 to point 2 along the streamline**

Assume **incompressible flow** ($\rho = \text{constant}$):

$$\int_{1}^{2} dp = -\rho g \int_{1}^{2} dz - \rho \int_{1}^{2} V \, dV$$

$$p_2 - p_1 = -\rho g (z_2 - z_1) - \rho \left(\frac{V_2^2}{2} - \frac{V_1^2}{2}\right)$$

**Step 5: Rearrange to obtain Bernoulli's equation**

$$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$

$$\boxed{\frac{p}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + z = \text{constant}}$$

This is **Bernoulli's Equation** — the celebrated result of integrating Euler's equation.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Physical Interpretation of Each Term

| **Term in Euler's Eq.** | **Mathematical Role** | **Physical Meaning** | **Real-World Example** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\rho \dfrac{D\vec{V}}{Dt}$ | $\text{Mass} \times \text{Acceleration}$ | Inertia of fluid element | A water slug in a pipe |
| $-\nabla p$ | Pressure gradient force | Net force from pressure difference | Suction at a Venturi throat |
| $\rho \vec{g}$ | Body force per unit volume | Weight of fluid element | Hydrostatic pressure in a tank |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram: Logic Chain from Newton's Law to Bernoulli

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Newtons Second Law F equals m a] --> B[Identify Forces on Fluid Element]
    B --> C[Surface Forces Pressure]
    B --> D[Body Forces Gravity]
    C --> E[Apply to Infinitesimal Volume dV]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Derive Euler Equation Vector Form]
    F --> G[Resolve Along Streamline s]
    F --> H[Resolve Normal to Streamline n]
    G --> I[Euler Equation Along Streamline]
    H --> J[Euler Equation Normal to Streamline]
    I --> K[Integrate Assuming Incompressible]
    K --> L[Bernoulli Equation]
    J --> M[Centripetal Pressure Variation]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Forces on a Fluid Element

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Pressure p on Left Face] -->|Pushes Right| EL[Fluid Element dV]
    P2[Pressure p plus dp on Right Face] -->|Pushes Left| EL
    G[Body Force rho g dV] -->|Pulls Downward| EL
    EL -->|Net Effect| A[Acceleration Du Dt]
    A --> R[Balanced by Pressure Gradient]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Cylindrical Streamline Element

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Streamline Direction s] -->|Tangential| CYL[Cylindrical Fluid Element dA x ds]
    N1[Normal Direction n] -->|Perpendicular| CYL
    G1[Gravity g sin theta along s] --> CYL
    G2[Gravity g cos theta along n] --> CYL
    P1[Pressure Force along s] --> CYL
    P2[Pressure Force along n] --> CYL
    A1[Acceleration V dV ds along s] --> CYL
    A2[Acceleration V squared R along n] --> CYL
```

### 4.4 Schematic Representation (ASCII) — Forces on a Cubic Fluid Element

```
              Top face: (p + dp/dy)dy acting DOWNWARD
              +-----------------------------------+
              |                                   |
   Left face: |                                   | Right face:
   p acting   |        Fluid Element              | (p + dp/dx)dx
   to RIGHT   |           dV = dxdydz             | acting to LEFT
              |                                   |
              |                                   |
              +-----------------------------------+
              Bottom face: p acting UPWARD
              
              Gravity: rho * g * dV acting DOWNWARD
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram: Three Forms of Euler's Equation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    EV[Euler Vector Form] --> EX[X component]
    EV --> EY[Y component]
    EV --> EZ[Z component]
    EX --> ES[Along Streamline s]
    EY --> ES
    EZ --> ES
    EX --> EN[Normal to Streamline n]
    EY --> EN
    EZ --> EN
    ES --> BE[Bernoulli Equation]
    EN --> CP[Centripetal Pressure Variation]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
>
> **Q1.** State the assumptions made in deriving Euler's equation of motion.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. The fluid is a **continuum** (continuous medium with no voids). *(1 mark)*
> 2. The fluid is **inviscid** — viscosity is neglected ($\mu = 0$). *(1 mark)*
> 3. The flow is assumed to be **steady or unsteady** but the field is differentiable; pressure is a continuous function. *(1 mark)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
>
> **Q2.** Differentiate between Euler's equation and Bernoulli's equation.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> | **Euler's Equation** | **Bernoulli's Equation** |
> |---|---|
> | It is a **differential** equation relating pressure, velocity, and elevation at a point. *(1 mark)* | It is the **integrated** form of Euler's equation, valid between two points. *(1 mark)* |
> | Applicable to **both compressible and incompressible** flow (in differential form). | Strictly valid for **incompressible, steady, inviscid** flow along a streamline. |
> | Does not assume a constant total energy along the flow. | States that the total energy $\frac{p}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + z$ is **constant**. *(1 mark)* |

---

### 5.2 PART B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> > [!WARNING]
> > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** When writing Euler's equation, students often **forget to include the convective acceleration terms** ($u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x}$, etc.) in the material derivative. The examiner **deducts 2 marks** for this oversight. Always write the full material derivative expansion.
> >
> > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:** When integrating Euler's equation to obtain Bernoulli's equation, students often **skip the justification for assuming $\rho$ = constant**. The examiner expects an explicit mention of the **incompressibility assumption**. Missing this costs **1 mark**.
> >
> > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #3:** Sign errors in the gravity term ($\sin\theta$ vs. $-\sin\theta$, depending on the sign convention) are the **most common reason for losing 2–3 marks**. State the sign convention explicitly at the start of your answer.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — Derivation-Focused

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]**
>
> **(a)** Derive the general Euler's equation of motion in vector form, starting from Newton's second law applied to a fluid element. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Starting from Euler's equation along a streamline, integrate it to obtain **Bernoulli's equation** for an incompressible, steady, inviscid flow. State all integration assumptions. **(7 marks)**

---

### **MODEL SOLUTION (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Assumptions (1 mark)**

1. Fluid is a continuum.
2. Fluid is inviscid (no shear stress).
3. Flow field is differentiable.
4. Pressure is a scalar, continuous function.

**Step 2: Consider an elemental fluid cube of sides $dx$, $dy$, $dz$ (1 mark)**

- Volume: $dV = dx \cdot dy \cdot dz$
- Mass: $dm = \rho \cdot dV$
- Pressure at center: $p$

**Step 3: Pressure forces on each face in x-direction (2 marks)**

- Left face (at $x$): $p \cdot dy \, dz$ (pushing in $+x$)
- Right face (at $x + dx$): $\left(p + \frac{\partial p}{\partial x}dx\right) dy \, dz$ (pushing in $-x$)
- Net: $F_{px} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} \, dx \, dy \, dz$

**Step 4: Body force (gravity) in x-direction (1 mark)**

$$F_{gx} = \rho \, g_x \, dx \, dy \, dz$$

**Step 5: Apply Newton's second law in x-direction (1 mark)**

$$-\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} \, dx \, dy \, dz + \rho \, g_x \, dx \, dy \, dz = \rho \, dx \, dy \, dz \cdot \frac{Du}{Dt}$$

**Step 6: Simplify and write vector form (1 mark)**

$$\frac{Du}{Dt} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + g_x$$

Extending to y, z and combining into vector form:

$$\boxed{\rho \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \rho \vec{g}}$$

---

### **MODEL SOLUTION (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Write Euler's equation along streamline (1 mark)**

$$\frac{\partial p}{\partial s} = \rho g \sin\theta - \rho V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s}$$

**Step 2: Convert gravity component to elevation form (1 mark)**

Using $\sin\theta \, ds = -dz$ (streamline geometry):

$$\frac{\partial p}{\partial s} = -\rho g \frac{dz}{ds} - \rho V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s}$$

**Step 3: Multiply through by $ds$ and rearrange (1 mark)**

$$dp = -\rho g \, dz - \rho V \, dV$$

**Step 4: State integration assumption (1 mark)**

Assume **incompressible flow** ($\rho = \text{constant}$), so $\rho$ can come out of the integral.

**Step 5: Integrate from point 1 to point 2 (2 marks)**

$$\int_{p_1}^{p_2} dp = -\rho g \int_{z_1}^{z_2} dz - \rho \int_{V_1}^{V_2} V \, dV$$

$$p_2 - p_1 = -\rho g(z_2 - z_1) - \rho\left(\frac{V_2^2}{2} - \frac{V_1^2}{2}\right)$$

**Step 6: Rearrange to obtain Bernoulli's equation (1 mark)**

$$\boxed{\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2}$$

**Physical interpretation:** The three terms represent **pressure head**, **velocity (kinetic) head**, and **elevation (potential) head**, and their sum is **constant** along a streamline for steady, incompressible, inviscid flow.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — Conceptual + Application-Focused (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]**
>
> **(a)** Derive Euler's equation in a direction **normal to a streamline** for inviscid flow. Explain the physical significance of each term. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A horizontal pipe of diameter 200 mm carries water at a flow rate of 40 L/s. At a **90° bend**, the gauge pressure is 100 kPa. Compute the **force exerted by the water on the bend**, neglecting friction. **(7 marks)**

---

### **MODEL SOLUTION (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Consider a cylindrical fluid element of length $ds$ and cross-section $dA$ on a curved streamline of radius of curvature $R$ (1 mark).**

**Step 2: Identify the normal forces (1 mark):**
- Net pressure force in the inward normal direction: $-dA \dfrac{\partial p}{\partial n} ds$
- Gravity component normal to streamline: $\rho g \cos\theta \, dA \, ds$ (outward)

**Step 3: Identify the normal acceleration (1 mark):**

The normal acceleration is the **centripetal acceleration**: $a_n = \dfrac{V^2}{R}$, directed **inward** (toward the center of curvature).

**Step 4: Apply Newton's second law in the normal direction (1 mark):**

$$-dA \frac{\partial p}{\partial n} ds - \rho g \cos\theta \, dA \, ds = (\rho \, dA \, ds) \cdot \left(-\frac{V^2}{R}\right)$$

(negative sign because centripetal acceleration is inward)

**Step 5: Simplify to obtain the equation (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial p}{\partial n} = \rho g \cos\theta + \rho \frac{V^2}{R}}$$

**Step 6: Physical significance (2 marks):**
- The term $\rho \dfrac{V^2}{R}$ shows that **pressure increases toward the outside of a curve**. This is why the outer wall of a pipe bend experiences higher pressure than the inner wall.
- The term $\rho g \cos\theta$ accounts for the **gravity component** perpendicular to the streamline.
- This equation is used to design **curved ducts, bends, and turbine blades**.

---

### **MODEL SOLUTION (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Given data (1 mark)**
- Diameter: $D = 200 \text{ mm} = 0.2 \text{ m}$
- Discharge: $Q = 40 \text{ L/s} = 0.04 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- Pressure: $p = 100 \text{ kPa} = 100,000 \text{ Pa} = 10^5 \text{ N/m}^2$
- $\rho_{\text{water}} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Step 2: Calculate area and velocity (1 mark)**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.2)^2}{4} = 0.0314 \text{ m}^2$$

$$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.04}{0.0314} = 1.273 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3: Apply momentum equation to the bend (2 marks)**

For a 90° bend with equal inlet and outlet diameters (no change in $V$), and neglecting friction:

Force on fluid by bend in x-direction (inlet direction):

$$F_x = \rho Q (V_{out,x} - V_{in,x}) + p_1 A_1 - p_2 A_2 \cos(90°)$$

Since $V_{out,x} = 0$, $V_{in,x} = V$:

$$F_x = \rho Q (0 - V) + p_1 A_1 = -(1000)(0.04)(1.273) + (10^5)(0.0314)$$

$$F_x = -50.93 + 3141.6 = 3090.7 \text{ N}$$

Force on fluid in y-direction (outlet direction):

$$F_y = \rho Q (V_{out,y} - 0) + p_2 A_2 - 0 = (1000)(0.04)(1.273) + (10^5)(0.0314)$$

$$F_y = 50.93 + 3141.6 = 3192.5 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4: Resultant force on fluid by bend (1 mark)**

$$F = \sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2} = \sqrt{(3090.7)^2 + (3192.5)^2} = 4440 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5: Force on the bend by fluid (Newton's third law) (1 mark)**

$$\boxed{F_{\text{on bend}} = 4440 \text{ N (opposite in direction to } F \text{)}}$$

**Direction:** $\tan\alpha = \dfrac{F_y}{F_x} = \dfrac{3192.5}{3090.7} \Rightarrow \alpha = 45.93°$ from the inlet direction.

> [!NOTE]
> This problem combines Euler's underlying principles (steady, inviscid flow) with the **momentum equation** (which is itself derived from Newton's second law — the same foundation as Euler's equation). KTU often combines Module 2 (Euler) and Module 3 (momentum) in a single 14-mark question.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — The 10 Most Critical Points for KTU Board Exam**
>
> 1. **Euler's equation = Newton's 2nd law for a fluid element.** It is **NOT** an empirical formula — it must be derived from $\Sigma F = ma$.
> 2. **Always write the full material derivative:** $\frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$. The convective term $(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$ is non-negotiable.
> 3. **Three forms of Euler's equation exist:** vector, Cartesian (x, y, z), and directional (along $s$ and along $n$).
> 4. **Along a streamline:** $\frac{\partial p}{\partial s} = \rho g \sin\theta - \rho V \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial s}$.
> 5. **Normal to a streamline:** $\frac{\partial p}{\partial n} = \rho g \cos\theta + \rho \dfrac{V^2}{R}$.
> 6. **Bernoulli's equation = integrated Euler's equation** (along a streamline, assuming incompressible, steady, inviscid flow).
> 7. **Euler's equation does NOT assume incompressibility** in its differential form. It is the **integration step** that requires $\rho$ = constant.
> 8. **Euler's equation is more general than Bernoulli's:** it works for both rotational and irrotational flows, while Bernoulli's (in its strict form) is valid only along a streamline.
> 9. **Sign convention matters:** The gravity term in the streamline equation is $\rho g \sin\theta$, and the relation $\sin\theta \, ds = -dz$ comes from the geometry of the streamline.
> 10. **For curved streamlines,** the term $\rho V^2 / R$ is the **centripetal pressure rise** — this is why the outer wall of a bend has higher pressure.

---

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bernoulli's Equation (Board-Examiner Definition):**
> *Bernoulli's equation is the statement of conservation of mechanical energy for a steady, incompressible, inviscid (non-viscous) flow along a streamline. It relates the pressure head, velocity head, and elevation head of a flowing fluid element such that their sum remains constant between any two points on the same streamline.*

Mathematically, between any two points (1) and (2) on a streamline:

$$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^{2}}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^{2}}{2g} + z_2$$

where,
- $p$ = static pressure (Pa or N/m²)
- $V$ = flow velocity (m/s)
- $z$ = elevation above a chosen datum (m)
- $\rho$ = mass density of the fluid (**1000 kg/m³** for water at 4°C)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**)

The three terms represent three forms of energy per unit weight of fluid:
- **Pressure head** $\frac{p}{\rho g}$ — flow energy
- **Velocity head** $\frac{V^{2}}{2g}$ — kinetic energy
- **Elevation head** $z$ — potential energy

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The Marble-on-a-Hill Analogy:**
> Imagine rolling a marble down a frictionless curved hill. As the marble climbs higher, it slows down (potential energy up, kinetic energy down). As it descends, it speeds up (potential energy down, kinetic energy up). The *total mechanical energy* stays constant.
> 
> A fluid particle behaves the *exact same way* — except it also carries **pressure energy** (think of pressure as the "push" being stored in the fluid). Bernoulli's equation simply says: **"Pressure + Kinetic + Potential energy of a fluid particle = CONSTANT"**, just like energy conservation for the marble.

**Engineering context:** This is why:
- A **Pitot-static tube** measures aircraft speed (high velocity → low static pressure on the nose).
- A **Venturi meter** measures pipe flow rate (constriction raises velocity, drops pressure).
- An **aircraft wing** generates lift (faster air over the curved upper surface → lower pressure).

---

## 1.3 Standard Assumptions (KTU Board Must-Mention)

> [!WARNING]
> **Assumptions of Bernoulli's Equation — Examiners repeatedly test these:**
> 1. Fluid is **inviscid** (frictionless, μ = 0).
> 2. Flow is **steady** ($\partial / \partial t = 0$).
> 3. Fluid is **incompressible** (ρ = constant).
> 4. Flow occurs **along a single streamline** (or flow is irrotational).
> 5. No shaft work is extracted/added (no pump or turbine between the points).

---

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Two Routes to Bernoulli's Equation — Conceptual Roadmap

Bernoulli's equation can be reached by **two independent mathematical roads**. Both ultimately converge to the same result, but the physical insights differ:

**Route A — From First Principles (Newton's Second Law on a fluid element):**
- Start with a small fluid parcel sliding along a streamline.
- Sum the real forces acting on it (pressure + gravity).
- Apply $F = ma$ along the streamline direction.
- Integrate → Bernoulli's equation.

**Route B — From Euler's Equation (Differential Momentum Equation):**
- Start with Euler's equation — the differential form of Newton's second law for an ideal fluid.
- Specialize it along a streamline.
- Integrate the resulting ODE → Bernoulli's equation.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Heuristic:** Route A (First Principles) is the *board-favourite* derivation. Route B (from Euler) is asked when the examiner tests your grasp of *vector fluid mechanics*.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Flow — Route A (From First Principles)

1. **Identify the control volume:** an infinitesimal cylindrical fluid element of cross-section $dA$ and length $ds$ along a streamline, with the upstream face making angle $\theta$ with horizontal.
2. **Identify forces:** Pressure force on upstream face ($p\,dA$), pressure force on downstream face ($(p + dp)\,dA$), and weight component along the streamline ($\rho g\,dA\,ds\sin\theta$).
3. **Apply Newton's second law** along the streamline.
4. **Substitute** the kinematic relation $V\,dV = V\frac{dV}{ds}ds$.
5. **Cancel** the geometric factor $dA$ and divide through by $\rho$.
6. **Integrate** along the streamline between sections 1 and 2.

---

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logical Flow — Route B (From Euler's Equation)

1. **Recall Euler's equation** in vector form:
$$\frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\nabla p + \vec{g}$$

2. **Expand the material derivative:**
$$\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V}\cdot \nabla)\vec{V} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\nabla p + \vec{g}$$

3. **Specialize to steady flow** ($\partial \vec{V}/\partial t = 0$) and restrict motion along a streamline (so $\vec{V}\cdot \nabla \equiv V\,d/ds$).
4. **Project** Euler's equation onto the streamline direction; gravity contributes $-g\,dz/ds$.
5. **Integrate** with $\rho = $ constant; note $\int V\,dV = V^{2}/2$.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Board-Revision Table)

| Symbol | Expression | Physical Meaning | Standard Unit |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---:|
| $H$ | $\frac{p}{\rho g} + \frac{V^{2}}{2g} + z$ | Total hydraulic head | m |
| $\frac{p}{\rho g}$ | Pressure head | Flow energy per unit weight | m |
| $\frac{V^{2}}{2g}$ | Velocity head | Kinetic energy per unit weight | m |
| $z$ | Elevation head | Potential energy per unit weight | m |
| $p$ | Static pressure | Normal stress at a point | Pa $\equiv$ N/m² |
| $\rho$ | Density (water = **1000 kg/m³**) | Mass per unit volume | kg/m³ |
| $g$ | Gravity = **9.81 m/s²** | Gravitational acceleration | m/s² |
| $V$ | Flow velocity | Speed of fluid particle | m/s |
| $\frac{dV}{dt}$ | Acceleration along streamline | Material derivative | m/s² |
| $\int \frac{dp}{\rho}$ | Pressure work term | Work done by pressure | J/kg |
| $\vec{g}$ | Body force per unit mass | Gravity vector $\approx (0,0,-g)$ | m/s² |

> [!NOTE]
> **Useful alternate (energy-per-unit-mass) form of Bernoulli:**
> $$\frac{p}{\rho} + \frac{V^{2}}{2} + gz = \text{constant} \quad \text{(J/kg)}$$
> Multiply by $\rho g$ to obtain the head form. Multiply by $Q$ (discharge) to obtain power form (W).

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Physical Use of Bernoulli |
|:---|:---|
| Pitot-static tube on aircraft | Measures aircraft airspeed |
| Venturi meter | Measures discharge in pipes |
| Orifice meter | Measures flow rate via pressure drop |
| Atomizer / paint sprayer | Creates low pressure to suck fluid up |
| Aerofoil / aircraft wing | Pressure difference generates lift |
| Chimney / chimney draught | Velocity difference creates draft |
| Carburettor | Pressure drop draws fuel into air stream |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 DERIVATION ROUTE A — Bernoulli's Equation from First Principles (Newton's Second Law)

### 3.1.1 Schematic of the Fluid Element

Consider a small cylindrical fluid element of cross-sectional area $dA$ lying along a streamline between section (1) and section (2). Let $ds$ be the length of the element measured along the streamline. Let $\theta$ be the angle the streamline (i.e., the central axis of the cylinder) makes with the horizontal at the location of the element.

```
         p (upstream)               
          ↓                          
   ←—————————————→  ds                
   |    element    |                 
   ←—————————————→                   
                  ↓                  
          p+dp (downstream)          
```

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Identify the Mass of the Element

The volume of the cylindrical fluid element is $dA \cdot ds$. With fluid density $\rho$, the mass is:

$$dm = \rho \, dA \, ds$$

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Identify the Real Forces on the Element

There are exactly **three real forces** acting on the element along the streamline direction:

**(i) Pressure force on the upstream face** (acting in the direction of flow, taken as positive):
$$F_1 = p \cdot dA$$

**(ii) Pressure force on the downstream face** (acting opposite to the direction of flow):
$$F_2 = (p + dp) \cdot dA$$

**(iii) Component of weight along the streamline** (acts downward; component along the streamline opposes flow when the streamline goes upward):

The weight of the element is $W = dm \cdot g = \rho \, dA \, ds \, g$.

Since the streamline is inclined at angle $\theta$ to horizontal, the component of weight *along* the streamline (opposing motion when going uphill) is:

$$F_3 = -\rho \, dA \, ds \, g \, \sin\theta$$

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Net Force Along the Streamline

Summing forces along the streamline (positive in the direction of flow):

$$F_{\text{net}} = p \, dA - (p + dp) \, dA - \rho \, dA \, ds \, g \, \sin\theta$$

Simplify by cancelling $p \, dA$:

$$F_{\text{net}} = -dp \, dA - \rho \, dA \, ds \, g \, \sin\theta$$

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Apply Newton's Second Law ($F = ma$)

The acceleration of the fluid particle along the streamline is the material derivative:

$$a_s = \frac{DV}{Dt} = V \frac{dV}{ds}$$

(The time derivative of velocity for a particle moving along a streamline — since the particle covers distance $ds$ in time $dt$, $V = ds/dt$, hence $dV/dt = (dV/ds)(ds/dt) = V\,dV/ds$.)

Applying $F = ma$ along the streamline:

$$F_{\text{net}} = dm \cdot a_s = \rho \, dA \, ds \cdot V \frac{dV}{ds}$$

### 3.1.6 Step 5 — Equate the Two Expressions

$$\rho \, dA \, ds \cdot V \frac{dV}{ds} = -dp \, dA - \rho \, dA \, ds \, g \, \sin\theta$$

Divide both sides by $\rho \, dA$ (note: $dA$ cancels entirely — that's the beauty of using an *infinitesimal* control volume):

$$ds \cdot V \frac{dV}{ds} = -\frac{dp}{\rho} - g \, ds \, \sin\theta$$

### 3.1.7 Step 6 — Substitute the Geometric Identity

The vertical rise of the streamline over length $ds$ is:

$$dz = ds \, \sin\theta \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \sin\theta = \frac{dz}{ds}$$

Substituting:

$$V \frac{dV}{ds} \, ds = -\frac{dp}{\rho} - g \, dz$$

The left-hand side simplifies to $V\,dV$ (chain rule):

$$V\,dV = -\frac{dp}{\rho} - g\,dz$$

### 3.1.8 Step 7 — Integrate Between Sections (1) and (2)

Since the fluid is **incompressible**, $\rho$ is constant, so $\int dp/\rho = \Delta p / \rho$.

$$\int_{V_1}^{V_2} V\,dV = -\int_{p_1}^{p_2} \frac{dp}{\rho} - g\int_{z_1}^{z_2} dz$$

Carrying out the integrations:

$$\left[\frac{V^{2}}{2}\right]_{V_1}^{V_2} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\bigl[p\bigr]_{p_1}^{p_2} - g\bigl[z\bigr]_{z_1}^{z_2}$$

$$\frac{V_2^{2}}{2} - \frac{V_1^{2}}{2} = -\frac{p_2 - p_1}{\rho} - g(z_2 - z_1)$$

Rearranging (move all terms to one side):

$$\frac{p_1}{\rho} + \frac{V_1^{2}}{2} + gz_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho} + \frac{V_2^{2}}{2} + gz_2$$

### 3.1.9 Step 8 — Convert to Head Form by Dividing by $g$

Dividing every term by $g$:

$$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^{2}}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^{2}}{2g} + z_2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is Bernoulli's Equation in its standard (head) form — derived purely from Newton's Second Law applied to a fluid element, with no reference to Euler's equation.**

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION ROUTE B — Bernoulli's Equation from Euler's Equation

### 3.2.1 Step 1 — State Euler's Equation of Motion

Euler's equation (1757) is the differential form of Newton's second law for an **inviscid** fluid. In vector form:

$$\rho \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \rho \vec{g}$$

Expanding the material derivative $D/Dt = \partial/\partial t + (\vec{V}\cdot \nabla)$:

$$\rho \left[\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V}\cdot \nabla)\vec{V}\right] = -\nabla p + \rho \vec{g}$$

### 3.2.2 Step 2 — Restrict to Steady Flow

For **steady** flow, $\partial \vec{V}/\partial t = 0$. Hence:

$$\rho (\vec{V}\cdot \nabla)\vec{V} = -\nabla p + \rho \vec{g}$$

### 3.2.3 Step 3 — Project onto the Streamline Direction

The dot product $(\vec{V}\cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$ represents the rate of change of velocity along the streamline. Mathematically, for a unit tangent vector $\hat{s}$ to the streamline, we have the vector identity:

$$(\vec{V}\cdot \nabla)\vec{V} = V\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial s} = V\frac{d\vec{V}}{ds}$$

Since $\vec{V}$ is along $\hat{s}$, $\frac{d\vec{V}}{ds} = \frac{d(V\hat{s})}{ds} = \hat{s}\frac{dV}{ds}$ (because $\hat{s}$ rotates slowly and the directional change of $\hat{s}$ over $ds$ is of higher order in many problems — but for clean derivation along streamline we accept $V\,dV/ds$).

So $(\vec{V}\cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$ projected on $\hat{s}$ becomes $V\,\dfrac{dV}{ds}$.

Similarly, projecting $\nabla p$ along the streamline gives $dp/ds$, and $\rho \vec{g}$ projected along the streamline contributes $-\rho g \, dz/ds$ (negative because gravity acts downward while $z$ is measured upward).

So Euler's equation along the streamline becomes:

$$\rho \, V \frac{dV}{ds} = -\frac{dp}{ds} - \rho g \frac{dz}{ds}$$

### 3.2.4 Step 4 — Rearrange to Isolate Differentials

Multiply both sides by $ds / \rho$:

$$V \, dV = -\frac{dp}{\rho} - g \, dz$$

This is **identical** to the differential equation we obtained in Step 6 of Route A — confirming the consistency of both derivations.

### 3.2.5 Step 5 — Integrate for Incompressible Flow

For incompressible flow, $\rho = $ constant:

$$\int V\,dV + \int \frac{dp}{\rho} + \int g\,dz = \text{constant}$$

$$\frac{V^{2}}{2} + \frac{p}{\rho} + gz = \text{constant (J/kg)}$$

Dividing by $g$:

$$\frac{p}{\rho g} + \frac{V^{2}}{2g} + z = \text{constant (m)} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.3 Side-by-Side Comparison Matrix (KTU Board-Ready)

| Aspect | Route A — First Principles | Route B — From Euler's Equation |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Starting point | Newton's $F = ma$ on a fluid parcel | Euler's differential momentum equation |
| Intermediate result | $V\,dV = -dp/\rho - g\,dz$ | $V\,dV = -dp/\rho - g\,dz$ |
| Final form | $\frac{p}{\rho g} + \frac{V^{2}}{2g} + z = C$ | $\frac{p}{\rho g} + \frac{V^{2}}{2g} + z = C$ |
| Assumption made explicit | Incompressibility, steady flow, inviscid | Incompressibility, steady flow, inviscid |
| Geometric content | Uses $\sin\theta = dz/ds$ directly | Uses projection along $\hat{s}$ |
| Vector content | Scalar along streamline | Vector equation projected to scalar |
| Examiners' view | Tests mechanics fundamentals | Tests vector fluid mechanics |

---

## 3.4 Limiting Case — Fluid at Rest (Hydrostatics)

If $V = 0$ everywhere, Bernoulli's equation reduces to:

$$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + z_2 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad p_2 - p_1 = -\rho g (z_2 - z_1) = \rho g h$$

This recovers the **hydrostatic pressure equation** — the basis of manometers and barometers.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Illustration (Builds Exam Readiness)

> **Problem:** Water ($\rho = 1000$ kg/m³) flows through a pipe. At section (1), the diameter is 200 mm, the pressure is 200 kPa, and the elevation is 4 m. At section (2), the diameter is 100 mm and the elevation is 6 m. The discharge is 30 L/s. Find the pressure at section (2).

**Solution:**

Velocities from continuity ($Q = AV$):

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.2)^{2} = 0.03142 \text{ m}^{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_1 = \frac{0.030}{0.03142} = 0.955 \text{ m/s}$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.1)^{2} = 0.007854 \text{ m}^{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_2 = \frac{0.030}{0.007854} = 3.820 \text{ m/s}$$

Apply Bernoulli (head form, divided by $\rho g$):

$$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^{2}}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^{2}}{2g} + z_2$$

$$\frac{200{,}000}{1000 \times 9.81} + \frac{(0.955)^{2}}{2 \times 9.81} + 4 = \frac{p_2}{9810} + \frac{(3.820)^{2}}{19.62} + 6$$

$$20.387 + 0.0465 + 4 = \frac{p_2}{9810} + 0.744 + 6$$

$$24.434 = \frac{p_2}{9810} + 6.744$$

$$\frac{p_2}{9810} = 17.690 \quad\Rightarrow\quad p_2 = 173{,}540 \text{ Pa} \approx 173.5 \text{ kPa}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Pressure drops from 200 kPa to 173.5 kPa because kinetic energy rose ($V_2 > V_1$) and the pipe rose ($z_2 > z_1$).

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**State Bernoulli's equation for the flow of an ideal fluid. List all the assumptions involved.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Bernoulli's equation for steady, incompressible, inviscid flow along a streamline between sections (1) and (2) is:
> $$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^{2}}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^{2}}{2g} + z_2$$
> 
> **Assumptions:**
> 1. Fluid is **inviscid** (frictionless).
> 2. Flow is **steady** (time-invariant).
> 3. Fluid is **incompressible** (ρ = constant).
> 4. Equation holds **along a single streamline** (or flow is irrotational).
> 5. **No shaft work** (no pump or turbine) is added or extracted between the sections.

*(Mark split: 1 for the equation, 2 for listing the five assumptions.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**The three terms of Bernoulli's equation represent three forms of energy per unit weight of a flowing fluid. Name them and write their SI units.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Term | Name of Energy | SI Unit (per unit weight) |
|:---:|:---|:---:|
| $\dfrac{p}{\rho g}$ | Pressure head (flow energy) | m |
| $\dfrac{V^{2}}{2g}$ | Velocity head (kinetic energy) | m |
| $z$ | Elevation head (potential energy) | m |

> The sum $H = \dfrac{p}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V^{2}}{2g} + z$ is the **total hydraulic head**, expressed in metres of fluid.

*(Mark split: 1 mark per correctly named term with unit.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Module 2 carries internal choice. Question A and Question B are independent alternatives. Each contains sub-parts (a) worth 7 marks and (b) worth 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Derive Bernoulli's equation from **first principles**, starting from Newton's second law applied to a fluid element moving along a streamline. State clearly the assumptions involved. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A horizontal pipe of diameter 300 mm carries water at a velocity of 3 m/s. The pipe narrows to a diameter of 150 mm. If the pressure at the larger section is 150 kPa, find the pressure at the narrower section. Take $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³ and $g = 9.81$ m/s². **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A (a) — Derivation from First Principles (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Define the element:** Consider an infinitesimal cylindrical fluid element of cross-section $dA$, length $ds$ along a streamline, with the streamline inclined at angle $\theta$ to horizontal. **(0.5 mark)**

**Step 2 — Mass of element:** $dm = \rho \, dA \, ds$. **(0.5 mark)**

**Step 3 — Forces:**
- Upstream pressure force = $p\,dA$ (forward)
- Downstream pressure force = $(p + dp)\,dA$ (backward)
- Component of weight along streamline = $\rho\,dA\,ds\,g\sin\theta$ (backward when uphill)

Net force = $p\,dA - (p+dp)\,dA - \rho\,dA\,ds\,g\sin\theta = -dp\,dA - \rho\,dA\,ds\,g\sin\theta$ **(2 marks)**

**Step 4 — Newton's second law along streamline:**
$$-dp\,dA - \rho\,dA\,ds\,g\sin\theta = \rho\,dA\,ds \cdot V\frac{dV}{ds}$$
**(1.5 marks)**

**Step 5 — Cancel $dA$ and use $\sin\theta = dz/ds$:**
$$V\,dV = -\frac{dp}{\rho} - g\,dz$$
**(1 mark)**

**Step 6 — Integrate between sections 1 and 2:**
$$\frac{V_2^{2}}{2} - \frac{V_1^{2}}{2} = -\frac{p_2 - p_1}{\rho} - g(z_2 - z_1)$$
Rearranging and dividing by $g$:
$$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^{2}}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^{2}}{2g} + z_2 \quad \blacksquare$$
**(1 mark)**

**Assumptions (any 3):** Steady, incompressible, inviscid, no shaft work, along a single streamline. **(0.5 mark)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A (b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

**Given:** $D_1 = 0.3$ m, $D_2 = 0.15$ m, $V_1 = 3$ m/s, $p_1 = 150$ kPa, horizontal so $z_1 = z_2$.

**Step 1 — Continuity equation:** $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$, so:
$$V_2 = V_1 \left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^{2} = 3 \times \left(\frac{0.3}{0.15}\right)^{2} = 3 \times 4 = 12 \text{ m/s} \quad \text{...(1.5 marks)}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Bernoulli** (horizontal, $z_1 = z_2$):
$$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^{2}}{2g} = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^{2}}{2g}$$
$$\frac{p_2}{\rho g} = \frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^{2} - V_2^{2}}{2g}$$
$$\frac{p_2}{\rho g} = \frac{150{,}000}{9810} + \frac{9 - 144}{19.62}$$
$$\frac{p_2}{\rho g} = 15.290 + \frac{-135}{19.62} = 15.290 - 6.881 = 8.409 \text{ m}$$
$$\boxed{p_2 = 8.409 \times 9810 = 82{,}492 \text{ Pa} \approx 82.5 \text{ kPa}} \quad \text{...(5.5 marks)}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Starting from Euler's equation of motion for an inviscid fluid, derive Bernoulli's equation. Clearly bring out the role of the streamline assumption. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Water flows steadily up a vertical pipe of diameter 150 mm at a velocity of 4 m/s. At a height of 6 m above the inlet, the pipe diameter reduces to 75 mm and the pressure is 80 kPa. Determine the pressure at the inlet. Assume ideal flow. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B (a) — From Euler's Equation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — State Euler's equation (vector form):**
$$\rho\frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \rho \vec{g}$$
$$\rho\left[\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)\vec{V}\right] = -\nabla p + \rho \vec{g} \quad \text{...(1.5 marks)}$$

**Step 2 — Steady flow** $\Rightarrow \partial \vec{V}/\partial t = 0$:
$$\rho(\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)\vec{V} = -\nabla p + \rho \vec{g} \quad \text{...(0.5 mark)}$$

**Step 3 — Project onto streamline direction $\hat{s}$:**
Use the vector identity $(\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)\vec{V} = V\,d\vec{V}/ds$, and $\nabla p \cdot \hat{s} = dp/ds$, $\vec{g}\cdot\hat{s} = -g\,dz/ds$:
$$\rho V \frac{dV}{ds} = -\frac{dp}{ds} - \rho g\frac{dz}{ds} \quad \text{...(2 marks)}$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange and integrate:**
Multiply by $ds/\rho$:
$$V\,dV + \frac{dp}{\rho} + g\,dz = 0$$
For incompressible flow ($\rho$ constant), integrate between sections 1 and 2:
$$\frac{V^{2}}{2} + \frac{p}{\rho} + gz = \text{constant}$$
Dividing by $g$:
$$\frac{p}{\rho g} + \frac{V^{2}}{2g} + z = \text{constant} \quad \blacksquare \quad \text{...(2 marks)}$$

**Role of the streamline assumption:** The reduction $(\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)\vec{V} \to V\,dV/ds$ is valid only when motion is along a *single streamline* (because the cross-stream terms vanish identically only along such a line). Hence Bernoulli's equation strictly holds between two points on the **same streamline**. **(0.5 mark)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B (b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

**Given:** $D_1 = 0.15$ m, $V_1 = 4$ m/s, $z_2 - z_1 = 6$ m, $D_2 = 0.075$ m, $p_2 = 80$ kPa.

**Step 1 — Find $V_2$ from continuity:**
$$V_2 = V_1\left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^{2} = 4 \times \left(\frac{0.15}{0.075}\right)^{2} = 4 \times 4 = 16 \text{ m/s} \quad \text{...(1.5 marks)}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Bernoulli's equation between (1) and (2):**
$$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^{2}}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^{2}}{2g} + z_2$$
With $z_2 = z_1 + 6$:
$$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^{2} - V_1^{2}}{2g} + (z_2 - z_1) \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:**
$$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} = \frac{80{,}000}{9810} + \frac{256 - 16}{19.62} + 6$$
$$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} = 8.155 + 12.232 + 6 = 26.387 \text{ m} \quad \text{...(3.5 marks)}$$

$$\boxed{p_1 = 26.387 \times 9810 = 258{,}857 \text{ Pa} \approx 258.86 \text{ kPa}} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks — Board Examiner Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to state assumptions explicitly (–1 mark).** Examiners always allocate marks for the *assumptions* of Bernoulli's equation. Omitting "steady", "incompressible", "inviscid", or "along a streamline" costs you dearly.
> 
> 2. **Sign errors on the gravity term (–1 to –2 marks).** The component $\rho g \, dz$ is *subtracted* (or written as $-g\,dz$) in the integrated form. Many students flip the sign.
> 
> 3. **Confusing the two derivation routes (–1 mark).** The differential form $V\,dV = -dp/\rho - g\,dz$ is the *pivot*. Both routes arrive at it. If you write it incorrectly, every subsequent step collapses.
> 
> 4. **Unit inconsistency (–1 mark).** Always express in **SI units**: m, m/s, Pa, kg/m³. Mixing mm, cm, bar, and kgf/cm² is the fastest route to marks being deducted.
> 
> 5. **Ignoring the elevation head** when the problem says "horizontal". For a horizontal pipe, $z_1 = z_2$, so the $z$ term cancels — *mention this explicitly* (examiners reward the explicit recognition).
> 
> 6. **Not converting kPa → Pa** before dividing by $\rho g$. The pitfall: writing $p/\rho g$ with $p$ in kPa without conversion gives a 1000× wrong answer.
> 
> 7. **Stating "Bernoulli's equation is the law of conservation of energy".** This is *partially correct but imprecise*. The technically correct statement is: *conservation of mechanical energy along a streamline for an inviscid, incompressible, steady flow*. Examiners want the qualifiers.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 2 / Bernoulli's Equation**
> 
> **The Equation Itself**
> - ☐ Standard head form: $\frac{p}{\rho g} + \frac{V^{2}}{2g} + z = $ constant along a streamline.
> - ☐ Energy-per-unit-mass form: $\frac{p}{\rho} + \frac{V^{2}}{2} + gz = $ constant (J/kg).
> - ☐ Energy-per-unit-volume form: $p + \frac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} + \rho g z = $ constant (Pa).
> 
> **The Five Assumptions**
> - ☐ Inviscid (μ = 0)
> - ☐ Steady flow ($\partial/\partial t = 0$)
> - ☐ Incompressible (ρ = constant)
> - ☐ Along a single streamline (or irrotational flow)
> - ☐ No shaft work (no pump/turbine in the control volume)
> 
> **The Derivations**
> - ☐ Route A: Newton's $F = ma$ on a fluid element with three forces (pressure upstream, pressure downstream, weight).
> - ☐ Route B: Euler's vector equation projected onto the streamline.
> - ☐ Both yield the *same* differential form: $V\,dV + dp/\rho + g\,dz = 0$.
> - ☐ Geometric identity: $dz = ds\sin\theta$.
> - ☐ Kinematic identity: $\frac{DV}{Dt} = V\frac{dV}{ds}$.
> 
> **Numerical Must-Knows**
> - ☐ Continuity equation: $A_1V_1 = A_2V_2$.
> - ☐ Velocity ratio from diameter ratio: $V_2/V_1 = (D_1/D_2)^{2}$.
> - ☐ Standard values: $\rho_{\text{water}} = 1000$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s².
> - ☐ $1 \text{ atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 10.33 \text{ m of water}$.
> 
> **Limiting Cases**
> - ☐ $V = 0$ everywhere $\Rightarrow$ hydrostatic: $p_2 = p_1 + \rho g(z_1 - z_2)$.
> - ☐ Horizontal pipe ($z_1 = z_2$) $\Rightarrow$ $p + \frac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} = $ constant.
> 
> **Engineering Tie-Ins to Memorize**
> - ☐ Pitot tube, Venturi meter, orifice meter, aerofoil lift, atomizer — all are direct applications of Bernoulli.
> 
> **Examiner Pet Questions**
> - ☐ "State and prove Bernoulli's equation from Euler's equation" (every Dec/May cycle).
> - ☐ "Differentiate between Euler's equation and Bernoulli's equation" (3-mark short answer).
> - ☐ "Why does Bernoulli's equation hold strictly along a streamline and not between streamlines?"

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Limitations of Bernoulli's Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT741 - FLUID MECHANICS & MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Limitations of Bernoulli's Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Bernoulli's Equation — Formal Statement

Bernoulli's equation is the **principle of conservation of mechanical energy** applied to a steady, incompressible, inviscid fluid flowing along a streamline. Mathematically, it states that the sum of the **pressure head**, **velocity head (kinetic head)**, and **elevation head (potential head)** remains constant between any two points along a streamline.

$$\frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + z = \text{Constant}$$

Where each term represents energy per unit weight of the fluid (i.e., a "head" measured in metres):

- $\frac{P}{\rho g}$ = Pressure head (m)
- $\frac{V^2}{2g}$ = Velocity head / Kinetic head (m)
- $z$ = Elevation head / Datum head (m)
- $\rho$ = Mass density of the fluid (kg/m³)
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Expectation):**
> "For a steady, incompressible, frictionless fluid flow along a streamline, the sum of pressure head, velocity head, and elevation head at any point is constant."

### 1.2 The 6 Fundamental Assumptions Behind Bernoulli's Equation

| # | Assumption | Implication |
|---|------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Steady Flow** | Flow parameters (V, P) do not change with time |
| 2 | **Incompressible Flow** | Fluid density $\rho$ is constant |
| 3 | **Inviscid Flow** | No viscous/shear stresses (frictionless) |
| 4 | **Flow Along a Streamline** | Equation applies point-to-point along the *same* streamline |
| 5 | **No External Energy Addition** | No pump, turbine, compressor, or heat transfer |
| 6 | **Uniform Velocity Profile** | Single value of V across any cross-section |

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — "The Garden Hose Effect"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Hold your thumb partially over a garden hose nozzle. What happens?
> - The water **speeds up** (Velocity head $\uparrow$)
> - The **pressure drops** at the nozzle (Pressure head $\downarrow$)
> - The elevation is essentially constant
>
> **Bernoulli's equation explains this trade-off**: if total energy is conserved, an increase in kinetic energy *must* be balanced by a decrease in pressure energy. This is the same principle that makes aeroplane wings generate lift — air moving faster over the curved upper surface creates lower pressure.

When *any* of the 6 assumptions is violated, the equation **breaks down**. Understanding these violations is the key to identifying the **limitations** of Bernoulli's equation — the focus of this module.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Limitations of Bernoulli's Equation

#### Limitation 1: Violation of Steady Flow Assumption

Bernoulli's equation is derived assuming $\frac{\partial V}{\partial t} = 0$. If the flow is **unsteady** (e.g., pulsating flow in a reciprocating pump line, water hammer, surge tanks), the local acceleration term must be retained, and Bernoulli's equation **no longer holds**.

**Why it fails:** The Euler equation contains a $\rho \frac{\partial V}{\partial t}$ term. Setting it to zero removes a major energy component.

#### Limitation 2: Violation of Incompressible Flow Assumption

If $\rho \neq \text{constant}$ (e.g., high-speed gas flow in rockets, steam in turbines, or flow with significant temperature/pressure variations), the density cannot be taken outside the integral $\int \frac{dP}{\rho}$. The equation becomes:

$$\int \frac{dP}{\rho} + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz = \text{constant}$$

This **compressible form** must be used. **Rule of thumb:** Bernoulli's standard form is valid when Mach Number $M < 0.3$.

#### Limitation 3: Violation of Inviscid Flow Assumption (Most Critical Limitation)

Real fluids have viscosity, which causes **frictional losses** between fluid layers and with the pipe walls. This leads to:

- **Friction head loss** ($h_f$) in pipes (Darcy-Weisbach equation)
- **Minor losses** ($h_m$) at fittings, bends, valves, contractions, expansions

The **Modified (or Real) Bernoulli's Equation** accounts for this:

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$$

Where $h_L = h_f + h_m$ is the total head loss (m).

#### Limitation 4: Violation of Streamline Constraint

Bernoulli's equation applies **only along a single streamline**. In **rotational flow** (e.g., flow inside a vortex, centrifugal pump impeller, fluid in a stirred tank), the constant value of Bernoulli's equation differs from one streamline to another.

**Practical consequence:** You cannot use the same Bernoulli constant at the inlet and outlet of a centrifugal pump impeller because the flow is highly rotational.

#### Limitation 5: Violation of No External Work Assumption

If a pump or turbine is present between sections 1 and 2, an additional energy term is required:

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 + H_{pump} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + H_{turbine} + h_L$$

#### Limitation 6: Violation of Uniform Velocity Profile

Bernoulli's equation assumes a single velocity V at each cross-section. In practice, **boundary layers** develop near walls with $V=0$ at the wall and $V=V_{max}$ at the centre. An **average velocity** $\bar{V}$ must be used with a **kinetic energy correction factor** $\alpha$:

$$\frac{P}{\rho g} + \alpha \frac{V^2}{2g} + z = \text{constant}$$

Where:
- $\alpha = 2$ for laminar flow (parabolic profile)
- $\alpha = 1.05 - 1.10$ for turbulent flow

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Equation | Mathematical Form | Applicability / Limitation Addressed |
|---|---|---|
| Standard Bernoulli's Equation | $\frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + z = C$ | Ideal case (all 6 assumptions hold) |
| Modified Bernoulli's Equation | $\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$ | Viscous flow (Limitation 3) |
| Bernoulli with Pump/Turbine | $\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 + H_p - H_t = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$ | External work (Limitation 5) |
| With Kinetic Energy Correction | $\frac{P}{\rho g} + \alpha \frac{V^2}{2g} + z = C$ | Non-uniform velocity (Limitation 6) |
| Compressible Form | $\int \frac{dP}{\rho} + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz = C$ | $\rho \neq$ constant (Limitation 2) |
| Darcy-Weisbach (friction loss) | $h_f = \frac{f L V^2}{2 g D}$ | Pipe friction component of $h_L$ |
| Minor Loss | $h_m = K \frac{V^2}{2g}$ | Fittings, valves, sudden changes |
| Mach Number Criterion | $M = \frac{V}{c} < 0.3$ | Incompressibility threshold |
| Reynolds Number | $Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu}$ | Flow regime (laminar / turbulent) |
| Energy Added by Pump | $H_p = \frac{P_{shaft}}{\rho g Q}$ | Power input per unit weight flow |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Symbols:** Always use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `\vert` literal pipe characters when writing `V \vert \vert` inside tables. The above uses proper mathematical notation to remain table-safe.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility of These Limitations

| Engineering Application | Why Limitations Matter |
|---|---|
| **Water Supply Networks** | Friction losses in long pipelines demand the modified form; pump energy must be added |
| **Aircraft Aerodynamics** | Inviscid assumption is *acceptable* outside boundary layer; fails in boundary layer near wings |
| **Rocket Nozzle Design** | Compressibility dominates ($M > 0.3$); standard Bernoulli is **invalid** |
| **Centrifugal Pump Design** | Rotational flow inside impeller means streamline assumption breaks |
| **Turbomachinery (Steam/Gas Turbines)** | Energy addition by blades and compressibility both violate assumptions |
| **Venturi Meters** | Inviscid Bernoulli works well at high Re; correction factor needed at low Re |
| **Pitot-Static Tubes** | Steady, nearly inviscid flow near stagnation point → Bernoulli is valid here |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Bernoulli's Equation from Euler's Equation of Motion

The purpose of this derivation is to show **exactly where each assumption enters** so that the limitations become self-evident.

#### Step 1: Start with Euler's Equation of Motion (for a fluid particle along a streamline)

For an inviscid fluid, Newton's second law applied to a fluid element along a streamline gives:

$$-\frac{\partial P}{\partial s} = \rho \frac{\partial V}{\partial t} + \rho V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s} + \rho g \frac{\partial z}{\partial s}$$

Where $s$ is the direction along the streamline.

#### Step 2: Apply the Steady Flow Assumption (Limitation 1 Anchor)

For steady flow, local acceleration vanishes:

$$\frac{\partial V}{\partial t} = 0$$

The Euler equation reduces to:

$$-\frac{\partial P}{\partial s} = \rho V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s} + \rho g \frac{\partial z}{\partial s}$$

**Note for KTU:** If $\frac{\partial V}{\partial t} \neq 0$, we cannot proceed — this is the **unsteady flow limitation**.

#### Step 3: Apply the Incompressible Flow Assumption (Limitation 2 Anchor)

For incompressible flow:

$$\rho = \text{constant}$$

Dividing Euler's equation by $\rho g$:

$$-\frac{1}{\rho g} \frac{\partial P}{\partial s} = \frac{V}{g} \frac{\partial V}{\partial s} + \frac{\partial z}{\partial s}$$

**Note for KTU:** If $\rho$ varies with $P$ (compressible flow), we cannot divide-and-integrate easily — this is the **compressibility limitation**.

#### Step 4: Rewrite Each Term as an Exact Differential

Recognize the velocity term as:

$$\frac{V}{g} \frac{\partial V}{\partial s} = \frac{\partial}{\partial s}\left(\frac{V^2}{2g}\right)$$

Thus:

$$-\frac{\partial}{\partial s}\left(\frac{P}{\rho g}\right) = \frac{\partial}{\partial s}\left(\frac{V^2}{2g}\right) + \frac{\partial z}{\partial s}$$

#### Step 5: Cross-Multiply by $ds$ (Infinitesimal Displacement Along Streamline)

$$-\frac{\partial P}{\partial s} ds = \rho V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s} ds + \rho g \frac{\partial z}{\partial s} ds$$

Multiply through:

$$-dP = \rho V dV + \rho g dz$$

#### Step 6: Divide by $\rho g$

$$-\frac{dP}{\rho g} = \frac{V dV}{g} + dz$$

Rearrange:

$$d\left(\frac{P}{\rho g}\right) + d\left(\frac{V^2}{2g}\right) + dz = 0$$

#### Step 7: Integrate Along the Streamline Between Section 1 and Section 2

$$\int_1^2 d\left(\frac{P}{\rho g}\right) + \int_1^2 d\left(\frac{V^2}{2g}\right) + \int_1^2 dz = 0$$

$$\left[\frac{P}{\rho g}\right]_1^2 + \left[\frac{V^2}{2g}\right]_1^2 + [z]_1^2 = 0$$

#### Step 8: Apply Boundary Conditions

$$\frac{P_2}{\rho g} - \frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} - \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_2 - z_1 = 0$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$

**This is Bernoulli's equation** — proven rigorously.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight (Exam Favourite):** The derivation used *inviscid* assumption right at Step 1 (Newton's law had no shear stress $\tau$). It used *steady* at Step 2. It used *incompressible* at Step 3. It used *along streamline* at Steps 4-7. **Every limitation maps to a specific derivation step.**

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Assumption-to-Equation Mapping

| Assumption | Corresponding Derivation Step | What Happens If Violated | Engineering Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Steady flow | Step 2: $\frac{\partial V}{\partial t} = 0$ | Local acceleration term must remain | Water hammer in pipelines |
| Incompressible | Step 3: $\rho = $ const | Density varies with P, must use $\int dP/\rho$ | Rocket nozzle exit |
| Inviscid | Step 1: No shear stress $\tau$ | Friction term must be added as $h_L$ | Oil flow in long pipes |
| Along streamline | Steps 4-7: Integration along $s$ | Different streamlines have different constants | Vortex core flow |
| No external work | Energy balance closure | Pump/turbine term $H_p$ or $H_t$ added | Pump station in pipeline |
| Uniform V | Single-value V used | Use $\alpha$ (correction factor) | Flow near pipe walls |

### 3.3 Modified Bernoulli's Equation — Step-by-Step Modification

Starting from the standard form, we **add** head loss terms and energy input/output terms to overcome limitations 3 and 5:

**Step A:** Start with ideal form:

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$

**Step B:** Add head loss $h_L$ to the right side (energy lost between 1 and 2):

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$$

**Step C:** Add pump head $H_p$ to the left and turbine head $H_t$ to the right:

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 + H_p = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + H_t + h_L$$

**Step D:** Apply kinetic energy correction factor for non-uniform velocity:

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \alpha_1 \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 + H_p = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \alpha_2 \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + H_t + h_L$$

This is the **complete form used in real KTU numerical problems**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Assumption Validation Logic for Bernoulli's Equation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Apply Bernoulli's Equation?"] --> B{"Is Flow Steady?"}
    B -- "NO" --> B1["INVALID: Unsteady flow"]
    B -- "YES" --> C{"Is Fluid Incompressible?"}
    C -- "NO, M greater than 0.3" --> C1["INVALID: Use compressible form"]
    C -- "YES" --> D{"Is Flow Inviscid or Losses Negligible?"}
    D -- "NO, high friction" --> D1["USE MODIFIED form with hL term"]
    D -- "YES" --> E{"Along Single Streamline?"}
    E -- "NO, rotational flow" --> E1["INVALID across streamlines"]
    E -- "YES" --> F{"No Pump or Turbine Between Sections?"}
    F -- "NO, pump present" --> F1["ADD Hpump or Hturbine term"]
    F -- "YES" --> G["STANDARD Bernoulli Equation VALID"]
    G --> H["Apply P/rho g plus V squared by 2g plus z equals constant"]

    B1 --> X["STOP"]
    C1 --> X
    D1 --> X
    E1 --> X
    F1 --> X
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Energy Flow Between Two Sections of a Real Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["SECTION 1: Inlet"] -->|Total Head H1| P1["Pipe + Friction h_f"]
    P1 -->|Major Loss h_f| P2["Fittings + Minor Loss h_m"]
    P2 -->|Minor Loss h_m| P3["Pump Input H_pump"]
    P3 -->|Energy Added H_pump| S2["SECTION 2: Outlet"]
    S1 -->|Without Pump path| T1["Turbine Extracts H_turbine"]
    T1 --> S2

    S1 --> S1A["P1 by rho g"]
    S1 --> S1B["V1 squared by 2g"]
    S1 --> S1C["Elevation z1"]

    S2 --> S2A["P2 by rho g"]
    S2 --> S2B["V2 squared by 2g"]
    S2 --> S2C["Elevation z2"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map — Hierarchy of Limitations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT["LIMITATIONS OF BERNOULLI'S EQUATION"] --> L1["L1: Steady Flow Required"]
    ROOT --> L2["L2: Incompressible Flow Required"]
    ROOT --> L3["L3: Inviscid Flow Required"]
    ROOT --> L4["L4: Single Streamline Constraint"]
    ROOT --> L5["L5: No External Energy Exchange"]
    ROOT --> L6["L6: Uniform Velocity Profile"]

    L1 --> L1A["Fails in water hammer"]
    L1 --> L1B["Fails in pulsating flow"]
    L2 --> L2A["Fails when Mach greater than 0.3"]
    L2 --> L2B["Fails in gas turbines"]
    L3 --> L3A["Fails in viscous oil flow"]
    L3 --> L3B["Fails at low Reynolds number"]
    L4 --> L4A["Fails in vortex flow"]
    L4 --> L4B["Fails in centrifugal pump"]
    L5 --> L5A["Fails in pump pipelines"]
    L5 --> L5B["Fails in turbine systems"]
    L6 --> L6A["Fails near boundaries"]
```
