## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Define Reynolds number. What is its significance in pipe flow? State the limits of Reynolds number for laminar and turbulent flow in pipes.**

**Model Answer:**

Reynolds number (Re) is a dimensionless number that represents the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces in a fluid flow. It is defined as:

$$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{V D}{\nu}$$

**Significance**: Reynolds number indicates the nature of the flow regime — whether the flow is laminar, transitional, or turbulent. It also helps in dynamic similarity between model and prototype.

**Limits for pipe flow**:
- **Laminar flow**: $\text{Re} < 2000$
- **Transitional flow**: $2000 \leq \text{Re} \leq 4000$
- **Turbulent flow**: $\text{Re} > 4000$

---

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**What is meant by minor losses in pipes? List any four types of minor losses and give the corresponding loss coefficients for sharp-edged entries.**

**Model Answer:**

Minor losses are the losses of head (energy) in a pipe flow system due to the presence of pipe fittings, bends, valves, sudden contractions, sudden enlargements, and entry/exit conditions — as opposed to the **major loss** due to friction along the straight length.

**Four types of minor losses:**

| # | Type of Loss | Loss Coefficient $K$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sharp-edged entrance | $K = 0.5$ |
| 2 | Re-entrant (Borda) entrance | $K = 1.0$ |
| 3 | 90° standard elbow | $K \approx 0.9$ |
| 4 | Globe valve (fully open) | $K \approx 10$ |

The head loss for any minor loss is computed as:

$$h_L = K \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

**(a)** Derive the **Hagen–Poiseuille equation** for steady, laminar flow through a circular pipe. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Oil with dynamic viscosity $\mu = 0.9$ N·s/m² and specific gravity 0.85 flows through a horizontal pipe of diameter 50 mm and length 12 m under laminar conditions. The pressure drop across the pipe length is 200 kPa. Determine: **(i)** the maximum velocity, **(ii)** the average velocity, **(iii)** the discharge, and **(iv)** the friction factor. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Assumptions**:
1. Steady, incompressible, laminar flow.
2. Newtonian fluid (constant viscosity).
3. Pipe is horizontal, of constant circular cross-section.
4. Fully developed flow (velocity profile does not change along the length).
5. No-slip condition at the wall.

**Derivation** (refer to Section 3.1 for full derivation):

1. Force balance on a cylindrical fluid element of radius $r$ and length $L$:

$$p_1 \pi r^2 - p_2 \pi r^2 = \tau \cdot 2 \pi r L$$

2. Shear stress from Newton's law of viscosity:

$$\tau = -\mu \frac{du}{dr}$$

3. Equating and integrating with boundary conditions $u = u_{\max}$ at $r = 0$ and $u = 0$ at $r = R$:

$$u(r) = \frac{(p_1 - p_2)}{4 \mu L} (R^2 - r^2)$$

4. Integrating velocity over the cross-section to obtain discharge:

$$Q = \int_0^R u(r) \, 2 \pi r \, dr = \frac{\pi (p_1 - p_2) R^4}{8 \mu L} = \frac{\pi \rho g h_f D^4}{128 \mu L}$$

5. Head loss form:

$$h_f = \frac{32 \mu V L}{\rho g D^2}$$

---

**Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given**:
- $\mu = 0.9$ N·s/m²
- $SG = 0.85 \Rightarrow \rho = 850$ kg/m³
- $D = 50$ mm $= 0.05$ m
- $L = 12$ m
- $\Delta p = 200$ kPa $= 200{,}000$ Pa

**Step 1: Compute head loss**

$$h_f = \frac{\Delta p}{\rho g} = \frac{200{,}000}{850 \times 9.81} = \frac{200{,}000}{8338.5} = 23.987 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Compute the maximum velocity** $u_{\max}$

For laminar flow, the velocity profile is:

$$u_{\max} = \frac{(p_1 - p_2) R^2}{4 \mu L}$$

where $R = D/2 = 0.025$ m.

$$u_{\max} = \frac{200{,}000 \times (0.025)^2}{4 \times 0.9 \times 12} = \frac{200{,}000 \times 0.000625}{43.2} = \frac{125}{43.2}$$

$$\boxed{u_{\max} = 2.894 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Step 3: Compute the average (mean) velocity** $V$

For laminar flow, $V = u_{\max} / 2$:

$$\boxed{V = \frac{2.894}{2} = 1.447 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Step 4: Compute the discharge** $Q$

$$Q = A \cdot V = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \times V = \frac{\pi (0.05)^2}{4} \times 1.447$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \times 0.0025}{4} \times 1.447 = 0.001963 \times 1.447 = 2.841 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{Q = 2.841 \text{ L/s} = 2.841 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}}$$

**Step 5: Verify the flow is laminar — compute Reynolds number**

$$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{850 \times 1.447 \times 0.05}{0.9} = \frac{61.50}{0.9} = 68.3$$

Since $\text{Re} = 68.3 \ll 2000$, the laminar assumption is valid. ✓

**Step 6: Compute the Darcy friction factor**

$$f = \frac{64}{\text{Re}} = \frac{64}{68.3}$$

$$\boxed{f = 0.937}$$

**Cross-verification using Darcy–Weisbach:**

$$h_f = \frac{f L V^2}{2 g D} = \frac{0.937 \times 12 \times (1.447)^2}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.05}$$

$$h_f = \frac{0.937 \times 12 \times 2.094}{0.981} = \frac{23.55}{0.981} = 24.00 \text{ m} \approx 23.99 \text{ m} \quad \checkmark$$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe **Reynolds' experiment** to determine the critical Reynolds number for pipe flow. Explain the observations made during the experiment. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Water flows through a 100 mm diameter pipe at a velocity of 3 m/s. The pipe has a sudden enlargement to 200 mm diameter. **(i)** Calculate the head loss due to sudden enlargement. **(ii)** What is the pressure rise in the enlarged section? **(iii)** If the pipe length between the two sections is 6 m with friction factor 0.025, find the total head loss. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Apparatus** (refer to Section 4.1 for the schematic):
1. A constant-head overhead tank (A) supplies water through a **bell-mouthed inlet** to a **transparent glass test pipe** of constant diameter.
2. A **dye reservoir** injects a fine thread of colored dye (potassium permanganate solution) along the **centerline** of the glass pipe via a needle.
3. A **control valve** at the outlet regulates the discharge.
4. A **measuring tank** collects the outflow for discharge measurement.
5. A **thermometer** records water temperature (for viscosity correction).
6. A **U-tube manometer** measures pressure drop across a known length of the pipe.

**Procedure**:
1. The tank is filled and water flows steadily through the glass pipe.
2. Initially, the valve is **almost closed**, so velocity is very low.
3. Dye is injected; it travels as a **straight, unbroken, smooth line** along the centerline — this indicates **laminar flow**.
4. The valve is **opened gradually** to increase velocity.
5. At a **critical velocity** $V_c$, the dye thread **oscillates and breaks** into eddies — this is the **lower critical velocity** marking the **onset of turbulence** (the **upper critical velocity** marks the return from turbulent to laminar).
6. The discharge and corresponding head are measured to compute velocity and Reynolds number.

**Observations**:
- **Laminar zone** ($\text{Re} < 2000$): Dye remains a continuous thread. Flow is smooth, orderly, predictable.
- **Transitional zone** ($2000 < \text{Re} < 4000$): Dye thread fluctuates; intermittent bursts of turbulence.
- **Turbulent zone** ($\text{Re} > 4000$): Dye diffuses rapidly across the entire cross-section; flow is chaotic, with eddies and mixing.

**Reynolds concluded**: The dimensionless ratio $\text{Re} = \rho V D / \mu$ determines the flow regime, independent of the specific pipe dimensions or fluid used.

---

**Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given**:
- $D_1 = 100$ mm $= 0.1$ m
- $D_2 = 200$ mm $= 0.2$ m
- $V_1 = 3$ m/s
- $L = 6$ m
- $f = 0.025$

**Step 1: Compute the velocity in the enlarged section** $V_2$ using continuity

$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\pi D_1^2}{4} V_1 = \frac{\pi D_2^2}{4} V_2$$

$$V_2 = V_1 \left( \frac{D_1}{D_2} \right)^2 = 3 \times \left( \frac{0.1}{0.2} \right)^2 = 3 \times 0.25 = 0.75 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Compute the head loss due to sudden enlargement** (Borda–Carnot)

$$h_L = \frac{(V_1 - V_2)^2}{2g} = \frac{(3 - 0.75)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{(2.25)^2}{19.62} = \frac{5.0625}{19.62}$$

$$\boxed{h_L = 0.258 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 3: Compute the pressure rise** $\Delta p$ in the enlarged section

Apply the energy equation between sections 1 and 2 (horizontal pipe, no friction in the short enlargement region):

$$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + h_L$$

Pressure rise (the increase from $p_1$ to $p_2$, accounting for loss):

$$\frac{p_2 - p_1}{\rho g} = \frac{V_1^2 - V_2^2}{2g} - h_L$$

$$\frac{p_2 - p_1}{\rho g} = \frac{(3)^2 - (0.75)^2}{2 \times 9.81} - 0.258 = \frac{9 - 0.5625}{19.62} - 0.258$$

$$\frac{p_2 - p_1}{\rho g} = \frac{8.4375}{19.62} - 0.258 = 0.4300 - 0.258 = 0.172 \text{ m}$$

Taking $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³ for water:

$$p_2 - p_1 = 0.172 \times 1000 \times 9.81 = 1688 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta p = p_2 - p_1 = 1.688 \text{ kPa (pressure rise)}}$$

**Step 4: Compute the friction head loss in the 6 m pipe length**

Using Darcy–Weisbach with the velocity in section 1 ($V_1 = 3$ m/s) and $D_1 = 0.1$ m:

$$h_f = \frac{f L V_1^2}{2 g D_1} = \frac{0.025 \times 6 \times (3)^2}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.1} = \frac{0.025 \times 6 \times 9}{1.962} = \frac{1.35}{1.962}$$

$$\boxed{h_f = 0.688 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 5: Compute the total head loss**

$$H_{\text{total}} = h_f + h_L = 0.688 + 0.258$$

$$\boxed{H_{\text{total}} = 0.946 \text{ m}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Deduction Points**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $D^4$ term**: In the Hagen–Poiseuille equation, a unit-conversion mistake (mm vs m) is the #1 cause of wrong answers. Always convert diameters to **meters** before substituting.
> 2. **Mixing Darcy and Fanning $f$**: KTU uses the **Darcy form** $h_f = f L V^2 / (2gD)$ with $f = 64/\text{Re}$. The Fanning form has an extra factor of 4. State the form clearly in the solution.
> 3. **Sign convention in Borda–Carnot**: Some students write $V_1 - V_2$ instead of $(V_1 - V_2)^2$. The squared term is **mandatory** — both positive and negative differences give the same head loss.
> 4. **Pressure rise vs. pressure drop**: When the pipe enlarges, there is a **partial pressure recovery** (Bernoulli effect), but the **net** pressure downstream is still lower than upstream by $h_L$ (loss). Compute carefully.
> 5. **Reynolds number regime check**: After solving, always verify $\text{Re} < 2000$ (laminar) or $\text{Re} > 4000$ (turbulent) to confirm the assumed formula is valid. KTU examiners **deduct up to 1 mark** for missing this verification.
> 6. **Chezy vs. Manning confusion**: KTU sometimes asks for **Chezy's coefficient** $C$ vs. **Manning's $n$**. They are related but not interchangeable.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 3 (Flow Through Pipes)**

- [x] **Reynolds Number** is the master classification parameter: $\text{Re} = \rho V D / \mu$.
- [x] **Laminar** ($\text{Re} < 2000$): Parabolic profile, $V = u_{\max}/2$, $f = 64/\text{Re}$.
- [x] **Transitional** ($2000 \leq \text{Re} \leq 4000$): Unstable; avoid designing in this range.
- [x] **Turbulent** ($\text{Re} > 4000$): Blunter profile, use Colebrook / Moody chart / Blasius.
- [x] **Hagen–Poiseuille**: $h_f = 32 \mu V L / (\rho g D^2)$ — **laminar only**.
- [x] **Darcy–Weisbach**: $h_f = f L V^2 / (2 g D)$ — **universal**, but $f$ differs in each regime.
- [x] **Chezy's Formula**: $V = C \sqrt{R i}$; discharge form $Q = A C \sqrt{R i}$.
- [x] **Borda–Carnot Loss** (sudden enlargement): $h_L = (V_1 - V_2)^2 / (2g)$.
- [x] **Sudden Contraction Loss**: $h_L \approx 0.5 V_2^2 / (2g)$ for sharp-edged entry.
- [x] **Generic Minor Loss**: $h_m = K V^2 / (2g)$ — $K$ depends on fitting type.
- [x] **Total Head Loss**: $H_L = h_f + \sum h_m$ (superposition principle).
- [x] **Hydraulic Radius for circular pipe**: $R = D/4$ (since $A = \pi D^2/4$, $P = \pi D$).
- [x] **Always verify the flow regime** (laminar vs. turbulent) before choosing the formula.
- [x] **Convert all units to SI** (m, m/s, Pa, N·s/m²) before any numerical substitution.
- [x] **Remember** $g = 9.81$ m/s² and $\rho_{\text{water}} = 1000$ kg/m³ unless stated otherwise.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Turbomachines: Definition, Parts of a Turbomachine, Hydraulic Turbines - Classification, Constructional Features
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT741 - FLUID MECHANICS & MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Turbomachines: Definition, Parts of a Turbomachine, Hydraulic Turbines - Classification, Constructional Features

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of a Turbomachine

A **Turbomachine** is defined as a device in which energy is transferred between a moving fluid (liquid or gas) and a rotating element (the rotor), either to extract energy from the fluid (as in turbines) or to impart energy to the fluid (as in pumps, compressors, and blowers).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "A turbomachine is a fluid machine in which a continuous flow of fluid is modified by dynamic interaction between the fluid and the rotating element, resulting in a continuous exchange of energy."

The word **"Turbo"** is derived from the Latin word *turbo* meaning *whirlwind* or *spinning*, signifying the rotational nature of energy transfer.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a turbomachine like a **pinwheel** held under running water:
- If water hits the blades and makes the wheel spin → **Turbine** (extracts energy from fluid).
- If you blow air into the wheel and the spinning blades push the air forward → **Pump/Fan/Compressor** (imparts energy to fluid).

Just as a pinwheel converts water's kinetic energy into rotational motion, a turbomachine uses the same principle, but at an industrial scale — handling massive volumes of water, steam, or air to generate electricity or transport fluids.

### 1.3 Two Broad Classifications of Turbomachines

> [!NOTE]
> All turbomachines fall into one of two fundamental categories based on **energy transfer direction**:

| S.No | Category | Energy Transfer | Examples |
|------|----------|-----------------|----------|
| 1 | **Turbines** | Fluid → Rotor (Energy Extraction) | Hydraulic Turbines, Steam Turbines, Gas Turbines |
| 2 | **Compressors / Pumps** | Rotor → Fluid (Energy Addition) | Centrifugal Pumps, Axial Compressors, Blowers |

### 1.4 Parts of a Turbomachine (Universal Components)

Every turbomachine, regardless of type, contains the following essential parts:

1. **Rotor** — The rotating member fitted with blades that interact with the fluid.
2. **Stator (Casing)** — The stationary housing that guides the fluid into and out of the rotor.
3. **Guide Vanes / Stator Blades** — Stationary blades that direct the fluid at the desired angle onto the rotor blades.
4. **Shaft** — Transmits the mechanical energy from/to the rotor.
5. **Bearings** — Support the rotating shaft and reduce friction.
6. **Seals** — Prevent leakage of fluid along the shaft or between stationary/rotating parts.
7. **Inlet and Outlet Nozzles (or Ducts)** — Channel the fluid into and out of the machine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy metrics to remember:**
> - **Net Head (H):** Measured in **metres (m)** of fluid — characteristic of hydraulic machines.
> - **Specific Speed (Ns):** Dimensionless or in **metric units (rpm·m·kW⁻⁰·⁷⁵)**.
> - **Shaft Power:** Measured in **kW or MW**.

### 1.5 Introduction to Hydraulic Turbines

A **Hydraulic Turbine** is a turbomachine that converts the **potential energy and kinetic energy of water** into useful **mechanical (rotational) energy**, which is then converted to electrical energy by a coupled generator.

The driving force is the **head of water** — a vertical distance between the upstream reservoir level and the turbine's tailrace.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Brake Power of a Hydraulic Turbine** is given by:

$$P = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H \cdot \eta$$

where:
- $P$ = Brake Power (W)
- $\rho$ = Density of water (**1000 kg/m³**)
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**)
- $Q$ = Volume flow rate (m³/s)
- $H$ = Net head (m)
- $\eta$ = Efficiency of the turbine

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle of a Hydraulic Turbine

The working of any hydraulic turbine is governed by **Euler's Turbomachine Equation**, which relates the energy transfer to the change in angular momentum of the fluid:

$$W = \rho \cdot Q \cdot (V_{w1} \cdot r_1 - V_{w2} \cdot r_2)$$

Where:
- $W$ = Work done per unit time (W)
- $V_{w1}, V_{w2}$ = Whirl (tangential) velocities of fluid at inlet and outlet
- $r_1, r_2$ = Radii at inlet and outlet
- $\rho$ = Fluid density
- $Q$ = Flow rate

> [!NOTE]
> For a **radial flow machine**, the energy transfer depends purely on the change in whirl velocity component. This equation forms the theoretical backbone of all hydraulic turbine analysis.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Energy Conversion

The energy transformation in a hydraulic turbine follows this sequence:

1. **Potential Energy** of stored water at a height (head, H).
2. Conversion to **Pressure Energy + Kinetic Energy** as water flows down through penstock.
3. **Nozzle / Guide Vanes** direct high-velocity water jet onto the **runner blades**.
4. The change in momentum of water causes the **runner to rotate** (mechanical work extracted).
5. Water exits with reduced velocity into the **tailrace**.
6. The shaft transmits mechanical energy to a **generator** (electrical output).

### 2.3 Classification of Hydraulic Turbines

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Hydraulic turbines are classified based on **three primary criteria**:

**A) Based on the Direction of Water Flow Through the Runner:**

| S.No | Type | Description | Typical Head |
|------|------|-------------|--------------|
| 1 | **Tangential Flow (Impulse)** | Water strikes runner tangentially; pressure remains atmospheric | High (>300 m) |
| 2 | **Radial Flow (Reaction)** | Water flows radially inward (Francis) or outward | Medium (30–300 m) |
| 3 | **Axial Flow (Reaction)** | Water flows parallel to shaft axis (Kaplan, Propeller) | Low (<30 m) |
| 4 | **Mixed Flow** | Combination of radial and axial flow | Medium |

**B) Based on the Head at Inlet:**

| S.No | Type | Head Range |
|------|------|------------|
| 1 | **High Head Turbine** | > 300 m (Pelton Wheel) |
| 2 | **Medium Head Turbine** | 30 m – 300 m (Francis Turbine) |
| 3 | **Low Head Turbine** | < 30 m (Kaplan / Propeller) |

**C) Based on the Action of Water on Blades:**

| S.No | Type | Mechanism |
|------|------|-----------|
| 1 | **Impulse Turbine** | All pressure energy → KE at nozzle; runner operates at atmospheric pressure |
| 2 | **Reaction Turbine** | Pressure drop occurs both in guide vanes AND runner; water under pressure inside runner |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Cheat Sheet — Hydraulic Turbines**

| S.No | Formula | Description |
|------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $P = \rho g Q H \eta$ | Brake Power Output |
| 2 | $V_1 = \sqrt{2gH}$ | Jet Velocity at Nozzle Exit |
| 3 | $u = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ | Blade / Runner Peripheral Velocity |
| 4 | $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$ | Specific Speed (Metric units) |
| 5 | $W = \rho Q (V_{w1} r_1 - V_{w2} r_2)$ | Euler's Turbomachine Equation |
| 6 | $\eta_h = \dfrac{V_{w1} \pm V_{w2}}{2gH}$ | Hydraulic Efficiency |
| 7 | $\eta_o = \eta_h \times \eta_v \times \eta_m$ | Overall Efficiency |
| 8 | $\eta_m = \dfrac{\text{Shaft Power}}{\text{Water Power}}$ | Mechanical Efficiency |
| 9 | $\eta_v = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}$ | Volumetric Efficiency |
| 10 | $H = \dfrac{P}{\rho g Q \eta}$ | Net Head from given Power |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** These formulas are used daily in hydropower plant design (India: Bhakra Nangal uses Francis; Idukki uses Pelton; Sardar Sarovar uses Kaplan), in dam engineering consultancy, and in renewable energy project commissioning.

### 2.5 Constructional Features — Major Hydraulic Turbines

#### A) **Pelton Wheel (Impulse, Tangential Flow)**
- **Runner:** Circular disc with double-cup shaped buckets mounted on its periphery.
- **Nozzle:** One (or more) jet-producing nozzle(s) with a spear needle for flow regulation.
- **Casing:** Does NOT contain water; only protects from splashing.
- **Brake Jet:** Deflects water jet onto back of buckets to stop the wheel.
- **Draft Tube:** **NOT used** (atmospheric discharge).
- **Specific Speed Range:** $N_s \approx 10 - 35$ (metric).

#### B) **Francis Turbine (Reaction, Radial/Mixed Flow)**
- **Runner:** Closed type with curved blades arranged radially (inward flow).
- **Guide Vanes:** Concentric ring of adjustable stationary vanes regulate flow.
- **Scroll Casing:** Spiral casing that distributes water uniformly around the runner.
- **Draft Tube:** Used — gradually expanding area recovers pressure energy as kinetic energy at runner exit.
- **Specific Speed Range:** $N_s \approx 60 - 400$ (metric).

#### C) **Kaplan Turbine (Reaction, Axial Flow)**
- **Runner:** Looks like a ship's propeller with 3 to 6 adjustable blades.
- **Guide Vanes (Wicket Gates):** Adjustable to control flow direction.
- **Scroll Casing & Draft Tube:** Similar to Francis but with axial flow geometry.
- **Specific Speed Range:** $N_s \approx 300 - 1000$ (metric).

### 2.6 Selection Criterion Using Specific Speed

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Specific speed ($N_s$) is the single most important parameter for selecting a hydraulic turbine. It is the speed of a geometrically similar turbine that would produce **1 unit of power under 1 unit of head**.

| Specific Speed Range | Recommended Turbine |
|----------------------|---------------------|
| $N_s < 30$ | Single-jet Pelton Wheel |
| $30 < N_s < 60$ | Multi-jet Pelton Wheel |
| $60 < N_s < 400$ | Francis Turbine (Slow, Medium, Fast) |
| $N_s > 400$ | Kaplan / Propeller Turbine |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Euler's Turbomachine Equation

This is the **fundamental equation** governing all rotating fluid machinery — both pumps and turbines.

**Statement:** *The work done per second by the fluid on the rotor equals the product of the mass flow rate and the change in the angular momentum of the fluid as it passes through the rotor.*

#### Step 1: Consider a Control Volume around the Rotor
Let mass flow rate $= \dot{m}$ (kg/s) entering at section 1 and leaving at section 2.

#### Step 2: Velocity Triangles at Inlet and Outlet
At any station on the rotor, the absolute velocity $V$ of the fluid has two components:
- **Whirl component** $V_w$ (tangential)
- **Flow / Radial / Axial component** $V_f$ (normal to whirl)

#### Step 3: Tangential Momentum of Fluid at Inlet
The tangential momentum per second entering the rotor:

$$\text{Momentum}_{in} = \dot{m} \cdot V_{w1} \cdot \cos\alpha_1$$

For a typical hydraulic turbine, $\alpha_1$ is close to 0 (or sometimes adjusted). The simplified expression uses the absolute whirl component directly.

#### Step 4: Tangential Momentum at Outlet
Similarly, the tangential momentum leaving per second:

$$\text{Momentum}_{out} = \dot{m} \cdot V_{w2} \cdot \cos\alpha_2$$

#### Step 5: Applying the Impulse-Momentum Theorem
By Newton's second law for a rotating system:

$$F_t \cdot r = \dot{m} (V_{w1} \cdot r_1 - V_{w2} \cdot r_2)$$

where $F_t$ is the tangential force exerted by the fluid on the rotor blade.

#### Step 6: Work Done per Unit Time (Power)
Power transferred to rotor = Force × Velocity of the point of application:

$$W = F_t \cdot u = \dot{m} \cdot u \cdot (V_{w1} - V_{w2})$$

where $u = \omega r$ (tangential velocity of the blade at that radius).

#### Step 7: Generalised Euler Equation
Since $u = \omega r$, we can write:

$$\boxed{W = \dot{m} \cdot \omega \cdot (V_{w1} \cdot r_1 - V_{w2} \cdot r_2)}$$

Or in terms of velocities:

$$W = \dot{m} \cdot (V_{w1} \cdot u_1 - V_{w2} \cdot u_2)$$

Where $u_1 = \omega r_1$ and $u_2 = \omega r_2$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention:** For turbines, $V_{w1} \cdot r_1 > V_{w2} \cdot r_2$, so $W > 0$ (work extracted). For pumps/compressors, the opposite holds (work input).

### 3.2 Derivation of Specific Speed ($N_s$)

**Step 1: Homological Scaling of Turbines**
Two geometrically similar turbines operating under similar conditions obey dimensional analysis principles.

**Step 2: Dimensionless Grouping**
For dynamic similarity, the ratio of inertial to gravitational forces is constant. This yields the relationship:

$$\frac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}} = \text{constant}$$

**Step 3: Define Specific Speed**
By convention, this constant is set to $N_s$ — the speed of a geometrically similar turbine delivering 1 kW under a head of 1 metre:

$$\boxed{N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}}$$

Where:
- $N$ = Speed in rpm
- $P$ = Power in kW
- $H$ = Head in metres

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $H^{5/4}$?** From dimensional analysis, $P \propto N^3 D^5$ and $H \propto N^2 D^2$. Eliminating $D$ gives $P^{1/2} \propto N D^{5/2}$ and $H^{5/4} \propto N^{5/2} D^{5/2}$. Taking the ratio yields the form above.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Turbine Comparison Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Comparison — Pelton vs Francis vs Kaplan**

| Parameter | Pelton Wheel | Francis Turbine | Kaplan Turbine |
|-----------|--------------|-----------------|----------------|
| Type | Impulse | Reaction | Reaction |
| Flow Direction | Tangential | Radial / Mixed | Axial |
| Head Range | 300 m – 2000 m | 30 m – 300 m | < 30 m |
| Specific Speed | 10 – 35 | 60 – 400 | 300 – 1000 |
| Runner | Disc with buckets | Closed with curved blades | Propeller-like |
| Number of Vanes/Blades | 16 – 24 buckets | 14 – 24 blades | 3 – 6 blades |
| Adjustable Blades | No | No | **Yes** (pitch control) |
| Guide Vanes | Not required | **Yes** (wicket gates) | **Yes** |
| Draft Tube | Not used | **Used** | **Used** |
| Casing Contains Water | No | Yes | Yes |
| Pressure at Inlet | Atmospheric (after nozzle) | Above atmospheric | Above atmospheric |
| Pressure at Outlet | Atmospheric | Sub-atmospheric | Sub-atmospheric |
| Construction | Simple | Moderate | Complex |
| Typical Application | Mountain hydropower | Reservoir dams | Run-of-river, tidal |

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Numerical Demonstration

> [!NOTE]
> **Illustrative Example:** A Pelton wheel develops **750 kW** at a head of **200 m** running at **500 rpm**. Determine the specific speed.

**Given:** $P = 750$ kW, $H = 200$ m, $N = 500$ rpm.

**Step 1:** Apply the formula:

$$N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $\sqrt{P}$:

$$\sqrt{750} = 27.386$$

**Step 3:** Compute $H^{5/4}$:

$$H^{5/4} = 200^{1.25}$$

$$200^{1.25} = 200 \times 200^{0.25} = 200 \times 3.76 = 752.1$$

**Step 4:** Substitute:

$$N_s = \frac{500 \times 27.386}{752.1}$$

**Step 5:** Final Result:

$$\boxed{N_s \approx 18.2 \text{ (metric)}}$$

**Step 6: Interpretation:** Since $N_s < 30$, the choice of **single-jet Pelton wheel** is appropriate.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Hydraulic Turbine System

This diagram represents the energy conversion pathway in a typical hydropower setup (mapped for Francis / Pelton / Kaplan as a generalised flow):

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Reservoir / Headwater Storage] --> B[Penstock Pipeline]
    B --> C[Inlet Valve and Trash Rack]
    C --> D[Spiral Scroll Casing]
    D --> E[Guide Vanes / Wicket Gates]
    E --> F[Runner / Rotor Blades]
    F --> G[Draft Tube Outlet]
    G --> H[Tailrace Downstream Channel]
    F --> I[Rotating Shaft]
    I --> J[Coupled Generator]
    J --> K[Electrical Output to Grid]
    L[Governor Control System] --> E
    L --> F
    M[Brake Jet Mechanism] --> F
    N[Bearings and Seals Assembly] --> I
```

### 4.2 Classification Tree of Hydraulic Turbines

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hydraulic Turbines] --> B[Based on Action]
    A --> C[Based on Head]
    A --> D[Based on Flow Direction]
    B --> B1[Impulse Type]
    B --> B2[Reaction Type]
    B1 --> B1A[Pelton Wheel]
    B1 --> B1B[Turgo Turbine]
    B2 --> B2A[Francis Turbine]
    B2 --> B2B[Kaplan Turbine]
    B2 --> B2C[Propeller Turbine]
    C --> C1[High Head greater 300m]
    C --> C2[Medium Head 30m to 300m]
    C --> C3[Low Head less 30m]
    C1 --> C1A[Pelton Wheel]
    C2 --> C2A[Francis Turbine]
    C3 --> C3A[Kaplan or Propeller]
    D --> D1[Tangential Flow]
    D --> D2[Radial or Mixed Flow]
    D --> D3[Axial Flow]
    D1 --> D1A[Pelton]
    D2 --> D2A[Francis]
    D3 --> D3A[Kaplan]
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Parts of a Turbomachine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Inlet Nozzle or Duct] --> P2[Stator Casing]
    P2 --> P3[Guide Vanes Stationary Blades]
    P3 --> P4[Rotor Blades Runner]
    P4 --> P5[Draft Tube or Outlet Casing]
    P4 --> P6[Rotating Shaft]
    P6 --> P7[Bearings Assembly]
    P6 --> P8[Sealing Arrangement]
    P6 --> P9[Output to Load or Generator]
    P10[Governor Control] --> P3
    P11[Lubrication System] --> P7
    P12[Braking System] --> P4
```

### 4.4 Velocity Triangle Diagram (Schematic Mapping)

For an **impulse turbine (Pelton)**, the inlet and outlet velocity triangles are mapped below:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Absolute Velocity V1 at Inlet] --> A1[Whirl Component Vw1]
    A --> A2[Flow Component Vf1]
    B[Blade Velocity U1 at Inlet] --> B1[Bucket moving with U1]
    C[Relative Velocity Vr1 inlet] --> C1[Relative to bucket]
    D[Absolute Velocity V2 at Outlet] --> D1[Whirl Component Vw2 reversed]
    E[Blade Velocity U2 at Outlet] --> E1[Bucket moving with U2]
    F[Relative Velocity Vr2 outlet] --> F1[Leaving the bucket]
    G[Euler Work Equation] --> G1[W is proportional to Vw1 U1 minus Vw2 U2]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** This block-level architecture maps the velocity components at the bucket inlet and outlet. For maximum power, the whirl at outlet should be in the direction opposite to blade motion, hence $V_{w2}$ is taken as negative in the standard sign convention.

### 4.5 Constructional Schematic — Cross-section of Francis Turbine

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Top Cover / Head Cover] --> S2[Stay Ring]
    S2 --> S3[Guide Vane Ring with Wicket Gates]
    S3 --> S4[Guide Vane Operating Ring]
    S4 --> S5[Servomotor for Vane Control]
    S6[Spiral Casing] --> S3
    S7[Runner with Curved Blades] --> S8[Shaft]
    S8 --> S9[Draft Tube]
    S9 --> S10[Tailrace]
    S11[Main Shaft Seal] --> S8
    S12[Guide Bearing] --> S8
    S13[Thrust Bearing] --> S8
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a turbomachine. With the help of a neat block diagram, list the various parts of a turbomachine.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A turbomachine is a fluid machine in which a continuous flow of fluid interacts dynamically with a rotating element (rotor) resulting in a continuous exchange of energy between the fluid and the rotor.

The various parts are:
1. **Inlet nozzle or duct** — admits fluid to the machine.
2. **Stator / Casing** — stationary housing that contains the fluid and the rotor.
3. **Guide vanes / Stator blades** — direct fluid onto rotor blades at desired angle.
4. **Rotor / Runner** — rotating member fitted with blades that exchanges energy with fluid.
5. **Shaft** — transmits mechanical energy.
6. **Draft tube / Outlet** — recovers pressure energy (reaction turbines) and discharges water.
7. **Bearings and seals** — support shaft and prevent leakage.

*(Block diagram can be referred from Section 4.3.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Compare impulse and reaction hydraulic turbines in any six aspects.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| S.No | Aspect | Impulse Turbine | Reaction Turbine |
|------|--------|-----------------|------------------|
| 1 | Pressure at runner inlet | Atmospheric | Above atmospheric |
| 2 | Pressure throughout runner | Constant (atmospheric) | Drops across runner |
| 3 | Water contact with runner | Only at bucket | Continuously under pressure |
| 4 | Energy conversion site | Only at buckets | Throughout the flow path |
| 5 | Draft tube | Not required | Required |
| 6 | Examples | Pelton Wheel | Francis, Kaplan |

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Classify hydraulic turbines in detail based on (i) the action of water on the turbine, (ii) the direction of water flow through the runner, and (iii) the head at the inlet. Give one example for each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the constructional features of a **Pelton wheel**. Why is a draft tube not used in it? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A (a):

Hydraulic turbines are classified as follows:

**(i) Based on the Action of Water on the Turbine:**

| Type | Description |
|------|-------------|
| **Impulse Turbine** | All pressure head is converted to velocity head at the nozzle. The jet impinges on the bucket at atmospheric pressure. Example: **Pelton Wheel**. |
| **Reaction Turbine** | Water under pressure flows over the moving blades; pressure drop occurs both in guide vanes and inside the runner. Example: **Francis Turbine, Kaplan Turbine**. |

**(ii) Based on the Direction of Water Flow Through the Runner:**

| Type | Flow Direction | Example |
|------|----------------|---------|
| Tangential flow | Water flows tangentially to the runner | Pelton Wheel |
| Radial/Mixed flow | Water enters radially and exits axially | Francis Turbine |
| Axial flow | Water flows parallel to shaft axis | Kaplan / Propeller Turbine |

**(iii) Based on the Head at Inlet:**

| Type | Head Range | Example |
|------|------------|---------|
| High Head | > 300 m | Pelton Wheel |
| Medium Head | 30 – 300 m | Francis Turbine |
| Low Head | < 30 m | Kaplan Turbine |

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A (b):

**Constructional Features of a Pelton Wheel (7 Marks):**

A Pelton wheel consists of the following components:

1. **Nozzle and Needle Assembly:**
   - A circular nozzle produces a high-velocity jet of water.
   - A spear-shaped needle inside the nozzle regulates the flow rate.
   - The needle is moved by a **governor mechanism** via a hand wheel / servomotor.

2. **Runner with Buckets:**
   - The runner is a circular disc mounted on the shaft.
   - **Double-cup (or twin hemispherical) buckets** are bolted on the periphery.
   - A **splitter ridge** in the middle of each bucket divides the jet and deflects it by nearly 180°, reversing its direction.

3. **Casing (Brake Housing):**
   - Does not carry water; only protects machinery from splashing.
   - Provides a safe enclosed area for the runner.

4. **Brake Jet (Deflector):**
   - Used to stop the wheel quickly by deflecting the jet away from the buckets during emergency shut-down.

5. **Bearings:**
   - **Main bearing** supports the shaft weight; **thrust bearing** absorbs axial loads.

**Why no draft tube in Pelton Wheel:**

- The water exits the bucket at atmospheric pressure (since the entire pressure energy was converted to KE at the nozzle).
- A draft tube is useful only when water leaves the runner at sub-atmospheric pressure, to recover that pressure energy.
- Hence, in an impulse turbine like Pelton wheel, **no draft tube is required**, and the water simply discharges into the tailrace through a channel at atmospheric pressure.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model / Equivalent Module Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **Francis turbine** with a neat sketch. Mention the role of the **draft tube** in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Euler's turbomachine equation** from first principles and explain its significance in the analysis of hydraulic turbines. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B (a):

**Francis Turbine — Construction and Working (7 Marks):**

A Francis turbine is a **reaction, radial (mixed) flow turbine** that operates under medium heads (30 m – 300 m).

**Constructional Parts:**

1. **Spiral Casing (Scroll Casing):**
   - The water from the penstock enters a spiral-shaped casing that distributes water **uniformly around the entire circumference** of the runner.
   - Its cross-sectional area progressively decreases as water flows inward — keeping the **velocity of whirl constant** (free-vortex flow principle).

2. **Guide Vanes (Wicket Gates):**
   - A ring of curved, adjustable stationary vanes placed between the casing and the runner.
   - They direct water onto the runner blades at the desired angle and control the discharge.
   - All guide vanes can be rotated simultaneously by a **governor-controlled regulating ring**.

3. **Runner:**
   - A closed-type runner fitted with **curved blades** arranged radially (inward flow).
   - Water enters the runner radially and leaves axially into the draft tube.

4. **Draft Tube:**
   - A gradually expanding tube connecting the runner outlet to the tailrace.
   - **Functions of the draft tube:**
     1. **Converts the kinetic energy at the runner exit into pressure energy** (recovers head).
     2. Creates a **suction head** at the runner outlet, allowing the turbine to be installed **above the tailrace** without losing head.
     3. Increases the **net available head** by the height between runner and tailrace (termed *draft head*).
   - Types: Conical, Elbow type, Moody's diffusion type.

**Working:**
High-pressure water enters the spiral casing → flows through guide vanes → enters runner radially → exerts pressure on curved blades causing rotation → water leaves axially into draft tube → velocity is reduced and converted to pressure → discharged to tailrace.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B (b):

**Derivation of Euler's Turbomachine Equation (7 Marks):**

Refer to Section 3.1 for the full derivation. Summary for board answer:

**Statement:** Euler's equation gives the work done per unit time by a fluid on a rotor in terms of the change in angular momentum.

**Assumptions:**
1. Flow is steady and uniform at inlet and outlet.
2. Fluid is incompressible.
3. No friction; velocity is uniform across the cross-section.

**Derivation (7 Steps):**

1. Let $\dot{m}$ = mass flow rate (kg/s).
2. At inlet, absolute velocity $V_1$ has whirl component $V_{w1}$.
3. Tangential momentum entering per second = $\dot{m} \cdot V_{w1} \cdot r_1$.
4. Tangential momentum leaving per second = $\dot{m} \cdot V_{w2} \cdot r_2$.
5. Change in angular momentum = $\dot{m}(V_{w1} r_1 - V_{w2} r_2)$.
6. Torque on rotor = $\dot{m}(V_{w1} r_1 - V_{w2} r_2)$.
7. Power = Torque × Angular velocity ($\omega$):

$$\boxed{W = \dot{m} \cdot \omega \cdot (V_{w1} r_1 - V_{w2} r_2)}$$

Or equivalently:

$$W = \dot{m} \cdot (V_{w1} u_1 - V_{w2} u_2)$$

**Significance:**
- It is the **governing equation** for all rotating fluid machinery.
- For turbines, $V_{w1} > V_{w2}$, so work is extracted.
- For pumps/compressors, $V_{w2} > V_{w1}$, so work is supplied.
- It relates work transfer directly to measurable velocity components from velocity triangles.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Common Mark-Loss Areas:**
> - Forgetting to state **units** of specific speed as **metric** (rpm, m, kW) when using the formula $N_s = N \sqrt{P} / H^{5/4}$.
> - Mixing up the **direction of whirl velocity** at outlet — in Pelton wheels, $V_{w2}$ is in the **opposite direction** to blade motion; students often forget the negative sign.
> - Confusing **"head"** with **"pressure"** — head is energy per unit weight (metres), pressure is force per unit area (Pa).
> - In classification answers, missing the **"based on action"** category (impulse vs reaction) — examiners deduct 2 marks.
> - Not mentioning the **governor mechanism** in Pelton/Francis/Kaplan descriptions (often asked as a sub-part for 1 mark).
> - Sketches without **labelled parts** — full marks require clear, named labels.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Fire Revision Points — Memorise Before Exam:**

- ✅ A **turbomachine** exchanges energy between a fluid and a rotating element via dynamic interaction.
- ✅ Two families: **Turbines** (extract) and **Pumps/Compressors** (impart).
- ✅ **Universal parts**: Inlet, Stator, Guide Vanes, Rotor, Shaft, Bearings, Seals, Outlet.
- ✅ **Pelton** = Impulse + Tangential + High head + No draft tube.
- ✅ **Francis** = Reaction + Radial/Mixed + Medium head + Draft tube used.
- ✅ **Kaplan** = Reaction + Axial + Low head + Adjustable blades + Draft tube.
- ✅ **Euler Equation:** $W = \dot{m}(V_{w1} u_1 - V_{w2} u_2)$.
- ✅ **Specific Speed formula:** $N_s = N \sqrt{P} / H^{5/4}$ (metric).
- ✅ **Specific speed ranges**: Pelton (10–35), Francis (60–400), Kaplan (300–1000).
- ✅ **Brake Power:** $P = \rho g Q H \eta$ — use $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s².
- ✅ **Draft tube** is mandatory for reaction turbines; **NOT** for impulse turbines.
- ✅ **Spiral casing** ensures uniform distribution of water with constant whirl velocity (free-vortex design).
- ✅ **Guide vanes** (wicket gates) regulate flow in reaction turbines.
- ✅ **Brake jet** is exclusive to Pelton wheel for emergency stopping.
- ✅ **Sign convention**: Turbines → positive work output; Pumps → negative work output (work input).
- ✅ **Pelton bucket** has a splitter ridge to divide and reverse the jet (nearly 180°).
- ✅ Remember the **$H^{5/4}$** exponent in $N_s$ formula — a common numerical-error trap.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classifications - Types of Casing and Impellers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT741 - FLUID MECHANICS & MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Classifications - Types of Casing and Impellers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Centrifugal Pump Components

A **Centrifugal Pump** is a rotary machine that imparts energy to a fluid by means of a rotating **impeller** enclosed in a stationary **casing**. The fluid enters the impeller axially, gets accelerated radially (or axially) by the rotating vanes, and the kinetic energy is partially converted to pressure energy within the casing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMRT741)**, the heart of a centrifugal pump consists of two principal components:
> 1. **The Impeller** — The rotating element that transfers mechanical energy to the fluid.
> 2. **The Casing** — The stationary, pressure-tight enclosure that collects the fluid, guides it to the delivery port, and converts kinetic energy into pressure energy (pressure recovery).

## 1.2 What is a Casing?

The **Casing** is the airtight outer shell of a centrifugal pump. It performs three critical functions:
- **Contains** the high-pressure fluid and directs it from the impeller outlet to the delivery pipe.
- **Converts** a major portion of the kinetic energy of the fluid leaving the impeller into pressure energy.
- **Provides** mechanical support and alignment for the shaft and bearings.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Spiral Slide!**
> Imagine a playground spiral slide. Water (the child) flies off the impeller (the spinning platform) with high velocity. The slide (casing) winds around and gradually widens, which slows the water down — but in doing so, raises its pressure (potential to do work). This is exactly how a volute casing works!

## 1.3 What is an Impeller?

The **Impeller** is the rotating, bladed component mounted on the shaft. It is the only moving part inside the pump and is responsible for imparting kinetic energy to the fluid.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Spinning Fan!**
> Think of a table fan's blades. When the fan spins, it pushes air outward. A pump impeller does the same with water — except the water is enclosed in a casing so it cannot escape sideways, and is forced through a delivery nozzle under pressure.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU 2024 Convention)

- Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
- Density of water (standard): **ρ = 1000 kg/m³**
- Specific weight of water: **γ = 9810 N/m³**
- Specific speed classification range for **radial flow**: **N_s = 10 to 35**
- Specific speed classification range for **mixed flow**: **N_s = 35 to 80**
- Specific speed classification range for **axial flow**: **N_s = 80 to 300**

> [!TIP]
> The specific speed (N_s) is the most powerful single parameter to classify the type of impeller used in a centrifugal pump. Memorize these ranges for KTU ESE — examiners **love** asking this!

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Casings

The casing of a centrifugal pump is classified into **three principal types** based on how the kinetic energy of the discharged fluid is converted to pressure energy.

### 2.1.1 Volute Casing
- **Shape:** A spiral-shaped passage of progressively increasing cross-sectional area (resembling a snail's shell — hence the name *volute* from Latin *voluta*).
- **Operation:** As fluid leaves the impeller periphery at high velocity, it enters the volute chamber. The cross-section of the chamber increases as it spirals outward toward the delivery port. This causes a gradual **deceleration of the fluid**, converting kinetic energy (½mv²) into pressure energy (P/ρg).
- **Key Features:**
  - Simple construction — least expensive.
  - No guide vanes; relies purely on geometric area increase.
  - Hydraulic losses are higher than diffuser type.
  - Radial pressure imbalance creates a **side thrust** on the impeller.
- **KTU Application:** Used in low-to-medium head, high-discharge applications (e.g., irrigation pumps, water supply schemes).

### 2.1.2 Vortex Casing (Whirlpool or Concentric Casing)
- **Shape:** An **annular chamber** (circular ring) placed between the impeller outlet and the volute passage. The volute tongue is set back from the impeller.
- **Operation:** Fluid leaves the impeller and enters this circular chamber, where it forms a strong vortex (whirlpool). The volute passage is then designed with a **constant cross-sectional area**, which simply carries the swirling fluid to the delivery.
- **Key Features:**
  - Pressure conversion is more uniform around the impeller circumference → **minimum side thrust** on the impeller.
  - More efficient than a pure volute at high heads.
  - More complex to cast — moderately expensive.
- **KTU Application:** Preferred in high-pressure boiler feed pumps and multi-stage pumps.

### 2.1.3 Casing with Guide Blades (Diffuser / Turbine Casing)
- **Shape:** A series of stationary, gradually expanding **guide vanes (diffuser passages)** are arranged around the impeller periphery, similar to a reaction turbine's guide ring.
- **Operation:** The fluid leaving the impeller impinges on the curved guide vanes. The expanding passage of the vanes **decelerates the fluid smoothly**, converting kinetic energy into pressure with minimum loss.
- **Key Features:**
  - **Highest hydraulic efficiency** of all three casing types.
  - Eliminates side thrust almost completely (symmetric flow).
  - Expensive and difficult to cast.
  - Not suitable for handling dirty/muddy water (vanes get clogged).
- **KTU Application:** High-pressure, high-efficiency industrial pumps (chemical, refinery, and multi-stage boiler feed pumps).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does the volute area increase progressively?**
> By the **Principle of Continuity** (Q = A × V), if the discharge Q remains constant and the velocity V must decrease (to convert KE to PE), then the area A must increase. The volute is therefore designed with a uniformly expanding cross-section.

## 2.2 Classification of Impellers

Impellers are classified along **two independent axes**:

### 2.2.1 Classification by Mechanical Construction

| Type | Description | Pros | Cons | Typical Use |
|------|-------------|------|------|-------------|
| **Open Impeller** | Vanes are fixed to a central hub; no side shrouds (no side walls). | Easy to manufacture; handles muddy/dirty water; can be adjusted for wear. | Low efficiency; high leakage back to suction. | Sewage, slurry, pulp & paper industry. |
| **Semi-Open Impeller** | Has a single shroud (back wall) on one side only. | Better sealing than open; handles moderate solids. | Susceptible to axial thrust; wear on the open side. | Chemical pumps handling slightly contaminated fluids. |
| **Closed (Enclosed) Impeller** | Has shrouds on **both** sides of the vanes — fully enclosed. | Highest efficiency; minimum leakage; best NPSH characteristics. | Cannot handle dirty fluids; costly; clog-prone. | Boiler feed pumps, clear water pumps, high-head applications. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** Students often confuse **open and closed** impellers. Remember: *closed = enclosed on both sides* (like a sealed envelope), whereas *open = no walls* (vanes exposed). For KTU numerical problems, assume the impeller is **closed (shrouded)** unless stated otherwise.

### 2.2.2 Classification by Direction of Flow Through the Impeller

| Type | Inlet | Outlet | Vane Shape | Specific Speed N_s | Typical Head |
|------|-------|--------|------------|---------------------|--------------|
| **Radial Flow** | Axial (center) | Radial (perpendicular to shaft) | Backward-curved (most common), radial, or forward-curved | **10 – 35** | High head, low discharge |
| **Mixed Flow** | Axial | Diagonal (between radial and axial) | Twisted (helical) vanes | **35 – 80** | Medium head, medium discharge |
| **Axial Flow** | Axial | Axial (parallel to shaft) | Propeller / airfoil vanes | **80 – 300** | Low head, high discharge |

> [!NOTE]
> **Backwards-Curved Vanes:** Over 90% of radial flow centrifugal pumps use backward-curved vanes (β₂ < 90°). This is because they give a **stable head–discharge characteristic** (head decreases as discharge increases), avoiding the runaway condition of forward-curved vanes.

### 2.2.3 Classification by Suction

- **Single-Suction Impeller:** Fluid enters from one side only. Has a net **axial thrust** that must be balanced by a thrust bearing or balancing holes.
- **Double-Suction Impeller:** Fluid enters from both sides symmetrically. Axial thrust is **automatically balanced**, allowing higher flow rates.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit / Notes |
|---|---------|----------------|--------------|
| 1 | $N_s = \dfrac{N\sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$ | Specific speed | N in rpm, Q in m³/s, H in metres |
| 2 | $A_c = \dfrac{Q}{V_{f2}}$ | Cross-sectional area of volute at outlet | m² |
| 3 | $V_{f2} = \dfrac{\pi D_2 N}{60}$ | Peripheral velocity of impeller (≈ volute entry velocity) | m/s |
| 4 | $A_c = \dfrac{60\,Q}{\pi D_2 N}$ | Volute area from continuity | m² |
| 5 | $u_2 = \pi D_2 N / 60$ | Blade tip speed at outlet | m/s |
| 6 | $H = \dfrac{v_{w2} u_2}{g}$ | Euler head (Euler's equation) | m |
| 7 | $\eta_{man} = \dfrac{g H_m}{V_{w2} u_2}$ | Manometric efficiency | dimensionless |
| 8 | $V_{r2} = \dfrac{Q}{\pi D_2 b_2 \psi}$ | Radial velocity at outlet (for radial flow) | m/s |
| 9 | $\psi = 1 - \dfrac{\pi}{z} \sin\beta_2$ | Blockage / vane thickness factor | dimensionless |
| 10 | $v_{w2} = u_2 - \dfrac{V_{r2}\cos\beta_2}{\sin\beta_2}$ | Whirl velocity component (velocity triangle) | m/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - The choice of **casing** determines the pump's efficiency and ability to handle solids.
> - The choice of **impeller** (open/closed, radial/axial) determines the **Q–H characteristic curve**, NPSH requirement, and the application's suitability.
> - In **KTU ESE problems**, the specific speed $N_s$ is the gateway parameter to identify whether a pump is radial, mixed, or axial flow.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cross-Sectional Area of a Volute Casing

The volute casing is a spiral passage whose cross-sectional area must increase uniformly from the cutwater (tongue) to the discharge nozzle. We derive the area using the **principle of continuity**.

### Given Data (KTU Standard Convention)
- $Q$ = Discharge handled by the pump (m³/s)
- $D_2$ = Outer diameter of the impeller at outlet (m)
- $N$ = Speed of the impeller (rpm)
- $b_c$ = Width of the volute passage (m) — assumed equal to impeller outlet width $b_2$
- $A_c$ = Cross-sectional area of the volute at any section (m²)
- $V_{f2}$ = Velocity of flow at the volute inlet (tangential component at impeller periphery)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Apply the Principle of Continuity at the volute inlet.**

The fluid leaves the impeller periphery and immediately enters the volute. The discharge through the volute at any cross-section equals the pump discharge Q.

$$Q = A_c \times V_{f2}$$

> *Logic:* Mass (or volume, for incompressible flow) entering per second must equal mass leaving per second. The volute passage acts as a single flow channel of area A_c carrying fluid at velocity V_f2.

**Step 2 — Relate the volute inlet velocity to the impeller geometry.**

In a volute casing, the fluid enters the volute tangentially at the impeller periphery. The tangential velocity at the impeller tip is the **peripheral (blade) velocity**:

$$V_{f2} = \frac{\pi D_2 N}{60}$$

> *Logic:* A point on the impeller rim traces a circle of circumference $\pi D_2$ per revolution. At N revolutions per minute, the linear velocity of the rim is $(\pi D_2 N) / 60$. The volute is designed so that the fluid enters with approximately this velocity.

**Step 3 — Substitute Equation 2 into Equation 1.**

$$A_c = \frac{Q}{V_{f2}} = \frac{Q}{\left(\dfrac{\pi D_2 N}{60}\right)}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the expression.**

$$A_c = \frac{60\,Q}{\pi D_2 N}$$

> *Logic:* This compact form gives the **volute area at the impeller outlet section** directly from the pump's operating parameters. The volute area is then *linearly* increased around the spiral to the discharge nozzle.

**Step 5 — Express the area in terms of width and height (rectangular approximation).**

The volute cross-section is often approximated as a rectangle of width $b_c$ and height $h_c$:

$$A_c = b_c \times h_c \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; h_c = \frac{A_c}{b_c} = \frac{60\,Q}{\pi D_2 N\, b_c}$$

> *Logic:* The width $b_c$ is usually held constant (equal to the impeller width $b_2$); hence the height $h_c$ must grow as the fluid progresses through the spiral to slow it down.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Whirl Velocity Component from the Outlet Velocity Triangle

The outlet velocity triangle of a radial-flow impeller is critical for determining the head developed. We derive the tangential (whirl) component $v_{w2}$.

### Given Data
- $u_2$ = Peripheral velocity of impeller at outlet
- $V_{r2}$ = Relative velocity of fluid at outlet (with respect to blade)
- $\beta_2$ = Blade angle at outlet (measured from tangential direction)
- $v_{w2}$ = Absolute tangential (whirl) velocity of fluid at outlet

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Construct the outlet velocity triangle.**

The triangle has three sides:
- $u_2$ → in the direction of impeller rotation (tangential)
- $V_{r2}$ → at angle $\beta_2$ behind the tangent (for backward-curved blade)
- $v_{w2}$ → the absolute velocity's tangential component

**Step 2 — Apply vector addition.**

The absolute velocity is the vector sum of blade velocity and relative velocity:

$$\vec{v_2} = \vec{u_2} + \vec{V_{r2}}$$

**Step 3 — Take the tangential (whirl) component.**

Resolving along the direction of $u_2$:

$$v_{w2} = u_2 - V_{r2} \cos\beta_2$$

**Step 4 — Use trigonometry on the relative velocity vector.**

The relative velocity makes angle $\beta_2$ with the negative tangential direction. Its tangential component is $V_{r2} \cos\beta_2$ (opposing $u_2$ for backward-curved blades).

$$v_{w2} = u_2 - V_{r2} \cos\beta_2$$

**Step 5 — Express $V_{r2}$ in component form using the flow angle.**

For a radial-flow impeller, the absolute velocity is decomposed as:

$$\tan\beta_2 = \frac{V_{r2}}{V_{f2}}$$

> *Logic:* The flow is purely radial at outlet ($V_{f2}$ is the radial component), and the relative velocity $V_{r2}$ has both radial and tangential parts. The blade angle $\beta_2$ is the angle the relative velocity makes with the tangential direction at the trailing edge of the blade.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Casing & Impeller

| Application / Requirement | Recommended Casing | Recommended Impeller Type | Reason |
|---------------------------|-------------------|--------------------------|--------|
| High head, clear water (boiler feed) | Diffuser (guide vane) | Closed, radial flow, backward-curved | Highest efficiency, symmetric thrust balance |
| Sewage / sludge / muddy water | Volute casing | Open or semi-open, radial flow | Tolerates solids, easy to clean |
| High discharge, low head (flood control) | Volute or axial | Mixed / axial flow | High Q, low H characteristic |
| Multistage boiler feed pump | Vortex or diffuser | Closed, double-suction, radial | Balanced thrust, high efficiency at high pressure |
| Chemical process, clean corrosive fluid | Volute (FRP-lined) | Closed, radial, stainless steel | Sealed impeller prevents leakage; corrosion resistance |
| Cooling water circulation | Volute | Closed, single-suction, radial | Cost-effective, high flow availability |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Centrifugal Pump

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Suction Pipe and Strainer] --> B[Suction Eye of Impeller]
    B --> C[Impeller Hub with Vanes]
    C --> D[Impeller Outlet Periphery]
    D --> E{Type of Casing}
    E -->|Volute Type| F1[Spiral Passage with Increasing Cross Section]
    E -->|Vortex Type| F2[Annular Vortex Chamber plus Constant Area Volute]
    E -->|Diffuser Type| F3[Stationary Guide Vanes Expanding Passage]
    F1 --> G[Delivery Nozzle]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Delivery Pipe to System]

    I[Drive Shaft from Motor] --> C
    J[Bearing and Seal Assembly] --> I

    style A fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064
    style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style C fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style D fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style E fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,stroke-width:3px
    style F1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style F2 fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92
    style F3 fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b
    style G fill:#f0f4c3,stroke:#827717
    style H fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#263238
    style I fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style J fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#263238
```

## 4.2 Classification Tree — Casings and Impellers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Centrifugal Pump Internal Components] --> B[CASING]
    A --> C[IMPELLER]

    B --> B1[Volute Casing]
    B --> B2[Vortex Casing]
    B --> B3[Diffuser Casing with Guide Vanes]

    C --> C1[By Construction]
    C --> C2[By Flow Direction]
    C --> C3[By Suction Type]

    C1 --> C1a[Open Impeller]
    C1 --> C1b[Semi Open Impeller]
    C1 --> C1c[Closed Impeller]

    C2 --> C2a[Radial Flow Ns 10 to 35]
    C2 --> C2b[Mixed Flow Ns 35 to 80]
    C2 --> C2c[Axial Flow Ns 80 to 300]

    C3 --> C3a[Single Suction]
    C3 --> C3b[Double Suction]

    style A fill:#fff176,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:3px
    style B fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px

    style B1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style B2 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style B3 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style C1 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style C2 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style C3 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style C1a fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1
    style C1b fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1
    style C1c fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1
    style C2a fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1
    style C2b fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1
    style C2c fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1
    style C3a fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1
    style C3b fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1
```

## 4.3 Volute Casing Architecture — Spiral Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Impeller Outlet High Velocity Fluid] --> B[Cutwater Tongue Entry]
    B --> C[Volute Section 1 Small Area High Velocity]
    C --> D[Volute Section 2 Medium Area Medium Velocity]
    D --> E[Volute Section 3 Large Area Low Velocity]
    E --> F[Discharge Nozzle High Pressure Low Velocity]

    G[Continuity Q constant, V decreases] --> C
    G --> D
    G --> E

    H[Bernoulli Energy Conversion] --> E
    H --> F

    style A fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style C fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style D fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20
    style E fill:#81c784,stroke:#1b5e20
    style F fill:#66bb6a,stroke:#1b5e20
    style G fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style H fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f
```

## 4.4 Impeller Construction — Open vs Semi-Open vs Closed

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Impeller Type Selection] --> B{Solids in Fluid?}
```
