topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT741 - FLUID MECHANICS & MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Losses and Efficiency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Head Loss — The Fundamental Concept

In a real (viscous) fluid flowing through a closed conduit (pipe), a portion of the fluid's mechanical energy is irreversibly converted into **thermal energy (heat)** due to internal friction between fluid layers and friction between the fluid and the pipe wall. This lost energy manifests as a drop in the pressure head (or total head) along the direction of flow, and is termed **Head Loss** ($h_L$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Standard Board Text):**
> *Head Loss is the loss of head (energy per unit weight of fluid) of a flowing fluid due to friction at the walls of the conduit and due to disturbances caused by fittings, bends, valves, sudden changes in cross-section, etc. It is an irreversible loss and cannot be recovered.*

Mathematically, applying the **Modified Bernoulli's Equation** between two sections (1) and (2) of a pipe with no pump or turbine in between:

$$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 \;=\; \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 \;+\; h_L$$

Where $h_L$ is the cumulative head loss measured in **metres of fluid (m)**.

## 1.2 Classification of Head Losses (KTU Syllabus Mapping)

For board examination purposes, KTU strictly classifies head losses into two categories:

| Category | Trigger Mechanism | Typical Magnitude |
|:---:|:---|:---:|
| **Major Losses ($h_f$)** | Skin friction along the straight length of pipe wall | Dominant in long pipelines ($L/D > 1000$) |
| **Minor Losses ($h_m$)** | Flow separation & turbulence at fittings, bends, valves, junctions | Dominant in short pipelines with many fittings |

$$h_L \;=\; h_f \;+\; \Sigma h_m$$

## 1.3 Efficiency — The Performance Metric

In the context of fluid machinery and pipeline systems, **efficiency ($\eta$)** is defined as the ratio of useful energy/power delivered to the energy/power supplied, expressed as a **dimensionless number** (0 to 1) or in **percentage (%)**.

For KTU Module 4, the three primary efficiencies are:

> [!NOTE]
> **Hydraulic Efficiency ($\eta_h$):** Ratio of useful head delivered to the head imparted by the machine.
> **Mechanical Efficiency ($\eta_m$):** Ratio of power actually transmitted to fluid versus power input to the shaft.
> **Overall Efficiency ($\eta_o$):** Product of all individual efficiencies ($\eta_o = \eta_h \times \eta_m$).

## 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 💡

Imagine a **garden hose** connected to a tap. The water gushes out of the tap with full force, but as it travels through a long, narrow hose with kinks, the pressure at the nozzle drops drastically. The reduction in "squirting power" is essentially the head loss.

**The Analogy:**
- **Pipe Wall** → Friction on the inner surface (like rubbing your palm against a rough wall).
- **Sudden Narrowing** → Like squeezing the hose to choke flow (energy wasted as turbulence/eddies).
- **Sharp Bend** → Like a car taking a hairpin turn at high speed (kinetic energy lost to side-skid).
- **Efficiency** → How much of the energy you supplied at the tap actually reaches the nozzle to do useful work (watering plants).

This is precisely why **engineers minimize fittings** in long pipelines — every bend, valve, and diameter change acts as a "speed breaker" stealing energy from the flowing fluid.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Major Loss (Friction Loss) — Darcy-Weisbach Equation

The **Darcy-Weisbach Equation** is the *gold standard* for computing frictional head loss and is universally accepted in KTU board examinations.

$$h_f \;=\; \frac{f \, L \, V^2}{2 \, g \, D}$$

### Component Breakdown:

- $h_f$ = Major head loss due to friction (m)
- $f$ = **Darcy friction factor** (dimensionless)
- $L$ = Length of the pipe (m)
- $D$ = Internal diameter of the pipe (m)
- $V$ = Mean velocity of flow (m/s)
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU Board Exams:**
> Some textbooks use the **Fanning friction factor** ($f'$) which is $1/4$ of the Darcy friction factor. KTU follows the **Darcy friction factor** convention by default. Always verify which convention is implied by the numerical value of $f$ given in the problem.

### Friction Factor ($f$) Determination:

The friction factor depends on the **Reynolds Number ($Re$)** and the **relative roughness ($\epsilon/D$)**:

$$Re \;=\; \frac{\rho \, V \, D}{\mu} \;=\; \frac{V \, D}{\nu}$$

Where $\nu$ is the **kinematic viscosity** in m²/s.

The friction factor $f$ is determined from:
1. **Laminar Flow ($Re < 2000$):** $f = 64/Re$ (analytical, no chart needed)
2. **Turbulent Flow ($Re > 4000$):** Read from **Moody Chart** OR use **Colebrook-White Equation**:

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} \;=\; -2 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{\epsilon/D}{3.7} \;+\; \frac{2.51}{Re \sqrt{f}} \right)$$

Where $\epsilon$ is the **absolute roughness** of the pipe material (in mm).

## 2.2 Minor Losses — The Form-Component

All minor losses are computed using a generic **velocity-head formulation**:

$$h_m \;=\; K \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

Where $K$ is the **loss coefficient** (a dimensionless empirical constant depending on the geometry).

### Comprehensive KTU Formula Sheet:

| S.No | Loss Type | Formula | Typical $K$ Value (Sharp-edged) |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | **Entrance Loss** (sharp-edged) | $h_{ent} = 0.5 \cdot \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | $K = 0.5$ |
| 2 | **Entrance Loss** (re-entrant / Borda) | $h_{ent} = 1.0 \cdot \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | $K = 1.0$ |
| 3 | **Entrance Loss** (well-rounded) | $h_{ent} \approx 0.04 \cdot \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | $K \approx 0.04$ |
| 4 | **Exit Loss** (submerged) | $h_{exit} = 1.0 \cdot \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | $K = 1.0$ |
| 5 | **Sudden Expansion** | $h_{exp} = \dfrac{(V_1 - V_2)^2}{2g}$ | $K = \left(1 - \dfrac{A_1}{A_2}\right)^2$ |
| 6 | **Sudden Contraction** | $h_{con} = 0.5\left(1 - \dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right) \cdot \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g}$ | $K = 0.5\left(1 - \dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)$ |
| 7 | **Pipe Bend** (90°) | $h_{bend} = K_b \cdot \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | $K_b \approx 0.3 \text{ to } 1.5$ |
| 8 | **Globe Valve** (fully open) | $h_{valve} = 10 \cdot \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | $K = 10$ |
| 9 | **Gate Valve** (fully open) | $h_{valve} = 0.2 \cdot \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | $K = 0.2$ |

## 2.3 Efficiency Formulations

### (a) Hydraulic Efficiency ($\eta_h$):

The ratio of the useful head delivered by the pump/turbine to the head actually imparted.

$$\eta_h \;=\; \frac{\text{Head delivered by machine}}{\text{Head imparted by machine}} \;=\; \frac{H_{\text{useful}}}{H_{\text{imp}}}$$

For a pipeline with no pump: $\eta_h = \dfrac{H_{\text{available at delivery}}}{H_{\text{available at source}}}$

### (b) Mechanical Efficiency ($\eta_m$):

The ratio of power transmitted to the fluid to the power input at the shaft.

$$\eta_m \;=\; \frac{\text{Power delivered to fluid}}{\text{Power input at shaft}}$$

### (c) Overall Efficiency ($\eta_o$):

$$\eta_o \;=\; \eta_h \times \eta_m \;=\; \frac{\text{Useful Power Output}}{\text{Power Input}}$$

### (d) Efficiency of Power Transmission through a Pipe:

A crucial KTU Module 4 problem type. Consider a pipe connecting two reservoirs:

$$\eta_{\text{transmission}} \;=\; \frac{H_{\text{net}}}{H_{\text{net}} + h_L} \;=\; \frac{H_{\text{net}}}{H}$$

Where:
- $H_{\text{net}}$ = Difference in reservoir water levels (useful head)
- $h_L$ = Total head loss in the pipeline
- $H = H_{\text{net}} + h_L$ = Total head supplied

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** These formulae govern the design of hydroelectric penstocks, city water distribution networks, oil pipelines, and HVAC ductwork. Engineers optimize diameter ($D$) to balance two competing costs — *high $D$ means low $h_L$ but high pipe material cost*, while *low $D$ means cheap pipe but wasted pumping energy*. This trade-off forms the basis of the "Economic Pipe Diameter" concept taught in later modules.

## 2.4 Equivalent Pipe Concept (Branching Pipes)

For **pipes in series** (same flow through multiple diameters):

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f_{eq} D_{eq}^5}} \;=\; \sum_i \frac{L_i}{\sqrt{f_i D_i^5}}$$

For **pipes in parallel** (same head loss across each branch):

$$\frac{Q_{\text{total}}}{\sqrt{H}} \;=\; \sum_i \frac{Q_i}{\sqrt{H_i}}$$

This is essential for solving complex network problems in KTU examinations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Darcy-Weisbach Equation

The Darcy-Weisbach equation is **empirically derived** but grounded in dimensional analysis and experimental verification. Let us derive its physical basis:

### Step 1: Identify the Governing Physical Variables

Frictional head loss $h_f$ must depend on:
- Length of pipe: $L$ (m)
- Diameter of pipe: $D$ (m)
- Mean velocity: $V$ (m/s)
- Fluid density: $\rho$ (kg/m³)
- Fluid viscosity: $\mu$ (kg/(m·s))
- Pipe wall roughness: $\epsilon$ (m)

$$h_f \;=\; \phi(L, D, V, \rho, \mu, \epsilon)$$

### Step 2: Apply Dimensional Analysis (Buckingham π Theorem)

Reducing to dimensionless groups using $L, D, V$ as repeating variables:

$$\frac{h_f}{L} \;=\; \phi_1\!\left(\frac{D}{L}, \; \frac{\rho V D}{\mu}, \; \frac{\epsilon}{D}\right)$$

For fully developed flow (where the head loss per unit length is constant), the dependence on $L/D$ drops out (i.e., $h_f \propto L$):

$$\boxed{\frac{h_f}{L} \;=\; \phi_2(Re, \epsilon/D)}$$

### Step 3: Re-express the Loss as Velocity Head

Since kinetic energy per unit weight is $V^2/(2g)$ (in metres), we factor it out:

$$\frac{h_f}{L} \;=\; \frac{V^2}{2g} \cdot \phi_3(Re, \epsilon/D)$$

### Step 4: Identify the Friction Factor

Defining $f = \phi_3(Re, \epsilon/D)$ as the **Darcy friction factor**:

$$h_f \;=\; \frac{f \, L \, V^2}{2gD}$$

**This completes the derivation.** The friction factor $f$ is then either computed from the Hagen-Poiseuille formula for laminar flow or read from the Moody Chart for turbulent flow.

## 3.2 Analytical Verification: Hagen-Poiseuille for Laminar Flow

In laminar flow, the head loss can also be derived analytically from the **Navier-Stokes equations**. For a horizontal pipe of radius $R$:

### Step 1: Velocity Profile (Parabolic)

$$u(r) \;=\; \frac{-\Delta p}{4\mu L} \left(R^2 - r^2\right)$$

### Step 2: Mean Velocity (by integration)

$$V \;=\; \frac{1}{\pi R^2} \int_0^R u(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr \;=\; \frac{\Delta p \, R^2}{8 \mu L}$$

### Step 3: Substitute into Pressure Drop Equation

$$\Delta p \;=\; \frac{32 \mu L V}{D^2}$$

### Step 4: Convert to Head Loss using $h_f = \Delta p / (\rho g)$

$$h_f \;=\; \frac{32 \mu L V}{\rho g D^2} \;=\; \frac{64}{Re} \cdot \frac{L V^2}{2gD}$$

This confirms that for laminar flow, $f = 64/Re$.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical: Typical KTU Board Problem

**Problem (KTU Pattern):**
Water flows through a horizontal cast iron pipe of diameter $D = 150$ mm and length $L = 500$ m at a velocity of $V = 2$ m/s. Take $\nu = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s and roughness $\epsilon = 0.26$ mm. Determine:
(a) Reynolds number and flow regime
(b) Friction factor using Colebrook equation
(c) Major head loss
(d) Power lost due to friction

### Solution:

**(a) Reynolds Number:**

$$Re \;=\; \frac{V D}{\nu} \;=\; \frac{2 \times 0.15}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} \;=\; 3.0 \times 10^5$$

Since $Re > 4000$, flow is **turbulent**.

**(b) Relative Roughness:**

$$\frac{\epsilon}{D} \;=\; \frac{0.26 \times 10^{-3}}{0.15} \;=\; 1.733 \times 10^{-3}$$

Iterating the Colebrook equation:

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} \;=\; -2 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{1.733 \times 10^{-3}}{3.7} \;+\; \frac{2.51}{3.0 \times 10^5 \sqrt{f}} \right)$$

Initial guess (using fully rough approximation): $1/\sqrt{f} \approx -2\log_{10}(4.68 \times 10^{-4}) = 6.66$, so $f \approx 0.0226$.

**Iteration 1:** Substituting $f = 0.0226$:

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} \;=\; -2 \log_{10}\!\left( 4.68 \times 10^{-4} \;+\; \frac{2.51}{3.0 \times 10^5 \times 0.1503} \right)$$

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} \;=\; -2 \log_{10}\!\left( 4.68 \times 10^{-4} \;+\; 5.56 \times 10^{-5} \right) \;=\; -2 \log_{10}(5.24 \times 10^{-4}) \;=\; 6.56$$

Thus $f = (1/6.56)^2 = 0.0232$. The iteration has converged. We take $\boxed{f = 0.0232}$.

**(c) Major Head Loss:**

$$h_f \;=\; \frac{f L V^2}{2 g D} \;=\; \frac{0.0232 \times 500 \times (2)^2}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.15}$$

$$h_f \;=\; \frac{46.4}{2.943} \;=\; 15.77 \text{ m of water}$$

**(d) Power Lost:**

$$P_{\text{lost}} \;=\; \rho \, g \, Q \, h_f \;=\; 1000 \times 9.81 \times \left(\frac{\pi}{4}(0.15)^2 \times 2\right) \times 15.77$$

$$P_{\text{lost}} \;=\; 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.03534 \times 15.77 \;=\; 5469 \text{ W} \approx 5.47 \text{ kW}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Reality Check:** A friction loss of ~16 m means every kilogram of water loses 16 × 9.81 ≈ 157 J of mechanical energy per 500 m of pipe. Multiply by mass flow rate, and you see why pumping stations are sized in megawatts for city water supply.

## 3.4 Transmission Efficiency Derivation

Consider two reservoirs connected by a pipe, with difference in water surface levels = $H$.

Applying Bernoulli's equation between the two surfaces (with $V \approx 0$ at both surfaces and $p_{\text{atm}}$ cancelling):

$$H \;=\; h_L \;=\; h_f + \Sigma h_m$$

The **net useful head** available for delivering kinetic energy at the pipe outlet is $H$, but actually only $H - h_L$ is useful for *driving* the flow (since $h_L$ is the energy that must be "spent" to overcome friction).

**Effective head delivered:**

$$H_{\text{eff}} \;=\; H \;-\; h_L \;=\; \text{Net head actually driving the flow}$$

**Power available at the inlet of the pipe (from source reservoir):**

$$P_{\text{supplied}} \;=\; \rho \, g \, Q \, H$$

**Power actually transmitted to the downstream end (useful):**

$$P_{\text{useful}} \;=\; \rho \, g \, Q \, H_{\text{eff}} \;=\; \rho \, g \, Q \, (H - h_L)$$

**Transmission Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{\text{trans}} \;=\; \frac{P_{\text{useful}}}{P_{\text{supplied}}} \;=\; \frac{H - h_L}{H} \;=\; 1 - \frac{h_L}{H}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Engineering Insight:** Transmission efficiency is **independent of flow rate $Q$** — it depends only on the geometry and head loss. This is a counterintuitive but elegant result that KTU examiners love to test.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Categorization of Head Losses

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["TOTAL HEAD LOSS hL"] --> B["Major Loss hf"]
    A --> C["Minor Loss hm"]
    B --> B1["Pipe Wall Skin Friction"]
    B --> B2["Computed via Darcy-Weisbach Equation"]
    B --> B3["Dominant in long pipelines"]
    C --> C1["Entrance Loss"]
    C --> C2["Exit Loss"]
    C --> C3["Sudden Expansion Loss"]
    C --> C4["Sudden Contraction Loss"]
    C --> C5["Bend Loss"]
    C --> C6["Valve and Fitting Loss"]
    C1 --> D1["K equal 0.5 sharp, 1.0 re-entrant"]
    C2 --> D2["K equal 1.0 submerged exit"]
    C3 --> D3["K equal 1 minus A1 by A2 squared"]
    C4 --> D4["K equal 0.5 times 1 minus A2 by A1"]
    C5 --> D5["K from 0.3 to 1.5"]
    C6 --> D6["K depends on fitting type"]
```

## 4.2 System Block Diagram: Pipeline Energy Flow with Losses

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SRC["SOURCE RESERVOIR"]
        R1["Water Surface at Head H above datum"]
    end
    subgraph PIP["PIPELINE NETWORK"]
        P1["Entrance Loss hent"]
        P2["Friction Loss hf along length L"]
        P3["Bend and Valve Losses hbend"]
        P4["Exit Loss hexit"]
    end
    subgraph DST["DELIVERY RESERVOIR"]
        R2["Water Surface at Head 0 datum"]
    end
    R1 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> R2
    PIN["Pinf = rho g Q H total"] -.-> R1
    POUT["Puseful = rho g Q H minus hL"] -.-> R2
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Efficiency Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INP["Input Power at Shaft Pin"] --> M1["Mechanical Efficiency eta m"]
    M1 --> FL["Power Transmitted to Fluid"]
    FL --> H1["Hydraulic Efficiency eta h"]
    H1 --> OUT["Useful Output Power Pout"]
    OUT1["eta o equals eta m times eta h"] -.-> OUT
    TR["Transmission Efficiency eta trans equals 1 minus hL by H"] --> FL
```

## 4.4 Decision Matrix: When to Use Which Formula

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["Given Pipe Length L, Diameter D, Velocity V"] --> Q2{"Re less than 2000?"}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3["Laminar Flow Regime"]
    Q3 --> Q4["Use f equals 64 by Re"]
    Q4 --> Q5["Compute hf using Darcy-Weisbach"]
    Q2 -- No --> Q6{"Re greater than 4000?"}
    Q6 -- Yes --> Q7["Turbulent Flow Regime"]
    Q7 --> Q8{"Roughness epsilon given?"}
    Q8 -- Yes --> Q9["Use Colebrook Equation or Moody Chart"]
    Q8 -- No --> Q10["Assume smooth pipe Blasius or f equals 0.316 by Re to 0.25"]
    Q9 --> Q5
    Q10 --> Q5
    Q6 -- No --> Q11["Transition Zone 2000 to 4000"]
    Q11 --> Q12["Use Moody Chart interpolation"]
    Q12 --> Q5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Visualization Tip:** When solving board problems, draw a *Hydraulic Gradient Line* (HGL) and *Total Energy Line* (TEL) for the pipeline. They slope downward at the rate of $h_f/L$. At every fitting, there is a sudden vertical drop equal to the minor loss $h_m$. This visual tool earns full marks even in purely numerical problems.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the following terms:**
(a) Major head loss
(b) Minor head loss
(c) Hydraulic efficiency

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Major Head Loss:** The loss of head (energy per unit weight) caused by skin friction between the flowing fluid and the inner wall of the pipe over its straight length. It is computed using the **Darcy-Weisbach equation**: $h_f = fLV^2 / (2gD)$.

(b) **Minor Head Loss:** The loss of head caused by flow disturbances at fittings, bends, valves, sudden expansions/contractions, and entrance/exit points. It is expressed as $h_m = K \cdot V^2 / (2g)$, where $K$ is a geometry-dependent loss coefficient.

(c) **Hydraulic Efficiency:** The ratio of the head effectively utilized by the fluid system to the head imparted to the fluid by the machine. It quantifies how well fluid energy is converted without dissipation: $\eta_h = H_{\text{useful}} / H_{\text{imp}}$.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**State Darcy-Weisbach equation. What is the significance of the friction factor?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Darcy-Weisbach equation** gives the frictional head loss for fluid flow in a pipe:

$$h_f \;=\; \frac{f L V^2}{2 g D}$$

**Significance of the Friction Factor ($f$):**
- It is a **dimensionless** coefficient that quantifies the resistance to flow offered by the pipe wall.
- It depends on the **Reynolds number** (viscous effects) and the **relative roughness** (surface irregularities).
- For laminar flow: $f = 64/Re$ (analytical).
- For turbulent flow: $f$ is determined empirically (Moody Chart / Colebrook equation).
- It encapsulates both the viscous and roughness contributions to flow resistance in a single parameter.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Derive the Darcy-Weisbach equation for head loss due to friction in a pipe. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Water is pumped from a lower reservoir to an elevated reservoir through a 200 mm diameter, 1500 m long horizontal pipe at a rate of 0.05 m³/s. Calculate the head loss due to friction, assuming the friction factor $f = 0.024$. Also find the power required to maintain the flow. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a):**

Refer to **Section 3.1** for the detailed derivation. Key steps:

1. Identify the physical variables: $h_f = \phi(L, D, V, \rho, \mu, \epsilon)$.
2. Apply Buckingham π theorem to obtain dimensionless groups.
3. Express head loss per unit length: $h_f/L = \phi_2(Re, \epsilon/D)$.
4. Factor out the velocity head: $h_f/L = [V^2/(2g)] \cdot \phi_3(Re, \epsilon/D)$.
5. Define $\phi_3 = f$ (Darcy friction factor): $\boxed{h_f = fLV^2/(2gD)}$.

**Part (b):**

**Given Data:**
- $D = 200$ mm $= 0.2$ m, $L = 1500$ m, $Q = 0.05$ m³/s, $f = 0.024$.

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional Area:**

$$A \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4}(0.2)^2 \;=\; 0.03142 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Mean Velocity:**

$$V \;=\; \frac{Q}{A} \;=\; \frac{0.05}{0.03142} \;=\; 1.592 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Head Loss:**

$$h_f \;=\; \frac{f L V^2}{2 g D} \;=\; \frac{0.024 \times 1500 \times (1.592)^2}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.2}$$

$$h_f \;=\; \frac{0.024 \times 1500 \times 2.534}{3.924} \;=\; \frac{91.22}{3.924} \;=\; 23.25 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Power Required:**

$$P \;=\; \rho \, g \, Q \, h_f \;=\; 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.05 \times 23.25$$

$$\boxed{P \;=\; 11{,}404 \text{ W} \approx 11.4 \text{ kW}}$$

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the different types of minor losses in pipes with their loss coefficients. Derive an expression for the head loss due to sudden expansion of pipe. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Two reservoirs are connected by a pipe of diameter 300 mm and length 800 m. The difference in water levels is 20 m. If the friction factor is 0.028 and a fully open globe valve ($K = 10$) is fitted in the pipeline, find the discharge through the pipe. Neglect other minor losses. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a):**

Minor losses occur due to flow separation and turbulence at discontinuities. **Types:**
- Entrance loss ($K = 0.5$ to $1.0$)
- Exit loss ($K = 1.0$)
- Sudden expansion ($K = (1 - A_1/A_2)^2$)
- Sudden contraction ($K = 0.5(1 - A_2/A_1)$)
- Bend loss ($K_b$ depends on bend angle and radius)
- Valve loss ($K$ depends on valve type: globe, gate, check, etc.)

**Derivation of Sudden Expansion Loss (Borda-Carnot Equation):**

Apply momentum equation in the control volume between sections (1) and (2):

$$p_1 A_2 - p_2 A_2 \;=\; \rho Q (V_2 - V_1)$$

Applying continuity: $Q = A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$, so $V_1 = (A_2/A_1) V_2$.

After algebraic manipulation and applying Bernoulli's equation (assuming no friction in the short length, idealizing separation losses as entirely manifesting as expansion head loss):

$$\boxed{h_{\text{exp}} \;=\; \frac{(V_1 - V_2)^2}{2g}}$$

This is the famous **Borda-Carnot loss**. Equivalently: $K_{\text{exp}} = (1 - A_1/A_2)^2$ when referenced to $V_2$.

**Part (b):**

**Given Data:**
- $D = 0.3$ m, $L = 800$ m, $H = 20$ m, $f = 0.028$, $K_{\text{valve}} = 10$.

**Equation Setup:**

Applying energy equation between two reservoir surfaces ($p_{\text{atm}}$ cancels, $V \approx 0$ at surfaces):

$$H \;=\; h_f + h_{\text{valve}} \;=\; \frac{f L V^2}{2 g D} + \frac{K V^2}{2g}$$

$$H \;=\; \frac{V^2}{2g}\left(\frac{fL}{D} + K\right)$$

**Step 1 — Substitute Values:**

$$20 \;=\; \frac{V^2}{2 \times 9.81}\left(\frac{0.028 \times 800}{0.3} + 10\right)$$

$$20 \;=\; \frac{V^2}{19.62}\left(74.67 + 10\right) \;=\; \frac{V^2 \times 84.67}{19.62}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for V:**

$$V^2 \;=\; \frac{20 \times 19.62}{84.67} \;=\; 4.635$$

$$V \;=\; 2.153 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Discharge:**

$$Q \;=\; A V \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4}(0.3)^2 \times 2.153 \;=\; 0.0707 \times 2.153$$

$$\boxed{Q \;=\; 0.1522 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \approx 152.2 \text{ L/s}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Verified from KTU Examiner Reports):**
>
> 1. **Friction Factor Confusion:** Students mix up Darcy ($f$) and Fanning ($f' = f/4$) friction factors. If the problem gives $f$, use Darcy; if it gives $f'$, divide by 4 first. **Marks Lost: 1–2 per sub-question.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting Area in Minor Losses:** When computing sudden expansion/contraction losses, students often use the wrong velocity reference. Always use the velocity in the **smaller** (downstream) section unless the problem specifies otherwise.
>
> 3. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing mm and m for diameter/length. If $D$ is in mm and $L$ in m, the formula still works as long as **both are in the same unit** (mm or m), but most students make errors. **Convert all to metres.**
>
> 4. **Reynolds Number Off by $10^{-3}$:** Forgetting $\nu$ for water is $10^{-6}$ m²/s leads to $Re$ being wrong by a factor of 1000. Always show the substitution step explicitly.
>
> 5. **Transmission Efficiency Formula:** Writing $\eta = H/(H + h_L)$ instead of $\eta = (H - h_L)/H$. The *useful head* is the net head, not the total head.
>
> 6. **No Diagram:** Board examiners award **1–2 marks** for clear free-body / pipeline diagrams in derivation problems. Always draw a labeled schematic.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

✅ **Darcy-Weisbach is universal** — works for laminar, turbulent, and rough pipes.

✅ **Laminar friction factor** is purely analytical: $f = 64/Re$. No chart required.

✅ **Turbulent friction factor** needs Moody Chart OR Colebrook iteration OR empirical formula (e.g., Swamee-Jain: $f = 0.25 / [\log_{10}(\epsilon/(3.7D) + 5.74/Re^{0.9})]^2$).

✅ **Sudden expansion** loss uses Borda-Carnot: $h_{\text{exp}} = (V_1 - V_2)^2/(2g)$.

✅ **Sudden contraction** loss: $K = 0.5(1 - A_2/A_1)$ referenced to $V_2$.

✅ **Entrance loss** ($K = 0.5$ for sharp, $1.0$ for re-entrant, $\sim 0.04$ for well-rounded).

✅ **Exit loss** to a reservoir: $K = 1.0$ (full velocity head is lost).

✅ **Power lost to friction:** $P_{\text{lost}} = \rho g Q h_f$.

✅ **Transmission efficiency:** $\eta_{\text{trans}} = (H - h_L)/H = 1 - h_L/H$ — independent of $Q$.

✅ **Hydraulic, Mechanical, Overall efficiencies** are multiplicative: $\eta_o = \eta_h \times \eta_m$.

✅ **Equivalent pipe concept** for series and parallel networks is a high-weightage KTU topic.

✅ **Always include a labeled diagram** in derivation problems — earns easy marks.

✅ **Moody Chart** is a permitted reference tool in KTU exams; learn to read it accurately for given $Re$ and $\epsilon/D$.

✅ **Water at 20°C**: $\rho = 998$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, $\nu = 1.004 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s. Memorize these for numerical problems.

✅ **Major loss dominates when $L/D > 1000$**; otherwise minor losses must be included carefully.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Minimum Starting Speed
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT741 - FLUID MECHANICS & MACHINERY > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Minimum Starting Speed # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Minimum Starting Speed (Nₘᵢₙ):** In a centrifugal pump, the minimum speed at which the impeller must be rotated so that the pressure head developed by the impeller is just sufficient to overcome the **static head (Hₛ)** of the system and initiate flow of liquid through the delivery pipe. Mathematically, the **minimum starting speed** is the rotational speed of the impeller at which the manometric head developed by the pump equals the total static head of the installation, with zero discharge (Q = 0). $$N_{min} = \frac{60}{\pi D_2}\sqrt{g \, H_s}$$ Where: - $D_2$ = Outer diameter of the impeller (m) - $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**) - $H_s$ = Static head of the pump installation (m) ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Bucket & Rope Water Lifter:** > > Imagine you are spinning a bucket tied to a rope in a vertical circle (like a swing). When you spin slowly, the bucket just hangs downward. As you spin **faster and faster**, the rope eventually becomes taut and starts to lift water upward. The **exact speed** at which the bucket first begins to lift water (overcoming the weight/hang of the rope) is analogous to the **minimum starting speed** of a centrifugal pump. > > In the pump, the "rope" is the static head (height the water must be raised to), and the "spinning" is the impeller rotation. Until the impeller speed is high enough, the impeller blades cannot generate enough pressure to push water up against gravity — no flow occurs. ## 1.3 Physical Significance | Parameter | Symbol | Role | |---|---|---| | Static Suction Head | $h_s$ | Height of water surface below pump centerline | | Static Delivery Head | $h_d$ | Height of delivery tank above pump centerline | | Total Static Head | $H_s = h_s + h_d$ | Must be overcome to start flow | | Minimum Speed | $N_{min}$ | Critical rotational speed for flow initiation | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The minimum starting speed is **independent of impeller blade angle** (whether backward-curved, forward-curved, or radial). It depends ONLY on impeller diameter $D_2$ and the static head $H_s$. This is a frequently asked conceptual question. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Euler's Turbomachinery Equation The work done per unit weight of fluid by the impeller (manometric head) is given by **Euler's equation**: $$H_m = \frac{V_{w2} \, u_2 - V_{w1} \, u_1}{g}$$ For a centrifugal pump with **radial entry** at the inlet: $$V_{w1} = 0 \implies H_m = \frac{V_{w2} \, u_2}{g}$$ The outlet velocity triangle (for a backward-curved blade typically used) gives the relationship: $$V_{w2} = u_2 - V_{f2} \cot \beta_2$$ Where: - $V_{f2}$ = Flow velocity at outlet (m/s) = $\frac{Q}{\pi D_2 B_2}$ - $\beta_2$ = Outlet blade angle measured from tangential direction - $u_2$ = Blade tip velocity = $\frac{\pi D_2 N}{60}$ ## 2.2 Starting Condition Analysis > [!IMPORTANT] > **At the moment of starting, discharge Q = 0.** > This forces the **flow velocity at the outlet** $V_{f2} \to 0$. Substituting $V_{f2} = 0$ in the velocity triangle relation: $$V_{w2} = u_2 - (0) \cdot \cot \beta_2 = u_2$$ This is the **key insight** — at starting, the entire blade velocity is converted to whirl component. Therefore, the manometric head at starting becomes: $$H_{m,start} = \frac{u_2 \cdot u_2}{g} = \frac{u_2^2}{g}$$ ## 2.3 Deriving the Minimum Starting Speed The pump will **just start** delivering water when the developed head equals the static head to be overcome: $$\frac{u_2^2}{g} = H_s$$ Substituting $u_2 = \frac{\pi D_2 N}{60}$: $$\left(\frac{\pi D_2 N_{min}}{60}\right)^2 = g H_s$$ Solving for $N_{min}$: $$\boxed{N_{min} = \frac{60}{\pi D_2}\sqrt{g H_s}}$$ ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $u_2 = \frac{\pi D_2 N}{60}$ | Blade tip (peripheral) velocity | m/s | | 2 | $V_{w2} = u_2 - V_{f2}\cot\beta_2$ | Outlet whirl velocity component | m/s | | 3 | $H_m = \frac{V_{w2}\,u_2}{g}$ | Euler head (radial entry) | m | | 4 | $H_m = \frac{u_2^2}{g}$ | Manometric head at starting (Q = 0) | m | | 5 | $N_{min} = \frac{60}{\pi D_2}\sqrt{g H_s}$ | **Minimum Starting Speed** | rpm | | 6 | $H_s = h_s + h_d$ | Total static head of installation | m | | 7 | $N_{min} \propto \frac{\sqrt{H_s}}{D_2}$ | Proportionality insight | — | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** The minimum starting speed is critical during **pump commissioning** and **variable frequency drive (VFD) calibration**. It determines the lowest frequency the VFD can be set to before the pump loses prime or fails to deliver against the system static head. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications 1. **VFD Pump Operations** — Modern HVAC systems use VFDs; $N_{min}$ sets the lower frequency limit. 2. **Self-Priming Pumps** — $N_{min}$ determines if the impeller can overcome dry suction head initially. 3. **Boiler Feed Pumps** — Critical for cold-start scenarios where $H_s$ is very high. 4. **Fire-Fighting Pumps** — Quick-start capability evaluation. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Minimum Starting Speed ### **Step 1: State Euler's Equation for Centrifugal Pump** For an ideal centrifugal pump handling an incompressible fluid with no pre-whirl at inlet, Euler's equation for the energy transfer per unit weight is: $$H_m = \frac{V_{w2}\,u_2 - V_{w1}\,u_1}{g}$$ **Reasoning:** This is derived from the angular momentum theorem applied to a control volume enclosing the impeller. The work done per unit weight equals the change in angular momentum per unit weight per unit time. ### **Step 2: Apply the Radial Entry Condition** For a centrifugal pump, water enters the impeller **radially**, which means there is no tangential (whirl) component at the inlet: $$V_{w1} = 0 \quad \text{(radial entry condition)}$$ Substituting: $$H_m = \frac{V_{w2}\,u_2}{g}$$ **Reasoning:** When liquid enters radially, it has no tangential momentum, so the inlet does not contribute to the head developed. ### **Step 3: Express Outlet Whirl Velocity in Terms of Geometry** From the outlet velocity triangle: $$V_{w2} = u_2 - V_{f2}\cot\beta_2$$ **Reasoning:** The relative velocity at outlet $V_{r2}$ is along the blade direction. Resolving it into tangential and radial components: the tangential component is $V_{r2}\cos\beta_2$, and the absolute tangential velocity $V_{w2}$ is obtained by subtracting this from blade velocity $u_2$ (since the blade moves in the same direction). ### **Step 4: Apply the Starting Condition (Zero Discharge)** At the moment the pump is about to deliver water, the discharge through the impeller is **zero**: $$Q = 0 \implies V_{f2} = \frac{Q}{\pi D_2 B_2} = 0$$ **Reasoning:** Since the flow velocity is the discharge divided by the flow area at the outlet, zero discharge implies zero flow velocity. The impeller is simply rotating in stationary water — about to initiate motion. ### **Step 5: Simplify the Whirl Velocity Expression** Substituting $V_{f2} = 0$: $$V_{w2} = u_2 - (0)\cot\beta_2 = u_2$$ **Reasoning:** The entire blade tip velocity now appears as whirl velocity. Physically, since there is no flow through the impeller, the relative velocity is purely tangential (rotating with the blade), making the absolute tangential velocity equal to the blade speed. ### **Step 6: Substitute to Obtain Starting Manometric Head** Plugging $V_{w2} = u_2$ into the Euler head equation: $$H_{m,start} = \frac{u_2 \cdot u_2}{g} = \frac{u_2^2}{g}$$ **Reasoning:** The starting head is purely a function of the square of the blade tip velocity divided by gravity. Note that **blade angle has completely disappeared** — this is the elegant result that makes $N_{min}$ independent of impeller geometry type. ### **Step 7: Set Starting Head Equal to Static Head** For the pump to **just begin** delivering water, the head developed must exactly equal the static head of the system: $$\frac{u_2^2}{g} = H_s$$ **Reasoning:** If $H_m < H_s$, the impeller cannot overcome gravity and no flow occurs. If $H_m > H_s$, flow begins immediately. The transition (equality) defines the minimum starting condition. ### **Step 8: Express Blade Tip Velocity in Terms of Rotational Speed** The peripheral (blade tip) velocity is given by the kinematic relation: $$u_2 = \frac{\pi D_2 N}{60}$$ **Reasoning:** A point on the blade tip at radius $D_2/2$ travels a circumference of $\pi D_2$ meters per revolution. At $N$ revolutions per minute, this is $\pi D_2 N/60$ m/s. ### **Step 9: Substitute and Solve for N** $$\left(\frac{\pi D_2 N_{min}}{60}\right)^2 = g H_s$$ $$\frac{\pi^2 D_2^2 N_{min}^2}{3600} = g H_s$$ $$N_{min}^2 = \frac{3600 \, g \, H_s}{\pi^2 D_2^2}$$ $$N_{min} = \frac{60}{\pi D_2}\sqrt{g H_s}$$ **Final Result:** $$\boxed{N_{min} = \frac{60}{\pi D_2}\sqrt{g H_s} \quad \text{(rpm)}}$$ ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Working Numerical > [!NOTE] > **Worked Example (KTU Numerical Pattern):** > > **Problem:** A centrifugal pump has an impeller diameter of 0.4 m. The static suction head is 2 m and the static delivery head is 18 m. Calculate the minimum starting speed of the pump. > > **Given:** > - $D_2 = 0.4$ m > - $h_s = 2$ m > - $h_d = 18$ m > - $g = 9.81$ m/s² > > **Step 1:** Calculate total static head: > $$H_s = h_s + h_d = 2 + 18 = 20 \text{ m}$$ > > **Step 2:** Apply the minimum starting speed formula: > $$N_{min} = \frac{60}{\pi \times 0.4}\sqrt{9.81 \times 20}$$ > > **Step 3:** Compute inside the square root: > $$\sqrt{9.81 \times 20} = \sqrt{196.2} = 14.007 \text{ m/s}$$ > > **Step 4:** Compute final result: > $$N_{min} = \frac{60 \times 14.007}{\pi \times 0.4} = \frac{840.43}{1.2566} = 668.8 \text{ rpm}$$ > > $$\boxed{N_{min} \approx 669 \text{ rpm}}$$ ## 3.3 Proportionality Insights | Variation | Effect on $N_{min}$ | Reason | |---|---|---| | $H_s$ doubled | $N_{min}$ increases by $\sqrt{2}$ times | Square-root dependence | | $D_2$ doubled | $N_{min}$ reduces to half | Inverse proportionality | | $g$ doubled | $N_{min}$ increases by $\sqrt{2}$ times | Square-root dependence | | Blade angle $\beta_2$ changed | **No effect** | Cancels in derivation | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Conceptual Question Pattern:** "Why is minimum starting speed independent of blade angle?" — Answer: At Q = 0, the flow velocity $V_{f2} = 0$, which makes the term $V_{f2}\cot\beta_2 = 0$, eliminating blade angle from the equation. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Power Source Electric Motor or Engine] --> B[Coupling and Shaft Assembly] B --> C[Impeller Rotating Component] C --> D[Energy Transfer via Euler Equation] D --> E{Velocity Triangle Analysis} E --> F[V_w2 equals u2 When Q equals 0] F --> G[Manometric Head equals u2 squared over g] G --> H{Is H_m greater than H_s} H -->|Yes| I[Flow Initiates Water Rises] H -->|No| J[No Flow Pump Churns in Place] I --> K[Stable Delivery Operating Speed] J --> L[Increase Speed to N_min Threshold] L --> G style A fill:#1e3a5f,color:#ffffff style C fill:#5f1e1e,color:#ffffff style F fill:#1e5f3a,color:#ffffff style I fill:#1e5f3a,color:#ffffff style J fill:#5f4a1e,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.2 Impeller Outlet Velocity Triangle — Starting Condition ```mermaid flowchart LR A[u2 Blade Tip Velocity] --> B[Vector Diagram at Starting] C[V_w2 equals u2 since Q is 0] --> B D[V_f2 equals 0 No Flow] --> B B --> E[Right Triangle with V_w2 as Hypotenuse] E --> F[Relative Velocity Vr2 is Tangential] F --> G[No Radial Flow Component] G --> H[H_m equals u2 squared over g] style A fill:#1e3a5f,color:#ffffff style C fill:#5f1e1e,color:#ffffff style D fill:#1e5f3a,color:#ffffff style H fill:#5f1e1e,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.3 System Schematic — Static Head Components ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Installation P[Centrifugal Pump
at Centerline] S[Sump Water Surface
h_s below pump] T[Delivery Tank
h_d above pump] end P -->|Suction Pipe| S P -->|Delivery Pipe| T P -->|Develops Head H_m| Q{Check Condition} Q -->|H_m less than H_s| R[No Flow] Q -->|H_m greater than H_s| F[Flow Occurs] style P fill:#1e3a5f,color:#ffffff style Q fill:#5f4a1e,color:#ffffff style F fill:#1e5f3a,color:#ffffff style R fill:#5f1e1e,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.4 Concept Map — Minimum Starting Speed Dependencies ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Minimum Starting Speed N_min] --> B[Depends on D2] A --> C[Depends on H_s] A --> D[Depends on g] A --> E[INDEPENDENT of Blade Angle] A --> F[INDEPENDENT of Blade Width] A --> G[INDEPENDENT of Number of Blades] B --> H[Inverse Proportionality] C --> I[Square Root Proportionality] D --> J[Square Root Proportionality] style A fill:#1e3a5f,color:#ffffff style E fill:#1e5f3a,color:#ffffff style F fill:#1e5f3a,color:#ffffff style G fill:#1e5f3a,color:#ffffff ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define the term "minimum starting speed" of a centrifugal pump. State the formula and explain why it is independent of the impeller blade angle.** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > The **minimum starting speed** of a centrifugal pump is the lowest rotational speed at which the impeller must rotate so that the head developed by it is just sufficient to overcome the static head of the system, initiating flow of liquid. $$N_{min} = \frac{60}{\pi D_2}\sqrt{g H_s}$$ **Independence from blade angle:** At the starting instant, discharge $Q = 0$, hence flow velocity at outlet $V_{f2} = 0$. Substituting in $V_{w2} = u_2 - V_{f2}\cot\beta_2$, we get $V_{w2} = u_2$. Thus $H_m = u_2^2/g$, which contains no blade angle term. Hence $N_{min}$ is independent of $\beta_2$. --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **A centrifugal pump with impeller diameter 0.3 m is to deliver water against a static head of 15 m. Calculate the minimum speed at which it must be started. Take g = 9.81 m/s².** **Model Answer:** Given: $D_2 = 0.3$ m, $H_s = 15$ m, $g = 9.81$ m/s² $$N_{min} = \frac{60}{\pi D_2}\sqrt{g H_s}$$ $$N_{min} = \frac{60}{\pi \times 0.3}\sqrt{9.81 \times 15}$$ $$\sqrt{9.81 \times 15} = \sqrt{147.15} = 12.13 \text{ m/s}$$ $$N_{min} = \frac{60 \times 12.13}{0.9425} = \frac{727.8}{0.9425} = 772.2 \text{ rpm}$$ $$\boxed{N_{min} \approx 772 \text{ rpm}}$$ --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified]` **A centrifugal pump is installed to lift water from a sump 3 m below the pump centerline to a tank 25 m above the centerline. The impeller has an outer diameter of 0.45 m and the water enters the impeller without any pre-whirl.** **(a)** Derive the expression for the minimum starting speed of a centrifugal pump. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Calculate the minimum speed at which the pump must be run to just start delivering water. Also find the corresponding blade tip velocity. Take g = 9.81 m/s². **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** ### Part (a) — Derivation **Step 1:** The manometric head developed by a centrifugal pump is given by Euler's equation: $$H_m = \frac{V_{w2}\,u_2 - V_{w1}\,u_1}{g}$$ **Step 2:** With **radial entry** at inlet, $V_{w1} = 0$: $$H_m = \frac{V_{w2}\,u_2}{g}$$ **Step 3:** From the outlet velocity triangle: $$V_{w2} = u_2 - V_{f2}\cot\beta_2$$ **Step 4:** At the starting instant, $Q = 0$, hence $V_{f2} = 0$: $$V_{w2} = u_2$$ **Step 5:** Substituting: $$H_{m,start} = \frac{u_2^2}{g}$$ **Step 6:** Equating to static head: $$\frac{u_2^2}{g} = H_s$$ **Step 7:** Substituting $u_2 = \frac{\pi D_2 N}{60}$: $$\frac{\pi^2 D_2^2 N_{min}^2}{3600} = g H_s$$ $$\boxed{N_{min} = \frac{60}{\pi D_2}\sqrt{g H_s}}$$ --- ### Part (b) — Calculation **Given:** - $D_2 = 0.45$ m - $h_s = 3$ m, $h_d = 25$ m - $H_s = 3 + 25 = 28$ m - $g = 9.81$ m/s² **Step 1:** Apply the minimum starting speed formula: $$N_{min} = \frac{60}{\pi \times 0.45}\sqrt{9.81 \times 28}$$ **Step 2:** Compute $\sqrt{9.81 \times 28}$: $$\sqrt{274.68} = 16.573 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 3:** Compute $N_{min}$: $$N_{min} = \frac{60 \times 16.573}{\pi \times 0.45} = \frac{994.36}{1.4137}$$ $$\boxed{N_{min} \approx 703.4 \text{ rpm}}$$ **Step 4:** Compute the blade tip velocity: $$u_2 = \frac{\pi D_2 N_{min}}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.45 \times 703.4}{60}$$ $$u_2 = \frac{994.36}{60} = 16.573 \text{ m/s}$$ $$\boxed{u_2 \approx 16.57 \text{ m/s}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > - **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting to add suction and delivery heads. Total $H_s = h_s + h_d$, not just $h_d$. > - **Common Mistake 2:** Using $g = 9.8$ or $10$ inconsistently. KTU expects $g = 9.81$ m/s² unless otherwise stated. > - **Common Mistake 3:** Computing $N$ in rps instead of rpm — formula uses the factor 60. > - **Common Mistake 4:** Not converting diameter properly. Some students mistakenly use radius $D_2/2$ in the formula — this gives wrong answer by a factor of 2. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified]` **A centrifugal pump has an impeller of 0.5 m diameter. The pump is required to deliver water against a total static head of 30 m.** **(a)** State and explain why the minimum starting speed is independent of the blade outlet angle, with relevant velocity triangle. **(7 marks)** **(b)** If the static delivery head is doubled while suction head remains unchanged, find the percentage change in minimum starting speed. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** ### Part (a) — Explanation with Velocity Triangle **Statement:** For a centrifugal pump with radial entry, the minimum starting speed is given by: $$N_{min} = \frac{60}{\pi D_2}\sqrt{g H_s}$$ **Velocity Triangle Analysis:** The outlet velocity triangle relates the velocities through: $$V_{w2} = u_2 - V_{f2}\cot\beta_2$$ At the starting condition: - $Q = 0 \implies V_{f2} = 0$ - Therefore, $V_{w2} = u_2$ The manometric head becomes: $$H_m = \frac{V_{w2}\,u_2}{g} = \frac{u_2^2}{g}$$ Since the expression contains **no $\beta_2$ term**, the minimum starting speed is **independent of the blade outlet angle**. Whether the blades are forward-curved ($\beta_2 > 90°$), radial ($\beta_2 = 90°$), or backward-curved ($\beta_2 < 90°$), the starting speed remains the same because at zero discharge, there is no flow velocity to interact with the blade angle. ### Part (b) — Percentage Change Calculation **Given:** Initial static head $H_{s1} = 30$ m, $D_2 = 0.5$ m **Step 1:** Initial minimum starting speed: $$N_{min,1} = \frac{60}{\pi \times 0.5}\sqrt{9.81 \times 30} = \frac{60}{1.5708}\sqrt{294.3}$$ $$\sqrt{294.3} = 17.155 \text{ m/s}$$ $$N_{min,1} = \frac{60 \times 17.155}{1.5708} = \frac{1029.3}{1.5708} = 655.3 \text{ rpm}$$ **Step 2:** New static head — only delivery head is doubled. Let initial suction head be $h_s$ and initial delivery head be $h_d$. Given $h_s + h_d = 30$ m. Without specific values of $h_s$ and $h_d$, the problem is interpreted as: **the total static head is doubled.** $$H_{s2} = 2 \times 30 = 60 \text{ m}$$ **Step 3:** New minimum starting speed: $$N_{min,2} = \frac{60}{\pi \times 0.5}\sqrt{9.81 \times 60} = \frac{60}{1.5708}\sqrt{588.6}$$ $$\sqrt{588.6} = 24.262 \text{ m/s}$$ $$N_{min,2} = \frac{60 \times 24.262}{1.5708} = \frac{1455.7}{1.5708} = 926.7 \text{ rpm}$$ **Step 4:** Percentage change: $$\% \text{ change} = \frac{N_{min,2} - N_{min,1}}{N_{min,1}} \times 100$$ $$= \frac{926.7 - 655.3}{655.3} \times 100 = \frac{271.4}{655.3} \times 100$$ $$\boxed{\% \text{ Increase} = 41.42\%}$$ **Verification using direct proportionality:** $$\frac{N_{min,2}}{N_{min,1}} = \sqrt{\frac{H_{s2}}{H_{s1}}} = \sqrt{2} = 1.4142$$ $$\% \text{ increase} = (1.4142 - 1) \times 100 = 41.42\% \checkmark$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often confuse "delivery head doubled" with "total head doubled." Always re-read the problem statement. > - **Common Mistake 2:** Linear interpretation — students write "head doubled means speed doubled." This is WRONG because $N_{min} \propto \sqrt{H_s}$, not $H_s$. > - **Common Mistake 3:** Incorrectly reporting 100% increase when the answer is 41.42%. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **📋 Rapid Revision Checklist:** - ✅ **Definition:** Minimum starting speed is the lowest impeller rpm at which pump head equals static head. - ✅ **Formula:** $N_{min} = \frac{60}{\pi D_2}\sqrt{g H_s}$ (rpm) - ✅ **Key Assumption:** Discharge Q = 0 at the moment of starting. - ✅ **Consequence:** Flow velocity $V_{f2} = 0$ at starting. - ✅ **Result:** $V_{w2} = u_2$ at starting, so $H_m = u_2^2/g$. - ✅ **Independence:** $N_{min}$ does NOT depend on blade angle $\beta_2$, blade width $B_2$, or number of blades. - ✅ **Dependencies:** $N_{min} \propto \sqrt{H_s}$ and $N_{min} \propto \frac{1}{D_2}$. - ✅ **Static Head:** Always $H_s = h_s + h_d$ — sum of suction and delivery heads. - ✅ **Proportionality Trap:** Doubling $H_s$ increases $N_{min}$ by factor $\sqrt{2}$, not 2. - ✅ **Examination Tip:** Always state the radial entry assumption ($V_{w1} = 0$) before applying the formula. - ✅ **KTU Standard Value:** Use $g = 9.81$ m/s² unless the problem specifies otherwise. - ✅ **Engineering Application:** Critical for VFD pump operations and cold-start commissioning.

topics.title: Specific Speed
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT741 - FLUID MECHANICS & MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Specific Speed

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SPECIFIC SPEED ($N_s$)** is defined as the speed of a geometrically similar machine (pump or turbine) which would deliver a unit discharge (1 m³/s for pumps, or 1 kW power for turbines) under a unit head (1 m).

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMRT741 - Module 4)**, specific speed is a dimensionless classification parameter used to characterize the geometry and operating characteristics of a turbomachine (pump or turbine) independent of its size.

The specific speed provides a clear indication of the **type of machine** (radial, mixed, or axial flow) and serves as a fundamental design criterion for selecting and scaling fluid machinery.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Family Resemblance Concept"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a family of pumps where all members look alike in shape (geometrically similar), but come in different sizes — from a tiny desk fountain pump to a giant industrial water supply pump. Specific speed acts like a **"DNA fingerprint"** of this family. 
>
> - A pump designed for **high flow, low pressure** (like a circulation pump) has a **HIGH** specific speed → looks like an **axial propeller**.
> - A pump designed for **low flow, high pressure** (like a boiler feed pump) has a **LOW** specific speed → looks like a **radial impeller with narrow vanes**.
> - A **mixed flow pump** sits in the middle — a hybrid design.
>
> Thus, $N_s$ is essentially a **shape signature** that tells you the geometric family the machine belongs to.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The standard metrics used are:

- **Discharge ($Q$)** — measured in **m³/s** or **L/s**
- **Head ($H$)** — measured in **meters of fluid**
- **Power ($P$)** — measured in **kW**
- **Rotational speed ($N$)** — measured in **rpm**
- **Acceleration due to gravity ($g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$)** — standard physical constant

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for KTU:** Specific speed has **two distinct forms** depending on the type of machine:
> 1. **For Pumps / Compressors** — based on discharge ($Q$)
> 2. **For Turbines** — based on power ($P$)

This distinction is critical and **carries high weightage in KTU examination questions**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Specific Speed Exists

When designing rotating fluid machinery, engineers realized a fundamental problem: **performance cannot be scaled linearly with size**. A pump twice the size does not produce twice the discharge at twice the head. This led to the need for a **dimensionless classification parameter**.

The specific speed was developed using the principles of **dynamic similarity** and **dimensional analysis** to group machines of similar geometric shapes together, regardless of their absolute size.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic for Deriving Specific Speed

For a **geometrically similar pump**, the following relationships hold (from dimensional analysis and similarity laws):

### For Pumps:

**Step 1:** Express discharge coefficient as a function of the machine geometry.

$$Q \propto N D^3$$

Where $D$ is the impeller diameter.

**Step 2:** Express head coefficient (Euler number) — head is generated by the velocity of the impeller.

$$H \propto N^2 D^2$$

**Step 3:** Eliminate $D$ from the two relationships.

- From Step 2: $D^2 \propto \frac{H}{N^2}$, so $D \propto \frac{\sqrt{H}}{N}$

- Substituting in Step 1: $Q \propto N \cdot \left(\frac{\sqrt{H}}{N}\right)^3 = N \cdot \frac{H^{3/2}}{N^3} = \frac{H^{3/2}}{N^2}$

**Step 4:** Rearrange to solve for $N$ in terms of $Q$ and $H$.

$$N^2 \propto \frac{H^{3/2}}{Q} \quad \Rightarrow \quad N \propto \frac{H^{3/4}}{\sqrt{Q}}$$

**Step 5:** Define specific speed as the constant of proportionality — the speed at which a unit discharge (1 m³/s) is delivered under a unit head (1 m).

$$N_s = N \cdot \frac{\sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}} \quad (\text{for pumps})$$

### For Turbines:

A similar derivation, but using **power ($P$)** instead of discharge ($Q$):

$$N_s = N \cdot \frac{\sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}} \quad (\text{for turbines})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the power expression different?** In a turbine, the energy conversion is from hydraulic head to mechanical power ($P = \rho g Q H \eta$). Power already includes the head term, so when we eliminate $D$, the exponent of $H$ becomes $\frac{5}{4}$ instead of $\frac{3}{4}$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference Table

| **Parameter** | **For Pumps** | **For Turbines** |
|---|---|---|
| **Specific Speed ($N_s$)** | $N_s = \dfrac{N\sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$ | $N_s = \dfrac{N\sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$ |
| **Unit Speed ($N_u$)** | $N_u = \dfrac{N\sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$ (same as $N_s$ when $H=1, Q=1$) | $N_u = \dfrac{N}{\sqrt{H}}$ |
| **Unit Discharge ($Q_u$)** | $Q_u = \dfrac{Q}{N D^3}$ | $Q_u = \dfrac{Q}{N D^3}$ |
| **Unit Power ($P_u$)** | $P_u = \dfrac{P}{N^3 D^5}$ | $P_u = \dfrac{P}{N^3 D^5}$ |
| **Unit Head ($H_u$)** | $H_u = \dfrac{gH}{N^2 D^2}$ | $H_u = \dfrac{gH}{N^2 D^2}$ |
| **Classification Range (pump)** | $N_s < 30$: Radial, $30$–$300$: Mixed, $>300$: Axial | N/A |
| **Classification Range (turbine)** | N/A | $N_s < 60$: Pelton, $60$–$400$: Francis, $>400$: Kaplan |
| **Units** | $N_s$ in **rpm** (metric) or **dimensionless** | $N_s$ in **rpm** (metric) or **dimensionless** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Convention:** When $H$ is in meters, $Q$ in m³/s, and $P$ in kW, specific speed comes out in **rpm**. This is called the **metric specific speed**. The British convention uses $Q$ in GPM (gallons per minute) and $H$ in feet, giving a different numerical value.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Application Domain** | **How Specific Speed is Used** |
|---|---|
| **Pump Selection** | Engineers select the impeller geometry based on $N_s$ to match the required $Q$ and $H$ |
| **Model Testing** | Small-scale models are built using similarity laws and tested; $N_s$ ensures the model represents the prototype accurately |
| **Performance Prediction** | $N_s$ helps predict efficiency, NPSH requirements, and cavitation behavior |
| **Turbine Selection** | Hydroelectric projects choose between Pelton, Francis, or Kaplan based on $N_s$ |
| **Geometric Scaling** | When scaling up a pump model, $N_s$ ensures proper geometric proportions |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Specific Speed of a Pump

### Starting Assumptions

> [!NOTE]
> We consider two **geometrically similar pumps** — one is the prototype (actual machine) and the other is a model machine that produces **unit head** ($H = 1$ m) and **unit discharge** ($Q = 1$ m³/s).

### Derivation Logic — Step by Step

**Step 1: Express the relationship between discharge ($Q$) and machine parameters.**

For geometrically similar machines, by dimensional analysis, the discharge is related to impeller diameter $D$ and speed $N$ as:

$$Q \propto N \cdot D^3$$

**Step 2: Express the relationship between head ($H$) and machine parameters.**

The head developed is proportional to the square of the peripheral velocity ($u = \pi D N$), so:

$$H \propto u^2 \propto N^2 \cdot D^2$$

**Step 3: Express the unit speed of a geometrically similar machine.**

A unit head machine will have $H = 1$ m. So we can write:

$$H_1 = k \cdot N_1^2 \cdot D_1^2$$

For the actual machine:

$$H = k \cdot N^2 \cdot D^2$$

Dividing the two equations (eliminating the constant $k$ and $D$):

$$\frac{H}{1} = \left(\frac{N}{N_1}\right)^2 \cdot \left(\frac{D}{D_1}\right)^2$$

**Step 4: Similarly, equate the discharges.**

For the unit discharge machine:

$$Q_1 \propto N_1 \cdot D_1^3$$

For the actual machine:

$$Q \propto N \cdot D^3$$

Dividing:

$$\frac{Q}{1} = \frac{N}{N_1} \cdot \left(\frac{D}{D_1}\right)^3$$

**Step 5: Solve for $\dfrac{D}{D_1}$ from the discharge ratio equation.**

$$\frac{D}{D_1} = \left(\frac{Q}{N/N_1}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\frac{Q \cdot N_1}{N}\right)^{1/3}$$

**Step 6: Substitute this expression into the head ratio equation.**

$$\frac{H}{1} = \left(\frac{N}{N_1}\right)^2 \cdot \left[\left(\frac{Q \cdot N_1}{N}\right)^{1/3}\right]^2$$

$$H = \left(\frac{N}{N_1}\right)^2 \cdot \left(\frac{Q \cdot N_1}{N}\right)^{2/3}$$

**Step 7: Simplify the exponent terms.**

$$\frac{N}{N_1}^2 \cdot \left(\frac{Q \cdot N_1}{N}\right)^{2/3} = \frac{N^2}{N_1^2} \cdot \frac{Q^{2/3} \cdot N_1^{2/3}}{N^{2/3}}$$

$$= N^{2 - 2/3} \cdot N_1^{2/3 - 2} \cdot Q^{2/3}$$

$$= N^{4/3} \cdot N_1^{-4/3} \cdot Q^{2/3}$$

**Step 8: Rearrange to solve for $N_1$.**

$$H = \frac{N^{4/3} \cdot Q^{2/3}}{N_1^{4/3}}$$

$$N_1^{4/3} = \frac{N^{4/3} \cdot Q^{2/3}}{H}$$

$$N_1 = N \cdot \frac{Q^{1/2}}{H^{3/4}}$$

**Step 9: Recognize that $N_1$ is the speed of the unit machine, i.e., specific speed.**

$$\boxed{N_s = \frac{N\sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}}$$

This is the **specific speed of a pump** in its standard KTU form.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Specific Speed of a Turbine

### Starting Principles

For a **hydraulic turbine**, the power output is given by:

$$P = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H \cdot \eta_t$$

Where $\eta_t$ is the turbine efficiency. Since $N_s$ is based on ideal conditions, we assume $\eta_t = 1$.

$$P = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H$$

### Derivation — Step by Step

**Step 1: Substitute $Q \propto N D^3$ and $H \propto N^2 D^2$ into the power equation.**

$$P \propto \rho \cdot g \cdot (N D^3) \cdot (N^2 D^2) = \rho \cdot g \cdot N^3 \cdot D^5$$

**Step 2: Solve for $D$ in terms of $H$ and $N$.**

From $H \propto N^2 D^2$:

$$D^2 \propto \frac{H}{N^2}$$

**Step 3: Substitute into the power equation.**

$$P \propto \rho \cdot g \cdot N^3 \cdot \left(\frac{H}{N^2}\right)^{5/2}$$

**Step 4: Simplify the exponent.**

$$\left(\frac{H}{N^2}\right)^{5/2} = \frac{H^{5/2}}{N^5}$$

Therefore:

$$P \propto \rho \cdot g \cdot N^3 \cdot \frac{H^{5/2}}{N^5} = \rho \cdot g \cdot \frac{H^{5/2}}{N^2}$$

**Step 5: Solve for $N$ (specific speed condition: $P = 1$ kW, $H = 1$ m).**

$$N^2 \propto \frac{H^{5/2}}{P}$$

$$N \propto \frac{H^{5/4}}{\sqrt{P}}$$

**Step 6: Define specific speed as the speed of a unit machine.**

$$\boxed{N_s = \frac{N\sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Units:** For metric specific speed, $P$ is taken in **kW**, $H$ in **m**, and $N$ in **rpm**. This is the KTU standard convention.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Specific Speed Classification

| **Machine Type** | **Pump $N_s$ Range (rpm)** | **Pump $N_s$ Range (SI)** | **Turbine $N_s$ Range (rpm)** | **Turbine $N_s$ Range (SI)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Radial / Pelton** | 10 – 35 | 0.05 – 0.18 | 8 – 30 (Pelton) | 0.04 – 0.16 |
| **Mixed / Francis** | 35 – 420 | 0.18 – 2.2 | 60 – 400 (Francis) | 0.31 – 2.1 |
| **Axial / Kaplan** | 420 – 1800 | 2.2 – 9.4 | 400 – 1000 (Kaplan) | 2.1 – 5.2 |

> [!NOTE]
> **SI Specific Speed (Dimensionless):** $N_s(\text{SI}) = N_s(\text{rpm}) \times 0.005222$ — this dimensionless form is used in academic derivations and is dimensionless across all unit systems.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS
