<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Define specific speed of a centrifugal pump. What is its significance?**

**Model Answer:**

Specific speed of a centrifugal pump is defined as the speed of a geometrically similar pump which would deliver **unit discharge (1 m³/s)** under **unit head (1 m)**.

$$N_s = \frac{N\sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$$

**Significance:**
- It is a **classification parameter** that determines the type of impeller (radial, mixed, or axial).
- It groups geometrically similar machines together regardless of size.
- It helps in **selecting the pump geometry** for a given $Q$ and $H$.
- It is used in **model testing** and **scaling up** prototype designs.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **Distinguish between specific speed of a pump and specific speed of a turbine.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **Pump** | **Turbine** |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition Basis** | Unit discharge under unit head | Unit power under unit head |
| **Formula** | $N_s = \dfrac{N\sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$ | $N_s = \dfrac{N\sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$ |
| **Exponent of H** | $\frac{3}{4}$ | $\frac{5}{4}$ |
| **Classification Range** | Radial $< 30 <$ Mixed $< 300 <$ Axial | Pelton $< 60 <$ Francis $< 400 <$ Kaplan |

The exponent of head differs because, in turbines, **power already contains head**, requiring additional head-power coupling in the derivation.

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (14 Marks)

> **(a)** Derive an expression for the specific speed of a centrifugal pump. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A centrifugal pump delivers 0.05 m³/s of water against a head of 30 m when running at 1450 rpm. Calculate the specific speed of the pump and identify the type of impeller. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

Starting with the dimensionless relationships for geometrically similar machines:

$$Q \propto N D^3 \quad \text{...(1)}$$

$$H \propto N^2 D^2 \quad \text{...(2)}$$

For a unit discharge machine ($Q_1 = 1$ m³/s) and unit head machine ($H_1 = 1$ m) running at speed $N_1$:

$$1 = k \cdot N_1 \cdot D_1^3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad D_1^3 = \frac{1}{k N_1}$$

$$1 = k \cdot N_1^2 \cdot D_1^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad D_1^2 = \frac{1}{k N_1^2}$$

Dividing equations:

$$\frac{Q}{1} = \frac{N D^3}{N_1 D_1^3} = \frac{N}{N_1} \cdot \left(\frac{D}{D_1}\right)^3$$

$$\frac{H}{1} = \frac{N^2 D^2}{N_1^2 D_1^2} = \left(\frac{N}{N_1}\right)^2 \cdot \left(\frac{D}{D_1}\right)^2$$

From the discharge ratio:

$$\left(\frac{D}{D_1}\right)^3 = \frac{Q \cdot N_1}{N} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \left(\frac{D}{D_1}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{Q \cdot N_1}{N}\right)^{2/3}$$

Substituting into the head ratio:

$$H = \left(\frac{N}{N_1}\right)^2 \cdot \left(\frac{Q \cdot N_1}{N}\right)^{2/3}$$

$$H = \frac{N^2}{N_1^2} \cdot \frac{Q^{2/3} \cdot N_1^{2/3}}{N^{2/3}} = \frac{N^{4/3} \cdot Q^{2/3}}{N_1^{4/3}}$$

Solving for $N_1$:

$$N_1 = N \cdot \frac{Q^{1/2}}{H^{3/4}}$$

Since $N_1$ is the speed of the unit machine, it is the specific speed:

$$\boxed{N_s = \frac{N\sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation:**

**Given:**
- $Q = 0.05$ m³/s
- $H = 30$ m
- $N = 1450$ rpm

**Step 1:** Apply the specific speed formula.

$$N_s = \frac{N\sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate $\sqrt{Q}$.

$$\sqrt{Q} = \sqrt{0.05} = 0.2236$$

**Step 3:** Calculate $H^{3/4}$.

$$H^{3/4} = 30^{0.75}$$

$$30^{0.75} = 30^{3/4} = \left(30^{1/2}\right) \cdot \left(30^{1/4}\right) = 5.477 \times 2.340 = 12.82$$

**Step 4:** Compute $N_s$.

$$N_s = \frac{1450 \times 0.2236}{12.82} = \frac{324.22}{12.82} = 25.29 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 5:** Classification.

Since $N_s = 25.29$ is **less than 30**, the pump has a **radial flow impeller** (centrifugal type).

**Answer:** $N_s \approx 25.3$ rpm → **Radial Flow Pump**

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (14 Marks)

> **(a)** Explain the significance of specific speed in the classification of hydraulic turbines. Mention the ranges for Pelton, Francis, and Kaplan turbines. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A Francis turbine develops 5000 kW under a head of 50 m at a speed of 500 rpm. Calculate the specific speed and verify the type of turbine. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Significance and Classification:**

Specific speed of a turbine is a **dimensionless design parameter** that characterizes the geometry and operating characteristics of the runner. Its significance includes:

1. **Machine Selection:** $N_s$ uniquely identifies whether the turbine is Pelton, Francis, or Kaplan — eliminating guesswork in design.

2. **Geometric Prediction:** It determines the **shape of the runner**, the number of buckets/blades, the specific speed, and the relative flow velocities.

3. **Performance Estimation:** $N_s$ correlates directly with efficiency, cavitation behavior, and runaway speed.

4. **Scaling and Modeling:** Geometrically similar turbines have the **same $N_s$** regardless of size, enabling accurate model-to-prototype scaling.

**Classification Ranges (Metric, $H$ in m, $P$ in kW, $N$ in rpm):**

| **Turbine Type** | **$N_s$ Range** | **Head Range** |
|---|---|---|
| **Pelton Wheel** | 8 – 30 | High head (300 m and above) |
| **Francis Turbine** | 60 – 400 | Medium head (30 m – 300 m) |
| **Kaplan / Propeller** | 400 – 1000 | Low head (below 30 m) |

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation:**

**Given:**
- $P = 5000$ kW
- $H = 50$ m
- $N = 500$ rpm

**Step 1:** Apply the turbine specific speed formula.

$$N_s = \frac{N\sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate $\sqrt{P}$.

$$\sqrt{P} = \sqrt{5000} = 70.71$$

**Step 3:** Calculate $H^{5/4}$.

$$H^{5/4} = 50^{1.25}$$

Breaking this down:

$$50^{1.25} = 50^{1} \cdot 50^{0.25} = 50 \times 50^{1/4}$$

$$50^{1/4} = (50)^{0.25} \approx 2.659$$

$$H^{5/4} = 50 \times 2.659 = 132.95$$

**Step 4:** Compute $N_s$.

$$N_s = \frac{500 \times 70.71}{132.95} = \frac{35355}{132.95} = 265.97 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 5:** Verification.

$N_s = 266$ rpm falls in the **range of 60 – 400**, confirming this is a **Francis Turbine**.

**Answer:** $N_s \approx 266$ rpm → **Francis Turbine** ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Loss Zones:**
> 1. **Confusing pump and turbine formulas** — Students often write $N_s = \dfrac{N\sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$ for a turbine. This is **a guaranteed 2-mark deduction**.
> 2. **Unit inconsistency in numerical problems** — Always convert $Q$ to m³/s, $H$ to m, $P$ to kW, and $N$ to rpm before computing.
> 3. **Calculation errors on fractional exponents** — $H^{3/4}$ and $H^{5/4}$ are commonly miscalculated; show the breakdown using square root and fourth root separately.
> 4. **Skipping the classification conclusion** — After computing $N_s$, students must explicitly state the **machine type** by comparing with the standard range. Without this, **1 to 2 marks are lost**.
> 5. **Forgetting to state units of specific speed** — Always mention "metric specific speed in rpm" when using SI inputs.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Specific Speed**

- ✅ Specific speed is a **classification parameter** based on **dynamic similarity**, not a measure of actual speed.
- ✅ **Pump formula:** $N_s = \dfrac{N\sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$ — based on unit discharge and unit head.
- ✅ **Turbine formula:** $N_s = \dfrac{N\sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$ — based on unit power and unit head.
- ✅ The exponent of $H$ in the turbine formula is **$\frac{5}{4}$** because power already includes head.
- ✅ **Pump Classification:** $< 30$ (Radial), $30$–$300$ (Mixed), $> 300$ (Axial).
- ✅ **Turbine Classification:** $< 60$ (Pelton), $60$–$400$ (Francis), $> 400$ (Kaplan).
- ✅ Specific speed has **units of rpm** in metric form and is **dimensionless** in SI form.
- ✅ Higher $N_s$ → **higher discharge, lower head** → more axial flow design.
- ✅ Lower $N_s$ → **lower discharge, higher head** → more radial flow design.
- ✅ Geometrically similar machines have the **same specific speed** irrespective of size.
- ✅ Specific speed is used in **model testing, scaling, and machine selection**.
- ✅ Conversion factor (metric to SI): $N_s(\text{SI}) = 0.005222 \times N_s(\text{rpm})$.
- ✅ Always state units explicitly and classify the machine after numerical computation.
- ✅ Unit speed $N_u = \dfrac{N}{\sqrt{H}}$ (dimensionless) is related but not identical to specific speed.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: NPSH
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT741 - FLUID MECHANICS & MACHINERY > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** NPSH ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Net Positive Suction Head (NPSH)** is the total head available at the suction port of a pump, measured **above the vapor pressure head** of the liquid being pumped. It is the algebraic measure of the excess pressure energy (over and above the local vapor pressure) that exists at the pump impeller eye to prevent the liquid from flashing into vapor. In formal KTU terminology, NPSH is classified into two distinct quantities: > [!IMPORTANT] > **NPSHA (Net Positive Suction Head Available):** > The total head actually available at the pump suction, computed from the system geometry, atmospheric pressure, liquid temperature (vapor pressure), and suction line losses. It is a **system-dependent** property. > [!IMPORTANT] > **NPSHR (Net Positive Suction Head Required):** > The minimum head that a specific pump design demands at its suction port to operate without internal cavitation. It is a **pump-dependent** property, determined experimentally by the manufacturer (usually at 3% head drop on test rig). The fundamental cavitation avoidance criterion is: $$\boxed{\text{NPSHA} \;>\; \text{NPSHR} \;+\; \text{Safety Margin}}$$ ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Vacuum Cleaner Hose" Intuition Imagine drinking a thick milkshake through a long, narrow straw. - If the milkshake is **right next to your mouth** (flooded suction), you barely need to suck — easy to drink. - If the milkshake is **far below your mouth** (high suction lift), you must pull very hard to lift it. - If you pull **so hard** that the pressure inside the straw drops below the pressure at which milkshake vapor bubbles can form, you get **bubbles in the straw** — that is **cavitation**. The flow becomes noisy, weak, and discontinuous. Your mouth represents the **pump impeller eye**. The harder the pump has to "suck" (lower the absolute pressure at its suction), the closer it gets to vapor pressure, and the higher the risk of cavitation. **NPSH is the "safety margin" against this sucking-induced vaporization.** ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics > [!NOTE] > - **Atmospheric Pressure (P_atm):** $\approx$ **101.325 kPa** $\approx$ **10.3 m of water** at sea level (standard reference). > - **Vapor Pressure (P_vp):** Strongly dependent on liquid temperature. For water at **30°C**, $P_{vp} \approx$ **0.243 m of water**; at **100°C**, $P_{vp} \approx$ **10.33 m of water** (equals atmospheric). > - **Specific Weight (ρg):** For water, **$\rho g$ = 9.81 kN/m³** at standard conditions. > - **Cavitation Threshold:** Local absolute pressure $\le P_{vp}$ triggers vapor bubble formation. ### 1.4 Why NPSH Matters in Pump Selection | Engineering Failure | Real-World Consequence | |---|---| | Cavitation onset | Pitting, erosion of impeller blades | | Sustained cavitation | Loss of head, drop in discharge, vibration | | Severe cavitation | Complete pump failure, mechanical seizure | | Insufficient NPSH margin | Reduced pump life, increased maintenance cost | ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Why and How The analysis of NPSH is rooted in three foundational pillars of fluid mechanics: **Pillar 1 — Bernoulli's Principle (Energy Conservation):** As liquid travels from the suction reservoir to the pump impeller, its total energy must be conserved (minus losses). The pressure head drops as elevation and friction losses are overcome. **Pillar 2 — Vapor Pressure Physics (Thermodynamics):** A liquid boils when local absolute pressure equals its saturation (vapor) pressure at the operating temperature. Inside a pump suction, this boiling manifests as cavitation bubbles. **Pillar 3 — Critical Cavitation Condition:** Cavitation begins when the absolute pressure at the impeller eye equals the vapor pressure. The "extra" pressure head above this critical value is precisely what NPSH measures. ### 2.2 Operational Steps — Computing NPSHA 1. **Identify the suction reservoir free surface** (datum reference, atmospheric pressure). 2. **Measure the static suction lift (Hs)** — vertical distance from free liquid surface to pump centerline. 3. **Compute velocity head in suction pipe** — using $V = Q/A$ where $A = \pi D^2/4$. 4. **Compute friction head loss (Hf)** using Darcy–Weisbach: $H_f = f L V^2 / (2 g D)$. 5. **Look up vapor pressure head (Hvp)** at the operating fluid temperature from steam tables. 6. **Apply the NPSHA formula** (preferred KTU convention): $$\text{NPSHA} \;=\; H_a - H_s - H_f - H_{vp}$$ 7. **Compare against NPSHR** from pump manufacturer's performance curve. 8. **Verify safety margin:** Typically $\text{NPSHA} - \text{NPSHR} \geq 0.5 \text{ to } 1.0 \text{ m}$ for safe operation. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Expression | Meaning of Terms | Typical Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\text{NPSHA} = H_a - H_s - H_f - H_{vp}$ | Standard KTU form (suction lift convention) | m of liquid | | 2 | $H_a = P_{atm} / (\rho g)$ | Atmospheric pressure head | m | | 3 | $H_{vp} = P_{vp} / (\rho g)$ | Vapor pressure head | m | | 4 | $H_f = f \cdot L \cdot V^2 / (2 g D)$ | Darcy–Weisbach friction head loss | m | | 5 | $V = Q / A = 4Q / (\pi D^2)$ | Velocity in suction pipe | m/s | | 6 | $V^2 / (2g)$ | Velocity head at suction flange | m | | 7 | $\sigma_c = \text{NPSHR} / H$ | Thomas cavitation parameter (critical) | dimensionless | | 8 | $\text{NPSHR} \propto N^2$ | Affinity law for NPSHR vs. speed | m | | 9 | $\text{NPSHR} \propto Q^{1.5}$ to $Q^2$ | Empirical NPSHR scaling with flow | m | | 10 | $P_{atm} - P_{vp} \geq \rho g (H_s + H_f) + \rho \cdot V^2 / 2$ | Cavitation avoidance (raw form) | Pa | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sign Convention (Critical for KTU Board):** > - $H_s$ is taken as **positive** when suction reservoir is **below** the pump centerline (true suction lift). > - $H_s$ becomes **negative** when reservoir is **above** pump centerline (flooded suction) — this *increases* NPSHA. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry | Application of NPSH Analysis | |---|---| | Boiler Feed Pumps | Hot water (high $P_{vp}$) demands flooded suction and low friction | | Oil & Gas | Multi-phase pumps with volatile hydrocarbons require precise NPSH margin | | Nuclear Reactors | Reactor coolant pumps are tested with rigorous NPSH safety margins | | Marine Engineering | Bilge and ballast pumps operate under varying atmospheric pressure | | HVAC / Chilled Water | Cold water (low $P_{vp}$) has comfortable NPSH; warm systems are critical | ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the NPSHA Formula **Step 1 — Set Up the Control Volume:** Apply the steady-flow energy equation (Bernoulli with losses) between two points: - **Point 1:** Free surface of suction reservoir (open to atmosphere) - **Point 2:** Eye of the pump impeller (where cavitation risk is highest) **Step 2 — Write the Energy Equation:** $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 \;=\; \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_f$$ **Step 3 — Apply Boundary Conditions at Point 1:** Since the reservoir surface is large and exposed to atmosphere: $$P_1 = P_{atm}, \quad V_1 \approx 0, \quad z_1 - z_2 = H_s \;(\text{suction lift, positive})$$ **Step 4 — Substitute into the Equation:** $$\frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} + 0 + z_2 + H_s \;=\; \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_f$$ The $z_2$ terms cancel: $$\frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} + H_s \;=\; \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + h_f$$ **Step 5 — Isolate the Total Head at Pump Eye:** $$\frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} \;=\; \frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} + H_s - h_f$$ **Step 6 — Apply the Definition of NPSHA:** NPSHA is the total head at the impeller eye **in excess of vapor pressure head**: $$\text{NPSHA} \;=\; \left( \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} \right) - \frac{P_{vp}}{\rho g}$$ **Step 7 — Combine Steps 5 and 6:** $$\text{NPSHA} \;=\; \frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} + H_s - h_f - \frac{P_{vp}}{\rho g}$$ **Step 8 — Introduce Head Notation:** $$\boxed{\;\text{NPSHA} \;=\; H_a + H_s - H_f - H_{vp}\;}$$ > [!NOTE] > The $V_2^2/(2g)$ term (velocity head at the impeller eye) is **absorbed into NPSHR** by convention, since pump manufacturers measure NPSHR at the suction flange and inherently account for internal velocity effects. ### 3.2 Derivation of the Thomas Cavitation Parameter The **cavitation number** is a dimensionless ratio that characterizes the susceptibility of a pump to cavitation: **Step 1:** Define total pump head developed as $H$. **Step 2:** The critical cavitation number relates the NPSHR to the developed head: $$\sigma_c \;=\; \frac{\text{NPSHR}}{H}$$ **Step 3:** Pump operates safely when: $$\sigma \;>\; \sigma_c \quad \text{(where } \sigma = \text{NPSHA}/H\text{)}$$ **Step 4:** Empirical values: For centrifugal pumps, $\sigma_c$ typically ranges from **0.05 to 0.4**, depending on specific speed. ### 3.3 Derivation of NPSHR Affinity Law From the pump affinity laws (assuming geometric similarity): $$\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1}, \quad \frac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2, \quad \frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3$$ Since pressure (and hence head) scales as $N^2$, the NPSHR — being a head quantity — must also scale as $N^2$: $$\boxed{\;\frac{\text{NPSHR}_2}{\text{NPSHR}_1} \;=\; \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2\;}$$ This is critical for variable-speed pump operations (e.g., VFD-driven pumps). ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Influence of Each Parameter on NPSHA | Parameter | Increase in Parameter | Effect on NPSHA | Engineering Implication | |---|---|---|---| | Atmospheric pressure $P_{atm}$ (altitude ↓) | Increase | NPSHA increases | Pump easier to operate at low altitudes | | Altitude (height above sea level ↑) | Increase | NPSHA decreases | High-altitude installations are critical | | Suction lift $H_s$ | Increase | NPSHA decreases | Avoid deep suction wells | | Friction loss $H_f$ | Increase | NPSHA decreases | Use larger diameter, smoother pipe | | Vapor pressure $P_{vp}$ (temperature ↑) | Increase | NPSHA decreases | Cold-water systems are safer | | Pipe diameter $D$ | Increase | NPSHA increases (via lower V and Hf) | Always preferred for long suction lines | | Liquid density (e.g., sea water vs fresh) | Slight increase | Negligible direct effect (ρg cancels) | Indirect: vapor pressure changes | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of NPSH Analysis The following Mermaid diagram maps the analytical flow for evaluating cavitation safety in a pumping system. Each block represents a computational or physical stage in the NPSHA-NPSHR comparison pipeline. ```mermaid graph TD nodeA[ATMOSPHERIC PRESSURE INPUT
Patm from barometer or altitude correction] nodeB[VAPOR PRESSURE INPUT
Pvp from steam tables at fluid temperature] nodeC[SUCTION GEOMETRY INPUT
Hs vertical distance and sign convention] nodeD[DISCHARGE AND PIPE DATA
Q flow rate D diameter L length] nodeE[VELOCITY COMPUTATION
V = 4Q over pi D squared] nodeF[FRICTION LOSS COMPUTATION
Hf = f L V squared over 2gD] nodeG[NPSHA FORMULA APPLICATION
NPSHA = Ha plus Hs minus Hf minus Hvp] nodeH[PUMP MANUFACTURER DATA
NPSHR from performance curve at duty point] nodeI[SPEED CORRECTION IF NEEDED
NPSHR scales with N squared via affinity law] nodeJ[COMPARISON AND MARGIN CHECK
NPSHA minus NPSHR versus safety threshold] nodeK{IS NPSHA GREATER THAN NPSHR PLUS MARGIN} nodeL[SAFE OPERATION
No cavitation Pump runs cleanly] nodeM[CAVITATION WARNING
Reduce Hs Reduce Hf Lower fluid temperature or increase suction size] nodeA --> nodeG nodeB --> nodeG nodeC --> nodeG nodeD --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeG nodeG --> nodeJ nodeH --> nodeI nodeI --> nodeJ nodeJ --> nodeK nodeK -->|YES| nodeL nodeK -->|NO| nodeM ``` ### 4.2 Physical System Schematic (ASCII Representation) The diagram below shows a typical pump suction installation with the four key head components labelled. This is the standard KTU textbook illustration used in pump cavitation problems. ``` P_atm (open to atmosphere) | v ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ | SUCTION RESERVOIR | | | (free surface) | | z1 ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ | | | <----- H_s (suction lift) -----> | | (vertical drop) | | | v | +--------+ +----------------+ | | SUCTION| L | FRICTION | | | PIPE |------->| LOSS Hf | | | D | | + V²/2g | | +--------+ +-------+--------+ | | | v | +---------------+ | | PUMP SUCTION| | z2 | FLANGE | | (datum) +---------------+ | | v +---------------+ | IMPELLER EYE | <-- NPSHA measured here | (V2, P2) | +---------------+ ``` ### 4.3 Cavitation Inception Threshold Visualization ``` ABSOLUTE PRESSURE [Pa] ^ | P_atm (atmospheric) | | | | P_atm - rho*g*Hs (pressure at suction flange, before losses) | | | | | | NPSHA measured here | | | | | | | | P_vp (vapor pressure threshold) | | | | | | | | | | DANGER ZONE (cavitation) | | | | | | |--+--+--+--+---+--------> PUMP INLET ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > The vertical distance between the suction flange pressure line and the $P_{vp}$ line **is** NPSHA. If this distance falls below the manufacturer's NPSHR, the operating point enters the danger zone. ### 4.4 Tabular Mapping — Head Components in NPSH Equation | Symbol | Head Component | Physical Origin | Direction | |---|---|---|---| | $H_a$ | Atmospheric pressure head | Air pressure on reservoir surface | Adds to NPSHA | | $H_s$ | Static suction lift | Elevation difference | Subtracts from NPSHA | | $H_f$ | Friction head loss | Pipe wall + fittings resistance | Subtracts from NPSHA | | $H_{vp}$ | Vapor pressure head | Liquid temperature property | Subtracts from NPSHA | ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` Define the following terms with their physical significance: (a) NPSHA (b) NPSHR (c) Cavitation **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **(a) Net Positive Suction Head Available (NPSHA):** It is the total head available at the suction port of the pump, measured above the vapor pressure head of the liquid. Mathematically, $$\text{NPSHA} \;=\; H_a + H_s - H_f - H_{vp}$$ It represents the **actual energy margin** the system provides to prevent cavitation. **(b) Net Positive Suction Head Required (NPSHR):** It is the minimum head required at the pump suction port by a particular pump design to operate without internal cavitation. It is a **pump-specific property** determined experimentally by the manufacturer and depends on impeller geometry, speed, and flow rate. **(c) Cavitation:** It is the phenomenon of formation and subsequent collapse of vapor bubbles inside a pump when the local static pressure at the impeller eye falls **at or below** the vapor pressure of the liquid. The collapse of these bubbles near metal surfaces causes pitting, vibration, noise, and loss of pump performance. --- **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` State and explain the condition to avoid cavitation in a centrifugal pump. What is the role of vapor pressure in this condition? **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Cavitation Avoidance Condition:** $$\boxed{\;\text{NPSHA} \;>\; \text{NPSHR}\;}$$ A safety margin of **0.5 m to 1.0 m** is generally recommended over and above this minimum requirement for reliable operation. **Role of Vapor Pressure:** The vapor pressure head ($H_{vp} = P_{vp}/\rho g$) appears as a **subtractive term** in the NPSHA formula. It represents the absolute lower limit below which the liquid will begin to vaporize. As the liquid temperature rises, vapor pressure rises sharply, thereby **reducing NPSHA** and increasing cavitation risk. This is why hot-water pumps and boiler feed pumps demand flooded suction arrangements and very low suction line losses. --- ### PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE) --- #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` A centrifugal pump handles water at a rate of **50 L/s** from a sump whose free surface is **4 m below** the pump centerline. The suction pipe has a diameter of **100 mm** and length of **8 m**. The Darcy friction factor is **0.025**. The water temperature is **30 °C**, for which the vapor pressure is **0.243 m of water**. Atmospheric pressure is equivalent to **10.3 m of water**. The pump manufacturer specifies a required NPSH of **3.5 m** at the duty point. **Find:** **(a)** Calculate the Net Positive Suction Head Available (NPSHA) at the pump suction. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Determine whether the pump will operate without cavitation. If not, suggest two remedies. **(7 Marks)** --- ##### **Solution to Question A:** **(a) Step-by-Step Calculation of NPSHA (7 Marks):** **Step 1 — Compute velocity in suction pipe:** $$V \;=\; \frac{Q}{A} \;=\; \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$$ $$V \;=\; \frac{4 \times 0.050}{\pi \times (0.1)^2} \;=\; \frac{0.200}{0.03142} \;=\; 6.366 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 2 — Compute velocity head:** $$\frac{V^2}{2g} \;=\; \frac{(6.366)^2}{2 \times 9.81} \;=\; \frac{40.53}{19.62} \;=\; 2.066 \text{ m}$$ **Step 3 — Compute friction head loss in suction pipe:** $$H_f \;=\; \frac{f L V^2}{2 g D} \;=\; \frac{0.025 \times 8 \times 40.53}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.1}$$ $$H_f \;=\; \frac{8.106}{1.962} \;=\; 4.132 \text{ m}$$ **Step 4 — Apply NPSHA formula (suction lift convention):** $$\text{NPSHA} \;=\; H_a + H_s - H_f - H_{vp}$$ $$\text{NPSHA} \;=\; 10.3 + 4.0 - 4.132 - 0.243$$ $$\boxed{\;\text{NPSHA} \;=\; 9.925 \text{ m}\;}$$ **(b) Cavitation Safety Check and Remedies (7 Marks):** **Step 5 — Compare NPSHA with NPSHR:** $$\text{NPSHA} = 9.925 \text{ m} \quad \text{vs.} \quad \text{NPSHR} = 3.5 \text{ m}$$ Since **NPSHA (9.925 m) > NPSHR (3.5 m)**, the pump **will operate without cavitation**. **Step 6 — Compute safety margin:** $$\text{Safety Margin} \;=\; \text{NPSHA} - \text{NPSHR} \;=\; 9.925 - 3.5 \;=\; 6.425 \text{ m}$$ This is an excellent margin (typically only 0.5 to 1.0 m is required). **Step 7 — If Margin Were Insufficient, Remedies Would Be:** | Remedy | Effect | |---|---| | Increase suction pipe diameter | Reduces velocity and friction loss $H_f$ | | Reduce suction lift (lower pump or raise sump) | Reduces $H_s$ in NPSHA equation | | Use flooded suction (sump above pump) | $H_s$ becomes negative, increasing NPSHA | | Reduce number of fittings/bends | Reduces minor losses in suction line | | Cool the liquid | Reduces $H_{vp}$, increasing NPSHA | | Reduce pump speed (via VFD) | NPSHR scales as $N^2$, decreases rapidly | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** > - **Sign of $H_s$:** Forgetting that $H_s$ is **positive** when suction reservoir is below the pump. Many students use $H_a - H_s - H_f - H_{vp}$ with $H_s$ subtracted even when it's a flooded suction, losing 1-2 marks. > - **Units of $Q$:** Always convert Q to **m³/s** before applying $V = Q/A$. Using Q in L/s directly is a common error. > - **Vapor pressure value:** Ensure you use $P_{vp}$ converted to **head of liquid**, not in Pa or bar. Always check units before substitution. > - **Missing safety margin comment:** Examiners frequently award 1 mark for stating "with adequate safety margin" — do not omit. --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` *(ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)* A centrifugal pump is to be installed at a location **1500 m above mean sea level**, where atmospheric pressure is equivalent to **8.6 m of water**. The pump draws water at **40 °C** (vapor pressure head = **0.174 m of water**) through a suction pipe of length **10 m** and diameter **75 mm** with friction factor **0.028**. The static suction lift is **3 m**. The discharge is **15 L/s**. The pump's required NPSH at the duty point is **4.0 m**. **Find:** **(a)** Determine the available NPSH (NPSHA) at the pump suction. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Check whether the pump is safe from cavitation. What is the maximum permissible suction lift if a safety margin of **1.0 m** is required? **(7 Marks)** --- ##### **Solution to Question B:** **(a) Calculation of NPSHA (7 Marks):** **Step 1 — Velocity in suction pipe:** $$V \;=\; \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2} \;=\; \frac{4 \times 0.015}{\pi \times (0.075)^2} \;=\; \frac{0.060}{0.01767} \;=\; 3.395 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 2 — Velocity head:** $$\frac{V^2}{2g} \;=\; \frac{(3.395)^2}{2 \times 9.81} \;=\; \frac{11.526}{19.62} \;=\; 0.5875 \text{ m}$$ **Step 3 — Friction head loss:** $$H_f \;=\; \frac{f L V^2}{2 g D} \;=\; \frac{0.028 \times 10 \times 11.526}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.075}$$ $$H_f \;=\; \frac{3.227}{1.4715} \;=\; 2.193 \text{ m}$$ **Step 4 — NPSHA using corrected atmospheric pressure:** $$\text{NPSHA} \;=\; H_a + H_s - H_f - H_{vp}$$ $$\text{NPSHA} \;=\; 8.6 + 3.0 - 2.193 - 0.174$$ $$\boxed{\;\text{NPSHA} \;=\; 9.233 \text{ m}\;}$$ **(b) Cavitation Check and Maximum Permissible Suction Lift (7 Marks):** **Step 5 — Cavitation safety check:** $$\text{NPSHA} = 9.233 \text{ m} \quad \text{vs.} \quad \text{NPSHR} = 4.0 \text{ m}$$ Since $\text{NPSHA} > \text{NPSHR}$, the pump operates **without cavitation**. **Step 6 — Required NPSHA for 1.0 m safety margin:** $$\text{NPSHA}_{\text{required}} \;=\; \text{NPSHR} + 1.0 \;=\; 4.0 + 1.0 \;=\; 5.0 \text{ m}$$ **Step 7 — Solve for maximum permissible suction lift $H_s$:** Rearranging the NPSHA equation: $$H_{s,\max} \;=\; \text{NPSHA}_{\text{required}} + H_f + H_{vp} - H_a$$ $$H_{s,\max} \;=\; 5.0 + 2.193 + 0.174 - 8.6$$ $$\boxed{\;H_{s,\max} \;=\; -1.233 \text{ m (i.e., flooded suction of 1.233 m above pump centerline is required)}\;}$$ The **negative sign** indicates the sump must be **above** the pump (flooded suction) by at least 1.233 m to maintain the 1.0 m safety margin at this high-altitude installation. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** > - **Atmospheric pressure correction:** Students often use $H_a = 10.3$ m blindly without accounting for altitude. At 1500 m elevation, $H_a$ is approximately **8.6 m** — a 1.7 m error that completely changes the conclusion. Always adjust $P_{atm}$ for site altitude. > - **Negative $H_s$ interpretation:** When the calculated permissible suction lift is negative, students often "ignore the sign" instead of correctly concluding flooded suction is mandatory. This is a **2-mark deduction** in valuation. > - **Sign of $H_s$ in formula:** With the convention used here, $H_s$ is always added; the geometry determines its sign (negative for flooded suction). --- ### KTU Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Things to Remember for NPSH (Module 4):** - ✅ **NPSHA** = $H_a + H_s - H_f - H_{vp}$ — system property (compute from geometry). - ✅ **NPSHR** = pump-specific property (read from manufacturer's curve at duty Q and N). - ✅ **Cavitation Rule:** $\text{NPSHA} > \text{NPSHR}$ with **0.5–1.0 m** safety margin. - ✅ **Atmospheric pressure $H_a$:** $\approx$ **10.3 m water** at sea level; **decreases with altitude** (use barometer or altitude-corrected tables). - ✅ **Vapor pressure $H_{vp}$:** **increases sharply with temperature** — hot fluids have much lower NPSHA. - ✅ **Friction loss $H_f$:** Use **Darcy formula** with correct units (Q in m³/s, D in m). - ✅ **Sign of $H_s$:** Positive for suction lift (reservoir below pump); negative for flooded suction (reservoir above pump). - ✅ **Thomas cavitation number:** $\sigma = \text{NPSHA}/H$; $\sigma_c = \text{NPSHR}/H$. Operate at $\sigma > \sigma_c$. - ✅ **Affinity Law:** $\text{NPSHR} \propto N^2$ — reducing pump speed drastically reduces NPSHR demand. - ✅ **Common remedies for low NPSHA:** Larger pipe diameter, flooded suction, fewer fittings, lower fluid temperature, lower pump speed. - ✅ **Velocity head $V^2/2g$** at the suction flange is **absorbed into NPSHR** by convention (manufacturer includes it). - ✅ **High-altitude installations** are the most common real-world cavitation case — always correct $P_{atm}$ for elevation. - ✅ **Cavitation damage:** Pitting, vibration, head drop, noise, eventual impeller destruction — never run a pump in cavitation. > [!NOTE] > **Board Exam Strategy (KTU 2024 Scheme):** > - Always write the formula first before substituting values. > - State units explicitly at every step. > - Conclude every numerical with a comparison statement: *"Therefore, NPSHA (X m) > NPSHR (Y m), hence the pump operates without cavitation."* > - Draw a clear labelled diagram wherever the question asks for NPSHA calculation.

topics.title: Multistage Pump
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT741 - FLUID MECHANICS & MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Multistage Pump

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Multistage Pump** is a centrifugal pump that incorporates two or more impellers mounted on a common shaft within a single casing to achieve a higher total head (pressure) or a larger discharge capacity than what a single-impeller pump can practically deliver. The impellers can be arranged either in **series** (to multiply the head) or in **parallel** (to multiply the flow rate), depending on the system demand.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**: A pump in which the total head developed is the sum of the heads developed by each impeller is termed a *multistage pump with impellers in series*. A pump in which the total discharge equals the sum of the discharges from individual impellers at the same head is termed a *multistage pump with impellers in parallel*.

Mathematically, for *n* identical impellers:

$$H_{\text{multistage, series}} = n \times H_{\text{single stage}}$$

$$Q_{\text{multistage, parallel}} = n \times Q_{\text{single stage}}$$

## 1.2 Need for Multistage Pumps

A single-stage centrifugal pump has practical limitations:

- **Head Limitation**: A single impeller cannot develop more than approximately **80–100 m** of head because of mechanical constraints on impeller tip speed.
- **Suction Limitations**: Beyond a certain head, **NPSH (Net Positive Suction Head)** problems occur at the suction eye.
- **Erosion & Efficiency Drop**: Very high peripheral speeds cause cavitation and erosion.

Therefore, when system requirements demand heads greater than this practical limit, **multiple impellers are hydraulically coupled** so that the output of one stage feeds the input of the next.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Application**: Multistage pumps are extensively used in **boiler feed systems**, **RO desalination plants**, **oil & gas pipelines**, **fire-fighting systems**, and **high-rise building water supply**.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine you are **lifting buckets of water** from a deep well onto a 10th-floor rooftop.

- **Single-Stage Analogy**: One person lifting water all the way in one go from the well to the rooftop. The person quickly tires — equivalent to a single-stage pump failing at high heads.
- **Multistage Analogy**: Instead, set up **3 platforms at intermediate floors**. Person 1 lifts water from the well to Platform 1. Person 2 lifts from Platform 1 to Platform 2. Person 3 lifts from Platform 2 to the rooftop. Each person does only 1/3 of the total work, but the **combined head** delivered to the rooftop is the **sum** of all three lifts. **This is precisely how impellers in series work in a multistage pump!**

Similarly, if you need **more water per minute at the same height**, you can have multiple pumps (impellers) working **side-by-side in parallel**, each delivering water to the same tank at the rooftop, but **adding up the flow**.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Multistage Pumps

| Configuration | Result Achieved | Typical Use Case |
|---------------|----------------|------------------|
| **Impellers in Series** | Heads add up | High-pressure boiler feed, high-rise pumping |
| **Impellers in Parallel** | Discharges add up | Large flow irrigation, cooling water circulation |
| **Mixed (Series-Parallel)** | Both head & flow multiplied | Large industrial plants (e.g., 2 stages in series × 2 in parallel) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU examinations, **Impellers in Series** is the most frequently tested configuration, especially the derivation of work done, head, and manometric efficiency.

---

## 1.5 Salient Design Features

1. **Common Shaft**: All impellers mounted on a single shaft rotate at the **same angular velocity N**.
2. **Diffuser/Volute Casing**: Inter-stage passages guide the flow between consecutive impellers.
3. **Return Guide Vanes**: In some designs, guide vanes redirect the flow radially before it enters the next impeller, **recovering kinetic energy** and improving efficiency.
4. **Balancing Drum/Disk**: Used to balance the **axial thrust** generated by the high-pressure stages.
5. **Sealing**: Inter-stage seals and throat bushings prevent leakage between stages.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Impellers in Series — Conceptual Breakdown

### Operational Logic (Step-by-Step Reasoning)

1. **Stage 1**: Liquid enters the suction pipe, passes through the **eye (inlet)** of impeller 1, and gains kinetic energy and pressure.
2. **Intermediate Casing**: The liquid leaving the **volute / diffuser of impeller 1** enters a connecting passage that channels it to the **eye of impeller 2**.
3. **Stage 2**: The second impeller imparts *additional* kinetic energy. The velocity head is partially converted into pressure head in the next diffuser.
4. **Final Discharge**: This cumulative energy addition continues through all *n* stages until the liquid is discharged at the delivery pipe with **total head H = n × H₁**.

> [!NOTE]
> The discharge **Q** through each impeller remains **identical** in a series configuration because the same mass flow passes through every stage (conservation of mass).

### Velocity Triangle Recap (for Each Stage)

At the inlet of any impeller stage (radius $r_1$, blade angle $\beta_1$):

$$V_{w1} = u_1 - V_{f1}\cot\beta_1$$

At the outlet (radius $r_2$, blade angle $\beta_2$):

$$V_{w2} = u_2 - V_{f2}\cot\beta_2$$

The work done per unit weight by **one stage** (Euler's equation for pumps) is:

$$W_{\text{stage}} = \frac{1}{g}\left(u_2 V_{w2} - u_1 V_{w1}\right)$$

## 2.2 Impellers in Parallel — Conceptual Breakdown

In a parallel multistage arrangement:

- All impellers draw liquid from a **common suction manifold**.
- All impellers discharge into a **common delivery manifold**.
- The total discharge is the **sum** of individual stage discharges.
- The total head developed equals the head developed by a **single impeller**.

$$Q_{\text{total}} = n \times Q_{\text{single}}, \quad H_{\text{total}} = H_{\text{single}}$$

This arrangement is **less common** in centrifugal pumps but finds application in **axial-flow pumps** for high-flow irrigation systems.

## 2.3 Symmetrical Impeller Assumption

For identical impellers (same diameter, same blade angles at inlet and outlet):

$$u_1 = u_2 = u = \frac{\pi D N}{60}, \quad \beta_1 = \beta_2 = \beta$$

Also, if flow is purely radial at inlet (no whirl), $V_{w1} = 0$, simplifying Euler's head per stage to:

$$H_{\text{stage}} = \frac{u V_{w2}}{g} = \frac{u^2}{g \tan\beta}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table — Master These for KTU ESE**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Peripheral velocity at radius *r* | $u = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ | m/s | D = impeller dia (m), N = rpm |
| 2 | Work done per stage per unit weight | $W_s = \dfrac{u_2 V_{w2} - u_1 V_{w1}}{g}$ | m | Euler's head equation |
| 3 | Head per stage (symmetrical, no whirl at inlet) | $H_s = \dfrac{u^2}{g \tan\beta}$ | m | $\beta$ = outlet blade angle |
| 4 | Total head (n stages in series) | $H = n \times H_s$ | m | Identical stages assumed |
| 5 | Discharge through series | $Q_s = Q$ | m³/s | Same flow through each stage |
| 6 | Total discharge (n stages in parallel) | $Q_p = n \times Q_s$ | m³/s | Each stage same head |
| 7 | Head in parallel configuration | $H_p = H_s$ | m | Identical to single stage |
| 8 | Power required by pump | $P = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{\eta_{\text{overall}}}$ | W | $\rho$ = 1000 kg/m³ for water |
| 9 | Manometric efficiency | $\eta_{\text{man}} = \dfrac{g H}{u_2 V_{w2} - u_1 V_{w1}}$ | — | Ratio of Euler head to manometric head |
| 10 | Minimum starting speed (series, $n$ stages) | $N_{\min} = \dfrac{60}{\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{n g H_{\text{man, single}}}{u^2/g\tan\beta}}$ | rpm | Head across all *n* stages |
| 11 | Shaft power | $P_{\text{shaft}} = T\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | W | T = torque (N·m) |
| 12 | Specific speed (dimensional) | $N_s = \dfrac{N\sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$ | — | For pump classification |
| 13 | Velocity of flow relation | $V_f = Q / (\pi D B)$ | m/s | B = impeller width |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Multistage pumps are critical in:

- **Thermal Power Plants**: Boiler feed pumps (BF pumps) operate at **300–600 m head** — impossible with a single impeller.
- **Petrochemical Refineries**: Multistage barrels in **API 610 (BB5)** design for high-pressure hydrocarbon transfer.
- **Reverse Osmosis (RO) Desalination**: High-pressure (70–80 bar) multistage pumps push seawater through membranes.
- **Hydroelectric Systems**: Multistage pump-turbines in **pumped storage** plants.
- **Fire-Fighting**: NFPA-compliant high-rise pumping systems use staged impellers to overcome elevation.

> [!NOTE]
> The maximum practical single-stage head is around **80 m of water column** for standard centrifugal designs. Beyond this, **multistage construction** is mandatory in practice.

---

## 2.6 Losses in Multistage Pumps

1. **Mechanical Losses**: Bearing friction, shaft seal friction (~2–5%).
2. **Volumetric Losses**: Recirculation from delivery back to suction through clearance gaps (~3–7%).
3. **Hydraulic Losses**: Friction in passages, sudden expansion/contractions, shock losses (~5–10%).
4. **Leakage Losses**: Inter-stage leakage degrades the cumulative head.

The overall efficiency is:

$$\eta_{\text{overall}} = \eta_{\text{man}} \times \eta_{\text{vol}} \times \eta_{\text{mech}}$$

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Head for *n* Identical Impellers in Series

### Given Data
- Number of identical impellers: *n*
- Discharge through each impeller: *Q*
- Peripheral velocity at outlet of each impeller: $u_2$
- Peripheral velocity at inlet of each impeller: $u_1$
- Velocity of whirl at outlet: $V_{w2}$
- Velocity of whirl at inlet: $V_{w1}$
- Outlet blade angle: $\beta_2$
- Inlet blade angle: $\beta_1$

### Step 1: Apply Euler's Turbomachine Equation to a Single Impeller

The energy transfer per unit weight by one impeller is:

$$W_1 = \frac{1}{g}\left(u_2 V_{w2} - u_1 V_{w1}\right)$$

This represents the **manometric head** developed by a single impeller in metres of liquid.

### Step 2: Assume Symmetry (Identical Impellers)

For identical stages mounted on a common shaft:

$$u_1^{(1)} = u_1^{(2)} = \cdots = u_1^{(n)} = u_1$$

$$u_2^{(1)} = u_2^{(2)} = \cdots = u_2^{(n)} = u_2$$

$$V_{w1}^{(i)} = V_{w1}, \quad V_{w2}^{(i)} = V_{w2} \quad \text{for all } i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$$

### Step 3: Work Done per Stage

Each impeller does identical work. So the work done by stage *i* is:

$$W_i = \frac{1}{g}\left(u_2 V_{w2} - u_1 V_{w1}\right)$$

### Step 4: Total Work Done by All *n* Stages

The total energy added to the fluid is the **sum** of the work done by each impeller, since the exit pressure of stage 1 becomes the inlet pressure of stage 2.

$$W_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i = n \times \frac{1}{g}\left(u_2 V_{w2} - u_1 V_{w1}\right)$$

### Step 5: Convert Work per Unit Weight to Manometric Head

By definition, work per unit weight equals head. Therefore, the **total manometric head** developed by the multistage pump is:

$$\boxed{H_{\text{man}} = \frac{n}{g}\left(u_2 V_{w2} - u_1 V_{w1}\right)}$$

### Step 6: Special Case — Symmetrical Impeller, No Inlet Whirl

If $V_{w1} = 0$ and $u_1 = u_2 = u$, and the flow at outlet satisfies $V_{w2} = u - V_{f2}\cot\beta_2$:

$$H_{\text{man}} = \frac{n u V_{w2}}{g} = \frac{n u^2}{g \tan\beta_2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The above expression is the **most tested form** in KTU previous year papers.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Minimum Starting Speed for Multistage Pump

The condition for the pump to **just start delivering water** against the static head is that the head developed must equal the **manometric head required**:

$$H_{\text{developed}} = H_{\text{required}}$$

### Step 1: Head Required for *n* Stages

Let $H_m$ be the head that a single stage must develop. Then:

$$n \times H_m = H_{\text{total required}}$$

### Step 2: Substituting Euler's Expression

$$\frac{n u^2}{g \tan\beta} = H_{\text{man, required}}$$

### Step 3: Solve for Peripheral Velocity *u*

$$u^2 = \frac{g H_{\text{man, required}} \tan\beta}{n}$$

$$u = \sqrt{\frac{g H_{\text{man, required}} \tan\beta}{n}}$$

### Step 4: Convert Peripheral Velocity to Rotational Speed

Using $u = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$:

$$\frac{\pi D N_{\min}}{60} = \sqrt{\frac{g H_{\text{man}} \tan\beta}{n}}$$

$$\boxed{N_{\min} = \frac{60}{\pi D}\sqrt{\frac{g H_{\text{man}} \tan\beta}{n}}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Power Required by Multistage Pump

### Step 1: Water Power (Output Power)

The useful power imparted to the fluid is:

$$P_{\text{water}} = \rho g Q H_{\text{man}}$$

### Step 2: Shaft Power (Input Power)

Accounting for overall efficiency $\eta_o$:

$$P_{\text{shaft}} = \frac{P_{\text{water}}}{\eta_o} = \frac{\rho g Q H_{\text{man}}}{\eta_o}$$

### Step 3: Expressing with Number of Stages

For *n* identical stages with single-stage head $H_s$:

$$P_{\text{shaft}} = \frac{\rho g Q \cdot n H_s}{\eta_o}$$

---

## 3.4 Solved Analytical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem (Model)**: A 3-stage centrifugal pump running at 1450 rpm delivers 0.05 m³/s of water against a total head of 90 m. The impeller diameter is 0.30 m, and the outlet blade angle is 30°. Assuming no whirl at inlet and symmetrical impellers, calculate: (a) The manometric efficiency, (b) The power input to the pump if overall efficiency is 70%.

### Solution

**Given:**
- $n_{\text{stages}} = 3$
- $N = 1450 \text{ rpm}$
- $Q = 0.05 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- $H_{\text{total}} = 90 \text{ m}$
- $D = 0.30 \text{ m}$
- $\beta_2 = 30°$
- $\eta_o = 0.70$

#### Step 1: Peripheral Velocity

$$u_2 = \frac{\pi D N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.30 \times 1450}{60}$$

$$u_2 = \frac{\pi \times 435}{60} = \frac{1366.19}{60} = 22.77 \text{ m/s}$$

#### Step 2: Head per Stage (Euler's Equation)

$$H_s = \frac{u_2^2}{g \tan\beta_2} = \frac{(22.77)^2}{9.81 \times \tan 30°}$$

$$H_s = \frac{518.47}{9.81 \times 0.5774} = \frac{518.47}{5.665} = 91.52 \text{ m}$$

#### Step 3: Total Theoretical Head (3 Stages)

$$H_{\text{Euler}} = n \times H_s = 3 \times 91.52 = 274.56 \text{ m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake**: Students often directly equate $H_{\text{Euler}}$ to the required head of 90 m. The correct approach is to compute **manometric efficiency** as the ratio of required head to theoretical head.

#### Step 4: Manometric Efficiency

$$\eta_{\text{man}} = \frac{H_{\text{required}}}{H_{\text{Euler}}} = \frac{90}{274.56} = 0.3278 = 32.78\%$$

#### Step 5: Shaft Power Input

$$P_{\text{shaft}} = \frac{\rho g Q H_{\text{required}}}{\eta_o} = \frac{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.05 \times 90}{0.70}$$

$$P_{\text{shaft}} = \frac{44145}{0.70} = 63064.3 \text{ W} \approx 63.06 \text{ kW}$$

### Final Answers

- **(a) Manometric efficiency $\eta_{\text{man}} = 32.78\%$**
- **(b) Shaft power $P_{\text{shaft}} = 63.06 \text{ kW}$**

---

## 3.5 Comparison Matrix: Series vs Parallel

| Parameter | Impellers in Series | Impellers in Parallel |
|-----------|---------------------|-----------------------|
| Total Head | $H = n H_s$ | $H = H_s$ |
| Total Discharge | $Q = Q_s$ | $Q = n Q_s$ |
| Pressure at Delivery | Very High | Same as single stage |
| Application | High-pressure systems | High-flow systems |
| Shaft Speed | Single common speed | Single common speed |
| Power | $P = \rho g Q n H_s$ | $P = \rho g n Q_s H_s$ |
| Typical Pump Type | Boiler feed, RO feed | Cooling water, irrigation |

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Suction Reservoir] --> B[Common Suction Pipe]
    B --> C[Stage 1 Impeller Inlet Eye]
    C --> D[Stage 1 Impeller Rotating at Speed N]
    D --> E[Stage 1 Diffuser Volute Casing]
    E --> F[Interstage Connecting Passage]
    F --> G[Stage 2 Impeller Inlet Eye]
    G --> H[Stage 2 Impeller Rotating at Speed N]
    H --> I[Stage 2 Diffuser Volute Casing]
    I --> J[Interstage Connecting Passage]
    J --> K[Stage N Impeller]
    K --> L[Final Volute Discharge Casing]
    L --> M[Delivery Pipe to System]
    M --> N[High Pressure High Head Output]
    
    O[Common Shaft Driven by Motor] -.powers.-> D
    O -.powers.-> H
    O -.powers.-> K
    
    P[Balancing Drum or Disk] -.reduces axial thrust.-> O
    Q[Interstage Seals] -.minimizes leakage.-> E
    Q -.minimizes leakage.-> I
    R[Return Guide Vanes] -.recovers kinetic energy.-> F
    R -.recovers kinetic energy.-> J
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Energy Transfer Stages

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Stage 1: Adds H1 metres] --> S2[Stage 2: Adds H2 metres]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3: Adds H3 metres]
    S3 --> S4[Stage N: Adds Hn metres]
    S4 --> T[Total Head H equals n times Hs]
    
    U1[Input Energy: Rotational KE] --> S1
    U2[Hydraulic Losses: 5 to 10 percent] -.deducts.-> S2
    U3[Mechanical Losses: 2 to 5 percent] -.deducts.-> S3
    U4[Volumetric Losses: 3 to 7 percent] -.deducts.-> S4
    
    V[Overall Efficiency eta o] --> T
```

## 4.3 Schematic Representation — Velocity Triangles (Symmetrical Impeller Stage)

For each stage in a multistage pump with symmetric blades ($\beta_1 = \beta_2 = \beta$) and no inlet whirl:

```
        Inlet Triangle              Outlet Triangle
                                    
   Vw1 = 0                      Vw2 = u - Vf2 cot β
   Vf1 = Vf                     Vf2 = Vf
                                    
       *V1                       *V2
       /|                        /|
      / |                       / |
     /  |Vf                    /  |Vf2
    /   |                     /   |
   *----*----> u1            *----*----> u2
   u1                          u2
   (no whirl)                  (whirl exists)
```

**Key Vector Relations:**

$$\vec{V_2} = \vec{u_2} + \vec{V_{r2}}$$

Where $V_{r2}$ is the relative velocity at outlet, and:

$$V_{w2} = u_2 - \frac{V_{f2}}{\tan\beta_2}$$

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** 
**Define a multistage pump. Under what circumstances are impellers connected in series?**

**Model Answer:**
A multistage pump is a centrifugal pump consisting of two or more impellers mounted on a common shaft within a single casing. When the head required by the system exceeds the practical head that a single impeller can develop (typically >80 m), impellers are connected in **series**. In this configuration, the delivery from one impeller feeds the suction of the next, so the **heads add up** while the discharge remains the same. This is essential in high-pressure applications such as boiler feed systems and reverse osmosis plants.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish clearly between impellers arranged in series and impellers arranged in parallel in a multistage centrifugal pump.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Impellers in Series | Impellers in Parallel |
|---------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| **Total Head** | $H = n H_s$ | $H = H_s$ |
| **Total Discharge** | $Q = Q_s$ | $Q = n Q_s$ |
| **Flow Path** | Sequential (one feeds next) | Simultaneous (common manifold) |
| **Application** | High head (boilers, RO) | High discharge (irrigation, cooling) |
| **Pressure at Outlet** | Very high | Same as single impeller |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern**, every Module carries either **two full 14-mark questions** OR **one 14-mark question with internal choice**. Below, **Question A** and **Question B** are presented as the two-choice options for Module 4 on Multistage Pumps.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive an expression for the work done per second by a multistage centrifugal pump having *n* identical impellers arranged in series. State clearly the assumptions made.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 4-stage centrifugal pump delivers 0.06 m³/s of water against a total head of 120 m. The diameter of each impeller is 0.40 m and the speed is 1500 rpm. Assuming the impellers are symmetrical with an outlet blade angle of 35° and no whirl at the inlet, calculate:
   (i) The head developed per stage
   (ii) The manometric efficiency of the pump
   (iii) The power input if the overall efficiency is 75%

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A**

##### Part (a) — Derivation

**Assumptions:**
1. All *n* impellers are **identical** (same diameter, blade angles, width).
2. All impellers rotate at the **same speed N** (common shaft).
3. The flow is **steady, incompressible, and axisymmetric**.
4. **No leakage** between stages (ideal case).
5. The discharge through each stage is the **same** (mass conservation).

**Derivation:**

For a single impeller, Euler's turbomachine equation gives work done per unit weight:

$$W_s = \frac{u_2 V_{w2} - u_1 V_{w1}}{g}$$

For identical impellers, the work done by each impeller is the same. Hence total work done by *n* impellers:

$$W_{\text{total}} = n \cdot W_s = \frac{n}{g}\left(u_2 V_{w2} - u_1 V_{w1}\right)$$

The total manometric head:

$$H_{\text{man}} = \frac{n}{g}\left(u_2 V_{w2} - u_1 V_{w1}\right)$$

If the impeller is symmetric ($u_1 = u_2 = u$) and $V_{w1} = 0$:

$$\boxed{H_{\text{man}} = \frac{n u V_{w2}}{g} = \frac{n u^2}{g \tan\beta_2}}$$

---

##### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:**
- $n_{\text{stages}} = 4$
- $Q = 0.06 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- $H_{\text{total}} = 120 \text{ m}$
- $D = 0.40 \text{ m}$
- $N = 1500 \text{ rpm}$
- $\beta_2 = 35°$
- $\eta_o = 0.75$

**Step 1: Peripheral Velocity**

$$u_2 = \frac{\pi D N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.40 \times 1500}{60} = 31.416 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Theoretical Head per Stage (Euler)**

$$H_s = \frac{u_2^2}{g \tan\beta_2} = \frac{(31.416)^2}{9.81 \times \tan 35°} = \frac{986.96}{9.81 \times 0.7002} = \frac{986.96}{6.869} = 143.68 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Total Theoretical Head for 4 Stages**

$$H_{\text{Euler, total}} = 4 \times 143.68 = 574.72 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Manometric Efficiency**

$$\eta_{\text{man}} = \frac{H_{\text{required}}}{H_{\text{Euler, total}}} = \frac{120}{574.72} = 0.2088 = 20.88\%$$

**Step 5: Shaft Power**

$$P_{\text{shaft}} = \frac{\rho g Q H_{\text{total}}}{\eta_o} = \frac{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.06 \times 120}{0.75} = \frac{70632}{0.75} = 94176 \text{ W} \approx 94.18 \text{ kW}$$

**Final Answers:**
- **(i)** Head developed per stage $H_s = 143.68$ m
- **(ii)** Manometric efficiency $\eta_{\text{man}} = 20.88\%$
- **(iii)** Power input $P_{\text{shaft}} \approx 94.18$ kW

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a multistage centrifugal pump with impellers in series. Discuss the function of return guide vanes and balancing drum.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Two identical centrifugal pumps are connected in series. Each pump develops a head of 25 m at a discharge of 0.04 m³/s. The combined pump delivers water to a tank at 60 m above the pump centerline through a 100 m long pipe of 75 mm diameter. The friction factor is 0.02. Determine:
   (i) Total head developed by the combined pump
   (ii) Total discharge delivered
   (iii) Power required at overall efficiency of 70%

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B**

##### Part (a) — Descriptive Answer

**Construction:** A multistage centrifugal pump consists of multiple impellers (typically 2 to 10) mounted on a **common shaft** housed within a single casing. The casing is divided into multiple compartments by **inter-stage partitions**. Each impeller has its own suction eye and is preceded by a diffuser or volute casing. A **balancing drum or disk** at the high-pressure end counteracts the net axial thrust. The shaft is supported by bearings and is driven by an external motor.

**Working:** Water enters at the suction pipe into the eye of the **first impeller**, gains kinetic energy and pressure, and is discharged into the volute. The diffuser converts kinetic energy to pressure. The high-pressure water flows through a return passage into the eye of the **second impeller**, where it gains additional head. This process repeats through all stages, and the cumulative head is delivered through the final discharge nozzle.

**Function of Return Guide Vanes:** These are stationary blades placed in the inter-stage passage. Their function is to **redirect the swirling flow** leaving the diffuser of one impeller into a purely radial flow entering the next impeller. This **recovers kinetic energy** that would otherwise be lost, improving overall efficiency by 4–8%.

**Function of Balancing Drum:** In multistage pumps, the high-pressure fluid exerts a **net axial thrust** pushing the shaft toward the suction side. A balancing drum is a small cylindrical drum attached to the shaft, exposed to high-pressure fluid on one side and low-pressure fluid on the other. The resulting pressure difference generates a thrust in the **opposite direction**, balancing the axial load and preventing bearing damage.

---

##### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:**
- 2 pumps in series, identical
- Each pump head $H_{\text{single}} = 25$ m
- Each pump discharge $Q_{\text{single}} = 0.04 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- Static head $H_s = 60$ m
- Pipe length $L = 100$ m
- Pipe diameter $d = 0.075$ m
- Friction factor $f = 0.02$
- Overall efficiency $\eta_o = 0.70$

**Step 1: Total Head Developed by Combined Pump**

For series configuration, heads add:

$$H_{\text{total, developed}} = 2 \times 25 = 50 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Friction Head Loss**

$$h_f = \frac{4 f L V^2}{2 g d}$$

First, compute velocity from discharge:

$$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.075)^2}{4} = 0.004418 \text{ m}^2$$

$$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.04}{0.004418} = 9.054 \text{ m/s}$$

Friction loss:

$$h_f = \frac{4 \times 0.02 \times 100 \times (9.054)^2}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.075} = \frac{8 \times 81.97}{1.4715} = \frac{655.76}{1.4715} = 445.6 \text{ m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **High friction loss detected!** This indicates the system requires a much higher head than the pump provides. In a real engineering scenario, the actual discharge would be less than 0.04 m³/s. However, in this **academic problem**, we proceed with the given $Q = 0.04$ m³/s.

**Step 3: Total Head Required by System**

$$H_{\text{required}} = H_s + h_f = 60 + 445.6 = 505.6 \text{ m}$$

But the pump provides only 50 m — this confirms the discharge will be lower in practice. The problem likely expects us to compute based on the given parameters directly.

**Step 4: Total Discharge Delivered (in series, discharge remains same)**

$$\boxed{Q_{\text{total}} = 0.04 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}}$$

**Step 5: Power Required**

$$P_{\text{shaft}} = \frac{\rho g Q H_{\text{developed}}}{\eta_o} = \frac{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.04 \times 50}{0.70}$$

$$P_{\text{shaft}} = \frac{19620}{0.70} = 28028.6 \text{ W} \approx 28.03 \text{ kW}$$

**Final Answers:**
- **(i)** Total head developed $H_{\text{total}} = 50$ m
- **(ii)** Total discharge $Q = 0.04$ m³/s (same as single pump)
- **(iii)** Power required $P_{\text{shaft}} \approx 28.03$ kW

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by *n* in series head** — Students often give the head of a *single* impeller as the answer instead of total multistage head. This alone causes a **3-mark deduction**.
> 
> 2. **Unit errors in peripheral velocity** — Using $N$ in rps instead of rpm, or $D$ in cm instead of m. Final answer will be off by 60× or 100×.
> 
> 3. **Confusing manometric head with Euler head** — Manometric head is the **actual useful head** delivered; Euler head is the **ideal theoretical head**. Always ratio them carefully for manometric efficiency.
> 
> 4. **Sign convention for blade angles** — In KTU problems, $\beta$ is the **blade angle measured from the tangent** (not from radial direction). The expression $\cot\beta$ becomes $\tan\beta$ when angle is measured from radial. **Read the problem statement carefully.**
> 
> 5. **Power formula oversight** — Using $P = \rho g Q H$ gives water power. The question asking for *shaft power* requires division by overall efficiency $\eta_o$.
> 
> 6. **Missing mass conservation principle** — In parallel, students sometimes mistakenly add heads instead of discharges.
> 
> 7. **Neglecting density** — For water, use $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³ unless otherwise specified. For oils, density is given explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Multistage Pumps**

- ✅ Multistage pump = 2 or more impellers on a **common shaft** within **one casing**.
- ✅ **Series** configuration → Heads add ($H = nH_s$), discharge unchanged.
- ✅ **Parallel** configuration → Discharges add ($Q = nQ_s$), head unchanged.
- ✅ Peripheral velocity: $u = \dfrac{\pi DN}{60}$ (always in SI units: m, rpm).
- ✅ Euler head per stage: $H_s = \dfrac{u_2 V_{w2} - u_1 V_{w1}}{g}$.
- ✅ For symmetric impeller + no inlet whirl: $H_s = \dfrac{u^2}{g \tan\beta_2}$.
- ✅ Total theoretical head for *n* stages: $H = \dfrac{n u^2}{g \tan\beta_2}$.
- ✅ Manometric efficiency: $\eta_{\text{man}} = \dfrac{H_{\text{actual}}}{H_{\text{theoretical}}}$.
- ✅ Shaft power: $P = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{\eta_o}$.
- ✅ Minimum starting speed: $N_{\min} \propto \sqrt{H/n}$ — more stages → lower starting speed.
- ✅ Return guide vanes **recover kinetic energy** between stages.
- ✅ Balancing drum **neutralizes axial thrust** in high-pressure multistage pumps.
- ✅ Standard density of water in KTU problems: $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$.
- ✅ Standard acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.
- ✅ Applications: Boiler feed, RO desalination, fire-fighting, oil pipelines, hydro-storage.
- ✅ Maximum practical single-stage head ≈ 80 m; beyond this, multistage is mandatory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Tip**: When solving any multistage pump numerical in KTU ESE, **always write down the given data first**, identify series/parallel configuration explicitly, draw the velocity triangle, then apply the Euler equation with the *n*-stage multiplier. This structured approach typically fetches **full marks** in valuation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pumps in Parallel and Series
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT741 - FLUID MECHANICS & MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pumps in Parallel and Series

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Pumps in Series

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Formal Definition (Series Operation)**
> Two or more pumps are said to be operating in **series** when they are connected such that the **discharge (flow rate) of the first pump is fed into the suction of the next pump**. In series operation, the **head delivered by each pump is additive**, while the **discharge through each pump remains the same** as the system discharge.

$$Q_{series} = Q_1 = Q_2 = Q_3 = \dots = Q_{system}$$

$$H_{series} = H_1 + H_2 + H_3 + \dots + H_n$$

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Bucket Brigade (Series):**
> Imagine a row of 3 people standing on a staircase, each one passing a bucket of water upward to the next person. The *same bucket* of water flows through everyone's hands, but the total vertical height the water reaches is the *sum* of what each person individually lifted it. That is exactly how pumps in series work — the **same fluid parcel is "boosted" in pressure/elevation multiple times**.

**Engineering use:** Used to overcome **high static heads** (e.g., pumping water up a tall building, multi-stage boiler feed pumps, high-rise water supply systems).

---

## 1.2 Pumps in Parallel

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Formal Definition (Parallel Operation)**
> Two or more pumps are said to be operating in **parallel** when they are connected such that the **suction and delivery sides of all pumps are connected to a common manifold**. Each pump draws from the same source and delivers to the same common delivery line. In parallel operation, the **discharge is additive**, while the **head developed by each pump is the same** and equal to the system head.

$$H_{parallel} = H_1 = H_2 = H_3 = \dots = H_{system}$$

$$Q_{parallel} = Q_1 + Q_2 + Q_3 + \dots + Q_n$$

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — Multiple Taps on One Pipe (Parallel):**
> Imagine 3 fire hoses connected to the same hydrant and all spraying water into the same pool. Each hose pushes water against the *same back-pressure* (the pool's surface level), but the *total volume of water* delivered to the pool is the sum of what each hose contributes. The pressure (head) at the manifold is the same; the flow adds up. This is pumps in parallel.

**Engineering use:** Used to deliver **large discharges at moderate heads** (e.g., city water distribution networks, large irrigation systems, fire-fighting installations).

---

## 1.3 Quick Comparison Snapshot

| Parameter | Series | Parallel |
|---|---|---|
| **Discharge (Q)** | Same through each pump | Additive (sums up) |
| **Head (H)** | Additive (sums up) | Same across each pump |
| **Power (P)** | Additive | Additive |
| **Primary Purpose** | Increase **Head** | Increase **Discharge** |
| **System curve intersection** | At same Q, on summed H | At same H, on summed Q |
| **Typical Application** | High-rise pumping, boiler feed | Water supply, irrigation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> For the 2024 scheme, students are expected to (i) construct the *combined characteristic curve* for both series and parallel configurations, and (ii) determine the *operating point* of the system using the system resistance curve. Problems on this topic frequently appear in **Part B (14 marks)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Construction of Combined Characteristic Curves

### 2.1.1 Pumps in Series — Construction Logic

For **n identical pumps** connected in series, the operating logic is:

**Step 1:** Identify a common discharge value $Q$ (the x-coordinate).
**Step 2:** Read the head delivered by a single pump at this discharge from its H-Q curve: $H_{pump}(Q)$.
**Step 3:** Multiply by the number of pumps: $H_{combined}(Q) = n \cdot H_{pump}(Q)$.
**Step 4:** Plot the point $(Q,\; n \cdot H_{pump})$ for many such $Q$ values to get the combined curve.

> [!NOTE]
> For **non-identical** pumps in series, the construction is more subtle: at any common discharge $Q$, the **head of each individual pump** at that $Q$ is read, and these heads are **algebraically added**. The combined H-Q curve is the *vertical sum* of the individual H-Q curves.

$$H_{series}(Q) = H_1(Q) + H_2(Q) + \dots + H_n(Q)$$

### 2.1.2 Pumps in Parallel — Construction Logic

For **n identical pumps** in parallel, the operating logic is:

**Step 1:** Identify a common head value $H$ (the y-coordinate).
**Step 2:** Read the discharge delivered by a single pump at this head: $Q_{pump}(H)$.
**Step 3:** Multiply by the number of pumps: $Q_{combined}(H) = n \cdot Q_{pump}(H)$.
**Step 4:** Plot $(n \cdot Q_{pump}(H),\; H)$ for many $H$ values to get the combined curve.

For **non-identical** pumps in parallel:

$$Q_{parallel}(H) = Q_1(H) + Q_2(H) + \dots + Q_n(H)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Rule of Combination:**
> - **Series** ⇒ Heads add at **constant Q** ⇒ **Vertical addition** of H-Q curves.
> - **Parallel** ⇒ Discharges add at **constant H** ⇒ **Horizontal addition** of H-Q curves.

---

## 2.2 Operating Point of the System

The system curve (also called the system resistance curve) is governed by the Bernoulli equation applied across the suction and delivery reservoirs. For a simple pumping system with static head $H_{static}$ and a single pipe of length $L$, diameter $D$, friction factor $f$:

$$H_{system}(Q) = H_{static} + \frac{8 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g D^5}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **system curve is parabolic** in $Q$ (since $H \propto Q^2$ for frictional losses), and it **always passes through the point $(0, H_{static})$** — meaning that even at zero flow, the pump must overcome the static lift.

**Operating Point:** The intersection of the **pump characteristic curve** and the **system resistance curve**.

- For a single pump: intersection of $H_{pump}(Q)$ and $H_{system}(Q)$.
- For pumps in series: intersection of the *combined* H-Q curve and $H_{system}(Q)$.
- For pumps in parallel: intersection of the *combined* H-Q curve and $H_{system}(Q)$.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas for KTU 2024 exams.** Note: vertical bars use `\vert` for safe LaTeX rendering in tables.

| # | Configuration / Concept | Governing Equation | Variable Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pumps in Series — Head | $H_s = \sum_{i=1}^{n} H_i$ | Common $Q$ for all pumps |
| 2 | Pumps in Series — Discharge | $Q_s = Q_1 = Q_2 = \dots$ | $Q_i$ is the system flow |
| 3 | Pumps in Series — n identical | $H_s = n \cdot H_{single}$ | At any $Q$ |
| 4 | Pumps in Parallel — Discharge | $Q_p = \sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i$ | Common $H$ for all pumps |
| 5 | Pumps in Parallel — Head | $H_p = H_1 = H_2 = \dots$ | Each pump sees the same $H$ |
| 6 | Pumps in Parallel — n identical | $Q_p = n \cdot Q_{single}$ | At any $H$ |
| 7 | Combined Power (Series or Parallel) | $P_{combined} = \sum P_i$ | $P_i = \rho g Q_i H_i$ |
| 8 | System Curve | $H_{sys} = H_{static} + K Q^2$ | $K = \frac{8 f L}{\pi^2 g D^5}$ |
| 9 | Operating Point | $H_{pump}(Q) = H_{sys}(Q)$ | Solve simultaneously |
| 10 | Affinity Law (Speed change) | $\frac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \frac{N_1}{N_2}$, $\frac{H_1}{H_2} = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^2$ | Used when speeds vary |
| 11 | Pump Efficiency | $\eta = \frac{\rho g Q H}{P_{shaft}}$ | $P_{shaft}$ in **W** |

### Real-World Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in industry:**
> - **Boiler Feed Pumps (BFP)** in thermal power stations often use **2 or 3 pumps in series** to reach the very high pressure (~200 bar) required.
> - **Municipal water supply** to large cities uses **multiple pumps in parallel** to meet peak hour demand without raising pressure.
> - **Fire-fighting systems** keep a primary pump in standby parallel with the duty pump for redundancy.

---

## 2.4 Selection Between Series and Parallel

A critical KTU concept is: **when to use which configuration?**

Consider a single pump delivering $(Q_0, H_0)$ against a system that requires $(Q_{req}, H_{req})$.

- If $H_{req} > H_0$ **at the required $Q_{req}$** → use **Series** to boost head.
- If $Q_{req} > Q_0$ **at the required $H_{req}$** → use **Parallel** to boost discharge.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:**
> Students often use parallel when they need to overcome a *higher static head*. This is **incorrect** — parallel pumps do **not** increase the head a single pump can deliver; they only multiply the discharge at that head.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: System Resistance Curve (Parabolic Head vs Discharge)

Consider a pump delivering water from a **lower reservoir (1)** to an **upper reservoir (2)**, both at atmospheric pressure, through a single pipe of length $L$ and diameter $D$.

**Step 1:** Apply the energy equation between the free surface of the suction reservoir (point 1) and the free surface of the delivery reservoir (point 2):

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 + H_{pump} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$$

**Step 2:** Apply boundary conditions: Both surfaces are open to atmosphere, so $P_1 = P_2 = P_{atm}$. Both surfaces are large, so $V_1 \approx V_2 \approx 0$. The elevation difference is $z_2 - z_1 = H_{static}$:

$$0 + 0 + z_1 + H_{pump} = 0 + 0 + z_2 + h_L$$

$$H_{pump} = (z_2 - z_1) + h_L = H_{static} + h_L$$

**Step 3:** The head loss $h_L$ consists of friction loss in the pipe and minor losses. Considering the dominant friction loss using the Darcy-Weisbach equation:

$$h_f = \frac{f L V^2}{2 g D} = \frac{f L}{2 g D} \cdot \left(\frac{Q}{A}\right)^2 = \frac{f L}{2 g D} \cdot \frac{Q^2}{A^2}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the pipe cross-sectional area $A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$, so $A^2 = \frac{\pi^2 D^4}{16}$:

$$h_f = \frac{f L}{2 g D} \cdot \frac{16 Q^2}{\pi^2 D^4} = \frac{8 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g D^5}$$

**Step 5:** Combining all contributions (with $K_{minor}$ lumped for fittings/valves):

$$H_{system}(Q) = H_{static} + \frac{8 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g D^5} + K_{minor} \cdot \frac{Q^2}{2 g A^2}$$

**Step 6:** Lumping all quadratic terms into a single constant $K$:

$$\boxed{H_{system}(Q) = H_{static} + K \cdot Q^2}$$

This is the **parabolic system curve** that the pump must overcome.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Combined Curves for n Identical Pumps

Let a single pump have the quadratic H-Q characteristic:

$$H_{pump}(Q) = H_{shut} - C \cdot Q^2$$

where $H_{shut}$ is the **shut-off head** (head at $Q = 0$) and $C$ is a constant depending on pump geometry.

### 3.2.1 n Identical Pumps in Series

In series, **all pumps pass the same discharge Q**, but **heads add**:

$$H_{series}(Q) = n \cdot H_{pump}(Q) = n \cdot (H_{shut} - C \cdot Q^2)$$

$$H_{series}(Q) = n \cdot H_{shut} - n \cdot C \cdot Q^2$$

**Result:** The combined series curve has a **shut-off head of $n \cdot H_{shut}$** but the **same operating discharge** as a single pump at the same head.

### 3.2.2 n Identical Pumps in Parallel

In parallel, **all pumps develop the same head H**, but **discharges add**. To construct the curve, we invert the single-pump relation:

$$Q_{pump}(H) = \sqrt{\frac{H_{shut} - H}{C}}$$

Total discharge for n parallel pumps at the same head $H$:

$$Q_{parallel}(H) = n \cdot \sqrt{\frac{H_{shut} - H}{C}}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{parallel}(H) = n \cdot \sqrt{\frac{H_{shut} - H}{C}}}$$

**Result:** The combined parallel curve has the **same shut-off head $H_{shut}$** but the **shut-off discharge** is $n \cdot Q_{max,single}$ where $Q_{max,single} = \sqrt{H_{shut}/C}$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example (Model for KTU 14-mark problems):**

**Problem:** Two identical centrifugal pumps each have the characteristic: $H = 30 - 0.4 Q^2$ (with H in m and Q in L/s). The system curve is $H_{sys} = 10 + 0.1 Q^2$. Determine the operating point for:
**(a)** A single pump
**(b)** Two pumps in series
**(c)** Two pumps in parallel

---

### (a) Single Pump

Set pump head = system head:

$$30 - 0.4 Q^2 = 10 + 0.1 Q^2$$

$$30 - 10 = 0.1 Q^2 + 0.4 Q^2$$

$$20 = 0.5 Q^2$$

$$Q^2 = 40 \implies Q_{single} = \sqrt{40} = 6.32 \text{ L/s}$$

$$H_{single} = 30 - 0.4 (40) = 30 - 16 = 14 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{(Q, H)_{single} = (6.32 \text{ L/s},\; 14 \text{ m})}$$

---

### (b) Two Pumps in Series

Combined head: $H_{series} = 2(30 - 0.4 Q^2) = 60 - 0.8 Q^2$

Set equal to system:

$$60 - 0.8 Q^2 = 10 + 0.1 Q^2$$

$$50 = 0.9 Q^2$$

$$Q^2 = 55.56 \implies Q_{series} = 7.45 \text{ L/s}$$

$$H_{series} = 60 - 0.8(55.56) = 60 - 44.44 = 15.55 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{(Q, H)_{series} = (7.45 \text{ L/s},\; 15.55 \text{ m})}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Series operation **increases both head and discharge** for this system. The pump curve steepens, so the operating point moves up and slightly to the right.

---

### (c) Two Pumps in Parallel

For parallel, we need the discharge as a function of head for a single pump:

$$Q_{single}(H) = \sqrt{\frac{30 - H}{0.4}}$$

For two in parallel:

$$Q_{parallel}(H) = 2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{30 - H}{0.4}} = \sqrt{\frac{4(30 - H)}{0.4}} = \sqrt{10(30 - H)} = \sqrt{300 - 10 H}$$

Equivalently, in the form $H(Q)$: from $Q^2 = 300 - 10 H$, we get $H = 30 - 0.1 Q^2$.

Set equal to system curve:

$$30 - 0.1 Q^2 = 10 + 0.1 Q^2$$

$$20 = 0.2 Q^2$$

$$Q^2 = 100 \implies Q_{parallel} = 10 \text{ L/s}$$

$$H_{parallel} = 30 - 0.1(100) = 30 - 10 = 20 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{(Q, H)_{parallel} = (10 \text{ L/s},\; 20 \text{ m})}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight from this example:**
> - Series nearly doubled head (14 → 15.55 m) and modestly increased Q (6.32 → 7.45 L/s).
> - Parallel increased discharge by 58% (6.32 → 10 L/s) and increased head (14 → 20 m).
> - Parallel was **more effective** for this system because the system curve is moderately steep (a system with high static lift would favor series).

---

## 3.4 Comparison Summary Table

| Configuration | Q (L/s) | H (m) | Increase in Q | Increase in H |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Single Pump | 6.32 | 14.00 | — | — |
| 2 in Series | 7.45 | 15.55 | +18% | +11% |
| 2 in Parallel | 10.00 | 20.00 | +58% | +43% |

---

## 3.5 Analytical Derivation: Conditions for Beneficial Use

It can be proven (and is often asked in KTU 14-mark questions) that:

- **Series** is beneficial when the system has a **high static head** ($H_{static}$ is large). Adding pumps in series is the only way to overcome a tall lift.
- **Parallel** is beneficial when the system curve is **steep** (large friction losses) or when the system has a **low static head but high flow demand**.

Mathematically, two pumps in parallel will produce a *higher operating discharge* than two pumps in series if and only if:

$$H_{shut} > 2 \cdot H_{static}$$

(For the parabolic case with identical pumps and $K_{sys} = C$ for simplicity.)

> [!NOTE]
> Students are not expected to memorize this inequality, but they are expected to understand its meaning: parallel helps when there's "head room" — i.e., the single pump can produce more head than the static lift demands.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Combined Performance and System Curves — Block-Level Functional Architecture

Below is a detailed Mermaid flowchart that maps the **functional architecture** of how combined pump curves interact with the system curve, since the physical H-Q diagram is not directly renderable in Mermaid but can be functionally described.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[KTU Module 4 Pumps in Parallel and Series] --> B[Identify Configuration]
```
