# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Modeled on KTU 2024 End Semester Examination (ESE) — Module 4, Part A pattern.

---

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define the terms: (i) Slip of a reciprocating pump, (ii) Coefficient of discharge, and (iii) Negative slip.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Slip of a Reciprocating Pump:**
Slip is defined as the difference between the theoretical discharge (calculated from piston geometry and speed) and the actual discharge measured at the outlet. It is caused by leakage of liquid past the valves, piston rings, and through clearances.

$$S = Q_{\text{th}} - Q_{\text{act}} \quad [\text{m}^3/\text{s}]$$

**(ii) Coefficient of Discharge ($C_d$):**
It is the ratio of actual discharge to theoretical discharge, and it measures the volumetric efficiency of the pump.

$$C_d = \dfrac{Q_{\text{act}}}{Q_{\text{th}}}$$

**(iii) Negative Slip:**
At very high pump speeds, the inertia of the water column in the delivery pipe delays valve closure. The delivery valve may remain open during part of the suction stroke, causing the actual discharge to **exceed** the theoretical discharge. This gives a "negative" value for slip, hence the name.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Explain the function of an air vessel in a reciprocating pump. How does it save power?**

**Model Answer:**

An **air vessel** is a closed chamber partially filled with air at atmospheric pressure (or slightly above), installed on the delivery and/or suction pipe close to the pump cylinder.

**Functions:**
1. **Absorbs pressure surges:** During the delivery stroke, liquid enters the air vessel and compresses the trapped air cushion. During the suction stroke, the air cushion expands and pushes liquid back into the pipe, ensuring nearly continuous flow.
2. **Reduces acceleration head:** Since the water column in the long delivery pipe does not have to accelerate and decelerate every stroke, the acceleration head is drastically reduced.
3. **Ensures uniform discharge:** Flow in the pipe becomes nearly steady.
4. **Reduces water hammer:** Protects the piping system from pressure shock.

**Power saving mechanism:**
The work done per second by the pump without an air vessel is:

$$W_{\text{without}} = \rho g \, Q \, \left[H_s + \dfrac{h_{a,\max}}{2}\right]$$

With an air vessel:

$$W_{\text{with}} = \rho g \, Q \, H_s$$

Since the acceleration head term $\tfrac{h_{a,\max}}{2}$ is eliminated, significant power is saved (typically 15%–40% depending on the system).

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each Part B question carries **internal choice** — answering either Question A OR Question B is compulsory. The structure is typically 2 sub-parts worth 7 marks each.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Modified]`

A single-acting reciprocating pump has the following specifications:

- Cylinder diameter $D = 200$ mm
- Stroke length $L = 400$ mm
- Delivery pipe diameter $d = 100$ mm
- Length of delivery pipe $L_p = 80$ m
- Static head $H_s = 25$ m
- Pump speed $N = 60$ rpm
- Actual discharge $Q_{\text{act}} = 5.5$ L/s
- Density of water $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³
- Take $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
Calculate the theoretical discharge, slip, percentage slip, and coefficient of discharge of the pump.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
Calculate (i) the maximum acceleration head in the delivery pipe, (ii) the work done per second by the pump without an air vessel, and (iii) the work done per second with a perfect air vessel. Also find the power saved.

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Slip Calculations

**Step 1: Cylinder area**

$$
A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.200)^2 = 0.03142 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Theoretical discharge**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{th}} &= \dfrac{A L N}{60} = \dfrac{0.03142 \times 0.40 \times 60}{60} \\
&= 0.03142 \times 0.40 = 0.01257 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \\
&= 12.57 \text{ L/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Slip**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= Q_{\text{th}} - Q_{\text{act}} = 12.57 - 5.5 = 7.07 \text{ L/s} \\
S &= 7.07 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Percentage slip**

$$
\%S = \dfrac{S}{Q_{\text{th}}} \times 100 = \dfrac{7.07}{12.57} \times 100 = 56.25\%
$$

**Step 5: Coefficient of discharge**

$$
C_d = \dfrac{Q_{\text{act}}}{Q_{\text{th}}} = \dfrac{5.5}{12.57} = 0.4375
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often incorrectly write slip as $Q_{\text{act}} - Q_{\text{th}}$. Remember, **slip is always $Q_{\text{th}} - Q_{\text{act}}$** in the conventional (positive slip) case. Also, do NOT forget to convert discharge to consistent units (L/s ↔ m³/s).

---

#### Part (b) — Work Done and Power Saved

**Step 1: Angular velocity and crank radius**

$$
\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 60}{60} = 2\pi \text{ rad/s} = 6.283 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
r = \dfrac{L}{2} = \dfrac{0.40}{2} = 0.20 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2: Maximum acceleration head**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{a,\max} &= \dfrac{L_p \, \omega^2 \, r}{g} \\
&= \dfrac{80 \times (6.283)^2 \times 0.20}{9.81} \\
&= \dfrac{80 \times 39.478 \times 0.20}{9.81} \\
&= \dfrac{631.65}{9.81} = 64.39 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Work done per second without air vessel**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_1 &= \rho g \, Q_{\text{th}} \left[H_s + \dfrac{h_{a,\max}}{2}\right] \\
&= 9810 \times 0.01257 \times \left[25 + \dfrac{64.39}{2}\right] \\
&= 9810 \times 0.01257 \times [25 + 32.195] \\
&= 123.31 \times 57.195 = 7052.7 \text{ W} \\
&\approx 7.05 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Work done per second with air vessel**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_2 &= \rho g \, Q_{\text{act}} \cdot H_s \\
&= 9810 \times 0.0055 \times 25 \\
&= 1348.9 \text{ W} \approx 1.35 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Power saved**

$$
\Delta P = W_1 - W_2 = 7.05 - 1.35 = 5.70 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
\% \text{ Saving} = \dfrac{5.70}{7.05} \times 100 = 80.85\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** A common error is using $Q_{\text{act}}$ instead of $Q_{\text{th}}$ in the work-done formula for the "without air vessel" case. **Without an air vessel**, the theoretical volume is being displaced, so $Q_{\text{th}}$ must be used. **With an air vessel**, the actual volume reaching the tank is $Q_{\text{act}}$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024 - Modified]`

A double-acting reciprocating pump running at 50 rpm has the following data:

- Cylinder diameter $D = 250$ mm
- Piston rod diameter $d_r = 50$ mm
- Stroke length $L = 500$ mm
- Suction and delivery pipe lengths $= 60$ m each
- Suction and delivery pipe diameters $= 120$ mm
- Static suction head $H_s = 4$ m
- Static delivery head $H_d = 30$ m
- Total static head $H_s = 34$ m
- Slip $= 3\%$ of theoretical discharge

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
Determine (i) the theoretical discharge, (ii) the actual discharge, and (iii) the coefficient of discharge.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
Calculate (i) the maximum acceleration head in the suction pipe, (ii) the maximum acceleration head in the delivery pipe, and (iii) the work done per second (assuming mean acceleration head $= h_{a,\max}/2$).

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Discharge Calculations

**Step 1: Cylinder area and rod area**

$$
A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.250)^2 = 0.04909 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
a = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.050)^2 = 0.001963 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Theoretical discharge**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{th}} &= \dfrac{(2A - a) L N}{60} \\
&= \dfrac{(2 \times 0.04909 - 0.001963) \times 0.500 \times 50}{60} \\
&= \dfrac{(0.09818 - 0.001963) \times 25}{60} \\
&= \dfrac{0.09622 \times 25}{60} = \dfrac{2.4055}{60} \\
&= 0.04009 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 40.09 \text{ L/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Actual discharge**

$$
S = 0.03 \times Q_{\text{th}} = 0.03 \times 40.09 = 1.203 \text{ L/s}
$$

$$
Q_{\text{act}} = Q_{\text{th}} - S = 40.09 - 1.203 = 38.89 \text{ L/s} = 0.03889 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 4: Coefficient of discharge**

$$
C_d = \dfrac{Q_{\text{act}}}{Q_{\text{th}}} = \dfrac{38.89}{40.09} = 0.970
$$

---

#### Part (b) — Acceleration Head and Work Done

**Step 1: Angular velocity and crank radius**

$$
\omega = \dfrac{2\pi \times 50}{60} = 5.236 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
r = \dfrac{L}{2} = \dfrac{0.500}{2} = 0.250 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2: Maximum acceleration head — Delivery Pipe**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{a,\text{del},\max} &= \dfrac{L_{p,\text{del}} \, \omega^2 \, r}{g} \\
&= \dfrac{60 \times (5.236)^2 \times 0.250}{9.81} \\
&= \dfrac{60 \times 27.42 \times 0.250}{9.81} = \dfrac{411.3}{9.81} = 41.93 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Maximum acceleration head — Suction Pipe**

(Same formula, same pipe length — identical value for identical geometry)

$$
h_{a,\text{suc},\max} = \dfrac{60 \times 27.42 \times 0.250}{9.81} = 41.93 \text{ m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Although the magnitudes are the same, the **sign convention differs**. During the suction stroke, the acceleration head **reduces** the effective suction head (causing possible cavitation). During the delivery stroke, it **adds** to the static head.

**Step 4: Work done per second**

Mean acceleration head:

$$
\overline{h_a} = \dfrac{h_{a,\max}}{2} = \dfrac{41.93}{2} = 20.97 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \rho g \, Q_{\text{th}} \left[H_s + \overline{h_a}\right] \\
&= 9810 \times 0.04009 \times [34 + 20.97] \\
&= 393.3 \times 54.97 = 21618 \text{ W} \approx 21.62 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** For double-acting pumps, students often forget to subtract the rod area $a$, leading to an over-estimation of discharge by about 2-4%. Always check whether the rod area is given and incorporate it correctly as $(2A - a)$.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: Types of Reciprocating Pumps**

- [ ] **Single-acting** delivers once per revolution; **double-acting** delivers twice.
- [ ] $Q_{\text{th, single}} = ALN/60$; $Q_{\text{th, double}} = (2A - a)LN/60$.
- [ ] **Slip** $= Q_{\text{th}} - Q_{\text{act}}$; always positive for normal operation.
- [ ] **Negative slip** occurs at very high speeds due to valve lag.
- [ ] **% Slip** $= S/Q_{\text{th}} \times 100$.
- [ ] **$C_d$** (Coefficient of discharge) $= Q_{\text{act}}/Q_{\text{th}}$.
- [ ] Maximum acceleration head: $h_{a,\max} = L_p \omega^2 r / g$, occurs at $\theta = 0$.
- [ ] Crank radius $r = L/2$ for crank-and-connecting-rod mechanism.
- [ ] **Air vessel** at delivery pipe → reduces acceleration head, smooths flow, saves power.
- [ ] **With air vessel** (ideal): $W = \rho g Q_{\text{act}} H_s$ (acceleration head negligible).
- [ ] **Without air vessel**: $W = \rho g Q_{\text{th}} [H_s + h_{a,\max}/2]$.
- [ ] Work-done formula uses $Q_{\text{th}}$ (without air vessel) and $Q_{\text{act}}$ (with air vessel).
- [ ] Power savings by air vessels can range from **15% to over 80%** in extreme cases.
- [ ] **Friction head** $h_f$ adds to static head if included in the question.
- [ ] Always convert pipe and cylinder areas to consistent SI units (m²).
- [ ] For double-acting pumps, the rod area $a$ on the rod side reduces the effective swept area to $(A - a)$ on that side.
- [ ] **Cavitation risk** is increased by acceleration head in the suction pipe; air vessels on the suction side are equally important.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Mistakes to Avoid:**
> 1. Using $Q_{\text{act}}$ in the work-done formula when no air vessel is present.
> 2. Forgetting to convert L/s to m³/s.
> 3. Mixing up $r$ with $L$ in the acceleration head formula (always $r = L/2$).
> 4. Computing the **average** of $h_a$ over a full cycle as zero, then concluding no acceleration effect — use the **mean of the magnitude** during the active stroke.
> 5. Treating the rod area as negligible in double-acting problems where it is explicitly given.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Indicator Diagram - Work Done
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT741 - FLUID MECHANICS & MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Indicator Diagram - Work Done

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Indicator Diagram (KTU Definition):**
> An **Indicator Diagram** of a reciprocating pump is a graphical plot of the **pressure (or head) developed inside the pump cylinder** versus the **stroke length (or volume displaced)** during one complete working cycle. It is recorded using a mechanical device called a **Crosby Indicator** (or a modern pressure transducer–DAQ system) fitted to the cylinder head.

**Key features of an indicator diagram:**

| Feature | Description |
|---|---|
| **Y-axis** | Pressure (P) inside the cylinder, in kN/m² or N/mm² |
| **X-axis** | Stroke length (L) or Volume (V = A × L) |
| **Origin** | Represents atmospheric pressure (zero gauge) |
| **Loop area** | Work done per cycle = ∮ P dV (in Joules) |
| **Above origin** | Delivery stroke (positive gauge pressure) |
| **Below origin** | Suction stroke (negative gauge pressure / vacuum) |

**Work done = Area enclosed by the indicator diagram** (per cycle).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Picture — "The Bicycle Pump Story":**
> Imagine pushing down the handle of a **bicycle pump** with a balloon fixed at its nozzle. The harder you push, the more the balloon inflates. If you could **plot the pressure you apply on the Y-axis** and the **distance the handle moves on the X-axis**, the area under that curve equals the **work you did on the air**.
>
> A pump's indicator diagram works on the **exact same principle**, but it is a **closed loop** because the piston moves back and forth. The **area inside the closed loop is the net work done by the piston on the fluid per cycle**.

A reciprocating pump has **two distinct loops** stacked on either side of the atmospheric line:
- **Top loop (delivery):** Positive pressure pushing water up to the delivery tank.
- **Bottom loop (suction):** Negative pressure (vacuum) lifting water up from the sump.

The total work done per cycle = Sum of the absolute areas of both loops.

---

## 1.3 Why the Indicator Diagram Matters (Engineering Perspective)

> [!TIP]
> The indicator diagram is the **"fingerprint" of a reciprocating pump's health**. A skilled engineer can diagnose the following purely by examining its shape:
> 1. **Slant/lean of the vertical sides** → Reveals leaks past the suction or delivery valves.
> 2. **Rounded corners** → Indicates worn-out valves that close too slowly.
> 3. **Height difference between two delivery peaks** → Reveals acceleration effects and slip.
> 4. **Excess vacuum at the end of suction** → Cavitation risk.
> 5. **Comparison with the ideal rectangle** → Quantitative measure of friction loss.

---

## 1.4 Standard Reference Constants and Units

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Acceleration due to gravity | **g = 9.81 m/s²** | m/s² |
| Density of water | **ρ = 1000 kg/m³** | kg/m³ |
| Specific weight of water | **w = ρg = 9810 N/m³** | N/m³ |
| Atmospheric pressure | **P_atm = 101.325 kN/m²** | kN/m² |
| 1 horsepower | **1 HP = 745.7 W ≈ 0.746 kW** | W |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Construction of the Indicator Diagram

The indicator diagram is essentially a **Pressure–Volume (P–V)** plot. The recording is done as follows:

- A small rotating drum moves horizontally in proportion to the **piston stroke**.
- A stylus, driven by the cylinder pressure, moves **vertically** on the drum.
- Over one full crank revolution, a **closed curve** is traced.
- The atmospheric pressure line is taken as the **zero datum**.

For a **single-acting reciprocating pump**, two loops are obtained:
- The **suction loop** lies below the atmospheric line (negative gauge pressure).
- The **delivery loop** lies above the atmospheric line (positive gauge pressure).

For a **double-acting reciprocating pump**, the diagram is **doubled horizontally** because each revolution has two suction and two delivery strokes.

---

## 2.2 Stages of the Theoretical Indicator Diagram (Ideal Pump)

### A. Single-Acting Pump — Ideal Diagram (No Friction, No Acceleration)

> [!NOTE]
> **Ideal Indicator Diagram (Rectangle Shape):**
> - **Suction stroke (point 1 → 2):** Pressure remains constant at −Hs (vacuum). The line is horizontal, lying below the datum.
> - **Delivery stroke (point 2 → 3):** Pressure instantly jumps to +Hd.
> - **Delivery continuation (point 3 → 4):** Pressure remains constant at +Hd. The line is horizontal, lying above the datum.
> - **Stroke return (point 4 → 1):** Pressure instantly drops back to −Hs.
>
> The two rectangles formed represent:
> - **Area below the datum** = Work done by the pump in lifting water from the sump to the pump centreline.
> - **Area above the datum** = Work done by the pump in lifting water from the pump centreline to the delivery tank.

### B. Single-Acting Pump — Actual Diagram (With Acceleration but No Friction)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When the piston accelerates, the pressure head at the cylinder deviates from the static head by an amount called the **acceleration head** (h_a).
>
> The total head against which the pump works:
> - During **suction**:  H_s(total) = H_s + h_a
> - During **delivery**: H_d(total) = H_d + h_a
>
> This causes the indicator diagram to develop **curved, sloping sides** (parabolic shape) instead of vertical lines.

### C. Single-Acting Pump — Actual Diagram (With Friction and Leakage)

In a real pump, friction causes a **pressure drop** during each stroke. The result is:
- The suction loop is **taller** at the start (since friction is maximum at low velocities and zero at mid-stroke).
- The delivery loop is **shorter** at the start of delivery.
- The diagram resembles a **closed cardiod or "tilted figure-8" shape**.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!TIP]
> **The 10 Most Critical Formulas for Indicator Diagram and Work Done:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{(1) Work done per cycle} &= \text{Area enclosed by indicator diagram} \\
&= \oint P \cdot dV
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{(2) Work done per second (Single-acting)} &= \frac{\rho \, g \, A \, L \, N}{60} \times (H_s + H_d) \\
&= \frac{w \, A \, L \, N}{60} \times (H_s + H_d)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{(3) Work done per second (Double-acting)} &= \frac{2 \, \rho \, g \, A \, L \, N}{60} \times (H_s + H_d) \\
&= \frac{w \, A \, L \, N}{30} \times (H_s + H_d)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{(4) Volume of water pumped per second (Single-acting)} &= \frac{A \, L \, N}{60}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{(5) Volume of water pumped per second (Double-acting)} &= \frac{2 \, A \, L \, N}{60}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{(6) Acceleration head} \quad h_a &= \frac{L}{g} \cdot \frac{A}{a_s} \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r \cdot \cos\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{(7) Maximum acceleration head} \quad (h_a)_{max} &= \frac{L}{g} \cdot \frac{A}{a_s} \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r \\
&= \frac{L}{g} \cdot \frac{A}{a_s} \cdot \left(\frac{2\pi N}{60}\right)^2 \cdot r
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{(8) Work done per second WITH acceleration} &= \frac{w \, A \, L \, N}{60} \times \left[ H_s + H_d + \frac{4}{3}(h_{as} + h_{ad}) \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{(9) Power required by pump} \quad P &= \frac{\text{Work per second}}{1000} \quad \text{(in kW)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{(10) Slip} \quad (\%) &= \frac{Q_{th} - Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

### Symbol Glossary

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| A | Cross-sectional area of piston | m² |
| L | Stroke length | m |
| N | Crank speed | rpm |
| H_s | Static suction head | m |
| H_d | Static delivery head | m |
| h_{as} | Max acceleration head in suction pipe | m |
| h_{ad} | Max acceleration head in delivery pipe | m |
| a_s, a_d | Area of suction / delivery pipe | m² |
| r | Crank radius = L/2 | m |
| ω | Angular velocity = 2πN/60 | rad/s |
| ρ | Density of water | kg/m³ |
| g | Acceleration due to gravity | m/s² |
| w | Specific weight of water = ρg | N/m³ |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in industry:**
> 1. **Pump Selection:** Indicator diagrams help match the pump to the required duty (H, Q).
> 2. **Condition Monitoring:** A "worn-out" indicator diagram is an early warning of valve leakage, piston ring wear, or air ingress.
> 3. **Power Audit:** Comparing theoretical work (from the ideal rectangle) with actual work (from the real diagram) gives the **frictional efficiency** of the pump.
> 4. **Legal Metrology:** In many process industries, the indicator diagram is the basis for **pump acceptance testing** under IS 14106 / API 674 standards.
> 5. **Cavitation Avoidance:** A deep vacuum loop warns the engineer of impending cavitation damage.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Work Done by a Single-Acting Reciprocating Pump (Without Acceleration)

### Given
- A = cross-sectional area of the piston (m²)
- L = stroke length (m)
- N = crank speed (rpm)
- H_s = suction head (m)
- H_d = delivery head (m)
- w = specific weight of water = ρg (N/m³)

### Step 1 — Volume of Water Pumped Per Stroke

During one stroke (one complete movement of the piston from one end to the other), the volume of water displaced equals the swept volume of the cylinder:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{stroke} &= A \times L
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Number of Strokes Per Second

For a single-acting pump, **one revolution = one working stroke**. Therefore, the number of working strokes per second is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{stroke/sec} &= \frac{N}{60}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Volume Pumped Per Second (Theoretical Discharge)

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{th} &= V_{stroke} \times n_{stroke/sec} \\
       &= A \times L \times \frac{N}{60} \\
       &= \frac{A \, L \, N}{60} \quad \text{(m}^3\text{/s)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Work Done During the Suction Stroke

During the suction stroke, the pump **lifts water from the sump** up to the pump centreline through a vertical height H_s.

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{suction} &= w \times Q_{th} \times H_s \\
            &= w \times \frac{A \, L \, N}{60} \times H_s
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Work Done During the Delivery Stroke

During the delivery stroke, the pump **pushes water up** from the pump centreline to the delivery tank through a vertical height H_d.

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{delivery} &= w \times Q_{th} \times H_d \\
             &= w \times \frac{A \, L \, N}{60} \times H_d
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Total Work Done Per Second

The total work done per second is the sum of work during the suction and delivery strokes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{total/sec} &= W_{suction} + W_{delivery} \\
              &= \frac{w \, A \, L \, N}{60} \times H_s + \frac{w \, A \, L \, N}{60} \times H_d \\
              &= \frac{w \, A \, L \, N}{60} \times (H_s + H_d)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7 — Final Compact Form

$$
\boxed{W_{total/sec} = \frac{\rho \, g \, A \, L \, N}{60} \times (H_s + H_d) \quad \text{(Watts)}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Work Done by a Double-Acting Reciprocating Pump (Without Acceleration)

### Given (in addition to previous)
- A double-acting pump has **two pistons/sides** of the same area A.
- One complete revolution = 2 suction strokes + 2 delivery strokes.

### Step 1 — Volume Pumped Per Second

Since 2AL volume is displaced per revolution:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{th} &= \frac{2 \, A \, L \, N}{60} \quad \text{(m}^3\text{/s)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Total Work Done Per Second

Since each stroke (whether suction or delivery) does work on the fluid, and there are 4 working strokes per revolution:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{total/sec} &= 2 \times \frac{w \, A \, L \, N}{60} \times (H_s + H_d) \\
              &= \frac{w \, A \, L \, N}{30} \times (H_s + H_d)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Final Compact Form

$$
\boxed{W_{total/sec} = \frac{2 \, \rho \, g \, A \, L \, N}{60} \times (H_s + H_d) \quad \text{(Watts)}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Effect of Acceleration Head on Work Done

### Step 1 — Pressure Head With Acceleration

When the piston accelerates, the pressure inside the cylinder exceeds (during delivery) or falls below (during suction) the static head by the **acceleration head h_a**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{During delivery: } P_{cyl} &= H_d + h_{ad} \\
\text{During suction: } P_{cyl} &= -H_s - h_{as}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Instantaneous Acceleration Head

For a crank rotating at angular velocity ω, the piston position at angle θ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= r(1 - \cos\theta) + \frac{r^2 \sin^2\theta}{2L} \quad \text{(approximated as } r(1-\cos\theta) \text{ for } r/L \text{ small)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The acceleration of the piston is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a &= \frac{d^2 x}{dt^2} = r \omega^2 \cos\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

The pressure fluctuation in the suction pipe (using continuity) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_a &= \frac{L}{g} \cdot \frac{A}{a_s} \cdot r \omega^2 \cos\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Maximum Acceleration Head

Maximum occurs at θ = 0 (start of stroke):

$$
\begin{aligned}
(h_a)_{max} &= \frac{L}{g} \cdot \frac{A}{a_s} \cdot r \omega^2 \\
            &= \frac{L}{g} \cdot \frac{A}{a_s} \cdot \left(\frac{2\pi N}{60}\right)^2 \cdot r
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Average Acceleration Head Over a Stroke

The average value of cosθ over a half-cycle (0 to π) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(h_a)_{avg} &= \frac{1}{\pi} \int_0^{\pi} (h_a)_{max} \cos\theta \, d\theta \\
            &= \frac{2}{\pi} (h_a)_{max} \\
            &\approx 0.637 \times (h_a)_{max}
\end{aligned}
$$

But KTU commonly uses the **mean effective acceleration head** approximation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(h_a)_{mean} = \frac{4}{3} \times (h_a)_{max} \quad &\text{(commonly used in KTU problems)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Total Work Done Per Second With Acceleration

The pump now has to overcome the static head plus the average acceleration head. The extra work done per second due to acceleration is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{accel} &= 2 \times w \times Q_{th} \times (h_a)_{mean} \\
         &= 2 \times w \times \frac{A \, L \, N}{60} \times \frac{4}{3} (h_{as} + h_{ad}) \\
         &= \frac{w \, A \, L \, N}{60} \times \frac{4}{3} (h_{as} + h_{ad})
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Final Work Done Equation

$$
\boxed{W_{total/sec} = \frac{w \, A \, L \, N}{60} \times \left[ H_s + H_d + \frac{4}{3}(h_{as} + h_{ad}) \right] \quad \text{(Watts)}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Ideal vs Actual Indicator Diagram

| Feature | Ideal Diagram (No Loss) | With Acceleration Only | With Friction Only | Real Diagram (Both) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Shape** | Perfect rectangle | Parabolic curves at corners | Slanted vertical sides | Cardioid / tilted figure-8 |
| **Vertical sides** | Vertical (sharp 90° corners) | Sloped outward at start/end | Slanted (slope = friction loss) | Both sloped and curved |
| **Top loop area** | A × L × Hd | A × L × (Hd + h_a) | A × L × Hd | A × L × (Hd + h_a) - friction loss |
| **Bottom loop area** | A × L × Hs | A × L × (Hs + h_a) | A × L × Hs | A × L × (Hs + h_a) - friction loss |
| **Total work** | Minimum (theoretical) | Higher than ideal | Slightly higher | Maximum (actual input) |
| **Diagnosis** | — | Cavitation, water hammer | Valve/piston wear | Combined fault |

---

## 3.5 Numerical Demonstration — Worked Example

> **Problem:** A single-acting reciprocating pump has a bore of 150 mm and a stroke of 300 mm. It runs at 60 rpm and lifts water from a sump 4 m below the pump to a tank 16 m above the pump. Calculate the work done per second.
>
> Take g = 9.81 m/s² and ρ = 1000 kg/m³.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Compute the area of the piston.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.150)^2 \\
  &= \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.0225 \\
  &= 0.01767 \quad \text{m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Identify the static heads.

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_s &= 4 \quad \text{m (suction)} \\
H_d &= 16 \quad \text{m (delivery)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the single-acting work formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \frac{\rho \, g \, A \, L \, N}{60} \times (H_s + H_d) \\
  &= \frac{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.01767 \times 0.300 \times 60}{60} \times (4 + 16) \\
  &= 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.01767 \times 0.300 \times 20 \\
  &= 1040.07 \quad \text{W} \\
  &\approx 1.04 \quad \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The work done per second ≈ **1.04 kW**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Indicator Diagram Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Crank rotation at N rpm] --> B[Piston reciprocates in cylinder]
    B --> C[Pressure transducer at cylinder head]
    C --> D[Stylus traces pressure vs stroke on rotating drum]
    D --> E[Raw indicator diagram obtained]
    E --> F1[Identify atmospheric datum line]
    E --> F2[Measure area of suction loop]
    E --> F3[Measure area of delivery loop]
    F1 --> G[Compute net work done per cycle]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H1{Compare with theoretical rectangle}
    H1 -- Difference --> I1[Frictional loss quantified]
    H1 -- Curved sides --> I2[Acceleration head quantified]
    H1 -- Vertical sides --> I3[Healthy pump confirmed]
    G --> H2[Multiply by strokes per second]
    H2 --> J[Work done per second in Watts]
    J --> K[Divide by 1000 to get kW]
    K --> L[Final power requirement of pump]
    L --> M[Compare with motor nameplate rating]
    M --> N1[Match: Pump sized correctly]
    M --> N2[Mismatch: Re-evaluate pump or motor]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Shape Variants of Indicator Diagrams

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Indicator Diagram Variants] --> B1[Ideal: Two Rectangles]
    A --> B2[With Acceleration: Rounded Corners]
    A --> B3[With Friction: Slanted Vertical Sides]
    A --> B4[Real: Tilted Figure Eight with Cardioid Loops]
    B1 --> C1[Area = AL x Hs + AL x Hd]
    B2 --> C2[Area = AL x Hs + AL x Hd + 4 by 3 x AL x ha]
    B3 --> C3[Area = AL x Hs + AL x Hd minus friction loss]
    B4 --> C4[Area = sum of loop areas with corrections]
```

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Indicator Diagram Interpretation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Phase 1: Piston starts suction stroke] --> P2[Phase 2: Acceleration builds up]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 3: Constant velocity middle]
    P3 --> P4[Phase 4: Deceleration near TDC]
    P4 --> P5[Phase 5: Delivery valve opens]
    P5 --> P6[Phase 6: Delivery stroke begins]
    P6 --> P7[Phase 7: Acceleration during delivery]
    P7 --> P8[Phase 8: Constant velocity delivery]
    P8 --> P9[Phase 9: Deceleration at end of delivery]
    P9 --> P10[Phase 10: Suction valve reopens - cycle repeats]
    P1 --> R1[Pressure below atmospheric - traces lower loop]
    P5 --> R2[Pressure jumps above atmospheric - traces upper loop]
    P10 --> R3[Pressure drops back to lower level - completes cycle]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading a Mermaid block diagram in exams:**
> Although the actual indicator diagram is a 2D Cartesian plot, examiners award full marks for a labelled block diagram showing the **phases of the cycle** and the **corresponding pressure behaviour** in the cylinder. The block diagram above covers all 10 key phases of a single-acting pump cycle.

---

## 4.4 Tabular Summary of Indicator Diagram Components

| Component | Location on Diagram | Magnitude |
|---|---|---|
| Atmospheric line | Horizontal datum | Zero gauge pressure |
| Suction line | Below datum | −H_s (constant) |
| Delivery line | Above datum | +H_d (constant) |
| Suction loop area | Below datum, enclosed | A × L × H_s |
| Delivery loop area | Above datum, enclosed | A × L × H_d |
| Net work per cycle | Sum of two loop areas | A × L × (H_s + H_d) |
| Acceleration bulge | At start and end of each stroke | ±h_a |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**"What is an indicator diagram? Sketch a typical indicator diagram for a single-acting reciprocating pump and label its salient features."**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Definition (1 Mark):**
An indicator diagram is a **graphical plot of the pressure (head) inside the cylinder versus the stroke length (or volume) of the piston** during one complete working cycle of a reciprocating pump. It is recorded using an indicator instrument fitted to the cylinder head.

**Sketch (1 Mark):**

```
   Pressure (P)
       ^
   P_d |    ____________
       |   |            |
       |   |  Delivery  |
   P_atm|--|------------|------------> Stroke (L)
       |   |   Suction  |
       |   |____________|
   P_s |
```

**Salient Features (1 Mark):**
- The **upper rectangle** represents the delivery stroke at pressure P_d.
- The **lower rectangle** represents the suction stroke at pressure P_s (vacuum).
- The **horizontal datum** corresponds to atmospheric pressure.
- The **area enclosed** by both rectangles equals the **work done per cycle**.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"A single-acting reciprocating pump delivers 0.005 m³/s of water against a total head of 30 m. If the pump runs at 60 rpm, calculate the work done per second."**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Given:**
- Q = 0.005 m³/s
- H = 30 m (total head)
- N = 60 rpm
- ρ = 1000 kg/m³, g = 9.81 m/s²

**Formula (1 Mark):**
$$
W = \rho \, g \, Q \, H
$$

**Substitution and Calculation (1 Mark):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.005 \times 30 \\
  &= 1471.5 \quad \text{W} \\
  &\approx 1.47 \quad \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result (1 Mark):**
Work done per second = **1471.5 W ≈ 1.47 kW**.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 — Module 4 — Internal Choice Option 1]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive an expression for the work done per second by a **single-acting reciprocating pump** without acceleration. Clearly state all assumptions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A single-acting reciprocating pump has the following specifications:
- Bore = 200 mm
- Stroke = 400 mm
- Speed = 50 rpm
- Suction head = 3 m
- Delivery head = 25 m
- Acceleration head in suction pipe = 0.5 m
- Acceleration head in delivery pipe = 0.6 m

Calculate the work done per second by the pump. Also find the power required in kW.

#### Model Solution for Question A

##### Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)

**(Assumptions — 2 Marks):**
1. There is no acceleration of water in the suction and delivery pipes.
2. There is no friction loss in the pipes and valves.
3. There is no slip (theoretical discharge = actual discharge).
4. The pump runs at a steady, constant speed.
5. The fluid is incompressible.

**Derivation Steps (5 Marks):**
- **Step 1 (1 Mark):** Volume of water pumped per stroke = A × L
- **Step 2 (1 Mark):** Number of working strokes per second = N/60
- **Step 3 (1 Mark):** Volume pumped per second = ALN/60
- **Step 4 (1 Mark):** Work done in suction per second = wALN/60 × H_s
- **Step 5 (1 Mark):** Work done in delivery per second = wALN/60 × H_d
- **Final formula:**
$$
\boxed{W = \frac{w \, A \, L \, N}{60} \times (H_s + H_d) = \frac{\rho \, g \, A \, L \, N}{60} \times (H_s + H_d)}
$$

##### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (2 Marks):** Calculate the piston area.
$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.200)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.04 = 0.03142 \quad \text{m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** Apply the work formula with acceleration.
$$
W = \frac{w \, A \, L \, N}{60} \times \left[ H_s + H_d + \frac{4}{3}(h_{as} + h_{ad}) \right]
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \frac{9810 \times 0.03142 \times 0.400 \times 50}{60} \times \left[ 3 + 25 + \frac{4}{3}(0.5 + 0.6) \right] \\
  &= \frac{9810 \times 0.03142 \times 0.4 \times 50}{60} \times \left[ 28 + \frac{4}{3}(1.1) \right] \\
  &= 102.74 \times \left[ 28 + 1.4667 \right] \\
  &= 102.74 \times 29.4667 \\
  &= 3028.06 \quad \text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Convert to kW.
$$
P = \frac{3028.06}{1000} = 3.03 \quad \text{kW}
$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** Result.
- **Work done per second ≈ 3028 W**
- **Power required ≈ 3.03 kW**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Module 4 — Internal Choice Option 2]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **ideal and actual indicator diagram** of a single-acting reciprocating pump. Mention the effects of acceleration and friction on its shape.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A double-acting reciprocating pump has:
- Bore = 150 mm
- Stroke = 250 mm
- Speed = 80 rpm
- Suction head = 4 m
- Delivery head = 20 m

Calculate (i) the theoretical discharge, and (ii) the work done per second.

#### Model Solution for Question B

##### Part (a) — Theoretical Description (7 Marks)

**Ideal Indicator Diagram (3 Marks):**
- The ideal diagram consists of **two perfect rectangles**, one above the atmospheric line (delivery) and one below it (suction).
- Pressure remains **constant** at +H_d during delivery and −H_s during suction.
- Transitions are **instantaneous** (vertical lines) at the start and end of each stroke.

**Actual Indicator Diagram (4 Marks):**
- **Effect of acceleration (2 Marks):** The pressure head at the cylinder head varies parabolically with piston position. Maximum deviation occurs at the start and end of each stroke, where the piston acceleration is highest. The diagram develops **curved, sloped sides** at both ends of the strokes. The acceleration head is given by h_a = (L/g)(A/a_s)·r·ω²·cosθ.
- **Effect of friction (2 Marks):** Friction causes a **continuous pressure drop** during the stroke. The vertical sides of the diagram become **slanted** (tilted inward). The work done against friction appears as the difference between the ideal and actual loop areas.

The real diagram thus has **rounded corners, slanted sides, and tilted loops**, resembling a **tilted figure-8 or cardiod shape**.

##### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (2 Marks):** Calculate the area.
$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.150)^2 = 0.01767 \quad \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** Calculate the theoretical discharge (double-acting).
$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{th} &= \frac{2 \, A \, L \, N}{60} \\
       &= \frac{2 \times 0.01767 \times 0.250 \times 80}{60} \\
       &= \frac{0.7068}{60} \\
       &= 0.01178 \quad \text{m}^3\text{/s} \\
       &\approx 11.78 \quad \text{L/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Apply the work formula (double-acting, no acceleration).
$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \frac{2 \, \rho \, g \, A \, L \, N}{60} \times (H_s + H_d) \\
  &= \frac{2 \times 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.01767 \times 0.250 \times 80}{60} \times (4 + 20) \\
  &= 1155.62 \times 24 \\
  &= 27734.9 \quad \text{W} \\
  &\approx 27.73 \quad \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Final result.
- **(i) Theoretical discharge ≈ 0.01178 m³/s (11.78 L/s)**
- **(ii) Work done per second ≈ 27.73 kW**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing single-acting and double-acting formulas:** Students frequently use the single-acting formula for double-acting pumps (or vice versa), losing 3–4 marks per question.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in discharge:** A double-acting pump pumps **2AL** per revolution, not AL. Always verify which type of pump is given.
> 3. **Unit conversion errors:** Bore is often given in **mm**, which must be converted to **meters** before squaring. Stroke length, too. Speed N is in **rpm**, but formulas require it in **rps** (N/60).
> 4. **Neglecting acceleration head:** If the problem mentions acceleration heads h_as and h_ad, students often forget the **(4/3) factor** — this is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction in KTU valuation.
> 5. **Not drawing the indicator diagram:** Even if the problem is numerical, KTU examiners expect a **rough sketch** of the indicator diagram for full marks in descriptive parts.
> 6. **Confusing work and power:** Work is in **Joules per second (Watts)**, while the power rating is often expressed in **kW** or **HP**. Show the conversion explicitly.
> 7. **Forgetting to use ρg (specific weight):** The formula needs w = ρg = 9810 N/m³, not just ρ = 1000 kg/m³ alone. Some students write "W = ρALN(H_s + H_d)" — this is **dimensionally incorrect**.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

### Core Concepts
- ☐ **Indicator diagram** = Pressure vs. Stroke (or Volume) plot for one cycle.
- ☐ **Work done per cycle = Area of indicator diagram.**
- ☐ Ideal diagram = **two perfect rectangles** (one for suction, one for delivery).
- ☐ Real diagram = **tilted, rounded loops** due to acceleration and friction.

### Key Formulas (Memorize These)
- ☐ Single-acting work/sec = **(w·A·L·N/60) × (H_s + H_d)**
- ☐ Double-acting work/sec = **(2w·A·L·N/60) × (H_s + H_d)** = **(w·A·L·N/30) × (H_s + H_d)**
- ☐ With acceleration: **Add (4/3)(h_as + h_ad)** inside the bracket
- ☐ Acceleration head: **h_a = (L/g) × (A/a_s) × r × ω² × cosθ**
- ☐ Max acceleration head: **at θ = 0**, equals (L/g)(A/a_s)rω²
- ☐ Slip (%) = **[(Q_th − Q_act)/Q_th] × 100**

### Key Constants
- ☐ g = **9.81 m/s²**
- ☐ ρ_water = **1000 kg/m³**
- ☐ w_water = **9810 N/m³**
- ☐ 1 HP ≈ **746 W**

### Common Mistake Zones
- ☐ Always convert **mm → m** before squaring
- ☐ Always divide N by 60 to get **revolutions per second**
- ☐ Double-acting means **2 working strokes per revolution**
- ☐ Acceleration head is always **positive** (additive) in the work formula
- ☐ Atmospheric datum is the **reference line** for the diagram

### Diagnostic Clues from the Diagram
- ☐ Slanted vertical sides → **friction** loss
- ☐ Curved corners → **acceleration** effect
- ☐ Small loop area → **valve leakage** or worn piston rings
- ☐ Asymmetric loops → **air ingress** or partial cylinder filling
- ☐ Deep vacuum loop → **cavitation** risk
- ☐ Sharp peaks in delivery loop → **water hammer** risk

### Practical Engineering Insights
- ☐ A real pump needs **more power** than the theoretical work, due to friction and acceleration.
- ☐ Indicator diagrams are used in **field testing** (IS 14106, API 674 standards).
- ☐ The ratio **(actual area / ideal area)** is the **volumetric–mechanical efficiency indicator** of the pump.
- ☐ For high-speed pumps, **air vessels** are fitted near the cylinder to reduce acceleration head effects.

---

**END OF NOTES — INDICATOR DIAGRAM & WORK DONE**
**Module 4 — PEMRT741 Fluid Mechanics & Machinery — KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: Effect of Acceleration and Frictional Resistance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT741 - FLUID MECHANICS & MACHINERY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Effect of Acceleration and Frictional Resistance ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Unsteady Flow in Pipes** is a transient condition where the velocity and pressure of a fluid change with time, typically caused by sudden or rapid changes in boundary conditions such as valve closure, pump failure, or sudden demand. When the velocity of fluid in a pipe is altered (accelerated or decelerated), the kinetic energy of the fluid column must change, requiring a force. This force is supplied by a **pressure surge** or **water hammer**, which travels as a wave along the pipe at the **celerity (wave speed), denoted as `c` or `a`**. **Water Hammer** is defined as the phenomenon of rapidly increasing pressure (positive surge) or rapidly decreasing pressure (negative surge) when fluid in motion is forced to stop or change direction abruptly. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Exam Focus:** This topic is a **high-weightage area** in Module 4. Students are expected to derive the Joukowsky equation, calculate pressure rise, determine critical time, and account for frictional effects using Allievi's equation. ### 1.2 The Two Governing Effects | Effect | Description | |---|---| | **Acceleration Effect** | Caused by the inertia of the moving fluid column when velocity changes. Dominates during *sudden* valve closure. Governed by **Joukowsky's Equation**. | | **Frictional Resistance** | Caused by shear stresses at the pipe wall resisting fluid motion. Becomes significant for *gradual* valve closure or long pipelines. Governed by **Allievi's Equation**. | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The Garden Tap Analogy (Intuitive Explanation):** > > Imagine you are watering the garden with a long hose. The water is flowing steadily at velocity V. Now, if you quickly twist the tap shut (sudden closure), you hear a loud **"bang"** — this is the water hammer effect. The moving water column has momentum, and when suddenly stopped, this momentum is converted into a pressure spike that travels back up the hose like a shock wave. > > Now, imagine you close the tap slowly. You hear nothing — because the water has time to decelerate gradually, and friction along the pipe walls dissipates the energy smoothly without a violent pressure surge. > > - **Sudden closure (t < tc):** Acceleration effect dominates → **Joukowsky's Equation** > - **Gradual closure (t > tc):** Friction effect dominates → **Allievi's Equation** ### 1.4 Standard Physical Constants | Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Density of water | ρ | **1000** | kg/m³ | | Acceleration due to gravity | g | **9.81** | m/s² | | Bulk modulus of water | K | **2.07 × 10⁹** (≈ 2.0 × 10⁹) | N/m² (Pa) | | Wave speed in rigid pipe (water) | c₀ | **1425** (≈ √(K/ρ)) | m/s | ### 1.5 Key Terminology - **Celerity (a or c):** The speed at which the pressure wave travels in the pipe medium. For a rigid pipe conveying water, $c_0 = \sqrt{K/\rho} \approx 1425$ m/s. - **Joukowsky Pressure Rise (ΔH):** The instantaneous pressure rise when flow is stopped completely in time $t < 2L/c$. - **Critical Time (tc):** The time required for the pressure wave to travel from the valve to the reservoir and return. $t_c = 2L/c$. - **Surge:** A general term for transient pressure changes in a pipeline. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic #### Step 1: Identification of Unsteady Flow When a valve at the downstream end of a pipe is closed, the velocity at the valve end drops from V to 0. This deceleration creates an unbalanced force in the fluid, which propagates upstream as a pressure wave at speed $c$. #### Step 2: The Wave Propagation Mechanism - At time $t = 0$: Valve closed. A high-pressure region forms at the valve. - At time $t = L/c$: The wave reaches the upstream reservoir. The entire pipe is now under high pressure, and fluid is stationary. - At time $t = 2L/c$: The reflected wave returns to the valve. Fluid starts moving backward, and pressure drops. - At time $t = 2L/c$: This round-trip time is the **critical time (tc)**. #### Step 3: Classification Based on Closure Time > [!IMPORTANT] > **Case 1 — Sudden Closure:** $t < t_c = 2L/c$ > - Pressure wave cannot reach the reservoir and return before the valve is fully closed. > - Maximum pressure rise is given by **Joukowsky's Equation**. > - Friction is negligible compared to inertial effect. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Case 2 — Gradual Closure:** $t > t_c = 2L/c$ > - Pressure wave reaches reservoir, reflects, and dissipates during closure. > - Frictional resistance significantly reduces the peak pressure rise. > - Pressure rise is given by **Allievi's Equation** (with friction coefficient). #### Step 4: Why Friction Matters for Gradual Closure During gradual closure, fluid moves slowly over a longer duration. Wall shear stresses (friction) have sufficient time to oppose the momentum change, reducing the net pressure surge. This is the **effect of frictional resistance**. #### Step 5: Effect of Pipe Elasticity In a rigid pipe, the wave speed is $c_0 = \sqrt{K/\rho}$. In an elastic pipe, the pipe wall also deforms, absorbing some energy and reducing the wave speed. --- ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Application | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $c_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{\rho}}$ | Wave speed in rigid pipe | Baseline wave speed | | 2 | $c = \dfrac{\sqrt{K/\rho}}{\sqrt{1 + \dfrac{K}{E}\cdot\dfrac{D}{t}}}$ | Wave speed in elastic (thin) pipe | Account for pipe wall elasticity | | 3 | $\Delta P = \rho \cdot c \cdot V$ | **Joukowsky Pressure Rise (Pa)** | Sudden valve closure | | 4 | $\Delta H = \dfrac{c \cdot V}{g}$ | Pressure rise in metres of fluid | Sudden closure, head form | | 5 | $t_c = \dfrac{2L}{c}$ | Critical time of closure | Boundary between cases | | 6 | $\Delta H = \dfrac{2 L V}{g\, t_c}$ | Pressure rise (gradual, without friction) | $t > t_c$ | | 7 | $\Delta H = \dfrac{2 L V}{g\, t_c} \cdot \left[1 - \dfrac{t_c}{2t}\right]$ | Gradual closure without friction (intermediate) | $t > t_c$ but no friction | | 8 | $\Delta H = \dfrac{L V}{g\, t}\left[1 - \dfrac{L\,V\,f}{2 g D H_s}\right]$ | **Allievi's Equation (with friction)** | $t > t_c$, friction included | | 9 | $\dfrac{\partial H}{\partial t} + \dfrac{c^2}{g}\cdot\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial x} + \dfrac{f\,V\,\vert V\vert}{2 D} = 0$ | Continuity Equation (with friction) | Unsteady flow continuity | | 10 | $\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial t} + V\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial x} + g\dfrac{\partial H}{\partial x} + 4\,f\,\dfrac{V^2}{2D} = 0$ | Dynamic Equation (with friction) | Unsteady flow momentum | > [!NOTE] > **Where:** $K$ = Bulk modulus, $E$ = Elastic modulus of pipe, $D$ = pipe diameter, $t$ = pipe wall thickness, $L$ = pipe length, $V$ = initial velocity, $f$ = Darcy friction factor, $H_s$ = static head, $g$ = 9.81 m/s², $\rho$ = density. --- ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Field | Application | |---|---| | **Hydroelectric Power Plants** | Designing penstocks to handle water hammer during turbine load rejection. | | **Water Distribution Networks** | Sizing surge tanks and air vessels to protect pumps and pipes. | | **Oil & Gas Pipelines** | Preventing pipe rupture during emergency shutdowns. | | **Nuclear Power Plants** | Critical safety analysis for primary coolant loops. | | **Pump Stations** | Choosing non-return (reflux) valves and analyzing pump startup transients. | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Joukowsky's Equation (Acceleration Effect) #### Setup Consider a horizontal pipe of length $L$, diameter $D$, conveying fluid of density $\rho$ at steady velocity $V$. A downstream valve is closed instantaneously at $t = 0$. When the valve is closed, a high-pressure wave travels upstream at wave speed $c$. In time $dt$, the wave moves a distance $c \cdot dt$, bringing a column of length $c \cdot dt$ to rest. #### Step 1: Identify the Mass Decelerated The cross-sectional area of the pipe is $A$. The mass of fluid brought to rest in time $dt$ is: $$dm = \rho \cdot A \cdot (c \cdot dt)$$ *This is the mass of fluid in the cylindrical column of length $c\,dt$ and area $A$.* #### Step 2: Apply the Momentum Equation The change in momentum of this mass (from velocity $V$ to $0$) must equal the impulsive force exerted by the pressure rise. By Newton's Second Law (Impulse = Change in Momentum): $$F \cdot dt = dm \cdot (V - 0)$$ Substituting $dm$: $$F \cdot dt = \rho \cdot A \cdot c \cdot dt \cdot V$$ #### Step 3: Express Force in Terms of Pressure Rise The force $F$ arises from the pressure rise $\Delta P$ acting on area $A$: $$F = \Delta P \cdot A$$ Therefore: $$(\Delta P \cdot A) \cdot dt = \rho \cdot A \cdot c \cdot dt \cdot V$$ #### Step 4: Cancel Common Terms The terms $A$ and $dt$ cancel from both sides: $$\Delta P = \rho \cdot c \cdot V$$ #### Step 5: Express in Head Form Dividing by $\rho \cdot g$ to convert pressure rise to head rise: $$\boxed{\Delta H = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho g} = \dfrac{c \cdot V}{g}}$$ This is the **Joukowsky Pressure Rise Equation** — a foundational result in transient pipeline analysis. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of Critical Time (tc) #### Setup A pressure wave generated at the valve travels at speed $c$ towards the reservoir (distance $L$). At the reservoir, it reflects and returns. #### Step 1: Time for Wave to Reach Reservoir $$t_1 = \dfrac{L}{c}$$ #### Step 2: Time for Reflected Wave to Return $$t_2 = \dfrac{L}{c}$$ #### Step 3: Total Round-Trip Time = Critical Time $$\boxed{t_c = \dfrac{2L}{c}}$$ **Physical Meaning:** If valve closure time $t < t_c$ (sudden closure), the valve is fully closed before the reflected wave returns. Maximum pressure rise = Joukowsky value. If $t > t_c$ (gradual closure), the reflected wave returns while the valve is still closing, reducing the effective pressure rise. --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Pressure Rise for Gradual Closure (Without Friction) #### Setup For $t > t_c$, the effective velocity change seen by the wave system is reduced. The maximum velocity that the system experiences is: $$V_{eff} = \dfrac{V \cdot t_c}{2t}$$ (The velocity reduces linearly from $V$ at $t = t_c/2$ to 0 at $t = t$; average velocity reduction factor). #### Step 1: Apply Joukowsky with Effective Velocity $$\Delta H = \dfrac{c \cdot V_{eff}}{g} = \dfrac{c}{g} \cdot \dfrac{V \cdot t_c}{2t}$$ #### Step 2: Substitute $t_c = 2L/c$ $$\Delta H = \dfrac{c}{g} \cdot \dfrac{V \cdot (2L/c)}{2t}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta H = \dfrac{LV}{gt}}$$ This is the simplified pressure rise for **gradual closure without friction**. --- ### 3.4 Derivation: Allievi's Equation (With Frictional Resistance) #### Setup When the valve closes gradually over time $t$, the fluid column of length $L$ decelerates. The deceleration force is opposed by: 1. The pressure rise force (which we want to find). 2. The frictional resistance from the pipe walls. #### Step 1: Apply Newton's Second Law to the Fluid Column Let the average velocity at time $t$ be $V' = LV/t$ (assuming linear deceleration). The fluid mass is $m = \rho A L$. Net force on column = mass × deceleration: $$(\Delta P \cdot A) - (F_{friction}) = m \cdot a_{decel}$$ #### Step 2: Calculate Frictional Resistance The wall shear stress is $\tau_0 = \rho f V^2 / 8$ (Darcy friction). The friction force on the wetted surface $P = \pi D L$ is: $$F_{friction} = \tau_0 \cdot \pi D L = \dfrac{\rho f V^2}{8} \cdot \pi D L$$ Simplifying using $A = \pi D^2/4$: $$F_{friction} = \dfrac{\rho f V^2 L}{2D} \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4D}\cdot\frac{1}{A}\cdot A$$ A cleaner form: $$F_{friction} = \dfrac{\rho f L A V^2}{2D}$$ #### Step 3: Apply Head Form (Divide by $\rho g A$) $$\Delta H - h_f = \dfrac{L}{g} \cdot \dfrac{dV}{dt}$$ #### Step 4: Substituting Linear Deceleration Assumption For a linear closure, $V_{avg} = LV/t$, and $dV/dt \approx -V/t$: $$\Delta H - h_f = \dfrac{L}{g} \cdot \dfrac{V}{t}$$ #### Step 5: Substitute Friction Head Loss $h_f = fLV^2/(2gD)$: $$\Delta H = \dfrac{LV}{gt} + \dfrac{fLV^2}{2gD}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta H = \dfrac{LV}{gt}\left[1 + \dfrac{fVt}{2D}\right]}$$ Or, equivalently expressed as (when using **Joukowsky's formulation** with $t_c$): $$\boxed{\Delta H = \dfrac{LV}{gt}\left[1 - \dfrac{f L V t}{2gD H_s t_c}\right] \quad \text{(Allievi's form)}}$$ The friction term reduces the pressure rise from the frictionless case. --- ### 3.5 Comprehensive Worked Example **Problem:** A steel penstock of length $L = 1000$ m, diameter $D = 1.0$ m, wall thickness $t = 25$ mm carries water at velocity $V = 3$ m/s. The valve is closed in $t = 4$ seconds. Determine: (a) Wave speed in the pipe. (b) Critical time. (c) Whether closure is sudden or gradual. (d) Pressure rise without friction. (e) Pressure rise with friction (Darcy $f = 0.02$). **Given Data:** - $L = 1000$ m, $D = 1.0$ m, $t_w = 0.025$ m - $V = 3$ m/s, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $K = 2.07 \times 10^9$ N/m² - $E_{steel} = 2.0 \times 10^{11}$ N/m², $t_{closure} = 4$ s, $f = 0.02$ **Solution:** **(a) Wave Speed:** $$c_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2.07 \times 10^9}{1000}} = \sqrt{2.07 \times 10^6} = 1438.9 \text{ m/s}$$ Accounting for pipe elasticity: $$c = \dfrac{c_0}{\sqrt{1 + \dfrac{K}{E}\cdot\dfrac{D}{t_w}}}$$ Compute the elasticity factor: $$\dfrac{K}{E}\cdot\dfrac{D}{t_w} = \dfrac{2.07 \times 10^9}{2.0 \times 10^{11}} \cdot \dfrac{1.0}{0.025} = 0.01035 \times 40 = 0.414$$ $$c = \dfrac{1438.9}{\sqrt{1 + 0.414}} = \dfrac{1438.9}{\sqrt{1.414}} = \dfrac{1438.9}{1.189} = 1210.2 \text{ m/s}$$ **(b) Critical Time:** $$t_c = \dfrac{2L}{c} = \dfrac{2 \times 1000}{1210.2} = 1.653 \text{ s}$$ **(c) Classification:** Since $t_{closure} = 4$ s $> t_c = 1.653$ s, the closure is **gradual**. Friction must be considered. **(d) Pressure Rise Without Friction:** $$\Delta H = \dfrac{LV}{gt} = \dfrac{1000 \times 3}{9.81 \times 4} = \dfrac{3000}{39.24} = 76.45 \text{ m of water}$$ **(e) Pressure Rise With Friction (Allievi's Equation):** Using the form: $$\Delta H = \dfrac{LV}{gt} + \dfrac{fLV^2}{2gD}$$ Compute friction head term: $$\dfrac{fLV^2}{2gD} = \dfrac{0.02 \times 1000 \times 9}{2 \times 9.81 \times 1.0} = \dfrac{180}{19.62} = 9.17 \text{ m}$$ Wait — friction term **adds** to the deceleration requirement in this simplified form. The pressure rise **increases** because friction adds to the resistive force the pressure must overcome. $$\Delta H = 76.45 + 9.17 = 85.62 \text{ m of water}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight from Worked Example:** For gradual closure, friction adds resistance that the upstream pressure must overcome. The **peak surge pressure** is actually *higher* when friction is included in this particular formulation. However, in practice, friction also dissipates wave energy over long pipes, reducing the sustained surge magnitude — the net effect depends on pipe length and closure profile. --- ### 3.6 Summary Table of Cases | Case | Condition | Governing Equation | Friction | |---|---|---|---| | **1** | Sudden Closure | $\Delta H = cV/g$ | Neglected | | **2** | Gradual Closure (No Friction) | $\Delta H = LV/(gt)$ | Neglected | | **3** | Gradual Closure (With Friction) | Allievi's Equation | Included | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Unsteady Pipe Flow Analysis ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Steady State Flow
Initial Velocity V] --> B{Trigger Event} B -->|Valve Closure| C[Boundary Disturbance] B -->|Pump Trip| C B -->|Demand Change| C C --> D[Pressure Wave Generation
at Disturbance Point] D --> E[Wave Propagation
at Celerity c] E --> F{Reflection at Reservoir} F --> G[Reflected Wave Travels
Back to Valve] G --> H{Wave Superposition} H --> I[Net Pressure Surge
Determined] H --> J[Vapor Cavitation Risk
If Negative Surge] I --> K{Compare Closure Time
t vs Critical Time tc} K -->|t less than tc| L[Sudden Closure Case] K -->|t greater than tc| M[Gradual Closure Case] L --> N[Apply Joukowsky Equation
Delta H equals cV over g] M --> O{Friction Significant?} O -->|No, Short Pipe| P[Apply Simplified Equation
Delta H equals LV over gt] O -->|Yes, Long Pipe| Q[Apply Allievi Equation
with Friction Term] N --> R[Final Surge Pressure
Reported in m of Fluid] P --> R Q --> R R --> S[Compare with Pipe
Allowable Pressure Rating] S -->|Exceeds Rating| T[Design Protective Device
Surge Tank or Air Vessel] S -->|Within Rating| U[System Safe for Operation] ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram — Component Interactions in Water Hammer System ```mermaid flowchart LR RS[Reservoir] -->|Steady Flow| PIPE[Pipe of Length L] PIPE -->|Initial Velocity V| VALVE[Downstream Valve] VALVE -->|Sudden Closure| WAVE[Pressure Wave at Celerity c] WAVE -->|Travels Upstream| REFLECT[Reflection at Reservoir] REFLECT -->|Returns as Tension Wave| VALVE2[Valve End] VALVE2 -->|Superposition| SURGE[Net Surge Pressure] SURGE --> CHECK{Exceeds Rating?} CHECK -->|Yes| PROTECT[Activate Surge Tank
or Pressure Relief Valve] CHECK -->|No| SAFE[Safe Operation] FRIC[Frictional Resistance
Wall Shear Stress] -.->|Opposes Flow| SURGE ``` ### 4.3 Schematic of Pressure Wave Behavior (Phase Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Phase 1: t equals 0
Valve Closed, Wave Initiated] --> P2[Phase 2: t equals L over c
Wave Reaches Reservoir] P2 --> P3[Phase 3: t equals 2L over c equals tc
Reflected Wave Returns to Valve] P3 --> P4[Phase 4: t greater than tc
Wave Reflections Continue
with Frictional Damping] P4 --> P5[Phase 5: t much greater than tc
System Reaches New Steady State] P1 -.->|High Pressure
Positive Surge| P2 P2 -.->|Pressure Released
via Reservoir| P3 P3 -.->|Negative Surge
Possible Cavitation| P4 P4 -.->|Oscillations Damped
by Friction| P5 ``` ### 4.4 Decision Matrix for Analysis Approach ```mermaid flowchart TD START[Given Pipe Length L and Closure Time t] --> CALC_TC[Calculate Critical Time
tc equals 2L over c] CALC_TC --> COMPARE{Compare t with tc} COMPARE -->|t less than tc
Sudden Closure| JOUKO[Use Joukowsky Equation
Delta H equals cV over g] COMPARE -->|t greater than tc
Gradual Closure| FRIC_CHK{Check Pipe Length
and Friction Factor} FRIC_CHK -->|Negligible Friction| GRAD[Use Simplified Equation
Delta H equals LV over gt] FRIC_CHK -->|Significant Friction| ALLIEVI[Use Allievi Equation
Include fLV squared over 2gD term] JOUKO --> RESULT[Report Final Surge Head] GRAD --> RESULT ALLIEVI --> RESULT RESULT --> VALIDATE{Check Pipe
Pressure Rating} VALIDATE -->|Exceeds| WARNING[Add Surge Protection
Modify System Design] VALIDATE -->|Within Limits| OK[System Adequately Designed] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question A1:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define water hammer and state Joukowsky's equation. Under what condition is this equation applicable?** **Model Answer:** Water hammer is the phenomenon of rapid pressure rise (or fall) in a pipe when fluid in motion is forced to decelerate (or accelerate) abruptly, typically due to sudden valve closure or pump trip. **Joukowsky's Equation:** $\Delta P = \rho c V$ or in head form: $\Delta H = cV/g$ **Applicable Condition:** This equation is valid for **sudden (instantaneous) closure** of the valve, i.e., when the closure time $t < t_c = 2L/c$, where the pressure wave cannot reflect back from the reservoir before full closure. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for equation, 1 for condition)* --- #### **Question A2:** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **What is the critical time of closure? Why is it significant in water hammer analysis?** **Model Answer:** The critical time $t_c$ is the time required for the pressure wave to travel from the valve to the upstream reservoir and back: $$t_c = \dfrac{2L}{c}$$ **Significance:** - If actual closure time $t < t_c$: Closure is "sudden," and Joukowsky's equation applies (maximum surge pressure). - If $t > t_c$: Closure is "gradual," and the pressure rise is reduced due to wave reflections; friction must be considered. - It serves as the **boundary criterion** between using Joukowsky's equation and the modified Allievi/simplified equations. *(3 marks: 1 for formula, 2 for significance)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a) Derive Joukowsky's equation for the pressure rise due to sudden closure of a valve in a pipeline. (7 Marks)** **Solution:** **Step 1 — Setup:** Consider a horizontal pipe of length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$, conveying fluid of density $\rho$ at velocity $V$. A valve at the downstream end is closed instantaneously. **Step 2 — Mass Decelerated:** In time $dt$, the pressure wave travels a distance $c \cdot dt$, bringing a column of fluid of mass: $$dm = \rho A (c\,dt)$$ to rest. **Step 3 — Impulse-Momentum Equation:** $$F\,dt = dm \cdot V$$ $$\Delta P \cdot A \cdot dt = \rho A c\,dt \cdot V$$ $$\boxed{\Delta P = \rho c V}$$ **Step 4 — In Head Form:** $$\boxed{\Delta H = \dfrac{cV}{g}}$$ *(7 marks: 2 for setup, 2 for momentum application, 2 for derivation, 1 for final equation)* --- **(b) A steel penstock of length 1500 m, diameter 1.2 m, wall thickness 30 mm carries water at a velocity of 2.5 m/s. The valve is closed in 5 seconds. Determine the celerity, critical time, and pressure rise. Take $E = 2 \times 10^{11}$ N/m², $K = 2.07 \times 10^9$ N/m². (7 Marks)** **Solution:** **Step 1 — Celerity in Rigid Pipe:** $$c_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2.07 \times 10^9}{1000}} = 1438.9 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 2 — Celerity in Elastic Pipe:** $$c = \dfrac{c_0}{\sqrt{1 + \dfrac{K}{E}\cdot\dfrac{D}{t}}}$$ Compute elasticity factor: $$\dfrac{K}{E}\cdot\dfrac{D}{t} = \dfrac{2.07 \times 10^9}{2 \times 10^{11}} \cdot \dfrac{1.2}{0.030} = 0.01035 \times 40 = 0.414$$ $$c = \dfrac{1438.9}{\sqrt{1.414}} = \dfrac{1438.9}{1.189} = 1210.2 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 3 — Critical Time:** $$t_c = \dfrac{2L}{c} = \dfrac{2 \times 1500}{1210.2} = 2.479 \text{ s}$$ **Step 4 — Classification:** Since $t = 5$ s $> t_c = 2.479$ s → **Gradual Closure**. **Step 5 — Pressure Rise (No Friction):** $$\Delta H = \dfrac{LV}{gt} = \dfrac{1500 \times 2.5}{9.81 \times 5} = \dfrac{3750}{49.05} = 76.45 \text{ m}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta H = 76.45 \text{ m of water};\quad t_c = 2.479 \text{ s};\quad c = 1210.2 \text{ m/s}}$$ *(7 marks: 2 for celerity, 1 for critical time, 1 for classification, 2 for pressure rise, 1 for final answer)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **(a) Explain the effect of frictional resistance on water hammer pressure. Derive Allievi's equation for gradual closure accounting for friction. (7 Marks)** **Solution:** **Step 1 — Explanation:** During gradual valve closure, the fluid column decelerates slowly. Wall friction (shear stress) opposes this deceleration by absorbing momentum. The effect of friction is to: - Modify the net pressure rise required to decelerate the fluid column. - Dissipate wave energy over long pipes, reducing sustained surge. - Provide a friction head term that adds to (or sometimes reduces) the inertial pressure rise, depending on formulation. **Step 2 — Derivation of Allievi's Equation:** Apply Newton's Second Law to the decelerating fluid column of length $L$ and mass $\rho A L$: $$(\Delta P \cdot A) - F_f = m \cdot \dfrac{dV}{dt}$$ Friction force from wall shear: $$F_f = \tau_0 \cdot \pi D L = \dfrac{\rho f V^2}{2D} \cdot A \cdot L$$ Dividing through by $\rho g A$ to convert to head form: $$\Delta H - h_f = \dfrac{L}{g}\dfrac{dV}{dt}$$ For linear closure, $dV/dt \approx -V/t$ (magnitude $V/t$), so: $$\Delta H - \dfrac{fLV^2}{2gD} = \dfrac{LV}{gt}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta H = \dfrac{LV}{gt} + \dfrac{fLV^2}{2gD} = \dfrac{LV}{gt}\left[1 + \dfrac{fVt}{2D}\right]}$$ *(7 marks: 2 for explanation, 5 for derivation)* --- **(b) A 2000 m long pipeline of diameter 0.8 m carries water at 2.0 m/s. The valve at the downstream end is closed gradually in 6 seconds. Calculate the pressure rise (i) without friction, and (ii) with friction, taking $f = 0.025$. Take wave speed $c = 1000$ m/s. (7 Marks)** **Solution:** **Step 1 — Critical Time:** $$t_c = \dfrac{2L}{c} = \dfrac{2 \times 2000}{1000} = 4 \text{ s}$$ **Step 2 — Classification:** Since $t = 6$ s $> t_c = 4$ s → **Gradual Closure**. **(i) Pressure Rise Without Friction:** $$\Delta H = \dfrac{LV}{gt} = \dfrac{2000 \times 2.0}{9.81 \times 6} = \dfrac{4000}{58.86} = 67.96 \text{ m}$$ **(ii) Pressure Rise With Friction (Allievi's Equation):** Friction head term: $$\dfrac{fLV^2}{2gD} = \dfrac{0.025 \times 2000 \times 4}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.8} = \dfrac{200}{15.696} = 12.74 \text{ m}$$ Total pressure rise: $$\Delta H = 67.96 + 12.74 = \boxed{80.70 \text{ m of water}}$$ *(7 marks: 1 for critical time, 2 for case (i), 3 for case (ii), 1 for final answer)* --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting to classify closure:** Students directly apply Joukowsky's equation without checking if $t < t_c$. Maximum 2 marks lost. > > 2. **Wrong wave speed:** Using $c_0 = 1425$ m/s without accounting for pipe elasticity ($D/t$ term). Always check if pipe is elastic. > > 3. **Sign errors in Allievi's equation:** Friction term is often written with the wrong sign or in the wrong position. Recall: $\Delta H = LV/(gt) + fLV^2/(2gD)$. > > 4. **Units inconsistency:** Mixing $V$ in m/s with $L$ in km without conversion. Always use SI consistently. > > 5. **Forgetting $g$:** Pressure rise in head form must have $g$ in the denominator. Some students forget it in $\Delta H = cV/g$. > > 6. **Misinterpreting "gradual closure with friction":** The friction term **adds** to the required pressure rise (in the simple linear Allievi form). It does **not** always reduce surge magnitude. > > 7. **Skipping the "given" identification:** Always state $t_c$ calculation explicitly to justify which case you're solving. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **⚡ HIGH-YIELD REVISION BULLETS — MEMORIZE FOR EXAM ⚡** - ⭐ **Water Hammer:** Rapid pressure surge due to abrupt velocity change in pipe flow. - ⭐ **Joukowsky's Equation:** $\Delta P = \rho c V$ ; $\Delta H = cV/g$ — for **sudden closure** only. - ⭐ **Wave Speed (rigid pipe):** $c_0 = \sqrt{K/\rho} \approx 1425$ m/s for water. - ⭐ **Wave Speed (elastic pipe):** $c = c_0 / \sqrt{1 + (K/E)(D/t)}$. - ⭐ **Critical Time:** $t_c = 2L/c$ — the boundary between sudden and gradual closure. - ⭐ **Sudden Closure:** $t < t_c$ → Use Joukowsky. No friction considered. - ⭐ **Gradual Closure (no friction):** $\Delta H = LV/(gt)$. - ⭐ **Gradual Closure (with friction — Allievi):** $\Delta H = LV/(gt) + fLV^2/(2gD)$. - ⭐ **Friction Effect:** Wall shear stresses oppose momentum change; significant for long pipes and slow closures. - ⭐ **Bulk Modulus of Water:** $K = 2.07 \times 10^9$ N/m². - ⭐ **Elastic Modulus of Steel:** $E = 2.0 \times 10^{11}$ N/m². - ⭐ **Always classify first:** Calculate $t_c$ and compare with $t$ before choosing the equation. - ⭐ **Always use SI units:** $L$ (m), $V$ (m/s), $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\rho$ (kg/m³). - ⭐ **Pressure rise is reported in "m of fluid"** when using head form. - ⭐ **Convert pressure to head:** $H = P/(\rho g)$. - ⭐ **Engineering Application:** Used in penstock design, pump stations, water supply networks, oil pipelines. - ⭐ **Protection Devices:** Surge tanks, air vessels, pressure relief valves, flywheels on pumps.

topics.title: Slip and Coefficient of Discharge
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT741 - FLUID MECHANICS & MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Slip and Coefficient of Discharge

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Slip — The "Lost Speed" of Water Inside a Moving Bucket

**Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Slip** is defined as the ratio of the **relative velocity of the water jet at the inlet of a moving bucket (vane)** to the **velocity of the jet**, expressed as a percentage. It quantifies the kinetic energy lost because the water cannot perfectly catch up to (or be carried along by) the fast-moving vane of an impulse turbine such as the **Pelton Wheel**.

$$\text{Slip} = \frac{V_1 - u}{V_1} = \frac{V_{r1}}{V_1}$$

where:
- $V_1$ = Absolute velocity of the water jet emerging from the nozzle (m/s)
- $u$ = Tangential velocity of the bucket (m/s)
- $V_{r1} = V_1 - u$ = Relative velocity of water entering the bucket (m/s)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Slip Occurs:**
> The water jet does not instantaneously "lock on" to the bucket. Due to the finite time the water spends inside the bucket and the finite curvature available, there is always a residual relative velocity $V_{r1}$ at entry. This residual kinetic energy (relative to the bucket) is partially lost — hence the name **slip**.

**Intuitive Analogy — The Moving Escalator:**
Imagine you are running at full speed $V_1$ (the jet) trying to step onto a moving escalator that travels at speed $u$. The instant you step in, your speed **relative to the escalator** is $(V_1 - u)$. This residual relative speed is the "slip" — it represents effort expended without propelling the escalator. Similarly, water entering a Pelton bucket always carries some relative kinetic energy that does **not** contribute to bucket rotation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> For a Pelton wheel running at **maximum hydraulic efficiency**, $u = V_1/2$, which gives:
> $$\text{Slip}_{\text{at max efficiency}} = \frac{V_1 - V_1/2}{V_1} = 0.5 \;\; (50\%)$$

---

### 1.2 Coefficient of Discharge — The "Reality Discount" on Flow Rate

**Formal KTU Definition:**
> The **Coefficient of Discharge ($C_d$)** of a nozzle is the ratio of the **actual discharge** of the water jet to the **theoretical discharge** that would occur if there were no frictional losses and no jet contraction. It is a dimensionless number always less than unity.

$$C_d = \frac{Q_{\text{actual}}}{Q_{\text{theoretical}}} = \frac{C_c \cdot C_v \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2gH}}{A \cdot \sqrt{2gH}} = C_c \cdot C_v$$

where:
- $C_c$ = **Coefficient of Contraction** = $\dfrac{A_{\text{jet at vena contracta}}}{A_{\text{nozzle}}}$
- $C_v$ = **Coefficient of Velocity** = $\dfrac{V_{\text{actual}}}{V_{\text{theoretical}}} = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{2gH}}$
- $A$ = Nozzle exit area (m²)
- $H$ = Head available at nozzle inlet (m)

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Values:**
> - **Acceleration due to gravity**: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
> - For a well-designed **convergent nozzle**: $C_v \approx 0.97$ to $0.99$, $C_c \approx 0.98$ to $1.0$
> - Combined **$C_d$ for nozzle**: typically **0.95 to 0.99**

**Intuitive Analogy — The Leaky Garden Hose:**
Imagine a hose where, theoretically, all water should emerge as a tight cylindrical jet. In reality, some water spirals, some sticks to the nozzle walls due to viscosity, and the jet cross-section is slightly smaller than the nozzle opening (the "vena contracta"). The actual volume coming out per second is slightly less than the ideal calculation — this "discount factor" is exactly $C_d$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:**
> $C_d$ is the single most important nozzle parameter because **power output** of a Pelton wheel is directly proportional to $Q$ (and hence $C_d$). A 5% drop in $C_d$ means a 5% drop in power.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Slip Occurs — Mechanism Step-by-Step

1. **Jet Ejection:** Water leaves the nozzle with absolute velocity $V_1 = C_v\sqrt{2gH}$.
2. **Bucket Motion:** The Pelton bucket is mounted on the runner rotating at tangential velocity $u = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$.
3. **Relative Entry:** From the bucket's reference frame, water enters at relative velocity $V_{r1} = V_1 - u$ along the bucket's lip.
4. **Bucket Deflection:** Water is deflected through an angle of nearly $165°$–$170°$ inside the double-cupped bucket.
5. **Frictional Slowdown:** Due to friction along the curved inner surface of the bucket, the relative velocity decreases:
   $$V_{r2} = k \cdot V_{r1}$$
   where $k$ = **bucket friction coefficient** $\approx 0.85$–$0.98$.
6. **Kinetic Energy Loss:** The energy lost to friction manifests as the **slip** — water exiting still has some relative motion wrt bucket.

### 2.2 Why $C_d < 1$ — Mechanism Step-by-Step

1. **Friction Along Nozzle Walls:** Viscous shear at the nozzle inner surface reduces the average exit velocity below $\sqrt{2gH}$.
2. **Vena Contracta Effect:** Just outside the nozzle exit, streamlines continue to converge for a short distance, forming a minimum cross-section (vena contracta) that is smaller than the nozzle area.
3. **Combined Effect:** $C_d = C_c \cdot C_v$ — both effects compound to reduce actual discharge.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Typical Range / Units |
|---|-----------|---------|----------------------|
| 1 | Theoretical jet velocity | $V_t = \sqrt{2gH}$ | m/s |
| 2 | Actual jet velocity | $V_1 = C_v \sqrt{2gH}$ | m/s, $C_v \in [0.95, 0.99]$ |
| 3 | Discharge through nozzle | $Q = C_d A \sqrt{2gH}$ | m³/s |
| 4 | Coefficient of discharge | $C_d = C_c \cdot C_v$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | Bucket tangential velocity | $u = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ | m/s |
| 6 | Relative velocity at inlet | $V_{r1} = V_1 - u$ | m/s |
| 7 | Relative velocity at outlet | $V_{r2} = k \cdot V_{r1}$ | m/s, $k \in [0.85, 0.98]$ |
| 8 | Slip (fraction) | $\text{Slip} = \dfrac{V_{r1}}{V_1} = \dfrac{V_1 - u}{V_1}$ | 0 to 1 |
| 9 | Slip (%) | $\% \text{Slip} = \dfrac{V_1 - u}{V_1} \times 100$ | 0% to 100% |
| 10 | Slip at max efficiency | $\text{Slip}_{\max \eta} = 0.5 = 50\%$ | Constant |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hydropower Plant Design:** Slip directly determines the optimal bucket speed; engineers use it to size generators.
- **Nozzle Manufacturing Quality Control:** $C_d$ is the benchmark metric — poorly machined nozzles (rough bore, sharp edges) drop $C_d$ and revenue.
- **Pelton Wheel Servicing:** Bucket pitting and wear reduce $k$ (friction coefficient), which is directly linked to slip behavior.
- **High-Pressure Cleaning Jets / Fire-Fighting Nozzles:** Both $C_d$ and slip-like losses dictate throw distance and coverage area.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Slip in Terms of Speed Ratio ($K_u$)

**Speed Ratio Definition:**
$$K_u = \frac{u}{V_1}$$

This is the standard Pelton wheel design parameter (typically $K_u \approx 0.43$–$0.48$ for max efficiency).

**Starting from the slip definition:**

$$\text{Slip} = \frac{V_1 - u}{V_1}$$

**Dividing numerator and denominator by $V_1$:**

$$\text{Slip} = \frac{1 - \dfrac{u}{V_1}}{1} = 1 - \frac{u}{V_1}$$

**Substituting $K_u = u / V_1$:**

$$\boxed{\;\text{Slip} = 1 - K_u\;}$$

**Evaluating at maximum hydraulic efficiency ($K_u = 0.5$):**

$$\text{Slip} = 1 - 0.5 = 0.5 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; 50\%$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Derivation of $C_d = C_c \cdot C_v$

**Theoretical Discharge (ideal frictionless nozzle):**

$$Q_{\text{th}} = A \cdot V_t = A \sqrt{2gH}$$

**Actual Discharge (with contraction AND friction):**

The jet area at vena contracta is $A_c = C_c \cdot A$ and the velocity there is $V = C_v \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$.

$$Q_{\text{act}} = A_c \cdot V = (C_c A)(C_v \sqrt{2gH})$$

**Taking the ratio:**

$$\frac{Q_{\text{act}}}{Q_{\text{th}}} = \frac{C_c \, C_v \, A \sqrt{2gH}}{A \sqrt{2gH}}$$

$$\boxed{\;C_d = C_c \cdot C_v\;}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Discharge Loss Due to Slip (Impact on Power)

**Work done per unit weight of water striking a Pelton bucket (per second):**

For a single bucket, considering absolute velocity triangles at inlet and exit, the tangential force on the bucket is:

$$F_t = \rho Q (V_{w1} + V_{w2})$$

**Work done per second (Power transferred to runner):**

$$P_{\text{runner}} = F_t \cdot u = \rho Q u (V_{w1} + V_{w2})$$

Using velocity-triangle geometry for a Pelton wheel (jet splits symmetrically, $\beta \approx 165°$):

$$P_{\text{runner}} = \rho Q u (V_1 - u)(1 + k \cos\beta)$$

**Showing the explicit Slip dependence:**

Since Slip $= \dfrac{V_1 - u}{V_1}$, we have $(V_1 - u) = (\text{Slip}) \cdot V_1$. Substituting:

$$P_{\text{runner}} = \rho Q u \cdot (\text{Slip}) \cdot V_1 \cdot (1 + k \cos\beta)$$
