**At maximum efficiency ($u = V_1/2$, $k \approx 1$, $\beta = 168°$):**

$$P_{\text{runner,max}} = \rho Q \cdot \frac{V_1}{2} \cdot 0.5 \cdot V_1 \cdot (1 - 0.978)$$

$$P_{\text{runner,max}} \approx 0.011 \, \rho Q V_1^2$$

This confirms **why high slip (close to 50%) is desirable** — it represents optimal energy transfer between jet and bucket.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Slip & $C_d$ Across Nozzle Types

| Nozzle Type | $C_v$ | $C_c$ | $C_d = C_c C_v$ | Slip @ Max $\eta$ |
|-------------|-------|-------|-----------------|-------------------|
| **Convergent (standard)** | 0.97 | 0.98 | 0.95 | 50% |
| **Convergent-Divergent** | 0.99 | 1.00 | 0.99 | 50% |
| **Worn/Damaged** | 0.85 | 0.90 | 0.765 | 50% (geometry-dependent) |

> [!NOTE]
> The slip of **50%** is a **geometric consequence** of optimal speed ratio ($u = V_1/2$) and is independent of the nozzle's $C_d$. This is a frequently-tested conceptual trap in KTU exams.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Architecture — Slip & Discharge in a Pelton Wheel System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Reservoir at Head H metres] --> B[Penstock Pipe]
    B --> C[Needle Nozzle Assembly]
    C --> D[Nozzle Exit - Jet Velocity V1]
    D --> E{Coefficient of Discharge Cd = Cc × Cv}
    E --> F[Actual Jet Velocity V1 = Cv × √2gH]
    E --> G[Effective Jet Area = Cc × A]
    F --> H[Moving Bucket Tangential Velocity u]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Relative Velocity Vr1 = V1 - u]
    I --> J[Slip = Vr1 / V1 = 1 - Ku]
    J --> K[Bucket Friction reduces Vr2 = k × Vr1]
    K --> L[Work Done on Bucket = ρQu Vr1 1 + k cosβ]
    L --> M[Runner Power = ω × T]
    M --> N[Generator Electrical Output]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Energy Path with Losses

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HEAD["HEAD INPUT"]
        H1[Gross Head H in metres]
    end
    subgraph NOZZLE["NOZZLE STAGE"]
        N1[Theoretical Velocity √2gH]
        N2[Actual Velocity Cv × √2gH]
        N1 --> N2
    end
    subgraph JET["JET FORMATION"]
        J1[Discharge Q = Cd × A × √2gH]
        J2[Jet Diameter at vena contracta]
        N2 --> J1
        N2 --> J2
    end
    subgraph BUCKET["BUCKET INTERACTION"]
        B1[Bucket Speed u]
        B2[Relative Entry Vr1 = V1 - u]
        B3[Slip Indicator = Vr1 / V1]
        B4[Relative Exit Vr2 = k × Vr1]
        B1 --> B2
        B2 --> B3
        B2 --> B4
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["POWER OUTPUT"]
        O1[Hydraulic Power = ρgQH × η]
        O2[Shaft Power to Generator]
        B4 --> O2
        J1 --> O1
    end
    H1 --> N1
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Velocity Triangle at Bucket Inlet

```
                V1 (jet velocity)
                ↓
                |
                |  α (small angle ≈ 0°)
                |↘
               / Vr1 = V1 − u
              /________________→
                              u (bucket velocity)

   KEY:   V1  = Absolute velocity of jet (stationary frame)
          u   = Bucket tangential velocity (moving frame)
          Vr1 = Relative velocity of jet wrt bucket
          α   = Angle between V1 and u (ideally 0° for Pelton)
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Decision Flow — When Does Slip Matter Most?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Operating Point Selection] --> Q1{Is Turbine at Max Efficiency?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> A1[Set Ku = 0.5]
    Q1 -- No --> A2[Adjust Ku to load demand]
    A1 --> S1[Slip = 50% - Acceptable]
    A2 --> S2[Slip deviates from 50%]
    S2 --> S3{Is Slip > 50%?}
    S3 -- Yes --> L1[Bucket too slow - Energy Wasted]
    S3 -- No --> L2[Bucket too fast - Jet cannot enter cleanly]
    L1 --> Action1[Reduce u or Increase V1]
    L2 --> Action2[Reduce u or Decrease V1]
    Action1 --> Q1
    Action2 --> Q1
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define slip in a Pelton wheel and derive an expression for it in terms of the speed ratio $K_u$.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
Slip in a Pelton wheel is defined as the ratio of the relative velocity of the water jet at the inlet of the bucket to the absolute velocity of the jet.

$$\text{Slip} = \frac{V_{r1}}{V_1} = \frac{V_1 - u}{V_1}$$

**Derivation (1.5 Marks):**
Dividing numerator and denominator by $V_1$:

$$\text{Slip} = \frac{1 - u/V_1}{1} = 1 - \frac{u}{V_1}$$

Since speed ratio $K_u = u/V_1$:

$$\boxed{\text{Slip} = 1 - K_u}$$

For maximum efficiency, $K_u = 0.5$, giving **Slip = 50%**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the coefficient of discharge of a nozzle. Show that $C_d = C_c \cdot C_v$.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
The coefficient of discharge $C_d$ is defined as the ratio of the actual discharge through a nozzle to the theoretical discharge.

$$C_d = \frac{Q_{\text{actual}}}{Q_{\text{theoretical}}}$$

**Proof (2 Marks):**
Theoretical discharge: $Q_{\text{th}} = A\sqrt{2gH}$
Actual discharge: $Q_{\text{act}} = A_c \cdot V = (C_c \cdot A)(C_v \cdot \sqrt{2gH})$

$$C_d = \frac{C_c \cdot A \cdot C_v \cdot \sqrt{2gH}}{A \cdot \sqrt{2gH}} = C_c \cdot C_v$$

---

### 📕 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[Internal Choice — Answer ANY ONE from each Module per KTU ESE 2024 Pattern]**

---

#### ✏️ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** A Pelton wheel is to be designed for a head of **300 m** and a discharge of **0.6 m³/s**. The nozzle has a coefficient of velocity $C_v = 0.98$ and coefficient of contraction $C_c = 0.97$. The bucket friction coefficient is $k = 0.95$ and the bucket deflection angle is $165°$. The speed ratio for maximum efficiency is $K_u = 0.46$.

**Find:**
1. The diameter of the jet. **(3 marks)**
2. The bucket velocity and slip. **(4 marks)**

**(b)** If the same Pelton wheel is to operate at a different speed ratio $K_u = 0.40$, calculate the new slip and comment on the impact on efficiency. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✏️ QUESTION A — MODEL SOLUTION

**(a) Part 1 — Jet Diameter (3 marks):**

**Step 1 — Theoretical velocity:**

$$V_t = \sqrt{2gH} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 300} = \sqrt{5886} = 76.72 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Actual jet velocity:**

$$V_1 = C_v \cdot V_t = 0.98 \times 76.72 = 75.19 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Coefficient of discharge:**

$$C_d = C_c \cdot C_v = 0.97 \times 0.98 = 0.9506$$

**Step 4 — Jet area from discharge equation:**

$$Q = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$$

$$0.6 = 0.9506 \times A \times 76.72$$

$$A = \frac{0.6}{0.9506 \times 76.72} = \frac{0.6}{72.93} = 0.008228 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Jet diameter:**

$$\frac{\pi d^2}{4} = 0.008228 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; d^2 = \frac{4 \times 0.008228}{\pi} = 0.01048$$

$$\boxed{d = 0.1024 \text{ m} \approx 102.4 \text{ mm}}$$

---

**(a) Part 2 — Bucket Velocity and Slip (4 marks):**

**Step 1 — Bucket velocity:**

$$u = K_u \cdot V_1 = 0.46 \times 75.19 = 34.59 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Relative velocity at inlet:**

$$V_{r1} = V_1 - u = 75.19 - 34.59 = 40.60 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Slip:**

$$\text{Slip} = \frac{V_{r1}}{V_1} = \frac{40.60}{75.19} = 0.540 \;\; (54.0\%)$$

**Verification using formula:**

$$\text{Slip} = 1 - K_u = 1 - 0.46 = 0.54 \;\;\checkmark$$

$$\boxed{u = 34.59 \text{ m/s}, \quad \text{Slip} = 54\%}$$

---

**(b) New Slip and Efficiency Comment (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — New bucket velocity:**

$$u_{\text{new}} = K_u^{\text{new}} \cdot V_1 = 0.40 \times 75.19 = 30.08 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — New slip:**

$$\text{Slip}_{\text{new}} = 1 - 0.40 = 0.60 \;\; (60\%)$$

**Step 3 — Efficiency comparison:**

The hydraulic efficiency of a Pelton wheel is given by:

$$\eta_h = 2K_u(1 - K_u)(1 + k\cos\beta)$$

**Case 1 (Original):** $K_u = 0.46$, $k = 0.95$, $\beta = 165°$, $\cos 165° = -0.9659$

$$\eta_{h,1} = 2(0.46)(1 - 0.46)(1 + 0.95 \times (-0.9659))$$

$$\eta_{h,1} = 2(0.46)(0.54)(1 - 0.9176) = 2(0.46)(0.54)(0.0824)$$

$$\eta_{h,1} = 0.04093 = 4.09\% \;\; \text{(hydraulic component only)}$$

**Case 2 (New):** $K_u = 0.40$

$$\eta_{h,2} = 2(0.40)(0.60)(0.0824) = 0.03955 = 3.96\%$$

**Efficiency drops by:**

$$\Delta \eta = 4.09 - 3.96 = 0.13\% \;\;(\text{relative drop} \approx 3.4\%)$$

**Conclusion:** Reducing $K_u$ from 0.46 to 0.40 increases slip from 54% to 60%. This means the bucket is moving slower than the optimal jet-catching speed, causing more kinetic energy to be carried away unconverted, lowering the hydraulic efficiency.

$$\boxed{\text{Slip}_{\text{new}} = 60\%, \quad \eta_h \text{ decreases by approximately } 3.4\%}$$

---

#### ✏️ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the terms **coefficient of velocity ($C_v$)**, **coefficient of contraction ($C_c$)**, and **coefficient of discharge ($C_d$)** for a nozzle. How are they experimentally determined? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a laboratory test on a Pelton wheel, the following observations were recorded:
- Head at nozzle inlet, $H = 80 \text{ m}$
- Nozzle exit diameter, $d_n = 50 \text{ mm}$
- Jet diameter (at vena contracta), $d_j = 49 \text{ mm}$
- Jet velocity measured by pitot tube, $V_1 = 38.5 \text{ m/s}$
- Bucket wheel diameter, $D = 1.2 \text{ m}$
- Rotational speed, $N = 600 \text{ rpm}$

**Calculate:**
1. $C_v$, $C_c$, and $C_d$ of the nozzle. **(3 marks)**
2. The slip of the bucket. **(4 marks)**

---

#### ✏️ QUESTION B — MODEL SOLUTION

**(a) Explanation of Coefficients (7 marks):**

**Coefficient of Velocity $C_v$ (2 Marks):**
The ratio of the actual velocity of the jet at the vena contracta to the theoretical velocity obtained from Torricelli's theorem.

$$C_v = \frac{V_{\text{actual}}}{\sqrt{2gH}}$$

**Experimental Determination:** A long jet is allowed to travel horizontally under gravity from a nozzle at head $H$. The horizontal distance $x$ and vertical drop $y$ are measured:

$$C_v = \frac{x}{2\sqrt{Hy}}$$

**Coefficient of Contraction $C_c$ (2 Marks):**
The ratio of the area of the jet at the vena contracta to the area of the nozzle orifice.

$$C_c = \frac{A_c}{A_n} = \frac{d_j^2}{d_n^2}$$

**Experimental Determination:** Direct measurement of jet and nozzle diameters using a travelling microscope or vernier calipers.

**Coefficient of Discharge $C_d$ (3 Marks):**
The ratio of actual discharge to theoretical discharge.

$$C_d = \frac{Q_{\text{actual}}}{Q_{\text{theoretical}}} = C_c \cdot C_v$$

**Experimental Determination:** Collect the jet discharge in a tank over a measured time interval; compute $Q_{\text{actual}}$; theoretical $Q$ is calculated from nozzle area and head.

---

**(b) Part 1 — Coefficient Calculations (3 marks):**

**Step 1 — Theoretical velocity:**

$$V_t = \sqrt{2gH} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 80} = \sqrt{1569.6} = 39.62 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Coefficient of velocity:**

$$C_v = \frac{V_1}{V_t} = \frac{38.5}{39.62} = 0.9717$$

**Step 3 — Coefficient of contraction:**

$$C_c = \frac{d_j^2}{d_n^2} = \frac{49^2}{50^2} = \frac{2401}{2500} = 0.9604$$

**Step 4 — Coefficient of discharge:**

$$C_d = C_c \cdot C_v = 0.9604 \times 0.9717 = 0.9332$$

$$\boxed{C_v = 0.972, \quad C_c = 0.960, \quad C_d = 0.933}$$

---

**(b) Part 2 — Slip of the Bucket (4 marks):**

**Step 1 — Bucket tangential velocity:**

$$u = \frac{\pi D N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 1.2 \times 600}{60} = \frac{2261.95}{60} = 37.70 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Relative velocity at inlet:**

$$V_{r1} = V_1 - u = 38.5 - 37.70 = 0.80 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Slip:**

$$\text{Slip} = \frac{V_{r1}}{V_1} = \frac{0.80}{38.5} = 0.02078 \;\; (2.08\%)$$

**Step 4 — Speed ratio check:**

$$K_u = \frac{u}{V_1} = \frac{37.70}{38.5} = 0.9792$$

$$\text{Slip} = 1 - 0.9792 = 0.0208 \;\;\checkmark$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Students frequently confuse **slip** with **bucket friction coefficient $k$**. Remember:
> - **Slip** is at the **inlet** — depends on $V_1 - u$.
> - **Friction loss** is at the **outlet** — depends on $V_{r2}/V_{r1} = k$.
> Mixing these two will cost **2–3 marks** in the KTU valuation. Also, never forget to **square the diameter ratio** when calculating $C_c$ — this is a common slip-up worth 1 mark.

---

### 🔑 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Slip** = $\dfrac{V_1 - u}{V_1}$ — measures how much the bucket "lags" the jet at entry.
- ✅ **Slip** = $1 - K_u$ — links directly to the design speed ratio.
- ✅ At **maximum Pelton efficiency**, slip is **always 50%** (independent of $C_d$).
- ✅ **$C_d = C_c \cdot C_v$** — the "holy trinity" of nozzle discharge coefficients.
- ✅ **$C_v \approx 0.97$–$0.99$** for sharp-edged convergent nozzles; **$C_c \approx 0.98$–$1.00$**.
- ✅ **Actual jet velocity** $V_1 = C_v\sqrt{2gH}$ — never use $\sqrt{2gH}$ directly in power calculations.
- ✅ **Actual discharge** $Q = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$ — multiplied by $C_d$, not just $A$.
- ✅ **Bucket velocity** $u = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ — distinguish from jet velocity $V_1$.
- ✅ **Slip increases** when bucket speed is reduced relative to jet (lower $K_u$).
- ✅ Higher slip → more residual kinetic energy → lower efficiency.
- ✅ For convergent-divergent nozzles, $C_d$ can approach **0.99** — preferred for high-head Pelton installations.
- ✅ KTU often asks the **derivation of $C_d = C_c \cdot C_v$** as a 3-mark direct question — memorize it.
- ✅ When pitot tube data is given in a problem, use it to find $C_v$ directly; do **not** assume ideal velocity.
- ✅ **Slip (%) and bucket friction coefficient ($k$)** are two different quantities — do not interchange in formulas.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Separation in Suction and Delivery Pipes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT741 - FLUID MECHANICS & MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Separation in Suction and Delivery Pipes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Separation** in the suction and delivery pipes of a reciprocating pump is the phenomenon in which the absolute pressure of the liquid column inside the pipe falls below the **vapor pressure** of the liquid at the prevailing temperature. When this happens, the dissolved liquid vaporizes locally, forming cavities (vapor bubbles) that are subsequently carried away by the flowing liquid. When these bubbles reach a region of higher pressure, they collapse violently — a process known as **cavitation** — which causes noise, vibration, loss of efficiency, and ultimately damage to the pump and piping system.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology:
- **Separation in Suction Pipe** → Pressure at pump inlet $\leq$ Vapor pressure of liquid (most common occurrence).
- **Separation in Delivery Pipe** → Pressure at pump outlet during deceleration $\leq$ Vapor pressure (less common, but possible in long delivery lines at high speeds).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Module 4):** The governing condition for separation is derived from the **Acceleration Head** of the liquid column set into motion by the reciprocating piston. Mastery of this derivation is mandatory for both Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) questions.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are **drinking water through a very long straw** from a glass placed on a table. The further you pull the water up by sucking, the harder your mouth must work. Now imagine the straw is **10 meters long** and you are sucking at a very high pulsing rate (like a reciprocating pump). The water has mass — it is heavy! Each time you suddenly accelerate the pull, the water column resists because of inertia. The pressure at the top of the straw **drops sharply** during these acceleration phases. If the drop is so severe that the pressure at the top falls below the vapor pressure of water, the water inside the straw will **boil** even at room temperature! This in-line boiling is **separation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Insight:** It is the **acceleration** (not velocity) of the liquid column that causes pressure fluctuation. Therefore, the problem is most severe at the **inner dead center (IDC) / outer dead center (ODC)**, where the piston acceleration is maximum.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

- Atmospheric pressure head $H_{atm} = 10.3 \text{ m of water}$ (at sea level).
- Vapor pressure of water at 20 °C: $H_v = 0.24 \text{ m of water}$.
- Vapor pressure of water at 100 °C: $H_v = 10.3 \text{ m of water}$ (equals atmospheric).
- Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.
- Standard value used in KTU problems: $H_{atm} = 10.3 \text{ m}$ and $H_v$ is given in problem data.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Physical Mechanism — Structured Logic

1. **Piston Motion:** The crank-PIston assembly follows approximately simple harmonic motion (SHM) for small crank-radius-to-connecting-rod ratios ($r/L_c \le 1/4$).
2. **Liquid Column Inertia:** The water column in the long pipe (length $L$, area $a$) has mass $m = \rho L a$. To accelerate this mass, the pressure at the pump end must drop below the static pressure at the source.
3. **Forced Pressure Drop:** The pressure at the pump inlet becomes:
   $$H_{inlet} = H_{atm} - H_s - h_a$$
   where $H_s$ is the static suction lift and $h_a$ is the **acceleration head**.
4. **Critical Condition:** Separation begins when $H_{inlet} \le H_v$.
5. **Resolution:** Air vessels fitted at the pump inlet/outlet act as accumulators, eliminating the need to accelerate the entire column — separation is prevented.

### 2.2 The Acceleration Head Equation (The Heart of the Topic)

The displacement of the piston from the IDC is:
$$x = r(1 - \cos\theta) + \frac{r^2}{2L_c}(1 - \cos 2\theta)$$

Differentiating twice gives the acceleration of the piston:
$$\ddot{x} = r\omega^2\left(\cos\theta + \frac{r}{L_c}\cos 2\theta\right)$$

The acceleration head of the liquid column of mass $\rho L a$ in a pipe of area $a$, with piston area $A$:

$$h_a = \frac{L \cdot A}{g \cdot a} \cdot \omega^2 r \left(\cos\theta + \frac{r}{L_c}\cos 2\theta\right)$$

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Piston acceleration | $f = r\omega^2\!\left(\cos\theta + \frac{r}{L_c}\cos 2\theta\right)$ | m/s² | Maximum at $\theta = 0$ |
| 2 | Acceleration head (general) | $h_a = \dfrac{L \cdot A}{g \cdot a} \cdot r\omega^2\!\left(\cos\theta + \dfrac{r}{L_c}\cos 2\theta\right)$ | m | Valid for single-acting pump |
| 3 | Max acceleration head | $h_{a,\max} = \dfrac{L \cdot A \cdot r\omega^2}{g \cdot a}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{r}{L_c}\right)$ | m | Occurs at $\theta = 0$ (start of stroke) |
| 4 | Pressure at pump inlet | $H_{inlet} = H_{atm} - H_s - h_a$ | m | For suction side |
| 5 | Pressure at pump outlet | $H_{outlet} = H_d + h_a$ | m | For delivery side |
| 6 | No-separation condition (suction) | $H_{atm} - H_v \ge H_s + h_{a,\max}$ | m | Master inequality |
| 7 | Critical angular speed | $\omega_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{(H_{atm} - H_v - H_s)\,g\,a}{L\,A\,r\,(1 + r/L_c)}}$ | rad/s | Speed at which separation just begins |
| 8 | Critical speed in rpm | $N_c = \dfrac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{(H_{atm} - H_v - H_s)\,g\,a}{L\,A\,r\,(1 + r/L_c)}}$ | rpm | Board-exam-friendly form |
| 9 | Minimum velocity for no separation | $v_{min} = \dfrac{A \cdot \omega^2 r}{a}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{r}{L_c}\right)$ | m/s² | Equivalent form |
| 10 | Delivery side separation condition | $H_d + h_{a,\max} \ge H_v$ (always true) | m | Rare; check when $H_d$ is small |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Tip:** Examiners expect the **maximum acceleration head** at $\theta = 0$ because $\cos\theta$ and $\cos 2\theta$ are both $+1$ — this gives the largest pressure drop on the suction side and the largest pressure rise on the delivery side. **Plugging in $\theta = 0$** is the most common 2-mark shortcut in KTU exams.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Municipal water supply pumps** in Kerala's hilly terrain — long suction pipes and high lifts make separation a routine design concern.
- **Boiler feed pumps** in thermal power stations — operate near the vapor pressure of water.
- **Oil pipeline booster pumps** — cavitation damage is a major maintenance cost driver.
- **Marine bilge pumps** and **fire-fighting pumps** on ships — must operate against high static heads with limited NPSH.
- **High-rise building water systems** — long vertical risers produce large acceleration heads.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Acceleration Head of Liquid Column in the Suction/Delivery Pipe

**Assumptions:** Single-acting reciprocating pump, simple harmonic motion of the piston (inertia of connecting rod neglected), incompressible liquid, rigid pipe.

**Step 1 — Piston Displacement from IDC.**
For a slider-crank mechanism with crank radius $r$, connecting rod length $L_c$, and crank angle $\theta$ from IDC, the exact piston displacement is:
$$x = r(1 - \cos\theta) + \frac{r^2}{2L_c}(1 - \cos 2\theta)$$

**Step 2 — Piston Velocity and Acceleration.**
Differentiating once with respect to time, using $\dot\theta = \omega$ (constant):
$$v = \frac{dx}{dt} = r\omega\sin\theta + \frac{r^2\omega}{L_c}\sin 2\theta$$

Differentiating again to get acceleration:
$$a_p = \frac{d^2x}{dt^2} = r\omega^2\cos\theta + \frac{r^2\omega^2}{L_c}\cos 2\theta$$

Factor out $r\omega^2$:
$$a_p = r\omega^2\left(\cos\theta + \frac{r}{L_c}\cos 2\theta\right)$$

**Step 3 — Force Required to Accelerate the Liquid Column.**
Mass of liquid in the pipe of length $L$ and cross-section $a$:
$$m = \rho \, a \, L$$

Force required by Newton's second law:
$$F = m \cdot a_p \cdot \frac{A}{a} = \rho \, a \, L \cdot r\omega^2\left(\cos\theta + \frac{r}{L_c}\cos 2\theta\right) \cdot \frac{A}{a}$$

The factor $A/a$ arises because the piston pushes the pipe cross-section; the equivalent head must be expressed per unit weight of the piston displacement.

**Step 4 — Convert Force to Pressure Head.**
Head equivalent of this force per unit area of the pipe:
$$h_a = \frac{F}{(\rho g)\, a} = \frac{L \, A}{g \, a} \cdot r\omega^2\left(\cos\theta + \frac{r}{L_c}\cos 2\theta\right)$$

This is the **Acceleration Head** equation, valid for a single-acting pump.

> **The factor $\frac{A}{a}$ is the crucial bridge** between piston motion and pipe flow — it amplifies the head by the area ratio (typically 20–100× for industrial pumps).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum Acceleration Head and Critical Speed

**Step 1 — Maximize the trigonometric term.**
$$f(\theta) = \cos\theta + \frac{r}{L_c}\cos 2\theta$$
$$\frac{df}{d\theta} = -\sin\theta - \frac{2r}{L_c}\sin 2\theta = 0$$

For $r/L_c \le 1/4$, the dominant solution is $\theta = 0$ (IDC/ODC), giving:
$$f_{\max} = 1 + \frac{r}{L_c}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the acceleration head formula.**
$$h_{a,\max} = \frac{L \, A \, r\omega^2}{g \, a}\left(1 + \frac{r}{L_c}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Pressure at the Pump Inlet at $\theta = 0$ (Suction Stroke Start).**
$$H_{inlet,\,\min} = H_{atm} - H_s - h_{a,\max}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the No-Separation Limit.**
To prevent separation:
$$H_{inlet,\,\min} \ge H_v$$
$$H_{atm} - H_s - h_{a,\max} \ge H_v$$
$$h_{a,\max} \le H_{atm} - H_v - H_s$$

**Step 5 — Equate to Get Critical Speed.**
At the critical condition, equality holds:
$$\frac{L \, A \, r\omega_c^2}{g \, a}\left(1 + \frac{r}{L_c}\right) = H_{atm} - H_v - H_s$$

Solve for $\omega_c$:
$$\omega_c^2 = \frac{(H_{atm} - H_v - H_s) \cdot g \cdot a}{L \, A \, r \left(1 + \dfrac{r}{L_c}\right)}$$

$$\omega_c = \sqrt{\frac{(H_{atm} - H_v - H_s) \cdot g \cdot a}{L \, A \, r \left(1 + \dfrac{r}{L_c}\right)}}$$

In rpm:
$$N_c = \frac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{(H_{atm} - H_v - H_s) \cdot g \cdot a}{L \, A \, r \left(1 + \dfrac{r}{L_c}\right)}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Separation in the Delivery Pipe

The pressure head at the pump outlet during the **delivery stroke** is:
$$H_{outlet} = H_d + h_a$$

where $H_d$ is the static delivery head (positive). The maximum value of $H_{outlet}$ occurs at $\theta = 0$ (start of delivery stroke) and equals:
$$H_{outlet,\,\max} = H_d + h_{a,\max}$$

**Wait — the danger on the delivery side is NOT at $\theta = 0$.**
The danger is during the **deceleration phase** (near $\theta = 180°$, the ODC of delivery stroke), where the liquid column is still moving forward but the piston has begun to decelerate and reverse. The pressure at the pump outlet momentarily **drops** because the decelerating column tends to continue forward (inertia), momentarily creating a low-pressure region at the piston.

The minimum pressure at the pump outlet (which can cause separation in the delivery pipe) is:
$$H_{outlet,\,\min} = H_d - h_{a,\max}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU:** On the **suction side**, the danger moment is the **start of the stroke** (maximum acceleration of column towards the pump). On the **delivery side**, the danger moment is the **end of the stroke** (column continues forward, pump decelerates). Many students lose marks by treating both cases symmetrically — the master equation is identical, but the **physical moment of danger is reversed**.

Separation in the delivery pipe is rare in practice because $H_d$ is usually much larger than $h_{a,\max}$. It can occur in:
- Very long delivery pipes at very high speeds.
- Low delivery heads with high piston speeds (e.g., pumping into a nearly-full open tank).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Suction-Side and Delivery-Side Separation

| Parameter | Suction Pipe | Delivery Pipe |
|-----------|--------------|----------------|
| Governing pressure | $H_{inlet} = H_{atm} - H_s - h_a$ | $H_{outlet} = H_d - h_a$ (deceleration phase) |
| Critical moment | $\theta = 0$ (start of suction stroke) | $\theta = 180°$ (end of delivery stroke) |
| Critical expression | $H_{atm} - H_v \ge H_s + h_{a,\max}$ | $H_d \ge H_v + h_{a,\max}$ |
| Typical severity | **High** — very common | **Low** — $H_d$ is usually large |
| Common solution | Air vessel at pump inlet | Air vessel at pump outlet |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Cause-Effect Chain of Separation

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **chain of physical events** leading to separation, designed as a Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (per protocol, this maps interactions rather than depicting the physical pump, which is impossible to draw accurately in Mermaid).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Crank Rotation] --> B[Slider Crank Kinematics]
    B --> C[Piston Motion SHM Approx]
    C --> D[Piston Acceleration Function of Theta]
    D --> E[Liquid Column Inertia Force]
    E --> F[Pressure Fluctuation in Pipe]
    F --> G{Separation Check}
    G --> H[Press ge than Hv]
    G --> I[Press less than Hv]
    I --> J[Vapor Bubble Formation]
    J --> K[Bubble Collapse Cavitation]
    K --> L[Noise Vibration Efficiency Loss]
    L --> M[Remedy Air Vessel at Pump Inlet or Outlet]
    H --> N[Safe Operation]
    M --> O[Reduced Acceleration Head]
    O --> N
```

**Reading the diagram (left to right):**
- The chain starts at the **crank** and ends at the **physical damage symptom**.
- The diamond node `G` (Separation Check) is the **decision point** — the bubble forms only when the absolute pressure falls below $H_v$.
- The remedy block `M` closes the loop by feeding back to safe operation through an **air vessel** that decouples the column inertia from the piston motion.

### 4.2 Pressure Variation Schematic — Conceptual Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SuctionStroke[Suction Stroke Theta 0 to 180]
        S1[Theta 0 Max Acceleration] --> S2[Theta 90 Max Velocity]
        S2 --> S3[Theta 180 Deceleration]
    end
    subgraph PressureCurve[Pressure at Pump Inlet]
        P1[Min Press at Theta 0] --> P2[Max Press at Theta 90]
        P2 --> P3[Press at Theta 180]
    end
    subgraph DangerZone[Danger Zone for Separation]
        D1[If Min Press less than Hv SEPARATION] --> D2[If Min Press greater than Hv SAFE]
    end
    S1 --> P1
    S2 --> P2
    S3 --> P3
    P1 --> D1
    P1 --> D2
```

### 4.3 System-Level Block Diagram: Reciprocating Pump with Air Vessel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sump[Sump] -->|Static Suction H sub s| spipe[Suction Pipe Length L sub s]
    spipe --> avs[Air Vessel at Inlet]
    avs -->|Decoupled flow| pump[Reciprocating Pump Cylinder]
    pump --> avd[Air Vessel at Outlet]
    avd -->|Static Delivery H sub d| dpipe[Delivery Pipe Length L sub d]
    dpipe --> tank[Delivery Tank]
    spipe -.->|Risk at theta 0| sep1[Separation in Suction]
    dpipe -.->|Risk at theta 180| sep2[Separation in Delivery]
    avs -.->|Suppresses| sep1
    avd -.->|Suppresses| sep2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** The air vessel is the **standard textbook remedy** for separation. By providing a small compliant volume of air near the cylinder, the piston "draws from" and "discharges into" the air vessel — the long pipe column is no longer forced to oscillate. The acceleration head is effectively reduced to a small value associated only with the short pipe between cylinder and air vessel.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`
**Define separation in the suction pipe of a reciprocating pump. State the condition under which separation occurs.**

**Model Answer:**
Separation in the suction pipe of a reciprocating pump is the phenomenon in which the absolute pressure of the liquid inside the suction pipe falls below the **vapor pressure** of the liquid, causing the liquid to vaporize and form cavities. These vapor bubbles, when carried to regions of higher pressure, collapse violently — a process called **cavitation** — which causes noise, vibration, and damage to the pump.

**Condition for separation:**
$$H_{inlet,\,\min} = H_{atm} - H_s - h_{a,\max} \le H_v$$

i.e., the absolute pressure head at the pump inlet must be greater than the vapor pressure head of the liquid to avoid separation. Equivalently:
$$h_{a,\max} \le H_{atm} - H_v - H_s$$

---

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`
**What is acceleration head? Write the expression for the acceleration head of the liquid in the suction pipe of a single-acting reciprocating pump.**

**Model Answer:**
The acceleration head is the additional head required to accelerate the liquid column in the suction or delivery pipe of a reciprocating pump from rest to the velocity of the piston (and back to rest) during each stroke. It represents the **inertial resistance** of the liquid column.

**Expression (single-acting pump):**
$$h_a = \frac{L \cdot A}{g \cdot a} \cdot r\omega^2 \left(\cos\theta + \frac{r}{L_c}\cos 2\theta\right)$$

where $L$ = length of pipe, $A$ = piston area, $a$ = pipe area, $r$ = crank radius, $L_c$ = connecting rod length, $\omega$ = angular velocity, and $\theta$ = crank angle measured from IDC.

**Maximum value** (at $\theta = 0$):
$$h_{a,\max} = \frac{L \cdot A \cdot r\omega^2}{g \cdot a}\left(1 + \frac{r}{L_c}\right)$$

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

#### Question A
**(a)** Derive an expression for the **acceleration head** of the liquid column in the suction pipe of a single-acting reciprocating pump. **\[7 Marks\]**
**(b)** A single-acting reciprocating pump has a bore of **150 mm** and a stroke of **300 mm**. The suction pipe is **6 m long** and **75 mm in diameter**. The pump runs at **60 rpm** and lifts water from a sump **3 m below the pump centreline**. The connecting rod length is **600 mm**. Determine whether separation will occur at the pump inlet, given $H_{atm} = 10.3 \text{ m}$ and $H_v = 0.24 \text{ m}$ of water. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### Solution to Question A

##### Part (a) — Derivation
*[See the full derivation in Section 3.1 above. The key result to be presented in the exam is:]* $h_a = \frac{L \cdot A}{g \cdot a} \cdot r\omega^2 \left(\cos\theta + \frac{r}{L_c}\cos 2\theta\right)$, with $h_{a,\max}$ at $\theta = 0$.

##### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given data:**
- Bore (piston diameter) $D = 150 \text{ mm} = 0.150 \text{ m}$
- Stroke $= 2r = 300 \text{ mm}$, so crank radius $r = 0.150 \text{ m}$
- Suction pipe length $L = 6 \text{ m}$
- Suction pipe diameter $d = 75 \text{ mm} = 0.075 \text{ m}$
- Speed $N = 60 \text{ rpm}$, so $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 60}{60} = 2\pi \text{ rad/s} = 6.283 \text{ rad/s}$
- Static suction lift $H_s = 3 \text{ m}$
- Connecting rod length $L_c = 600 \text{ mm} = 0.6 \text{ m}$
- $H_{atm} = 10.3 \text{ m}$, $H_v = 0.24 \text{ m}$

**Step 1 — Calculate areas.**
$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.150)^2 = 0.01767 \text{ m}^2$$
$$a = \frac{\pi}{4}d^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.075)^2 = 0.004418 \text{ m}^2$$
$$\frac{A}{a} = \left(\frac{D}{d}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{0.150}{0.075}\right)^2 = 4$$

**Step 2 — Maximum acceleration head.**
$$h_{a,\max} = \frac{L \cdot A \cdot r\omega^2}{g \cdot a}\left(1 + \frac{r}{L_c}\right)$$

Compute $\omega^2 = (2\pi)^2 = 39.48 \text{ rad}^2/\text{s}^2$.

$$\frac{L \cdot A \cdot r\omega^2}{g \cdot a} = \frac{6 \times 0.01767 \times 0.150 \times 39.48}{9.81 \times 0.004418}$$

Numerator: $6 \times 0.01767 \times 0.150 \times 39.48 = 0.6280$

Denominator: $9.81 \times 0.004418 = 0.04334$

$$\text{Quotient} = \frac{0.6280}{0.04334} = 14.49 \text{ m}$$

Multiplicative factor: $1 + \dfrac{r}{L_c} = 1 + \dfrac{0.150}{0.600} = 1.25$

$$h_{a,\max} = 14.49 \times 1.25 = 18.11 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the no-separation condition.**
$$H_{atm} - H_v - H_s = 10.3 - 0.24 - 3.0 = 7.06 \text{ m}$$

Since $h_{a,\max} = 18.11 \text{ m} > 7.06 \text{ m}$, **separation WILL occur** at the pump inlet.

**Step 4 — Critical speed for this installation.**
$$\omega_c = \sqrt{\frac{(H_{atm} - H_v - H_s) \cdot g \cdot a}{L \cdot A \cdot r \left(1 + r/L_c\right)}}$$
$$\omega_c = \sqrt{\frac{7.06 \times 9.81 \times 0.004418}{6 \times 0.01767 \times 0.150 \times 1.25}}$$

Numerator: $7.06 \times 9.81 \times 0.004418 = 0.3060$

Denominator: $6 \times 0.01767 \times 0.150 \times 1.25 = 0.01988$

$$\omega_c = \sqrt{\frac{0.3060}{0.01988}} = \sqrt{15.39} = 3.92 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$N_c = \frac{60 \times 3.92}{2\pi} = 37.4 \text{ rpm}$$

**Conclusion:** The pump must be operated below **37.4 rpm** to avoid separation. At 60 rpm, separation is inevitable — an air vessel is mandatory.

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme]`

#### Question B
**(a)** With the help of neat sketches, explain the phenomenon of **separation in the suction pipe** of a reciprocating pump. Discuss the role of an **air vessel** in preventing it. **\[7 Marks\]**
**(b)** A single-acting reciprocating pump running at **50 rpm** delivers **0.015 m³/s** of water. The suction pipe is **8 m long** and **100 mm in diameter**. The piston has a stroke of **400 mm**. The static suction head is **2.5 m**. Determine the **minimum pressure head** at the pump inlet. Take $H_{atm} = 10.3 \text{ m}$ of water and neglect the connecting rod correction. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### Solution to Question B

##### Part (a) — Phenomenon Explanation
*[Refer to Section 1 and 2 above. The exam answer should cover:]*
- Explanation of separation with the pressure equation $H_{inlet} = H_{atm} - H_s - h_a$.
- Identification of the critical moment at $\theta = 0$ (start of suction stroke).
- Working of the air vessel as an accumulator that decouples the long pipe from the piston.

##### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given data:**
- $N = 50 \text{ rpm}$, so $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi \times 50}{60} = 5.236 \text{ rad/s}$
- $Q = 0.015 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- $L = 8 \text{ m}$, $d = 0.100 \text{ m}$
- Stroke $2r = 0.400 \text{ m}$, so $r = 0.200 \text{ m}$
- $H_s = 2.5 \text{ m}$, $H_{atm} = 10.3 \text{ m}$

**Step 1 — Find piston area $A$.**
Discharge of a single-acting pump: $Q = A \cdot L_{stroke} \cdot \dfrac{N}{60}$
$$A = \frac{Q \times 60}{L_{stroke} \times N} = \frac{0.015 \times 60}{0.400 \times 50} = 0.0045 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Pipe area $a$.**
$$a = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.100)^2 = 0.007854 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Maximum acceleration head (neglecting connecting rod correction).**
$$h_{a,\max} = \frac{L \cdot A \cdot r\omega^2}{g \cdot a}\left(1 + \frac{r}{L_c}\right) \approx \frac{L \cdot A \cdot r\omega^2}{g \cdot a}$$

(Neglecting $r/L_c$ as per problem statement.)

$$\omega^2 = (5.236)^2 = 27.42 \text{ rad}^2/\text{s}^2$$

$$h_{a,\max} = \frac{8 \times 0.0045 \times 0.200 \times 27.42}{9.81 \times 0.007854}$$

Numerator: $8 \times 0.0045 \times 0.200 \times 27.42 = 0.1974$

Denominator: $9.81 \times 0.007854 = 0.07704$

$$h_{a,\max} = \frac{0.1974}{0.07704} = 2.563 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Minimum pressure head at the pump inlet.**
$$H_{inlet,\,\min} = H_{atm} - H_s - h_{a,\max}$$
$$H_{inlet,\,\min} = 10.3 - 2.5 - 2.563 = 5.237 \text{ m of water}$$

$$\boxed{H_{inlet,\,\min} \approx 5.24 \text{ m of water}}$$

Since this is comfortably above $H_v \approx 0.24 \text{ m}$ for cold water, **no separation** is expected.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\cos 2\theta$ term:** At $\theta = 0$, both $\cos\theta = 1$ and $\cos 2\theta = 1$, so they add up. Students often write $h_{a,\max} = \frac{L A r\omega^2}{g a}$ missing the factor $(1 + r/L_c)$. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 2. **Using stroke instead of crank radius:** Stroke $= 2r$, so $r = \text{stroke}/2$. Substituting the full stroke instead of $r$ gives **4× the correct answer**. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 3. **Confusing suction-side and delivery-side critical moments:** Suction danger is at $\theta = 0$; delivery danger is at $\theta = 180°$ (deceleration). The formula structure is the same; the physical interpretation differs. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
> 4. **Mixing up units:** $H_{atm}$ and $H_v$ must be expressed in **meters of water**, not kPa or bar. $1 \text{ bar} \approx 10.2 \text{ m of water}$.
> 5. **Neglecting $L_c$ when it is given:** If the connecting rod length is given, include the factor $(1 + r/L_c)$ — examiners provide it intentionally.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Separation** = pressure in pipe $\le$ vapor pressure of liquid → vaporization → cavitation.
- [x] The **acceleration head** $h_a$ is the cause; it depends on $L$, $A$, $a$, $r$, $\omega$, and $\theta$.
- [x] **Maximum** acceleration head occurs at $\theta = 0$ (IDC of suction stroke / ODC of delivery stroke for symmetric pumps): $h_{a,\max} = \frac{L A r \omega^2}{g a}\left(1 + \frac{r}{L_c}\right)$.
- [x] **Suction-side pressure equation:** $H_{inlet} = H_{atm} - H_s - h_a$.
- [x] **Delivery-side pressure equation:** $H_{outlet} = H_d + h_a$ (rising) and $H_{outlet} = H_d - h_a$ (deceleration phase).
- [x] **No-separation condition (suction):** $H_{atm} - H_v \ge H_s + h_{a,\max}$.
- [x] **No-separation condition (delivery):** $H_d \ge H_v + h_{a,\max}$ (rarely violated).
- [x] **Critical speed formula:** $N_c = \dfrac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{(H_{atm} - H_v - H_s)\,g\,a}{L\,A\,r\,(1 + r/L_c)}}$.
- [x] **Remedy:** Air vessel at pump inlet (for suction-side) and/or pump outlet (for delivery-side).
- [x] **Higher speed = more separation risk** ($\omega^2$ dependence).
- [x] **Longer pipe = more separation risk** (linear $L$ dependence).
- [x] **Smaller pipe area = more separation risk** (inverse $a$ dependence).
- [x] **Higher suction lift $H_s$ = more separation risk.**
- [x] **Hot liquid = more separation risk** (higher $H_v$).
- [x] Remember to convert **stroke to crank radius** by dividing by 2.
- [x] Always check the **physical moment of danger** — IDC for suction, ODC for delivery.
- [x] Use $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ in all numerical problems unless specified otherwise.
- [x] Atmospheric pressure head at sea level: $H_{atm} \approx 10.3 \text{ m of water}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Air Vessel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT741 - FLUID MECHANICS & MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Air Vessel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AIR VESSEL — KTU 2024 Definition**
> An **Air Vessel** is a large, closed, air-tight chamber fitted on the delivery pipe (and/or suction pipe) of a reciprocating pump in close proximity to the cylinder. It is partially filled with air (or any inert gas) at atmospheric or pre-charged pressure, and the remaining volume is occupied by water from the pump. Its primary function is to **dampen the pulsations in fluid flow** caused by the reciprocating (to-and-fro) motion of the piston, thereby ensuring a **continuous and uniform rate of discharge**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification by Position (KTU Syllabus Mandate):**
> 1. **Air Vessel on the Delivery Side** – Fitted on the delivery pipe to smoothen discharge to the delivery tank.
> 2. **Air Vessel on the Suction Side** – Fitted on the suction pipe to maintain a continuous inflow, prevent separation (cavitation), and ensure uniform suction head.
> 3. **Compound Air Vessels** – Fitted on both sides for maximum efficiency in high-head installations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Lungs of the Pump"**
> Imagine blowing air into a syringe connected to a thin tube, then tapping the plunger. Without any reservoir, the water comes out in spurts. Now, place a balloon (an "air vessel") on the tube: when you push, water flows into the balloon compressing the air; when you pull back, the compressed air pushes water out smoothly. **The balloon acts as a cushion that absorbs the jerks.** A reciprocating pump's piston behaves exactly like your finger on the plunger, and the air vessel behaves like the balloon — converting **intermittent (pulsating) flow** into **near-continuous (steady) flow**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
- Density of water: **ρ = 1000 kg/m³**
- Specific weight of water: **w = ρg = 9810 N/m³**
- Atmospheric pressure: **P_atm = 101.325 kPa**
- Polytropic index for air compression (adiabatic assumption): **γ = 1.4** (for rapid pulsations)

### 1.4 Why is an Air Vessel Required? (Syllabus Highlight)

| Problem Without Air Vessel | Solution With Air Vessel |
|---|---|
| Flow in delivery pipe is **pulsating** (stop–go) | Flow becomes **continuous and uniform** |
| Acceleration head is high → extra friction loss | Acceleration head is **drastically reduced** |
| Pump has to accelerate **entire water column** every stroke | Pump only accelerates water **up to the air vessel** |
| Risk of **water hammer / pipe bursts** | Pressure surges are **absorbed by compressed air** |
| Net work done per second is **high** | Net work done per second is **significantly lower** |
| Risk of **separation** at suction (cavitation) | Suction side air vessel prevents separation |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Acceleration Head

The fluid in the delivery pipe is alternately **accelerated** (during delivery stroke) and **decelerated** (during suction stroke). The inertia of the fluid column produces an **acceleration head** `h_a` that adds to or subtracts from the static delivery head.

For Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM) of the piston, with crank rotating at angular velocity `ω` and crank radius `r`:

**Instantaneous velocity of fluid column:**
$$v = \omega r \sin\theta$$

**Instantaneous acceleration of fluid column:**
$$a = \omega^2 r \cos\theta$$

**Acceleration head (hₐ) — the heart of the air vessel problem:**

$$h_a = \frac{a}{g} = \frac{\omega^2 r}{g}\cos\theta$$

The acceleration head is **maximum** at the start of the stroke (θ = 0° at Inner Dead Centre, IDC) and **zero** at mid-stroke (θ = 90°).

### 2.2 Operational Logic — "How the Air Vessel Works"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Mechanics in 5 Bulleted Steps**
> 1. **During delivery stroke:** Piston pushes water; some water goes to the delivery tank, the rest enters the air vessel and **compresses the trapped air**.
> 2. **At the end of delivery stroke:** Air pressure inside the vessel is at its **maximum** (above delivery line pressure).
> 3. **During suction stroke:** Piston retreats; the **compressed air expands** and expels the stored water into the delivery pipe, **maintaining forward flow**.
> 4. **Net Effect:** Water column **upstream of the air vessel** is accelerated/decelerated by the piston, but water column **downstream of the air vessel** flows at a nearly **uniform mean velocity `u`**.
> 5. **Energy Saved:** The pump no longer accelerates the entire delivery column — only the short length between cylinder and air vessel.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Table 1 — Master Formula Sheet for Air Vessels (KTU 2024 Exam-Ready)**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Mean velocity in delivery pipe | $u = \dfrac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60 \cdot A_p}$ | m/s | $A$ = cylinder area, $L$ = stroke, $N$ = rpm, $A_p$ = pipe area |
| 2 | Acceleration head (general) | $h_a = \dfrac{\omega^2 r}{g}\cos\theta$ | m | θ measured from IDC |
| 3 | Max. acceleration head (SHM) | $h_{a,\max} = \dfrac{\omega^2 r}{g}$ | m | Occurs at θ = 0° |
| 4 | Work done per sec **without** air vessel (single-acting) | $W_1 = w A_p L \cdot \dfrac{u^2}{2g} \cdot \dfrac{\pi^2}{4}(1+k^2)$ | W (J/s) | $k = N/n$ correction factor |
| 5 | Work done per sec **with** air vessel (single-acting) | $W_2 = w A_p L \cdot \dfrac{u^2}{2g} \cdot \dfrac{\pi^2}{4} \cdot k^2$ | W (J/s) | Only partial column accelerated |
| 6 | **Work Saved per second** | $W_s = W_1 - W_2 = w A_p L \cdot \dfrac{u^2}{2g} \cdot \dfrac{\pi^2}{4}$ | W (J/s) | **Most asked formula** |
| 7 | For double-acting pump | $W_{1,DA} = 2 \times W_1$ (SA formula) | W | Multiply by 2 (or use combined discharge) |
| 8 | Head loss due to friction (Darcy) | $h_f = \dfrac{4 f L u^2}{2 g d} = \dfrac{f' L u^2}{2 g d}$ | m | $f' = 4f$ (KTU convention) |
| 9 | Acceleration head reduced to at distance `x` from air vessel | $h_{a,x} = h_a \left(\dfrac{x}{L}\right)$ | m | Linear reduction; valid up to L/2 |
| 10 | Optimum position of air vessel (on delivery) | $L/2$ from cylinder (for 2/3rds efficiency) | m | At L/2, savings ≈ constant |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation Convention (Strictly follow in KTU exams):**
> - $A$ = Area of pump cylinder, $A_p$ = Area of pipe
> - $L$ = Stroke length OR pipe length (context-dependent)
> - $w = \rho g$ = Specific weight of water = **9810 N/m³**
> - $k$ = Connecting rod correction factor (ratio $\frac{N}{n}$ or $\frac{r}{L_{rod}}$ depending on textbook)

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Municipal water supply schemes:** Reciprocating pumps used in high-rise buildings need air vessels to prevent pipe bursts.
- **Hydraulic presses & oil hydraulic systems:** Air-over-oil accumulators (industrial counterparts) use the same principle.
- **Boiler feed pumps:** Critical to maintain continuous flow and avoid column separation.
- **Bilge pumps in ships:** Air vessels prevent hammering in long discharge lines.
- **Oil & gas industry:** Pulsation dampeners on reciprocating compressors use identical physics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I: Acceleration Head of a Reciprocating Pump (SHM Assumption)

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumption:** The slider (piston) follows Simple Harmonic Motion, valid when the connecting rod is **infinite** or its length is much greater than the crank radius.

**Given:** Crank rotates with angular velocity $\omega$ (rad/s), crank radius = $r$ m.

**Step 1:** Displacement of the piston from Inner Dead Centre (IDC) at crank angle $\theta$:

$$x = r(1 - \cos\theta)$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate w.r.t. time to get velocity of piston:

$$v_p = \frac{dx}{dt} = r \sin\theta \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt} = \omega r \sin\theta$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate again to get acceleration of piston:

$$a_p = \frac{dv_p}{dt} = \omega r \cos\theta \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt} = \omega^2 r \cos\theta$$

**Step 4:** By continuity, the velocity (and hence acceleration) of the fluid in the delivery pipe is proportional to the piston velocity (and acceleration). Therefore, the acceleration of the fluid column is:

$$a = \omega^2 r \cos\theta$$

**Step 5:** The acceleration head is the head equivalent of this acceleration:

$$\boxed{h_a = \frac{a}{g} = \frac{\omega^2 r}{g}\cos\theta}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention:** $h_a$ is **positive** when $\cos\theta > 0$ (delivery stroke from IDC, 0° to 90°), meaning it adds to the static head. It is **negative** during the second half of delivery (90° to 180°), reducing the static head.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II: Work Done by Pump Without Air Vessel (Single-Acting)

**Discharge from a single-acting pump:**

$$Q = \frac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60} \quad [\text{m}^3/\text{s}]$$

**Mean velocity in delivery pipe:**

$$u = \frac{Q}{A_p} = \frac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60 \cdot A_p}$$

**Instantaneous velocity in pipe (for SHM):**

$$v = \frac{\pi u}{2}\sin\theta$$

**Instantaneous head loss due to friction in the delivery pipe of length $L_p$:**

$$h_f = \frac{f' L_p}{2 g d}\left(\frac{\pi u}{2}\sin\theta\right)^2 = \frac{f' L_p \pi^2 u^2}{8 g d}\sin^2\theta$$

**Work done against friction per cycle (one delivery + one suction):**

$$W_{cycle} = w \cdot Q_{cycle} \cdot \int_0^{2\pi} h_f \, d\theta \quad [\text{misleading; need refinement}]$$

The rigorous standard derivation yields:

**Final expression for work done per second against friction WITHOUT air vessel:**

$$W_1 = w A_p L_p \cdot \frac{u^2}{2g} \cdot \frac{\pi^2}{4} \cdot (1 + k^2)$$

where the factor $\frac{\pi^2}{4}$ arises from the SHM velocity profile and $(1 + k^2)$ accounts for the fact that during the **suction stroke** (without air vessel), the water column decelerates, stops, and reverses, losing kinetic energy equal to $\frac{1}{2} m u_{max}^2$ — this is the **major source of friction loss**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III: Work Done by Pump WITH Air Vessel

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** When an air vessel is fitted close to the cylinder, during the suction stroke the compressed air in the vessel **maintains forward flow** in the delivery pipe at nearly constant velocity $u$. Therefore, the **kinetic energy is NOT lost** — it is preserved.

**Work done against friction per second WITH air vessel:**

$$W_2 = w A_p L_p \cdot \frac{u^2}{2g} \cdot \frac{\pi^2}{4} \cdot k^2$$

The factor $k^2$ represents the small remaining oscillation between the cylinder and the air vessel (only a small length of pipe is accelerated).

---

### 3.4 Derivation IV: Work Saved Per Second (★ Most Important ★)

$$W_{saved} = W_1 - W_2$$

$$W_{saved} = w A_p L_p \cdot \frac{u^2}{2g} \cdot \frac{\pi^2}{4} \cdot (1 + k^2) - w A_p L_p \cdot \frac{u^2}{2g} \cdot \frac{\pi^2}{4} \cdot k^2$$

Factor out common terms:

$$W_{saved} = w A_p L_p \cdot \frac{u^2}{2g} \cdot \frac{\pi^2}{4} \cdot \left[(1 + k^2) - k^2\right]$$

$$\boxed{W_{saved} = w A_p L_p \cdot \frac{u^2}{2g} \cdot \frac{\pi^2}{4}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Striking Conclusion:** The work saved is **independent of the connecting rod correction factor $k$**! This is a frequently tested fact in KTU exams. The saving depends only on the mean velocity, pipe area, pipe length, and specific weight.

---

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (Analytical Matrix)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example 1: Work Saved Calculation**

**Given Data:**
- Single-acting reciprocating pump, $D = 25$ cm, $L = 30$ cm, $N = 60$ rpm
- Delivery pipe length $L_p = 20$ m, pipe diameter $d = 10$ cm
- Friction factor $f' = 0.005$ (NOTE: not used in work-saved formula)
- Connecting rod correction factor $k = 0.04$ (NOT needed in work saved)

**Step 1 — Calculate cylinder and pipe areas:**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.25)^2 = 0.04909 \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_p = \frac{\pi}{4}d^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.10)^2 = 0.007854 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Calculate discharge and mean velocity:**

$$Q = \frac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60} = \frac{0.04909 \times 0.30 \times 60}{60} = 0.01473 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$u = \frac{Q}{A_p} = \frac{0.01473}{0.007854} = 1.875 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Work Saved Formula:**

$$W_{saved} = w A_p L_p \cdot \frac{u^2}{2g} \cdot \frac{\pi^2}{4}$$

$$W_{saved} = 9810 \times 0.007854 \times 20 \times \frac{(1.875)^2}{2 \times 9.81} \times \frac{\pi^2}{4}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical substitution:**

$$W_{saved} = 9810 \times 0.007854 \times 20 \times \frac{3.5156}{19.62} \times 2.4674$$

$$W_{saved} = 1540.85 \times 0.1792 \times 2.4674$$

$$\boxed{W_{saved} \approx 681.5 \text{ W} \approx 0.682 \text{ kW}}$$

---

### 3.6 Position of Air Vessel — Analytical Discussion

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Place the Air Vessel Within L/2?**
> The acceleration head at a point distance $x$ from the cylinder is linearly reduced:
> $$h_{a,x} = h_a \cdot \frac{x}{L_p}$$
> For maximum work saving, the air vessel must be placed **as close to the cylinder as possible**, but not closer than $\frac{L_p}{2}$ from the cylinder for a substantial fraction of the work to be saved. KTU standard: **place at $L/2$ for optimum efficiency**.

| Position of Air Vessel | Work Saved (approx.) |
|---|---|
| At cylinder (x = 0) | Maximum (≈ 100%) |
| At $x = L_p/2$ | ≈ 50% of maximum |
| At end of pipe (x = L_p) | ≈ 0% (no effect) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Air Vessel in a Reciprocating Pump Circuit

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the functional flow of energy and mass through the pump–air vessel–delivery tank system, highlighting the role of the air vessel as a **flow-smoothing accumulator**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Drive Motor] --> B[Crank and Connecting Rod]
    B --> C[Piston in Cylinder]
    C --> D[Non Return Delivery Valve]
    D --> E[Air Vessel with Trapped Air]
    E --> F[Delivery Pipe to Tank]
    F --> G[Delivery Tank]
    H[Suction Source] --> I[Foot Valve]
    I --> J[Suction Pipe]
    J --> K[Non Return Suction Valve]
    K --> C
    E -.compresses.-> E
    E -.expands to push water.-> F
    C -.pulsating flow.-> E
    E -.uniform flow.-> F
```

**Block-Level Functional Description:**

| Block | Function | Energy/Mass State |
|---|---|---|
| A — Drive Motor | Provides rotational mechanical energy | Electrical → Mechanical |
| B — Crank Assembly | Converts rotation to reciprocation (SHM) | Mechanical (rotational) |
| C — Piston + Cylinder | Displacement chamber (positive displacement) | Mechanical → Hydraulic (pulsating) |
| D — Delivery NRV | One-way valve to delivery side | Hydraulic (unidirectional) |
| **E — Air Vessel** | **Stores hydraulic energy in compressed air** | **Hydraulic (pulsating) → Hydraulic (steady)** |
| F — Delivery Pipe | Conveys water to the tank | Hydraulic (steady) |
| G — Delivery Tank | Final storage | Potential energy reservoir |

### 4.2 Schematic — Crank Angle and Acceleration Head Relationship

The Mermaid diagram below maps the geometric relationship between crank position ($\theta$) and the resulting acceleration head $h_a$ — a concept that is often visualized in KTU board exam sketches.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Crank at IDC - theta equals 0] -->|rotation| P1[Crank at theta equals 30 deg]
    P1 -->|rotation| P2[Crank at theta equals 90 deg - mid stroke]
    P2 -->|rotation| P3[Crank at theta equals 150 deg]
    P3 -->|rotation| P4[Crank at ODC - theta equals 180 deg]
    P0 -.h_a is maximum positive.-> HA1
    P2 -.h_a equals zero.-> HA2
    P4 -.h_a is maximum negative.-> HA3
    HA1[Head h_a max]
    HA2[Head h_a zero]
    HA3[Head h_a min]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - At **IDC (θ = 0°)**: Acceleration head is **maximum positive** (adds to static head).
> - At **mid-stroke (θ = 90°)**: Acceleration head is **zero**.
> - At **ODC (θ = 180°)**: Acceleration head is **maximum negative** (subtracts from static head).

### 4.3 Diagram Fallback: Block Architecture of Pulsation Damping

For a more detailed block-level view of the pulsation damping mechanism:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Piston Discharge - Pulsating] --> B{Branch Point}
    B --> C[Path 1 - Upstream Pipe - Short - to Air Vessel]
    B --> D[Path 2 - Downstream Pipe - Long - to Tank]
    C --> E[Compress Trapped Air in Vessel]
    E --> F[Air Pressure Exceeds Delivery Pressure]
    F --> G[During Suction Stroke - Air Expands]
    G --> H[Pushes Water into Downstream Pipe at Constant Velocity]
    H --> D
    D --> I[Steady Uniform Flow to Tank]
    I --> J[Work Saved due to Reduced Acceleration Head]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define an air vessel. State its two main functions in a reciprocating pump.*

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** An air vessel is a closed, air-tight chamber fitted on the delivery (and/or suction) pipe of a reciprocating pump near the cylinder, partially filled with air at a pressure slightly above the delivery pipe pressure.

**Functions:**
1. It ensures a **uniform/continuous rate of discharge** from the delivery pipe by dampening pulsations.
2. It **reduces the work done by the pump** against friction (and the acceleration head), thereby saving power.
3. (Bonus) It prevents water hammer and risk of pipe bursts.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Derive the expression for acceleration head of a reciprocating pump, assuming simple harmonic motion of the piston.*

**Model Answer:**

Let crank radius = $r$, angular velocity = $\omega$, crank angle = $\theta$ from IDC.

Velocity of piston: $v_p = \omega r \sin\theta$

Acceleration of piston: $a_p = \omega^2 r \cos\theta$

By continuity, acceleration of fluid = $a = \omega^2 r \cos\theta$

$$\therefore h_a = \frac{a}{g} = \frac{\omega^2 r}{g}\cos\theta$$

Maximum value: $h_{a,\max} = \dfrac{\omega^2 r}{g}$ at $\theta = 0°$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Derive an expression for the work done per second by a single-acting reciprocating pump **without an air vessel** against friction in the delivery pipe. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single-acting reciprocating pump has cylinder diameter 20 cm and stroke length 30 cm. It runs at 60 rpm and discharges water through a delivery pipe of length 15 m and diameter 10 cm. The delivery head is 20 m and suction head is 4 m. Take $k = 0.04$. Calculate the work done per second **(i) without an air vessel** and **(ii) with an air vessel** on the delivery side. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-A:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

- **Discharge:** $Q = \dfrac{A L N}{60}$ — 1 mark
- **Mean velocity in pipe:** $u = \dfrac{Q}{A_p} = \dfrac{A L N}{60 A_p}$ — 1 mark
- **Instantaneous velocity (SHM):** $v = \dfrac{\pi u}{2} \sin\theta$ — 1 mark
- **Instantaneous friction head:** $h_f = \dfrac{f' L_p}{2 g d} v^2 = \dfrac{f' L_p \pi^2 u^2}{8 g d}\sin^2\theta$ — 2 marks
- **Work done/sec against friction (without AV):** $W_1 = w A_p L_p \cdot \dfrac{u^2}{2g} \cdot \dfrac{\pi^2}{4}(1+k^2)$ — 2 marks

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Geometric data:**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.20)^2 = 0.03142 \text{ m}^2, \quad A_p = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.10)^2 = 0.007854 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Velocity:**

$$u = \frac{A L N}{60 A_p} = \frac{0.03142 \times 0.30 \times 60}{60 \times 0.007854} = \frac{0.5655}{0.4712} = 1.200 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Work done/sec without air vessel:**

$$W_1 = w A_p L_p \cdot \frac{u^2}{2g} \cdot \frac{\pi^2}{4}(1 + k^2)$$

$$W_1 = 9810 \times 0.007854 \times 15 \times \frac{(1.2)^2}{2 \times 9.81} \times \frac{\pi^2}{4} \times (1 + 0.04^2)$$

$$W_1 = 1155.6 \times \frac{1.44}{19.62} \times 2.467 \times 1.0016$$

$$W_1 = 1155.6 \times 0.0734 \times 2.467 \times 1.0016 \approx 209.7 \text{ W (friction component)}$$

$$W_{static} = w Q H_{total} = 9810 \times 0.009425 \times 24 = 2218.8 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{W_{1,total} \approx 209.7 + 2218.8 = 2428.5 \text{ W} \approx 2.43 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step 4 — Work done/sec with air vessel:**

$$W_2 = w A_p L_p \cdot \frac{u^2}{2g} \cdot \frac{\pi^2}{4} \cdot k^2 = 209.7 \times \frac{k^2}{(1+k^2)} \approx 209.7 \times 0.0016 = 0.336 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{W_{2,total} \approx 2219.1 \text{ W} \approx 2.22 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step 5 — Work saved:**

$$W_{saved} = W_1 - W_2 = 2428.5 - 2219.1 \approx 209.4 \text{ W}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the working of an air vessel in a reciprocating pump with a neat sketch. Derive an expression for the **work saved per second** by fitting an air vessel on the delivery side. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **double-acting** reciprocating pump has a cylinder of diameter 25 cm and stroke 40 cm. It runs at 50 rpm. The suction and delivery pipes are each 12 m long with a diameter of 12 cm. The static suction head is 3 m and the static delivery head is 22 m. Take $f' = 0.005$ and $k = 0.05$. Determine the work done per second by the pump **(i) without air vessel** and **(ii) with air vessel on both sides**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-B:**

**(a) Explanation + Derivation (7 marks):**

**Working of Air Vessel (3 marks):**
- During **delivery stroke**, the piston displaces water into the air vessel, **compressing the trapped air**.
- During **suction stroke**, the piston retreats, but the **compressed air expands** and pushes water out of the vessel into the delivery pipe, maintaining continuous forward flow.
- Result: downstream of the air vessel, flow is **uniform**; only the small column between cylinder and vessel is accelerated.

**Derivation of Work Saved (4 marks):**
- $W_1 = w A_p L_p \cdot \dfrac{u^2}{2g} \cdot \dfrac{\pi^2}{4} (1 + k^2)$ — 2 marks
- $W_2 = w A_p L_p \cdot \dfrac{u^2}{2g} \cdot \dfrac{\pi^2}{4} \cdot k^2$ — 1 mark
- $W_{saved} = W_1 - W_2 = w A_p L_p \cdot \dfrac{u^2}{2g} \cdot \dfrac{\pi^2}{4}$ — 1 mark

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Geometry:**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.25)^2 = 0.04909 \text{ m}^2, \quad A_p = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.12)^2 = 0.01131 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Discharge (double-acting):**

$$Q = \frac{2 A L N}{60} = \frac{2 \times 0.04909 \times 0.40 \times 50}{60} = 0.03273 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Mean velocity in pipe:**

$$u = \frac{Q}{A_p} = \frac{0.03273}{0.01131} = 2.894 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Friction head loss (without AV, total for both sides):**

$$h_f = \frac{f' L_p u^2}{2 g d} = \frac{0.005 \times (12+12) \times (2.894)^2}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.12}$$

$$h_f = \frac{0.005 \times 24 \times 8.375}{2.3544} = \frac{1.005}{2.3544} = 0.4269 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Friction work (without AV):**

$$W_{f,1} = w Q h_f = 9810 \times 0.03273 \times 0.4269 = 137.1 \text{ W}$$

$$W_{static} = w Q H_{total} = 9810 \times 0.03273 \times 25 = 8027.6 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{W_{1,total} = 8027.6 + 137.1 = 8164.7 \text{ W} \approx 8.16 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step 6 — With air vessel on both sides:**

The work done against friction reduces to:

$$W_{f,2} = W_{f,1} \times \frac{k^2}{1 + k^2} = 137.1 \times \frac{0.0025}{1.0025} = 0.342 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{W_{2,total} = 8027.6 + 0.342 = 8027.9 \text{ W} \approx 8.03 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step 7 — Work saved:**

$$W_{saved} = 8164.7 - 8027.9 \approx 136.8 \text{ W}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Before You Submit!**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor $\pi^2/4$** in the work formula — this is the most common error and costs **2–3 marks** per question.
> 2. **Mixing up $A$ and $A_p$** — remember $A$ = cylinder area, $A_p$ = pipe area.
> 3. **Forgetting to multiply by 2 for double-acting pump** in the discharge term $Q$.
> 4. **Using $k$ in the work-saved formula** — work saved is **independent of $k$**! Writing it with $k$ is a wrong answer.
> 5. **Not converting N from rpm to rps** — the formula $\omega = 2\pi N/60$ must be used if $N$ is given in rpm. (For the SHM formula, $h_a = \omega^2 r/g$.)
> 6. **Using the wrong friction formula** — KTU expects $h_f = f' L u^2 / (2 g d)$ with $f'$ as the combined coefficient (not Darcy's $f$).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔥 Rapid Revision Checklist — Air Vessels**
> 
> - [ ] **Definition:** Air vessel = closed chamber with trapped air, fitted on delivery/suction pipe of a reciprocating pump to **dampen pulsations** and **save work**.
> - [ ] **Two primary functions:** (1) Uniform discharge, (2) Reduced friction loss / power saving.
> - [ ] **Acceleration head:** $h_a = \dfrac{\omega^2 r}{g}\cos\theta$ — maximum at IDC, zero at mid-stroke.
> - [ ] **Work without air vessel:** $W_1 = w A_p L_p \cdot \dfrac{u^2}{2g} \cdot \dfrac{\pi^2}{4}(1+k^2)$ — the $(1+k^2)$ term represents the energy lost in reversing the column.
> - [ ] **Work with air vessel:** $W_2 = w A_p L_p \cdot \dfrac{u^2}{2g} \cdot \dfrac{\pi^2}{4} \cdot k^2$ — only small oscillation between cylinder and vessel.
> - [ ] **★ Work saved (memorize!):** $W_{saved} = w A_p L_p \cdot \dfrac{u^2}{2g} \cdot \dfrac{\pi^2}{4}$ — **independent of $k$**.
> - [ ] **Mean velocity:** $u = \dfrac{A L N}{60 A_p}$ for single-acting, double it for double-acting.
> - [ ] **Friction head:** $h_f = \dfrac{f' L u^2}{2 g d}$ (KTU convention, $f' = 4f$).
> - [ ] **Optimum position:** Air vessel should be placed as close to cylinder as possible, ideally within $L_p/2$.
> - [ ] **For double-acting:** Discharge is doubled; friction loss calculated on total length (suction + delivery).
> - [ ] **Specific weight:** Always use $w = 9810$ N/m³ for water unless specified.
> - [ ] **Sign convention:** $h_a$ adds to head when $0° < \theta < 90°$ (first half of delivery); subtracts when $90° < \theta < 180°$.
> - [ ] **Why SHM assumption?** The KTU syllabus assumes SHM for simplicity; real motion includes connecting rod correction factor $k$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gear Pump
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT741 - FLUID MECHANICS & MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Gear Pump

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **GEAR PUMP (Positive Displacement Rotary Pump):**
> A **gear pump** is a type of *positive displacement rotary pump* that uses the **meshing of gears** to move fluid by displacing a fixed amount per revolution. The fluid is trapped between the gear teeth and the casing, then carried from the inlet (suction) side to the discharge (delivery) side, where it is expelled as the gears mesh. It is classified as a **fixed displacement pump**, primarily used for **viscous fluids** like lubricating oils, hydraulic oils, fuels, and resins.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, gear pumps belong to the family of **rotodynamic-positive displacement machines** (more precisely, *rotary positive displacement pumps*). They are characterized by:
- A definite, calculable **displacement volume per revolution**.
- A delivery that is (ideally) **independent of the delivery pressure**.
- Self-priming capability (since they can create vacuum at suction).

## 1.2 Two Principal Variants of Gear Pump

| Variant | Configuration | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **External Gear Pump** | Two identical spur gears mesh externally, one drives the other | High-pressure hydraulic systems, lubrication circuits |
| **Internal Gear Pump** | An external spur gear (rotor) meshes with an internal ring gear (stator); uses a crescent-shaped partition | Light oils, chemicals, low-pressure fuel transfer |

> [!NOTE]
> The **External Gear Pump** is the most common, most-tested, and most-asked variant in KTU examinations. All derivations below default to the **External Gear Pump** unless stated otherwise.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine two **interlocked rotating combs** sliding past each other inside a sealed chamber. Each space between adjacent teeth acts like a small **bucket**:
- On the **inlet side**, the teeth un-mesh, creating a brief vacuum that draws fluid into the gaps.
- The fluid is then **carried around the perimeter** of the chamber, trapped between the gear flank and the casing wall.
- On the **discharge side**, as the teeth re-mesh, the trapped fluid is **squeezed out** into the delivery line.

> **Plain-English Summary:** Two rotating toothed wheels act as fluid transporters; they "carry" fluid from suction to delivery on the outer side, and "squeeze it out" on the inner side where teeth interlock.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Design Metrics

- **Gear-tooth profile:** Involute (standard) or Cycloidal.
- **Standard involute pressure angle:** **α = 20°** (per ISO/AGMA standards).
- **Module (m):** Tooth-size parameter, m = D/z, where z = number of teeth.
- **Displacement per revolution:** Fixed and geometric.
- **Self-priming head:** Typically up to **5 to 7 m of fluid column** depending on seal design.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Constructional Anatomy of an External Gear Pump

The pump consists of the following critical components:

1. **Driving Gear (Driver)**: Connected to the external prime mover (electric motor via coupling or pulley).
2. **Driven Gear (Idler/Follower)**: Rotates due to meshing with the driving gear; no direct shaft connection to motor.
3. **Pump Casing/Housing**: A close-fitting enclosure (typically with end-clearances of **0.05 mm to 0.1 mm**).
4. **Suction Port (Inlet)**: Located where gears un-mesh (low-pressure zone).
5. **Delivery Port (Outlet)**: Located where gears mesh (high-pressure zone).
6. **Shaft Seals/Bearings**: To prevent leakage along the shaft and support rotation.
7. **Relief Valve (optional)**: A safety valve in the delivery line to protect against dead-heading.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Geometric Insight:** The casing does *not* touch the gear teeth. The tight radial and side-clearances (≈ 0.05–0.1 mm) allow free rotation while minimizing internal leakage (slip). The fluid is sealed on the outer side by the **casing**, on the sides by **end-plates**, and on the inner side by the **meshing contact point** of the gears.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Working Logic

The operational sequence can be decomposed into four discrete phases:

- **Step 1 — Suction (Induction):** As the gears rotate, the teeth disengage (un-mesh) at the suction port. This creates an **expanding volume** which generates a local **vacuum (low pressure)**. Atmospheric pressure (or upstream reservoir pressure) then pushes the fluid into the void through the suction port.
- **Step 2 — Conveying (Transportation):** The fluid is now trapped in the **spaces between consecutive teeth and the casing wall** (called *tooth-space volumes*). It is carried around the periphery of the casing from the suction side to the delivery side — physically, around ¾ of a full circle.
- **Step 3 — Delivery (Discharge):** As the gears approach the delivery port, the meshing point of the gear teeth sweeps through the fluid, **forcing it out** through the delivery port. The trapped volume is reduced to near zero.
- **Step 4 — Recompression Check (Why no backflow):** Because the meshing teeth form a near-hermetic seal, fluid **cannot return** from the delivery side to the suction side. This is the fundamental mechanism that makes a gear pump a *positive displacement* device.

## 2.3 The "Why" and "How" — Engineering Reasoning

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is it a Positive Displacement Pump?**
> The volume of fluid displaced per revolution is mathematically **fixed by geometry** (Do, Di, W, z). Therefore, regardless of the downstream back-pressure (within structural limits), the **same volume** is theoretically delivered each revolution. This contrasts sharply with centrifugal pumps, where flow drops sharply with rising head.

**How is Pressure Generated?**
The discharge port is connected to a resistive system (e.g., a hydraulic actuator or a throttling valve). The resistance creates a back-pressure. Because the gears continue to "push" fluid into this resistance, the pressure rises until equilibrium is reached with the leakage (slip) rate. The pump will keep building pressure until either a relief valve opens or a structural failure occurs.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Theoretical Discharge** | $Q_{th}$ | $Q_{th} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\,(D_o^2 - D_i^2)\,\cdot W \cdot N$ | m³/s | Per revolution basis: $V_{th} = \frac{\pi}{4}(D_o^2 - D_i^2) W$ |
| 2 | **Displacement Volume** | $V_{th}$ | $V_{th} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\,(D_o^2 - D_i^2)\,W$ | m³/rev | Geometric; depends on Do, Di, W |
| 3 | **Actual Discharge** | $Q_{act}$ | $Q_{act} = Q_{th} - Q_{slip}$ | m³/s | $Q_{slip}$ = internal leakage |
| 4 | **Volumetric Efficiency** | $\eta_v$ | $\eta_v = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_{slip}}{Q_{th}}$ | (decimal) | Typically 0.85 to 0.95 |
| 5 | **Slip Coefficient** | $C_s$ | $Q_{slip} = C_s \cdot \Delta p$ | — | Empirical; depends on clearances |
| 6 | **Pressure Rise** | $\Delta p$ | $\Delta p = p_d - p_s$ | Pa | $p_d$ = delivery, $p_s$ = suction |
| 7 | **Hydraulic (Water) Power** | $P_{hyd}$ | $P_{hyd} = Q_{act}\cdot \Delta p$ | W | Output fluid power |
| 8 | **Shaft Power Input** | $P_{shaft}$ | $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{Q_{act}\cdot \Delta p}{\eta_{overall}}$ | W | Mechanical input required |
| 9 | **Overall Efficiency** | $\eta_{o}$ | $\eta_o = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m$ | (decimal) | $\eta_m$ = mechanical efficiency |
| 10 | **Torque on Shaft** | $T$ | $T = \dfrac{Q_{act}\cdot \Delta p}{2\pi N \cdot \eta_m}$ | N·m | $N$ in rev/s |
| 11 | **Gear Pitch Line Velocity** | $u$ | $u = \dfrac{\pi\,D_P\,N}{60}$ | m/s | $D_P$ = pitch diameter |

> [!NOTE]
> In standard KTU notation: $D_o$ = **Outer (addendum-tip) diameter** of gear, $D_i$ = **Inner (dedendum-root) diameter** of gear, $W$ = **Face width** (axial length of gear), $N$ = **Rotational speed** in **rps (revolutions per second)**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Engine lubrication oil pumps, power-steering pumps, transmission oil pumps.
- **Hydraulic Systems:** Servo-control units, machine tools, injection-moulding machines.
- **Chemical Plants:** Transfer of viscous resins, bitumen, molasses, adhesives.
- **Aviation:** Hydraulic actuation in landing gear, flaps, and flight-control surfaces.
- **Power Generation:** Governor oil pumps, lubrication of turbines.
