<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# MODULE 4 — SCREW PUMP: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

A screw pump transports fluid by **trapping a fixed volume of fluid in the pockets formed between successive threads of the screw and the casing**, then **axially displacing** that pocket from suction to delivery as the screw rotates.

The mechanical sequence is as follows:

1. **Suction phase:** The rotating screw draws fluid from a sump or reservoir into the lowest pocket between two consecutive threads.
2. **Conveyance phase:** As the screw rotates, the thread geometry forces the fluid pocket to move *axially* (parallel to the screw axis), not circumferentially. This is because the helix angle is intentionally chosen so the axial component dominates.
3. **Delivery phase:** At the upper end, the fluid pocket is expelled into the delivery pipe as a new thread crest pushes it over the lip.
4. **Continuous cycle:** Because the screw has many threads, multiple pockets are in transit simultaneously — this is why flow is **smooth and non-pulsating**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind the Geometry:**
> If the helix angle is too steep, fluid slides back down by gravity between threads (back-flow → zero net lift). The condition for the screw to *self-prime* and lift water is:
>
> **Tilt angle (α) of the trough ≤ Friction angle (φ) of water on casing material.**
>
> Hence screw pumps are usually installed at **α = 30° to 45°** and operate at low rpm (typically **90 to 180 rpm**).

---

## 2.2 Geometry of a Single-Blade Archimedean Screw

Let:

- **D** = outer diameter of screw (m)
- **d** = inner core (shaft) diameter (m)
- **L** = length of the screw (m)
- **p** = pitch of the helix (axial distance per turn, m)
- **N** = rotational speed (rpm)
- **α** = angle of inclination of the screw axis to the horizontal (°)
- **H** = vertical lift (m)

The **cross-sectional area of one pocket** (the volume of fluid carried per pitch length) is:

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(D^{2} - d^{2}\right)
$$

The **volume of fluid displaced per revolution** equals the pocket area multiplied by the axial pitch:

$$
V_{\text{rev}} = A \times p = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(D^{2} - d^{2}\right) \times p
$$

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `|` to keep tables intact in markdown rendering.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|------|---------|
| 1 | Cross-sectional area of one pocket | $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\left(D^{2} - d^{2}\right)$ | m² | D = outer dia, d = shaft dia |
| 2 | Volume per revolution (single-flight) | $V_{rev} = A \cdot p = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\left(D^{2} - d^{2}\right) p$ | m³/rev | p = pitch |
| 3 | Theoretical discharge | $Q_{th} = \dfrac{V_{rev} \cdot N}{60}$ | m³/s | N in rpm |
| 4 | Actual discharge | $Q = \eta_{vol} \cdot Q_{th}$ | m³/s | $\eta_{vol}$ = volumetric efficiency |
| 5 | Vertical lift (head) | $H = L \sin\alpha$ | m | L = screw length, α = tilt angle |
| 6 | Power required (water HP) | $P_{water} = \rho g Q H$ | W | = $\gamma Q H$ |
| 7 | Shaft power (brake power) | $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{P_{water}}{\eta_{overall}}$ | W | $\eta_{overall} = 0.6$ to $0.8$ |
| 8 | Theoretical velocity of water up incline | $v = \dfrac{p \cdot N}{60}$ | m/s | Axial velocity |
| 9 | Condition for self-priming | $\alpha \le \phi$ (friction angle) | ° | Typically α ≤ 30°–45° |
| 10 | Specific speed (for comparison) | $N_{s} = \dfrac{N\sqrt{Q}}{H^{5/4}}$ | — | Dimensionless form per KTU notation |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Irrigation & Drainage:** Lifting water from rivers, canals, and drains to fields — most common deployment of large Archimedean screws (e.g., UK Environment Agency installs screws of **diameter up to 4 m** and **discharge up to 10 m³/s**).
- **Sewage & Sludge Treatment:** Progressive cavity (single-screw) pumps handle solids-laden fluids without clogging.
- **Marine & Oil Industry:** Three-screw pumps circulate lubricating oil in ship engines.
- **Hydroelectric Generation (Reverse Mode):** Inclined screws can run as turbines at low heads (1 m – 5 m) with **60–80 % efficiency**, gaining popularity in micro-hydro plants.
- **Food & Chemical Industry:** Gentle, low-shear handling of pastes, chocolate, starch slurries.

---

## 2.5 Advantages and Limitations

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Advantages:**
> - Handles **solids, fibrous matter, and slurries** without damage.
> - **Self-priming** at moderate tilt angles.
> - **Steady, non-pulsating discharge** (ideal for sensitive processes).
> - Can lift **viscous and abrasive fluids** (progressive cavity type).
> - Can be run in **reverse as a turbine** (Archimedean screw generator).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Limitations:**
> - **Fixed delivery** per revolution → no variable flow without speed change.
> - Requires **speed control** for flow regulation.
> - **Limited head** — typically ≤ 10 m per stage.
> - **Rotor-stator wear** in progressive cavity pumps (rubber stator degrades).
> - Large installation **footprint** due to inclined mounting.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# MODULE 4 — SCREW PUMP: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Discharge Equation (Archimedean Screw Pump)

### Step 1 — Identify one fluid pocket between two consecutive threads.

When the screw rotates, every pocket between two successive threads carries a fixed volume of water from the lower (suction) end to the upper (delivery) end. The cross-section of this pocket (perpendicular to the screw axis) is a **circular annulus** of outer diameter **D** and inner diameter **d** (the shaft).

The area of this annulus is:

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(D^{2} - d^{2}\right)
$$

**Logic:** The fluid occupies the space *between* the thread crest (D) and the shaft (d); this is geometrically an annulus. Subtracting the inner circle area from the outer circle area gives the pocket area.

---

### Step 2 — Compute the axial length occupied by one pocket.

The axial distance between two adjacent threads is the **pitch p**. Hence, the volume of fluid contained in one pocket is:

$$
V_{\text{pocket}} = A \cdot p = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(D^{2} - d^{2}\right) \cdot p
$$

**Logic:** Imagine "unrolling" the helix. Each turn of the helix produces one pocket of axial length *p*, and the volume is the cross-sectional area times the axial length.

---

### Step 3 — Relate pocket discharge to rotational speed.

If the screw makes **N revolutions per minute**, the number of pockets discharged per second equals the number of pockets per revolution times N/60:

$$
\text{Number of pockets discharged per second} = \frac{N}{60}
$$

Wait — that's only if *one* thread (single-flight screw). For a single-flight screw, **one pocket leaves per revolution**, so:

$$
V_{\text{per second}} = V_{\text{pocket}} \times \frac{N}{60}
$$

Substituting:

$$
Q_{th} = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(D^{2} - d^{2}\right) \cdot p \cdot \frac{N}{60}
$$

For a **multi-flight screw** with *n* parallel threads (helices), the discharge becomes:

$$
Q_{th} = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(D^{2} - d^{2}\right) \cdot p \cdot \frac{n \cdot N}{60}
$$

**Logic:** Each helix carries its own independent set of pockets. With *n* helices, you multiply the flow by *n*.

> **Final Compact Form:**
> $$
> \boxed{\,Q_{th} = \frac{\pi \cdot n \cdot N \cdot p}{240}\left(D^{2} - d^{2}\right)\,}
> $$

---

### Step 2 — Compute the actual (real) discharge.

Because of slip (back-flow) between the threads, not all the geometric volume is delivered. A **volumetric efficiency** $\eta_v$ is introduced:

$$
Q_{actual} = \eta_v \cdot Q_{th}
$$

Typical values:
- Clean water at proper tilt: $\eta_v \approx 0.7$ to $0.85$
- Sludge or viscous fluid: $\eta_v \approx 0.4$ to $0.6$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Power Required

### Step 1 — Useful work done per second (water power).

The water is lifted through a vertical head **H** at a rate of **Q** m³/s. The mass lifted per second is $\rho Q$, and the gravitational work per unit mass is $gH$. Hence:

$$
P_{water} = \rho \, g \, Q \, H = \gamma \, Q \, H
$$

**Logic:** Power = (force per unit weight) × (weight flow rate) × (head). Substituting $\gamma = \rho g$ in SI gives the same result.

---

### Step 2 — Vertical head for an inclined screw.

If the screw is mounted at tilt angle $\alpha$ and has axial length $L$, the vertical rise is:

$$
H = L \sin\alpha
$$

**Logic:** Decompose the axial length *L* along vertical and horizontal axes; the vertical component is $L \sin\alpha$.

---

### Step 3 — Shaft (brake) power.

The motor must supply more than the water power to overcome friction and slip losses. Using an overall efficiency $\eta_{o}$:

$$
P_{shaft} = \frac{P_{water}}{\eta_{overall}} = \frac{\gamma \, Q \, H}{\eta_{overall}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Work-Out Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** An Archimedean screw pump has an outer diameter **D = 0.6 m**, shaft diameter **d = 0.15 m**, pitch **p = 0.5 m**, speed **N = 90 rpm**, and is mounted at an angle of **α = 30°** to the horizontal. If the screw length **L = 6 m** and overall efficiency is **70 %**, calculate:
> (a) Theoretical discharge
> (b) Actual discharge (take volumetric efficiency = 75 %)
> (c) Power required by the motor

**Given:**
- D = 0.6 m, d = 0.15 m, p = 0.5 m, N = 90 rpm, n = 1 (single flight)
- L = 6 m, α = 30°, $\eta_{overall} = 0.70$, $\eta_v = 0.75$

**Step (a) — Theoretical discharge:**

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(0.6^{2} - 0.15^{2}\right) = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(0.36 - 0.0225\right) = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.3375)
$$

$$
A = 0.2651 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

$$
Q_{th} = \frac{\pi \times 1 \times 90 \times 0.5}{240} \times (0.6^{2} - 0.15^{2})
$$

**Logic:** Use $Q_{th} = \frac{\pi n N p}{240}(D^2 - d^2)$. Substitute values step by step.

$$
Q_{th} = \frac{3.1416 \times 90 \times 0.5}{240} \times 0.3375
$$

$$
Q_{th} = \frac{141.37}{240} \times 0.3375
$$

$$
Q_{th} = 0.5890 \times 0.3375 = 0.1988 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

**Result:** $Q_{th} \approx 0.199 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}$ (≈ 199 L/s)

---

**Step (b) — Actual discharge:**

$$
Q = \eta_v \times Q_{th} = 0.75 \times 0.1988
$$

$$
Q = 0.1491 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s} \quad (\approx 149 \text{ L/s})
$$

---

**Step (c) — Power required:**

Vertical lift:
$$
H = L \sin\alpha = 6 \times \sin 30° = 6 \times 0.5 = 3.0 \text{ m}
$$

Water power:
$$
P_{water} = \gamma Q H = 9810 \times 0.1491 \times 3.0
$$

$$
P_{water} = 9810 \times 0.4473 = 4388 \text{ W} \approx 4.39 \text{ kW}
$$

Shaft power:
$$
P_{shaft} = \frac{P_{water}}{\eta_{overall}} = \frac{4.39}{0.70} = 6.27 \text{ kW}
$$

**Result:** Motor must be rated for **≈ 6.3 kW** (≈ 8.4 HP) with a safety margin.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Key Trade-offs at a Glance

| Parameter | Increase Consequence | Decrease Consequence |
|-----------|----------------------|----------------------|
| **Outer diameter (D)** | ↑ Discharge, ↑ Power | ↓ Discharge, ↓ Power |
| **Pitch (p)** | ↑ Discharge (if N fixed) | ↓ Discharge |
| **Speed (N)** | ↑ Discharge (linear), ↑ Power (∝ N) | ↓ Flow |
| **Tilt angle (α)** | ↑ Head (sin α), possible slip if too steep | ↓ Head |
| **Shaft dia (d)** | ↓ Pocket area → ↓ Q | ↑ Pocket area → ↑ Q |
| **Volumetric efficiency** | ↑ Actual Q | ↓ Actual Q |
| **Number of threads (n)** | n × Discharge | Lower Q |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# MODULE 4 — SCREW PUMP: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of an Archimedean Screw Pump System

> The diagram below is a **block-level functional architecture** (not a physical drawing, which cannot be rendered by Mermaid for 3-D helical surfaces).

```mermaid

```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow — Working Cycle of Screw Pump

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[STEP 1: SUCTION\nWater enters lowest\npocket of helix] --> S2[STEP 2: CONVEYANCE\nRotation moves pocket\naxially upward]
    S2 --> S3[STEP 3: SEALING\nAdjacent thread\nprevents backflow]
    S3 --> S4[STEP 4: DELIVERY\nPocket ejects over\ntop lip of casing]
    S4 --> S1

    style S1 fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#0b5394,color:#000
```

# MODULE 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
> **Define a screw pump and state any two of its advantages.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A screw pump is a **positive displacement rotary pump** that uses one or more helical screws rotating in a close-fitting casing to move fluid axially from the suction side to the delivery side in discrete, sealed pockets formed between threads.

**Any two advantages (1.5 marks each):**
1. It can handle **solids, slurries, and viscous fluids** without clogging or damage.
2. It gives **steady, non-pulsating discharge**, suitable for sensitive applications.
3. It is **self-priming** at moderate tilt angles (α ≤ 30° to 45°).

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
> **Explain why the tilt angle of an Archimedean screw pump is limited to about 30°–45°.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The screw pump lifts water by trapping it in helical pockets. As the screw rotates, the water in each pocket experiences two competing motions:
- The **axial component of the screw surface** (which pushes water upward) — proportional to $\cos\alpha$.
- The **gravitational component pulling water back down the slope** — proportional to $\sin\alpha$.

If $\alpha$ is too steep, gravity along the helix surface overcomes the friction holding the pocket in place, and water **slips back** between threads. The condition for stable lift is:

$$
\alpha \le \phi
$$

where $\phi$ is the **friction angle** between water and casing material. Empirically, this requires $\alpha \approx 30°\text{ to }45°$ for typical steel and concrete casings. Exceeding this range causes the pump to lose prime and discharge drops sharply.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> **Question A (14 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working** of an Archimedean screw pump. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single-flight Archimedean screw has an outer diameter of **0.5 m**, shaft diameter **0.12 m**, pitch **0.4 m**, and runs at **120 rpm**. The screw is mounted at **35°** to the horizontal and has an axial length of **5 m**. If the overall efficiency is **65 %** and the volumetric efficiency is **80 %**, calculate the **discharge** and the **power required** by the motor. Take $\gamma = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — Construction and Working (7 marks):**

**Construction (3.5 marks):**
- **Screw Rotor:** A central shaft of diameter *d* carries a helical blade wound around it like a continuous screw thread. The blade outer diameter is *D*. The axial distance covered by one complete turn is called the **pitch p**.
- **Casing / Trough:** A semi-cylindrical or fully enclosed trough of inner diameter slightly larger than *D* (typical clearance 2–5 mm) holds the screw.
- **Inclined Support Frame:** The whole assembly is mounted at an angle $\alpha$ to the horizontal (typically 30°–45°).
- **Drive System:** An electric motor through a gearbox rotates the screw at low rpm (90–180 rpm).
- **Bearings:** Upper and lower bearings support the screw shaft; lower bearing is sealed against water ingress.
- **Inlet & Outlet:** A suction funnel at the bottom and a delivery spout at the top.

**Working (3.5 marks):**
- Water enters the lowest pocket from the sump.
- As the screw rotates, each pocket moves axially upward (one pitch per revolution).
- Adjacent threads seal the pocket, preventing back-flow.
- At the top, the pocket passes over the delivery lip and discharges into the outlet.
- Multiple pockets are in transit at any instant → **continuous, non-pulsating flow**.

---

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** D = 0.5 m, d = 0.12 m, p = 0.4 m, N = 120 rpm, n = 1, $\alpha = 35°$, L = 5 m, $\eta_o = 0.65$, $\eta_v = 0.80$, $\gamma = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$.

**Step 1: Pocket cross-sectional area**

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^{2} - d^{2}) = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.5^{2} - 0.12^{2}) = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.25 - 0.0144) = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.2356)
$$

$$
A = 0.1850 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2: Theoretical discharge**

$$
Q_{th} = \frac{\pi \cdot n \cdot N \cdot p}{240} (D^{2} - d^{2}) = \frac{\pi \times 1 \times 120 \times 0.4}{240} \times 0.2356
$$

$$
Q_{th} = \frac{150.80}{240} \times 0.2356 = 0.6283 \times 0.2356
$$

$$
Q_{th} = 0.1480 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

**Step 3: Actual discharge**

$$
Q = \eta_v \times Q_{th} = 0.80 \times 0.1480 = 0.1184 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

**Step 4: Vertical head**

$$
H = L \sin\alpha = 5 \times \sin 35° = 5 \times 0.5736 = 2.868 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5: Water power**

$$
P_{water} = \gamma Q H = 9810 \times 0.1184 \times 2.868
$$

$$
P_{water} = 9810 \times 0.3396 = 3330 \text{ W} \approx 3.33 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 6: Shaft power**

$$
P_{shaft} = \frac{P_{water}}{\eta_o} = \frac{3.33}{0.65} = 5.12 \text{ kW}
$$

**Final Answer:** 
- **Actual discharge Q ≈ 0.118 m³/s (118 L/s)**
- **Shaft power P_shaft ≈ 5.12 kW (≈ 6.87 HP)**

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified Model Paper]**

**(a)** Compare the **Archimedean screw pump** and the **progressive cavity pump** in terms of construction, working, and applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A screw pump of **pitch 0.6 m**, **outer diameter 0.8 m**, and **shaft diameter 0.2 m** rotates at **100 rpm**. It lifts water through a vertical head of **4 m**. If overall efficiency is **70 %** and volumetric efficiency is **75 %**, determine (i) the **discharge** in L/s, and (ii) the **power input** to the pump. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Feature | Archimedean Screw Pump | Progressive Cavity Pump |
|---------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Configuration** | Single helical screw in an inclined open trough | Single helical metal rotor inside a double-threaded elastomeric (rubber) stator |
| **Mounting** | Inclined at 30°–45° | Can be horizontal, vertical, or inclined |
| **Working fluid** | Clean water, sewage, irrigation | Sludge, viscous fluids, oil, chemicals |
| **Stator** | No stator — uses gravity & friction | Rubber stator deforms to form sealed cavities |
| **Sealing mechanism** | Helix pockets (gravity-dependent) | Continuous seal line between rotor and stator |
| **Head capability** | Low (≤ 10 m per stage) | Moderate (up to 50 m or more, multi-stage) |
| **Flow direction** | Always upward (gravity-lift) | Can lift in any direction |
| **Maintenance** | Simple — replace screw blade if worn | Stator replacement is costly and frequent |
| **Applications** | Drainage, irrigation, fish farms | Sewage, oil wells, chemical dosing |
| **Cost** | Low | Moderate to high |

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** p = 0.6 m, D = 0.8 m, d = 0.2 m, N = 100 rpm, n = 1, H = 4 m, $\eta_o = 0.70$, $\eta_v = 0.75$, $\gamma = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$.

**Step 1: Pocket area**

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.8^{2} - 0.2^{2}) = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.64 - 0.04) = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.60) = 0.4712 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2: Theoretical discharge**

$$
Q_{th} = \frac{\pi \times 1 \times 100 \times 0.6}{240} \times 0.60 = \frac{188.5}{240} \times 0.60 = 0.7854 \times 0.60 = 0.4712 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

**Step 3: Actual discharge (in L/s)**

$$
Q = \eta_v \times Q_{th} = 0.75 \times 0.4712 = 0.3534 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s} = 353.4 \text{ L/s}
$$

**Step 4: Water power**

$$
P_{water} = 9810 \times 0.3534 \times 4 = 9810 \times 1.4136 = 13{,}867 \text{ W} \approx 13.87 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 5: Shaft (input) power**

$$
P_{input} = \frac{P_{water}}{\eta_o} = \frac{13.87}{0.70} = 19.81 \text{ kW}
$$

**Final Answer:** 
- **(i) Discharge Q ≈ 353.4 L/s**
- **(ii) Power input ≈ 19.8 kW (≈ 26.6 HP)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Screw Pump Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 60** when converting rpm to rps. Always write $N/60$, not just $N$.
> 2. **Using $D^2$ instead of $(D^2 - d^2)$** in the discharge formula. Marks lost: ~2 out of 7.
> 3. **Mixing up $\sin\alpha$ with $\tan\alpha$** in the head calculation. For small-to-moderate angles ($\le 45°$), $\sin\alpha$ is the correct projection.
> 4. **Forgetting the multi-flight factor $n$.** A double-flight screw doubles $Q_{th}$.
> 5. **Computing water power but not dividing by $\eta_o$** for the motor rating — examiners specifically look for "**power required by the motor**" = shaft power.
> 6. **Not stating units in the final answer** — always write L/s for discharge and kW (or HP) for power.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> **Important Things to Remember (KTU 2024 Module 4 – Screw Pump):**

- ✅ **Type:** Positive displacement rotary pump.
- ✅ **Main formula (memorize):**
  $$
  Q_{th} = \frac{\pi \cdot n \cdot N \cdot p}{240}\left(D^{2} - d^{2}\right)
  $$
- ✅ **Head equation:** $H = L \sin\alpha$
- ✅ **Shaft Power:** $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{\gamma Q H}{\eta_{overall}}$
- ✅ **Volumetric efficiency:** $\eta_v \approx 0.7$ to $0.85$ for clean water.
- ✅ **Tilt angle limit:** $\alpha \le \phi$ (friction angle), usually **30° to 45°**.
- ✅ **Operating speed range:** **90 – 180 rpm** (slow speed to avoid cavitation/vibration).
- ✅ **Self-priming:** Yes, by design — no foot valve required.
- ✅ **Reverse operation:** Archimedean screw can act as a **low-head turbine** (efficiency 60–80 %).
- ✅ **Key variants to know:** Archimedean Screw, Progressive Cavity Pump, Twin-Screw, Three-Screw.
- ✅ **Applications:** Irrigation, drainage, sewage lift, sludge handling, oil wells, marine lubrication, micro-hydro.
- ✅ **Specific Speed Range:** Low to medium (positive displacement class).
- ✅ **Practical caveat:** In a multi-flight screw, the discharge is multiplied by the number of helices *n*.
- ✅ **The pocket volume per pitch** is the **fundamental geometric basis** of all screw-pump analysis — start every derivation from $V = A \cdot p$.
- ✅ **For KTU numericals:** always convert N (rpm) → N/60 (rps) and remember $1 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 1000 \text{ L/s}$.
